author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peaches and Cream Victoria Sandwich Recipe
commentBody:
Hi! Recommend the Peach Fresh Brew Iced Tea from Harney & Sons. The tea bags make very delicious iced tea, think it would work well in this peach Victoria sponge cake recipe. Also, you can use it in a Harney's drink called a Peach St. Claire: 

1 oz Peach Fresh Brewed Iced Tea
1 1⁄2 oz gin
3⁄4 oz fresh lemon juice
1⁄2 oz elderflower liqueur (substitute Triple Sec or Cointreau)
1 bar spoon peach preserves
3 basil leaves

Combine and enjoy! Happy Birthday to Vaughn! Happy summer everyone!

commentBody: I’ve made Victoria sandwiches since I was 12. A long time. This looks very fussy. Why not just use whipped cream? It doesn’t need sugar, vanilla etc etc. and a tea soak. A Victoria sandwich is supposed to be beautifully dry.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peaches and Cream Victoria Sandwich Recipe
commentBody:
This was absolutely delicious. 
I gets so annoyed when people reconstruct a recipe and make I something completely different. That said I did make a few changes.
I was making this for 12-14 so I increased the cake by one half and baked in a rectangular spring form. I knew I wouldn’t use the tea bags again so I opted for a bottle of peach tea that I reduced by half. And finally I did not want my gorgeous fresh peaches cooked, so I macerated with a very small amount of sugar and a few ounces of triple sec. This got rave reviews from my guests.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peaches and Cream Victoria Sandwich Recipe
commentBody: I am so charmed by this recipe, the peach tea for the soak gives it a 'je ne se quoi' complextity.  I used an herbal tea, but I imagine one with some black tea would go particularly well if actually serving with tea. I added some peach schnapps to the soak and peaches, as I usually have around. The addition of marscapone not only gives the whip a richer taste, but also naturally stabilizes it. Whole Foods uses this for their Berry Chantilly Cake. Marscapone overwhips to grainy quickly, careful!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peaches and Cream Victoria Sandwich Recipe
commentBody: Made this for my July bday! Followed the recipe quite closely for the cake and cream. In the video Vaughn says that he started testing this in the winter which explains the compote, but in summer I definitely want to show off the produce so just used fresh nectarines (simplifying the cooking process as well). Got a lot of compliments, this cake isn’t too sweet!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Scaled back by half because I had 1.5# Yukon Golds but made per recipe for (scaled) ingredients and instructions as a trial run of the unusual combo.  After only a couple of hours in the fridge it is tasty!  I imagine it will be even better by dinner time.  I'll make it again to take to a shared dinner.  I use all these ingredients in my kitchen and often make tahini sauce but would not have thought to make a potato salad with it.  I appreciate Vaughn Vreeland's creativity.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Swapping tahini for mayo sounds delicious and unique! And the idea that mayo is the culprit behind the risk of food poisoning in picnic potato salad is a myth; it's the cooked potatoes (and other even vegan foods like vegetables and pasta) that grow bacteria when left at unsafe temperatures. Hopefully this is helpful for readers and keeps summer picnics safe and delicious!!! https://minnesota.safefoodtraining.com/servsafe-neha-best-practices/the-mayonnaise-food-safety-myth-what-every-certified-food-protection-manager-in-minnesota-needs-to-know/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want the Tupperware! Nostalgic of my childhood! Great recipe too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Different and tasty! Ultimately, I think I prefer a straightforward vinaigrette for my potato salad, but this was very good. In the future I’ll add more pops of briny flavour (thinking crushed olives plus more pepperoncini) to potato salad, with or without the tahini dressing. I’ll definitely repeat the dressing as a dip and sauce too (using an immersion blender because I’m lazy.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wanted to love this..loved the idea of the pepperoncini in a potato salad…. But did not care for at all…. Truly disliked the tahini pepperoncini combo …oh well

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe. Frankly did not like the nutty flavor on potato salad. I know the goal is a safer potato salad for the heat, but I found myself wishing I had just used mayonnaise or sour cream. I also think some bell pepper would be tasty with the other ingredients and perhaps dill. Still, I probably won’t make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for a cookout this weekend and it was a major crowd pleaser. I added in some hot sauce and mustard to give it a bit more flavor. Great easy recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Really nice change of pace.  I didn’t have pepperoncini so used sliced cherry peppers.  I should have sliced more.  It needed salt and pepper and I also used seasons salt.  Didn’t have celery so put in smell amount of red onion.  I can’t wait to see how it improves tomorrow.

My tip: use a mini food processor rather than trying to whisk everything!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used to make these for our daughter's softball team all of the time.  I would make them in a muffin tin and then there wasn't any issue cutting them, you got a whole cookie in every treat.  I would make half of the batters using double stuff Oreos and the other half I would use a full sized peanut butter cup.  The girls gobbled them down and they were indeed called slutty brownies!  I believe I cooked them for 18 minutes, it has been a while since our daughter was in school.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are soo addictive! And dangerous! Beyond good, like should be illegal in all 50 states good. As a couple people mentioned below I went the lazy route (plus I eat gluten free) & used gf refrigerated cookie dough plus a gf brownie mix, along with gf sandwich cookies. It was so hard to wait until they cooled enough to cut. Because I like the flavor profile of mexican hot chocolate, I added cinnamon and some chile powder to the brownie mix along with chopped walnuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @AJ I made these from scratch gluten free for a funeral and they disappeared! Used KAF gf flour for the cookies & brownies and gf Oreos.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used to make a version of this for my daughter and her friends. We nicknamed it The Beast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t get the towel wrap part. Wrap the pan and continue baking in the oven? Wrap pan on the counter, sprinkle salt (still on counter), unwrap, place plain pan back in oven to continue baking? What? What’s the wrap purpose??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The towel is not wrapped around the pan. The towel is placed on the counter to protect the counter surface when you remove the hot pan from the oven and rap it to make the contents settle. No wrapping as in a gift wrap. It is rapping the pan as in to give a quick tapping. You rap the pan on the towel covered counter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Wrap towel the word is “rap” (not wrap), which here means to lift the sheetpan with both hands, one at each end of the pan and forcefully slam it down on the counter. The idea is to get air pockets to break up through the batter.  I’m not sure of the purpose of the towel, but I suppose to protect the countertop if necessary.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any suggestions on how to neatly cut the cookies in half?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@BeeJay
Take the cookie apart, then press down on the cookie with a bench scraper. Then put it back together again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

May I also recommend Supernatural brownies, the recipe for which is available at NYT Cooking.   Can heartily confirm these brownies are delicious and fare well at picnics.

Thanks for a wonderful article!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy button: We made with Nestle tollhouse cookie dough (used about 90% of a 36 oz tub), 24 of the double stuff oreos, and Ghirardelli Chocolate Supreme Brownie Mix, 18.75 oz.  9 x 13” pan. It was delicious, but SO, SO much better the next day straight out of the fridge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can anyone shed some light on the need to rap the pan on the countertop during and after the baking step? How does that contribute to fudgy texture?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jaywok I think it gets air bubbles out of the batter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Need more explanation about the kitchen-towel step ... is there a video?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't know if there's a video.  I made these today (they are currently cooling).  I think the kitchen towel is just to protect your counter and possibly to keep the pan from sliding/gliding on the surface.  I just held the pan by the rims on both ends and gently dropped it to the counter (on the towel) a couple of times.  The brownie dough dropped/sank/deflated visibly when I did this.  Same thing happened on the final drop after all baking time completed.

Perhaps I didn’t prepare the slaw to perfection but I found it to be bitter and it added little to the overall sandwich in terms of flavor. The red pepper spread was ok. It wasn’t a ton of effort but it wasn’t good enough to warrant any effort in making it a second time. Wanted to love it. Didn’t.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This Italian guy isn't touching one of those sandwiches with a 10 foot pole. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Carmine why not?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were excellent. Used small french bread…because I could not find hero rolls. The bread I decided upon was very soft inside but had some “tooth” on the outside. It was not a hard crust. I also could not find Calabrian chili paste, but did have the chilis so I threw some chilis into the red pepper mix. I also used a blender. I wasn’t up to getting the food processor out. I carefully blended on low setting using the pulse feature. I did not want a purée. Everything else was as suggested. This was really good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Swap mortadella for the turkey and add mustard. You are now in hero heaven

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
One of the keys to delicious, well-made sandwiches is very simple, and is specifically suggested in the recipe for several of the sliced ingredients...Slice whatever your ingredients are...very thinly. It helps meld the flavors, and simply will taste -and "eat"- better.
For Italian-style "sammies", I usually drizzle a bit of olive oil and balsamic over the ingredients before covering with the top bread slice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: ...as well as a little salt, and a generous grinding of black pepper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Trader Joe’s has Calabrian Chili paste

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these sandos and they were great. I used supermarket fluffy hard rolls and they were perfect (just like the rolls we get at deli's for BEC's). But a great hero roll would be ideal, obviously. I used red cabbage for the slaw, and added some mayo similar to Kay Chun's recipe here: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1023278-coleslaw. No turkey (that was a peculiar addition), just good prosciutto cotto and genoa salami. The roasted ted pepper spread was perfect with sambal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I admit I did not make the recipe and probably won’t, having grown up second generation in the US of Italian immigrant grandparents and have spent my whole lifetime making sandwiches like these. I have no problem with the myriad variations there are out there. Every neighborhood makes it differently, that’s a given. 
My only quibble is with the name of the recipes it should state “Italian-American.” This type of sandwich is nowhere to be found in Italy. It is purely and proudly of the Italian-American cuisine and should be noted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: All throughout the northeast US there are sandwiches like this called "Italians." Come to Maine some time and have a couple. Nobody thinks they are from Italy. Good grief.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Actually mortadella is a better substitute than ham. Add banana peppers, tomatoes, diced dill pickles as well as Genoa and Sopressatta. Cabbage? Not in my sub!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I live in Oregon. Never heard of soppressata or Calabrian pepper sauce.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @LynDYou can purchase Calabrian Chili Paste online. Same for the sopprassata. Add banana peppers, shredded lettuce and forget the ham and turkey. It’s not an Italian sub. Add Genoa salami and tomatoes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @LynD you can buy Calabrian chilies sauce in a jar in the condiments section of Albertsons or Safeway! (Fellow Oregonian here)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @LynD. If you have a world market store nearby, they sell a delicious Calabrian chili paste. Many regular grocery stores sell it. Also, Trader Joe’s has one it’s just not as good   It comes in a jar. I am never without it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In search of good store-bought hero/hoagie rolls, preferably seeded, any recs (brands)?? (Or seeded hero roll recipe?)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not sure what part of the country you are in, but a semolina roll or loaf is always our favorite.  Grocery store bakeries often carry them here on the east coast (if you have an Italian grocer-even better) .

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you live in the Buffalo-Niagara Falls area of New York state, the bakery called Vinnie’s Minis in Niagara Falls has the best seeded hero/hoagie rolls in the region. Enjoy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My preference would be no pickles or turkey. I actually think the Italian slaw and the pepper relish sound good. I would add mayo bc I’m a glutton when it comes to Italian hoagies, heroes and subs.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: sounds delicious and, for this salami/sopressata/mortadella hater (sue me!) turkey is perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone have cold cut substitution recommendation for vegetarian?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Turkey on a hero? Heresy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just look up the NYT muffuletta recipe and make them on a ciabatta loaf.  That’s my summer picnic potluck recipe and it’s always the first thing gone and people asking for the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Two things that should never touch an Italian sub/grinder/hero
1. Mayonnaise (this really offends me, yet a number of shops add it)
2. Turkey (if you want turkey, order a turkey sub for crying out loud!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A bunch of 3-year-olds. I'm not eating that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made these twice--the first time for a crowd who tore through them all and the second time, my family ate half on the first day and the rest the following--still good and the pepper spread didn't soggy up the roll even when made the day before. I especially loved that spread and the slaw here and they'd be great used in other sandwiches, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this but instead of red onions I used thinly sliced shallots that I quick pickled. Also used Dave’s killer bread. And added tomatos. Super yummy! And yes I know this is not “Italian”.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No turkey in this one.  Try cappacola.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these sandwiches as written and thought they were delicious. I’ve got no reference for what a traditional Italian sub is supposed to taste like, but I *do* appreciate a good sandwich the red pepper sauce and slaw added lovely heat, tang and crunch to balance the meat and cheese. Totally worth making on its own merits!

author: Vaughn Vreeland

Something curdled? The coconut cream perhaps and I don’t know why it would. I followed the directions perfectly. I finally strained it and it was then drinkable without gagging. It’s not a sweet cocktail and you don’t taste the rum. In fact, I didn’t feel the rum either and it would have been to. Especially today.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Phyllis I think you could just leave out the rum and have a great drink! Maybe a little sweeter and less complex, though… in which case, adding soda water, which is fizzy and a little salty, might be a nice way to counteract over-sweetness. And/or some lime juice? Ginger? Bitters are also a great way to add flavor with a negligible amount of alcohol and there are a ton of options. Angostura, cardamom, or Bittermens Burlesque could all be good choices here.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I served the punch in a beverage dispenser on a 100+ degree afternoon. It disappeared pretty quickly! I doubled the recipe, including the nutmeg, but did only 1.5x on the rum. I used both Appleton Estate Signature Blend and Planteray 3 Stars White Rum (couldn't find Probitas locally). The punch turned out very smooth and you don't taste the rum. Next time I would double all the ingredients, including the rum. The cream of coconut did separate somewhat after I added the frozen fruit.

Thinking strawberries, raspberries, or blueberries would do really well. I’ve done this for the buttercream frosting on my kids’ birthday cakes with much success.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sebastian To keep ganache shiny, if that's your goal, add just a tablespoon or two of corn syrup (regular supermarket corn syrup, which has been around for 125 years -- not high-fructose corn syrup). It inhibits the formation of crystals which can give "set" ganache a matte finish rather than a shiny one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Nicki when I was a teacher I always said no such thing as a stupid question! Probably the ice serves three purposes 1. lowering the temperature of the butter 2. melting and deglazing the browned bits from the pan 3. replacing the water evaporated during the browning process (most American butter is 80% fat, 17ish% water) if an average is cube is around 25 grams, and we start with 227 grams of whole butter, this seems likely

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
White chocolate is not my favorite, so after reading others' comments, I opted to make just 1/3 of the ganache using one 4 oz bar. It was enough for a thin layer so I could get the fun cosmic look. 

I didn't want to dirty up a bunch of bowls, so I poured a bit of the white ganache on the blondies, mixed in pink color to the bowl, poured a bit out,  then added purple to the remainder in the bowl. End result had unicorn-party feel, but those were the brightest colors I had on hand. Very happy with the end result, both taste and look.

commentBody: I did a nondairy version of this! I used Country Crock plant butter for butter in the blonde, as well as a mix of Enjoy Life mini chocolate chips and regular sized nondairy chocolate chips. The blondies look and taste great! The ganache was a little trickier. I used Enjoy Life dairy-free white baking chips and Country Crock homestyle dairy-free whipping cream. I’ve never made white chocolate ganache before, but I suspect it isn’t usually semi-transparent or yellowish… That said, it took the food coloring fine, and while the final effect was a bit less bright and dazzling, it was really tasty. My son and I had a great time making this!

My notes are that, as expected, this is incredibly sweet. The brown butter addition is delicious. author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These were a huge hit at my 4th of July party!

For those worried about these being too sweet, they turned out well for me when I used the cup measurement of brown sugar instead of the weight amount (was in the process of weighing it and it looked like WAY too much so I just measured out a packed cup). I also used JUST enough of the ganache to cover the surface and make the design, so I had a fair amount left over. I still found it helpful to make the full amount of ganache to ensure I had plenty of each color to use. A chopstick worked really well for swirling the colors! Definitely sprinkle flaky salt on top at the end, because that helps balance the sweetness really well.

Side note, I used white baking chips and just added the warmed heavy cream to the chips/salt in a microwave safe bowl. I had to microwave for 5-10s a few times, stirring well in between, but had no issues with seizing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is a terrific recipe and
Pretty much how I’ve been making scones for years. But chocolate chips? In such a delectable scone? Noooo!!! It’s like putting garlic in brownies!  Or serving rice krispie treats at your high tea. It’s just so wrong. Dried Currants. Diced dried apricots and chopped pecans. Dried cherries and roasted almonds. So many delicious add ins that don’t taint the glorious scone.

@Beth R

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
chocolate at breakfast sometimes gives me a migraine, so I'd love to have some suggestions for alternate add-ins that are not raisins or currants.  I could just try something out myself (perhaps crystallized ginger).

Also, I am confused a bit by the backstory.  You imply that crumbly scones are a bad thing.  I don't want my scones to just be a blueberry muffin in disguise.  A buttery-crumbly scone is a pretty good thing, IMO.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tony Zingerman’s Bakehouse makes a ginger scone; the recipe is in their cookbook; based on that I suggest you add diced crystallized ginger and some powdered. For roughly the same amount of flour, they used 82 grams diced crystallized ginger and 1/2 tsp powdered.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of my favourite scone flavour combos is pear and crystallized ginger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Amazing moist cake-like texture and my husband said it was the most delicious scone he’s ever had!

I had to make a few substitutions-
No cake flour - Used equal parts AP minus 2 T and added 2 T cornstarch
No cream - mixed whole milk with some melted butter (3 parts milk 1 part butter)

So easy to make, made last night and baked this AM. Will keep this one in my back pocket!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of my most favorite scone flavors is orange and chocolate. Try adding 1 tablespoon of orange zest to the recipe. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JenC Great idea! I added the zest to the scones, then I made a glaze to drizzle on top with orange and calamansi juice. I loved it with the chocolate :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For years I baked scones using the recipe from the Once Upon A Tart cookbook and they are delicious. I haven’t tried this NYT recipe but it sounds similar. 

But after a 2-week trip last year to the UK, where I had a traditional British scone with clotted cream and jam just about every day, there’s no going back for me. American style scones are much sweeter, more like dessert. British scones are a richer, sweeter version of what we Americans call a biscuit. I’ve come to prefer them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Michele A.King Arthur's had a tea scone recipe that is unflavored and not very seeet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Michele A., Completely agree that English Scones are a completely different breed. Irish Scones, are even less sweet and a little more tangy due to buttermilk instead of cream, if a little denser, as an alternative to the sweet American style Dump Scone.
