author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm a diehard scone lover and these were probably some of the best scones I've ever had. I made one batch as written, and made a second batch to use up some raspberries that I'd frozen right on the brink of going rotten. I threw a heaping cupful of them in instead of the chocolate chips, and both batches came out amazing! At some point (after mixing, and before shaping), I observed so much loose flour that I was sure I was doomed. But the 20-30 minutes of hydration time really does the trick!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonder if GF flour would produce the same result?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Katarina I’m trolling for gf comments too. I need to google if we have gf cake flour out there!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Katarina Use equal parts white rice flour and cornstarch. Plus 3/4 tsp xanthan gum per cup of flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Watched the video. Never have I heard anybody be so vague and feelly-feelly about all-purpose versus cake flour. Do not they have any idea about what they are talking about???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am glad this recipe calls for chocolate chips. I only eat baked goods with chocolate; I don't like them with fruit or nuts. I don't understand why people are complaining about the chocolate and wanting to substitute. I'd normally say "to each their own" and enjoy my chocolate scones (no, I can never eat just one) but I find it tiresome and frustrating that when I go to a cafe or bakery the scones and muffins and other baked goods so rarely contain chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could these be made smaller?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes. They might bake a little faster.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I just made 12. Patted out the rectangle a little wider and longer, eyeballed 3 rows of 4, rested them and shaped according to the video. Started checking at about 17 minutes because the bottoms were browning quite fast -- reduced heat to 325 for 5 minutes, tested the ugliest one (!), then turned off the oven and slid them back in to cook on residual heat. Perfect.

They are very yummy, and I'm glad I tried them, but I confess a prefer a classic British scone, like Paul Hollywood's.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MnMom My patted-out rectangle was more of a 9x9” square. I made 9 scones and reduced the baking time to about 12-14 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can the recipe be successfully doubled? Scones in our household are a family get together treat - a couple of dozen are sometimes called for!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Kathy, when I want to make 2 or 3x the recipe for scones, I make one batch at a time but have the ingredients for all 3 batches measured out and ready to go. So 3 bowls of dry ingredients, whisked together, 3 cups of cream/egg, 3 piles of butter, cubed, and so forth. Mix each batch separately but you could chill them all on one baking sheet and then put them on separate sheets to bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Personally, I find the shaping a little unwieldy with a double batch of this type. You can do it, but I'd just make multiple batches. It's a great opportunity to experiment with different mix-ins.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you freeze the dough before baking?  If so, how to thaw (at room temperature or in the fridge) and baking temp and time (is it the same as refrigerated dough?)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I routinely freeze unbaked scones.  Unless they are enormous, there's no need to defrost them fully before baking.  I just put them on the baking sheet while the oven heats, then brush them with egg wash before I put them in the oven.  I usually add a few minutes to the cooking time to ensure that the middles are done.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Gluten free substitution please.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just finished my last scone! This is an excellent recipe… will make time and time again. I’m not a chocolate chip fan, so I used dried cranberries soaked overnight in amaretto… oh my, delicious! I think I’ll try Gran Marnier next time. Thanks Vaughn for bringing this recipe to us!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am so confused by the video.  Isn’t it her recipe, but he is telling her how to make it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kerrie I agree. Why have her there. It seemed very condescending to me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am new to making scones and maybe that’s why I found this recipe difficult. The dough was so dry and crumbly that I re-read the recipe twice thinking that maybe I added too much flour. I finally managed to get the dry mixture jammed into six unattractive lumps and they are now on à baking pan in the refrigerator. Then I noticed there was a video and the mother admitted that she added extra cream ! What? Why didn’t the recipe tell me to add more cream ? This feels like a waste of my time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Salley, I had to add both more cream and then some water to get this to be a dough. I usually figure I may have to add more fluid to a recipe because I live in southern California's high desert and our flour gets dried out faster, so I get a crumbly mess rather than a dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Salley Sawyer The dough absolutely needed more cream. More salt and mix-ins would have been helpful as well. They were rather bland.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Watched the video and their dough didn't seem to want to hold together.  I wonder if cutting the slab after its fridge time would solve this problem due to the hydration time it would provide.  

Also wonder why so much handling (bad for any biscuit-like dough) to get them into mounds.  Why not leave them square?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am not a baker. I followed the recipe to the best of my ability and WOW these are the best scones I’ve ever had!!! Even my dad was impressed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these yesterday and baked this morning for a swim mom brunch/tailgating event before our kids swam in a meet.  I made these bite size (got around 20-22) and they were scrumptious.  I baked for 19’or so minutes.  I saw another comment about adding dried cherries and that’s a must for next time!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: First impression- I wish that Vaughn had let Susan do the instruction, to show how she mixed the butter into the dough, etc.  Instead, we’ve got his version, which I’m sure will be great (and she did seem okay with his technique).  Also, it looks like the ratio of flours will give a protein content more like you’d see in Irish plain flour. It looks very promising, so I’ll be trying this recipe very, very soon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for the first time today. Quite easy for a delectable result! They would certainly be a treat to prepare the night before serving for breakfast in the AM

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm going to try these with apples and walnuts! Or dehydrated strawberries! Or dried figs! Pear would be wonderful too--with cardamom and a little caraway seed!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So, if you substitute another ingredient for the chocolate chips do you use a 1:1 (by weight) amount of the alternative addition? For example, 126 grams of golden raisins or 126 total grams of cheddar/scallions/black pepper, as others have suggested to use in place of the chocolate chips. In other words, in what amount of alternative ingredients would you ‘overwhelm’ the recipe and affect the texture of the scones?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Good scones.  Try this with butterscotch chips and toasted pecans - my favorite scone additions.
Cake flour is a must.  I use a food processor disc to grate a stick of frozen butter into pieces and make it easier to incorporate into the flour mixture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These scones are 5 out of 5. Susan wins!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Like others, I'm not wild about chocolate chips.  but I like the recipe.  I've made it with dried apricots and dried (soaked) cherries.  I think that fresh fruit could work here as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@G.
And the zest of a lemon!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: OMG were these wonderful!  Not hard to make and flakey. MMMMM!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It is a terrific recipe.  LOVED the chocolate chips.  Wouldn’t change a thing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Soooooo delicious. Perfect scone. I added orange zest and more chocolate—irregular bits of cut chocolate instead of chips.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fabulous texture and flavor.  Easy to make.  Watching the video provided me with a better idea of the consistency of the dough.  I made 8 scones versus the 6 and still the scones were a bit on the large size for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used my 8-scone Nordicware pan for the baking, rather than a baking sheet with 6 portions on it. The round pan fit better into my refrigerator and the portions were a bit more manageable. I left the pan in overnight. The bake time was 24 minutes. Scrumptious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've already made this twice within the past week and they're so yummy! This most recent time I only had 3/4 cup of cream so I added 1/4 buttermilk and it really came out tender! This has to be the best chocolate chip scones I've ever made!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are way too big. I made 8, and they were definitely undercooked at 26 minutes. I’ll make 12 next time. Not as light as I had hoped.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh my goodness! Vaughn, you've made me a believer! I was highly skeptical of this recipe and planned to bake 'em and then tell you what a scone is really supposed to be. However, I just ate my first one and WOW! That is delicious. Yes, with chocolate. I'm looking forward to trying it again with other add-ins. Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these last weekend and they were a huge hit. My wife lamented that the chocolate chips overpowered the scone flavor.  So, for Mother’s Day I used this recipe to make cheddar and chive scones.  I decreased the sugar to 1/2 cup, used 2 cups of extra sharp cheddar and 1/4 cup of fresh chives.  They were great!  The feedback from the family was that they could use even more cheddar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hate to reheat scones in microwave. Better in toaster oven or regular oven to maintain crunchy exterior! Diced apricot and crystallized ginger are the best add ins.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very tender and cake-like. The chocolate was delicious but the flavor of the scone could be amped up. Maybe more vanilla, or vanilla bean or almond extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I liked them, but they need WAY more chocolate chips

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just baked these scones and they are wonderful. I will be adding this recipe to my list of scones to bring for refreshments to a British women’s group that I belong to. I followed the recipe and really enjoyed the added sweetness of the chocolate chips. However, I used biscuit cutters and pieced together left over bits to form other scones. I also made 10 smaller scones, which I felt made the scones more suitable for afternoon tea.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Did 8 scones instead of 6. Equal weight of half dried cranberries half walnuts instead of chocolate chips. It was excellent. 

Next time I would split the dough into 10 or 12. Even splitting into 8 resulted in REALLY big, filling scones. 

Recommend the cranberry walnut variation, but when I make this again I’ll double the walnuts and increase the cranberries by 1.5x. It’s a lot of dough. I’m also interested in trying an orange zest or blueberry variation.

My scones didn’t get as brown on top like the video, more like a pale golden brown esp where the turbinado sugar was, but they were so delicious anyway (plus the bottom got perfectly brown).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I am very disappointed in this scone recipe. The scones are dry and cake-like. And I used frozen strawberries and blackberries chopped into small pieces, which should have made them a tad moister. Alas. I have made hundreds of scones. These were easily the worst. 

I have found Paul Hollywood’s Fluffy scone recipe, Ina Garten’s Chocolate Pecan scone recipe, and Big Sur Bakery’s scone recipe to be the best. Big Sur is unbeatable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved this recipe! The scones did come out super tender and fluffy. And so smart to add the flaky sea salt/sugar on top. Only thing I’d note is that I rolled the dough out into a circle and cut into 8 wedges instead of 6 — and ended up with 8 large scones. You could probably even cut into 10 or 12 for a smaller portion!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Maddie I went with 8 too

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We used mini-semi sweet chocolate chips and they were spectacular.  Added a more refined feel and look.  Have made them three times already, and were perfect on a rainy Memorial Day breakfast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made a double batch of these with blueberries. They are extremely dense but very tender. Left mine round: I was afraid of overworking the dough in pursuit of squares but… think I worried for nothing. 

As an experiment, I let one tray chill for an hour and then baked; the other tray chilled overnight. I could not tell any difference. 

Only mods I would make next time would be to ease up on the vanilla (might have been fine with the chocolate but it overshadowed the berries). Otherwise, yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you suggest altitude adjustment please? Cake flour doesn’t work well over 7k feet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had homemade chocolate chip scones at a shower DECADES ago and have dreamt of them ever since. I may just have to give these a go. Thx for the gorgeous recipe—and fun vid with lovable pals!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you make these smaller? Mini scones? Has anyone tried?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could I cut these into 8 squares instead? If so, should I reduce the baking time slightly? I am always on the hunt for the perfect scone recipe...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Will the recipe still work if you use only all-purpose flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @KellyZ i’m curious to know the answer too, since I live at high elevation and cake flour can be difficult  in a recipe. I’m considering changing the ratio of cake flour to regular flour? Would love to hear what other people have to say about this

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @KellyZ: the recipe will work, to a degree. Cake flour is lower in protein (gluten) and produces a more tender result. So while AP flour will result in a scone, you’ll miss out on the degree of tenderness lauded in this recipe. You might be able to swap in a few tablespoons of corn starch for a little of the flour to improve your results, but I’d suggest doing some research on how much you can substitute and still get an acceptable result.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not give gram measurements for the vanilla, baking powder and salt?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Living at high elevation, I get nervous whenever a recipe includes cake flour. Do you think that will be a problem with this recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It probably won't be a problem as it's only part of the flour. If you find they're coming out denser than you'd like, use all all-purpose and it will make them slightly chewier, but airier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tony - Google lemon-ginger scones - they're terrific.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It seems a lot like the Joy of Cooking recipe - which I make with 1/2 a cup of currants.  Always a success.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Thank you Becky’s mom!  These are gorgeous scones. I used 1/2 c mini chocolate chips and 1/2 c dried cherries. These did take a bit longer in my oven, more like 30 mins. Perfect for Sunday morning with a great cup of coffee. 

As far as dry, crumbly, tasteless scones go they are what you find in grocery stores and coffee shops across America. They are found in corporate conference rooms, baby showers, and sad brunches. They do exist!  I avoid them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I will be trying this today...with choc chips.  I may add walnuts.  My favorite scone is with apricots and white chocolate.  But...hey, I LOVE chocolate and I love scones.  Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good. Ate them within minutes of coming out of the oven, so no data on how they hold up at room temp. Rolled into a rectangle (like the video) and cut into 8 squares - could cut into 10 squares since they're a nice size. Baked for 25 min. The chocolate chips were fine, but I like the idea of adding crystallized ginger/powdered ginger and maybe chopped walnuts next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I ate one the day after baking and it held up well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I halved the recipe and baked as instructed. The scones came out delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was a joy to make, loved the video too!  Saving me from overspending on the local chocolate chip scones at our local “little sky” bakery that I keep thinking about!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: would it be possible to sub berries for the chocolate chips and if so how would you modify the recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If it helps, I made two batches of these yesterday - one as written, and for the other, I subbed a heaping cupful of frozen raspberries that were about to go bad. They came out perfectly, and I even thought that I probably could've gotten away with adding a few more (because I like my scones pretty mix-in forward)! I think this is probably a pretty malleable recipe in that regard.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It looks delicious but only 6 scones?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so tender and delicious! I had never had a chocolate chip scone before, but I'm sold now. This would also be a wonderful base recipe for any other add-ins you can think of. Next time I would probably make the scones a touch smaller, maybe make 8-10 instead of 6, but no other edits from me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make a similar scone and mine have been beloved by my family for years. The key has always been mixing in the butter with my fingers, as this recipe calls for. Works every time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried making these with buckwheat flour? Asking for a GF friend! :-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: According to the King Arthur people, who know a thing or two about flour, "because buckwheat doesn’t have gluten, you can’t simply replace all of the wheat flour in a recipe with buckwheat. Instead, start by subbing buckwheat for 25% of the wheat flour in a non-yeasted recipe (by weight or volume)." They also say that using too much buckwheat flour in place of wheat flour in baked goods can yield a "gummy" texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I may try this - but i’ve made scones for years, using a recipe from Bernard Clayton’s “”Complete Book of Breads” - currants, AP flour and buttermilk, not cream.  Delicious and easy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t generally care for scone recipes that contain egg, but I’m giving this one a try, minus the chocolate (just not a fan of chocolate) and plus some bloobs.  Looking forward to being amazed!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were really tasty! They were pretty easy and turned out nice. I just thought they were way too big, 6 scones was not enough for the amount of dough. I also had to add a bit of extra milk so the dough would come together. I'm seeing a lot of people on here who didn't like the chocolate chips. I personally love chocolate so I thought they were good but I'm curious to try some other toppings in them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I had a surplus of chocolate chips so decided to give these a try.  The mixing and shaping instructions are straightforward and fuss free.  The result is 6 large luscious rich scones. I love the resulting texture and flavor of the bread itself.  I don’t think chocolate chips are my personal favorite mix-in, but as the recipe notes indicate, the add ins are up to the baker.  

I will make these again with fresh or dried fruit and or nuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and then cut into 20 diced sized squares!  6 scones per recipe would be more scone than anyone should eat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These scones are fantastic. Crunchy on the outside and dense but tender on the inside. Just enough chocolate chips. They are a bit too big for my preference. I cut them into 8 triangles instead of 6 rounds and they were still a little big. But the recipe is so good I foresee many opportunities to experiment with size!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This worked beautifully exactly as written including taking it out of the fridge and putting it right in the oven for 30-40 minutes. It went in the oven along with the stuffing just as the turkey was resting and the gravy was being made from pan drippings. It was utterly delicious and tasted freshly made. Thank you Melissa Clark, for saving me forever from having to make masked potatoes the afternoon of Thanksgiving Day. I hated that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these for my daughter’s birthday.  Put the dough together last night, and refrigerated overnight.  Baked for 27 minutes and cooled as directed.  They were not quite cooked through.  Perhaps because they went into the oven cold?  Will make half size next time.

Also subbed with 1/2 cup dried cranberries and 1/3 cup pecans.  Will add more cranberries next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are incredible! Small complaint: the size is a tad overwhelming albeit delicious, can these be portioned out differently at the same bake time + temperature or does that need adjusting for smaller scones (either 9/12)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these and WOW! Delicious, tender crumb, just as good the next day without microwaving. Highly recommend.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are SO good. I made as written and refrigerated them overnight. Bake time needed 5 extra minutes in my very accurate oven. Like other commenters we found the size to be overwhelming. Next time - and there will definitely be a next time - I will make them smaller maybe 10 or 12 per recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious, and even the ones that caved in held their final shape. This a very, very good recipe. And I actually like the 180g size. It’s meant to be a meal after all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good! I subbed cranberries for chocolate and almond flavor for vanilla.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Actually, I just made these for myself for a Mothers' Day treat and used Guittard Bittersweet baking wafers. I was delighted with the results! First, I watched Vaughn's wonderful video. I loved the crunchy exterior combined with the tender interior. Although, I wouldn't turn down a dry, crumbly textured scone either. Thanks to Susan Herbst for sharing this recipe. I can imagine all kinds of riffs, dried pear/ginger/hazelnut, cranberry/orange etc. A new favorite for sure!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them with dried cherries and berries and they were excellent. For my taste, I’ll cut the salt in half next time -1 tsp kosher used.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have never done much baking, though I do all our cooking, but with the video I thought I’d try this for my wife for Mother’s Day.  They were fantastic!  I added the zest of an orange and doubled the chocolate chunks.  Will add more zest next time.  Also will make them smaller-we halved one and that was plenty.  Excellent recipe and video!  Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious!!! Chocolate chips, and all. These may be the most tender scones I have ever had. My son and I made them for Mother’s Day breakfast and they went very well with salty scrambled eggs mixed with some leftover andouille sausage. Sorry, chocolate chip haters. If you don’t like them, this isn’t the recipe for you, although Vaughn, in his video, suggests some alternatives you could do. This wouldn’t be an everyday breakfast item, but special occasions like Mother’s Day call for something like chocolate chip scones. Oh, and for what it’s worth, we are staying at a little beach house on the Oregon coast. I mixed the dry ingredients together before we set off so it was easy too stir everything together last night and stick these babies in the rental’s fridge till after our morning, beach walk. These are delicious. Thank you, Vaughn

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Good results! I had to add 1/4 c. more cream and then a total of 1/2 c. water to get the dough to form a somewhat cohesive mass, and bake slightly longer than the recipe time, but these are good.
However, I just can't put chocolate chips in scones. I'm British. Scones have currants.
Next time I will probably make 12 small scones rather than 6 massive ones. But this is a good size to take as a travel meal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The best scones I have ever had and I made them!  Just make these and use whatever additions you like I love chocolate so that worked for me. Next time maybe lemon and poppyseeds why not?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made them twice, both times with a small can of diced green peppers and cheddar cheese. Great recipe, and I agree with some that you could make eight scones instead of six. 

I will continue to experiment. Peaches are next.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today, and my young chocolate loving grandchildren thought they were great.  They were indeed tender. Tasty. Great scone. That  said, I agree with the comment made that Chocolate chips in scones is like adding bacon to brownies!  They don’t belong!  Next time when I make them for me I will add some dried fruit. Great scone recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good recipe. Great future substitutions from previous posters. They are VERY large. Will probably experiment with creating 12 smaller scones in the future.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are good but not great. I agree with comments to make them smaller - they’re huge. I understand the comments about wanting to add spice - but I think they’re just “scone-y”?  I mean no complaints about melty chocolate scone but I’m not sure these are worth the effort

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I am positive this was baker’s error, but mine turned out pretty dry and crumbly. I made 2 batches, one with chocolate chips and one with raspberries and white chocolate. The raspberry ones fared far better than the chocolate chip! Here’s where I think I went wrong, for anyone who is maybe looking for what not to do? 
-butter from frozen: I have a feeling I should have done from fridge. Would have helped with the cutting and incorporating 
-being too cautious about mixing: I have a feeling I under mixed. I was so worried about over mixing I think I under did it! 
those are my thoughts but I don’t really know for sure. Delicious outcome, just not quite what I was expecting!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Ali so I made these again because I really felt like I could improve them. I did a lot different, so no idea what worked (lol) but they’re FANTASTIC! 
Things I changed:
-butter from fridge not frozen 
-smaller butter cubes (so they worked in better)
-worked the dough a bit more
-added a little extra cream for a wetter dough 
-increased chocolate chip amount (maybe overdid this one)
-split into 8 scones instead of 6 (so slightly shorter bake time)
…they are phenomenal!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dried chopped figs and crystallized ginger go well together in a scone.  Enjoy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: She is a home baker, he has professional training. He is her daughter's best friend. They are close and kidding around.@Kerrie

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The first time I made these according to the recipe. So good. The second time I played around subbing white whole wheat for AP flour. Added some flaxseed and an extra egg. Flavored it with lemon zest and fresh blueberries. Still so good. This is a great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great scones but I am totally confused by the video. It's supposed to be the mom's recipe but she acts like she doesn't know what she's doing and the guy is telling her how to make her own recipe.  Annoyed

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Excellent recipe. First I made 6 and they seemed really large. Next time I made 10 out of this dough and the surface 
-crunch to mass ratio was much more satisfying. If you love sweet scones these are a home run.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was perfect, soft & creamy inside just like an Irish scone, with one orange’s zest, 125g currants, and 2 tsp almond extract (instead of the chocolate chips and vanilla).  Crispy on the outside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used frozen blueberries instead of chocolate chips— delicious!!! Love both versions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly.  To be honest, I expected to be knocked out by the them, but both my husband and I felt that they were like dense cake rather than scones.  I don't think I would make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
First time making scones. Followed recipe exactly and am so pleased with the result. So so tasty! Why are people mad it’s chocolate flavored? It’s to die for. 

Next time I make I’m going to grate the cold butter like I do for the buttermilk sugar biscuit recipe by Eric Kim.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My kingdom for equivalent salt measurements for those of us who buy Morton kosher. Or maybe a gram weight for the salt?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This might be our favorite base scone recipe on NYT (and we’ve tried a lot!). Made this enough times that I felt comfortable subbing ingredients.

We subbed frozen wild blueberries instead of chocolate chips. And because we need the extra fiber we can get, I omit the eggs and soak some chia seeds in the heavy cream for an hour or so before adding to the dry ingredients. Shockingly, we prefer the version without eggs!!! So good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added 1.5 T of grated orange peel.....very tasty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make a version of these (almost) every morning for my wife, so can report on a lot of experiments. I recommend increasing the egg a bit (two eggs per half cup of flour and cottage cheese) and adding a pinch of salt and a tablespoon of light olive oil for every half batch. Lemon zest is a nice addition, especially if blueberries will be added. Using a good whole-wheat pastry flour (Whole Foods has one; Sunrise Flour Mill's is also good) instead of white takes them to the next level.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dal Lemon zest! Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t like cottage cheese BUT my family needs more protein and not everyone wants eggs every morning. THESE. ARE. DELICIOUS!  Just so good and made exactly - for once- as written. #loveastackofpancakes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've been making cottage cheese pancakes very similar to these for years. I just blend the wet ingredients in my blender, throw the dry ingredients in for a few pulses and then pour the batter directly from the blender onto my griddle. Saves some mess and less wasted batter. A quick blueberry compote and Greek yogurt served on the side - wonderful breakfast (or lunch!) Leftovers are almost as good and can be heated in the microwave or in a skillet. Yay NYT Cooking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Outstanding! Added slightly more milk (actually cream) than called for, but the overal texture and flavour were delicious. Made about 14 pancakes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What changes would be needed to make waffles instead? (I have a new waffle maker and love the thing)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Old Lady Jar of Lemons has a great cottage cheese waffle recipe https://www.jaroflemons.com/cottage-cheese-waffles/#wprm-recipe-container-163849

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Old Lady I made this in my waffle maker and they came out just fine. Not my favorite waffle recipe, but still pretty good. I made it with graham flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best pancake texture (light and spongy) and simple to make!  Followed instructions as written!  Used a vitamix to blend wet ingredients.  This one is a keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I live in a tiny place without a blender, so I made them by hand by using smooth cottage cheese I had left over from a lasagne - just beat all the step 1 ingredients together before adding in the cottage cheese, and then follow the recipe from there.

I also added more milk and next time will try adding a little lemon flavour to these pancakes, which taste quite a bit like blintzes to me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I remember making cottage cheese pancakes with a recipe from one of those old vegetarian cookbooks from the 80s. There wasn’t any need to blend the ingredients—they were light and delicate without being cheesy. Best with a light fruit compote. Syrup would be too heavy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I grew up eating these from the More-With-Less Cookbook. Their recipe calls for an extra egg and no sugar or baking soda. I also regularly swap half of the flour for ground oats to boost the fiber a little.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ricotta seems like it would mix more easily than cottage cheese and be more fluffy and creamy, and less dense and 'curdy'. I make ricotta Xmas cookies every year and they come out light as air.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just wanted to share a big success I had with this recipe. We recently started refocusing our diet to include more protein. Pancakes were something we sort of gave up on, so when I saw this recipe, I was really excited. I replaced half of the flour with vanilla protein powder, which added more protein and removed the need to add vanilla or sweetener. The pancakes came out amazing. The whole family ate them without noticing they weren’t the usual pancakes. The batter is super thick, almost like pudding, and it doesn’t get bubbles on top before you have to flip it so you kind of have to check it after a minute or so. But they were so great, and the protein was so high that this was a big win for us in our new regimen! The recipe couldn’t have come along at a better time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Our family has been making cottage cheese pancakes for decades. The recipe is very simple and we never really measure the 3 ingredients - small curd full fat cottage cheese, eggs and cornmeal (coarse or fine depending on preference). Whisk eggs ad cottage cheese together then add just enough cornmeal to bind the wet ingredients - ENJOY!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you sub ricotta cheese?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Pretty good with blueberries and whole wheat flour. Cooked some batter one day later and also good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Only had pre-portioned cottage cheese cups which were 5.3oz. Just used a single tub and left the rest of the quantities as written. I did use buttermilk what I had on hand instead of regular milk and these turned out great. My 11 yr old who wouldn’t come within 10 feet of a serving of cottage cheese ate them without issue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Also look up cheese pancakes in the Art of Jewish Cooking by Jennie Grossinger which also uses cottage cheese but uses whipped egg whites like a soufflé

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @varkentje Grossingers cottage cheese pancakes are the best!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are very easy to make and so delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have several questions for the author: why baking soda instead of baking powder? Why no fat in the batter? Why canola instead of butter? Why so little flour? (The batter is very wet and doesn’t stay very lofty after cooking). Curious to get your thoughts - thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I buy protein (20 grans) pancakes from Trader Joe’s. I eat them with syrup that I got from Canada and fresh strawberries. Done.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a little less milk (2 tbsp) and added 1 tbsp of canola oil since I only had low fat cottage cheese . They were delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really really good, I highly recommend them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added cinnamon and ginger as I do for any pancake and the overall flavor was solid (a pinch of salt as well). I think I prefer the texture of a more traditional pancake to this recipe, as these were rather dense. I would eat these again, as nutritionally they are really good for getting protein.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Works perfectly for waffles!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious ! Have shared with my sister for her grandkids who love cottage cheese and old friends who love it also. So simple, almost crepe-like in texture. Added to our other favorite pancake recipes like wheat and wheat pumpkin. Give up the Bisquick :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like oats in my pancakes. Any suggestions on how to incorporate oats into this recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Polly Rotan Try reducing the cottage cheese by 1/4 cup.  Add 7 tbs milk to 1/2 cup either quick cooking oats or very coarsely ground old fashioned oats.  Let the oats/milk mixture sit for a few minutes and add it to egg/remaining cottage cheese mixture.  Then reduce flour to 1/2 cup and proceed with recipe.  You might need to play around with the amount of oats, but something like this should work.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Polly Rotan

I substitute half of the flour with oat flour - I use Bobs Red Mill oat flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Grind up raw oats (that you make oatmeal from)in your food processor or blender and substitute for flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Baking soda ? Where is the leavening agent ? Does this recipe not require buttermilk, or does cottage cheese act in its place ?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I guess it must -- they rise fine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I use almond or coconut flour instead of AP flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I substitute whole wheat flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so yummy!  I put blueberries on 1/2 of them and froze the extras for another morning.  Such a good recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: WaPo ran a recipe like this with whole wheat flour & pineapple & honey a few years ago. Healthier, I think. I make them all the time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Full fat cottage cheese is hard to find. Unlike whole milk cottage cheese says 4% - my grocery says 4% is equivalent of full fat - so why isn’t it labeled that way.  Or maybe recipes should clarify that so readers don’t drive to lots of grocery stores looking for an ingredient that says full fat cottage cheese. Or maybe 4% is NOT full fat. Anyone know the answer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these as directed using gf flour (celiac), one with blueberries, one with banana,they were dense, a vessel for maple syrup. I think like a lot of pancake recipes the ratios can be adjusted and these need adjusting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone have the macros?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are they eggs really necessary? Has anybody tried without the eggs and got a good result?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Will the batter last a few days in the fridge?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made as written. They came together quickly. I did have the skillet too hot for the first batch, but they were still edible.
I like the flavor and texture, except they were quite dry. It took more butter and syrup than I would prefer. They also cooled off REALLY fast.
Could I add more milk without causing trouble? Could I add a tablespoon or two of yogurt to the batter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So easy & delicious . . . added a bit more milk so they weren't so thick and served with a wild blueberry compote to make it even healthier! I will definitely make again and experiment with using a healthier type of flour too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Halved the recipe so used 2 eggs per half cup as recommended with a pinch of kosher salt. Made mine larger and see why making them with 1/4 c is a bit nicer. Still delicious but they are thinner and eggier, so going with smaller makes sense. Cooked them in butter. I ate three right out of the pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is extra yummy with ricotta and lemon zest.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I also make a variation (with a greater proportion of cottage cheese). Some salient thoughts:
1. Full fat cottage cheese is by no means a requirement. I use fat-free and it’s just fine.
2. Absolutely no need for baking soda, at least not given the proportions of ingredients I use.
3. Also no need for milk. I use a lot of cottage cheese, eggs or egg whites, and adjust the proportion of dry ingredients to get the batter consistency I like.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Oh, I forgot …
4. With tge sugar and maybe some cinnamon, no need for syrup. But if I add a topping, it’s a fruit-sweetened fruit spread. 
5. Whole grain flour much tastier. I use whole wheat flour and often some corn meal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: wowowow. These are easy to make and delicious as written. will be adding these to my weekend pancake rotation

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good! I added the zest and used the juice from half a lemon to add to some blueberries, and added 1.5 T chia seeds to that, mashed a bit , let sit while doing everything else, then added a little maple syrup to top them. Delicious and adds more fiber and protein

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious, but note that the listed quantities don't make a whole lot of pancakes - about ten at a size of 4-5". They are filling thanks to all the protein, but three of us polished the whole recipe off easily, and we are not big eaters. If your crew are, you'll want to scale up the recipe a bit.

Separate note: I too was weirded out by the use of baking soda with no acid to make it rise, but it works fine.  Suspect it is actually the large number of eggs that are doing the heavy lifting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cottage cheese is acidic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a half recipe (one egg) which made four delicious pancakes.  Topped with greek yogurt, jam, walnuts and syrup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone worked out the nutritional stats?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out dense and chewy for me. Not the correct pancake texture. And I even undermixed the wet and dry ingredients to the point of having some too-large flour lumps. I think I would rather have normal pancakes and a bowl of cottage cheese.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My new favorite pancake recipe--texture, taste--very satisfying. You can sense the extra protein in every bite, but the pancakes are still light and absorb maple syrup perfectly. I'm eager to try some variations--maybe apples and cinnamon?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No salt?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have been making these for a while and I add half a teaspoon of salt. Good catch!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This works best if  you allow the flour to hydrate for a GOOD 15-20 minutes before cooking them.  Overnight is good too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have been making a nearly similar recipe for several years, and they're outstanding! I only use a teaspoon of sugar and they're still great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used this recipe a couple of times, it is a good one. A flour option I like is King Arthur's Golden Wheat flour, used to be called White Whole Wheat, I've even used half whole wheat and half all-purpose. They do freeze well, so in a two-person household, that's good. I also have used 4 eggs instead of three, but have used 3 eggs and an extra egg-white saved from other recipes. Don't add salt but use salted butter and sprinkle with powdered sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Surprisingly fluffy and delicious. I used half the vanilla, which was plenty—enough to taste it. This will be my usual pancake recipe from now on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just wondering why some recipes use baking soda (this one), some use baking powder (Mark Bitman’s), and some use both (Melissa Clark’s). What’s the best option?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A great alternative: turn these into cottage cheese patties. Swap the cup of flour for just 3 tablespoons of semolina, reduce to 2 eggs, and wring the cheese as dry as you can in a cloth (moisture is the enemy here). Everything else stays the same, though you’ll probably want to dial back the sugar and let your toppings do the work. Form into patties and fry. I love these far more than the pancakes and they feel healthier, though I’m not sure they actually are

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m not a huge pancake fan, but these were very yummy. Less bread-y and more rich. Next time I will add a pinch of salt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I started making something similar but with Bisquick. Couldn’t be easier. Most of the time I also use fresh bananas. I make a smaller batch for one. I just use consistency as my indicator adding the Bisquick after the other ingredients so I won’t beat it in too much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The minute I saw this recipe posted I gasped, pretty much sprinted to the grocery store, and whipped it up for a party my roommates and I hosted this evening. Two of my favorite desserts in one?! Incredible. It was a giant hit and guests took seconds and thirds! Thank you, Vaughn, for this delightful recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JuliaI felt exactly the same when I saw the recipe. As soon as I get my hands on some Mochico I will be making it. Thank you, Vaughn!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious chewy. Used dates instead of golden raisins. The dates were chopped fine and mashed into the heated butter. I used half sugar and was quite sweet. Overall delicious snack without the glaze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can plant based butter be used without compromising the uniqueness. I use Melt brand butter in cooking and baking but this seems like a unique blend resulting in a different textured cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sharon yes. I used earth balance and it came out fine for the dough.  For the icing, you could try kite hill cream cheese or go with a simple glaze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this last night (sans raisins) and it was AWESOME. The chewy texture is addictive and Is great room temp or straight from the fridge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Halved the recipe, left out the fresh ginger and raisins and used Bob's Red Mill Sweet Rice Flour--baked for exactly 45 minutes in an 8 by 8 Pyrex dish. Turned out perfectly and was delicious, will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: ooh it's gluten free. anyone know how to make this egg free? I know, I know. i wish I could do eggs.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Georgette P.-1 tablespoon of flaxseed w 3 Tbsp of water, let it set up. So, 1 tablespoon equals one egg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Georgette P. I haven’t tried this recipe yet, but I’ve always had good results from Bob’s Red Mill Egg Replacer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wonder if you could use JustEgg also, 2 eggs - 1/2 C JustEgg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made 1/2 a batch and baked in an 8” square pan. Omitted raisins because we don’t like them, it was delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What about dairy free? Substitute for the butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m convinced the negative comments here are from people who either simply don’t like mochi or somehow messed up this recipe, because I made it pretty much as written (used sweetened coconut because it was all I had and decreased the sugar a bit to compensate) and got exactly what I was expecting! Chewy, spicy, carrot-y, all around terrific. Delicious on its own, but I bet it’d be even more incredible with a cup of tea.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it as written. I’ve never had butter mochi but come to think of it, the pairing of butter and mochi doesn’t appeal to me. As others have posted here, it was hard to cut with the hard edges and the entire slab was the height of a brownie, a very low brownie at that. I wouldn’t call it a cake. The predominant note was the chewiness. I did not taste the carrots so I’m thinking I should have really packed the grated carrots in the measuring cup. In terms of taste, I primarily tasted the coconut. Also, the lemony glaze, which I found very good and I’ll make again for cookies calling for icing. But I didn’t like the chewiness at all. I agree with the comment that this was time consuming to make. Also, the ingredients were not cheap collectively.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm gonna try this first with glutinous rice flour, as there's no way I'd find mochiko here where I live ...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @J Mochiko is a brand of glutinous rice flour

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @J it’s the same thing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @J just remember to look for sweet rice flour. It makes a difference.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could this be made without the dried coconut? it sounds delicious but I don't like dried coconut. I don't have a problem with coconut milk though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could this be made without the coconut? It sounds delicious but I really hate dried coconut.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would think so, yes. The recipe looks as if it would be friendly to variations. Maybe sub toasted chopped walnuts or pecans if you want the texture but don't want the coconut.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with the Trader Joe’s boxed Mochi Mix? It’s really really good and would save some steps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: First time making mochi! Followed recipe and turned out great. Love the chew and the glaze. But watch it in the oven: Mine was a little over (edges too brown), and I pulled it at an hour and five min. Fun bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delish! I made it for our Easter dessert. It was bouncy and chewy, and the glaze is such a nice burst of citrus to cut the dense sweetness of the cake. I made mine extra lemony! Even my picky teenagers liked it. 

I had leftover Koda Farms Mochiko, since I bought two boxes, and I used the remaining to make Everything But the Bagel Mochi rolls to go with dinner. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This recipe requires further research and development in the test kitchen. Fresh from the oven, the texture was already akin to tough rubber.

And the flavor profile is overly complex. Cinnamon, coconut, ground ginger, grated ginger, cardamom, and carrots — it sounds intriguing on paper, but in reality, the flavors did not harmonize well.

As for the cream cheese lemon glaze, I had my doubts. Do carrot and lemon complement each other? Trust me, they do not.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It's okay.  So why all the rave reviews? Maybe they are all Tootsie Roll fanatics.  It's very "chewy".  The icing is heavy too.  I used the special recommended rice flour too.  So, I made it once...and I'm done.  I'll take a traditional carrot cake over this heavy dish every day...and twice on Sunday.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone cooked this yet? If so - reviews?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am going to request this for my birthday in a few weeks for sure. But... why butter *beneath* the parchment?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So the parchment stays put on the bottom of the pan and thus doesn't slide around when you're trying to even out and smooth the batter before it goes into the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @William Wroblicka anytime I put parchment in a pan, I lightly butter the pan first and that way the parchment doesn’t move around

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I omit the coconut?  I don't like it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I think I must have over baked this, even though it only was in the oven for 50-60 minutes. The top was just starting to create cracks and separate from the edges, but it was difficult to cut - the outside edges got so tough, and the inside was very dense and more chewy than I think it was supposed to be. Super disappointed, as this is pretty time consuming to bake! 
Also, my family did not appreciate a non-traditional dessert for Easter lol

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I couldn’t source the Koda Farms flour in time so I used the glutinous rice flour available at my local Asian market - perhaps that’s why it didn’t turn out as described.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same experience! Followed the recipe to a T, but found it dry, rather dense, and lacking in flavor. It was refrigerated for a day before we enjoyed it, so maybe that contributed to the dense crumb. But everyone was disappointed by the lack of the bounce/spring you expect from a mochi dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used the special rice flour...and mine came out the same as yours. Very dense and chewy.  If your favorite candy is tootsie rolls, this is your dessert.  For anyone else, this is a pass.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: LOVED this recipe, and my whole family (including people who had never had mochiko/rice flour goodies before) LOVED it too!!! Unfortunately I realized I didn’t have any ground ginger in the middle of making it, so just put in a teeny bit more cinnamon and cardamom. I’m sure it’s great with ground ginger but it was fab without too! I also added lemon zest to the frosting. Delish!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as written with two modifications for a large family gathering: coconut oil for the butter and vegan cream cheese (to make this a GF and DF dessert). As both these substitutions were coconut based, the overall flavour profile leaned more heavily towards coconut than a true butter mochi version would have. Still very tasty with lots of positive feedback from the family. Definitely worthy of a repeat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for an Easter gathering with friends and it was a hit! Great consistency + taste, and easy to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made this recipe and WOW.  It's definitely a do over and over and over.  The lemon in the cream cheese glaze is very nice.  I'd like to add nuts to mimic more of a carrot cake texture.  Does anyone know how this will work with rice flour?  Also, I will experiment and add some nutmeg and ground cloves to give it a stronger carrot cake flavour.  But overall, this is a really nice dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sadly, regular rice flour is really different than sweet rice flour, so the texture would be quite different.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I love cakes that use mochi flour, so this felt like a wonderful match for a carrot cake. It was really easy to make, and the result was not only really beautiful to look at, but delicious to boot. My family keeps having two or three servings each time.
It's chewy with a little bounce to it, and it's surprisingly not as sweet as I would have expected. Would definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Well received at my condo's Cinco de Mayo celebration. I zested the lemon and added the zest to the glaze, which seemed to be the same to do. I also made the cake and refrigerated it one day and then glazed it right before serving and it was fine. Very tasty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Another NYT 5-star recipe that baffles me. Weighed ingredients, used the recommended sweet rice flour...all the things. The recipe is terrible. The mochi is dense with competing flavors and less than ideal texture. It's not hard to make, but has many steps. I don't know, mashing carrot cake with mochi should have been my first clue. If you like carrot cake, make that. If you like mochi, stick with a basic recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does this only work as a sheet cake or do you think it could work as a Bundt cake too? Thx!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Probably too dense for a bundt

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@CJ
Waaaay too dense for a bundt cake. The texture would be nowhere near close. If you’ve not had mochi or used mochiko before, keep in mind it is a rice flour and therefore contains no gluten. Gluten is a long protein that when developed, give spring and lift to baked goods made with wheat-based flour. Notice the (2) teaspoons of baking powder in the recipe— that gives you a clue as to how much help rice flour needs to give it any kind of rise.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m about to hit the button on ordering the flour.  Any good ideas on what to do with the rest of the pound of mochiko that I’ll never use?

A one pound box Koda Farms Mochiko is 454 grams, so the recipe uses the entire box.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Looks right up my street and perfect for the upcoming Easter celebrations. I read it can be prepared in advance, but any experiences? And would you also add the frosting
a few days before serving or better on the day?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was so excited to make these, but was a little disappointed. I cooked less than the cook time, but the crust turned out a little too crunchy/hard. The taste was also a little too gingery for us, and we love ginger! I may try again cooking closer to 55-60 mins and less fresh ginger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any suggestions on using a non-refuned sugar sweetener option?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Jaggery, also known as gur, available in Indian grocery stores cheaper than on Amazon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine was very dense, it tasted great but it had no lift to it at all. The cake in the video looks a bit softer in the middle than mine did. I’m not sure if my baking powder is too old or if I measured something wrong. I always weigh all my ingredients. I didn’t add raisins either as my son hates them. But the flavor was excellent and everyone loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JD same experience for me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: substitute medjool dates diced up for raisins

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not for me.  Loved the spice and coconut flavors.  Maybe wheat flour next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this for Easter and it was a huge hit!! Multiple family members told me multiple times how good it was (this from Boomers not known to have adventurous palates). I accidentally made the following changes to it (due to forgetting or not having things):

only about 1/2 tsp cinnamon 
heaping 1/2 tsp cardamom 
no golden raisins
no candied ginger

With this spice balance the cardamom was really the star, but not overpowering.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is going to be my new potluck dessert. I got rave reviews when I brought this to work for a birthday.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine was done in 40 minutes. The time in the recipe does seem quite long.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nice Mochi texture. Love the ginger bite on top - could have used more. Mine were a little tough at the edges - even after the shorter end of the cooking time in a glass 9x13. These are pretty thin - like a brownie rather than a cake - I might try in a smaller pan for a petit four effect next time. My golden raisins had aged to brown in the cabinet and the store was out - I should have omitted them or subbed with apricots - next time. Would have been plenty sweet without them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kristin in AK i’ve had good luck with soaking dried fruit in warm water (or juice, liqueur, etc.) to soften it up. somehow there always seem to be two overly dried figs left in the package from whatever recipe i tried last.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used sweetened coconut instead and cut the sugar down to 1 cup and 1/4 cup. I also frosted half only (for those that like less sweet). Didn’t have powdered sugar so did a maple ginger frosting instead. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Really delicious, everyone had seconds! The glaze is an absolute must.

I baked this recipe in two 8-inch cake rounds for about 50 min around 365 F (poor temp control on my oven). The top was quite dry and crackly, and the edges were tough to cut – but the inside was delightfully and perfectly chewy. The raisins add great texture as well.

I used a generic glutinous rice flour instead of Mochiko. When weighing it out, I realized it was way past 3 cups at 440g. I stopped there, and next time I'd measure by volume rather than weight if not using Mochiko. Despite this hiccup, it turned out quite well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved the recipe and it was a hit but I did have to double the frosting recipe, FYI!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious. Left out most of the ground ginger based on comments about it being "too gingery," this was a mistake. Will use recipe amounts of both next time. The tang of cream cheese in the glaze was delicious but it took the sweetness level nearly over the top. Left out raisins because I don't like them. For fellow high altitude dwellers: at 5Kft, reduced sugar for the cake by about 20 g and baking powder to 1.5 tsp = perfect rise and texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Easter brunch and overall it was good! Next time I would skip the lemon in the cream cheese frosting (cream cheese has a bit of acidity by itself anyway) and sub milk because I think the citrus flavor clashed a bit with the mochi.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this minus raisins minus icing and it was perfectly sweet for us! Thanks for the recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I liked the flavor of this dessert, but it was a little too sweet for my wife and I and we both found the texture too chewy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you want to make a mochi cake, this recipe is a great option. I halved it, added chopped toasted pecans, omitted the coconut. Baked in a 8 x8 pan and it turned out great. The browned butter is a must, very tasty carrot cake flavor, nicely spiced, and delicious chewy texture. Even better the next day! Everyone enjoyed it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: how long will it keep in the fridge?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @John G 3-4 days

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: to skin the hazelnuts, I put the toasted nuts into a metal sieve and roll them around till the skins come off.  Easy peasy.  Its like using sandpaper on the nuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those who like chocolate mousse and are expecting a recipe for chocolate mousse to yield a result with a mousse-like texture, this recipe may disappoint. The defining characteristic of a mousse is a light and airy texture, achieved by folding beaten egg whites or whipped cream into a denser base.  As noted in the prologue, this here is actually a recipe for chocolate custard -- quite delicious no doubt, but having a completely different "mouthfeel."

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @William Wroblicka Even as I’m making this, I’m noting that the ‘mousse’ is quite thin, even after I’ve beaten it, so I’m expecting more of a custard texture after it’s chilled.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: “Over medium heat, scald the cream mixture, whisking occasionally, by heating it to 170 degrees, 5 to 7 minutes” - sorry, are we keeping it at 170 for 5 to 7 minutes or it’ll take 5 to 7 minutes to heat it to 170?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mel It will take 5 to 7 minutes to reach 170.  You will want to remove it from the heat at 170 degrees.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What brand and product of chocolate does the restaurant use? Surely a really good outcome turns on this — they are not interchangeable. Scharffenberger? Callebaut? Valrhona?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would assume that is in the eye of the beholder. I used Verkade. It was delicious, but I look forward to trying it with different chocolates as I find them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tina i use trader joes dark chocolate (the 18oz bars). Red package. Why not? Tasted amazing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dallas I also used Trader Joe’s dark chocolate bars for this recipe and it was superb.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Adele Your hesitation is understandable...but supermarket corn syrup (Karo's or whatever) is NOT the same as High Fructose Corn Syrup, which is the one with various health concerns. Standard corn syrup is 100% glucose (zero fructose), a much simpler, straightforward product that's been around for over 200 years, and is no more suspect than baking soda or table sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: From a non-US subscriber: What would you suggest as a substitution for corn syrup? Golden syrup has no real flavour beyond sweetness, so would that work, or is something flavourful like maple syrup better?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Golden Syrup should be fine, (light) corn syrup has literally zero flavour, much less even than Golden, so there's nothing to miss out on there

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Indonesian Patchouli corn syrup also has no flavor— my guess is that it is used instead of some of the sugar because of the smoother texture provided to the final product. I would bet you could sub golden syrup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Indonesian Patchouli
Glucose syrup (no flavour) would be closer to the original recipe rather than golden syrup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Interesting recipe but having discovered the best chocolate pudding recipe about 35 years ago - Richard Sax's Double Chocolate Pudding in his book Classic Home Desserts - I am not tempted by this. Richard Sax's pudding is the best - full of chocolate but not as sweet or fatty as this.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Blueberrygirl, Ottawa, Ontario Canada
I ❤️ Richard Sax so much!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Blueberrygirl, Ottawa, Ontario Canada Have you tried his Cloud Cake. Peerless decadence!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Corn syrup, such as Karo, is not the same as the dreaded high-fructose corn syrup, so using a bit here and there in life should not be a concern.

I'm more curious about what type of olive oil to use - pure? extra-virgin? fruity? not?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't love using corn syrup -- is there a good replacement?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Adele mild honey

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Corn syrup is used to prevent crystallization, add moisture, and to facilitate a smooth mouth feel. It has pros and cons like any other sugar and it can shine here.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Adele Don’t confuse corn syrup with high fructose corn syrup. That one’s the villain.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wondering if anyone who actually made it could comment and tell us how it went, was it good, etc?  With 17 ratings, someone has... Love reading comments for tips, real cooking times, cautionary tales etc. although all of these comments so far are helpful too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it yesterday, almost exactly per the instructions, and it was GREAT. Very rich. I wasn't able to get hazelnuts, so I substituted raw macadamia nuts in the caramel/brittle. They are delicious! My grocery store had beautiful raspberries, so I put three on each serving for a little bit of color and slight tartness. It was delicious, and I will DEFINITELY make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this yesterday and it is fantastic. I used extra virgin olive oil which really gives this mousse great complexity.  I have made many mousse and I think this is the best.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i just made it & thought it was overly complicated- it could be simplified a bit. For example the whole hazelnut thing was not worth it...i would use strawberries/ raspberries on top w a dollop of whipped cream. Roasting, getting the skins off was a nightmare & they werent even that good..the caramel thing-- not good-- not enough to coat the nuts before it solidified - i'd omit entirely next time & go w berries. Also there is no need to strain the mixture IMHO. but taste of choc was divine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If one makes the vanilla oil, do you add 1 tsp instead of extract or do you pour the oil over a serving as in the video?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful dessert! Used Lyle’s golden syrup, Trader Joe 70% dark chocolate bar and macadamia nuts. Watching the video helped to visualize the steps (and Vaughn is very entertaining). No worries if you can’t wait the 4 hours for it to firm up-it’s great after it cools for 1 hour in refrigerator.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely fantastic, albeit extremely rich! Here’s a tip for leftovers: we just tested, and you can indeed freeze the mousse. Just thaw it for at least 15 minutes on the counter, or longer in the fridge, before serving it. We didn’t notice any difference in the texture; it was just as creamy and rich as before freezing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: you can use a bar of good chocolate with right % cocoa or i used almost a full bag of ghirardelli 72% dark choc chips (used the 200 grams in the recipe) and it was great. With the chips you dont have an extra step to cut the choc into small pieces.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made half a recipe just to try it and it was easy and heavenly. But I totally failed at melting the sugar for the caramel. I was doing a full recipe for the hazelnut part since I knew we would be snacking on them. Being from Oregon, I had plenty of hazelnuts on hand.  They toasted easily and the skins came off pretty well.  However, I tried 3 times to melt the sugar and each time got a clump of crystals, even the last time where I barely stirred the sugar. I’ve melted sugar before for flan, so I’m not sure what the problem was. I finally gave up and added a bit of corn syrup and was successful.  We all loved the outcome and will definitely make this again for guests. I love a make-ahead dessert!  Depending on the season I will top with fresh strawberries or raspberries but will also include toasted hazelnuts for that crunch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i too had trouble with the sugar but the "swirling" worked ..and i used a spoon - it eventually all melted like for flan. However it set too quickly and didnt cover all the nuts and that was disappointing to me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am in Europe and couldn't find corn syrup. Honey did the job just fine. Otherwise, I have nothing to say except that I will be making this for the rest of my life, and only swapping out different chocolate bars in search of perfection. The texture is incredible, and it's extremely easy to make. Also, don't worry that if it seems a bit loose when you stick it in the fridge, just know that it will take all of the recommended time to get firm.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: That's a great point about the texture - trust the process and it will be fine after chilling! I'm so curious to try this with different chocolates as you mentioned. It would be some fun experimenting!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What would be a good substitute for hazelnuts for someone allergic to them?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Judy H-M  I used Costco’s roasted and salted peanuts. I did not put in the oven. Just added directly to the caramel. Like the staff at Penny’s I cannot walk by without grabbing a few.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I feel ut is safe to say that you can use any other nut you want, just choose your favorite one. swapping out the nuts will do nothing to the actual structureor flavor of the custard,  only change the flavor profile if the dessert in its entirety. He mentions in the video this tastes like a deconstructed Nutella,  so say you used peanuts instead, it will taste more like a deconstructed Resses PB Cup. I say have some fun and play around with different options until you find your favorite(s) @Judy H-M

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Deeeeelicious. Felt fancy on a Monday night. Used Aldi Specially Selected 62% Belgian chocolate chunks, result was smooth and creamy and delicious. Low effort, very high reward—exactly what I look for in a recipe like this. Instead of the quart size container I strained into a 8-cup measuring jug, then poured into little glass jar containers, plastic wrap on top, cooled for about 6 hours. Served in the jars with candied nuts on top.  Five stars from 2 grown ups and 2 teens. 

Instead of the hazelnuts (didn’t have any), I did some quick candied pecans on the stove (rough chopped about a cup of pecan halves, toasted for a couple minutes in a cast iron skillet, add about 1/3 cup granulated sugar and a pinch of salt, stir stir stir until the sugar is melted and caramelized, pour out on parchment paper and cool).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I can't get corn syrup in the UK, can I use golden syrup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Glucose syrup would be a better sub. Use it 1:1.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The video states that the corn syrup, used here to help prevent crystallization of the caramel, is a “workaround” to inverted sugar. Golden syrup is only a partially inverted sugar with a different flavor profile than inverted sugar. There are recipes on the web for inverted sugar, but I don’t know if they are reliable. Perhaps the best thing would be to stick with the corn syrup, per this recipe.

Okay I made this & did not have corn syrup so I made my own with water, sugar, cream of tarter & salt. I added the two Tbsp & made everything else as directed. My friend came over going on about her signature mousse. I was happy to serve mine. Chilled for 5 hours & my spoon could barely penetrate the most perfect fudge I have made! It was a bowl of fudge, smooth, set up perfectly. It had to be my homemade corn syrup that is also hard now that it’s cooled.
I will be making it again with the store bought corn syrup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this exactly according to the recipe. Extraordinary mouthfeel, super-sexy texture. Delish brittle. Used Ghirardelli 72% chocolate. I was shocked that I wanted it to be MORE chocolaty. If repeated, might swap in an once of unsweetened for the bittersweet…and try swapping the olive oil for hazelnut oil! Made half recipe to good effect.

The hazelnut brittle is delicious! if I made this again I might go even darker with the chocolate than bittersweet, or combine some bittersweet with unsweetened. A dollop of creme fraiche or lightly sweetened whipped cream would be a good addition

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was amazingly good. The only thing I did differently was to put in the 8th ounce of chocolate - didn't want to have a single square hanging out in my pantry. This will definitely become a "go to." Vaughn, thank you for sharing this with us!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was delicious and very simple to whip together. I definitely recommend having all your ingredients portioned out as the “active” cooking time is quick! I didn’t have nuts so I crush freeze dried raspberries on top and it was delicious. I think chopped pistachios would be great too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It took me 2 times to get this right (operator error), but it was worth going back for another try for Valentine's Day dinner. It's ethereal .. and holds up well several days in the fridge! Also, it did not spike my husband's blood sugar!! When you make the caramel, do it in a light colored pan.. I couldn't see the color well enough in my cast iron to judge the color and thus was too anemic (again, operator error). I loved how the mixture can be sculpted onto the serving plate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This should not be made in a stand mixer (huge mess, not enough aeration). It’s glorified pudding. Maybe if you make it into a blender it aerates it enough to be a little different, but that wasn’t my experience.  Not making it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe twice. In 2 days. First batch I followed every step  precisely and yet after being refrigerated overnight it had a soft pudding like consistency and was not nearly as dark as the mousse pictured. I used 64% valhrona chocolate. Was that why? So stubborn me tried again, using a 72% valhrona and my immersion blender vs hand mixer. Refrigerated 9 hrs and it was definitely more firm/thick, but still nothing like Vaughns thick dark glossy “mousse”. Still tasted great. But I’m baffled as to why the more pudding like consistency for some of us vs others?!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There may be bits of cooked egg in the mixture. Don't want rubbery pieces of egg in your silky smooth mousse.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It is impossible to keep all of the egg white out when separating the yolks. The little bits of egg white will cook solid and may be noticeable in the final pudding. This is what you are straining out. Same with any type of custard preparation. No big deal to press it thru a sieve. (The more yolks used, the more “scrambled egg white” pieces will sieve out)

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If it came out grainy, the sugar likely crystallized, which happens if you stir it while it’s caramelizing in the pan, so avoid stirring it; better to tip the pan and swirl it around gently for more even caramelization.
