Very good, but a better chocolate would take it to the next level, if you can afford something especially good, like Valrhona.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, but mine was so incredibly thick. It was like eating ganache-not sure what I did wrong. The candied hazelnuts were my snack all day long so good thing I didn’t need many for topping.  Whipped up some cream for the top-heavenly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @SFM001 Interesting…mine was too thin, even being in the fridge over night! I used honey instead of corn syrup but didn’t change anything else.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is pretty straightforward to make and absolutely delicious.  That said, it’s very very rich.  We served a small amount topped with a lot of fresh raspberries.  I don’t like nuts with smooth custardy desserts, so we had shortbread on the side.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This isn’t very sweet, which is okay, but I was surprised and thought it could be sweeter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yummmm! Made with walnuts because we had them on hand and was fab!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
So delicious! And the nuts really make it. 

The only problem is it never got to the right texture, even over night. It was more like soft/melted ice cream. Anyone know what causes this? I did have to sub honey for the corn syrup…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious…surprisingly light and silky smooth on the tongue, not as intensely rich as a typical mousse….the hazelnuts are a nice accent; I wouldn’t change a thing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just finished our third batch.  This is what my dad would have called bowl-licking good!  As a matter of personal preference I dialed back the olive oil by a half-teaspoon.  I am wondering if anyone has made a batch as written and then folded in sweetened whipped cream to lighten the mix?  I'm thinking about trying this myself and am wondering how much whipped cream to use for this purpose.  And BTW, the hazelnut praline is outstanding, too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this.  The texture is somewhere between a pudding and a mousse.  I used 72% chocolate bars from a cacao supplier in Belize, and it was not sweet enough for most of my family.  As for toasting hazelnuts and peeling skins, this goes on my list of cooking tasks that I have done once and will never do again.  I  tried the metal sieve as recommended in these comments, and it just worked okay.  Will I make this again?  Probably, but I will add more sweetener.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Other than setting not one but TWO oven mitts on fire while making the caramel (my pan didn't have long handles!), I loved this and it was very easy. As Vaughn says, get your mise en place done and it comes together quite easily. When I tasted it before chilling, I did NOT like the olive oil taste at all. But it was very mellow after chilling. I had to hide the hazelnut praline from my husband so we'd have some left for the dinner party!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Swapped out the hazelnuts for macadamia nuts. Success! (And one less step… taking skins off.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't really like the texture of this mousse. Even though I placed it through a fine strainer, it was a tad granular.  I have a pot de creme recipe that I think I prefer. That said,  I loved the candied hazelnuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would this work with whole milk and no heavy cream?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Valrohna chocolate was delish.  Great mouth feel must be in great part due to the addition of olive oil - I used Heritage Olive Oil from California (smooth and fruity). DEFINITELY making this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We lost our minds over how good this dessert is. I make it for my husband's birthday. Without the hazelnuts, good olive oil, and sea salt it would merely be an amazing silky chocolate pudding. But all together these components are out of this world- complex, sophisticated. I agree with other commenters that this is not a mousse- so if that's what you're looking for go elsewhere (my vote: Julia Child). This is texturally like a pudding crossed with a pot de creme.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've been making this cake for a while using a similar recipe. Mashed potato flakes are the way to go. Just reconstitute unseasoned ones using the chart on the package.  Also, this recipe looks like it might be a little too thick for an 8-inch square pan.  The Moravian Sugar Cakes in Winston Salem are thinner so that there's a higher ratio of cinnamon sugar to soft crumb.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I know it’s not authentically Moravian, but…  Oh! cut back the cinnamon and add in cardamom.  Heaven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm sure this could be adapted to use instant mashed potato flakes. The trick would be to find the right ratio. I'm guessing it'll be around 160g water to 30g potato flakes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe on Serious Eats has the substitution for instant potato flakes in the notes section. This recipe seems to be adopted from that one, but unfortunately don't have that attribution listed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh my goodness my jaw just hit the floor. My family has been eating this cake every Christmas for over 40 years!!! We call it “The Christmas Coffee Cake”! I didn’t know anyone else made it. My ancestors were German-American. If you do it right you get a layer of cinnamon GOO at the bottom of each crevice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Martha my alsatian family makes aversion without potato but with cream
pooled on the top… it’s a forever favorite!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Curses! When my kids ask for Moravian Sugar Cake I tell them we can only get that in Bethlehem PA when they visit their grandparents.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lara M. Bethlehem sure. But also Winston-Salem. Don’t forget the Southern Province of the a Moravian Church!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I agree with the previous comment re the effectiveness and convenience of instant mashed potato flakes. 

I made the cake twice and discovered one very helpful trick. Instead of poking holes with damp fingers and spreading the cinnamon sugar on top, spread the cinnamon sugar mixture evenly over the entire cake, then dampen your fingers and push the mixture down into holes on the batter. Works like a charm and provides deeper indentations and better coverage.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Perry Rhew This seems very sensible and effective. I wonder: did you sprinkle the butter on together with the sugar and then poke holes or did you sprinkle the sugar, poke holes and then sprinkle with butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I grew up in Winston-Salem and we ate Dewey‘s sugarcake throughout the year. So delicious! It (along with Moravian ginger cookies) is my favorite taste of home. I might try this recipe, but if anyone wants to cheat, it is possible to order Moravian sugarcake mixes from Winkler Bakery in Old Salem (now the “Salem” in Winston-Salem), where Moravians settled in the late 18th century. https://www.oldsalem.org/product/moravian-sugar-cake-mix/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @keyfollin we are in Bethlehem PA - an original Moravian village and have been enjoying Moravian sugar cake for decades - looking forward to trying both recipes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My grandparents lived in Winston-Salem. One of my favorite childhood memories is visiting Dewey's Bakery with them for Moravian sugar cake and little chess tarts.  Every year we order sugar cake from Dewey's, but I never considered making it myself. Thanks for this recipe, and the suggestion to order the mix from Winkler Bakery. I will try both!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m Moravian and this makes me so happy.  My mom’s recipe uses instant potato flakes.  So happy for sugar cake to finally get its due!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lauracsn you share your mom’s recipe? How much instant potato flakes?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t have a stand mixer or a mixer with a dough hook. What’s the manual alternative?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Aunt Lucy - Re making this w/o a mixer w/dough hook.  My instinct after watching the excellent video was a wooden spoon and beating by hand to get to the right sticky stretchy consistency bcuz too sticky to knead.  Googled "making Moravian sugar cake without dough hook" & got that very instruction & also link to Serious Eats article on how to use bread scraper to do it.   400 years tradition in making this and then there were no machines so by hand should work.  Good luck for yummy outcome!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just use a wooden spoon, though if you happen to have a dough whisk, this is the time to pull it out. You'll need to beat/work the dough a bit longer with your spoon than you would with an electric aid, but the same texture cues apply. This holds for nearly every recipe that calls for a stand mixer!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What about microwaving the potato? Seems like it would cut its cooking time in half and I don't need extra potato flakes lying around. Does the recipe need the moisture from boiling them?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this gluten free with cup for cup - baked at 345F for 32 minutes, and it came out perfectly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Reserve half a cup of the potato water to bloom the yeast. (Of course after letting it cool a bit!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This one's a keeper - my husband claims it's his new favourite cake. SO delicious , and easy to prepare, too. My older son and I used a standard European 9.5 inch round springform pan, which proved to be a good idea, as you can remove the rim of the pan and easily slide the cake onto a cake platter. Otherwise we stuck to the recipe, except that we accidentally emptied all of a 7 gram dry yeast pouch, but that didn't do any harm.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Anna in Winston Salem we'd use a half sheet pan for the same quantity of batter

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve never heard of Moravian sugar cake, but sounded good and I love a secret ingredient. Made exactly as written, and it is so, so good. Fluffy, moist, and sweet, and amazing with a cup of coffee.  Take this as your sign to try it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As I write, a batch of Sugar Cake is in my oven rising.  It's a recipe I found in the Winston-Salem Sentinel almost 50 years ago.  Along w/ Cornish Saffron buns (think Hot Cross Buns but yellow-orange w/ red streaks in them) they're my only Christmas gift to adults.  The batch currently rising is only a half-batch - 8C flour, 1/2 stick of butter, 3/4 # of brown sugar.  I also put in equal amounts of cinnamon and nutmeg.  So, try the recipe and your kitchen will smell as good as mine does.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I saw the headline for this article, “if a cinnamon roll and focaccia had a baby,” and thought that it seemed interesting, but I’d never stray from Moravian sugar cake on Xmas morning (I’m from NC). When I pulled out my sugar cake to heat up for my family, I realized— oh my god, this is focaccia and cinnamon roll’s baby. This has to be what the article’s about. 

Thank you, Vaughn, for taking the time to develop and share something that feels special to my home <3

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is great holiday morning bread, my family always makes it when we are making a larger portion of mashed potatoes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Every year as long as I can remember my mom has made Moravian sugar cakes for friends and neighbors at Christmas. Her recipe apparently comes from a non-descript church cookbook. After helping her get rid of some things one summer, she called me in a panic thinking I had donated the book by accident. I had not, all was well, and I’m waiting for the package with my sugar cakes to arrive any day now.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was baptized a Moravian at the Moravian church in Bethlehem, PA over 67 years ago, although I was later raised as a Presbyterian.  I went to college in Bethlehem, and my great aunt called me when I was a freshman (in 1975) and asked if I wanted to go to Lovefeast.  Aunt Bessie was quite progressive, and visions of orgies danced in my head.  Which dissipated once she told me it involved coffee cake and strong coffee.  I'll definitely try this one for the memories.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: well I made it! everything you show me how to make is delicious. sadly this went all as planned and I pulled it out of the oven at 33 minutes when my whole stove was smoking from the butter and sugar overflow. let it cool and it was raw inside 😞 I didn't even think to put a toothpick in it

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have never tried the original version to compare it to but I think I would up the ratio of the butter/cinnamon sugar to cake ratio or perhaps my divots weren’t deep enough to distribute the mix throughout the cake but this didn’t stop me from eating pretty much the whole thing.  Not sure if I would make again but had opened my eyes to yeasted cakes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a wonderful bread. I make this every year for Christmas Breakfast. Yes this version ooks a bit thick. We like a thinner dough so that the butter/sugar topping is the highlight. I also use instant mashed potatoes. And finally, I make it vegan and it turns out just as good. This year I am going to also try a gluten free version so that will be interesting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ivy  sounds fabulous!  You mentioned Veganising it… how did you replace the eggs?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was able to microwave the potato rather than boil. Scooped out the insides once it was done, mashed, and then whisked in the milk. Make sure to use a large potato or 2 if you mix or in order to get the full cup needed for the recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is IT!  I am a baker and have tried for years to make a great Moravian Sugar Cake like Dewey's, and this it the recipe!  However:  I would back off slightly on the salt and use a 9 by 9 pan - the 8 by 8 makes the cake too thick. Otherwise: I gave my hubby, who grew up on Winston Salem Dewey's, and he said it was even better than the original! I used a dough hook no problem by the way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This do be taking way too long to make but by god is it worth it

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe brings back happy memories…I grew up in the Moravian church and we celebrated love feasts a few time air year in church services. The women of the church would make Moravian sugar cake nd we would eat it during the service!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those of us without stand mixers, is there a way to use a food processor or just a hand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Corlan - Re your question of how to w/o stand mixer, food processor won't work & hand mixer w/o hook won't either. But by hand will.  My instinct after watching the excellent video was a wooden spoon and beating by hand to get to the right sticky stretchy consistency bcuz too sticky to knead. Googled "making Moravian sugar cake without dough hook" & got that very instruction & also link to Serious Eats article on how to use bread scraper to do it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What if I made the dough tonight and stuck it in the fridge to rise slowly and then sugared and baked it in the morning?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Urgent practical pointer: place a rimmed cookie sheet underneath the pan or on the oven shelf beneath! The buttery sugar dripped out of my 8x8 Pyrex and smoked up the kitchen like crazy :0. Taste comments to follow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same thing happened to me.  I had even briefly considered using the rimmed cookie sheet underneath.  So my house smells like smoke but the cake is divine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can anyone make a suggestion for starting the recipe and slow rising in the fridge overnight so it can be assembled and baked first thing in the morning?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this exactly as written and honestly, it's one of the best pastries I have ever tasted!  I think I looked more forward to Christmas morning to enjoy this than I did to see what Santa had brought!  :-)))

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wonder if this can be doubled and put into a 9x13 pan for a larger group?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ed C - re your question about doubling this and using 13x9, you could try it BUT an 8x8 is 64 sq in and 13x9 is 117 so you'll have more dough to pan ratio and it may be too much for the pan at that point and the cake / bread will be too thick.  (The video is great, watch it to see what I mean.)  I'd be inclined to make 2 separate batches in 2 pans of 8x8 size.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @dimmerswitch I doubled it and it worked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How about adding raisins(pre soaked)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What is the purpose of setting aside a bowl of lukewarm water at the end of Step 6?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ann I think it’s for dampening hands in steps 7 and 9.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @AnnTi damien your fingers for dimpling the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Well, as I love everything Vaughn makes, I made this right away.. All went splendidly until I pulled it out of the oven since it was bubbling over and nicely browned(And since I forgot the tray, my oven was smoking from all the butter and sugar).. I let it cool and went to cut it to find out it was still gooey inside :(. I didn't think to test with a toothpick before thinking it was ready!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is delicious! I included both cinnamon and cardamom in the sprinkle, but it felt like too much dough for the pan. Parts in the center were undercooked and I think a larger pan is called for.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We really liked it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good! But when it says to cool in the pan, definitely do that…I was so keen to try it I burnt my finger on the caramelised sugar! But the taste made it all better! 😘

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a sit down incredible recipe.  I did my best to remain true to the directions.  That said my tweaks did not change the foundation of this recipe.  If you have not made it try it.  I am a big fan of mashed potatoes in dough....it's amazing.  It spongy, moist, light, and just plain good.This cake is like the best cinnamon roll you ever tasted. (I was generous with the cinnamon).  It was magic to combine butter, potatoes, cinnamon and brown sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I grew up eating Moravian sugar cake from Dewey's, so I had to try the recipe! It turned out really well, though I'm wondering if I let it rise long enough. It was a little dense, but still delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I had never heard of this before but was very excited to try it! 
Making dimples after I sprinkled it with sugar worked much better and led to a great result! I also added half a teaspoon of cardamom to the sugar. I also used a full packet (7g) of yeast and a standard European springform pan and it's looking very good. Ended up baking it for 39 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not use parchment??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had the same question, so I tried it on my second cake. Buttered the pan and the parchment. Worked great and much easier transferring the cake out of the pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The video for this recipe is great and explains where it comes from and how the recipe was developed. I found it really helpful when I was trying to figure out why my dough was not rising that much. This is more of a bread than a cake and except for the topping is not overly sweet. I followed the recipe exactly except added time to the first rise (which I did not need to do). Everyone liked this. The potato really helps keep the cake tender.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Some cakes concept are just too good for not being universal. This is a traditional cake in Denmark - although we omit the cinnamon and potatoes. It can be found in bakeries and people bake it at home. In the western part of Denmark it is served with whipped cream! That can really divide the nation! Will try the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes, this is an excellent recreation. Yes, it is  thicker by about 50% so don't fear putting it in a bigger pan. I used an 8x12 pan, and it could still be thinner if I was after a more faithful version. Don't skimp on the cinnamon quality and increase if you feel your cinnamon is not strong. There is a lot of butter taste in this recipe and your cinnamon needs to predominate. The product tastes identical to me using a fresh potato, or potato flakes. (I use equal quantities of water and flakes.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Christmas morning and it was such a wonderful treat we made it for New Year’s too. I reduced the sugar by 1/3 and used King Arthur Flour’s keto flour mix (both changes I make with almost any dessert recipe). Otherwise followed the recipe and will definitely make this for other special brunches in the future.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely accidentally double the sugar topping ☃️

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why would one need to use 2 liters of water to cook one potato? Waste of energy and water for no conceivable reason.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could this be made like a bundt somehow?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Trish - I don't think this recipe works for a Bundt pan.  If you're looking for a yeasted bread-meets-cake baked in a Bundt pan, you might look at the recipe for Election Cake by Yewande Komolafe on NYT Cooking for ingredients and technique.  You could leave the dried fruit out of that recipe (I wouldn't but...) and also omit the glaze (I do).  It contains brown sugar and cinnamon plus other spices that you could eliminate (I wouldn't but...) and up the cinnamon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You really couldn't dimple a bundt cake in the same way you can a square cake. Genevieve Ko's Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake with Cream Cheese Frosting might give you what you're looking for.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I left an unwrapped cut onion half on the top shelf of my fridge, my Moravian sugar cake would taste like allium.  Am I missing some new culinary technique lol?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any tips for making this gluten-free?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MMD This is my rule for anything calling for cinnamon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Also possible to simply grate the potato into water, strain and put in a pot with fast boiling water. Takes about 2 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone know if this would work well in a bread machine on the dough setting? I am hoping to not use the stand mixer if at all possible due to a current medical issue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I freeze the “unpuffed” dough at the final stage (before adding the sugar?)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: okay. I just threw everything in the bread machine on the dough setting. It definitely didn't come out completely right (too much liquid in the end), but is still tasty and has a great texture. @Cambree

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can someone share the ratio/amount of potato flakes they use instead of the boiled potato? And do you make any other changes to the recipe if so? Mine seems to be very batter-like and not very dough-like...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I do this with flakes rather than a fresh potato, I use one cup of boiling water and one cup of potato flakes. Combine, mix gently, and measure out a full cup for your recipe. It will give you slightly more than a full cup.Yes, it is a loose, batter-like dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Xmas brunch and it was delicious! I used a 9 X 9 pan but the topping spilled over and the smoke filled my kitchen. Lots of open windows on a very cold AM helped and I slipped a cookie sheet underneath so it could finish baking. Any suggestions for a different pan for next time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My partner is from Bethlehem PA and had Moravian Sugar Cake for Christmas each year, so I decided to make this as a treat for him.  First, those Moravian Housefrau must have been up at the crack of dawn to get this thing on the table for Christmas breakfast (but no doubt they were).  And they didn't have either stand mixers or dough hooks.  But 4 hours later we sat down to an breakfast featuring this absolutely delicious and decadent cake. YUM!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So yummy. I put half the sugar mixture on the dough BEFORE I dimpled the dough so I jammed the sugar into the dimples as I made them. Worked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I wanted to eat this warm from the oven for breakfast do you think I could make it through step 7 (spreading the dough in the pan), refrigerate overnight, and then bring to room temp before topping and baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Amy no. It won’t work. Many have tried. Make it all in one day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We tried making the cake the night before and letting it rise a second time before baking on Christmas morning. Unfortunately it didn’t cook through all the way, no matter how long we left it in the oven. The parts that we could eat were very tasty, though, so we will try again with a straight timeline!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made the soup that the box calls for and then added more flakes until it looked like it should. Maybe in the future I will measure, but for now it works that way too.  @KJ Dude

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How did you get the flakes to not come out like a soup? Did you only add the actual water amount the package calls for or did you use the full liquid (milk&water) to do so? @Anna

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: the last one I made using potato flakes as well. it came out very soupy but didn't seem to impact the cake that much? I am making it again with potato flakes today and will try to remember to weigh stuff out. @J Hamilton

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cake is so delicious! I even forgot to add the sugar until after the first rise and it still turned out lovely.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can we do this? follow the instructions exactly and then bake in half sheet pan? I'd love to have a thinner version. @soup lyons

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was excellent! My dough was still a little sticky at the end, so the only thing I did a little differently was sprinkle some of the cinnamon sugar and then make the dimples, which helped get the sugar down to the bottom more easily. Seemed to work really well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can this be made with almond milk????

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made with oatmilk with no issue!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: By far our new favorite. So much so that it was a birthday cake tonight. My only alteration, if you can call it that, was to line the pan with buttered parchment paper, purely to make life a smidge easier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My Eastern European roots are telling me, "a few raisins . . . would be good". Can't hurt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Incredible. It tasted like a cinnamon roll and I couldn't stop eating it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was really good! Def worth the time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried making this with sweet potato instead of a russet?? It sounds delicious to me, but I wasn’t sure if it would affect how the cake bakes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This must've been inspired by the floor at the movie theater.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Amy I came here for the comments, and you delivered! Thanks for the belly laugh.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2 words:  Pop. Rocks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were surprisingly delicious and so fun to make! Don’t knock ‘em til you’ve tried ‘em. I used Trader Joe’s equivalents for Reese’s, m&m’s, Raisinets, Heath bar, and Kit Kats, plus sea salt caramel chips. And the sour patch kids on top really worked well with the popcorn. My kids call them Buddy the Elf cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fun, delicious, and really nostalgic! Tip: to avoid spending so much money on all of the candy, I went to the bulk candy bins at my grocery store and probably saved about $20 (why are name-brand Raisinettes $6?!).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Because the bulk ones often have dried out raisins ( spoken as a Raisinette connoisseur). My Kroger and Walgreen’s stores have a large selection of movie candy in the boxes, often on sale, as another option. The Dark Chocolate Raisinettes might be a nice option.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @AH wait, there are dark chocolate raisinettes? My world just rocked.!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Barrett I bought the small movie theater size boxes of various candy at the grocery store, which cost about $1.50 each. I didn’t even use all of what I bought. Good way to get variety without having so much candy left.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Solid and fun bake. Don’t skip the hour of chilling. Smaller popcorn crumbles stick better. Also having Vaughn as a judge on a baking completion in the background helps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I worried that the candy was making these too crumbly, but they were fine once they cooled. Really addictive! I do wish the NYT would amend written recipes to match best practices in the videos: The recipe instructs us to stir in the candy by hand. Vaughn uses his stand mixer for this step in the video, and mutters something about his knowing when to stop mixing -- unlike us, presumably. It's  actually not rocket science, and mixing all that candy into stiff dough by hand is a lot of work! Better to use a mixer. The written recipe instructs pressing the dough scoops into the popcorn, while in the video, he's seen firmly pressing popcorn into the dough, which definitely works much better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Won best tasting at a cookie swap! You can't help but smile with these. They bring a lot of joy and fun. On my first batch I added gummy bears as soon as they came out and they melted into a puddle. Second batch, I froze the gummies first and it worked beautifully. So cute!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can the dough balls be refrigerated overnight and completed the next day?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Vicki I have some in the freezer actually, I’ll let you know how that goes next week when I bake them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Vicki I did this and it worked great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Victoria thank you. I need to do this too. My energy is depleted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are gonna be a huge hit at my kid's birthday party! (He prefers cookies to birthday cake). This is the most chaotic cookie recipe I've ever seen and I absolutely love it. I can not wait to try it. (Helpful hint - go to Five Below for cheaper candy!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this recipe and the texture is phenomenal! In terms of flavor profile, I wish the popcorn I used had more of a "clarified butter" taste. The option I chose felt like it didn't stand up to the sweetness in the candies. Next time, I might brush the cookies with clarified butter instead of regular. Have fun. Make this cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could this recipe be completed up to (and including) step 3 and freeze those dough portions until ready to bake?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: rhirhi - you ask if cookies can be made through step 3 and frozen.  Watch the video of Vaughn making these (link is at top of recipe page) and at about 5:54 as he is putting scooped balls onto parchment lined tray he says if you don't want to take them off you can freeze them and bake later.  I guess my question would be when you take the scooped cookie 'balls' from the freezer, how does one butter coat and press popcorn into frozen dough balls? I might take them to that point then freeze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @dimmerswitch for other recipes where I’ve frozen dough and baked later, I typically let the dough rest at room temperature for 5 minutes before adding any finishes (in this case the butter and popcorn). I find frozen dough warms up pretty quickly so you should be able to push the popcorn in. I will be testing this technique on December 23 right before the holidays!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were really yummy. I added chocolate maple almond brittle, m and ms, heath bar. I chopped up haribo gummy bears and poked them in after baking. They got gooey, jammy and oh-so-good. Bake these for yourself on a tough day and pop on a Christmas movie and be a kid again. They’ll make you smile.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are super fun and delicious! Kids had a great time helping with this recipe. 
Tip - if you want nerds on these, pour them into a small ziploc bag. Then cut your gummy bears and/or sour patch kids into thirds and put those in the bag. Zip it and shake. The nerds stick to the cut sides and then you can push the gummy pieces into the warm cookies with nerds attached!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thanks for this superfun idea. I have some friends who are movie aficionados and we’re not exchanging gifts this year but I can put together some of these in a little box. I set aside a couple for myself and watch “White Christmas” or “The Holdovers.” I know my friends will love these. Have a wonderful holiday season.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were fun!! I made them for Christmas gift boxes and used Heath, reeces, red and green m and ms, and red and green sour patch on top. They look and taste great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a lot of fun to make and loved that you could customize them based on the candy you like. I mixed the candy in with a stand mixer and had no issue with over mixing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nerds Clusters!! Vaughn, you're my favourite genius.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you make these ahead and store them?? How?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone know if you can freeze them after step with the popcorn on it instead of freezing them at step 3? TIA! I'd love to make these for my cookie exchange this weekend but need to make the dough in advance.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jill I suspect the popcorn coating is not stable and will come off when freezing - it might be ok if you have a lot of space in your freezer and store the entire baking sheet. I’d recommend freezing the popped popcorn separately in a Ziploc bag and complete step 3 at bake time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the recipe with half the sugar and a bit less candy. The cookies turned out great. They were sweet enough thanks to the candy. I had so much fun baking these cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for my family and coworkers, and they were (mostly) an instant classic! People loved the different flavors, and the salt with the buttered popcorn added nice salt to balance the sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I probably wouldn’t have made these if I wasn’t pregnant, but with that being said I’m glad I trusted the process!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made a batch and am not crazy about. Did watch the video which was very fun. I think I’m going to take the idea & run w it. Use a dough more like Toll House and maybe just Health inside and def popcorn outside. Then dot the tops w a few of the other candies right out of oven. The Raisinettes are really a blast from the past and some other candies, too. Fantastic idea.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to make these for my annual cookie boxes, which I mail to family across the country. Any tips on keeping the popcorn topping fresh for up to a week, or is mailing these a bad idea?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a fun cookie full of nostalgia.  I used Goobers, Raisinets, Sno Caps, Reeses Pieces, and M&Ms. After baking I added Sour Patch Kids. A nice chewy cookie with a perfect sweet and salty balance. Fun to make and eat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A great cookie to make for a party. They weren’t as moist and chewy as a classic chocolate chip, but the combo of Reese’s pieces and popcorn was deliciously sweet and savory. Plus a conversation starter since people wanted to know what the heck they were.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My tween watched the video, followed the recipe, baked these and is now all in on cookie week. My sincere thanks for sharing your creativity and family candy favorite - toffee. Sour candy or starbursts taste great on top to counter the chocolate. Toffee is a must in the dough. Gummy bears melted on top but the chew was great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I call these "It's a Wonderful Life" cookies. Perfect companion to watching a good movie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were such a big hit! I used butterfinger, buncha crunch, Reese's pieces, plain m&ms, snickers, and the heath pieces. Definitely don't skip the sour patch kids on top, they actually added a nice balance to the sweet and salty from the candy and popcorn! These were so fun and I will be making them for all movie nights from now on!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These tasted better than I thought they were going to.  The first group out of the oven I added the gummy bears right away and they melted. The next group I waited five minutes and then stuck in the gummy bears.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic cookie. Favorite of the year by my family and I made a lot of the NYT cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Vicki yes! I believe he says so in the video.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found that these spread a lot, as written. Would make a bit smaller and chill overnight if I was doing again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Okay, feel better about end results. Had one w coffee this morning. A good cookie. Still like my idea of sticking all colorful candies on top rather than all in but I’m going to take it a step further and simply stick one or two full popcorns on top w colorful candies instead of coating w crushed. Classier & not as messy/waste…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ha! This is how my family makes popcorn at home (strange, but true!). I will definitely be trying these!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these fun cookies!! And we watched Elf 🎄

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Barrett I am using all my kids halloween candy. yippeeeeee - NYT found a purpose!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
You have to trust the recipe—these are so awesome. In addition to the toffee bits, I put Mini M&Ms, Reese’s Pieces, Peanut M&Ms, and Buncha Crunch in the dough. After they came out of the oven, I put about three pieces of cut-up Sour Patch Kids on each cookie. They’re sweet, salty, crunchy, chewy, and gummy all at once, and it is so, so good. 

My only modification was making each cookie smaller than the recipe describes; I used a heaping tablespoon torn in half for each one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the NYT cookie week & have made many over the years. These, unfortunately, were a messy disappointment. Way, way too much work, effort, time, cost. Won’t make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found these cookies to be mediocre. They are very sweet and I don’t find that the popcorn adds flavor to the cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Overall a delicious and fun cookie to make and eat. I like the mix of textures and candies. The one critique I received was that they could use more salt. I think if you used melted salted butter instead of unsalted to roll the dough in before sticking in the popcorn, that might help. Or just use salted butter in the dough instead of unsalted, as I know many do anyway :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Reminded me of the Milk Bar Compost Cookie which is a cookie staple in our house so had to try. Like with the Company Cookie, I used the candy we had, which was mostly the same as the recipe but not identical. I'm not a big gummy fan to begin with so I just skipped that last step. 

These came out great! The popcorn elevated this, giving it almost a Kettle Corn flavor. If anything I wish I had more popcorn in the cookie. I might mix some into the dough next time and then top it like the recipe says. A really fun cookie to make with the kids. I will make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a half batch and they were flat (my leavening should’ve been okay since I bake quite a lot). I suspect this recipe doesn’t translate well to halving it. Tastes great though. I’m watching “It’s A Wonderful Life.”

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good way to use up excess Easter candy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wish ALL the recipes had videos. They're not only entertaining (Vaughn is a case in point) but they're also so enlightening. I can't tell you how many Aha! moments I've had over the years watching these NYT Cooking personalities do their thing on video. So many helpful tips, hints, how-tos, cues and clues that I can't recall them all. I'm not saying I'm a better cook, but I'm definitely a more confident one. Thank you NYT Cooking! And Happy Holidays to all!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am 77 y. o., and do not have enough days  left on this earth to make this multi step dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Celia K. I do hope that's not true! Enjoy all of your days and happy Thanksgiving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Celia K. I hope someone makes it for you. And me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Celia K. - my 90-year-old father is thinking of making this recipe this Thanksgiving. If he can knock it out the day before, I’m sure you can too.

commentBody: This sounds and looks fantastic! And now I'm already thinking about experimenting with a variation using pistachio butter for a green bottom layer for Christmas

commentBody: Can confirm: this recipe uses many bowls, but they’re all worth it. Also opted to make a homemade cookie butter with leftover ginger snaps and lady fingers! I don’t think I can wait the 4 hours until it’s ready!

commentBody: @K in Oakland I used Trader Joe’s Almond Windmill cookies for the crust because sometimes I get sick of Biscoff/Speculos. (I did use the TJ cookie spread, the cheese cake flavor is similar). Really I think any crumbly cookie that works for you would be fine for crust. I added more cinnamon to mimic the warmth of the Biscoff. You could brown the butter a bit, too.

commentBody:
@K in Oakland 
I also didn’t have the cookie butter on hand, but did have an extra packet of the Biscoff cookies, so I decided to make it myself. Fortunately, it does have to stay in the fridge for a while to set up. I was short on time and just added it anyway although it was very liquidy.

commentBody: @Tina I froze mine because I did not have room in my fridge and it was firm but not icy. It was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The previous comments are spot on - you will use every bowl in your kitchen! Also, allow plenty of time because this isn’t something you can pull off at the last minute. Is it worth the effort? Oh my, yes! Also, definitely watch the video. I never watch them, but because of the other comments, I did and probably avoided mistakes that way. The flavor combination is amazing and something I’d never think of myself!

commentBody: Looks fantastic. But what is cookie butter?

commentBody: @julie it’s a sweet spread that’s usually made of the same speculoos-style cookies that make up the crust in this recipe. There are several different brands—I know of a lotus biscoff one and Trader Joe’s has their own version. It’s usually available near the peanut butter at the grocery store!

I went to three stores including Trader Joe’s. I finally found the cookies and cookie butter at Walmart. It looks yummy. I have it chilling for Thanksgiving dessert!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it! Each step was easy to execute, and as a mediocre baker, I appreciated that this is not actually a lot of ingredients (you use the juice of an orange in the cranberry sauce and the zest in the cheesecake layers, for example). I used four bowls, for those keeping score at home, and I had to wash my spatulas about eight times to alternate them between tasks and layers. The cranberry layer overpowers the mild cookie butter layer, sadly. But the crust is out of this world.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Meg A. As a not very dexterous baker, I felt like I needed containers to set spatulas and spoons in. I appreciated my husband working as my assistant. It’s an involved recipe, but kind of fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mother is having her Thanksgiving dinner tonight, and I have this cooling in the fridge right now. It looks beautiful and everything tasted so good along the way, I can't wait to try it! Great video, too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It sounds like you could just use jellied cranberry sauce instead of making your own. Am I right?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Fay Wouk Cranberries are so great fresh. I put the strained out skins back in the pot and added orange marmalade and more cranberries for cranberry orange marmalade. It’s great on toast!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
GLUTEN FREE family here! So, I used Tate’s Gluten Free Ginger Zinger cookies in both the crust and in the cheesecake batter (which meant that I needed to make my own cookie butter with the Tate’s cookies. Easily accomplished using Stella Parks five star recipe for cookie butter that I found on the Serious Eats website.) Required two bags of the Tates cookies for both the crust and the cookie butter.

Otherwise, I stuck with the recipe as written. I invited six friends over for a taste test, and they all raved. Next time, and there will be a next time, I will probably reduce the sugar in both the crust and the cookie butter. But that’s just me. My six taste testers loved it as made.

The Tates Gluten Free Ginger cookies are very ginger forward and have large chewy pieces of ginger in them. I really liked that combination of crunch and chew in the crust. There were a few flecks of ginger in the cookie butter that looked like the flecks in vanilla bean ice cream. If you prefer a more emulsified outcome, then I would go with more traditional ginger snaps rather than the Tates. I chose them because I needed a gluten-free option. Five star outcome!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JJ thanks for this, I will take your advice for a gluten free and less processed version!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Suzanne I had the same thought! Let’s hear how it tastes! My hesitation is that it can only be out of the fridge for an hour? During thanksgiving, I can not afford such fussiness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Suzanne It looks like many of us have made this now. I made it the day before Thanksgiving. It’s not as complicated as it seems, though watching the video is helpful. It was a big hit along with Cybelle Tondu’s Dutch apple pie recipe.

It is delicious, an excellent combo of the cranberry and cookie butter. I could see adapting well to other combos (Nutella and raspberry?). My only criticism, a nitpick really, perhaps the extra sugar in the crust might be a bit much. The cookies are already sweet and since it doesn’t bake, the sugar adds a bit more graininess and sweetness than seems necessary.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would try raspberries and maybe reduce the added sugar so it is more tart @LCooper

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: thank you for all your many blessings, V

commentBody:
@Terry 
a quick google search and you can make your own cookie butter! I used leftover homemade ginger snaps and lady fingers with some butter and milk until I got my desired consistency. Super yummy and hopefully within your allergy constraints!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oops - forgot I had a question: I have a sweet-tooth like nobody's business, but am I the only one who thought this cake might be too sweet or too gingery? I realize the cranberry and the cream cheese will add tartness and tang, but the idea of a crust made of speculoos and sugar (and yes, a bit of salt), and then the layer of cream cheese with speculoos and sugar (and yes, some salt), gives me pause. I might try half speculoos/half graham cracker crumbs when the recipe calls for all speculoos.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
What's all the fuss about age? I"ll be 93 in a couple of weeks and LOVE a complicated multi step recipe. Made mine for a group of friends for a pot luck. Did watch the video a couple of times and wrote hints, like refrigerating the whipped cream, on the recipe. It came out perfectly and everyone raved about it. I'll be making it again for our family Christmas dinner.
So it used a lot of pans, we were warned, so who cares ?
 Carole, North Chatham Ma. Dec 14, 2025

author: Vaughn Vreeland
Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am going to steal parts of this recipe.  The cookie butter idea sounds a bit insane: more sugar added to a lot of sugar.  But doing a less sweet ground nut crust, real baked cheesecake and this cranberry topping sounds fabulous.  Apologies for suggesting such horrific corruptions to what is likely a recipe many people will love.  It’s just that, for those of us out here who really hate massive amounts of sugar, there is still something special here to enjoy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I ignore the sugar amounts and often reduce by half or use a combo with stevia or allulose for sweetness. Doubt that it makes a huge difference in how the item sets up.  (Love cream cheese and any butter!!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm not sure if "Minimal effort" can be used to describe a recipe that requires using and therefore cleaning a food processor, a springform pan, a small bowl, a medium saucepan, a strainer, a medium bowl, a large bowl, another large bowl, a hand mixer, as well as all the miscellaneous utensils. The recipe is fine, but how about some realistic expectations?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This recipe might use a bunch of bowls, but it’s a stunner! Very appreciative of the Vaughntent (content) on this recipe, as watching the combination and consistencies I was looking for was helpful for the first time through it. 

I hosted a Friendsgiving party and made 12 mini (3”) cheesecakes with a single batch (I had to rely on 9th grade geometry to do cylindrical volume to determine if the original recipe would work in this way — it did!) to great celebration. Such a fun flavor combo!

I’m a small bakery owner and since they say if you never ask, you’ll never know: Vaughn, can we please bake together one day? It would launch my baking career into the stratosphere, and I’d be forever grateful!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Brielle B Now that is a great idea and thanks for doing the math. 12 mini cheesecakes….I want to try that and then freeze them. I made this and it is great but had leftovers. I can only get cranberries this time of year, the holidays.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tiffany Lamp. I always buy 3-4 extra bags of fresh cranberries at this time of year and freeze them. They last (freshness-wise) for year, but I usually finish them off. By mid-summer. Get some now while you can and spread the cranberry love into next year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and wanted to circle back to say that you can make it a few days ahead (I made it Wednesday, served it Friday) but if you're putting candied orange peels and sugared cranberries on top, wait until you're ready to present/serve it because the sugar will dissolve and leave little puddles.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought this was a decent recipe, but I ran into a few issues. I lost a bit of butter to spatter in the microwave (should've melted in shorter bursts, my fault) so I think it made the crust more delicate with less butter to bind it. Also, the cranberry layer was pretty soft and never really set as firm as I would like. It didn't fall apart when taken out of the springform, but it would start to droop a bit when sitting out for 5-10 minutes and the individual slices were delicate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a huge success!  If you are having trouble with the cranberry mixture setting make sure you are cooking the cranberries down enough.  The video has a great visual.  I made the crust and cooked down the cranberries the day before.  It worked great.  The only thing you need to account for is you won't be able to use a whisk to mix the cranberries with the cream cheese mixture.  The cranberries will be too stiff and gelled.  You will need to use the hand mixer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this pretty much exactly as written and videoed. I did, however use my immersion blender on the cranberry sauce before straining it. Also, I used a chinois to strain the seeds and skin from the cranberry sauce. Felt like way more than five minutes, but probably wasn’t. Resulting curd was delicious. Everyone raved about this, particularly the cookie batter portion. In my opinion, I think the ratio of cranberry and cookie butter portions is a bit off. The cranberry is good and it works well with the cookie butter, but I think the ratio should be less 50-50 and more like 1/3 cranberry and 2/3 cookie butter.  It’s a real showstopper and at least four people from Thanksgiving have asked me for the recipe already.  Also, definitely do the sugared, cranberry and orange peels. Those received almost as many comments as the actual cheesecake. I used simple syrup since I had it and added a little bit of honey. Soaked the cranberries for more than 10 minutes because I forgot about them. Then let them dry on a plate for way more than 10 minutes because again I forgot about them. Then gently shook them and the candied orange peels with table sugar in a Ziploc bag. They came out picture perfect.  Then left them on a plate on my counter overnight to dry and decorated the cake in the morning. Happy baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you use raspberries, try adding a grated apple to up the pectin content.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What is cookie butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Avid Reader. It's like Nutella kinda but with crumbled speculoos instead of chocolate hazelnut?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is INCREDIBLE! You would think with all the sugar and Biscoff that this cheesecake would be heavy and overly sweet, but it’s light and tangy. The orange zest really makes this dessert taste refreshing and brings a much needed brightness to each bite. I added a bit more than a tablespoon, which I personally think enhanced the dish. Yes, it takes a lot of bowls and time, but because there’s no baking the cheesecake it is virtually foolproof. Highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For folks who like to make ahead: the day before, I parbaked the crust for 7-8 minutes at 350 degrees and kept it wrapped on the counter overnight. This worked out very well. I also made the cranberry gelee 1 day ahead, storing on the counter overnight. On the day of assembling, make sure to loosen the gelee with the electric beater before adding the cream cheese. The cheesecake comes together easily. Though I don't like overly sweet desserts this was surprisingly well-balanced.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They have good cookie butter at Trader Joe’s

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I make ahead and freeze?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t like cheesecake so my feelings are invalid here. My husband though, big cheesecake fan and he said he loved it. He said the tang of the cranberry really plays nicely into the sweetness of the cookie butter. I let it set overnight and cut into it after 12 hours of being in the fridge and it looks like the cranberry part hasn’t set yet (I used 1 cup of cranberry liquid as recommended). So maybe I messed up somewhere or it just needs more time. But overall, pretty easy to make after watching the video. Also, SO MANY BOWLS AND SPATULAS!!! lol

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this and not only was it delicious, it wasn’t that hard. Yes there are a lot of steps but the only tedious one is straining the cranberry concoction to get rid of the seeds and skins.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and while stunningly beautiful, I can't say I felt it was worth the time and effort. The cranberry mixture was great until the dairy was added, which sadly mellowed it's tang and made it very bland; the biscoff-cream layer was too mild to do anything except be a cream cheese carrier and the crust is underwhelming at just cookies and butter. Too sweet & too mild to let either the biscoff OR cranberries shine. Might as well take cream cheese and dunk in biscoff: done.

For ex, biscotti cookies are super sweet by themselves & do you need to add sugar to cook  the cranberries - OJ has a lot of sugar & having a more tart topping might be a nice contrast. And what was the purpose of the cake batter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Cookie butter is Belgian, exclusively, made by Biscoff, and only Biscoff.
Speculoos or speculaas are popular in many countries, but they are originally from the Netherlands, Belgium and Germany.
For some reason, Belgium doesn’t get the gastronomic credit it deserves.It’s not just cookies and waffles (btw, nothing like American Belgian waffles)
I suggest a visit.
 Belgium, per capita,  has more Michelin restaurants than France.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Thieusies You may or may not have a point about Belgian cooking but this example is inapposite. Cookie butter is not like champagne.  It can be mass-produced anywhere. In the USA the Trader Joe’s version is probably the most popular.  Its ingredients are substantially similar to the Biscoff one and I can verify that they are equally delicious.  From other comments it sounds like you can also make it at home using different types of cookie.

I highly recommend this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: "1 teaspoon kosher salt, such as Diamond Crystal" Huh? You're recommending a brand of NaCl?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Andy Yes! Kosher salt and regular table salt are two completely different “animals”. Kosher salt has larger, lighter, flakier crystals as compared with regular table salt. Diamond Crystal is the gold standard for most experienced bakers and cooks. If you prefer to use regular table salt, you definitely can. But you must use half the amount specified for kosher salt. If the recipe specifies 1 teaspoon of kosher salt, then you would use 1/2 teaspoon of table salt. Hope this helps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Andy.  As ATK, KAF and other respected recipe sites have pointed out, professional bakeries and restaurants use the Diamond Crystal brand because of its lighter in weight by volume. All kosher salts are not the same. If you use a different brand, your food will be too salty. The reason being is that 1 teaspoon of diamond crystal salt weighs approximately 2.8 to 3 g whereas Morton’s kosher salt weighs around 4.8 to 5 g per teaspoon with table salt rounding out to between 6 - 7 g per teaspoon. Diamonds larger less dense crystals, take up more volume. Thus when someone is formulating a recipe wanting you to have an exact reproduction of their creation when it comes to kosher salt, it is important to state the brand that was used along with the measurement. When I found this out, I instantly understood why so many of my recipes had turned out too salty over the years when following the recipe exactly. I did not understand that there was a massive difference in kosher salt depending on the brand.  I hope this has been helpful. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I believe if you use another salt, like Morton’s, you would use less.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I very rarely comment but there are a few things I discovered that I thought might be helpful to others. 
1) if you have small kids, this is a good recipe to make with them. Lots of mixing, no loud stand mixers, many steps, most of which can be paused at any time and returned to later.
2) if like me you have several large bowls but only one extra large bowl, use the extra large for the sugar mixture that the cream cheese is added to. Don't use it on the whipped cream. There was a fair amount of cream cheese flung around by my blender. (not including the inevitable cream cheese flung around by my 4 year old) 
3) I made this for a birthday, and the recipient requested strawberry instead of cranberry. I used frozen strawberries and only a half cup of sugar with them, but otherwise followed the recipe. It worked! If I make this again with a berry, I'm going to try it with Nutella instead of cookie butter, and use chocolate wafers for the crust. 
4) recognizing some of this is probably because of my adaptation, I did find this a little sweet and would probably reduce sugar in both steps. 

Great cake and technique overall!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the cranberry jam this morning and it is delicious. However, this is a public service about the way companies are packaging cranberries these days. They’re bagging them in cute little red nets made of plastic thread. That plastic thread is so fine.  I found thread in the cranberries and on my cutting board. But most of it was so fine that would be impossible to detect. I  worry about microfibers of plastic getting into my food. Please be careful as you cut the bags of netting. How can we encourage companies to use regular plastic bags and avoid the Marketing cuteness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland

commentBody: I've candies the oranges, dirtied all the bowls, and performed mad multi step science in the kitchen. I can already tell this recipe is amazing. Thanksgiving needs to get here faster or this cheesecake might be eaten before the family gatherings.

Just put it in the fridge and it looks great! Taste testing will happen tomorrow on Thanksgiving, but wanted to add this comment in case anyone else has a similar issue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2 things. I ended up making cookie butter, it was super easy and cheaper than buying it. And secondly I'd probably skip it if I made it again and maybe make that layer with a slightly loosened marmalade swirled in instead. I found the cookie flavour kept things a bit too heavy for my taste with the cream cheese and whip cream as well, it needed even more tart freshness .

commentBody: @Jacob C I agree. I just finished making it and after tasting from the bowls, I can’t imagine eating more than a spoonful during Thanksgiving. There is so much fat in here. I loved the idea but wish it was lighter. I’ll keep reading the comments looking for suggestions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this for my Thanksgiving gathering. I followed the instructions to a T. I let it chill overnight in the fridge and took it out of the fridge right before serving. It did not hold its shape when cut into. The flavors were delicious but it had the consistency similar to a stiff pudding. I wish I would have frozen it and served semi thawed so that it could have kept its shape.

But, it’s beautiful and tasty, so I’m curious how it held up for the others who’ve tried this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My son- in-law’s mother made this for Thanksgiving and it was delicious!  She said it did take a lot of bowls but all of the steps were easy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought this dessert was not only delicious, but super fun to make. I enjoyed the process and everyone thought it was a smash for our Thanksgiving dessert!  I even made my own orange skin garnish (through the help of another video). On Tuesday night I made the crust and froze it. On Wednesday, I finished the cheesecake and left it in the refrigerator overnight. On Thursday morning I made the garnish and decorated. What fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So much fun to make! It was absolutely delicious and the prettiest dessert on the Thanksgiving table. The video was very helpful and made it feel less complicated than the multiple steps as written.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and it was very well received. Even one of the younger family members who notoriously doesn’t like cream cheese or cheesecake enjoyed a whole slice. Confirmed it’s a lot of bowls. Didn’t have a hand mixer so very tediously switched back and forth the steps in my stand mixer. The crust was very flavorful and really appreciated the hint of salt to balance the sweet. Nice mix of flavors and textures. Appreciated it wasn’t too complicated or intimidating to make. Made it the day before serving and it held up fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was the most popular thing on the table. Very delicious. I saw that some commenters noted the cranberry layer could be loose so I melted some unflavored gelatin with water and added a tablespoon to each mixture. This resulted in very stable but still creamy layers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a hit at this year's Thanksgiving table.  You are right about all the bowls. Next time, I think I'll use a little less of the strained cranberries (just shy of 1 cup) as the cranberry mousse layer was a tad runny.  But it was all delicious.  And I agree, the video was QUITE helpful.  It's quite a bit more effort for me than a bain marie style cheesecake, but it was worth it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and followed the recipe exactly as is. A showstopper of a dessert. Friends were delighted by it. A tremendous amount of effort. I typically prefer an Alison Roman or Ina Garten dessert- unfussy, minimal bowls/equipment, full flavor. This was not that. Not something I’ll be adding to my repertoire but glad I made it for a special occasion. If you’re curious about the flavor, have the time, bowls, and energy then I suggest you try it at least once. If someone else brings this to a holiday party in 2025, I’d probably have a slice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made a half recipe in a 7.5” springform pan and that worked fine.  This is tasty and quite light for being a rich T-day dessert. There is no way prep time is 15 minutes, however. The cranberry sauce alone cooks for 10 minutes. Multiple bowls, multiple spatulas, multiple steps. Like buying a house you need to do it twice to learn from your first go-round. It’s pretty and we enjoyed it. The cookie butter has lots of ‘stuff’ in it; I wonder how almond butter would work.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Immediately out of the fridge, mine was just about perfect.  30 minutes later, the cranberry layer was beginning to run a little and we felt it was somewhat overpowering.  I only put in 7/8c of the cranberry sauce to start with, but next time I’d either drop down to 1/2c of cranberry (settling for slightly less flavor) or leave the whipped cream out of the cranberry layer (changing the texture for the firmer).

Speculoos layer & crust are totally perfect as-is.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cheesecake was not only beautiful but it was delicious.  Totally worth all the bowl washing.  I will definitely be making this again next year.  Thank you.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this on Thanksgiving Eve. I'm happy to report it is delicious! It didn't look exactly like the one in the video, but I don't consider this a flaw - my cranberry layer was thicker than my cookie butter one. My main issue was that the crust stuck to the springform pan when the ring was removed. Next time, I'll layer the filling into custard cups and top them with chunks of cookie crust. I think individual servings of this super soft cheesecake is the answer to the problems I had.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 10/10 incredible!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I used a graham cracker crust instead of biscoff cookies to make the cheesecake slightly less sweet and I highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Everyone raved about this dessert. It was not too sweet either which I appreciated.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving, it was a BIG hit. Harder to make than I would have thought -- even though it's a no-bake cheesecake, there are lots of steps, and I messed up a couple of times. But it was really, really good, everyone loved it. Might become a standard.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this with my 13 year old daughter for Thanksgiving. We had a lot of fun, and it was the star of the table. We had a hard time whipping the cream cheese mixture, as we don't have the strongest electric hand whisk. We whisked the top inch, then kind of turned it over with a spoon and did it one inch at a time. This beauty traveled really well, although I should have held back the sugared berries and added them at the last minute. Fabulous! Five stars!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was delicious. I found it to be a bit too much salt in the crust, so my suggestion is add half the amount and then add it to taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added the strained cranberry skins to orange marmalade for morning toast. I always make cranberry orange marmalade this time of year.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cheesecake was the hit of our Thanksgiving dinner! I ran out of steam to make the decorations, but it tasted great. The cranberry’s assertive with a nice tart-sweet balance, and the cookie-butter layer has a subtle flavor so there are no flavor wars. 100% would make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I have made this following the recipe and the flavors turned out to be great--the cranberry layer with the orange zest was bright and the speculous layer (with the TJ brand spread) was very good. I thought it would be too much, but it was quite delicate. 
I had a couple of issues: the first one was with the crust. It seems there was not enough butter in it to keep it together once out of the mold, so it crumbled completely when sliced. The second was with the cranberry layer, that collapsed fairly quickly once out of the fridge. I put the cake on a stand and we all witnessed mini landslide of pink goop that took the decorative cranberries down before we got to cut it. It was out maybe 1.5 hrs at the most. Not a good look, but we ate it anyway.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m curious if anyone has tried reducing the sugar. We liked the dessert very much but felt it was intensely sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The bad - you WILL use a lot of equipment. I dirtied five scrapers.
The good - you will have a lot of bowls, beaters, and scrapers to lick.
Terrific. I used all the cranberries, because reserving a quarter cup will NOT get you the garnish seen in the photos. Cook the cranberries until the jellied sauce is very reduced and it will set up beautifully. Don't whip the cream until right before you want to use it. I whipped it first, per the recipe, and had to rewhip. Don't risk overwhipping the cream

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was very well received! Took a solid 1.5 hours without the garnish. The time intensive piece was the cranberry filling. The labor intensive part was the cheesecake filling. Hope ya'll been diligent with upper body work outs

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was worth every bowl! I stored mine in the freezer instead of the fridge and it was perfectly edible - no need to thaw. We didn’t get around to eating it until the next day. It’s such a beautiful dessert and relatively easy to make! I love trying Vaughn’s recipes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made it today. Got Cookie butter at Trader Joe’s. Cheesecake was amazing. This recipe is a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

It was easy to make but it just felt like someone just made up the recipe without concern of it all working together. I served it to 9 people. No one cared for it. And 15 years of baking and never threw out leftovers except for this cake. I would never make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @TMHPerhaps, just maybe you forgot an ingredient in the cookie butter portion? It really is a perfect recipe as written. I served it to 8 people and they ALL loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

It ran over the sides of the cake when I removed the spring form.  The crust was disappointing as it was a hard block of cookie and couldn’t cut it.  Really wanted this to turn out so if anyone can weigh in on my mistakes I’d appreciate it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this yesterday. It looked great but was overly sweet even though I cut back on the sugar. The cranberry layer was great, but the cookie butter layer was too sweet and was overwhelmed by the cookie butter which tasted quite “processed” to me. The crust tasted like cookies with a lot of cold butter. Like others mentioned the recipe generated a lot of dishes! Would not make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the mistake of using salted butter, and the crust was way too salty. With the salted butter I think I could have eliminated the extra salt from the crust entirely.

commentBody: We forgot cookie butter for the middle layer but honestly, did NOT need it. We just added an extra package of cream cheese and about a 1/4 cup more sugar and it was perfect.  I don’t think I would want with the cookie butter since our way we could really taste the orange zest with the cream cheese. Definitely a holiday staple for us now but much prefer with our modification!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’d call it fussy but delicious. But I’m a pretty lazy cook to be fair.I think I was a little bit tricked by the no-bake description even though I watched the video :) Yes I used every bowl in my kitchen. Yes I also used almost every mixing bowl and cup too. And most of my other cooking tools. I’m not much of a baker so this was a level up for me, but hotdamn it was delicious. I think it tasted like a Christmas PB&J. Honestly next time I’ll skip the sugared cranberries, they just slowed me down when I was ready to eat my slice. And since I made it just for my husband and kid, we don’t need it to look cute.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My husband made this for Thanksgiving and it was beyond delicious. I was a bit skeptical about the combination at first, however, the flavors perfectly compliment each other. This is definitely a recipe we will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think I would substitute a "regular" cheesecake filling and graham cracker crust with the cranberry filling on the top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely amazing and beautiful. Directions were perfect. Made it for my adult daughter for a party. There wasn’t one bite left for me to taste. I’m making it again next week!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too sweet and too dense, unfortunately. Not worth the effort or the calories.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Totally delicious. Yes, yes many bowls are involved but it's really a breeze. I even cheated and used canned whole cranberry sauce. Took it to a party, everyone loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone else struggling to make the cookie crust. Doesn't seem to be enough crumbs and they keep lifting off the bottom of the pan...help!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavors on this were great. However our cranberry layer never quite firmed up, despite being very careful about the ratios (and even adding a tiny bit of clear-gel at the end when it looked a bit thin). I definitely recommend trying this recipe, but if the cranberry layer seems too thin, it is. Perhaps one should make a bit extra of the cream cheese mixture and/or whipped cream to have on hand in case you find yourself in that situation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good grief, did anyone commenting actually make this?  As they say, if you want to know the way, heed the one who's been to the top of the mountain.  Others are just dancing at the base.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This did not work for me. The crust was delicious but it cracked and broke up when I cut it. After being in the refrigerator overnight it was still so soft the layers slumped to the side when pieces were removed and served. It looked like a mess and almost no one at our 12-person Thanksgiving dinner wanted a piece. Very disappointing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe in 90 minutes, working at a very relaxed pace with two tiny sous chefs watching me. The cranberry sauce took some time to press through the strainer, and I panicked at first thinking I wouldn’t get a cup. Keep at it, it took at least 15 minutes of pressing and scraping with a spoon. Yes there are a few annoying beater switches and a fair share of dishes to wash. But, the make-ahead nature made it so stressfree and easy to take to my holiday party, and it was a huge hit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very festive and truly delicious. Prep time was definitely way more than the 15 min noted, but it was worth it

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: somebody try this and let me know cause ain't no cranberries on the shelf anymore and I still want to make this!! @Fay Wouk

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Beautiful dessert that doesn’t disappoint.  But 15 minute prep time?  By my count more like 90 minutes as you wait for the crust to chill, cook the cranberries for at least 15 minutes, and chill the cranberry sauce.  And oh yes, the time it takes me to clean my food processor and re-assemble it like a jigsaw for storage.   But well worth the time for a fancy show-off dessert

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great dessert for a holiday crowd - pretty, festive and goes a long way (it is RICH). The second time I made this, I omitted the sugar from both the base and the cream cheese mixture - to me the biscuits are sweet enough already and I like the tang of the cream cheese without extra sugar. Also added lemon zest in addition to orange. This for me was a better balance of flavours!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any gluten-free options for the speculoos-style cookie crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
