commentBody: @AmericanGirl I found a gf recipe for it that looks promising! https://www.letthemeatgfcake.com/gluten-free-biscoff-style-cookies/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
As he says “
It’s a symphony of flavors”  that I must make!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any suggestions for a gluten free crust option for celiac in the family?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jacki i found this recipe that looks promising. https://www.letthemeatgfcake.com/gluten-free-biscoff-style-cookies/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Jacki
There is a gluten-free speculoos recipe at delight-fuel.com. I just made it because there are only 2 tablespoons of maple syrup in the whole recipe. That makes about 30 cookies, it is not very sweet and will probably make a more neutral crust. Good luck!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to make this....I watched the video which is fantastic Vaughn......but what is cookie butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found this recipe helpful: https://flouringkitchen.com/homemade-cookie-butter/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where is the recipe or instructions for the cookie butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Biscoff cookie butter can be found at Target, Walmart and many supermarkets. Trader Joe’s also has their own speculoos cookie butter. I’m going to get a jar to make this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is it recommended to use fresh squeezed juice from an orange? or like the breakfast juice from a bottle (e.g. Simply Orange, Tropicana)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Does this recipe advise having an AED handy after eating a serving of this cheesecake?
Even one serving has over 800 calories.  That's a meal in itself.  Move over cheesecake factory, you have met your match!  Why not add in some beef tallow and really make it good!
Ow, my arteries are hardening as I write this!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thinking a pretzel crust would be great with this recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In the version he made, the top layer is a deep pink but in the photographed version, it’s a light pink. I wonder what the difference is? And yes, I enjoyed this video as well. :-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lee probably the lighting!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lee - did you - or anyone?! - figure out how Vaughn achieved that super vibrant pink in the video version?!  Mine is chilling now and looks lovely, but is definitely on the paler pink side of the spectrum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there a substitute for the orange juice. I do not prefer orange flavored cranberry sauce. I am afraid to skip the ingredient because 1/2 cup of liquid and sweetness from the juice could negatively impact the consistency.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How about an equivalent amount of cranberry juice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This looks like a lot of work for a lazy cook such as myself. But I'll bet the cranberry layer all by itself would be wonderful in ramekins, no crust required.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I whip cream, I often forget to put on an apron, so I put the bowl down in the kitchen sink, so when it spatters it just spatters the inside sides of my sink.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A great recipe. I would recommend sweetening the cranberries to taste if you have picky eaters in the family.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sorry, but I think of cooking as bringing basic, natural ingredients together to create something greater than the sum of its parts.  "Cookie Butter" is not an ingredient, cookie butter is considered an ultra-processed food. It is classified as such because it is an industrial product made with multiple ingredients and additives, which are often not found in a typical home kitchen, to create the final product. Please don't base NYT recipes on this kind of product.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am in love with this video and recipe! I made it today and while it was a lot of steps (yes) it is beautiful and so worth it!! Thank you for this beautiful recipe you have given us!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: any idea how I could downsize this to make in a 6"springform? Loved how this came out but it's huge when you have a small household. BTW used a mix of graham crackers and thin Swedish ginger snap cookies for the crust. ery good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Deborah you can halve the recipe. Any 9 inch round recipe should generally work in a 6 inch round if you halve it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Deborah Haven’t tried this yet but generally a 9 inch recipe like this can be halved and then made in a 6 inch pan. I bought a 6 inch springform pan. (This rule also applies to regular cakes.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Disappointing. Didn't have a lot of flavor even though I added suggested extras. Then read the Nutrition info. Yikes. Should have done that first. Used lots of utensils and bowls. Not a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: OK, loved the video, shopped the recipe and planning to make tomorrow. Suppose I wanted to add something green to the garnish decoration, any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made it today and just put in the freezer. Looks perfect. All components were delicious. I found a cookie butter without soy, Choc Zero. My son has a soy allergy. Ordered it from Amazon, it tastes good, also gluten free. Looking forward to serving this special dessert at my early Thanksgiving on Wednesday.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question on transporting: this has been chilling overnight and I am traveling 3.5 hrs today. should I leave it in the springform pan and chill it while traveling or remove from the pan?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Shanlee32 I would keep it in the springform pan and chill for the drive!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved the video, so I had to make it. I will admit, I think I used everything in my kitchen: regular mixer, hand mixer, multiple bowls, measuring cups, a scale, several spatulas and mixing spoons. I didn’t really mind because I loaded the dishwasher as I went and I’m not responsible for the rest of the meal. Definitely not a last minute recipe, so give yourself time. I’m Cuban and we don’t do cranberries, ever, so this was a fun intro for me. My kitchen did look like a crime scene for a while (so much red!), and at one point, I looked down at my sweatshirt to see an almost perfectly round bright red spot on my chest that looked like a bullet hole, but mercifully was only a splotch of cranberry something. But it looks really pretty in the fridge and I’m looking forward to decorating it tomorrow. Just in case it doesn’t taste as lovely as it looks, I’m also making his chocolate brownie cookies to take along. I have high hopes though, I tasted at every step, and that cookie butter was a delightful revelation. So, thank you, Vaughn, it really was fun to make!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm getting ready to make this.....maybe crazy with all the other things I have to do but.....it sounds so good and different from what we usually have.  I could only get crunchy cookie butter so wondering if I could make it all in a food processor? or does it make any difference?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Valerie how did this go for you? I’m in the same boat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did this with my 4 year old so we ended up doing it in stages throughout the day. Since we cleaned as we went, breaking it up allowed us to only use 3 bowls which was great! Fun recipe with lots of flavors, bright colors, and techniques to do with a kid. Crushing the cookies in a ziplock bag may have been the highlight, along with licking the bowl. 👍👍

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can anyone say how much cranberry sauce you use if you are not making it yourself?  About a cup and a half or how many ounces?  My grocer has really good cranberry dressing and it’s pretty much the same and I already have some, but I don’t know how much to use.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @NatBB You need one cup of strained cranberry sauce (without the solids). I had some cranberry sauce left over from Thanksgiving and tried straining it, but that didn’t work. It looks like you have to make the cranberry sauce in the recipe and strain it while it’s hot, before it sets, otherwise you won’t get enough liquid going through the sieve. It’s really easy to make the sauce by the way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this ahead of time (Tuesday of Thanksgiving). It’s beautiful and incredibly tasty. It does user every bowl (5), spoon (3), mixer (2), spatula (1), whisk (2), and food processor (1) in your kitchen. At first I was kinda annoyed by this. Then I thought about all the other layered desserts I’ve made. This is on a par. 

In terms of recipe notes, the sugar and orange zest mixture is sublime and adds so much to the layers. You could easily zest 2-3 oranges. 

I stored on Tuesday after letting it sit in the fridge uncovered for 4 hours. It is currently tightly covered. I figured if it looks bad because I made it ahead of time, I would just cover the top layer with whipped cream. It’s a no bake cheesecake - I’m sure it will be fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi, this recipe looks amazing and unfortunately Thanksgiving is only for 4 this year and we’ve also got a pumpkin pie making an appearance. Any idea if simply halving this for a 7” springform would work? I know the ratio is really 0.6… Or just go all in and stuff myself with the whole cheesecake?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just finished assembling and now we wait until tomorrow to eat. I can already tell it will be amazing (as the kids and I tried all parts before we assembled!). I couldn’t find biscoff cookies so used graham crackers instead. It won’t be as good but still works. I wish I watched the video before for tips on how to assemble as mine won’t be half as pretty as Vaughn’s, but at least I have some tips on how to slice it properly and decorate. I can’t wait to try this one!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Since so many people are giving this a trial run for Thanksgiving, I'd love to know if this was so delicate that you had to put this in the fridge after an hour.  I'd only make this for a party, I imagine, and that's just going to be too fussy.  Is this something really intended for small dinner parties?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I left mine in a cooler with an ice pack while everybody ate dinner. Even then, it was too soft to cut neatly. It really needs a cold refrigerator or several minutes in the freezer before serving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just finished topping the cheesecake with the orange and cranberry garnish. I wish I could include a photo. The cheesecake is beautiful, and delicious - how do I know that it’s delicious? I sampled plenty of the batter while I was making the cheesecake. It’s a modern twist for a Thanksgiving dessert!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So ... I am a 63 year old no-cook Dad who occasionally likes to make a mess in the kitchen. I made this cheesecake for my extended family Thanksgiving dinner. Really appreciated the video - the tip on how to cook the cranberry sauce was very helpful. Instead of store bought OJ, I juiced and strained a large orange I used for the zest; my cranberry sauce was extra delicious. I also candied 10 fresh blueberries along with as many cranberries for my garnish, which added a pop of color and flavor!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving. Everyone enjoyed it! The cookie butter and crust balance out the tartness of the cranberry. The only problem I ran into was that the crust was a little difficult to cut. I'm not sure if I packed it too firmly or because it had just come from the fridge. With a little extra effort, we were able to cut each layer nicely.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this, with substitutions because couldn't find some ingredients (gingersnaps instead of biscoff, granola butter in cookie dough  flavor instead of speculoos). (In line with NYT's tofu-thanksgiving, I substituted soft tofu for 2/3 the cream cheese). Added vanilla, nutmeg.
The good: delicious flavors, great use of cranberries and other ingredients
The bad: nothing, it's just really heavy, so take small portions
The ugly: nothing, except my efforts didn't look as pretty as the NYT picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The video is wondeful!!! Thank you, Vaughn, for a GREAT recipe.  I made this even before watching the video, and it was a HUGE hit for Thanksgiving dessert.  And so pretty.  But looks aren't everything, are they?  The cheesecake delivers for that sweet and tangy combination.  I added as little sugar as I could to the cranberries because the cookie butter is SO SO sweet.

Adding to my holiday rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wouldnt make those again. Didn’t really taste like cheesecake or have the same texture at all. Served it to a table of 8 and no one commented on it. It was a lot of work for zero payoff.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this because I'd never heard of cookie butter. Followed the recipe exactly. Disappointing. The flavor of the crust overwhelmed everything and the cranberry layer, although pretty, wasn't sharp enough. However, the skins and seeds of the cranberry jam are really good on toast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Soooooooooo good!!  The tart from the cranberries balances the richness so perfectly!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This turned out amazing. I didn’t do the candied cranberries, just kept it plain. This was a huge hit for Friendsgiving. I don’t ever want to bake a cheesecake again. So light and flavorful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This took A LOT of bowls. But we've just served it hosting this year's Thanksgiving and it was kind of a showstopper. Lots of work, but big payoff in the end.👍

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Halved the recipe and made a 6 in cake. I measured out it the cranberry sauce after straining and the cranberry part tasted great, not light at all. The whole thing was so so good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this a day ahead. Followed directions exactly and it was wonderful. Not too sweet or rich. Will definitely make this again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We do one fancy baking adventure a year usually and this was a good one!  The flavors are so good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm not a fan of cheesecake but made this dessert anyway for Thanksgiving. I followed the instructions as written and it took a while and got some dishes dirty but was the absolute surprise smash hit. 5 stars. 100% winner. Will make again. Washing dishes is easy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this dessert for Thanksgiving. It is definitely not for beginners having said that I think the effort is definitely worthy of the result. His recipe definitely exposed me to new things. I learned how to make homemade cookie butter and what speculoos are. BTW, you can buy these at Aldi.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this recipe as written.  Not difficult, but a lot of steps and components.  Plan ahead.  Good tasting, very very rich.  Probably not the best choice for my family when we were already full after a large holiday meal.  I loved the cranberry layer, but would prefer a vanilla layer instead of the hyper-sweet cookie butter layer.  Mine didn’t slice as neatly as the video despite an overnight in the fridg, but that’s likely due to operator error, not the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Many bowls were harmed in the making of this dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The sugared cranberries were delicious. Everything about this cheesecake was so good; my guests ate and left no crumbs. 10/10

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving, everybody was really delighted with the taste. Not what they were expecting, but delicious! It is a lot of steps, but worth it. For my Christmas party, though, I will turn this into a petit fours of sorts, a slice of this is really too rich and my guests (20+) will all get to enjoy a little bite of this.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I opted to just make the cranberry layer because the biscoff layer sounded too sweet for me.  (I tend to make recipes with 10-20% less sugar.)  I loved the taste of the cranberry layer, but I think I used too much cranberry sauce and the layer wouldn't set.  I recommend starting with 1/2 cup of the cranberry sauce and trying to match the color of the picture.  My layer was much darker pink. The biscoff crust was too sweet for my taste.  Loved the sugared cranberries on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Beautiful and hyper-sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best cheesecake I ever had!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've gotten everything needed BUT my springform is 9"!  Any conversion factor sites that can help?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I should have sent a picture of my kitchen after the cheesecake was in the fridge.  Yes, it was laborious at best, but the outcome was worth it.  The layers were colorful, and the Biscoff crust was more flavorful than the usual graham cracker.  Yes, it was a success... and I will make it again after I've recovered.
A note... I was using the NYT Cooking app on my phone. Next time, I'll print it out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this as written. It turned out beautifully and tasted so good! The flavor combination is fantastic. It’s not as sweet as I expected. A big hit of our Thanksgiving. 
I have to comment on how well tested and accurate Vaughn’s recipes are. You can count on them if you follow his instructions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made this in about 1 hour. Took my time and halved the recipe as there are only two of us. Super easy! Had about 1/2 cup extra mascarpone filling so I put it in a bowl topped with Italian cherries in sauce and handed it to the hubby. I think it went down in two gulps with yummy sounds abounding. Next I am trying the pumpkin chai. Thanks NYT!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this and it came out great! Loved the base, I used Danish gingersnaps. The cranberry topping set up perfectly and was punchy without being overwhelming. The cheesecake and cookie butter layer was a little lost between the flavorful crust and topping- I would definitely make this again but may add a bit of cinnamon or vanilla to the cheesecake layer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe for Thanksgiving.  My recommendations.  Line the cheesecake pan bottom and sides with parchment paper, it's easier to remove from the pan.  I didn't bother with the cranberry orange garnish.  I shaved milk chocolate in a very light layer between the cookie butter and cranberry layer.  I also sprinkled milk chocolate shavings on the top.  I really think this work well parfait style too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it. I love it. Will make again but will probably add more cream cheese to see if it sets up a little more firm.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this tonight for my FriendsGiving meal tomorrow, and the flavors are incredible--I can't wait to dig in tomorrow. (Suffice it to say that I didn't eat any dinner tonight after licking the mixing bowls and mixer blades--who needs to put them in the dishwasher now that they look clean??). Bravo, Mr V--this recipe is superb. Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Straining the cranberry mixture took a lot more effort than expected. It just sat in the fine steel mesh. 

When pressing the crust try to make it as thin as possible. I dumped the whole thing into the springform and ended up with brick like corners on some pieces. Next time I’m spooning and spreading and pressing — then using fingers to build up the sides.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was really great! Absolutely a hit at Thanksgiving (with lots of excellent dessert competition). The most significant change I made was adding one packet of powdered gelatin (after blooming/heating) to the whipped cream - basically making stabilized whipped cream that then folded in as normal. It held up to slicing well but was still very smooth and soft. Other small mods: I used half ginger snaps and half graham crackers rather than biscoffs in the crust. The only cookie butter I could find was crunchy, which worked just fine! And I upped everything by about 1/3 to fill a 10” springform pan. The whole time I was having doubts about whether these flavors go together, but it was a great and surprising result!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes, this week requires many bowls. I agree with other reviewers that I thought that the cranberry would be much more tart. I let this sit in the fridge overnight and it still was not firm enough. I think the recipe should recommend freezing it instead.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving for a crowd of cheesecake lovers. It was a crowd pleaser! I made it exactly as instructed except for leaving off the garnish. It is already being requested for Christmas. Definitely going in the keep folder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I won Thanksgiving this year with this dessert. 🦃Don’t worry though, I gave Vaughn all the credit. Such a great recipe! I opted for orange zest on top which was a nice color combo (and way easier than the sugared cranberries)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I posted earlier how amazing this was.  I also think it would be killer as a banana pudding style summer cheesecake for a BBQ. Leave out the orange zest.  Instead of cranberry purée, add mashed banana. The biscoff crust and cookie butter layer would pair well with the banana layer for more complexity than banana pudding.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone have suggestions on replacements for the biscoff? They are sold out everywhere and that is the only speculoos brand my area carries. As a side note, I am not baking cookies to make the crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Half graham crackers, half gingersnaps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Cambree
Hi, I made this for Thanksgiving and didn’t use the biscoff or the cookie butter (soy allergies). For the crust I used graham crackers- same amount by weight with a few dashes of nutmeg and about a 1 inch piece of ginger grated, all added into the food processor with the butter, sugar and salt. I’m not sure the butter ratio was good enough as my crust fell apart as we were lifting slices out. The flavor with the ginger, nutmeg and cinnamon already specified was delicious. I might add in an extra tablespoon of butter or prebake it for 10 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe and, despite many steps, easy to pull off. For me, straining enough cranberry sauce took longer than five minutes, or maybe it just felt like an eternity. Worth it for the final result. Love the fresh cranberry taste with the cookie butter flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This looks amazing! I cannot wait to make it for our family Christmas!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was an amazing dessert Vaughn, it will now be a regular on our Thanksgiving table. The number of bowls aside, this is an easy dessert to make, and there were raves all around.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My family raved about this beautiful and unusual cheesecake and insisted upon it becoming a tradition. 
The following worked beautifully:
—Reduce the sugar to about 5/8 of recommended.
—Dot about 1/2 the “discarded” cranberries between the two layers.
—Freeze one or two days ahead then thaw to serve.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful. Everyone loved it and wants it as a tradition. I used less sugar, put nearly half of the "discarded" cranberry mix between the layers since we're all cranberry fans, and froze it to serve after thawing. Truly outstanding.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Almost exactly as the recipe says - though I don't own a hand mixer and had to pull out the manual beater for the whipped cream. Tip: double the cranberry sauce recipe. Puree the sauce using an immersion blender. Sieve as much as you need to make 1 cup smooth sauce. Return the rest to the pan. Mix in. You have your cranberry sauce for the main course!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made this recipe and it came out absolutely delicious. I was a bit worried about it being too sweet considering all the sugars in the ingredients and added, but it was not overly sweet. I highly recommend giving this a try if you like biscoff flavors and cranberry-orange flavor combinations. I would recommend reducing the salt amount in the crust to half though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it! It was delicious but…incredibly calorie dense so after we had our wee slices I brought it over to my neighbors who were in the midst of their Thanksgiving desserts. Fun to make, and fairly easy. I even made the cookie butter. My favorite part is the cranberry.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was worth any effort you may be worried about!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A big hit at Friendsgiving. I cut back the sugar by about a third, and it turned out slightly sweet for my taste and perfect for everyone else’s. 

I used a scale to divide the cream cheese mixture and the whipped cream, and both layers set up well. I’m not sure it would have held up for a full hour out in a warm room filled with people, but as it was, the people got it before the warmth did.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just finished making this. Since I always make a cranberry sauce for Thanksgiving, I made extra and used that for my 1 cup. Worked and tasted great. It does use a lot of bowls, but each of the steps are not terribly long. I portioned some into a ramekin for my gluten-free coworker. The cake gets cut tomorrow at work for a maternity leave send-off, but I put all of the components on a spoon - delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you make the individual components ahead at all?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mia I made this recipe and put some thought into this because I didn’t enjoy how much cleaning up I had to do (even with cleaning as I went). You could definitely make some aspects ahead:  the crust (wrapped and stored in fridge. The cranberry sauce. The cream cheese mixture. I definitely wouldn’t make the whipped cream ahead/I wouldn’t fold in the whipped cream to the cream cheese mixture until I was ready to assemble and let set.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Whew!!! After all the bowls, measuring tools, ingredients and the cleanup, I did not feel up to the cranberry garnish. But the cheesecake was so delicious and definitely a show stopper.  I could have skipped the sugar added to the cookie crust as the cookies were sweet enough.  The tartness of the cranberry layer, orange zest and the sweetness of the cheesecake all came together beautifully.  I’ll definitely make this again… but maybe next year. Watching someone make ( video)  this was a bit more fun than making it myself.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this yesterday and it was very good, but the cheesecake was very heavy and dense. I wonder if there’s a way to make it lighter. Maybe by replacing some of the cream cheese with Greek yogurt and using some gelatin to help it set? Also, following some comments, I froze the cake rather than refrigerating it. A good suggestion, but it was a little difficult to slice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Never made a cheesecake before abs starting with this one was … a choice! 😂 The video was so helpful. The part I missed (again, novice baker) in the recipe is that it’s best to have this chill overnight. I saw no bake and just thought I could make it a couple of hours before the party I made it for. Looks like I’ll be taking some slices over today! It taste great. It looks pretty highly recommend making it and yes, you will use all the bowls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this cake for Thanksgiving for my family in CT and my friend made it for hers in Germany, where happily, she found fresh cranberries. Being from New England, fresh cranberries in all forms are an essential food group, but we didn't think customs would approve her taking them from NYC. Both cakes were delicious and festive, while decorated differently. I copied Vaughn, she used all sugared cranberries and orange rounds. I watched Vaughn's excellent video many times for directions and cheerful encouragement. Pink cranberry cookie-butter cheesecake would be pretty on Valentine's Day also.  Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can i make the cookie butter. there is no mention for what goes into that concoction. i have diligently read through tons of comments and do not see info about that. please share

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Huge hit at book club holiday party (the host said it was the best cheesecake he'd ever had).  Made as per the recipe except I used long thin wisps of orange zest instead of candied orange.  If you're going to cut directly from the springform pan (as per the video) consider lining it.  I made it a day in advance, leaving the garnish for the next day.  It can all be done with a hand beater, if you have a decent one (and room temperature ingredients!!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it and it was indeed magical. So beautiful and yummy. AND EASY!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Success! I made this for a Christmas party and it turned out marvelously! When I make it again, I will all a circle of parchment on the bottom of the pan and a 3” wide strip to encircle the springform. This will allow for a cleaner and more perfect release for a picture perfect presentation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this beautiful cheesecake for Thanksgiving dessert.  I very much appreciated the video as it helped tremendously.  Initially I was worried that the cranberry layer was not going to set, but it held when I removed the ring of my springform pan.  I decorated it with sprigs of rosemary and candied cranberries.  It was a huge hit with all of my guests.  Thank you for a this delicious and very creative recipe Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Super confused. What's the cookie butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Im concerned about the note at the end not to leave out at room temp for more than an hour. Id like to bring to a work party and it may be out for at least that, if not longer. Does the cake melt if left out for too long? May need to pivot!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @LS reporting back that it was delicious!!! I made it exactly as written and it turned out beautifully. The parchment round idea helped to lift it off to a platter easily and it held firm for an hour, then started to droop.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Genuinely such a delicious cheesecake!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Augh I wanted to be fancy so I made biscoff like cookies from scratch and cookie butter from that and candied my own orange peel…and I FORGOT to fold in the whipped cream at the end!! What will I get tomorrow? Just a denser, less fluffy but still good version? Or will it not hold up? Going to cry into my leftover cookies….

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for my family’s Christmas dinner and it was an absolute hit! I also made it with dairy free alternatives and made sure to keep everything cold and it worked out perfectly! This flavor combination was amazing thank you Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Christmas dinner and it was fabulous. The cranberry layer was noticeably softer but made a nice contrast with the firmer cookie butter layer. It was sweet but not overly so, and the candied peel and cranberries provided great flavour and textural contrast. I do think this cake will comfortably serve 16 (especially following a big holiday meal).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was good. Not great. It is very very sweet and for that reason I would not make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this for dessert on Christmas.  I made the crust Tuesday after work and left the pan in the freezer until it was ready for the filling, which I made on Christmas Eve. Both my husband and I found it absolutely delicious. This is a keeper that I will make again!  My only change is that we could only find crunchy cookie butter so that's what I used. I did follow another commenter's tip to freeze the cheesecake, which I did, however I put it in the refrigerator the next morning and likely pulled it out a little too early as I did find both fillings a little softer than I would have liked. And I agree with Vaughn, I think this was better than a baked cheesecake, not to mention easier!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this on 12/23 for a Christmas Eve dessert. Everyone loved it. It was the highlight of the evening. It helped everyone forget that I nearly set the kitchen on fire by over-broiling the garlic bread.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i think i used the right proportions but the crust dough came out way too wet to work with. uncertain what went wrong as i am quite used to doing this style of crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made 2 of these; one as a trial and one to take for Christmas dinner. The trial one came out perfectly even though we didn't have the best springform pan, but we made it work. The second was not so perfect as the top layer would not set properly even if we put it in the freezer for 30 minutes. Maybe the cream with stiff peaks wasn't stiff enough or maybe the proportions between the 2 parts weren't correct. When your measurements are about 1/2, that could be a problem. Either way, SUCCESS!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a winner! Made it as written, used the Trader Joe’s brand cookie butter. Delicious and not  too sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very pretty seasonal cheesecake. I prefer baked cheesecakes, but wanted to give this a go. I made it and served it as my dessert on Christmas day. It came out picture perfect! I enjoyed watching the video on this as well. However, I felt like the cookie crust along with the cookie butter layer was a bit much. I would back off on the cup of cookie butter if I made it again. Just too much of that favor. The cranberry layer was perfect, just the right amount of tartness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this as written. I used Biscoff cookies for the crust and Biscoff Cookie Butter for the cookie butter layer. It came together easily enough. Let it sit overnight in the fridge and unmolded easily in the morning. While visually stunning, we thought the cookie butter layer lacked flavor. Maybe next time I would add some additional cinnamon to that layer?  The cranberry layer and the crust were both delicious. The entire dessert was creamy and sweet, with just the right amount of tartness. Would look great on any holiday table!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Light cheesecake that is very modifiable! Couldn't find cookie butter for base cheesecake layer so subbed with pistachio butter to make a FANTASTIC Christmas colored dessert (green and red!). Got rave reviews from family members who previously did not like cheesecake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Echoing the reviews that mentioned the cranberry layer not setting (my cranberry sauce was like jelly and I used one cup) and the recipe using many bowls. It was almost a whole dishwasher load just for the cake! It tasted pretty good but way too sweet. I’d try again cutting the sugar but would be worried to mess with proportions since it already seems fussy with setting. Probably won’t repeat but family seemed to enjoy it, it was worth a try!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Our grocery store was completely out of fresh or frozen cranberries, so I subbed in canned cranberry jelly (1 cup). It actually worked out perfectly in terms of texture, though I wish the flavor would have been more pronounced. Still a lovely combination of flavors though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for my family at Christmas because my daughter-in-law cannot eat eggs or nuts. This was the perfect dessert for her and everyone else. Everyone raved about it. It is a big effort - lots of bowls to wash! - but entirely worth it. I will make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this with my 17 yr old great-nephew. He watched the video several times and added tips and commentary from Vaughn.  Excellent project and we had a wonderful time working through all the steps.  The result was totally worth it!  Family devoured it and helped with the clean up.  Would definitely do this again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for a NYE dinner and it got rave reviews. I was worried from reading the comments that the layers wouldn't set so I whipped the cream to stoliffer peaks in the video. TJs didn't have cookie butter so I made my own a few days earlier and refrigerated it - which made it thicker and a little drier than a creamy peanuter butter. I also didn't bring the cream cheese fully to room temp. I'm not sure if all of this helped the cheesecake layer to be more solid but it worked! no problem getting the cake out of the spring form and it lasted at room temp very well. Enough for people to get seconds. Like others in the comments, I could not find fresh or frozen cranberries in any store. Fortunately I had 1.5 cups left from Christmas so I topped it off with canned jellied cranberry sauce. All came together well. To the point that one of our foody friends demanded I send the leftovers home with him. It's a labor intensive cake and I'm not a baker at all but I'll be doing this one again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This a wonderful cheesecake recipe.  I enjoyed it with my family for the new year.  Everyone one loved it.  I followed the recipe excatly except substitute raspberry for the cranberry.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved the idea of this cheesecake, but didn’t need a full sized one. I halved everything and it fit perfectly into a 7” springform pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I knew after watching the video back in November that I had to make this. It did not disappoint. I laughed at Vaughn's comment about the recipe ensuring every bowl in the kitchen would be used and he wasn't kidding. 

It's not difficult, just a little cumbersome.

Brought this to a neighbor holiday party and it was a big hit. When I make again and it just for me and my husband will try freezing half to enjoy later. Hoping the recipe will work well for that and not develop icy crystals...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: SO YUMMY!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious. I couldn't find fresh or frozen cranberries, so I used frozen dark sweet cherries. Two variations I can suggest:

1. Omit the fruit layer and go full Biscoff. Will certainly save a few time-consuming steps.
2. If that's too much Biscoff, swap out the Biscoff cookies for a dark chocolate graham crust.

If I were certain it wouldn't affect the structure, I'd recommend eliminating or reducing the sugar from the batter. It's plenty sweet as is with the Biscoff spread + cookie crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for my son’s birthday (he doesn’t like frosting, so we’re always looking for birthday cake alternatives). I thought the crust had too much salt, I’d suggest cutting back by at least 1/4 tsp. My top cranberry layer was very loose, it hardly set up at all (it was sliding off the slice on the plate). Maybe I didn’t portion out the cream cheese mixture well enough? It was definitely a project but none of the steps were difficult and I was happy to try it out for my son. He likes cheesecake more than I do, so he enjoyed this more than I did.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very delicious and surprisingly not that hard to make, just takes a good bit of time. My one critique is that the cranberry layer tastes more orangey to me, I barely pick up the cranberry flavor. I may try with water next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ive made this twice now and confirm it is well worth the effort. Everyone loves it. I make it with mascarpone instead of cream cheese. By planning it out I managed to use fewer bowls the second time I made it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nice idea, but Waaay too sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rather than making cranberry sauce from scratch, I think opening a can of good ol' jellied cranberry sauce works just as well -- and you don't have to go to the trouble of straining it, so you can omit steps 3 and 4. Beat one cup of the canned cranberry sauce with the electric mixer until smooth, and then proceed with step 6.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this at Christmas last year and it was stupid good.  The biscoff crust was lovely and the mix of tart and salty/sweet in the fillings was just right.  It was not difficult to put together, just plan enough time for all the steps.  It was all thumbs up from my daughter who had asked me to make it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make this every xmas to give out to friends and neighbors as gifts.  As soon as I pour the toffee from the pan, I sprinkle milk chocolate chips on top which melt into a smooth layer, then sprinkle toasted chopped pecans on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Over 60 years ago when I was growing up in England, my mother used to make toffee and she did not have a sugar boiling thermometer. Instead, she tested a little of the toffee by putting a drop of it into a glass of cold water. Toffee goes through different stages which (if I remember correctly) were called something like "soft", or "hard crack". You might mention this for those who would like to try making toffee but don't have the thermometer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Juliet
soft ball stage for fudge 
hard ball stage for divinity 
soft crack for nougat 
hard crack for toffee 

My grandma made a loooooot of candy :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Juliet this is much more fun and reliable than a thermometer.  I have gotten a candy habit and use the thermometer to watch progress but cold water for the finish.  Over time it’s fun to learn that you can even hear the change between stages.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make the best toffee. Most recipes have white sugar so I am glad to see brown in this one. Really, I do make the best. 2 cups salted butter, 2 cups brown sugar, cook to 296 degrees. Pour over pecans on a cooke sheet (whole or chopped), spread out, put 12oz Hersheys milk chocolate bars (yes, Hersheys) on top, melt and spread, chill. Best ever. Use good butter and do not use cheap brown sugar - pure cane is the only way. People beg me for it. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Rhonda Koetting do you line with parchment  or butter the baking sheet first? Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I haven't made this wonderful-looking recipe yet, but as someone who has to accommodate nut allergies in my household, and with most commercially-available toffees like Heath contain almonds, I wanted to express gratitude - this should be a great back pocket recipe for many toffee-containing bakes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dangerously easy to make for something so delicious (and I’m saying that as someone who had never made toffee before this). Next step: baking them into my first batch of salted butterscotch chocolate chunk cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a great recipe for a novice candy-maker like myself. I plan on adding chunks of it to chocolate chip cookies. Thankfully, even through it looks impossible, the saucepan cleans very well with soap and warm water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For me, 290 degrees resulted in burned toffee.  Could be my thermometer, but I will stop at 275 next time…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Heidi agree. Mine went from 278F to 293F in a blink and was smoking as I poured it out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same result and my thermometer is extremely accurate. 275 seems like a better stopping point.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Heidi I always calibrate my thermometer every season before making candy. Put the thermometer in a sauce pan with a 4-5 inches of water making sure the thermometer doesn’t touch the pan. Bring the water to a rolling boil - water boils at 212degrees  if your thermometer is off, make an adjustment. Before I learn this trick, I overcooked many batches of fudge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've heard that using salted vs unsalted butter helps to keep the oil in the butter from separating, which I have had happen. Does it really?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: David my experience is that can happen if you start with too high a heat. They say a medium-high heat but try it around medium.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is good for toffee purists but saltine toffee bark is even better- line the pan with saltines, pour toffee on, bake it a bit, sprinkle chocolate chips on to melt a little and then gently spread. Addicting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Family members are vegan. I wonder how this would work out with vegan butter? Would make a great festive holiday gifting addition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question for Juliet -- I don't have a candy thermometer. Would this be hard crack or soft? My grandmother (another Juliet) used to make fudge and it was to a soft crack.  Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have been making toffee with brown sugar for years and it has the best flavor. It works best when I used C&H brown sugar for some reason. Also, my recipe calls 8 T butter (one cube) and one cup of brown sugar (no water). Cook the same way to “hard crack” on your candy thermometer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MCH C & H sugar is always pure sugarcane derived, and the behavior is different from less expensive, sugarbeet based sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this toffee at 2am because I needed a distraction from something and it was so incredibly easy and absolutely delicious! I’m sure it’s wonderful in those cookies, but I’ve just been snacking on the pieces from a ziplock bag.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Easy and delicious. The dark brown sugar adds a wonderful depth. I covered half with some chocolate chips while hot and spread them over top.
The shards are great with a handful of salted nuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Also agree with the other commenter that 290 results in overdone toffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Cat 290° resulted in perfect toffee for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Cat me as well

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Suggestions wanted:  quantity of pecans that could be added to this recipe. 
Also would you toast whatever nut you choose with a bit of salt for more flavor?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes to toasting the nuts! Based on the quantum of the other ingredients, I’d start with 1/3 - 1/2 cup of nuts, roughly chopped. Then adjust up or down based on your preference of the nut-to-toffee ratio. Based on the peanut brittle recipe I follow, one possibly helpful tip is to toast your nuts on a parchment lined rimmed baking sheet while the toffee cooks, then pour the syrup over top of the warm nuts to give yourself more working time to spread the syrup/nuts evenly on the baking sheet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This makes a very dark brown toffee; it almost looked burnt, though it did not taste burnt. I looked at other recipes on the web and many of them called for white sugar, or light brown. Just be aware that if you use dark brown sugar you will get a very dark result.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make toffee every year using Naz Devarian’s recipe available on this platform and it comes out perfect every time. I tired this recipe here and it was completely burnt. I’m not sure if it was the dark brown sugar but I had to toss it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Anthony Odd, because Devarian’s recipe calls for cooking to a higher temperature than Vreeland’s.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made toffee for years and this was so dark and burned tasting. My thermometer gets a workout at this time of year, and seems to work well for everything else. I made a second batch and cooked to barely 275 and it still had a strong taste, so I didn't use it in the cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love toffee. But this scorched sugar version is not the toffee I love.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn Vreeland's demonstration in the video to the Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies recipe is VERY helpful, describing and illustrating his technique for getting this just right, right down to how he spreads it for cooling and later cuts it. Especially useful if one is going to use the toffee in the cookie recipe. See https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yxBiP2f6nNc

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Top with milk chocolate candy bars while still hot, then spread to cover when they melt. That’s how my mother made ersatz “Heath” candy at home.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made this toffee 6 times since this recipe came out and I’ve followed the instructions exactly as written and it has never come out burnt. It’s just rich and delicious. Not sure what others are doing wrong.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What would you change if you are at high altitude (7600 ft)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How about spreading chocolate over the top? Any suggestions on how to from you candy makers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @AC Wisner Sprinkle chopped chocolate on top of the hot toffee, wait for a minute and then spread with a knife. Then you can sprinkle chopped nuts on top of the melted chocolate if you wish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Use organic dark brown sugar and good butter, but try a ristretto shot instead of water!

I heat to 305 for crack candy temp and add chopped pecans or almonds just before spreading to cool. Top with an organic 80%+ chocolate - I use chocolate bar chunks and spread it over the toffee while it is still hot enough to melt the chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t usually make candy: is altitude something that needs to be considered when making this? If so, what adjustments need to be made?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I asked this question yesterday and have since done some checking around.  I live at 7500 ft and just finished the boiling water test.  My water boiled at 199 degrees (sea level, I understand is 212).  So I need to deduct 13 degrees (difference between sea level and my boiling point) from the suggested temp of 290.  Altitude will very definitely affect candy making.  That being said, I haven't made the toffee yet so we shall see!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Salted butter has the added benefit of acting as a stabilizer, preventing the butter from breaking. In my experience, it works better than simply adding salt to unsalted butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mom always said you need a dry, not humid, day to have success with toffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Patrice True, fudge, toffee really any candy you try to make will not set if the humidity is too high. I’m not sure why, but it is something I learned from my mom. She was great in the kitchen. I miss her.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this a few times now, love it. For a saltier flavour to offset the sweetness throw a couple of pinches of kosher salt on top just after you pour it out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Heated to ~278 degrees.
Perfect.
Definitely use salted butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wear shoes while doing this. I still have a scar on my left foot from spilling molten sugar on my teen age bare foot some 70 years ago.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: why such a large baking sheet? I guess it doesn't really matter, but a smaller one would have worked just as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Candy is not my go-to sweet treat. I am a cookie, cake pie fan.
But, I made this toffee and I gotta tell you, it is so good!
The stuff is addictive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I inherited a toffee recipe that came from a friend of my grandmother. No candy thermometer needed. The instructions say to stir until the mixture is the color of oak floors. Those instructions sound vague, but it works every time. After many years of trial and error, I found the best pan is my cast iron Dutch oven. Also, no reason to cut the toffee. Just slam the pan against the counter and the toffee breaks into perfect pieces. Sometimes old recipes are the best.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this using light brown sugar and vegan butter. My thermomador only goes to 220 so I used the hard crack method. I tested it right after the bubbles subsided. Poured 3/4 into a prepared pan, the rest I spread on a couple of rice cakes. Yum! My new go to guilty unhealthy snack pleasure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any advice how to best clean the heavy bottom saucepan after boiling the butter and sugar? That’s the only intimidation factor holding me back from making this right now.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @BA Englishteach - it cleans up with soap and hot water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @BA Englishteach Seconding what Arlington says. One of the wonderful things about working with sugar is that it dissolves, even when the mess looks impossible!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thanks, Elise!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s impossible to eat as it sticks horribly to our teeth. What did I do wrong?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If it's sticky, it's undercooked. The sugar needs to reach the hard crack stage.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this tonight to prep for the cookies. My thermometer said 293 but it is granulated like bad fudge. Gonna try again tomorrow but idk if I over did it or under did it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Cindy Anderson You have to use pure cane sugar, such as C & H or Domino. Lower grade sugar is often beet sugar and it becomes granulated.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Novice here. Is it supposed to fill the whole pan?   I barely covered half the pan and it was thin. Fine for the cookie recipe I will use it in but wondering how thick it should be.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really proud of myself for making this without a thermometer! Did the water trick and it worked beautifully. This stuff is delicious and the crack when you chop it is so satisfying.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Just made this toffee to add to Vaughan Vreeland’s new cookie, but it’s now been 40 minutes and it is still soft.  The directions stated 6-8 mins or 145c!
Comments please

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'd guess it is undercooked, still in the softball stage. I don't think you can fix it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is sooo good and sooo easy! I will never go back.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mother made toffee every year at Christmas and I inherited the job. Ours adds instant coffee granules. I made it for so many years I could do it with my eyes closed. Then I bought new (very nice) pans. Ever since, I have not been able to not have the butter separate when I pour mixture onto the cookie sheet. I have tried new thermometers, different butters, and finally got tired of throwing away a lot of butter, sugar and walnuts. I've attempted every trick I've googled -

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Non-stick coating in pans will cause separation of butter and sugar, every single time. You can only make candy in a pan that isn't teflon or otherwise non-stick coated.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made a batch in a nonstick pan. So far so good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy and quick, but also easy to overcook, even with a candy thermometer. I learned the hard way making maple syrup years ago – boiling at very high heat results in a darker end product – but forgot today. It started smoking at about 270°; I turned the heat down and got it out fast at 290°. It got there fast and was going higher. Result: crispy hard with a deep dark sugar flavor, nearly too dark. I'll use a medium saucepan next time and lower heat to get it more slowly to finished temperature.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where's the step for the chocolate topping?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is not chocolate-covered toffee. That is a different recipe. This is plain toffee to mix into chocolate chip cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wondering if a Le Creuset saucepan would be too "heavy" and hold the heat too well (causing burning). Anyone have success using one? My other option for a saucepan is Revere wear which doesn’t seem heavy enough…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Another NYT recipe that bombed! I’m done. And I am a good cook!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Butter and sugar are the best ingredients ever invented!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does this freeze well?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Based on comments, I checked my boiling point first. 207, so logically I would stop the toffee at 285. At 275 it already smelled burnt. I will have to try again and this time I’m going for around 265. What a waste of butter and sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can't assume that an inaccurate thermometer will be consistently inaccurate at every temperature. It's not a linear proportion. Get an accurate thermometer!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: how do you clean the pot???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @G Put some water in it and bring to a boil. That will quickly dissolve the leftover caramel and then use dish soap for the butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used jaggery instead of brown sugar and the hard crack test. This came together really quickly. Tastes good too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sheet pan seemed way too big.  My toffee only covered the center.  Otherwise delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The ratio for toffee is usually equal amount of butter and sugar. (ie one cup of butter and one cup of sugar). Could the complaints over the recipe being 'too burnt' be because this recipe calls for almost half the amount of butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought it had a harsh taste.  Won’t make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1 lb butter, 1 cup brown sugar….rolling boil 3 minutes, no thermometer needed…I’ve done it many times

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sorry I meant 1 cup butter 1 cup brown sugar… rolling boil 3 minutes, no thermometer needed…I’ve done it many times

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was soooo good and I was very proud of my accomplishment. I didn’t have a thermometer that went past 220, so I followed other comments to use the hard crack method. I added chopped chocolate and pistachios to the top (and was surprised how easily it melted). Will definitely be making this every year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hmmmmm.  I've been making toffee with white sugar and butter cooked to 310 degrees.  While I haven't tried this recipe, I doubt that it has the same flavor as sugar on the brink of being burnt.  I will have to experiment.......

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I confess to being confused by "large saucepan"  Are we talking 8 inch, or closer to 12 inch?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 8-inch! 12 inches will be too big and the layer of liquid will be too thin to cook evenly and way too thin to get an accurate thermometer reading.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Altitude can have a huge impact on candy making. For calibrating a thermometer or adjusting the candy target temperature:

Reduce the temp by 1°F for every 500 ft of elevation. 

I'm at ~5500, so I calibrate to boiling = 201°F, and reduce the temperature of any candy making recipe by 11°F.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: it's delicious. My first time making candy and burned it just little bit - still tasty. Unfortunately, however, I broke a tooth eating it. My dentist advised freezing it before eating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made three different toffee recipes from the NYT and had a family taste test.  This was by far the least favorite, primarily because of the texture from heating the toffee to only 290 degrees.  It was too soft and didn't have the satisfying crunch of toffee that was cooked longer.  Also, we weren't fans of using brown sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: David, the Spruce Eats has a page that answers this question. https://www.thespruceeats.com/why-did-my-butter-separate-when-making-toffee-or-caramel-520448 I use unsalted butter for everything, and have found that adding a couple of (3 fingers) pinch of salt helps keep the toffee stable so it does not foam so much. Hope this helps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! Very fun to make. Probably a good idea to set a timer after you take the toffee out of the oven—I let mine set up on the pan far too long and they got a lil burned. Still amazing tho!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody:
I never wrote before. ( I had a stroke and it takes me time.) 
However, I had to tell everyone that this is a Dynamite recipe! I've been cooking for 65years, thanks to my great grandmother, "Pearl". 
You really hit the nail right out the ballpark with this recipe.  
I didn't have any milk chocolate, so I sub with white chocolate.  Normally, I wouldn't make toffee tell the holidays, but I'm very glad I did.
This takes a little bit of time but all good things do!!! 
"THANKS VV"

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Rusty Nichols I love this, thank you for posting your review. Happy baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Incredible! I substituted King Arthur GF flour, but aside from that made it to recipe. Great flavor and texture. Love how the recipe includes simple ways to elevate a chocolate chip cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Sam did the GF flour work out well? It didn’t change the texture or anything?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: These are outstanding! I followed the recipe exactly, including the homemade toffee. My dough was a little loose when mixed, so I put the bowl in the fridge for 15 minutes and then it was perfect for scooping. I recommend rolling the dough in your hands after scooping or using the mold ring after baking to shape the cookies (see video), as they spread out a bit wonky otherwise. Mine rested for about 10 hrs. in fridge before baking. They are incredible!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: An easier way to add salt to compensate for unsalted butter in this kind of recipe is mix it directly to the butter when you melt it, since the water in the butter will dissolve the salt easily and guarantee even distribution in the dough. Additionally, since you're dissolving this salt, there's no need to use a fussy kosher variety - just add half as much (by volume) table salt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Barry from Baltimore I wish I would have done this. I didn’t have any salted butter on hand (it’s so rare for recipes to call for salted butter) and I completely forgot to add the salt to the flour mixture. It would be helpful to have that option included in the steps rather than a tip at the bottom.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Is there some reason that the dough could not be rested in the bowl in the fridge instead being scooped onto a baking sheet and wrapped in so much plastic wrap? It would be much easier to find room in the fridge for a bowl and it would cut down on the amount of plastic waste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @ron It’s far easier to scoop the dough prior to chilling. Once cold, it’s like a rock.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @ron I have broken two really nice cookie scoops (apparently took more than once to learn) scooping cold dough. Learn from my mistakes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @ron- I placed the dough on a sheet of parchment paper and created a log, placed in the refrigerator and easily cut them when I wanted to bake the cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I have never commented on a NYT recipe before, even though I have probably made hundreds of them. The number of rave reviews I received when I made these cookies compelled me to share. I used homemade toffee (yes, it really is easy to make) and followed the recipe (except I used 12oz. dark chocolate, instead of a combination of milk and dark because that’s all I had). Comments included: “Best cookie ever,” “Every bite was magic,” and “She needs to go into baking business.”

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Make The Toffee!  It’s sublime!  Made the cookie dough yesterday and baked them today.  They are unbelievable delicious.  I made them smaller and they did spread.  I think they needed longer in the fridge; next time, because there will definitely be a next time, I’ll leave them in for at least 24 hours. Again, Make The Toffee- you won’t be sorry!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Leslie I made the toffee. Not only was it delicious, but it took me less time than a trip to the store!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody:
I’m almost 79 years old. My earliest memories are helping my mamaw cook on the coal stove (we lived in Appalachia and my daddy was a coal miner). I’ve cooked for my entire life. I’ve always made candies and cookies to give as gifts at Christmas time. I’ll finished up baking tomorrow and deliver Saturday. Solo, I’ve made about 1500 hundred cookies this year, and a couple of pans of peanut butter fudge.  I hope to make peanut butter roll tomorrow

I tell you this to let you know that I’m not a novice. These cookies are top of the heap. They are delicious.  Next year I may make only this one variety!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: So, so, so good. Didn't change a thing. Don't rush this one; treat it like the project it is and IT WILL PAY OFF. Make the toffee (the store-bought "chunks" are too small), save a little toffee and chocolate to push onto the top of each cookie before chilling, refrigerate all day or over night. As always, good things come to those who wait. In this case, INCREDIBLE things are coming! Brought these to family dinner and my 75-yr-old dad has texted twice about how much he loves them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Lindsi AGREE— I will make the toffee next time, but even with the Heath “bits” (mostly crumbs and dust really) these were still quite delish!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: “Tightly wrap the baking sheet”? With what?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Jim: Saran Wrap? Glade Press ‘n Seal wrap? Wax paper? I believe the intent is so the dough doesn’t dry out too much while in the refrigerator.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I think I might just roll the dough into a log, wrap, chill and then slice & bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Jim - I rolled in wax paper - and then took off wax paper and sliced them.  Worked well.  Much smaller cookies than what recipe calls for - but that is how we like them.  Great recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: These cookies sound spectacular,  but they don't have nuts.  We insist on pecans or walnuts in our chocolate chip cookies, so I will add nuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Dotty toasted nuts are the trick. I used two cups of lightly toasted chopped walnuts - incredible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Although I did not make my own toffee (thank you Heath Bar toffee bits), I followed the recipe to the letter. I weighed each portion of dough. (Much easier than using a 1/4 cup solid measure).The cookies came out perfectly. These cookies are better than Levain, Big Fat Cookie, Chip City or Crumble cookies. Vaughn hit this one out of the park.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Holy moly you guys. Really really good. Highly recommend watching the video first — there’s some excellent level up advice in there. Also, these cookies taste even better when they’ve cooled a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Can I make them smaller and if so, how much time would they take?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Annie I made them 65 g since they were for boyfriend and his son and I didn’t want huge cookies for a child. They cooked for about 14 - 15 minutes and ended up delicious, but looking more rounded and soft.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Abby I made mine 35 grams each. Then I ate three of them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: A cautionary tale: do not add the vanilla to the hot butter mixture until it has cooled considerably, or you’ll risk an explosive boil that sends it overflowing and across your kitchen counter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Diane I had the same experience with the ice cubes, very messy. I won’t make that mistake again😳

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Diane I’ll vouch for this!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: This is super similar to one of the best cookie recipes of ALL TIME- Rick Martinez' Brown Butter Chocolate Chip Cookies with Toffee, for Bon Appetit. I believe a BA reader sent him the recipe and is credited. it's a bit simpler than this recipe - no need to use ice water, and the dough only needs to rest for a half hour; which is crazy! Not to divert attention away from VV/NYT, but I highly recommend them if you enjoy this sort of thing but want them faster.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Kau Agree with this! The BA version has been my go to for the past several years. I just made this dough and it’s currently in its long chill, but I’ll be interested to see if the extra effort with this recipe is worth it. I have a hunch the homemade toffee will make a difference, but that could always be transported over to the BA recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Kau the BA one has been my go to as well but it uses only half the flour as this recipe. I’m currently chilling my dough and it seems very dry compared to the BA one so I’m worried how these will turn out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I would love to try this recipe. Just wondering what changes I will need to make to turn this into a cookie bar, instead? Will be easier to bring to parties during the Holiday Season. Thanks

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I made these gluten-free and they were so over-the-top good. Nobody noticed, or cared that they were gluten-free, there is so much deliciousness going on here. I made the toffee bits per the attached recipe and added some of the bits and the chocolate to the tops of each dough ball which makes them look so yummy. I love the salt and sweet and the crunch. I baked only six. The rest go into the freezer for emergencies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody:
Two comments: Let the hot butter mixture cool in the heat proof measuring cup at least five minutes before adding the vanilla, or you will have a volcano of exploding butter and vanilla, from the combustion of the alcohol in the vanilla reacting to the hot butter.
And forming 100 g balls of cookie dough - seriously? That's about the size of a small tennis ball - why would you ever want a cookie that big??? I formed 50g balls and they made cookies that were 12-15 cm across (5 - 6"). Big enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I followed the directions to put the brown butter into a heat-proof container, add an ice cube and then the vanilla. Did anyone else experience a bit of an explosion, my butter turned into a geyser with butter spurting up and then landing on the counter and dripping down the cabinets then onto the floor?  Would have been nice to have a warning, or be told to cool the the butter adding the vanilla.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Amy - yes, but not quite a geyser! I poured my brown butter into a 4-cup measuring cup, followed by the ice (2 crescents), and then the vanilla. Resulted in a reaction, but no spillage.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: This happened to me the second time I made the recipe.  After adding ice cubes to the hot browned butter, I added vanilla and the mixture instantly caused a small explosion.  I believe this has to do with the alcohol content of vanilla extract, and the fact that alcohol has a much lower boiling point than the temperature of the butter.  I ended up just melting a bit of butter to make up for the lost butter, and the cookies still ended up great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Amy, @Hatch and @Jasmine – in the video, VV adds the ice cubes to the saucepan in which he is browning the butter. No chance they won't melt there, and if added to the butter when slightly cooled, there shouldn't be a geyser or an explosion. See https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yxBiP2f6nNc

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I agree with a previous comment by Lisa saying that this recipe is not the same as Bon Appetit's Brown Butter and Toffee Chocolate Chip Cookie. I used baker's percentage to compare the two recipes,  which puts the recipes on a level playing field by comparing the weight of each ingredient to the weight of the flour in the recipe. BA's looks to be sweeter, have more butter & chocolate, less toffee. Having a list of ingredients that is virtually the same does not make them the same cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody:
Except for portion size, made as written. Notes below to help others:
- Made each cookie approximately 50g, which is about a 2" cookie ball. Roughly made using a 2tbsp measuring spoon and then weighed on scale for similar baking result.
- Kept cookie balls in fridge for 6 hours and baked in batches directly into oven. Cookies spread about an inch outward on all sides. Recommend 2-3" spacing between cookies. Do Not Try to cook more than about 6-9 at a time unless you like living dangerously.
- First batch I cooked in oven at 350° for 18m, which I found too crispy. 2nd batch I did 325° for 18m, which I found better for my taste and given I made smaller cookie sizes. My oven temp may vary from yours.
- I took another baker's recommendation to test my candy thermometer with boiling water first (thanks for the tip). Surprised to find that my water boiled at 200°F. I still overcooked the toffee a bit (oops!) Probably ended up closer to 300 at end, which was too dark and will need to pull sooner next time.
- Didn't have round cookie cutters for shaping out of the oven. I used a glass tupperware bowl by just turning it upside down for same effect.
- The cookie is tasty and better cooled, IMO. Will likely tone down the sugar and chocolate a bit next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: These were so good that I teared up. Make the homemade toffee - it's so delicious and also makes you feel like a strong and powerful baker. Thank you Vaughn for this amazing recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Maybe I’m the only one who didn’t know this, but wait for the butter to cool a bit before adding the vanilla. I did not and ended up with a butter volcano on my counter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Hi, can we freeze the premade dough and then bake later?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
