author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I enjoyed these cookies a great deal as did many folks who I gave them to. Next time I might try to leave the toffee chunks, a little bit larger as well as the chocolate chunks to give more contrast to the flavors and to let the toffee stand out a bit more. I also did not save any of the chocolate or toffee to put on top of the cookies which I think I will do next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: The homemade toffee is delicious. But I felt it was lost in this cookie. While good, I did not find these cookies to be phenomenal as others apparently did. Honestly they reminded me of Chips Ahoy. Maybe I over-baked them…?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @Marie David I also believe the baking time to be too long.  From frozen I found 19 minutes was just right for my preferences of a crisp edge but soft center

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Just an FYI... the toffee recipe makes exactly enough for this recipe... in case you are wanting some for snacking... ;)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @banana57-thanks for the heads up! Much appreciated.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Do yourself a favor and make these!! I highly suggest making your own toffee… not as hard as it might seem and the flavor is amazing!! Don’t change anything about the recipe, it’s perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I must have done something wrong. The dough was *extremely* dry and after cooking the cookie didn’t spread like the ones in the photo and after cooling are very dry and hard. It seems like the eggs and vanilla just isn’t enough liquid for that much sugar and flour. I followed the recipe exactly, do don’t know where it went wrong.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @T Swan the vanilla wouldn’t add much moisture. Surprising bc it’s quite a wet dough. Did you use two eggs? And 2.5 sticks of butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @T Swan I had same experience.  The flavor was OK  They didn't spread much and were rock hard. I followed the instructions too

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: This is a very good cookie, but, when the toffee melts, it can cause the cookies to spread out in all directions, like a cookie that might be spotted in Dali's melty painting, "The Persistence of Memory." This is easily fixable by swirling the cookies, fresh out of the oven, in a round cookie cutter or ring mold. The recipe should really include a note about this, because bakers who don't know this trick may be crying over what looks like an absolute cookie disaster.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody:
This is a great recipe but I think made better with some modifications. I used all dark chocolate-Ghirardelli makes both a 60% and 72% dark chocolate chip, and I combined the two. I also used salted butter throughout, including in the toffee, and added 3/4 teaspoon salt in with the flour. 

Best of all I used my dough scoop and made 48 cookies and froze them on the freezer on parchment paper on a cookie sheet. You can the bake them in an hour or so. I bake them at 350 convection for about 9 minutes. Friends and family have said they are the best cookies they’ve ever had!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Sorry but I don’t understand the direction to “move the baking sheets between the racks”?Does that mean moving the sheet on the upper rack to the lower one and vice versa? Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: RE the brown butter: For more "toasty bits" add 2 TBS whole milk powder to the melting butter to really amp up the butterscotch flavor. Also for this specific recipe, the additional 2 TBS bring the liquid total up to 1-1/4 C (= 10 ounces) without having to add ice cubes or water. Lacking a right-sized heatproof measuring cup, I pour the melted butter into a 1-1/2 C wide-mouth Mason jar; without the toffee bits, this is the perfect chocolate chip cookie recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody:
Fantastic cookie. I did the home made toffee for this and wow, the flavors of this are so intensely deep and buttery!! 

Make this everyone! It’s so good

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I’m concerned about the toffee sticking in my teeth. Was that a problem for anyone?  I might just use extra chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @rk I was worried about this too, but the toffee pretty much melts/dissolves into the cookie to give you a nice pocket of flavor. It’s totally fine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Wow, this is my top chocolate chip cookie recipe, baked a bit smaller - 14 mins, golden, rich, chocolatety, carmelly happiness! Team warm and gooey cookies too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: For anyone wondering, the cookies are delicious even if they only sit in the frig while the oven preheats!! Not that I would ever do that, but just saying…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: 1st batch: I followed the directions as written and found them too sweet. Made 2nd batch with 12 oz. dark chocolate only (no milk chocolate) and liked them much better. Finishing with flaky seas salt is a must. With dark chocolate only, they pair extremely well with dulce de leche ice cream. Next batch I'll reduce chocolate to 10 oz. to decrease the chocolate/cookie ratio.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: This recipe is SOO good. But! I will say that it does call for a lot of toffee, so make a little bit less than the recipe calls for, or just make the amount it says and save some on the side for eating. On the chocolate front, I would recommend using only dark chocolate, but this is just a preference. These cookies are very sweet and it does have a lot going on, but they taste great and look even better. I made these for a bake sale and they were the first thing to sell out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Can anyone direct me to the “cooking the commons” video Vaughn was referring to?  Sounded like a great breakdown of how different ingredients and methods impact cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: I chilled half the batch and froze the rest to make later. My chilled batch were very good but a bit bready vs chewy. But I cooked the frozen batch a month later and they were perfection consistency-wise. Any reason that would be the case?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Love these cookies. I haven’t made them with toffee yet, but I’ve used Crispy Crunch candy bars and whoppers (separately) and both were great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: Excellent cookie. Made the toffee as recommended and would do that again. Only adjustment was I made them smaller - 53-55 grams each scoop and reduced baking time to 14 minutes. For me the perfect size. And, using a ring to create a more perfect circle is also a nice touch. Video is a good reference.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: @ron That's what I do.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody:
I made these yesterday. I've made quite a few variations of chocolate chip cookies over the years, including many with browned butter. This one is really fantastic. 
I made the toffee from the linked recipe. It's quite easy. 
A few notes: I made the cookies smaller.  I have a 3 tablespoon scoop (which really is quite large), and I used that. I think i got 26 cookies total. The cookies spread a bit because of the toffee. But they clean up nicely if you use the ring trick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: These cookies are INCREDIBLE! And if you forget the toffee bits (like I have), salted caramel chocolate chips work beautifully! Make these ASAP!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Salted Butterscotch Chocolate Chunk Cookies Recipe (with Video)
commentBody: These cookies are insane. I bake a lot and our friends have proclaimed these the best baked good I’ve ever made. Double the batch and stick some cookie balls in the freezer for later because they will go FAST!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a new fall favorite!  Made exactly per recipe.  The smell of fall was so intoxicating, and that (Canadian, no bourbon here) whiskey didn’t hurt, so aromatic.  I would layer apples with batter next time in thirds all the way through, rather than 1/3 & 2/3.  I think I’d prefer pecans on top over almonds, but almonds were good.  Showstopper!  It didn’t take me as long as others, but the cook time was closer to 95 minutes.  I used Cosmic Crisp apples.  They’re excellent bakers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn, you are a mind reader! I just saw an announcement for your new Bake Time digital newsletter that is launching on Saturday (9/27/25).The announcement mentioned that the first issue will include a video of you baking this amazing cake. That’s exactly what I was about to ask for. Thank you!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JJ    Glad to hear it. I am totally incapable of understanding, from the written instructions, how the apples get cut so they look anything like those in the photo. This is clearly a recipe that needs video.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Alicia ask and you shall receive.  The video is up and I accept the monthly challenge

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Alicia: I wonder if you could use a heavy duty stainless steel hard boiled egg slicer, which I also use for strawberries and mushrooms.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So many comments but no one seems to have actually made the recipe! I did today, and I am an exit was a project: 1.5 hours to assemble (not the 30 minutes indicated) and 40 minutes extra cooking time to ensure the apples were cooked through.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Donna C. I made it too. I used a mandolin and left the skin on the apples. It took a lot longer to bake than the recipe said, but it was perfect. The almonds and crunchy sugar almost gilded the lily. I will be making again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Donna C.also took me 90 min prep:  recipe only accounts for using 2/3 of the apples.  I did an extra layer to use up the apples and ran out of batter.  It’s in the oven.  It better taste good.  

Also never heard of checking apples and google was not helpful , and I have never cooked a cake til the top was “burnished”.  It boasts about simple ingredients, how about simple language.?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Reading instructions left a question.  Instructions say “ Add two-thirds of the apples, flat sides down, in a single layer (some overlap is fine). Pour in the rest of the batter and smooth into an even layer.”  It never goes on to explain what one does with remaining third of apples.  However demonstration on video shows him arranging ALL of the apples between 1st and 2nd layers of batter (not just 2/3!) It is the saved crowns which are placed on top of final layer of batter.  Strongly urge written instructions be corrected so as not to confuse those who only read recipe without watching video.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Adele Read step 6

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree!  I don't want to watch a video in the middle of baking.  Instructions should be clearly written.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Betsy L I will watch the video to see what they say about the 5 reserved segments that were set aside for decorating the top. Step six makes it sound like the top is decorated with the remaining 1/3 of the apples. This confused me too—only because I assumed the 5 segments (which I took to mean 5 thin slices) were what was to go on top. Maybe the video will clarify.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made the cake this afternoon following the recipe exactly and it is stunning, I wish we could add the picture. Definitely a keeper, delicious and moist. 
My 2 cents is that the batter was not really easy to work with: it looked perfect, but as soon as I stopped the stand mixer it seemed to congeal and was hard to spread. I was afraid I wouldn't have enough batter or I did something wrong. In the end it all worked perfectly though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi, Vaughn here! I would love to see how it turned out! If you'd like, send the photo to baketime@nytimes.com. Thanks for baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you think I could make this gluten free? It sounds delish!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Abby Schwarzwalder I love using Better Batter gf flour for these types of recipes and it turns out really nice. Thats what I’ll be using this weekend when I make this! I also use this gf flour for their butter swim biscuit recipe and it turns out great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Abby Schwarzwalder I usually use Bob’s Red Mill 1-1 Flour (the one in the blue bag, not the red—th red has chickpea flour which you don’t want to taste in sweeter bakes unless it’s called for.) 99.9% of the time it works perfectly! Just check for doneness diligently—I’ve found sometimes when I swap recipes to make them GF, which is always as I have celiac, they often need a few extra minutes. Happy baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @SL update! I made it with the Bobs red mill1-1 gluten free flour and omitted the cornstarch as gluten free flour can be “thirstier” and absorb more liquid. It turned out perfectly! I do think the apple instructions are weird though. I figured it out and it came out looking exactly like the picture!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this last night with a glut of apples from my CSA.  I added a tsp of cinnamon and cardamom to the batter and it was delicious.  I would add more of each next time to make them stand out more. It was definitely a whole big project. The careful apple cutting and butter browning are each long steps.  But the end result was impressive and pretty, while still accessible. Nice to bring to a gathering.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made it yesterday for a dinner party exactly as written (one small change below). It came out great and everyone commented how good it was. I did not have a 9" springform and used my 8". It did take about 15 minutes more to cook but think that was due to change in pan size. This also looks great sitting on the platter. Everyone noticed it and most said if they saw it in a shop they would buy it.  Lots of ingredients to gather. But once those were all on the counter it seemed to go together quickly. Will definitely make again.

Someone in the household drank all the bourbon so I had to look for a substitute and found one, in the back of the alcohol stash: an old bottle of calvados. Cake was 10 out of 10 with calvados. author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am going to make this cake tomorrow and serve it Wednesday. I so appreciated the comments about prep time for this cake. I am a really long time baker and by now when I look at prep times given in the beginning of a recipe, I look around my kitchen for an assistant to help me and finding no one else to help, I ignore the time written in the recipe and proceed to prep the ingredients I will need for whatever I am cooking or baking and I just enjoy the process. How fortunate we all are to be able to cook and/or bake from the bounty of  the ingredients available to so many of us.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Andrea Kern so true —we are incredibly lucky . Thanks for sharing the positive side.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Watched the entire video, thanks Vaughn. Excited to make this!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
ok guys - here's the modifications to make this cake amazing, and sort some of your issues:

1. half the recipe, using 2 small eggs and add 2 TBS of sour cream or yoghurt to the batter. check after 45 minutes baking time for doneness. 

2. parcook your apple slices, microwaving, until just soft (1-2 minutes)

3. use a 8-inch parchment lined springform pan.

4. try Fireball Whisky for the booze (goes incredible with apples).

5. go hard on the crunch, the more the better, try adding in a combo of flaked almonds with the slivered, if you can get it - large demerara sugar crystals (called "coffee crystals" in Australia).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this yesterday and as other commenters have noted, the prep took much longer than indicated. This is a project cake - multiple bowls, lots of components, and an array of ingredients. Mine needed an additional 20 minutes of bake time, so definitely use your bakers intuition and a cake tester to judge when it’s done. The batter seemed super scant to me and I was nervous  while putting it in the pan with the apples, but the finished result yielded an excellent apple to cake consistency. I will say the cake was delicious and the bourbon was a lovely favor. I’ll make this again for a fancy fall occasion, but for an every day apple cake I’ll probably stick with Alison Roman’s sticky apple cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe almost sent me to the ER! It calls for melting the butter until ‘toasty dark brown flecks appear” then remove from heat and carefully pour 1/4 of bourbon in. That’s when it exploded! All over me, all over the kitchen, stove, counter tops, and floor became an ice skating rink. I spent the next hour cleaning everything up and making the floor safe enough to walk on. I’ve got burns on my skin The recipe should have instructed cooling the butter down and provided a temp it should be before added the bourbon. After all that was done I proceeded to make the batter. Followed the instructions to the tee and wound up with a stiff, hard lump that I was then supposed to divide and spread into the pan. I was forced to improvised and add milk to it to make it pliable enough to work with. It’s cooking in the oven and I have no idea what the results will be, but right now I’m just plain angry.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Laurene Gilbert omg! I made this cake and screamed when I added the bourbon but luckily i used a pot with high sides and it didn’t go everywhere but really came close!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could I make this using a Bundt pan?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My apple-to-batter ratio was not working and I ended up not using at least a cup of sliced apples. Anyone else have this problem?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Laurie I had the same issue Laurie. A single layer of apples left me with a lot left over. Didn’t want to keep adding apple as I thought they wouldn’t cook like that. Maybe I sliced too thinly. Anyway, I  have plenty of sliced apple for my morning oatmeal 👍

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This looks great. However, I do not like the taste of cornstarch (find it bitter). Does anyone know: would it be ok to substitute cake flour for the a-p flour + cornstarch to get the same result?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Curious about this, too. Very cornstarch opposed in my baking, and this is quite a lot of it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Blergh I think cake flour would be perfect. It’s what I think of when I see cornstarch added to AP flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A crowd pleaser!! If you are wondering what to do with a rainy afternoon, make this. We used an OXO mandolin which made slicing swift and easy. We didn't veer from the recipe except we used pecans because we didn't have almonds. Watch the video for great instructions and entertainment.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great video!  Loved the extra tips for cutting the parchment paper and making browned butter.  Can’t wait to try the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these kind of cakes- any way to make them egg free? Doesn’t need to be vegan, but, what would be best type of egg substitute (so many different options from powdered Namaste, to flax or chia seed, Greek yogurt + baking soda etc! ) anyone try this yet?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this yesterday.  It’s a project that took way longer than indicated - lots of moving parts.  In addition, 6 apples were too many, I only used about 4 which was sufficient for the middle layer and for those on top.  Lastly, I’m not a bourbon drinker so I don’t know what I expected, but it left a bitter taste that overshadows everything else.  I might try the cake with something less overwhelming like Cointreau.  BTW, the cake looks beautiful when done but we just don’t want to eat it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I’ve made this cake twice and the bake is not quite right.  First time, made according to instructions and it was wet in the middle and dry in the bottom and cake was bland. 

Second time I made it, made the following changes, which helped the taste a lot:
Butter AND sugar the springform pan
Add 1tsp cinnamon, 1/2 tsp all spice, 1/2 tsp nutmeg to flour mixture
Instead of all granulated sugar, use 1/4 cup granulated sugar and 1/4 brown sugar

BUT still had the issue when the bottom was dry but middle was borderline raw.

Thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JCrimm the Chef, I have one of those “princess” stoves, so I rarely make cakes, given its complete unreliability. However, I have found that when I do use my dark metal springform, placing g it on either my insulated cookie sheet or a pair of stacked rimmed baking sheets seems to offer some protection against over baking. Saw this trick on Americas Test Kitchen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A suggestion for the recipe is that you let the cake rest in the fridge at least 24 hours after it's cooled. When I ate it out of the oven, the flavors were not incoporated well yet and the alcohol didn't seem to have a chance to meld with the underlying acid and sugar so tasted "bland". After 24 hours in the fridge this turned into the best apple-based dessert I've ever had.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mark Lock. Agree!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What a great, really helpful video! Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ok that is the best cooking video I’ve ever seen lol. Omg. Vaughn is awesome. Im now intrigued by this recipe and going to make it next week.  It’s Rosh Hashanah time so… that means it’s apple time!! May need to make it without the bourbon on top as that won’t work for the nondrinkers but I’m guessing it’ll still be amazing!! Thanks for the hilarious and informative video Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tried this last night for a dinner party with a few adjustments: left the skin on the apples (peeler has vanished) and arranged them skin side up in a sort of flower pattern that turned out beautiful—skip the peeling in my opinion! Because I’m pregnant I omitted the brandy wash after baking (boo) but the brandy-brown butter-extracts mixture smelled like heaven. I found this cake to have a tight, moist crumb, lightly sweetened. I had it in the oven for 65mins and it still came out with some gooeyness that may have been undercooking and may have been intentional? I made the mistake of trying to get it fully dried out and burnt the bottom a little bit - so my advice is to embrace the gooeyness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Will not remake, do not recommend. Prep took a looong time, baking even longer; at 65 minutes the cake was still raw in the middle. I watched Vaughn's vid so followed him precisely, except swapping cider for the booze (per Vaughn's tip). The cake itself was very dry and blah. The ONLY redeeming feature is the abundance of apples, many of which were still wonderfully crunchy and fresh-tasting. Hard pass.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I watched the video and then followed the recipe exactly as written. Served warm sans the ice cream. Absolutely delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great result. I used 6 Bosc pears that are in season and replaced the Bourbon by a good Pear Schnapps. I replace the maple syrup by golden syrup and used Country Crock Butter for dairy free diet. Baked 65mn and the last minute under the broiler. Came out perfect like, the picture. Beautiful and delicious !

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made this for book club and it disappeared, even without ice cream to go with it. There were a lot of apples in the center however they did cook through.
I used a springform pan but still lined it with parchment paper. Very easy to get the cake out intact.
When slicing the apples, I would advise putting the 5 sliced "cheeks" that you will put on top of the batter on a separate plate and drizzling those with lemon juice. That way they stay together.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This may be a foolish question but here it goes. I make vanilla bean extract using Brandy Would this be a good substitute for the bourbon or would it be ruing the cake?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Never tried it but if its truly vanilla extract, imagine pouring a quarter cup of that on a cake, I think it could overpower the apple flavor.  You must have spent weeks on making this vanilla extract for the flavor to permeate, I would save it for where vanilla extract is called for, which it is here, and just use plain bourbon as it's called for.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @LARRY  I have some vanilla extract made with bourbon that my son in law gifted to me last Christmas.  It is the same as regular vanilla and you only get a hint of the bourbon.  It would be overwhelming to pour that over the cake instead of the straight bourbon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Frank the recipe is for a quarter cup maple syrup, not vanilla

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland
