@Paige Boyer
The recipes are actually quite different, other than the fact that they both use apples and bourbon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious and gorgeous cake! The cake was not too sweet and the apples still retained their texture while the cake crumb was still tender. 

I don’t think it is a difficult cake to make, but there are a lot of components and generates a lot of dishes. Once all the ingredients were in place, it was easy to throw together. It is not a cake I would make all the time, but I would certainly make it again for special occasions. 

Changes: Subbed amaretto for bourbon because that was all I had. Didn’t have almonds, so used pearl sugar for contrasting color on top for decorative effect. Used Mutsu apples. No springform pan, so used a 9” cake pan lined with the foil sling and parchment round as described in the video. Had to let the cake cool completely in the pan before I felt confident about taking it out without breaking because it is a hefty cake. Had to cook a bit longer, closer to 90 min, for the center to completely cook thru. The additional cooking time did not dry out the cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This really is delicious. Didn’t have maple syrup so used honey instead with a 1-1 ratio and it worked perfectly! Also, I’d suggest adding in a few spices like ground nutmeg, cinnamon, allspice, and/or clove. I didn’t do so, but the cake overall was a little bit too sweet so I think the spices would’ve cut down the sweetness a bit and added to the depth of flavor.
Perhaps the overly-sweetness was brought on by my usage of honey instead of maple syrup… but either way! A little cinnamon and spice never failed to amplify the wonderful autumnal feel of an apple dessert :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great with rye in bourbon-free Canada!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this cake just as written. It was delicious and beautiful. I thought it might be too boozy, but it just barely had a bourbon fragrance and taste. The overwhelming flavor and fragrance was lemon from the zest. When I tested it with a cake tester, the tester did not come clean and I ended up baking it for closer to 75 minutes. It was possibly a little over baked because it was just slightly dry, but still golden. It never did get what I would call "burnished." It was a hit with my friends.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree with the previous comments that this cake is delicious, but the preparation takes longer than stated even for experienced bakers, more like 60 minutes.  I used calvados (had no bourbon), subbed 50 grams almond flour and 200 grams flour, and added a little cinnamon and cardamom to the dry mixture.  It took 65 minutes in my oven.  Besides being delicious, it's also pretty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn’t love it, although it was hoovered up by three generations of dessert lovers.  My first hint of a problem was that the batter consistency  wasn’t pancake like, but rather, cake like.  Did a little bit of research (after decades of baking), and found that our all purpose flour is indeed harder and more glutinous, more akin to your bread flour. In future I will do what I  do for pastry and go for the  cake & pastry flour.  Maybe I will add another egg.  Apparently, our large eggs must be min. 56 g while yours must be 63 g.  While I have sometimes adjusted in the past, I never noticed such a pronounced difference.  All of these factors must account for the long cooking time, dry tough edge and soft centre.  Too many apples might have been an issue as well.  Will tinker.  Sorry if this is boring in America.  Hopefully it helps foreigners.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As a non-american I'd ask where you are cooking! If it's in Canada I appreciate your details. If it's not, then I hope others will :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yup, Lara, over here on the Wet Coast in Vancouver

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don't love Bourbon so thinking about using Frangelico or Amaretto.  Comments?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it on Sunday and everyone loved it!  I served it with homemade whipped cream instead of ice cream.  I made a few modifications--8 inch round with parchment, 4 apples instead of 6, no alcohol at all (all I had was whiskey, beer and vodka), all apples were layered into the batter and no sugar on top.  It did take more time and bowels than I expected, but I started early, so it all worked out just fine.   My husband wants me to make another with the alcohol!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and it was a true show stopper to my holiday meal. One recommendation - weight your apples before preparing and slice to 1/8 inch. Batter was done and apples just the right texture after 50 minutes baking. I will be making this again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this cake last night and it turned out beautifully. I followed the recipe almost exactly except that it needed 25mins longer baking time. Some tips which helped me- I watched the video after reading through the recipe once. When I read through the recipe the second time to actually make the cake, I was able to understand the method for slicing the apples correctly, etc…. a little better. The batter was challenging to work with but using an icing spreader helped smooth out the layers with less hassle (like he does in the video). I used all Granny Smith apples and the flavor was bright and fresh.  I was quite liberal with the turbinado and almonds on top. Don’t skimp! I will, for sure, make this one again and I think I’ll be able to assemble with greater efficiency after having done this one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: PS I would never have considered making this without the video to guide me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is beautiful and delicious. I don’t bake often so the video instructions ensured I didn’t mess up the butter step. I used 6 apples (mix of Granny Smith and honey crisp) that were already rather soft to make three flat layers of apples. I used all the apples. I was worried I wouldn’t have enough batter for the final layer but it’s sticky so I was able to stretch it across the 9 inch springform. It needed about 15 minutes more cooking than recommended. I cut the final bourbon pour to two tablespoons, which ensured that flavor didn’t overwhelm the cake. I will absolutely make this again! Happy Fall Baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This delightful recipe is missing just one key instruction: “If you wish to make a boozy apple crunch cake from scratch, you must first invent the universe.”

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made once exactly per recipe- Good, but indeed a bit fussy. Made it again a way that is more family friendly & sustainable: no booze- substituted apple cider in the batter & didn't pour anything over finished cake. Made in a 9x13 cake pan & just put 2nd layer of apples flat like first layer (slice apples however you want- no fuss). I made more of streusel topping- mixed some flour & butter with the almonds and sugar. This got rave reviews at a potluck function and will be made again and again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made this today.  It is a fussy little cake that I think is not worth the trouble. I did it exactly as written and I think it turned out fine - it’s just a lot of work and pots and ingredients for something an apple frangipane tart does better and is easier to make.  My family of 5 adults thought is was good, but we wanted more cake.  We liked the bourbon taste and it looks cool.  It just doesn’t have enough of a personality to make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made for the second time only This Time had a 3 inch tall springform pan rather than the 10 inch cake pan. I had to use the first time.

Delicious. Everybody make this.

The new springform pan brand is Fat Daddio.   The bottom has a small reservoir around the outside, which worked pretty well for a small amount of liquid that leaked out    A new favorite recipe in a new favorite pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this using Bob's Red Mill 1 to 1 gluten free flour, for the sake of my daughter-in-law, who is gluten free. She loved it, but me, not so much.  While the taste was okay, the texture of the cake part was something else -- gritty, sandy even.  Fortunately there are a LOT of apples in the cake, so it's still edible (maybe I'll just eat around the cake...) I'll make this again using regular flour and see how it comes out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mary I had exactly the same experience with Bob’s GF flour.  Maybe I used the cornstarch and should have omitted?  Next attempt will not be GF.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mary, all GF flour is not created equal. You might give better batter or cup4cup brands a try. I make my own blend (GF family member) using Authentic Foods brown and white superfine rice flours plus other starches based on Nicole Hunn’s GF on a shoestring formulation. She has ones that emulate both better batter and cup4cup. My experience with Bob’s is the same as yours: very gritty and unpalatable. Good luck!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am going to be a contrarian and say that while my results were identical to the photo I felt that the excess labor did not pay off in added flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Worked in a Swiss bakery and they made a cake like this. The apples were chopped more finely and I think there was almond paste in the batter. I made this version and it’s growing on me, but it’s very mild. Nothing wrong with it as written but I feel the flavor could be deepened with more almond going on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm a pretty decent baker and was so excited to try this cake; apples, brown butter, maple syrup, bourbon: some of my favorite flavors! And while the final product *looked* amazing, those flavors just didn't blend quite right. The best my partner could muster was that it tasted "sophisticated." This was the only time we didn't actually finish eating an apple-based dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The entire time I was making this, I could not figure out why we were not seasoning the apples with cinnamon and nutmeg or something… Now that I’ve made it and eaten that I still don’t know why not

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Found the video incredibly valuable. I fear this is a recipe that looks better than it tastes, though it may be my lesser baking skills working against me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I’ve made this cake twice. It’s a great cake, very flavorful and toothsome with a lot of textures going on. The recipe needs a good editor. 
2 1/2 pounds of apples (6) is too much. 1.5-1.75lbs is plenty. I put one thickly overlapped layer of apples on the base layer of batter. I used pre-roasted hazelnuts or pecans instead of almonds for the topping for richer flavor and more crunch. I took it out of the oven around 50- 55 mins both times. The center was slightly undercooked and deliciously apple custard like while the periphery was cakey and the top crunchy. The booziness of the bourbon melded beautifully with the above attributes. Don’t give up on this cake! It’s an autumn/winter keeper!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this without a stand mixer and the batter was very thick but it still tasted incredible! I had to sub half of the lemon juice for lime juice because I only had one lemon, and I used brown sugar instead of turbinado sugar and whisky instead of bourbon, and it was still amazing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What an adventure. It's in the oven, but I must ask: what about the remaining 1/3 of the sliced apples in step 6?  Two thirds go in the cake; the cheeky pieces go on top; the final 1/3 goes where?  Also, I measured twice: 120 ml = 3/4 C bourbon.  One quarter cup in the cake, one quarter cup on top when cake is done.  The baker finished the final 1/4 cup. One does what one must.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly as written and it turned out perfectly. Per an earlier comment, the prep was at least an hour. Between peeling and slicing apples and zesting and juicing lemons and chopping nuts and browning the butter, it was at least 30 minutes before I got to work on the batter. It spent one hour and 15 minutes in the oven. Despite the time spent putting it together, I will definitely make it again. A fabulous cake!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this cake and we both totally love it. I substituted cognac but otherwise followed the recipe closely. Oh, my pan was 9 1/2", not 9, which I foolishly thought would mean it would take around 55 minutes, the lower end of the suggested temperature range, to bake. Or less. So I do not understand why it took a total of 95 minutes, instead of the suggested max 65. I see in the comments I am not the only one either.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i love this food

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I can’t wait to try this beautiful cake!  I think I’ll spread the apple slices around the cake so each piece will contain some. Maybe use more apples! Any thoughts on mixing the types of apples?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make them sweet-crisp, as the recipe says -- you could use more than one type of apple but stay with the ones that will hold their shape. And the recipe does instruct you to put a flat layer of apples on top of half the batter, then add the rest of the batter, then add the sliced apple quarters in shapes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you use a buttered bundt pan instead of a springform pan?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the point is the top of the cake is crunchy, browned and gorgeous. A bundt cake flips the top to the bottom, so I wouldn't make that choice myself.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make it more German by baking with the excellent Holstein variety, bred in Germany in the early 20th c. It has all the crisp structure and pronounced acidity of the recommended varieties, but far more flavor. Heirloom apple growers like Scott Farm in Vermont are good sources.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried to fan out the the five ‘cheeks’? I’m wondering if that would spread the baked apple love into more of the servings.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lost me at the apple cutting stage. Bad sign if this is the first recipe in the baking newsletter. I unsubscribed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So glad to see a lower refined sugar cake recipe! Thanks, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is an awesome cake and I plan to make it for Thanksgiving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaugn, please come pick apples at our permaculture fruit oasis summer house, on the official apple island of southern Denmark. the second harvest is in full swing! apples galore and we can bake together afterwards! come picking! :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with pears? Not a huge apple fan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I haven’t made this cake, but I have done a fair bit of baking with pears. I think pears would work very nicely here. Just make sure your pears are ripe but firm (i.e., not hard and not mushy). And you might want to think about whether you like the flavor of bourbon with pears. Personally, I think I’d prefer cognac or a pear liqueur like Poire Williams.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just watched the video. Totally inspired to try. Thank you. You are a delight.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I liked it and used three types of apples. Mackintosh, Winesap and Honeycrisp. It is not overly sweet. I did use bourbon in the cake batter, but left the dousing of the extra bourbon at the end so kids could eat it and used a glaze of pears instead of the half dome . One hour and ten minutes was how long I let it bake. Thank you for this loaded with apples cake recipe. It’s fresh tasting and delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Well, turned out delish.  Not a crumb left at a potluck this evening.  I’m a slow cook- this was a project.  

A looker wish I could post a photo.   

The batter was thick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this cake yesterday and followed the instructions as written (also watched the video to understand about cutting the apples). As others have noted, it required enough dishes to fill up my dishwasher when done. That said, it was absolutely fabulous and well worth the effort! Used 9" springform pan, baked for 60 minutes and it came out perfectly. Used Crimson Crisp apples.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone tried making this gluten free / paleo? Keen on trying almond flour w a wholesome sweetener.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I know ideally there wouldn’t be a substitution for the almonds, but some of us are very allergic and a good apple cake is always a good apple cake. So be my question is what would you recommend in place of nuts to really make a good crunch?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Shanipumpkin seeds?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was hoping someone would offer a substitute for bourbon since I don’t like bourbon. But I don’t mind other flavors like rum or Brandy or even some kind of whiskey. Has anybody tried other things?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tried calvados, amoretto.  Used less, since they’re liqueurs.  Less boozy, more flavorful.  If I make it again I’ll experiment with frangelico (hazelnuts).  Another reader tried poire william after substituting the apple with pears and it turned out well apparently.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe turned out just as described.  I decided to layer it all the way between the layers of cake without making the sliced apples clumped as he did at the very end.   I also added a bit of nutmeg and ginger to the apples when tossed with the lemon juice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mmmm ginger and nutmeg. Mace might be interesting too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Cake is cooling now! I substituted candied ginger for the almonds and added an extra 1/2 tsp of vanilla to avoid the almond extract. (Nut allergy, and not a substitution for no reason) 

The star of the show is the browned butter, bourbon, and vanilla extract (and almond if you are using) which I wanted to just DRINK but I had amazing willpower and let the cake have it instead. 

10/10 autumnal showstopper! A centerfold for real, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Turned out great, but definitely took a good 60+ minutes to prep. From the peeling coring slicing of apples to zesting lemon to browning butter this is a labor intensive cake. Also had to be very careful to make sure it was truly done—one of those cakes with confounding variables like sliced apples at the top that make it just that much harder to be sure that your tester is coming out clean.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this using the recommended types of apples and even though I baked it for an extra 15 minutes, they were still not soft enough. Anyone else have this problem?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: All the steps are worth it. The delicate carmelized brown butter in the batter made the apples shine. Simple, delicate, delicious. Plan for a long afternoon of cooking, really enjoy all the delish smells, and this cake warm with ice cream, oh honey, your belly will thank you. We used a healthy shot of cinnamon whiskey to the batter, just enough to give this girl a twinkle in her eye! For our sober friends, we made a simple brown butter, maple syrup and lemon juice syrup to pour over the cooked cake instead of more booze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even better with 100% whole grain flours like #Wheatsome!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My batter came out way too thick and I couldn't use all the apples.  DIdn't even attempt the apple cheeks on top of the cake because I could barely get the batter to cover the apples in the middle.  It's in the oven now so no idea how it will come out.  A lot of prep and a lot of cleaning up. Hope it was worth it.  The only thing I can think of as to why the batter was so thick is when I added the bourbon to the butter the butter was quite hot so it really evaporated much more vigorously.  Help!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The recipe is definitely a keeper. Oma's Kuchen ist der beste, but this one is less fussy.
Didn't have all the ingredients, made some adjustments.
Used a mix of Granny Smith + Elstar apples, rum and 200 g regular/50 g of almond flour. No almond extract, no baking soda, no turbinado sugar. 
While assembling it looked like I had too many apples for the amount of batter but it turned out perfectly fine. I think my springform pan is a little bigger than the one used in the video.
Thanks, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Takes time and a bit fiddly to make but patience is rewarded in the delicious taste and texture. Made for a Canadian Thanksgiving dessert with Annapolis Valley picked apples...great recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is very pretty, and it went over well with my partner. It would be a showstopper for Rosh Hashanah dinner. Like others, I found that it needed more time than the recipe called for, and generated more sliced apples than I needed. (The extra went into a mini-tart.) I also did not pour any bourbon over, because my 13-year-old didn't want to taste bourbon. There are some great ideas on this thread for substitutions. Mine was a little overbaked, so I might try adding the bourbon to one slice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I baked this yesterday, after reading through  the recipe and comments multiple times.  The instructions are quite clear if you read closely and follow step by step.  I used KAF measure for measure flour and Rye whiskey vs bourbon.  Turned out just lovely.  The Rye gives it just enough spice to have the sense there is spice added but you just can't put your finger on which.  I strongly recommend taking the time to measure out and prep each component before assembly......then it all comes together quickly.  Does take a few extra minutes to fully bake but not too much or gets dry.  Have to find your oven's sweet spot.  I use an oven thermometer.
Even better the next day!!  Partner says this could replace apple pie for Thanksgiving which is high praise!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the cake before I found video.  Written recipe MUST be edited:  it says place apple slices in ONE layer.  Impossible! Video shows MANY layers of apple.  Of course.  I almost gave up as I was assembling cake.  Luckily I did not.  As others noted: written recipe says use 2/3 of the apples kn the bottom layer of batter but video clearly shows use them all except those set aside for top apple domes. Loved the video but don’t  make us need one yo correct written instructions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Despite watching the video thrice I still couldn’t fit all the apples in the cake. Two were left out that I used to make apple sauce. I added cinnamon and cardamom to batter based on reviews that cake was blah. Cake was fine not something I would repeat again. MC has a much more easier and delicious recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The instructions and the video don’t match. The instructions say “ Add two-thirds of the apples, flat sides down, in a single layer (some overlap is fine). Pour in the rest of the batter and smooth into an even layer. Press the reserved sliced cheeks into the top of the batter, rounded sides up, in clusters to resemble domes of apples. Discard any juices in the bowl”. I did this and have most of my apples left over. In the video Vaughn dumps most of the slices on the batter AND adds the juices!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of my lesser favorite recipes! Came out a bit soggy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this last night and it was amazing. I agree with several comments above regarding leftover apples. I weighed them and ended up using closer to 2 lbs vs 2 1/2. Also-definitely let the butter cool a bit before adding the bourbon! It will bubble up and possibly overflow if you don’t.  One final change-instead of pouring straight bourbon on top, I made a simple vanilla glaze with powdered sugar, butter, milk and added lemon juice and 1 tsp brandy. The lemon flavor really added to the taste and it gave the cake a shiny finish that was incredible. I’ll definitely make this again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Bourbon and brandy?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've madd this 3 times -- GF! As always with GF conversion, weigh the ingredients, mix super well, let the batter rest at least 10 minutes after mixing. Perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made this yesterday & it does look stunning. That being said, should have baked it longer. Am thinking less apples might cut bake time? Agree with other comments that dough looks suprisingly spare but still works out. Oh & that slicing trick - too fiddly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not only absolutely gorgeous but totally delicious! Chock full of beautiful sliced apples, it was better than any apple pie, let alone cake, I’ve ever eaten! We loved it without reservation (not even from the cake curmudgeon).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this last night after staring at the recipe and video for several weeks. I’m not a natural baker so I tend to overthink sometimes. I followed the recipe & video to a T. It came out GREAT!   Beautiful to behold and delicious to eat. I will do this one again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as written, except for the topping: we used pecans because they were on hand and skipped the fancy sugar because it wasn’t.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Disappointing. Dry!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@john golden, Camden Maine
Much better the second day—moist and very flavorful from the apples didn’t have bourbon and substituted dark rum

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those of us who avoid alcohol, what can be substituted for the Bourbon?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Nancy Apple Juice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I often add rum to apple cakes with delicious result, & there are very good rum flavorings for an alcohol-free option.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So, the apple segments that decorate the top are not dressed with lemon juice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The 2/3's of the apple in step 6 are the ones that were sliced and mixed with lemon juice, correct?  The other 1/3 would be the 5 cheeks sliced and saved for the top?  Not exactly sure what the other 1/3 of apples are.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MW watch the video.  He uses all of the apples, not 2/3.  The ones on top are the reserved crowns.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tree nut intolerance - what do you suggest as a substitute for the chopped slivered almonds and the almond extract?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Double the vanilla for the extract, and maybe use pepitas or skip the nuts on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: forgot to add the lemon juice.......sigh.......but it's in the oven now and smells out of the world....long time to bake.....but am licking the spatula from the mixing bowl in anticipation.......yum

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Roughly how many servings will this make?  Can I get 7-8 servings?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How much butter? Not listed in the ingredients.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @wm wilbert 3/4 C of butter. 3rd item in the list of ingredients.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any way to do it without eggs? Sorry to ask but it looks spectacular! Congrats on it

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not sure yet what I think of this recipe yet. After the allotted baking time of 65 minutes, the center of the cake was still uncooked. In fact raw. After an additional 30 minutes, the same. My oven has been working as it should for other recent bakes. Not sure what all is happening.  Followed the recipe exactly.  Mmmmm......

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I watched the video and followed the instructions to the T.  I found the batter very difficult to work with.  It did not “spread” like in the video.  I ended up having to drop dollops everywhere and using wet hands to manipulate it.  Perhaps I overmixed after all?  They’d result was very flavorful with a nice crunchy top but the innards were mushy.  Perhaps at 5299 feet it needed another few minutes to crisp up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would this cake freeze alright or would the freeze thaw cycle cause the apples to release too much liquid?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am not a whiskey/bourbon fan.  Do you think a dark rum would work for a good flavor choice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A good rum would work, I think.  It will add a different dimension, but a good one!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this a few days ago-- delicious but definitely boozy. I ended up only using a bit of the bourbon for the top after taking it out of the oven. It only took about 55 min and I used the toothpick test and a thermometer as sometimes apple cakes are tough to get baked in the center.   . I think the instructions could be simplified. I also did not do the apples on top as noted- simple slices worked fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cake is quite a project! I found the apples on top never really cooked  in the time it took for the batter to cook (which was way longer then the recipe states) they are delicious flavors though, but the cooking time is a mystery if you want cooked apples

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This should have been much better for the level of effort. Our cake was cooked on top but uncooked on the bottom. The apples didnt really soften. The time estimates are way off.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So it looks like you made your own cake flour by adding the cornstarch. I enjoyed the cake but I think when I make it again, I would precook the apples a bit to soften them. They were a bit too crunchy for my taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would love an alternative to the eggs please

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where is the video??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
As several other readers have commented -- the recipe as written uses only ⅔ of the sliced apples, it doesn't use the remaining ⅓ of the apples.

I'm a very experienced baker, and I DIF watch the video a few days before making this cake but the recipe tripped me up. I had to stretch the final portion of batter by adding some water to thin it out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1/3 of the apples are saved to top the cake (first step instructs to save 5 full segments to decorate). But in any case, 6 apples/2.5 lb is way too much for this cake. And definitely not enough batter. The bottom batter layer, being so thin, becomes too dry as it bakes.  I'll try one more time with less apples and more batter before I am ready to give up on this cake altogether.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried to freeze this? After reading all the comments I would love to make this for an upcoming party but not sure how it would all hold up after freezing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As others noted, it takes 1.5h to prep including cutting the apples and butter melting. I only used 4 apples and felt it was too much. It's in the oven now. Hopefully it'll come out good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too many apples, too little batter. 1/3 was not enough to cover the bottom of the 9" springform pan. I did try, but at the end, needed to add a bit more. The rest was not enough to fully cover the top, so I ended up with some apples sticking out. And I have measured everything meticulously. It is currently in the oven, so I'll update when it is done, but I doubt it will look anything like the picture. I feel like trying it again with increasing batter by at least half.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just to update: had to keep it in the oven for 10 extra minutes; to my surprise, it came out looking just fine, but the taste was kind of “boring” (if one can describe a cake this way). There are so many other apple cakes recipes that are tastier with less work. Not making it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious!  Time consuming and a lot of clean up, but worth it for special occasions.  Followed directions, took 20 minutes longer.  Did not taste bourbon “y”, just another layer of sweet goodness!  Might add a little cinnamon to the flour mix if I make again.  Thank you for the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How would this freeze?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Scott Only one way to find out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this last night and it came out almost like the photo! And so good! I used Gala apples, only because it was what we had on hand. They were delicious, but I will try a couple different, maybe more tart apples, next time. It's definitely a project recipe - the prep time was longer than 30, maybe 45, because of the apple peeling, right-sized slicing, etc. I too had a much longer bake until the center was done. Approx 80 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really wanted to like this but dang - it really missed the mark! We each had a piece and tossed the rest. Not worth it at all. The lemon was too overpowering, for as many apples as there were in the cake it wasn't very apple-y and all in all it was just way too much work for very little reward.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this exactly as written but subbed in Irish whiskey cuz that’s what I had. It was fabulous! Cook time is definitely longer than 65 mins- closer to 75-80, but just go by smell and cake tester and you’ll be fine. Great fall dessert. Very highly recommend!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just like bourbon, maple syrup is a liquid, so it is measured in mL, not g.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1 mL = 1 g. I found this out when I started using a brew ratio when making coffee. I use a 1:16 ratio, so 40 g of coffee requires 640 mL of water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even better day 2! I omitted almond extract and almonds but added walnuts due to allergies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was much more complicated than it needed to be.  The batter is very thick and the bourbon flavor was nonexistent.  If I do this again, I’ll add more bourbon and put some of the almonds over the first layer of apples before the remaining batter.  With a 9 inch springpan, it took a LOT longer than 55 - 65 minutes to cook.  I packed in the apples and still had the equivalent of 1 1/2 apples left.  Dutch baby for breakfast tomorrow I guess.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm watching the video as my cake is in the oven. Here's what I messed up and where the recipe could be clearer: "Single layer" of apples to me only used about a quarter of what I sliced.  In the video, Vaughn dumps in the apples and it's more than what I would call single layer.  Should have watched that.  I used my extra apples to make applesauce that I'll serve with the cake if it's not apple-y enough.  Second, lots of comments on the batter being thick - in the video it is extremely thick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is very tasty and it came out looking picture perfect (I did watch the video first). My only concern is that at an hour, the center was still raw as was the batter under the pretty apples on top. I didn’t realize it was raw until after slicing into it because the batter is rather fatty and didn’t stick to the toothpick I was using as a cake tester. Not sure how to fix that except to bake the heck out of it, which I think would cause the exterior of the cake to be overdone.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Help, my cake was covered and now the top is mushy not crunchy! Any ideas?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Loved this video! A wee bit obsessed with you! My batter turned out very almost….bready. Difficult to spread. Nothing running about it. I checked and rechecked the ingredients to make sure I was doing it right and hadn’t missed a step but I feel like I needed to add some Juice or wetness. It’s in the oven now. I’ll let you know how it turns out.
MGH

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love the video!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow, my family enjoyed this! Thank you, Vaughn! I had too many apple slices so I flooded the top. Next time, I will add cardamom and/or other spices.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Keeping the top cheeks separate is a good trick. Cake is stunning looking. Needs a bit more oomph maybe cinnamon?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavors and presentation are stunning with this recipe... that being said, when I made it today I too had to bake it quite a bit longer (+30 min.) and I think there should be half again more batter for this amount of apples - maybe because of my high-altitude and very dry climate the batter was just too congealed (or just needs more liquid because of the cornstarch) and it turned out more like a yummy Apple Brown Betty than a cake. But YUMMY!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I baked this cake for a dinner party last night.  It looked great but was very dry and I would not bake it again.  I'm not sure if I did something wrong that caused it to be dry or not, I baked it for 60 minutes (guidance said 55 to 65 minutes).  It was golden brown and did not look over baked at all.  Anyone else experience this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Daryl I made this for the 3rd time recently. The first two times I took it out at 50-55min and the center was slightly undercooked in a good way…like an apple custard… and the periphery was caked but not too dry. The 3rd time I took it out about 5-7 min later and it was disappointedly drier. Hence, I’m sticking with the shorter bake time in the future… was really delicious and rather unique.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 65 minutes rather than 95 minutes in the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I too didn't want to taste more bourbon (ok, I admit I substituted brandy for the entire recipe!) poured over the top, so I put the remaining 1/4 c.on the stovetop with a tablespoon or so of Apricot Preserves. Gave it a simmer to reduce it a bit and used that instead. Yummy! Also, YES to wishing I'd thought to add some cinnamon at the least and as many have noted, too many apple slices for the amount of batter. Happy almost Holidays y'all!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this!   Reading the comments, I can certainly agree that the recipe badly needs editing.   You really need to watch the video to understand how to handle the apples.   And lay the apples flat in one layer?  No way.
Plus the instruction to use 3/4 of the apples.   What does that mean?
Nevertheless it came out looking like the picture, in other words quite impressive.   And it tasted good too.  The bourbon does not overwhelm, it just adds a nice flavor.  
All in all, a bit of a project, but worth the effort.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine has been in the oven for about 30 minutes longer than it said to bake for in the recipe. It also took longer to prep. The instructions were completely vague on the step of adding the apple layer. If I made a single layer, I’d only use a small fraction of the apples. The batter was extremely thick too. Im skeptical that this is going to yield a tasty desert. This recipe is very unlike other NYT recipes I’ve used. I usually can count on NYT recipes to be very well written - so much so, that I didn’t thoroughly read this recipe before starting. Had I done so, the number of questions I’d have had would have directed me to select a different recipe. I’ll preview them a bit closer from here on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The video is great I truly enjoyed it and I think that the cake is a show-picéé and delicious. It is true that the apple is too much unless you pile more in the middle. I found the browned butter and bourbon combination wonderful! I couldn't find comments about the apples used - well, I didn't read all 180 -plus comments. What I found is that my favorite cooking apple, the Granny Smith apples remained too crunchy for my liking. Next time, I will use McIntosh or Jonathan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cake was delicious!! I will admit that I sprinkled pumpkin pie spice in the batter and also on top of the apples.  I’m sure the pure apple taste is nice, but I want spice!! Booze is not too boozy, but compliments the cake (especially the spices) perfectly.  I forgot to add the cornstarch, but the cake was perfect, light and not dense.  Also, people who want a substitute for the booze, there are thousands of brown butter apple cakes.  Not every recipe is for everyone.  I will make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the cake yesterday exactly as recipe written and will make it again. I found the video to be very helpful. Made it with a 10 inch springform pan and baked for about 70 minutes. Turned out perfectly and our dinner guests loved it. Use good bourbon!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: And the 1/3 apples? Where do those go? Kindly update the instructions for the mystery 1/3 for clarity. The 5 apple cheeks on top never became totally tender, so we’re eating around them. This cake isn’t a snap to make, so get ready to invest some time. Weighing the effort against the finished cake, I don’t plan to make this one again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made this with some substitutions and it worked out wonderfully. 
Bob Mill's AP GF flour
Arrowroot for cornstarch
Coconut sugar for sugar in cake
Date sugar for topping

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I absolutely LOVED this recipe. The family couldn’t wait, so we ate it warm. It wasn’t super sweet - and a very adult cake, so my little one didn’t much like it, but the rest of us were on Cloud 9. Wondering if coating the apple slices in sugar would help make it sweeter? Has anyone tried adding cinnamon? Didn’t have bourbon so used a single malt which was excellent!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mallika I added a sprinkle of nutmeg and it was delicious, although I do wish I added cinnamon. I think it might cut through the almond flavor a bit (I could be wrong). The almond flavor i do find got a bit excessive the more I ate the cake. I do find it necessary, just wish I used a little less. I quite enjoyed the “not so sweetness” but I think adding another half cup of sugar (perhaps coating the apples) would do the trick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the cake last night. It came out well, but split in the middle when I took it out of the pan. No matter. Very tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 4.5! It was delicious. Note: We baked it for 71 minutes and then felt like we should take it out because the top looked perfect. Wish we had baked it longer, it was really not quite done in the center, but still great. Took much longer than 30 minutes to prep.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best things about this recipe: 1) it comes out looking EXACTLY like the photo here (gorgeous), and 2) the video explanation of how to simply cut parchment paper to fit your pan (how did I not know this already, after all these years?!).  However, the cake itself was good, not great - it was a bit dense, of course (due to all those apples) and could have used even more bourbon.  If I make it again I'll use fewer apples in the layer and aim for maximum cake rise.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made this for family dinner...showstopper for appearance, but a little lackluster for dessert, would be better for breakfast with coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought this was delicious, used up apples that had gone mealy. Ran out of time towards the end and my “cheeks” had dissembled so just piled most of the apples in then spread batter over and piled rest of the apples on top.  Did end up baking at least 1:15 before tester came out clean.  Skipped almonds as my kid doesn’t like them, and forgot to douse w second dose of bourbon. Everyone loved it, even better second day, esp since it made use of apples that were done.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really good. Heavy, but not overly sweet. I ran out of maple syrup while this was all in process, so I used half maple and have agave syrup and it worked great! Excellent for breakfast the next day, too. And I agree, the instructions for prepping the pared apples were totally useless. Just peel your apples and core them before slicing, however you like to do it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this today, to take to Thanksgiving dinner tomorrow. It smells soooo good! I added 1/4 cup of Quince Jelly to the whisky on top. Warmed it up to melt it and mix with the whisky. Also making some Vanilla Gelato to go with it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Although it was tasty, it was a hot mess to make.  Way too much trouble for mediocre results IMHO.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 4/5. Made almost exactly as written, substituted pecans for almonds because it’s what we had - 57 min bake time. Apples were still crunchy but I was in the mood for something more cooked down inside - the top and the cake itself were perfect though. A bit of cinnamon and cardamom with the apples would have elevated this cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was a bit nervous because my batter came out thicker than in the video and was hard to spread over the apples, but not to worry: the cake rose well and looked beautiful — got compliments from others. That said, I did have to leave it in around 10-15m longer (as other commenters mentioned) and the taste/texture combo was good but not exceptional. The bottom was a little dry and the top was mushy from the bourbon, but that might be because I accidentally used half of the batter below and half above, unlike the 1/3 + 2/3 recommend ratio.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used gluten-free flour and found this needed about 10-15 extra minutes to bake, in case anyone else is celiac. It came out a bit denser than st added flour obviously, but still delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had some trouble covering the apples with the remaining 2/3 of the dough and thought maybe I should have left out some of the apple. But it came out of the oven looking like the one in the video. Delicious, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I finally made this. Took longer than expected, probably because I was pressed for time, but it was worth it. I used a mandoline to slice the apples, but I still needed to cut through them with a knife after baking. Perhaps I used too many apples. Will try again with canned pears because they are always soft.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I know this sounds very ammeter-ish but is the sweet potato raw?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Monet it is.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Yes--you'll want to use raw sweet potato that you grate and then add to the mix.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Monetthat was MY question as well. Is it raw or cooked then grated??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: The measurements are close enough for the vegetable oil, flour, maple syrup, pecans, butter and cream cheese.  They are way off for the granulated sugar (1/2 cup=83 grams; 200 grams=1-1/4 cups), sweet potato (1-1/2 cups=156 grams; 180 grams=1-3/4 cups), and powdered sugar (1-1/2 cups=199 grams; 150 grams is close to 1 cup).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Gloria every resource I’ve checked has 1c sugar at 200g unlike flour which varies from 120g to 142g…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Gloria I didn’t notice your comment until I started. I went with 200g of sugar (because I had already added it to the oil) but a bit less maple syrup. I added more sweet potato and found that it’s a pretty forgiving recipe. I made it for a PTSA meeting and it was so good, I made another one but with 50% whole wheat flour and WAY more sweet potato (almost 2.5 cups) and it was still fantastic. I had enough of the frosting for a liberal frosting of cake one and a nice amount (enough) for cake two. I gave half of cake 2 to neighbors and they called telling me they love me!! Confusing to make, but worth it!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Christina ok, so the measurements have been updated (sugar is now 100g, not 200). I guess I will just have to make again!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I love cream cheese frosting but...not the candy sweet kind. Can I successfully cut the Powered sugar back to 1 cup or what do you suggest?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I’d make it a Chantilly frosting but with more of a cream cheese frosting punch. Beat together 40 g butter (about 3 Tbsp), 40 g cream cheese (about 3 Tbsp), 65–75 g powdered sugar (⅔–¾ cup), a pinch of salt, and a splash of vanilla until smooth. In another bowl whip 120 g heavy cream (½ cup) with 60 g mascarpone (¼ cup) to soft peaks, then fold into the cream cheese base. Add more sugar as needed. Or lookup cream cheese IMBC! Lmk how it goes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @PinkDreamer. More ingredients, more dirty bowls, no thank you…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Victoria R yes, you can cut back the powdered sugar. I almost always reduce the powdered sugar in frosting recipes. You can always add a little more if you need it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: The conversions are close enough for the vegetable oil, flour, maple syrup, pecans, butter and cream cheese.  They are way off for the granulated sugar (1/2 cup=83 grams; 200 grams=1.2 cups), the sweet potatoes (1-1/2 cups=156 grams; 180 grams=1-3/4 cups), and the powdered sugar (1-1/2 cups=199 grams; 150 grams=approx. 1 cup).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
The brown butter is the star of this show! I made it recently for a little get together and was asked to bring it again to a different get together. A few tips for the brown butter. I used a stainless steel saute pan so I could really see the color turn. In essence, we are toasting the butter and it goes from toasted to burnt quickly. Then, I poured it into a glass bottom baking dish to cool it in the fridge checking every 10 minutes. It will go faster or slower based on how much butter you have (I made a double) and the size of your glass bottom dish. I just used 9x12. For the cake I go by internal temp and shoot for 200-205 with this cake.
The brown butter is a secret weapon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I have a cream cheese frosting recipe that uses honey instead of sugar. I was amazed at how it tasted. It doesn’t seem to “keep” as well but with a cake this size, it probably won’t last long anyway.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Would this cake work using pureed pumpkin?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake without gluten free 1:1 flour and it was a big hit! Everyone loved the flavor, texture and frosting. I made it with 200 g of granulated sugar, but it could have been 1/2 c (100 g), as it was on the sweeter side.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: How does it taste without the frosting?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Rosemary. It’s a bit like a carrot / spice cake. I like the taste without the frosting but it’s not attractive without it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Does it matter if it is sweet potato or yam?  Could winter squash be used?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I’d love to turn this into a muffin (to sneak sweet potato into kids lunchboxes). I think I’d just decrease the sugar. Is anyone savvy enough with ingredient ratios to suggest other appropriate changes? 

I’ll also be subbing in maple sugar because I have a big bag I’m working through, but I can handle that conversion.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Any thoughts on whether this would freeze well? Sounds just marvelous!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
@Jill B.
I am wondering the same. With out without the topping.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Jill B. Wrap the unfrosted cake in plastic wrap, then tightly with heavy-duty foil.  It ought to keep in the freezer for a couple of months.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I know the icing is the "star of the show" but I'm curious to know if anyone has had it without the icing and what they think about it as a naked cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding. I used closer to 2.5 cups of grated sweet potato so as not to waste any. The frosting puts this over the top! Added to our Thanksgiving menu!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: This recipe looks amazing. And also, as a girl from California, who is constantly thinking about nutrition… I just love the idea that some people in the world make “snacking cakes”. Isn’t that so lovely?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: If the frosting is too sweet for your taste, try subbing some goat cheese for some of the cream cheese.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the recipe, added a bit of cloves and ground ginger, filled two round pans, and made it a layered cake for my best friend’s birthday. Collected compliments all night long.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
Monet,
I don't think you can grate a cooked sweet potato so it must be raw.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I’m going to try this with cooked sweet potato. Seems like it should work…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Amy I think so too - like pumpkin bread. Definitely easier than grating a sweet potato.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @malpal The flavor was good without the frosting. Wasn't a fan of the texture of the grated sweet potato though. Will look for another recipe using pureed sweet potato for next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Do people really snack on cake?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
@Michelle
Little Debbie snacks are a thing.  I wouldn’t, but no judgement.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: We worked on a farm in Ireland as youth; up at 6am, full breakfast, break at 10 for tea replete with cakes, lunch @ noon, 3pm, more tea and more cake, dinner of stew and potatoes and deserts. So, yes, people really do snack on cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: They were originally called snack cakes as a marketing tactics because they were box mixes with mix-ins that people might consider snacks, such as raisins, nuts, etc.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Monet - that’s not an amateur question, not to worry! But the sweet potato has to be raw in order to grate it, and it cooks while the cake is baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I make a version with grated zucchini (don't blot) and applesauce for the pecans. Same measures for everything, sugar and butter frosting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
Made this tonight and it was absolutely delicious. The brown butter and cream cheese is an ingenious combination, and like carrot cake, the sweetness of the frosting complements the earthiness and spice of the cake perfectly.

FYI: my cake took 45-50 minutes (my sweet potato was probably 20-30g over the recipe) and even with a clean cake tester the cake is moist to the point of being, as the Brits would say, claggy. But who cares? I'm already drooling thinking about this with coffee in the AM.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Only changes made were to reduce the granulated sugar by half and use about 2 cup grated sweet potato (so I didn't waste any). Only took about 28 minutes baking time for me. The cake is moist, earthy, and has a great texture from the sweet potatoes. As the recipe says, the frosting is the star -- it's decadent and unique, but so rich that I might cut the frosting recipe down next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Good cake but more than twice the amount of prep time than was indicated.  I used a full 2 cups of shredded sweet potato and subbed out half the sugar with applesauce, which resulted in 5 minutes of extra cook time.  Next time I'd probably skip the hassle of browning the butter.  While it adds that rich buttery taste to the frosting, I prefer a cream cheese frosting where you taste more of the cream cheese.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: No one will ever know this cake has sweet potatoe in it-unless you tell them! Wonderful snack cake

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Made this cake as written and it was lovely and perfect with a cup of tea in the afternoon or for breakfast! The frosting was perfect and not too sweet. Will definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written (using the measurement weights in grams), except that I doubled the recipe and used a 9"x13" pan (I did not double the frosting; a single recipe was plenty for just the top, although it wasn't as thick a layer as in the picture). I convection baked it for 35 min. It was absolutely delicious! It was moist, fluffy, and had the perfect amount of sweetness. The toasted pecans and browned butter in the frosting really made it special. And it made the house smell divine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake exactly as written.  Brought it to thanksgiving and it was a huge hit.  I think it's the best cake I have ever made.  It kept in the fridge for days, allowing us to enjoy it again and again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Love this cake! I use less sugar in the icing, beat so that it is extra fluffy, and add a bit of cream of tartar for extra tartness.  Serve deconstructed with mound of icing & pecans on the side.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Made following the recipe exactly using a scale. It's absolutely delicious. Only slight modification was maybe 25g less powdered sugar in the frosting. This whole mess about converting between weights and volumes in the recipe is ridiculous, just use a scale and get it right every time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Add fresh thyme on top thyme to elevate it even more. Such a winning recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous recipe. I think there’s enough frosting for two cakes. I was skeptical about the frosting bc I typically prefer more cream cheese to butter, but this is spot on. A keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I really wanted a cake with a sweet potato puree instead of grated.  I roasted in the skins, peeled and mashed to a puree and used 1.5 cups.  Everything else was about the same.  I think the icing is over-the-top my favorite, and is a perfect compliment to this lovely cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Yams vs sweet potatoes.   Can you use either?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Mary my understanding is we don’t get actual yams in the US, and that we call sweet potatoes yams sometimes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Can you use olive or avocado oil vs vegetable oil?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: All oils are functionally equivalent in baking, where smoke points aren't important. The key thing is to use an oil with a flavor that you like, that you think will taste good in the recipe, that you can (or are willing to) afford in the necessary quantity! Vegetable oil is inexpensive and flavor-neutral, but you could use avocado, olive, or even a flavorful nut oil so long as you enjoy those flavors in your cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Handy references for conversions: https://www.instacart.com/company/ideas/grams-to-cups-conversion/ https://www.instacart.com/company/ideas/cooking-measurement-conversion/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: And is this a. sweet potato, which has yellow flesh, or a yam, shich has orange flesh?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
@Robin from the internet- many Americans misunderstand what a tan is. 

“True yams have rough, bark-like skin, starchy, dry white flesh, and are typically less sweet and more cylindrical, while sweet potatoes have smooth, thinner skin, moist flesh ranging in color from white to deep orange or purple, and are significantly sweeter and more widely available.”

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: It would be so helpful to get clarity on the disparities between the measurements in cups vs grams. I’m unsure which way to go.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: If you have a scale, always use grams - measuring by weight is more precise than measuring by volume. One cup of flour can weigh different amounts depending on how tightly you pack it, but 160 grams of flour will always be the same amount.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Lauren - When baking, if weights are provided it is always best to use those and not measures.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Lauren make sure you’re measuring correctly if using volume measurements. Scoop the item into the measuring cup, using something smaller, like a spoon. Fill to excess, then level off with a finger, back and forth. Doing so will yield amounts closer to the weight measurements

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I used brown sugar in place of white and baked in a loaf pan for about 50 minutes.  Very moist but thankfully not wet like carrot cake tends to be.  The cinnamon seemed overpowering so will reduce to 1.5 tsp. next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly! It is fabulous! The cake is good even without the frosting!

author: Vaughn Vreeland

recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written. It was everything I wanted it to be. The brown butter frosting is heavenly. I shredded the sweet potatoes rather than grating. My family didn’t mind at all. Next time will sift the powdered sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I thought this was a lovely recipe..I was interested in the one that had been printed in the Sunday magazine on 10/20, and I was happy to find it something similar on a smaller scale. (I added black pepper as the other recipe had suggested.)

Even with grated sweet potatoes, I found this a far lighter cake than carrots (likely due to water contents) and I am curious as to what the taste and texture specific differences are between this and a cooked potato or pumpkin cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe. The resulting cake is similar to carrot cake but more earthy. I cut 1/3 of the sugar from the icing and didn't miss it one bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Can you grate the sweet potato in the food processor?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this three times (didn’t want any cream cheese to go to waste, doncha know!), and it is absolutely a favorite.  I always make the first one as written, and it is divine — look no further for your new favorite cake.  Just to mix it up a tiny bit, I added 1/4 teaspoon of allspice, cloves, and ginger to make it more of a spice cake — nice but not necessary.  A raw sweet potato on a box grater worked beautifully for me.  You could use a food processor, but it’s that much more to wash.  The brown butter in the frosting is a game-changer — I don’t think I’ll ever make cream cheese frosting any other way again.  Thanks, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Im not much of a baker. That being said, I made this exact to the recipe and served it to my in-laws for dessert. My mother-in-law asked for the recipe. Win!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe was big hit with the family, though I’ll continue to tweak a bit for altitude adjustments. It wasn’t completely successful when I first made it as the middle took a very long time to cook. I finally pulled it out when two toothpicks in the center came out clean, but unfortunately the toothpicks lied, as after cooling and icing the middle was still overly gooey when it was cut into. The outer pieces were excellent though. 
The sweet potato flavor wasn’t as prominent as I expected, but it was still a very delicious spiced cake very much along the lines of carrot cake. The icing was good, though I did reduce the powdered sugar, just adding it by taste until the tang of the cream cheese was tempered some and the browned butter flavor notes could be detected more readily. With future batches I’m going to try adding a touch of ginger or minced candied ginger to the batter, and also trying baking it in muffin tins and mini-loaf tins (I have one that’s shallower multiple loaves in one pan that would be perfect for making individual or shareable slices). The icing is nice, and can fancy it up (the addition of a little orange zest or candied orange peel for decor would likely be very yummy), but the cake is very nice on its own, and I foresee enjoying it a great deal making it in muffin form sans icing for an easy portable snack/breakfast. We’re definitely keeping this recipe around!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: This is SUPERB. Like others, I reduced the sugar in the frosting to one cup. I also used walnuts instead of pecans (that’s what I had, I didn’t feel like I was missing anything). You could halve the frosting or skip it altogether.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: So delicious! I reduced the sugar and syrup in the cake by 1/3 each. For the frosting, I only used about 3/4 c sugar, which was about perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I rarely make cakes but this recipe caught my eye. Made it for a neighborhood gathering. The cake received rave reviews, some folx said it was the best cake they’d ever eaten. I think the frosting makes it stand out. I’m now the sweet potato cake person. Made exactly as written. Loved it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Would this work using puréed roasted sweet potatoes? If yes: how much of it would I use?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Sls1200 reported: I really wanted a cake with a sweet potato puree instead of grated. I roasted in the skins, peeled and mashed to a puree and used 1.5 cups.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Made 1/2 of the icing, and it was enough.  Additionally reduced the powdered sugar in the icing, and everyone loved the result at a Thanksgiving dinner that also featured two excellent pecan pies, a bakery pumpkin pie, and a homemade sweet potato pie (round, unlike our snacking cake).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody:
thank you thank you thank you for including gram weights for this recipe!
and including for the liquids!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Wow, this was so good! The cake stands on its own; I’d make again without the frosting. But the frosting is worth the effort, the browned butter elevates the flavor and it goes perfectly with the hearty cake. I used a little less powdered sugar, about 1 1/4 cup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I have now made this delicious cake FIVE times! it is that well received. After the first time grating the sweet potato, i used a food processor on the next 4 and actually it came out even better. I use a little more sweet potato than is called for which increases the cooking time for me to 45 mins. I also add cardamom, clove and allspice to mine. Simply yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: I reduced the sugar and syrup by half and thought it was still a little too sweet, but will make again knowing that, since the flavor is really good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Excellent cake! I made it with a maple cinnamon glaze instead of the frosting and it was terrific.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato and Brown Butter Snacking Cake Recipe
commentBody: Very easy and good as is.   I’ve made several times and substituted apple and even mango for the sweet potato.   Just a nice snack cake by recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I’d use this as the bread for a pb&j sandwich bc pb&j is the best thing ever

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly according to the recipe here, except I didn't make the icing.  I thought it would be too sweet with the icing.  The cake came out more domed than in the picture.  I baked it for 1:15 and it has an odd dryness to it.  It doesn't feel or look dry, but the crumb is tight and not moist to taste.  But, it tastes great and the jam in the middle is a nice addition. I think it would be amazing with a chocolate glaze, or even a bacon chocolate glaze instead of the jam glaze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed. I used homemade natural peanut butter without a problem. This really does taste strongly of peanut butter - dont skimp on the jam. Tastes even moister the next day - who knows why. It seems like kind of a novelty item for die-hard PBJ fans (who might be just as happy with a regular PBJ sandwich) but it's all good...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: Tastes just like a PB and J.  Not sure it's dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
