author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: It really did taste like a peanut butter and jelly sandwich in cake form, but it took closer to 90 minutes to bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made with almond butter and toasted almonds? Any adjustments suggested? I have a houseful of non-PB eaters.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I'd love to know the answer to this question!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as recipe directed. Good, but will make again with these tweaks: Will use 9 X 5 loaf pan. Pan size in recipe is too small & was overly full of batter. Though the cake tested done at 75 minutes, bottom of loaf was very dense. Will also be sure to assure swirls of jam don't touch edges of the pan because jam seeped out after cake was removed from pan. As others have said, glaze never sets up. I pressed plastic wrap over the cut end of the cake and inverted a large bowl over the cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I had a feeling this could work as a fun & flawless coffee cake and it absolutely delivered - highly recommend. Meanwhile I discovered an unopened plastic fifth of Jim beam in the back of my liquor cabinet so tomorrow I'll test out Vaughn's Boozy Apple Crunch Cake :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: This was just ok, but easy to make and looked good as slices. If I make it again, I'll skip the glaze, it made it too sweet and it never set, making it really messy. Also, the top started to burn well before the loaf was done, so I would check it early and be ready to cover it with foil.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I baked it exactly as directions said.  As another said, came out very domed which is OK I guess.  It was very dry not like any other pound cake I’ve made. Used Jif Creamy peanut butter.  I actually threw the cake away. I have never thrown a baked good away before.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this according to the recipe, except that I used black cherry jam. The cake’s taste was good but it was dry. The next time I make it, I will use vegetable oil instead of butter and will add a third egg and an extra 1/4-cup of sour cream, to make it more moist. Also, I think it needs more jam. Oh, and I didn’t make the glaze because using a cup of confectioners sugar is just…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: Kids loved this! Very rich, with a glass of milk my 5 year old was very happy. Maybe not a cake to make for just adults.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: Not sure I like it. There are so many pb and jam brands that I think you can change the taste too easily based on your choices.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: The icing ruined it. And it's not icing, if you follow the directions. More like syrup. Very disappointing. My grandson said it tasted to strongly of peanut butter

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: Baked in October, 2025 while David was in Houston.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody:
This cake left me still searching for my itch-scratching PBJ cake recipe—the optional addition of chocolate feeling frankly not optional. I want to share what turned out to be a weird ideal, right on this “thread” in case anybody out there is lazy and exactly like me.

1) Make a 2-ingredient strawberry soda cake in an 8x11 pan, using strawberry Fanta leftover from the movie theater (e.g.) and a box of strawberry cake mix.

2) baked and mostly cooled, slice the soda cake in squares. It may be crackly on top with an intensely strawberry aroma and texture reminiscent of white bread. 

3) Top each portion with a small, spooned dollop of chocolate peanut butter (I used the peanut-based choc product resembling Nutella from Bonne Maman. The Jif one also looks good.) or use another frosting-like nut butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I too followed the instructions exactly, but also had more of a dome than a flat top. Took One hour 15 min in my oven for a tester to come out clean, but then the top was quite dark and the crust was thicker and harder than what I see in the pic. The interior was moist and delicious. I did add the glaze and the crunch of the chopped peanuts was a nice texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody:
This was the featured recipe in the fall baking supplement, and I found it disappointing
It was okay but not amazing
If I were to make it again, it needs more peanut butter taste, and more jam
And the pan size is wrong ~ the batter filled it all the way to the brim before baking so it overflowed

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody:
This was perfect. I used raspberry jam, but I can't think of a jam that wouldn't work here. The icing on top is great hint of sweetness.

It was so good, I had to give out slices to make sure I didn't eat the whole thing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: What happens with the 60ml of milk? It’s in the ingredients list but then completely disappears in the preparation stage. I would really like to know the answer to this riddle

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I haven't made this specific cake to say whether the milk is needed or not, but from my general cake-making knowledge - if you were adding it, it would be added at the same time as the sour cream and vanilla.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: @Jay It’s in step 4, gets mixed into the batter

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: It doesn’t say whether the cake should go in the upper, middle, or lower third of the oven. I don’t bake pound cakes very often so i opted for the middle rack which threw off my bake. My first attempt came out a little dry in places but it’s still quite tasty. Need to also mention the aroma from mixing the peanut butter and butter alone is divine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I love PB & J so I was excited to try this. Made as directed - didn’t like the taste of the glaze though, and it was way too sweet, so I didn’t use it. The cake initially looked amazing but wasn’t cooked in the middle so I had to put it back in the oven a few times. It’s tasty, but not exceptional. Toasted with some butter it’s even better!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I used an 8.5x4.5 pan, but my 1st clue should've been that the batter came almost to the top. At one hour, the top was burning so I took it out of the oven, hoping to rescue the cake by cutting into slices. When I lifted it out of the pan, the inside was still liquid and the whole thing collapsed into a runny mess. Seriously, it looked like lava running all over my counter with chunks of volcanic rock from the burned bits. I doubt I'll try again, but a bigger loaf plan would be a good start.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I loved this recipe. I was a bit worried about the glaze given some of the comments but didn’t have any issues with it and it ended up solidifying surprisingly well.

I agree though that the cake part of the pound cake isn’t quite dry but also isn’t quite the dense buttery pound cake texture I think a lot of people are probably used to. I plan to try again but replace the sugar with brown sugar and maybe at an extra egg yolk? I also think the direction of the jam spots in the center of the cake matters a lot here too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Peanut Butter and Jelly Pound Cake Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe but also noticed a slight dryness to it. Next I’ll try a slightly larger pan and more jam inside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out quite moist and cake-y after 18 minutes in a toaster oven, and tasted good. Pretty similar to blueberry muffin tops (top of the muffin to you!). Next time I would reduce the sugar and sprinkle slivered almonds on top - maybe even stir some silvered almonds into the batter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The other comment saying these are like muffin tops is absolutely right! They are soft and slightly crumbly (and even have the shape of a muffin top).
I reduced the granulated sugar to a scant 1 cup. They were still plenty sweet, especially with the glazed icing. I accidentally over poured the almond extract (a heavy spoonful) which worked in my favor I think.
I probably used ~3 tbsp worth of lemon zest (about 4 small lemons worth) in the dough and it comes through plenty still.

The recipe calls for a 1oz/2tbsp scoop but this made for 25 large-ish cookies. I think Vaughn uses a mini scoop in the video which I will probably do next time. These baked at 350 for 17 minutes. Then I left them in oven (turned off) for another 3.

They held their domed shape well on my dark cookie sheet, and spread a bit on my light cookie sheet. So just be aware!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Ingredients similar to absolutely deliciousNYT Blueberry, Almond and Lemon Cake, by Yotam Ottolenghi,
wherein finely ground almond flour was an ingredient, wondering whether folks think that could work

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wouldn't replace wheat flour with almond flour in most cookie recipes. Wheat flour absorbs some of the moisture in the wet ingredients and helps bind the cookies together. Almond flour can't do that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @RP You certainly can replace flour for almond flour. I would weigh the flour instead of volume measurements for best results. My gf blends is half almond flour, qtr buckwheat and a qtr Bobs Red Mill 1:1 gf flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these to bring to a cookout and they were a big hit. When making the glaze, I used my homemade limoncello instead of lemon juice. Not a strong boozy flavor, but it gave it a nice depth. I saved a few cookies for myself to go with coffee the following morning.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies have “Tea Party” written in that lemony icing drizzle!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The video states 325 deg while the recipe has oven temp at 350 deg fyi

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
mine spread quite a bit and we're much more purple than the ones in the video.  If you mix roughly (my mistake) the juice gets released and the dough is quite loose for cookie dough.

I chilled the second batch for 1hr, and the spread was reduced but I still would but fewer than 13 on a sheet.

tasted beautiful 🍋 🫐 (looked so so)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They taste great, but the dough is a pain to mix. Once you add the frozen blueberries, even after waiting 10 minutes it’s the consistency of frozen hard ice cream. If you wait longer for the dough to be malleable, the blueberries dye the batter light purple and you lose the nice marbling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maine blueberries are in season, could I use fresh instead of frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious! I made these exactly as written and the family loves them. In my oven, these cooked perfectly at 350 Fahrenheit for 14 minutes (did not use convection setting so rotated cookie sheets halfway through). Don’t omit the glaze!

I think these worked out so well because I weighed the flour to get just the right amount.

I understand the comment that these are like muffin tops but to me they are like blueberry scones because of all the butter!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added the frozen blueberries as per the recipe and they caused the room-temperature batter to turn ice cold.  I could not fold in the blueberries because the batter seized up. I tried putting the bowl with the batter into another, larger bowl with hot water in it.  After waiting about 20 minutes, the outer portion of the batter could be worked but the blueberries bled and turned much of the batter purple. What a mess!  Hoping they taste better than they look.  Not a well-written recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @shore cook now that the cookies are baked, and mostly eaten, I can say they turned out to be delicious, a wonderful combination of flavors and a nice texture. Such a big hit with my guests. In the future, I will either use fresh blueberries or thaw and drain frozen blueberries.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: any suggestions on adding the frozen blueberries? Batter completely seized up and could hardly mix them in. Still amazingly delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: incredibly tasty and made even more so by the addition of Meyer lemons lovingly gifted by a friend. my main complaint is that these store at room temp incredibly poorly - even very small frozen blueberries make these things very, very soggy within hours. i threw about half of my second batch (yes, they were that good) into the freezer and discovered that these make an excellent snack straight out of the freezer, with almost a mochi ice cream-like vibe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t understand the fussing in the comments. These are completely delicious, and yes, very cake-like. They may take a little longer to bake so watch them carefully.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dough is somewhat persnickety to deal with, but the taste is worth it!  The video is very helpful for these cookies - contains some great tips.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are good! Nothing crazy but an easy pantry staple “cookie” that is more reminiscent of a muffin top. I made with a gluten free flour and still turned out great. Definitely look for the light browning on edges before removing from oven and a matte surface of the dome.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m a new baker so be nice! Can I use a small hand mixer? No big kitchen aid in this home.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Bernie

Yes, absolutely you can! For future reference, you can make most recipes that use a standing mixer with a hand mixer. The exceptions are more complicated things like making bread. Just start your mixer really slowly so the ingredients don’t fly all over the place. 

I definitely recommend watching the video. Vaughan has some good cooking tips for this recipe specifically but also for baking in general.

I haven’t made this recipe yet, but it looks pretty forgiving. It will probably give you some good results for the effort. Good luck, and if you try it, please report back how it went!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Bernie Pastry chef here. Yes, absolutely. I would mix the blueberries in by hand though. Good luck!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi ~ I haven’t made these delicious sounding cookies yet, but plan to very soon.  I’m really intrigued by Lori’s comment that she used her homemade limoncello instead of lemon juice to make the glaze.  I’d love to know how much limoncello she actually used.  I presume it would be the same as the amount listed for the lemon juice (2 tablespoons) but would appreciate any feedback.  Thanks so much!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so delicious!  But…..don’t use regular frozen blueberries,  I am going to try the wild next time as the directions said.  When I added the frozen blueberries, my dough froze as well.  It was a mess but I made it work.  Because the dough was cold, it took much longer to bake as well.  But the taste was heavenly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Should you rinse the frozen blueberries?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made this and waiting for them to cool. Did not come out anything like the video. The frozen berries even though well mixed into the batter, stayed frozen and it was not easy getting the batter scooped. Ended up with batter baked in a dish. Am sure it will taste great with a cup of tea.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Should the frozen blueberries be defrosted before mixing in the dough?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these twice. They are 10/10 delicious and impressive enough to gift! But… If you don’t have a 2tbsp cookie scoop, you’ll likely end up in tears like I did!! A large measuring spoon just won’t cut it with the cold dough. On my next go at it, I will place the cookie dough rounds in the fridge for a few minutes for even puffier cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were incredibly good! Made them for a brunch party hoping to have leftovers... but most of them went. Highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were amazing, they taste so much better than they look. After reading comments about them tasting like a blueberry muffin, I thought they would be cakey and light, but I really liked how they had a richness to them and weren't too delicate. I didn't find them too sweet at all with given sugar amount. the mini size worked out great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them twice: muffin tops the first time and decadent cookies the next. For the cookie version, I refrigerated them in between and made them bigger. I had no problem mixing in the frozen blueberries after waiting a bit. The smaller blueberries were also easier, but both worked. The icing (which I normally don't like) took it to another level! Will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are the best, and even better the next day (super soft). I used salted butter (by accident) and reduced the sugar by 50g and for me, perfect amount of sweetness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t love almond extract. Controversial I know. Could I omit it? Would there be a different extract flavor that you’d suggest?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would say just leave it out, or use lemon extract or vanilla. 1/2 tsp of fluid, most of which will evaporate since it's an alcohol-based flavoring, is unlikely to make any difference in the finished cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Rhonda My daughter doesn’t like almond extract either. Just vanilla would be fine. An alternative is Fiori di Sicilia. It is a vanilla and orange combo that would be lovely with blueberry.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As previously mentioned, cookie dough came out too purple to judge the color of the cookie making it difficult to judge bake. Even after 'hydrating' for 15 minutes, dough very cold and I wonder about the 12-14 minute bake time. After 14 minutes cookies too soft but unable to gauge doneness by color. Frustrating!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you freeze them to keep longer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Carol did you try this? I’m curious too

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cooking time in the instructions is misleading. The cookies will not be light  brown or fully cooked if you only cook them for a total of 15 min. It says cook between 12-14 min switching the cookies sheet halfway. And why does it say at the top of the recipe that cooking time is 45 min? In my over which is accurate for all other recipes I needed to cook for 15 min then switch the sheets and cook another 15.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Susan Strange it took 30 minutes for your cookies to bake. Mine were done perfectly at 14 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

To preserve the lemon flavour I did have, I dropped the temp to 320F and baked for 26 minutes. They turned out a little unattractive, but that was probably my fault; the dough was quite crumbly and I didn't want to overincorporate the blueberries, but I have a better feel for it for next time. A little cakey, sweet but not too sweet. Delicious!

I skipped the glaze and sprinkled turbinado sugar on top instead and let the broiler melt down onto the top a little bit. Made it more like a muffin too in my opinion!

Definitely will add these to the rotation.

Added fresh blueberries before resting dough.
Perfect bake as cookie muffin!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These cookies were a lot of work...and totally worth it. So glad I took the time to read other reviews and had a plan to handle the blueberries.

My blueberries were large Alabama ones that I picked by hand and had frozen. To reduce risk that the batter would seize, I thawed them for 5 minutes, cut the largest berries in half, removed icy/watery residue, then measured them. Then I put them on baking sheet and returned them to the freezer so they would stay frozen until folded in.

If you let them thaw too long to where they get soft and start leaking, they will turn purple and will there will be extra water in the batter.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Big thanks to Polish Grandmothers and mothers for their creativity to keep feeding their children with what was available seasonally. I remember growing up tasting all sorts of flower based dishes like pierogi with blueberries, potato & apple pancakes, blintzes with cheese, dumplings with plums, etc. Yes, it may sound strange to some, however, strawberries with noodles can bring us great memories while our parents had to deal with all sorts of food shortages. Try it with your kids!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody:
@Andrze
I love what you’re saying here! Well, it doesn’t particularly appeal to me because I didn’t grow up with it, I completely agree with everything you’re saying

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @cocomero Fair enough, your’re absolutely right, there will be variations based on the region. However according to our future AI overseers (Chat GPT) “Makaron ze śmietanoą (sour cream/smietana)” while still popular is less iconic or traditional than "Makaron z serem (farmer’s cheese/twaróg).” And apparently the sour cream version is often savory served with mushrooms, bacon, onions or garlic. Who knew? Love how NYT brought out a lively nostalgic exchange about communist era staple. Thank you. The funny thing is being now vegan, I wouldn’t eat either.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I mean what’s not to like? Pasta, good! Strawberries, good! Sugar, good! My grandmother used to make it. My mother stills makes it. And I still love it. Polishness in a nutshell.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @sarah I literally did the same thing hahaha

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: A favourite Hungarian dish is similar; pasta with cottage cheese! It can be savoury with a sprinkle of bacon bits and chopped green onions OR sweet with fruit, jam, or a sprinkle of sugar. Comfort food!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: My Hungarian-American mom used to make this. Loved it as a kid. Cheese blintzes….reading these notes brings back a lot of childhood memories. Have to dig out those Eastern European cookbooks I have and get busy.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody:
@DFR

I also grew up eating pasta with cottage cheese when I was a kid, especially if I was home sick. Only the savory version appeals to me.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Comfort food! summer or winter. Cottage cheese slow cooked with wide egg noodles.  Our version is the border region of Lithuania - Poland -  Belarus . Then there is the Kugel version baked in a baking dish with sugar, cinnamon,  and raisons or it got really fancy with, pineapple, and maraschino cherries and about 1970 some had chocolate chips.  I went back to the traditional  raisons.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Back in the day, strawberry season was very short. We ate strawberries by the bucketful to get our fill for the year. They weren’t sold in teeny containers—vendors with huge scoops shoveled them into paper bags by the kilo. They had nothing in common with the plastic slingshot ammunition that passes for fruit today. One bowl of strawberries would perfume the entire house. They were delicious and aromatic but spoiled quickly—I suppose that’s how some of the bounty found its way into pasta.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody:
@MIK
I used to love strawberries as they were sweet and juicy but what i get in the store now has no flavor. They must pick them too early. Your connect about the how fragrant they are when ripe brought back memories of truly ripe strawberries from the farm.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: We used to go to a sandwich shop in a farmers market for decades and one of the employees there made homemade lekvar.  He would bring it into the shop in a 10 gallon drum and Eastern European women would line up with their own jars and tubs for him to fill.  Both he and the shop are long gone and the only commercial lekvar maker in the US discontinued it several years ago.  So it took me a few years to find an acceptable substitute for it.  Can't have Easter without lekvar cake!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @MIK @FancyNance Yes, grocery-store strawberries are usually not very good – bred and picked for shipping, not for flavor. Sometimes natural-food stores and co-ops will do a little better, especially if they buy locally or regionally grownproduce. If you can get to a farmers' market, or to a more rural area where there are farm stands and pick-your-own places, you might find the kind of strawberries you remember. The season is still short, but newer varieties do lengthen it a little. Good luck!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I spent a summer in Poland. How I miss the strawberry pierogies! How about a recipe for those? (Or if anyone knows where they can be purchased or ordered in NYC, do tell.)

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Berry pierogi are easy! Use any pierogi dough, and fill with a fresh, thick, not-too-sweet jam. For blueberry, I simmer frozen wild blueberries with a bit of sugar, nutmeg, and lemon zest until all of the juices have been released, then add an arrowroot (or cornstarch, or whatever thickener you prefer) slurry and cook until thick. For strawberry, defrosting frozen berries and draining the liquid (to mix w/ seltzer for a light soda) will concentrate flavors and shorten the cooking time.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @basiunka Once upon a time, there was a tiny storefront on 1st or 2nd Ave in the East Village where the lady sold all different kinds of pirogies and other assorted Polish/Ukrainian goods. I miss that place. She had the best borscht and farmer’s cheese. Try Little Poland diner.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody:
@Su
Blueberry or strawberry pierogis are amazing with just the fruit as a filling, served with sour cream mixed with a little sugar. Martha Stewart has a great pierogi dough recipe.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: MIght it taste better (and be more authentic?) with creme frâiche, instead of sour cream?

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @Austro Girl cream fraiche / sour cream in Poland is more or less the same. We use like 12% fat sour cream (śmietana) and the sourness is an additional kick

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: "more authentic"? Sour cream is ubiquitous in Polish cooking; creme fraiche, not so much.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @Michaelangelo Allocca. Virtually the same things. Fermented dairy. Differ in details( fat content), fat flavor profile quite similar.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Growing up in Poland, this dish was only made with farmer’s cheese (twaróg). Using sour cream or yogurt changes the consistency and personally, it’s seem sacrilegious. Also, the pasta can be browned in butter on high heat after it’s boiled to add some color and crunch. Use blueberries if out of strawberries. Sprinkler with desired amount of sugar to finish the dish.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Growing up in *your* part of Poland, maybe. There is a huge variety in Polish cuisine, and the way you grew up with is not necessarily the 'right,' never mind the 'only' way. My babcia made it like this.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: My Grandmother had a hard time finding farmers cheese like what she was used to "at home" when she came to America and settled in Philadelphia back in 1914.  After many experiments she ended up mixing ricotta and cottage cheese together and sometimes added cream cheese depending on what she was making.  Most people who immigrated to the US had to do the same thing.   I still make her Easter cheese and lekvar cake every year.  I am lazy and buy my pierogi at the Ukrainian supermarket near me.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: This is a classic way to make strawberries go further! My (English) mother was not allowed to eat ice cream without the accompanying bread and butter!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Iga Swiatek influenced my household and it paid off. Thank you NYT for the recipe!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I’ve lived in U.S. for over 25 years now. I make it to my 5-year old son. It always brings good memories of summer time. And, yes I made blueberry filled pierogi the other night! With a dollop of whipped cream and a sprinkle of sugar (a-must!).

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Is this eaten as a main course or as a dessert?

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @Curious On a hot, summer day as a lunch/ main course. You can serve it with chilled yoghurt.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Thank you, Iga! I'd never heard of this but as others have said, what's not to like?

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: why not peaches?

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Made it with my homemade crème fraîche, since I had it on hand, topped with some chopped fresh mint. Absolutely wonderful, very moreish!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: My Hungarian exchange student assured me that fruit on pasta was very common for her. Glad to finally see a recipe.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I remember mashed strawberries and sour cream like this served over store bought cheese blintzes in summer. My grandmother still made her own flat egg noodles until the 1950s when age and full grocery stores in America won over.  The spiral noodles look pretty in the photo but to me it is the wrong texture. We ate summer vegetables with sour cream for dinner. Cold fruit soup in summer and warm fruit soup in winter. My father told me that fruit that was getting too soft was used for fruit soup.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: My grandmom made her own egg noodles as well- her kitchen table was always covered with tea towels with noodles drying on top.  She also made pierogi and would keep bags of frozen pierogi on hand so that anyone who visited her left with a bag of her homemade pierogi.  She was still doing so in her 90's- her only concession to getting older was to hand me her stylus one Easter and tell me that I had to take over making the pysanky since her hand was no longer steady enough.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: My Polish mother made wide noodles with farmer cheese or cottage cheese and added a sprinkle of sugar and cinnamon.. adding fruit would be a bonus! Still love this comfort food.. my kids, not too much.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Love this fruit/pasta recipe and comments of others whose families made so many fruit dishes. My Slovak Nana used to make plum pirohy (similar to pierogies but slightly different) during plum season. She rolled out a big sheet of delicate dough, placed plums in sequential rows,  then sliced the dough horizontally to fold over each row of plums to bake. Once done, the priohy rows were  cut into individual puffy plum squares.  Fruit heaven in our mouth, right from the oven!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Loved reading all the comments.  My maternal grandparents home villages are now in southwest Ukraine- very close to the borders of Poland, Hungary, Romania and Slovakia.  So my grandmom's recipes were a mix from all those places.  My husband's father came from Poland but his grandfather was of Lithuanian and Belarus ancestry.  We have a lot of discussions about the correct way to make things most of which boil down to "My Grandmom (or Mom) made it like this."

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Reminded me of a typical milchig, dairy, dinner during any hot summer day.  We didn't keep kosher but often ate "dairy" because it was the right thing for the weather.  Noodles and cottage cheese or sour cream with mixed chopped veggies would be a typical part of the meal.  Cold borscht with potatoes and some cold stewed fruit might round out the meal.  We are a typical mish mash of Polish, Romanian and Ukrainian ancestors so who knows where the cuisine originated for us.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I topped this with fresh basil and every delicious bite was like summer.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody:
Now this traditional food combination, isn't any odder then a wedge of the sharpest cheddar cheese with a big slice of apple pie. 

Which I grew up eating and know people who never heard of it and cringe when you mention.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: @Heidi I love warm apple pie with a slice of good sharp cheddar. Grew up eating it that way.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Why not peaches? I think the point is to use any fresh seasonal fruit.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: My Dad's German mother used to make a dish called schmarrn which is a German pancake sliced or chopped up, sauteed in butter with fruits and nuts then sprinkled with sugar.  He never knew the recipe- just remembered the name and how much he loved it- and was thrilled when my German boyfriend (now husband) knew the dish and made it for my Dad.  I like mine with raisins, Dad liked sliced fresh peaches and my husband likes to top his with applesauce.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody:
This inspired me to make a strawberry lasagna.  Used double everything except the noodles, used 12 oz of cool, al dente lasagna noodles, layered with the berry-(coconut based)yogurt-sugar mixture, layer of lemon snaps (think a la Nilla wafers on banana pudding), repeat that twice, then top layer (third) of noodles smothered with yogurt mixture and all of the fresh berries (2 cups).  Leave in fridge overnight to set.  We loved it!  Lemon snaps soak up the liquid stratified from the mushed berries-yogurt layer nicely.

Might try the traditional way next time…

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: With all due respect, this was just weird.  Perhaps I should have cooked the pasta into oblivion, but I don't think it would have mattered.  After a few bites, I tossed the pasta and ate the fruit.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Polish makaron is usually made as an egg noodle, and I really recommend that flavor/texture for this dish! In my home, the length of noodle never mattered, and we frequently had strawberry noodles with long wide pasta (think tagliatelle). As others have commented, if you can get twarog (farmer's cheese) that isn't salty, it's an excellent addition.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: This is delicious but my wife’s favorite growing up was Strawberry blended with yogurt poured over rice

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I am Czech born but living in the US for a long time. We have a lunch dish of very ripe strawberries mashed with sugar and some cream cheese to form a strawberry “soup”. We eat that with some type of sweet pastry such as a plain croissant. I could maybe see egg noodles with this but not the type of pasta shown in the photo.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Reading thru the comments, it reminds me that my dad, whose grandparents came to Ohio from Poland, used to mix mashed potatoes with cottage cheese. I liked it as a kid.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: My father whose parents came to the US from ethnic German villages in what are now Romania and Serbia (what was called The Sudentenland" in my WW2 history lessons) used to put butter, sugar and cinnamon on cooked rice, butter and cinnamon sugar on white bread and apple butter and peaches on top of cottage cheese.  We kids thought he was a genius!  Mashed potatoes mixed with cottage cheese and cheddar cheese sounds pretty close to my Ruthenian grandmom's pierogi filling.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Sounds ok to me. Assume it’s served & eaten at room temperature?

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: No need to assume; just read Step 2: "Drain the pasta and briefly rinse with cool water. Set aside to cool slightly."

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I'm first generation polish (mom's from outside Warszawa and Dad's from Wrocław) and they made this all the time for us BUT WITH RICE NOT PASTA!!! Pasta is just...well, no. *shakes head*

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I’m made a single serving for breakfast with Siggis yogurt and vesuvio pasta with the last of a pound of strawberries. Very enjoyable! Give it a try! I probably won’t make it often because I’m in the boost your protein & fiber phase of life.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: She just won Wimbledon so this certainly didn’t hurt!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I get some initial cultural resistance for the less adventurous, sort of, but as a midwestern American, I very quickly thought of strawberry shortcake: Strawberries, macerated with sugar, cream and starchy (often sourdoughy) cake.  That is without a doubt my favorite cake of all.  So, I didn't find it too much of a stretch to get some tartness from sour cream and to alternate a different starchy carb.  I will be trying this in the very near future!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: As others pointed out, this childhood delight has many variations. In my house it was pasta with farmer’s cheese, sour cream, strawberries (fresh or homemade canned strawberries) and sugar. You would kind of mash the strawberries with the cheese and sugar which became almost like a thick sauce. Yum!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. I followed the recipe exactly using. Avoid Greek yogurt and it turned out perfectly.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Made with full fat creamy yogurt and fresh pasta, and delicious strawberries. Easy recipe. But once was enough! Neither dessert nor main course. Perhaps if I had grown up with it...

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Never would have thought of this in a million years, but it checks all the boxes for me and I found it delightful. Used whole wheat shells, whole-milk yogurt, and lovely fresh strawberries. Added only about a teaspoon and a half of sugar, just enough to get the strawberries to macerate but not oversweeten them. @Sandra the addition of basil was inspired. *chef's kiss*

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: This pasta is amazing in summer! Kids loved it. At first I was a little reculent to try it but it tasted great! After all it is two amazing things, Pasta and Strawberries!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Does it need to be whole milk yogurt? Will nonfat work?

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Somewhat related... I'd only had savory pierogies until my Ukrainian friends introduced me to blueberry and strawberry filled ones. Same idea!

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I used sour cream, but my sauce turned out runny: next time, I will use either Fage sour cream or Fage full-fat Greek yogurt.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: As a kid in NYC 1970s we’d eat broad noodles with sour cream and cottage cheese. I think maybe a little sugar, too. I think mostly in the warm months.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: We ate it with noodles, not italian pasta, and with sour cream.

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: Substitute yogurt for sour cream

author: Kasia Pilat
commentBody: I thought it was fine. My toddler said "too sour" and my pregnant wife said "weird."

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m 11 years old and just baked this recipe as an activity for a week without school. Unenlightened of the brownie’s incredible taste, I was bewildered by the perfect combination of these three types of chocolate. Surprisingly as well, it was not difficult to bake and didn’t matter that I didn’t add the espresso powder. Melting the chocolate was the hardest part for me, as I took it off the stove too early and left much sticking to the saucepan after I poured it. This recipe’s for sure a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Roland Hurrah for you! And you’ve convinced me to  give this decadence a try.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Roland Thank you for this great review, love to have a young person’s perspective. Yours in baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Roland your comment is so cool that one of my favourite bands, They Might Be Giants, shared it on their Facebook page!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If there is white chocolate, milk chocolate, baking chocolate and cocoa these are 4 chocolate brownies. Ha, think you can pull the wool over our eyes?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Rebecca white chocolate is not really chocolate. There’s no cocoa in it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kim actually white chocolate should have a good amount of cocoa butter in it. I know what you mean because the lack of cocoa liquor however real white chocolate tastes completely different than a lot of stuff that is sold as white chocolate. You have to check the label. I like to carmelize my white chocolate in an instant pot using ball jars for 80 minutes. it will be very stiff but add some more white chocolate and keep stirring it until it's the consistency of melted chocolate. Add sea salt or flaky Maldon. I promise it will change your perspective on white chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used entirely white sugar, mixed the eggs after the chocolate, omitted the espresso and cocoa powders, and omitted the mix-in chocolate. Now it’s an easy brownies recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MarkB if you watch the video at NYTimescooking on YouTube, you’ll see that he starts with a very basic recipe and makes several variations and tweaks to come up with this recipe. What you came up with is pretty much what he started with.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MarkB I appreciate this deep cut. Well played, sir.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MarkB kinda whats the point then? A recipe is written for an end purpose in mind. I cheer your spirit of experimenting but perhaps try with no recipe to follow next time? Wing it! It’s fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious!  I made them with a gluten free flour blend (Bob's Red Mill) and they came out beautifully.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Elizabeth Uppenbrink

Hi Elizabeth.  Do the gluten free brownies dry out after a day or so?  Hah!  Maybe they didn’t last that long in your household.  At any rate, thats been my experience when using gluten free flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lovvvve this. Used the gram measurements as I'm from Australia, but turned out perfect. I did a ratio 75% white chocolate and 25% milk for mine. So good served with cream and tart berries to balance sweetness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe, very rich and chocolate-y in flavor. One warning - I did the mistake of cutting back on the sugar and they turned out a bit dense.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this but replaced the white chocolate w semisweet chips. Had to use a stand mixer not a hand mixer and I had a glass pan not aluminum. All that said, these were sooooo good! They are much, much  better after sitting awhile, so wait to eat them until the next  day. Be patient, you will be rewarded!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @M H S  I suggest you eat 1/2 the day you make them - and the other 1/2 the next day - it’s like a win / win - for those of us (me) that cant wait!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I maintain that white "chocolate" isn't chocolate so calling these Triple Chocolate is a serious misnomer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hear, hear! If I make these, there will be no white chocolate -- it only adds fat and sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @mp lee I agree and will sub with dark chocolate!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Nancy P.  I feel like running into the kitchen to make these.  I absolutely love white chocolate!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made this for valentines and loved by all. Stuck to the recipe and will be making it again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: But where are the nuts, which are essential for any brownies I'll eat?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've developed similar recipe: 3oz. unsweetened chocolate, 1 stick butter, 3/4 cup white sugar, 1/4 cup brown sugar, 2 eggs, 1 tsp. good vanilla, 1/4 tsp. kosher salt, 1/8 cup Dutch cocoa powder, 1/2 cup + 1/8 cup AP flour, 1/4 cup dark chocolate chips, 1/4 cup milk or white chocolate chips (also good w/pnut butter chips). Mix all using a wooden spoon in the same pan you've melted chocolate and butter. No need to use a mixer or more bowls; just the 8"x8" buttered baking pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Although the brownies had great texture and a glossy top, this recipe was too sweet for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Two types of sugar, three (or four...) different types of chocolate....for the life of me, I cannot understand how these could be too sweet...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Amy Bailey
Did you read the recipe 🤷🏻‍♂️

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out amazing! After watching the initial YouTube video, I came straight to the app to find them and ended up baking these yesterday. Absolutely delicious

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I plan to make these but sub dark chocolate for the milk. I'm curious, why is a handheld mixer indicated over a stand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jen I suspect so you don’t over-beat them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not use a stand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In the recipe it calls for flaky salt, but that can be easily substituted for normal salt or, if you’re feeling wild, sliced almonds or another small nut.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These look like perfect February pick me up! Has anyone tried this in a 10x10 yet?  It’s either that or a 10” cast iron. Welcome suggested modifications for either. Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cast iron will hold heat longer so you will probably need to reduce the baking time.  It will also likely result in crispier edges.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is pretty much Tasty's Best Fudge Brownie recipe.  Bravo on the subtle changes to make it your own.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These need more than 27 minutes in the oven 
- the top says 40. Mine have been in for 25 and need at least another 15. They are still very liquidish.

.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are decadent. My take was to reduce white chocolate and milk chocolate and sprinkle in some semi sweet to balance the super duper sweetness of the milk and white. It is hard to go wrong here, really. 
I cheated and used Guittard chips for the bittersweet, milk, and semi-sweet chocolate and Valrhona white chunks. 
I will make these again 100%, plus quite easy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Heather G why use the recipe in the first place if you’re making so many changes to it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't usually like my sweets too sweet (lol) but I found these are not too sweet with the milk chocolate. The flaky salt balances it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These brownies will have one questioning their life’s choices. As in, “Will doing this thing keep me from eating these brownies?” Because if the answer is “yes,” then I want no part of it. Made exactly as written. Atheist, but declaring Vaughn a god. Thank you, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: notes are always absurd... but these three take the cake... (brownie)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too sweet (but also oddly salty--why did we start ruining desserts by making them salty too???) for my money, but the texture was nice. Next time will ditch the milk chocolate chunks, use unsweetened instead of bittersweet for the batter, and maybe 1/2 tsp salt. Which is a different recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As a "real chocolate lover" I have to say, white chocolate isn't chocolate. I'm going to try this without defiling it with that stuff, I'll replace it with some actual chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone baked these in a cast iron skillet?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were straightforward to make as directed, except I didn’t have white chocolate, so I subbed in 3 oz semisweet instead. Baked in a glass square pan, took 8 extra minutes. My husband says brownies don’t get any better than this. I think he might be right.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: what happens if I bang the baking dish and no cracking happens? I wasn't sure if I should be baking it a bit longer and trying the bang again or just keep on baking? Well I did a few minutes more and tried banging again and it was a fail. But the brownies were terrific. No one complained I gotta say.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I completely agree with some of the comments saying that it never fully set, when banging it on the counter no deflation or cracking actually occurred, and the taste is a bit odd. author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
After watching Vaughn’s video a few times I finally bake these today. I made a couple of variations, in case they are helpful for someone:

- Instead of espresso powder I made an espresso coffee and used 3 teaspoons of it. 
- For the bittersweet chocolate I used a mix of 50% dark chocolate (30gr) and 95% (75gr).
- I also reduced the sugars: 95gr granulated sugar and 100gr brown sugar.
- For the cocoa powder I used a 100% one (42gr). 
- Also reduced the milk (35gr) and white (50gr) chocolates.

The result? Just incredible, the best brownie I’ve ever tired, very moist, fudgy and so chocolaty. Still very sweet despite the reduced sugars. 

As a side note, if your kitchen is cold move fast, the butter solidifies quickly and it’s harder to fold in the flour and cocoa powder and it won’t be easy to even the batter in the pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What is so special about Diamond Crystal?? Morton not good enough?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Diamond and Morton use different processes in creating crystals — this affects texture and perceived saltiness. Diamond grows theirs in open brine creating delicate crystals that are light in flavor because their pyramid shape allows more air between each crystal. Morton's crystals are larger and taste saltier because they form crystals w/vacuum-evaporation then flatten them under rollers, making a more dense shape that's about 25% saltier that Diamond. Use either, but be careful w/measurements.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Mimi Runs I believe the question is more sbout why the Times is fairly recently trending towards recommending the more expensive, and less prevalent Diamond Crystal kosher salt. It makes substitutions trickier.

Yes. Use half the table salt (like the store brand) as a substitute for Diamond Crystal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Regardless of brand, it will be labeled either "kosher", "iodized", or "table" salt.  Iodized and table are pretty much the same thing, and much saltier than kosher, so you'll want to cut the salt way down.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Further clarification on the Diamond Crystal vs. Morton's KOSHER salt. (Thank you, @Mimi Runs with Scissors for your excellent explanation of the two types of KOSHER salt!) Morton also makes standard TABLE salt. Do not confuse the two. This recipe explicitly states KOSHER salt. If you use Morton's, be sure you are using KOSHER salt Morton's, not TABLE salt Morton's. In this, and any, recipe that specifically states KOSHER salt, the difference is critical to a successful outcome.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Susan
There is still a difference between the Morten Kosher and the Diamond Kosher.  I have seen recommendations that look like:
2 Tbl Diamond Kosher substituted by
1 1/2 Tbl Morton Kosher
OR 1 Tbl table salt.

I have heard that you don’t want to use Iodized salt for pickling vegetables or making cheese. It interferes with fermentation and growth of desired bacteria.  I think cheese-makers use an even finer grind of salt that tastes even saltier if it strays into your cooking! Some hard cheeses are aged or cured for years in this fine salt to further draw out moisture. Cured cheeses tend to be a salty food.

Using Diamond, your recipe is delicately salted. I think it’s more common on the East Coast than on the West Coast of the USA. Its original purpose was to gently salt fresh meat, just enough to draw all the blood out (a.k.a. “Koshering”) without making it taste too salty.

commentBody: I used basic off-the-shelf wonder-type bread, and despite being several weeks old it wasn't really stale.  The resulting texture was very off-putting. Also, it was swimming in butter and oil from the cheese when I served it, so I would recommend buttering the bread sparingly.

commentBody: Any cheese dish requires the addition of either Dijon mustard and / or hot pepper sauce to truly shine. Add some tapatio, sriracha, Valentina’s, Tabasco… doesn’t matter. The vinegar and tang of the mustard are going to accentuate the cheese flavor. It will also help the flavor cut through the taste dampener that is milk.

I used a deep dish ceramic pie pan and that worked well for baking. The cheddar develops a more sophisticated flavor in this recipe, you'd think it was something more exotic. Great recipe for when you're nearly out of groceries.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are great, made exactly as described. But I think 40 g cookies are too much. The math suggests 18 cookies will be more like 32 grams each. (the butter and sliced almonds don't go into the cookies before you size them. With 40 gram mounds (weighed individually) I got about 14 rather large cookies. author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious — very complex in both texture and flavors.  These are amazing with the recipe exactly as written.  Don’t skimp on the rum butter.  I’m GF so this was a very welcome contribution to cookie week 2024. 

I used the 40g/scoop guideline and the recipe yielded 16 cookies.  I would make the cookies smaller next time and aim more for 18-20.  The challenge if the cookie is on the bigger side is that the almonds become toasted and are ready before the cookie is fully cooked inside.  I put the oven temperature down towards the end to cook the cookies more while saving the almonds.  It’s not fatal by any means even if the cookie is a bit undercooked in the middle - it’s still great, but the texture is not as uniform and pillowy as it could have been.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe calls for brushing the rum butter over the unbaked cookies, then using the same rum butter to brush on the baked cookies. That seems a bit off to use the same pot of rum butter before and after baking. Anyone else have concerns? Not trying to be negative here, and maybe I'm being too worried about raw ingredients "contaminating" the baked cookie. Maybe I'll split the rum butter into two portions and use one for the raw cookies and one for the baked cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made the same comment above! I would definitely separate the butter into two containers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these this weekend and they were enjoyed by my book club.  A few pointers:  I used the only scoop I have and they weighed about 23 g per cookie which was plenty big enough.  I baked them the full time and even added a few minutes to get the almonds to toast but it caused the bottoms to get over browned and the very centers were just slightly underbaked to my taste.  They were still delicious, to be clear!  I also had extra rum butter even after dipping my scoop in it before making the balls (both to prevent sticking and to give the almonds something to cling to) and brushing them after baking.  I agree that the rum flavor could be more pronounced so I’d either use more rum in the dough or use some fat washed rum butter in the cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sara I have the exact same problem. The bottoms got overdone and the inside was still slightly raw. And the almond slivers were not toasted properly until I had them in the oven for about 24 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these with golden rum, as that's what we had, and used twice the amount called for, yet there was almost no rum flavor at all.  Still, no great loss, as the texture and flavor were big hits.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone know the purpose of brushing the parchment with rum butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @kirs10m since there is no butter in the dough itself, greasing the parchment ensures a nice golden brown cookie bottom. It might be the most delicious part IMO!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not sure what went wrong but totally tasteless - feel like there are some ingredients missing. Heavy dense and tasteless.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The second batch I made of these cookies, I didn't brush the rum butter on the parchment first and they turned out way better, not as burnt on the bottom. I also refrigerated my dough overnight and that made a big difference for the better as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Whipzi brand eggnog-flavored powdered sugar and even increased the rum, and added rum extract. The rum flavoring was still very faint. Would make smaller cookies next time. I liked that no mixer was needed. I was imagining an Italian lime Pignoli cookie but the flavoring was too mellow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My husband heard me yell from the kitchen and was alarmed. "Everything is fine . . . I just took a bit of these rum buttered almond cookies. OMG"

These are deliciously decadent. I made them smaller and dipped them in the butter rum before dipping the tops in the almond. (because they were smaller, I didn't want too much crunch).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was super excited to try this, especially based on the five star reviews and they came out ok but I struggled with a few things: 1). why a half a stick of butter to brush 18 cookies??  I wasted over a half a stick??  2) there isn't really a rum taste so that either needs to be scrapped or upped if you want that flavor.  3). how did anyone get the almonds to stick? 4) I needed to add another egg as there wasn't enough moisture to bind the cookies with two egg whites and one egg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies were like warm buttery marzipan with a hint of rum. I added orange zest in the almond flour and a tbsp of fresh orange juice to the egg mixture before mixing it to the almond flour mixture to make the dough. Also, added dried fruit bits to part of the dough and mixed a dash of rose water to the rum butter that I used to brush the cookies. The variations worked well, though were not necessarily required.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
You will laugh but I hadn’t read to separate the rum butter and brush the cookies….so
Into the batter it went! I mean I haven’t tried them as written, but my mistake produced an amazing cookie- everyone loved them! lol I’m about to make them again (1 week later as I’ve had requests) and will make the same…er…mistake again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did the same! And searched here for hope I wouldn’t have to start over. Thanks for sharing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly as written and these came out perfectly. I used an ice-cream scoop and it made 14 cookies. Delicious, easy, and a nice change from the usual fare.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Prepared exactly as directed ( with a few extra teaspoons of rum for good measure) and they were delicious. Doubled up on the recipe and took 24 of them to a cookie exchange. I only left with one. : )

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the batter for these, but mine seems too wet to be rolled into balls. Does anyone have comments about this or suggestions? I haven’t baked them yet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After making these twice, and hating the consistency (gritty) and unappealing color (light brown), I realized that even though I was using “super fine” almond flour, it included the skins (Red Mills brand). The third batch was made with pale yellow almond flour with no skins and it made a huge difference. The inside was pillowy and soft, and the color was beautifully golden. Otherwise, I followed the recipe almost exactly. Used one extra tablespoon of rum and only half of a stick of butter. Absolutely delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sobernaut here also wondering if rum extract would work (no, extract is not alcohol and no, I would not like to use orange juice). While I know eating one (or 5) of these cookies is not the equivalent of drinking, I'd rather not go and purchase a bottle to only two use two tablespoons.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Nicki buy one of those small bottles behind the cash register.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Nick You could buy a single serving size bottle (like those served on airplanes) of rum at the liquor store if size is your only concern.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: According to our sober living friends, extract contains enough alcohol to trigger some folks in recovery. I use emulsions instead, which are water-based. You can find rum emulsion as well as many other flavors online and in stores such as Michaels.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wanted to have a vegan offering this Christmas. Made some modifications with Bob's Red Mill egg replacer and Miyoko's butter. Followed all steps as directed and turned out delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Ohlala! I made this for the my office mates and neighbors. I've only tried one and found it yummy. It's not the type of cookie I usually venture in making, however, the video of  Vaughn making them and his energy inspired me so much I knew I just had to make them. And, I'm thrilled I did. 

Using a cookie scoop I was able to get about 14-15 cookies. I may make a second batch. I'm not sure yet. 

I definitely recommend this cookie for baker and eating satisfaction.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies surprised me and were wickedly delicious, a double bonus, because they were also easy to make.  I used spiced rum instead of dark and Captain approved.  I’ll be taking a batch of these gems to the Jets game on Sunday, and they’ll be the highlight for sure.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone know what the tip on the almonds is? It’s noted in the ingredients, but I could not find it or see it on the video. Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Here is the tip:
Be sure to use blanched super-fine almond flour for the optimal result; natural almond flour contains skins that lead to a drier, gritty texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Gran Where dies one get this type of flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ms. Bay - Costco has a good Almond flour made with blanched almonds. I haven't tried this recipe yet, but think it will work well for it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A perfect holiday cookie. Almost too easy to make. Definitely doubling the recipe next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these as written except I made a smaller ball. Still cooked 18-20 minutes. I couldn’t taste the good rum flavor I expected. They are good almond cookies but not rum balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My son found these “stressful” to bake together (age 6) but the end result was well worth. Light, fluffy and crispy edged.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I adapted these cookies to be VEGAN and they turned out absolutely delicious! I substituted plant-based butter (I used Country Crock) 1-for-1 for regular butter. For the eggs, I used 3 oz of aquafaba plus an additional tablespoon of plant-based butter to add extra fat. The extra butter might have been overkill, but the cookies came out rich, chewy, and indulgent. I served them at a Christmas party with several vegan guests, and they were a huge hit with everyone—vegan or not.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wondering how they freeze.  They sound amazing, but need them to keep for a few weeks...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe absolutely does not make 18 cookies. I weighed each ball to the recommended 40 g and there were only 15. 18 is few enough. 15 wasn’t worth the expense and time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A few notes: I doubled the recipe and weighed out 40g but only got 29 cookies total. Also had WAY too much butter left over. My cookies didn't spread at all so by the last couple of batches I was only putting about an inch between them. Didn't have rum on hand so I swapped it for amaretto - it worked just fine. The sugar on the parchment is a bad idea IMO - the first batch charred on the bottom before any of the almonds on top toasted. Really tasty cookies though, will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added to batter:  1/2 tsp. cinnamon; 1/4 tsp. nutmeg; To butter mixture added: 1/4 tsp. nutmeg' 1/4 tsp. cinnamon.  Flavor was a lot closer to hot buttered rum.  Loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with Amaretto instead of rum, and they are delicious! I also took the advice of some other reviewers and 1) used only a half stick of melted butter, and it was just the right amount, and 2) I used just 1 slightly rounded tablespoon of dough for each cookie. Even though they were smaller than the recipe called for, they still needed 20 minutes to bake. The recipe yielded 27 cookies. I really like that they are not too sweet, as many cookies recipes are.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1 six-ounce bag of sliced almonds is plenty. Use slightly less than 2Tbsps of dough per ball for 18 cookies. I got 16.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn't need all that butter mix. Easily can cut in half. Would make them smaller (1T vs the suggested 2T balls) and cook shorter so they don't burn on the bottom. Prep time is longer than listed

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It says see tips beside the sliced almonds ingredient. What’s the tip??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic ... with modifications. Easy to make though takes a lot longer than 5 min prep. My adjustments: 26 grams per cookie. Perfect size because they are dense little bombs. I halved the butter & doubled the rum in the glaze. Not too boozy. Also, skipped the step of brushing the parchment with the syrup. A huge hit with all the cookie lovers in my life! P.S. The uncooked dough balls freeze beautifully (don't douse with syrup until baking) AND the baked cookies also freeze great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are DELICIOUS. That is all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these today. Based on some other comments here, I made a few changes, though I usually go with a recipe as-is first time round. I browned my butter for more flavor and added vanilla extract as well. I used 4 tablespoons of rum because everyone remarked about the lack of rum flavor. I used a 1.5 tablespoon scoop and got 22 cookies.
The bottoms got a bit dark but they don't taste burned. I like them. The flavor is a bit subtle, but really quite pleasant. I may make them smaller next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the consistency to be a bit too sticky. Also, the amount of butter is excessive for its intended use.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and was really excited for these cookies! I expected them to be a touch sweeter and more flavorful, but to my taste, they ended up kind of bland. I would be curious to know the sweeter version of the recipe Vaughn mentions in the video. The texture of the cookie with the almonds is nice. If you separate them into cookies that are 40 grams each, the recipe makes about 15. I may try these again before the holiday, but will need to think on how make them a bit sweeter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would it not be a cross-contamination to brush the baked cookies with the same butter that touched the batter with the raw egg in it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Marianna I just made these and, to address the cross-contamination, divided the butter/rum in half, used one half before the cookies baked, then used the other half (with a clean brush) after baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is my first pick of this year’s cookie week’s cookie and I love it! The only sub I made is the almond extract - I used vanilla extract instead, as I realized I didn’t have any and don’t care for it anyways. One challenge in the process is un-lumping the flour sugar. Both were pretty lumpy, especially the powdered sugar with tiny lumps, so I ended up trying to sift them but it wasn’t easy (as I imagine almond flour granules are larger than wheat?) and ended up half-assing the sifting/un-lumping. Glad it turned out well regardless. If I make this again I’d sift the sugar first and whisk in the flour. I froze a small batch after brushing w rum butter and hope that turns out well too. Thanks Vaughn, this cookie is as fabulous as you <3

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with all purpose flour because I didn't have almond flour. The dough was definitely dry so I added some of the rum butter and about a teaspoon of water to the dough. Also doubled the almond extract. They just came out of the oven and are pretty good nonetheless if anyone is wondering whether all purpose flour will work.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These freeze well for anyone who is wondering. Go ahead and make ahead!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Incredibly delicious and easy. I love nutty and boozy cookies and I really love not needing to use my stand mixer. This hits all the spots. Admittedly they don't really taste of bourbon once baked. They aren't overly sweet and have the best texture. One tip I'd have is to do the melted butter mixture once all the dough balls have been formed and rolled into almond slices because it has a tendency to firm up too much which can make brushing it on the cookie balls a bit difficult.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just doubled recipe and it made 48 nice sized cookies. Made no changes for a win! Delicious and billowy! Hoping they freeze well.  PS. the extra leftover yolks prompted a dinner change to Eggs Benedict with homemade Hollandaise!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these cookies and they are delicious! I found the batter too sticky so after struggling with getting the first tray from my sticky fingers onto the pan, I put the bowl in the fridge and waited 20  mins to form the cookies on the second tray. Agreed that the rum taste is subtle more of an enhanced almond cookie flavor. Will definitely make this again and I loved watching Vaughn in the kitchen in the video! The cookies are like a warm hug! 🥰

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookie, but I recommend skipping the optional sea salt on top. I used so little, but the cookies came out too salty. (And I'm a salt lover.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy and delicious!  I used some amaretto I had along with the rum. You can add so many flavors to this recipe if you’d like. Texture is nice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thanks for offering a delicious gluten free cookie option! Flavors were best on day 2 and 3. 1/2c butter was excessive; only needed 1/4c. Rum flavor was light. Would increase the 2T of rum in butter (even if you're halving to 1/4c). I like Mary Carol Blauser's rec to make the cookies smaller, maybe 20-30grams.  Next time I'll try a mix of sliced and chopped almonds for the coating. The chopped will coat the dough more and give a nice texture, while the sliced offers visual appeal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were easy to make and pretty but lacked flavor, in my opinion. I increased the rum quantity in the butter and cookie but still found it lacking. It is not a sweet cookie, so a nice addition in a cookie box and worth a try. I will probably give these cookies another go and make more tweaks to yield more flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree with some other reviewers. These are too big, rather too salty and do not taste of rum. They look great though. I’m going back to my favorite macaroon recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very quick and easy to make.  Recommendation would be to use only1/2 stick of butter and add a little more rum to both the batter and rub.  Not super sweet, which is perfect.  Instead of rolling, I  kind of plopped and rolled.  Made scoops 1 tablespoon instead of 2 as suggested.   Be sure to use fine Kosher salt and not coarse.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow, these were the winner amongst our Christmas cookies this year! I substituted bourbon for rum as that's what we had on hand. Based on another comment, I made only half of the butter mixture which seemed to be just the right amount.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these! Def chill the batter, it’s quite sticky but otherwise, delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these this morning following recipe and video exactly. Wow. Doesn't disappoint.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these last night and am  very happy with the results. I made a two adjustments. I made them much smaller, using a one tablespoon scoop, and so I baked them for 18 minutes, rather than 20-22 minutes. I also only put the rum butter mixture under each cookie, not over the entire surface of the cookie sheet. (Why waste all that rum???) Next time, I will put the rum butter mixture into a squeeze bottle, which I think will be a little more efficient than the brush. But I will make these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious but I would skip the (optional) flaky sea salt next time. They’re pretty salty already. Might even need a bit more sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are wonderful! I followed the recipe exactly, except added a 1/2 tsp vanilla per recommendations in the Cooking Notes. I'm mystified by the people who report failure, as the recipe is easy and the results are a family hit. FYI I was a bit concerned my under-age son might reject them for the rum, but in reality there's no alcoholic taste and despite 3 applications, the rum flavor is subtle. It's like a delicate tasting note that enhances the dominant almond flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Brushing the rum-butter mixture resulted in burned bottoms; brushing on the top didn't result in much rum flavor. I rolled the dough balls in a mixture of powdered sugar and nuts, but even so it was very sticky. Nevertheless, the cookies were a hit. Next time I might add 1/2 C of rice flour or gluten-free flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious and simple. I've baked  exactly as directed three times and now make the following minor adjustments:
1) a 2 tablespoon cookie is a bit too large so I use a 1 tablespoon scoop and find that a better portion size
2) the brushing with rum-butter after baking seems a bit parsimonious, wanting more of a boozy kick I tend to be rather heavy handed at this stage, really more a dousing than a brushing
3) increase the almond extract to suit your marzipan desires

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out absolutely delicious! I intentionally made the cookies smaller and baked them for nearly 30 minutes to achieve a perfectly well-baked texture, just the way I like them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for a Memorial Day BBQ where the host is GF. So good! My value add here is that I used rum extract instead of rum and it worked perfectly. I went light on it because extract can be strong and used a couple of teaspoons. It's great. It could use another teaspoon but I wouldn't do like for like with rum and rum extract -- could be too powerful and take away from the delicate almonds. Also I noticed too late I didn't have sliced almonds so minced up walnuts and it's great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are AMAZING  cookies!!!!! The rum butter sauce is way too much for 18 cookies. My choice was to freeze the remainder, use it to butter my morning toast, or continue to paint on the rum-butter. I chose the last option (placing cookies back in the oven for a minute or two after each of the four coats it took). Than you Vaughn Vreeland for sharing your cookie recipe and for your quite amusing video.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is one of the best NYT christmas cookies I made ever! I'm not the best baker but felt like the recipe forgives a lot. Cookies came out with golden almonds and a perfectly soft center that becomes almost marzipan-like in texture. I didn't want to buy a new bottle of rum so used a mixture of amaretto and Cointreau, also added some orange zest to the batter. Super tasty, will make these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My advice: don’t waste your precious time and resources on this recipe. Followed directions to the letter along with input from several “helpful” comments. Result: a bland, tasteless, mushy mess.  Advice: get a copy of the King Arthur cookie  cookbook; Every recipe in there works great. I for one have had enough of uneven outcomes from NYT cookie recipes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these as the “experiment” cookie, which is an long-standing feature of my holiday cookie boxes. The recipients gave them a definite thumbs up! 

A couple of observations. First, the dough is extremely sticky. I lightly wet my hands after rolling every two or three balls, which ensured it didn’t stick.

Also, 40 grams each is way too much. Each of mine were 30 grams and still came out too large, IMO. Next time, I’ll try 25 grams or less. Making smaller cookies means you’ll need more sliced almonds.

Finally, I thought the rum flavor got lost in these and so will probably reduce the butter and up the rum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for a cookie exchange party and they are delicious. I followed many of the suggestions and they came out tasting of both almond and rum. I (1) doubled the recipe, (2) only made half the rum butter (ie, one batch worth), (3) used browned butter for the rum butter, (4) doubled the rum in batter and butter, (5) added vanilla extract, (6) brushed the balls with rum butter before dipping them in crushed almonds, (7) used a 1 tablespoon measurement for the balls and cooked at 18 min. Thanks for all the suggestions - really worked out! I got about 40 cookies out of it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Haven’t made these yet but baked frangipane soaked in buttered rum???? Sign me up!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried doubling the recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
