commentBody: I’m allergic to almonds but not other nuts.  Any suggestions on an alternative nut would be appreciated as I’d otherwise love to make these.  Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @K. Try hazelnuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @K. But the flour is almond

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sorry if this was already answered - would the dough freeze ok with these? I like to make my cookie dough in advance of the holidays and bake it off at a later time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: RUM SUB - Leah and Susan P:  I used rum extract (half the posted quantity of dark rum), and they tasted nice. Sort of like a smaller, denser almond poppyseed muffin without the poppyseeds. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I only got 9.5 cookies out of the dough for some reason.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Successfully made with orange juice   some zest   1/2 tsp orange extract to replace the rum. I found them to be extremely sticky. My tip is to drop each dough ball into the sliced almonds directly from the cookie scoop and then coat them.
So yummy that none of them made it back from book club!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made 25 cookies using small cookie scoop. Don’t need to use a full stick of butter, ½ stick (¼ cup) would be enough. I only used probably 4 ounces of slivered almonds. The cookies don’t spread much so I pressed down the second batch before putting them in and that worked! Make sure not to over bake as they won't necessarily look done but are (check the bottoms!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You mentioned in your video that the cornstarch in powdered sugar is an important component of the recipe. However, organic powdered sugar does not contain cornstarch. how much (if any) cornstarch would you recommend be added to compensate? Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these exactly as the recipe says and they might be the best cookies I’ve ever had! And they were so quick and easy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 40 g balls are too large... If I made again I'd aim for 20 grams.  Also I'd use half a stick of butter...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We really liked these. I added a tablespoon of cornstarch, since I was using organic powdered sugar, which doesn’t include it. I did everything by weight and measured 30 g cookie dough balls instead of 40 g balls, which made 21 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m very confused about the butter… did you put it in with the egg? It says wet ingredients but doesn’t mention butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @J I had to reread it a couple times myself to be sure. You don’t mix the rum butter in with the wet ingredients or dough itself. Instead, you use it in three stages: greasing the parchment, brushing before baking, and brushing after baking. I was able to use a lot of it, but still had some left over.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies turn out very moist in the center, is that normal or I under bake them? Help

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Carolina I had the same thought but I think it’s normal. The edges were just on the verge of burning so I took it out after 21 minutes. It was delightfully chewy and rich so I think that’s just how it’s supposed to be.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had a 2.5 T bit of butter so that was all I used for the rum butter. I brushed this on twice before baking and 2-3 times after (until it ran out) and am happy to share that the result is good and rum-forward!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is my first time commenting on nytimmes cooking though I have saved and cooked many recipes.  This is a delicious cookie recipes and so attractive.  My next batch will substitute a keto sweetener for the sugar so I can eat more of them.  Really delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you use light rum?  Will it be any different in flavor?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and they turned out beautifully!  Definitely will make again - highly recommend!! Great flavor, love the texture, easy to make -10/10

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dang those are good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
With no slivered almonds on hand, I substituted sesame seeds and pumpkin seeds. Both were delicious. 
I recommend making them smaller. Two tablespoons made pretty large cookies, and some were a little bit underbaked.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Obsessed.  Amazing to have a such a delicious gluten free cookie to share.  Assuming I can keep myself from eating all of them because the combination of tastes and textures demand consumption.  Next time will half the rum butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 5 star rating, really? I thought these were stodgy and had very little flavor as written. Vaughn’s video was 5 star fun, but the cookies, meh!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could powdered sugar be substituted with erythritol or monkfruit to make this a keto holiday cookie?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are very good. I didn't really taste the rum at all, you could up it a bit if inclined. But they are perfect as is.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just took these out of the oven - delicious!!  Subbed rye whiskey for the rum and they came out great.  Made half-size 20 g balls (it made 31 cookies) and only used half a stick of butter (but 2 tbsp of whiskey!) and a bit over half the slivered almonds called for.  Bake time was about 16-17 mins with 12/tray in the middle of the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these and I love them. They remind me of marzipan (and the ingredients are similar), but much lighter, fresh and rich and crunchy with nuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mary Lou They remind me of marzipan too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Disappointed with texture. Had high hopes based on other reviews, but mine turned out dense and overly salty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish!  Very fancy looking.  I love watching Vaughn's videos and cooking with him!! Will make more!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sooo good. I increased the rum and added almond paste and mandarin zest. These are really yummy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made these per instructions and they came out excellent! the one question i have- after letting them cool for 5 minutes on the wire rack before having a taste, i noticed the center was a bit raw. not sure if this is normal because of the almond flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 6 tablespoons butter was enough. As was one bag of 8oz of sliced almonds. Used three tablespoons of rum per comments. Otherwise Made according to directions. Got 16 cookies. Yum! So good and stoke the show! But took more like 29 min to start to be golden like the picture. At least with two pans in the oven. A bit smaller would be better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today and didn’t have the correct size scoop so weighed out 40gm pieces and ended up with 15 cookies. I thought the flavor was okay, but not a rum tasting as I had hoped.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We didn’t have rum, so I used bourbon instead and they were awesome. Cookies came out really bourbony and buttery, and I think they are even better the next day or after, warm it up a little in the toaster oven. Yum! Thanks Vaughn for making the GF cookie recipe my kitchen needed :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I started making these before realizing that there was no rum in the house! Instead, based on advice from readers, I added vanilla extract, orange juice, and a little more almond extract to the butter, and also added orange zest to the dry ingredients. I know, I know: not as written, but I was stuck. Tasted fine, though rum would probably have given it more zing.

Using a tablespoon scoop, I got twenty cookies. They all fit on a single tray. Tasty, but surprisingly soft and squishy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added a bit of vanilla with almond extract and rum. Used half as much butter as recipe called for, but same amount of rum. A #40 disher resulted in exactly 24 cookies, which were done in 18 minutes. Rolled balls in rum butter, then almonds. Used fewer sliced almonds than recipe required as well. Sliced almonds fell off when I brushed the finished cookies with rum butter.  Next time I might try flattening them rather than cooking as domes? Super tasty and worth making again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved these! Easy recipe to follow. Don’t skip the extra brush of rum butter. I chose to forego the salt. They didn’t need it. Perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Doubled the rum and added the optional salt flakes. a winning combination!  I made some chocolate ganache for something else, and so a few of the cookies got dipped half way in the ganache. Spectacular.

Note: The final cookie grows in size by about 50%. I found that a heaping teaspoon is plenty big, rather than 40 grams/2 TSP stated in the recipe. it yielded 24 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely my favorite NYT cookie this year (and maybe even last year, too!) SO easy and forgiving. I made much smaller balls (1 T) and got 30 balls out of it and they plump up to perfect size. Had to double the rum butter for coating. Used Captain Morgan rum as it was all I had but it turned out great and not too potent. Very festive and delicious. My 10th cookie so far this year and by far the best! Loved the video too! Thanks Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can the dough be frozen and then used to make cookies later?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @John Brooks yes. I freeze just the dough balls and do the syrup / almonds before baking. Turns out great

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish. And so easy. The video helped!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We loved these! And I used every bit of the rum butter. Now is not the time for moderation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A little confused right now. The recipe says to brush the cookies with more rum butter, but nowhere before did it say to use rum butter. Why the more?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mary DeJong Some of the rum butter went onto the parchment paper on the cookie sheet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Mary DeJong
It says to put some of the rum butter on the parchment paper at the beginning

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The 2 tbsp measurement was not equal to 40g for me, unfortunately! My 2 tbsp scoop actually made 70g cookies, so heads up about that. I tried doubling the recipe and wondered why I still ended up with 18 cookies. It was only after I measured the cookie dough balls that I realized the inaccuracy. Oops! These will be some big doozy, boozy cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious! I made them with vegan butter, which seemed to work out just fine. If you follow the portioning in the recipe, cookies are very large... which I guess cuts down on the messy labor of rolling them in the almonds.
I wonder if you could make into bar cookies instead and press into an 8"x8" pan? If I made again, I will try that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are wonderful! Not too sweet, multifaceted with the salt, butter, gentle rum flavor. The roll in almonds was easy when I relied on the two spoon drop method and then rolled them around in the almonds to forms the ball. Don’t skimp on the buttered rum or salt flakes as they really do impact the final taste. Also I only had clear rum and they still turned out great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Soooo, hypothetically, if you added the butter mixture into the dry ingredients along with the egg mixture, what would you do with that? 
(I made a batch that *actually* followed the recipe and they are fab! Sad to toss the ingredients for the oopsy one)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In what world did we need 2 cups of slivered almonds?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used the two cups

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazingly delicious!!! The butter rum sauce needs to be stirred throughout the process and may need to be warmed a bit as seen in the video. Your tastebuds will thank you! Bravo NYT!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found these to be dense and chewy and too almond y flavored .  I would have preferred a lighter fluffier cookie which I was hoping for in this recipe.  i’m sure it will be eaten anyway.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazingly delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used my kitchen scale to weigh the dough which was a mistake. The 40 grams specified in the instructions must be with the nuts on, because these were huge and I didn't make the specified amount, by far. 
My sliced almonds burned around the edges too, when I wanted an overall even browning. The do taste pretty good, especially with the rum butter and salt, but the recipe needs so tweaking. If I were to make them again, I would go with a 35 gram weight on the dough, maybe even 30 grams.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We loved the look of these cookies but prefer them on the smaller side. Chilled the dough and rolled it into 18- to 20-gram balls, slightly flattened. Be sure to press down the sliced almonds on each ball. It made 32 cookies altogether. They don't spread, so we baked 16 cookies per tray in two batches for 21-22 minutes. Also followed the recommendation of others and used 1/2 the butter and amped up the rum for flavor. It will make a lovely addition to this year's Christmas cookie platter!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these for a second time and wow are they delish! First time around I had some issues with baking time. I’m not sure if I made them too big, but after 22 minutes they were still quite doughy. This time around I used a spoon rather than an ice cream scoop to form the balls and ended up with 18 that baked perfectly in 22 minutes. I also took some earlier commenters advice and upped the rum (3TBSP) in the rum butter and the batter and it came out great. Happy Holidays, bakers!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These might be the best cookies I’ve ever tasted! I doubled the recipe for the dough (I recommend using a scale to weigh the dry ingredients), but I only needed 2cups sliced almonds to roll them and didn’t need to double the butter for basting the cookies. I doubled the dark rum in the butter though! Heavenly right out of the oven!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had better luck forming balls after chilling wrapped dough for a few hours. Otherwise the cookies spread, but were still delicious

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: follow up: Followed the recipe exactly, weighed ingredients. 40 gr. makes a dense ball. perhaps a smaller portion would allow the interior to bake better. Baked 2/3 way up in conventional oven set accurately to 350.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Unfortunately made the mistake of using almond flour that contained almond skins. They tasted like almond flavored Moon Sand. It was Bob’s Red Mill and said super fine but had the pieces in there. They were basically inedible. Do not make the mistake I did!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are OMG delicious! 2 things: def. use sliced almonds (my "slices" were just too chunky) and halve the butter. The 1st time I made them the pans were swimming in butter. The 2nd time I cut it in half  - all good. Oh, and after a few days we (amazingly) had a few left over. They were heading in the direction of maturity and my husband suggested drizzling some maraschino liqueur (NOT the stuff in the cherry bottles) over the top. Again, OMG. Truly (sorry) the cherry on top. Make these!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious! I only had 300 grams of almond flour, so I added 30 grams of AP flour. They spread a bit but were still delicious. I tried to be careful with my cookie scoop but still only got 16 cookies. Although I did as other commenters suggested and added an extra tablespoon of rum to the rum butter, the rum flavor wasn't pronounced in the final product.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good. Rich! Don't make too big - they won't cook through.  Also, I didn't nee 2 c of almonds.  Maybe I made them too big? Making another batch, smaller.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I do not recommend making the dough ahead of time and refrigerating unless you can allow it to slightly warm up again before scooping, otherwise the almonds will not stick to the batter. I made this a second time with no refrigeration and they were much easier and faster to assemble, and they also had a better texture overall after baking. I would also halve the rum butter. One batch was enough to make two batches of cookies. I brought these to a cookie exchange and they were everyone’s favorite!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these 2 weeks ago.  Came out great in center of oven for 20 minutes. They didn’t burn on the bottom as other commenters have mentioned. Recipe made 22 cookies not the 18 listed. Recipe made as written. Should have detected rum flavor in the cookie. Maybe increase the dark rum to batter to be 1 Tbsp.? Didn’t need 2 Cups sliced almonds, probably 1 Cup is enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very delicious, and easy. I halved the sugar and it turned out great. We are not very sweet eaters so half plus the tablespoon in butter worked really well. Someone else noted this as well but 40g made cookies very large so I did about 30g. Great recipe, very unique!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I won't make these again.  They're expensive (the almond flour!, the rum if you don't have it in a cabinet!) and not very flavorful.  They're also very heavy--a little goes a long way.  I followed directions but should have reduced the size of the dough balls by half.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too salty and actually not sweet enough. Halve the salt, up the sugar. I used whiskey instead of rum. Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were disappointing - I used the metric measures and wonder if something got lost in translation. The mixture was too dry so had to add extra egg, butter/rum and a dash of cream.  And there was still a huge amount of butter/rum leftover at the end of glazing. The 40 mg dough balls were way too big - the result was giant cookies that were dry, heavy and way too big to eat in one sitting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were spectacular, a big holiday hit…actually enjoyed making them and the presentation is impressive. Notes…would add more sugar, more almond extract, and would cut the size in half as they’re a big bite. After forming the balls I put each tray into the fridge before baking to prevent any spreading…and increased bake time by 5 min.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I was preparing to make these cookies, I didn't realize I was out of almond extract. I decided to press on and substitute half vanilla and half coconut extract for the almond extract - and the cookies were extraordinarily delicious! I'll probably continue making them the same way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Too buttery as instructed. Next time will skip the post-bake brushing.

Also tasted a bit medicinal. Perhaps it was the almond extract. Will try without it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't bake them, my sister did, and it took all of my restraint to not devour the whole batch! So delicious!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I use walnuts instead of almonds

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I halved the butted and doubled the rum, as some suggested. That’s great!
However, I also made smaller balls (someone recommended 26g, I went for 30g) and baked for a shorter amount of time, but I fear I don’t know the right amount of time because the bottoms were quite crisp, the centers seemed fairly underdone. Somebody help!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can these be frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is the finished texture inside supposed to be on the doughier side, or hard/crunchy?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you really want to ratchet up the rum taste, soak the almonds in rum overnight. You will need more than 2 tablespoons to make sure all the almonds get a coating of rum. They will soak up the flavor and provide an intense rum flavor. No need to keep brushing on rum butter that way either.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are excellent! I made a few small adjustments that I was happy with:
1.) I used a 1 tbsp cookie scoop and rolled each into a ball (22 grams)
2.) I crunched the almonds up a bit to get them to adhere better.
3.) After rolling in the almonds I rolled the balls again which really helped the almonds stay put.
4.) I baked for 20 minutes. My tops didn't get a brown as the picture, but the almonds toasted nicely.
5.) After the final butter coating, I sprinkled mine with sparkling sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you’re looking for a cookie that tastes like rum, I say pass. Despite using my favorite baking rum (Stroh), you couldn’t taste the rum at all on these. I think it just evaporated from the butter while cooking. There wasn’t nearly enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Erica I agree 100%. As written, this recipe just didn’t turn out good at all. The comments to add rum and other flavors are good suggestions that should be incorporated into the recipe. As is, the recipe is a bust. I won’t be making it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What exactly does "beat egg whites, egg, almond extract and remaining 2 teaspoons rum until no streaks remain" mean?  Other recipes for similar cookies state to beat egg whites until frothy or until soft peaks form.  My cookies are extremely dense, and I'm wondering if it's because my egg whites did not incorporate enough air.  Very frustrating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My first time making these.  I was afraid they might be icky-sticky-sweet like almond macaroons, but am glad they're not.  The texture and flavor improve after sitting a while and become distinctive due to the butter "glaze" and sea salt.  While I brushed them liberally, there was quite a bit left over.  So, next time - and there will be a next time - I'd use 5-6 tablespoons butter and up the rum to 3 tablespoons and sweeten the glaze a bit more.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Incredibly delicious cookies! Not a children's cookie and not because of the rum. Delicate Marzipan. Followed the recipe and just a little butter left. I brushed around liberally. Only used 1.5 C almonds. They wouldn't take more. A real winner!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent bake . I milled ‘ground almonds’ as I couldn’t get super fine almond flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for a Christmas party at work. I remembered I needed to bake something at like 9:30pm, so a simple quick recipe was key. At that point I didn't have almond flavouring so used a lot of vanilla and a bit of extra rum. It was an instant hit and I had three requests for the recipe. This will become a classic here. Absolutely delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious, and a perfect Christmas cookie for my celiac sister-in-law. The saltines a nice touch.However, the quantity of butter soecified is far too much - having brushed very generously, I still ended up throwing out half. The butter also took on an unpleasant consistency while waiting for the cookies to bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing them?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For people who are rum-averse-- heavens!--might Amaretto be an option? I know it doesn't echo the Hot Buttered Rum brilliance in the recipe, but could a light touch with the Amaretto  work with this recipe in place of the rum?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't see the Tip for the sliced almonds ?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Laurie Laurie, inexplicably the tips always appear at the end of the instructions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have blanched almond flour. Can I blitz this in the processor to get superfine?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @ PAULINA REE what a Genius substitute!!!! I'm gonna make 2 batches 1. The original way with the rum and the second with the Frangelico!!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried using dried/powdered eggwhites?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So thrilled with an excellent gf Christmas cookie that no one notices is gf!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If anyone tries making these egg free please report back. I think the flavor of these cookies sounds amazing but I don’t want to use eggs. I was thinking about trying it with aquafaba.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these. Perfect. But - can you freeze them?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don't substitute wheat flour! Instead share the gluten-free protein-rich wonder of these beauties.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Once again a serious question without an answer: can these be frozen? If anyone actually monitors this site, please answer the questions people submit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are Passover almond macaroons with too much salt. Otherwise, tastes pretty good, but I’ll save them for the holiday when I can’t cook with flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have a good Xmas cookie repertoire but was tempted to make these. As some others noted, this did not have detectable rum notes. Getting almond slices to adhere was tricky. Read all the comments for tips on this. More rum next time - maybe even some rum extract from Dr Oetker. For me - good but not a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Meh. Made to the recipe, am not impressed. Does NOT need salt on top. Almonds did not stick to cookies (dough as written is pretty sticky, but not enough) Taste of cookie itself needs help- either add more almond extract, or some rum extract, to the cookie dough. Could not discern rum in this at all. Try cooking down dark rum before adding to butter? (Note: I probably won't bother w/these again)
HS

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious — I will note that I only used 1/4 cup of butter and found it the perfect amount. Also rolled the cookies in shredded coconut because we had no sliced almonds and that was delicious too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I make them smaller? One tablespoon?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these last week and was underwhelmed.  I threw them in the trash rather than add them to my holiday cookie tray.  Very disappointing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made them today. They are great! Will add them to my Christmas Cookie collection!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really yummy! Soft and luxurious. Store well. Rum is not overwhelming. Have great flavor balance.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m planning to make these for someone who is GF. Has anyone here used “regular” almond flour (NOT used blanched superfine almond flour)? I could grind up blanched almonds in my food processor but would rather skip that step. I’m wondering if it really makes THAT much difference…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have raw sliced almonds at home. Should they be toasted prior to using?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @KM they’ll toast when baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a relatively easy cookie to make and a fun variation in my holiday cookie mix. The only comment I have, is you don’t nearly need as much melted butter. Half of it was left over.  Next time I would only use a quarter cup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these today. Came out great in center of oven for 20 minutes. Recipe made 22 cookies not the 18 listed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these today for a holiday cookie swap - they are substantial in size, look beautiful, and taste amazing!  I don't know much about rum, but my nearby liquor store only had "dark" rum in liter size, so I went with a gold colored brand that they offered in a 200 ml size.  Does that make a difference?  I plan to make another batch this week.  I did find it easier to chill the dough for about 20-30 minutes before rolling.  It's wonderful to see such great gluten-free holiday cookie recipes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very, very good and thanks for the great directions!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question for the community: Does it make a huge difference if I substitute kosher salt with regular salt? I don't think I can find that in my city.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @L I had to order from Amazon, nothing in our rural area. Just about everything on Amazon

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any large coarse-ground or flaked salt will work. The idea is that the salt crystals will be big enough to not dissolve on contact.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think they could have been smaller. Otherwise delish!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 40g each was too big -- would go 30-32g next time. I also agree that it needs more almond extract or some vanilla to pump up the flavor a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: At 40g per cookie I got 15 (the 15th was 50g).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t have a brush with me, so I just spooned on the butter rum sauce and no problem. I also didn’t have shaved almonds so I substituted chopped pecans to roll them in, and oh my!! They were incredible. This will definitely be a new tradition during the holidays.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Any suggestions for an alternate to rum? I know it's in the name, but it's not in my liquor cabinet, and I'd rather not run to the liquor store if I don't have to.

I have some Cointreau on hand, and feel like the orange and almond would work. 

Thoughts? Thanks in advance.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Rebecca B I am about to boldly add Cointreau instead of rum (because I love Cointreau and because I have no rum on hand and because it is snowing outside!!).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Per others suggetion doubled the rum in both batter and butter. Halved the butter. Added 1/4 tsp orange oil (didn't have zest) and a splash of contreau to the butter. Made 24 using #60 cookie scoop. Very nice, would make again. Just the right amount of orange, rum flavour was still pretty subtle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My pastry chef is gluten-free and I was inspired to make these for our holiday cookie swap. They were wonderfully soft, chewy and flavorful and a big hit among all of the pastry team!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For me, these cookies were very heavy in the sense of high fat content. I haven’t cooked with almond flour before and it’s just ground up nuts so to eat a bunch of sugary high fat nut cookies, my stomach didn’t love them. You can’t really eat a whole one since it’s very heavy and I even halved the cookie scoop amount, 24g instead of 40g. 
I do like the almond flavor and nutty flavor. But the trade off is too much fat and a heavy cookie that you can’t eat a lot of.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sooooo good!!! I was surprised how firm the dough was and hard to mix but it totally makes sense when you start scooping. Next time I’ll make the dough and bake on the day I’m serving them. I’ll need to store the rum butter too. The sea salt is a MUST. it takes these to a whole new level. Yum!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m confused!! Is the butter mixture part of the wet ingredients you add to the dry?? Or it is only for brushing??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Cayte butter mixture is for brushing the cookies before and after baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made per directions - except I did not add flake salt at the end (glad I did not).  

Loved the gooey taste when warm.   Cannot beat the time from start to finish, no mixer and pretty results.  The butter before and after baking gave cookies a nice gloss - and I used every drop of the butter/rum mix.      

I thought cookies were just a tad too salty (maybe it was because I was using Morton’s Kosher salt - will cut in half next time).   

Along with other commenters - Cookies are a too big for my tastes.  I’ll try making 36 cookies next time.   I tried to use most of the almonds - pushing extra almonds into the cookies.   This was a mistake -  some almonds were over- toasted which is not a good taste.  Next time I will crush the sliced almonds just a little to get more almond’s “sticking” and not worry about using all the almonds.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Have made these twice already and they are so good! Like tiny moist dense little almond cakes with a kick. Agree that the perfect size is smaller than the 40g, they are rich, and half a stick of butter is plenty to baste the batch twice with. Add more rum to the butter if you want em boozier. Happy holidays!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies are thick and cake like, not sweet enough so the rum flavor is very strong. Almond flour is dense and heavy. After cooking the first batch I threw them out. Thumbs down.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these last night.  Think the instructions could have been a bit more clear,  Roll the dough into balls then press into the almonds.  Flatten them a little with a fork after you've put them on the cookie sheet.  Skip the sea salt - takes away from the deliciousness of the almond flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Forgot to add - NO WAY the prep is 5 minutes!  And you have inconsistent baking times - cook time is 35 minutes while the instructions say, 20-25.  Mine came out of the oven at 20 minutes and were fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Cookies turned out great! Agree with other commenters about adding almond paste, it adds a lot.

I'm not sure if its just me, but when one of the ingredients on NYT Cooking is melted butter, I wish they would add that to the instructions list. Step 0 here is: melt the butter. Sometimes, it throws me off when I'm baking in a rush. (Same goes for softening butter, which is much trickier and requires more forethought)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you put a tray of these on the bottom rack of your oven, it might burn. I think brushing the parchment with butter made that happen. For the ones that didn’t burn, they’re delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same - my cookies on the bottom rack burned in the 20 minute cook time, tasting mostly of burned butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you use date sugarinplaceof the powdered sugar?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these cookies be frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I plan to freeze some.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What do you think about using bourbon instead of rum?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Just made these, they are delicious -I agree with another comment that these aren't really rum flavored that much, and there was a lot of the rum butter left over.    I added a spice rum so maybe that is why?  If I made these again I would
Add more rum to the batter 
Reduce the amount of butter to half
*kept the rum butter to add to french vanilla ice cream!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just an FYI, I tried to sub GF coconut flour for the almond flour by weight. DID NOT WORK. It was way too much coconut flour. If you want to do this for an almond allergy, maybe start off with the eggs and sugar etc and add just enough coconut flour to get the consistency from the video. I’ll have to try again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are fantastic. They're perfect for the holidays, but I think I'll make them all winter. The buttered rum really comes through, and the middle is like frangipane -- so delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made a double batch because 18 cookies didn't sound like enough for the party - ended up with 50.  I did not double the rum butter, but did end up needing 10 TB of butter (I was being very generous at the end).  Love that they are gluten free.  Easy to make.  I made two trays but still had dough left over, which I chilled while the two were baking.  Interestingly, the almonds did not want to stick to the chilled dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was sure that these were going to be a disappointment until I ate one. The dough was too soft to handle and was very bland so I did add 1/2 tsp each of vanilla and rum extract. I had to refrigerate it to make portions and roll in nuts.The end result is really good and I’d be proud to serve these to anyone gluten free or not. I do wish it had a stronger rum flavor. Also might be nice dipped in chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic flavor and texture.  Agree that using 1 Tblsp scoop produces a better sized cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are GOOD. My only regret is not roasting the leftover almonds along with the cookies on the baking sheet :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love this recipe! Is there anyway I can pin these recipes to Pintrest?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A friend gave me one of these perfect confections today.  Her neighbor made them with Amaretto instead of run, and it was divine!!  I'm so glad a recipe came with it!  I'm going to try them very soon!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are very similar to the Italian Pinoli cookies.  Has anyone added 8 oz (a tube or container of almond paste to these?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These have a delicious flavor (I didn't add the flaky sea salt)!  I found that chilling the dough for about 20 minutes before rolling out made them easier to roll and no problem with the almonds sticking to the dough.  I took them to a cookie swap at church and they were gone in no time.  The recipe makes 18 as stated and mine were about 35-36 grams; they are quite large.  Taste and texture were spot on!  I'll make these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The amount of butter should be at most 1/4 cup
I will substitute superfine berry sugar for the icing sugar
I will add 2 more egg whites and no egg yolk
Am disappointed

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great. Just add MORE RUM!! And prep time was much longer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely fantastic! I used a small scoop (1" wide), yielding 36 cookies. The size of the baked cookies were around 2", give or take, per cookie. I can't have dairy so I used Earth Balance (I know, I know...but what is a girl to do?) and they still came out wonderful. My partner and friends who can have dairy still said they were one of the best cookies they've had this holiday season! Score! Definitely will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm surprised so many like this cookie, and it's very rich and heavy. I found the butter taste a bit off-putting, and if I made again -- I won't -- I'd put the sliced almonds on the top only, as it was too much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made a few tweaks, but agree with others this is a fantastic recipe! I plan to make it again
- used only half the sugar, still plenty sweet IMO
- I tried rolling in the butter mixture first as recommended, but had leftover butter so also spread on top too after rolling in almonds
- I still had extra butter even though I halved the amount of butter (still kept the 2T of rum as another person recommended)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookies! I’d like to make more but only have 2 or so cups of flour. Any recommendations for halving this recipe? Not sure how to handle the eggs. Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent - added vanilla to rum sauce, and used white rum because that was what I had online.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wanted a little more rum, a little less butter. Will adjust next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I really liked this recipe a lot! I’m a newer baker and I was really happy to have a simple recipe to follow. Thank you!

Adjustments:
- I wanted a smaller cookie, so I shrunk them to 26g each and dropped the baking time to 18 minutes.
- I used Gosling Dark Rum.
- I love the texture, but I didn’t get a strong rum flavor. Since I reduced the cooking time, my almonds didn’t color either. With my last few cookies, I added rum to the butter and got what I was looking for in both flavor and color. I’ll double the rum next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used extra rum in butter and dough. Also topped with Maldon salt. I thought that it helped to bring out the flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these with along with the Eggnog Snickerdoodles as someone else suggested. Both are a hit - however taking the snickerdoodles to cookie exchange and keeping these for home. Flavor is on point!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today and they are delightful! I added a bit of orange zest to the rum and butter mixture, which was a nice addition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Adhered to the 40 gram drop but could definitely do smaller drops of 20 grams next time. I might try it with crushed almond slices to fit the smaller drop of dough. Deliciously not-too-sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved these. Though they turned out rather large - I did weigh so will make them a little smaller next time. And use half that amount of butter.  I ended up pouring a bunch down the sink. The rum taste was hard to discern but that was not really an issue for me since I’m all about almond.  Used Bob’s Red Mill almond flour (the super fine from blanched per the directions and was very happy with these.  Though they are very easy to eat several at one go!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: OMG-Love love love these cookies;  made as written, except used spiced rum as that is what I had.  Plan to make another batch to give away as gifts as I decided I wanted the first batch for me!  Made some of Vaughs Mexican Hot Chocolate cookies from last years cookie week for a beverage themed bag of cookies to give to friends.  Merry Christmas!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As others have said, I felt these were a bit on the large side even though I ended up with 24 cookies (didn't weigh dough).  Also, though the texture was nice, mine had almost no rum flavor at all (used Mount Gay).  If I made them again I might just use rum extract, which I find has a stronger flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these and used a scale for everything. 40gm mounds resulted in 15 cookies. I thought they were okay, but you could barely taste the rum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: these only made 9 cookies for me... not sure where i went wrong. they dont seem overly massive either

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These come together very nicely! Only adjustments I made was to make them half as large--they are very rich and come out on the "big" side. On a platter of cookies they display much nicer when smaller.  On my second batch I omitted the flaky sea salt as a topping. There was more than enough salt in the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Meh.  This is a fussy cookie that doesn't deliver.  I doesn't differ much from a far better recipe from Mrs. Fields that I have used for years.  I thought this one would be fun because of the added zip of rum, but I can barely taste it, even though I followed the recipe exactly.  The cookies don't even taste much like almond, even though it's almond flour and sliced almonds and extract.  I don't get it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Came here to report that freezing the balls after rolling them in almond slices and glazing them to bake later works just fine. Added two more minutes to the baking time. Smothered with saved rum butter after baking and sprinkled with maldon sea salt. Husband complained it’s not sweet enough for a cookie but this is perfect for me - nutty, crunchy, boozy, reminiscent of marzipan. Be generous on rum butter brushing and you won’t complain about this not being rum flavored

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a real hit with friends and family, but what's more, I loved them;  the interior is reminiscent of macaroons or amaretti, with lots of lovely almond-y flavor.  However, the rum flavor didn't come through -- even though I used a bit more rum than called for; might add rum extract to this next time.   I had a surfeit of slivered almonds so I used them instead of sliced, to very good effect:  a very pretty cookie, and also delish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies were good but didn’t really get a rum flavor. Will have to try again using a bit more rum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
YUM! These are delicious. Mine didn't look as neat / nice as VVs but I'm happy to have a cookie that is so tasty.
I loved last year's Mexican Hot Chocolate cookie and made those and the rum-buttered almond cookies for family visiting for the holidays.
Thanks for these great cookie recipes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Easy and very delicious!   
But why the short shelf time (4 days!!??)   Anybody know why?   I guess I can refrigerate them to make them last longer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are powdered sugar and confectioner's sugar interchangeable for this recipe?  Can you substitute anything else for confectioner's sugar (need to avoid the cornstarch!)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Google says yes - they're interchangeable. Try Google for a substitute - it's never let me down.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies have a lovely texture and the toasted almonds are delicious, but the cookie part needs more flavor. I think I would increase the almond extract and perhaps add imitation butter extract next time. The rum really gets lost in the cooking even with brushing with extra at the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazing! Just what I needed to round out my cookie boxes this year. Take the suggestion about halving the size of the cookie, and feel free to add more rum if you are feeling it. But do not take the advice to half the amount of butter -- just keep brushing all of it on your cookies. Why do you think they taste so good  ;-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dice or use a food processor on the almonds before trying to coat the dough balls. Trying to press flat almonds onto round cookies was just annoying and resulted in poor coverage. Use rum extract at normal conversion for full rum taste. I had to double the butter rum mixture to coat all the cookies twice. Also seems like I got a lot more cookies than the recipe describes. I used a two tbsp scoop and got close to 32 balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this cookie and while they taste great, they didn't keep their shape in the oven and "melted" away, they now look less like balls but more like a regular cookie... any tips on how to keep the shape next time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delightful! I added some additional rum and vanilla extract to the batter which made for great flavor but the cookie was slightly underbaked even after 24 minutes. Next time I will add about an 1/8 cup of additional almond flour. I also added additional run to the butter and balances out with more sugar. Per someone else’s comment I found that rolling in the butter and then rolling in the almonds made them stick better. Will definitely make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Greetings from Germany. I just made these and sadly my dough was way too tough, idk what went wrong? When I combined the dry and wet ingredients it felt like it needed way more liquid?  Added another egg but although it made the dough softer, it also made it drier. Would love to try again. I used ultra fine almond flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Christina, I wonder if this would be due to using what we call “Mandelmehl” (almond flour) in Germany instead of almond meal (“gemahlene Mandeln”)? Not entirely sure which one is meant in this recipe, or whether the options in the US are completely different than ours, but listening to him talking about preferring the white one over the brown one, makes me think, it could be our gemahlene Mandeln. The almond flour we buy here is very fine and needs loads of liquids, much more than every other flour I have tried so far.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great flavor and are quick and easy. But these are BIG cookies. I scaled out 16 -17g balls and after baking the cookies were about walnut size. I prefer petite cookies; so will further reduce next time. Will make smaller next time. And am I the only one who had about 4 tablespoons of the butter/rum mixture left over? I certainly wasn't skimpy when brushing the parchment or the cookies before and after baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were positively fabulous! It’s hard to make the gluten- free people happy, but this one is delicious whether you eat gluten or not. Lots of leftover butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The dough came out quite wet although I followed instructions exactly and was more like pouring into the almonds which kept them in shape. I had to use more sliced almonds too, due to the wetter than advertised batter. I would chill the dough next time. 40 grams is not two tablespoons, 30 grams is and they were way too big. Should be tasty though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They taste great! Refrigerating dough definitely made it easier to form the balls. I have a lot of extra rum butter left... What should I do with it? I saw one comment about putting it on French toast but I don't really make or love French toast

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi! Any ideas on how to substitute the almond extract? I thought vanilla extract would work but I’m not sure. Thanks in advance 😊

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The dough comes together fast, but that is the only thing fast about these cookies. Every step after the scooping of the dough is detailed and fussy. The cookies are huge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am thrilled to find an interesting cookie developed to be Gluten Free. My GF grandson is only 5 years old, so the rum or rum butter or even rum extract doesn't seem a good fit. I read the OJ sub and a poo-poo regarding the OJ option. I'll take my chances - but would it be a total waste to just brush the balls with butter in the last step?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can rum extract be substituted for the rum?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a really nice cookie. Not overly sweet and really easy. I'll amp up the rum next time and there will be a next time, like this weekend because these will be gone very soon. Keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used double the Grand Marnier instead of rum. I also added 1 tbsp of GM after baking to each cookie to give it a punch. Very nice!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I know the recipe says they can last up to four days at room temperature. Has anyone had experience with keeping them around for longer than 4 days (other than freezing)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this cookie exactly as stated and wow, it is delicious.
Thank you Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I hate it when folks change a recipe before they've ever made it according to directions, but I just did it myself.  I needed to make these dairy-free. I used a butter substitute that I bake with and it has turned out well.  I'm sure butter would be better, but I'm pleased with these results.  I did increase, accidentally, the amount of rum in the rum butter and agreed with other that more rum is not a problem.  I think I will increase the amount of almond extract next time as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very quick recipe, although a bit fussy. Definitely need to use dark rum and perhaps a bit of almond extract to punch up the flavor. Going to try these with hazelnut flour and hazelnuts but it seems very adaptable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these today as written. I ended up with 20 cookies somehow but glad i did. Fantastic flavor and texture. Shout out to Mad River Maple Cask Rum from Vermont, the perfect choice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these cookies yesterday. They are chewy and too sweet for me. I was hoping for crispy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The ingredients list should list 3 cups/330 gm superfine blanched almond flour rather than say in the tip that blanched flour is far better than natural flour

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What I thought to be a huge mistake (I poured the rum butter into the batter and I had doubled the recipe) thinking what a waste. I made a batch of the cookies anyway, sans the rum butter on top, and baked. The cookies ended up with a rather marzipan centre. Not bad. I  put the rest of my mistake on top a mix of the almonds, a little panko and melted butter, then into a cake pan. Sprinkled with some Maldon salt and baked. What a save! Served with vanilla ice cream. No one was the wiser!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was all set to make these cookies tonight and found I only have 222 g of almond flour. I have some all-purpose gluten free flour—anyone know if I could make up the rest with that?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these and WOW, they are fabulous! As others have suggested I will make smaller next time - eating one of these is quite filling -no room for any of the other cookies I’ve made - and add more almond extract to kick up that flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious! We’re making our second batch this afternoon. But did anyone else have the issue of the bottoms overcooking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: At 22 minutes they can start to overcook. I switched the pans at 10 minutes and that worked really well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy. Delicious!  I tried one too soon and thought I undercooked them, but when I had one later it was just right. I loved them as is, but also like the idea of swapping the rum for Amaretto.  Had some leftover butter sauce like other commenters.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookies! I weighed all ingredients and the scooped batter - 40g makes a pretty big cookie, and I only got 15 cookies out of the recipe. Also had a lot of sliced almonds and rum butter left. Would try smaller cookies next time and increase the rum, but they are delicious nonetheless! Marzipan-y inside, lovely crunch, and nice to have a GF recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is it possible to store excess rum butter (I've cooked half a batch and want to cook another half in a few days or so)? If so, how would you do it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tarn I’d put it in the fridge in a tightly sealed container. Preferably glass.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes. Put it in a covered container and refrigerate it. I got delayed in making my second batch and it the rum buttter sat on the counter overnight. Once reheated it was fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these. I just made a second batch. I'm not sure what I'm doing wrong but the bottoms are always burnt before the almonds brown. I suspect it's my oven. I just microplane the bottoms until I get rid of the burnt layer and no one is the wiser.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Try switching the trays halfway through. That worked for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m making these now and can’t imagine basting the cookies will use up a whole 1/2 cup of butter! Have gone back to review the recipe but don’t see it used for anything else - what am I missing?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh Vaughn, you've gone and done it - broken my resolve to bake but not eat so many cookies this season!  Baked as instructed, no substitutions required, but it did inspire me to buy an even better quality rum and bake a second batch, which I may actually share with guests on Christmas....

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the 2 tbsp too large and reduced the size. They are delicious. But is the bottom supposed to be burnt?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are incredible and will be a new addition to my holiday cookie plate!  And bonus they are gluten free, so a nice surprise for gf friends. I followed the recipe as stated and agree with others that half the rum butter would suffice. I also made them about half the size (20g) and kept an eye on them. They were done around 18-19 minutes for me. Thanks for a winner of a recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these yesterday and used cheap Bacardi dark rum. The rum butter was really watery and I could hardly taste the rum flavor at all. I would try better rum next time. I also agree with making them smaller and might flatten them a little and pop a couple bittersweet chocolate chips on top before a little flaky sea salt. More almond extract too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn’t do it for me and I love boozy cookies. The rum was oddly juxtaposed with the almond and the balance of flavors was off. Expensive ingredients for a not-delicious result.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with bourbon instead of rum because it was on hand. Very tasty! The alcohol taste was more pronounced than I think it would have been with rum. I also made these smaller than called for in the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed a couple of the modifications listed by others:  doubled the amount of rum in both the cookies and the butter sauce, doubled the amount of almond extract, and rolled the cookies in the rum butter sauce before rolling in the almonds.  I also made these slightly smaller so I ended up with 22 cookies.  And then I made my own modification—I didn’t just use melted butter, I browned it (aka the nectar of the gods).  The rum is not super-intense, but it has a light nod to it.  All in all a tasty cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cut back on the salt, 1 1/4 tsp is too much. Don't understand buttering the parchment paper before placing the cookie on the paper. Cookie bottoms burn. Overal, they look a lot better than they end up tasting. Given their expense, I'd pass.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: going to try and make these soon but would kahlua work?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone try making these with regular almond flour instead of superfine?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine took about 24 minutes to get a nice color on the surface. I made as written with the addition of the zest of 1/2 an orange in the almond cookie mix.  Like these very much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just delicious. Omitted rum to make them kid friendly and they were like an almond croissant cookie- buttery, marzipan chewy, crunchy from the slivered almonds- just great! I agree with other commenters on making them smaller, the crunchy almonds work well on a smaller cookie. I was worried they were underbaked at 22 minutes, but they kept their texture well on day 2. Thanks Vaughn for another hit!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious. I added an extra tablespoon of rum to the butter and made sure to reserve about a quarter of the melted butter/rum liquid in a small measuring cup to pour on the cookies right when they came out of the oven. I only had Morton’s kosher salt so I followed others’ suggestions to cut the salt in half. I also followed the suggestion to dip the dough balls in the butter/rum liquid before rolling in almonds. Recipe yielded 15 cookies for me and took about 22 minutes to bake. They looked beautiful and tasted amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these substituting coconut oil for butter - gluten and lactose free! Rum + almond + coconut = very tasty cookies. I was pleasantly surprised that the cookies were crunchy outside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This could easily be a Passover cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had a surplus of rum and almond flour to use up, so I tinkered with this according to a lot of the notes. Increase rum in the cookies to 1/4 cup, increase almond extract to 2 tsp, decrease eggs to 1 egg + 1 white. Scoop 30g cookies. Brown the butter, roll the cookies in the rum butter before firmly pressing almonds into exterior. Bake at 300 instead of 350. Bake for 12 minutes, generously drizzle with more rum butter, then bake for another 10 minutes. They turned out pretty good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: PS - I also cut the size of the cookies in half. They still make a nice two-bite size, while the full size is (for me) just a little too big. As MP said earlier, they still take 20-22 minutes to bake. So very good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'll never make these again.  The dough is very messy to work with and there are other tasty almond cookie recipes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A little fussy to work with getting the almonds onto the dough.  I had a lot of leftover almonds but they still look good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I only got 14 cookies out of this and that was after I adjusted to make them smaller after four cookies. If you made these all 40 g (as recommended in the recipe), you might only get 10 cookies. 40 g is way too big. I think I would cut them down to half that size. 25 grams maybe. This will also give you a higher ratio of crunchy outside to soft inside. Also, you might be able to use up all the almonds he calls for (I had about a cup left) and all of the rum butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used smith & cross Jamaica rum - it’s navy strength so rum flavor did come through. Also recommend adding an extra tbls of rum to the butter. Added a tps of vanilla to dough. Made cookies slightly smaller than advised. Baked for 18 mins. Did not skimp on the rum butter soak. Came out amazing! So many requests for the recipe at my holiday party. Will make again every Xmas!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are quick, easy, and delicious! I’ll be baking these treats for years to come.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These have the flavor and texture of marzipan. It’s too bad the description didn’t say that.  I do not like marzipan and you also couldn’t taste the rum. This was a big “no” for me. A total waste of $ and ingredients.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe mixing rum and Controi would be good, for an orangy touch

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can’t find almond extract, will these still come out ok? Or is there a substitute?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My neighbor gave me these for Christmas. They are absolutely divine!! Now I’ve subscribed and have the recipe :-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are great - for me, it’s the right balance of non-fussy with interesting taste and texture. Made them smaller - a large tablespoon scoop, which yielded 24 cookies. Baked about 17-18 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these twice and happily eaten!  Here’s my modifications:

80 g salted butter, 1 tbsp powdered sugar, 3/8 cup dark rum.
Simmer the butter 20 min or more to reduce the extra liquid, while increasing the flavor of the added rum.
355 g almond powder, 1/2 tsp salt, 185 g powdered sugar, 1/2 tsp allspice.
One egg, 2 egg whites, 4 tsp rum, 1 tsp almond extract.
100 g slivered almonds should be enough for single batch.
25-27 g balls yields 27 cookies of a perfect size for giving.  They don’t spread much so can fit many more than suggested on a baking tray.
Bake 22-24 min depending on pan and oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I weighed the dough as I rolled them; at 40g each I got only 13 cookies. 

I think the 40g may be a typo, and it should say a #40 scoop (rather than 40g).  A #40 scoop is about 2 T.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe doesn't need 2 cups of sliced almonds.  I'm going to make another round of the dough -- this doesn't make very many and I have leftover almonds!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree with the too large! I used a 1T scoop and they are still 2” across! Delicious and pretty—but I can’t imagine if I had used a 2T scoop!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wondering if anyone has made this with a vegan butter substitute??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were fabulous, with consistently rave reviews from family & friends.  I love that they are easy to make.  But mine spread more than the photos and video show.  Did anyone else have this experience?  Any thoughts on what I may have done wrong?  I live at high altitude, and it's possible the elevation had an impact, but I usually don't have altitude-related issues with cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe I needed to refrigerate the dough but they spread out like flat cookies. Nice Gluten free cookies. Flavor is ok.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today as written, but they were stodgy rather than pillowy. Any thoughts on what I did wrong?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing!!! And SO easy! I added 2 - 1/2 tps of rum extract... to the batter and to the butter. Plus a few extra tablespoons of rum. I would only add that nexr time, I will flatten them out a bit before baking. Otherwise excellent recipe!! And not too sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly as the recipe instructs and we loved them! Not overly sweet - I appreciate that.  I only got 1 1/2 dozen cookies so I will double the recipe next time. Maybe add a little more rum to the batter too. Happy holidays!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe! I halved the butter recipe and it was the perfect amount with brushing the cookie twice. Added a little extra almond extract and some vanilla extract to the egg mixture. First batch baked for 20 mins. Second batch 18mins (browned quicker for some reason). I prefer a smaller rounder cookie (made about 20-22). They held shape very well even in warm Hawaii weather. I didn’t refrigerate them like other cookie dough before popping in the oven. The taste gets even better a couple days later! Plan to make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious but ate more like an almond pastry than a cookie. Not super sweet but very rich. Recipe is good, just worth noting they don't hit the dessert sweet spot like a classic cookie. My family actually preferred them as breakfast with some coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Excellent. Quick. Easy.
A definite “keeper” in the annual rotation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavor and texture are wonderful. Making each cookie with 2 tbsp - too big and you won’t get 18 cookies. I’d use a heaping tbsp next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe: more delicious than any of us deserve, considering how easy and fast it is.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are FANTASTIC. We will be making these every year for eternity. Punched up the rum a bit for my tiki-loving husband. Thank you Vaughn!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was excited to have a gluten free cookie for a family member.  Alas, one bite was more than enough!  It is very heavy - more like marzipan than a cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are absolutely decadent. not only do they look great, they are beyond delicious. Request are already in to make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These Rum-Buttered Almond Cookies were a huge hit at my recent holiday party!  My husband declared they were the best Christmas cookie ever!  As an added bonus, they are gluten free, so I had some homemade GF cookies to serve my gluten-sensitive guests, that still tasted great!
The only change I made was to dip the cookies in the rum-butter (as Kim did) before rolling them in the sliced almonds, then brushed them again after baking.  Thanks for the recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They looked beautiful, but they did not taste like rum.  Needs more flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these as a gift and they turned out awful.  Don't know why since I followed reciope with no replacements.  Almonds had a hard time sticking to cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as directed, except added 1 Tblsp. Rum to rum butter, and measured each ball of dough at 33 gms. Taste fantastic, and a huge hit with guests.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was so disappointed in these cookies.  High hopes, but they were very dry.  Ended up throwing the entire batch in the trash…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had my doubts about this one (mostly cuz hubbie dislikes the taste of alcohol) but they barely have a hint of rum, lovely macaroon texture and not too sweet at all.  Be sure to add the salt at the end.  They are very big and I would make smaller next time.  Unlike some comments that there was too much butter left over - I used practically every bit of it between the two applications... could I have used too much butter?  ;-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. I thought they were very good. I like that they tasted like marzipan. I made 18 cookies. I could see that it would be nice to make them smaller and have more of them. There were fairly easy to make. I would make these again. Good cookie for the holidays.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fantastic! I made them for a dinner party that includes friends in recovery and gluten free friends, so I took the precaution of using rum extract instead of real rum.  I used about a teaspoon in the melted butter and a generous 1/2 teaspoon rum extract in the batter along with 1/2 teaspoon of almond extract.  The notes of rum were subtle; however my husband declared these to be possibly the best cookies I've ever made.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were my first cookies made from this year’s cookie week! I very much appreciated the speed with which everything came together. I’d make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Now a family favorite!!! The best cookie ever!!!! Crunchy on the outside and chewy inside!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine spread. IDK what I did wrong but they taste great?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nice little almond macaroons -- but I didn't get any rum flavor at all, and the salt on top was little weird.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kate adding sea salt on top of cookies is very very common

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delightful. I only had salted butter on hand so I reduced the salt, but these were awesome.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Total disappointment 😞. Am an accomplished cook and took some of the suggestions (adding rum drizzle after cooking, adding more flavor to the dough itself). Was just flavorless and usually when I make anise cookies (similar recipe) the flavor is so great. Those are made with flour and these are with almond flour so perhaps that is where the flavor gets just sucked up by the dough and needs so much more? Only ate them first day. Perhaps they were better a couple of days later. Brought to a cookie swap as recipe looked amazing and I was so disappointed in lack of flavor despite making changes. As well, lots of butter for no flavor. Hoping you have better luck!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Prepared these today and wrote myself a reminder -  “divided use” - after the ingredients of “2 tablespoons plus 2 teaspoons dark rum,” as I put all of that into the butter mixture…Even with the extra 2 teaspoons of rum, eventually used it all on the cookies. Instead of using two separate cookie sheets, I used one large 18”x13” pan and was able to fit on all 18 as they do not spread when baking. Not surprisingly, upon tasting, was reminded of the texture & taste of marzipan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love homemade cookies and really wanted to add this to my recipe box for a holiday cookie.  But, even though I followed the directions exactly as written, the bottoms of the cookies were almost burnt and the middles were still raw.  OK, I get it.  I needed to lower the heat, cook for a longer time, not brush the parchment sheets with the rum-butter mixture and even flatten the cookie.  I'm really glad that everyone else loved these cookies, but I will not be making them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Only had about 2/3 c confectioners sugar on hand, so they certainly aren’t sweet. I’d make them again, but probably with 1 c of the confectioners sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were, among my family, the biggest hit of all the holiday cookies I made this year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I made three types of cookies for the family Christmas dinner, all NYT-recipes, and these were everyone's absolute favorite.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Soooo delicious.  And gluten-free!  I am adding them to my regular "required" celebration recipes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 40g dollops of dough (it’s too runny to scoop) made 15 very large cookies. If you need the full 18 count, use 33 grams/cookie. Yummy, but not spherical like the picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It was SO good!  I followed the recipe and exactly what it was and watched the video , which was very helpful .  the results were just fantastic. I will bake this again and again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy, delicious, repeat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious but I don't know why mine spreads so much

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fabulous cookies,  just as described in the video.  I made a substitution using Amaretto instead of rum.  In my mind it lent more of a Valentine flavor than Christmas taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the guidance on halfing  the buttered rum and rolling dough in the butter prior to rolling in almond slivers. Came out of the oven and they are delicious. Not overly boozy which is great, I also added some vanilla extract along with almond extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Should the inside of the cookie be moist? It looks like the inside is bit cooked all the way through but the outside was fine. Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the recipe is good, I just made the cookies too large. They would not cook through, then by the time they were edible the outside was crunchy. Lessons were learned 😂

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is NOT an easy recipe.  Three bowls dirty, roll the cookies, then roll them in sliced almonds, then somehow put the melted butter on them?  An easy recipe uses one bowl, and you scoop the cookie dough onto the cookie sheet with minimal fuss.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these, and they are fantastic! I plan to make them again for our annual holiday cookie party. Unlike a few others here, I had exactly the right amount of butter mixture—I recommend following the recipe exactly and not cutting down on it. These are not super boozy, so if you want more rum flavor, you may want to add a little more liquor to the butter mixture and the dough. But I found the flavor subtle and pleasant.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Gotta say the video was a delight to watch! Can’t wait to make these. 😍

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made these three times, and they've been terrific every time, but they always spread/flatten.  I tried refrigerating the cookie balls overnight before baking, but they still spread.  Any ideas?  I live at high altitude (5800 feet); is elevation the issue?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@AEL
It might be the altitude. Check out the King Arthur Flour website. Lots of reliable info on baking at high altitude, and gluten free, etc

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy and so incredibly delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m curious if anyone has tried coating the balls in pine nuts, which would make more like pignoli cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added some melted semi sweet chocolate on top and it really did the trick

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Forwarned: These are addictive, BOTH the dough and the baked cookie! If you're on a diet, skip! 
After reading comments I made the following adjustments:
--doubled all the rum
--added 1/4 tsp rum to the just-baked cookies before the the buttered rum topping
--Basted 1 cookie sheet w/ buttered rum as directed, left one sheet plain. Both baked the cookies with a "crust"
--Seemed quite salty too me, so I didn't put the flaked salt on top
--Yes, 2 cups almonds, didn't use all of them

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you double the recipe, it also doubles the cookie amount in grams, which leads to way too big of almond drops! 40g is perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very tasty!  And so nice to have a GF option for family gatherings.  I definitely agree with others that more rum is needed, and that there is a lot of the butter glaze left over.  My second batch will double the rum; half the butter; and I’m going to very slightly chop my almonds.  My cookies came out looking a bit wild, not the prettiest.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies are good. I doubled the recipe because 18 cookies are hardly enough for a family. But the butter is way too much. A half cup would have been more than enough for even the doubled. And really, not much almond taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Absolutely loved these cookies, and they stayed nicely for a week in a Tupperware! Two suggestions:

1. Refrigerating the dough before balling makes the dough a bit easier to ball/roll in almonds
2. If doubling, no need to double rum butter unless you want to drench the cookies in rum butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A wonderful, quick holiday cookie—I used Frangelico as I didn’t have rum—delicious! Recommended making them a little smaller to get two dozen or so, easier to eat.
Such good flavors! A hit at the cookie exchange.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can you substitute almond paste and use self rising flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These baked up nicely but I didn't taste the rum that much. I may have mis-measured the rum in the butter and only put 1 tbsp, though :-/ Some other commenters mentioned using orange zest which I regret not doing! These do look nice and I like the texture and almond flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. I added dark chocolate chips to expand the recipe for a cookie exchange. I also substituted roasted almonds with sea salt. They were in the pantry and no one was eating them. This is a great gluten free cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would like to make these but realized I’m out of almond extract. Will I be ok with just vanilla extract?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had high hopes, the combination of almond flour and rum butter sounded delicious - the end result is meh. I ran out of almond extract and had to use a little less than half a teaspoon, but made up for it with equal parts of vanilla extract and rum, otherwise followed the recipe.
