The dough is very sticky, rolling the dough with wet hands is recommended. Even at 20 grams each you can't eat them in one bite, the texture is very dense. If I make them again, I will definitively increase the sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Update: We hoped the cookies would improve in flavor after a day or two - they didn't. Absolutely not worth the extra $$$ for the superfine almond flour. I try not to bin food, but these went into the trash with no regrets.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: followed directions exactly and got an odd, unfortunate cookie.  nope.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the 40g scoop may be a little too big. My first batch burned on the bottom (even with the butter mixture on the parchment) but the cookies weren’t entirely baked through. I think next time I’ll try a 30g scoop and see if that bakes better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Winner! I only got 15 cookies while using my scoop that says it's 1.5Tbs... I think next time I'll make sure the scoop isn't super full, b/c they were fairly large for my preference. I had a good amount of almonds left over, also, so next time I'll scale those back a bit just so I'm not wasting as much. I was worried these would be super sweet, but not at all - delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Obviously I don't know nut flours well.  When I read the tip, I was so super-focused on the phrase "super-fine."  I found a package that says natural super-fine almond flour (Vaughn's tip  only said not to buy natural almond flour) and bought it.   Realized after making the cookies that Vaughn was emphasizing the word 'blanched" over "natural."   The resulting cookies were awful; I dont think Ill try the recipe again for a good while.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Weighed my cookie balls and got 14 cookies out of this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with the eggnog snickerdoodles and they were so good. I used 1/4 tsp rum extract with orange juice instead of rum in the butter mixture, since I already had the rum extract for the snickerdoodles. It turned out great and I feel better about letting my 3 year old eat them without the alcohol. That being said, I’d love to make them with real rum someday!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Love the toasted almond and butter flavor. Don't get much rum flavor, but enough for me.  I'd recommend (like others to use half the amount of melted butter for brushing on top of the cookies.  I had 1/4 - 1/3 cup of melted butter left over - which killed me to not have a use for.
Made smaller cookies:  30g per cookie and yielded 21 even cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added to batter: 1/2 tsp. cinnamon; 1/4 tsp. nutmeg; To butter mixture added: 1/4 tsp. nutmeg' 1/4 tsp. cinnamon. Flavor was a lot closer to hot buttered rum. Loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies have good flavor but are almost cloyingly sweet- that's why I dislike using powdered sugar. I feel like if I reduced the sugar it would effect the overall structure of the cookie, so I don't think I will make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine cracked. Did anyone else experience this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are terrific!Based on comments, I used a half stick of butter, but kept the other ingredients the same except adding an extra tablespoon of rum in the rum butter and an extra teaspoon of rum and a 1/2 teaspoon of vanilla to the batter. I made half of them 1T size and half 2T size and definitely preferred the smaller size. The bigger size felt like eating a piece of cake! Some complained that these aren't that sweet, but that and the subtle rum flavor were the best qualities!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These don’t spread at all! They do puff up a little bit, but you can really fill the tray here. I used Grand Marnier— couldn’t taste it a ton, will measure with my heart next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The interior of the cookie is tasty, but the rolling in almonds and twice brushing with rum butter really added little to my batch except for a lot of extra work and burning the bottoms. If I made these again, I think I’d just make the dough and a little bit of rum butter to brush over once before going into the oven, and save myself the finickiness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is the cookie I thought about all holiday season but just got around to making today, January 22, after my pants were starting to feel less snug post holiday.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These aren’t as good as the much simpler almond balls in the Blackbird Bakery cookbook.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed recipe as written and found the cookies surprisingly good for being gluten-free. I felt like they were a tinge underbaked. Next time, I will try lowering the oven temperature to 325 and leaving them in a bit longer. The level of rum and almond flavors were spot on - no adjustments needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have made these several times and they are delicious, gorgeous and easy to make. Two pointers: to obtain 18 cookies from a single batch, the weight of each cookie, prior to rolling in almonds, should be 35 gms.  Number two, make the rum butter just prior actually needing it to brush parchment and cookies before placing into the oven. (It takes very little time to make the rum butter and the longer it sits out the more likely it is to begin to solidify, easy to remedy but, easier to avoid.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this tonight. Everyone loved it. My notes: 

--70 grams of sugar in the filling was plenty
--1/4 c water in the caramel works better than the 2T listed. The sugar does not dissolve easily enough with only 2T (I know this because I had to throw my first batch of caramel out and start over).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Bubbles, I agree. Also, I can’t remember where I read this suggestion, but recently I have started putting a plate on the sauce pan a couple of times when it looks like the sugar is about to crystalize. It rehydrates the sugar and prevents it from crystallizing. I prefer this method instead of using a brush and water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How is the prep 15 minutes? It took me that long to read the directions. Much as I love pie and love the sound of this pie- it would take me all day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Amy my first thought too! Then I worked out how much sugar per portion…..

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Amy if you consider prep to be assembling the ingredients, then 15 is about right. (I toasted the coconut while crust was baking so didn’t consider it as prep.) But yeah, it’s a very involved process! A nice change from our usuals but I don’t think it will earn a permanent spot in my pie rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the instructions as written led to an excessively hard caramel layer. Had to throw it out and start over twice. For a chewy caramel layer, caramelize the sugar in 1/4 cup water by bringing it to 360 degrees, not 320. Then add cream and bring to 230 degrees not 250.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Paul Gunn I get why you’d cook it to a lower temperature in the second step for a softer caramel, but what’s the reason you cook it with more water and to a higher temperature in the first step?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Laura after failing twice I started googling and learned that the consistency of cooled caramelized sugar depends on the temp to which the sugar is heated and that sugar caramelized at 320 will cool to a brittle consistency while sugar caramelized at 350-360 will cool to a softer consistency. Of course another strategy to produce a softer final consistency is to add cream and butter to make a caramel syrup. And as you clearly already know, the consistency of the cooled syrup will depend on the final temp to which the syrup is heated and a temp of 230 will result in a softer syrup than one heated to 250. In my case, I made both changes and got the texture right in the final product. But perhaps heating the sugar to 360 wasn’t necessary to that success and the critical step was to not overheat the final syrup? I guess I’ll have to try that next time. Cheers!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Paul Gunn this is helpful, thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could this be made in a square pan, then cut into small bars? It sounds divine, but I think I’d rather have bite sized portions rather than a whole slice of this pie. It’s enough calories for my whole day!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree that this would work well in small bars. It's nice in a very thin slice, but as a piece of pie it's too much--heavy, dense, and a bit dry. The best thing about this recipe, for me, is that in making it I learned to make caramel.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What would be the best nut-free flour alternative for this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Erika coconut

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Erika I wondered the same because although I’m not allergic, almonds battle with my digestion. Web search suggests oat flour is an acceptable 1:1 substitute when baking, so that’s what I’m going to try. Wheat flour is okay, if gluten formation is not a concern. A potential problem with coconut flour (suggested in another reply) is that it absorbs 5-8x as much water as almond flour so might mess up the texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing flavor and everyone loved this tart! Note the cool down times needed, definitely makes it take longer than I thought. Err on cooking the caramel slower and cooler, I think I overcooked it which made the top part hard to cut and chew. Still yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vegan tips?  Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
To veganize:
--For each egg, you can substitute 1T of ground flax seed plus 2.5T water (let soak for a few minutes first).
--For the butter, just use vegan butter or coconut oil.
--Use coconut milk instead of heavy cream in the caramel.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Bubbles what kind of coconut milk do you use?  Just regular coconut milk out of a can?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made this earlier this week and while I loved the coconut filling and caramel on top, the crust was dry and flavorless. I'd recco just saving yourself some time and either making it a bar with an oreo crust or just doing plain shortbread on the bottom-- GS Samoas are shortbread on the inside anyway! Maybe just add a bit more chocolate drizzle at the end ;)

Tips I followed from other comments:
--70 grams of sugar in the filling 
--1/4 c water in the caramel works better than the 2T listed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you're looking for a nut free crust google Gluten Free chocolate pie crust. I found a coconut flour based crust.  I've been able to interchange those elements in regular recipes and I think it will work.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can gluten free flour be used for the crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: High degree of difficulty. Caramel recipe needs to be reworked. Made it twice and it was too hard both times. I ended up with a toffee-like crust on the top, which was good but not yielding to the tooth. I used heavy cream instead of coconut cream, and did not use a candy thermometer. Probably advisable to add the butter one chunk at a time to dissolve, then add coconut.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Andy the caramel recipe is fine as is, the candy thermometer is just a necessary tool unless you’re an old hand at candy making and can recognize soft ball stage by sight.  Caramel can move from liquid to soft ball to hard crack pretty quickly, so without the thermometer, it’s pretty easy to undercook or overcook your caramel.  Dumping all of your butter in at once is just fine.  I’ve never done it any other way when making caramel.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The tart is beautiful but mine came out tasting dry overall. I used the 10" tart pan - perhaps a smaller pie plate would hold moisture better. Also too sweet for my taste. I think it's quite a bit of work with a higher degree of difficulty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JC mine was also dry. I used a 9” pie dish and we all thought it was dry… very pretty but not the decadent treat we expected.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really enjoyed this recipe, I would agree that it is super helpful to watch the pie video on their youtube. One issue that I ran into, is to make sure that if you are using mortons salt like I do, make sure you reduce the salt by half! Ours was salty but still delicious, great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this over the weekend end everyone enjoyed it! I did make a few modifications, based on other comments and ingredient availability:

— 70 grams of sugar in the filling (rather than 100 grams)
— 1/4 c water to the sugar for the caramel (rather than 2T
— for the crust and filling, I reduced the cooking time by ~2 minutes (this will depend on how calibrated your oven is)
— I used coconut flakes rather then shredded coconut sand this added to

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: That last sentence should read: I used coconut flakes rather than shredded coconut and this added a really nice texture to the caramel layer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made in a 10” tart pan and served with vanilla ice cream. Everyone had seconds! Next time I’ll make it in a square pan and cut into bars since it’s challenging to cut through with a fork. Some of us picked it up with our fingers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: if pressed for time - could i just use a premade chocolate crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cooling time seems to be an underestimate to me.  I wish I had made and blind baked the crust a day ahead.  My only change to the recipe was to add oat flour (same volume) in place of the almond, as one of our guests has an almond allergy.  I don't know what it would be like with the almond, but it was absolutely delicious with the oat if anyone else needs to make the swap.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Quite involved by very tasty. The walls of the shell partially collapsed when blind-baking and there was no room for the caramel layer so I made a sauce instead. Incidentally, also much easier than a 'real' caramel: dissolve 0.75c brown sugar in 1c coconut milk, add 1/4tsp salt (or more to taste), simmer for 15-20 mins until it coats the back of a spoon. Add some vanilla, 1.5tbsp coconut oil (for firmness when cold); add toasted coconut; cool; serve with the tart. Everybody loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie was almost spectacular. Almost. I followed it to a T, but the caramel set way too hard. It turned into a brick - people basically peeled it off the top and ate the rest. If it would've set properly, it would've been phenomenal. I even tried making the caramel component a few times, until I ran out of ingredients. Don't let the intro language fool you, this is still a really tough technique to master. I even watched their video for this on Youtube like 10 times - didn't prevent it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @RachelI had the exact same issue. I don’t know how to make the caramel without it being rock hard later.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Yes, tip for making crust by hand is below info on toasting coconut. 
Use pastry cutter, etc. it’s all in tip.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I use a can of Nestle Dulce de Leche instead of making my own caramel?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It will probably be pretty good, but it won't have the richness of flavor.  I'd suggest doing caramel, but with the dry method.  Simply put sugar in a heavy bottomed pot and use medium low heat.  As it melts, occasionally swirl the pan.  Lumps will melt out.  Add cream just as it begins to smoke, removing from heat.  If you then have an uneven texture, return to heat and stir.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This made a nice change from our usual Thanksgiving pies, but I don’t know that it will earn a permanent spot in my pie rotation. In the coconut filling I substituted oat flour for the almond, and reduced the sugar to 70 grams as suggested by others. I trimmed the crust as shown in the video but then it slumped while baking. And pro-tip: don’t take a break in the middle of caramelizing the sugar to flip over your turkey, or you risk ending up with burnt caramel hard candy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for thanksgiving. Honestly, to me, it wasn’t worth the effort. I found it sort of bland, but everyone else loved it, so it may just be me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can try half and half of bread crumbs and rice flour instead of nuts. Of course the texture will be slightly impacted but overall it should work. I saw this substitution in many British recipes for frangipane in place of nuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you think this could be made in advance — like a week or two before Thanksgiving — and then frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie is a major project. The crust is not great:  since it is baked twice it gets very dark.  I will re-work the crust next time.  All in all, it needs ice cream on top!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used King Arthur’s gluten-free flour for the chocolate crust. And I substituted coconut flour in the middle layer. I used soy cream instead of heavy cream for the top layer. I used real butter in all parts, but I browned the butter in the oven at 3:50 for an hour before I made it.  I brought this to a party last night and I have never had such a rave reviews for an item.

I would probably up the coconut in the middle layer, the flaked coconut. I also use sweeten coconut. A lot of people liked the idea that the desert is not too sweet, barely surprising, considering the top layers caramel. But I agree with that assessment.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had a hard time with this, and wasn't thrilled with the results.  The crust seemed overdone at the recommended time/temp, I'd do less time/lower temp next time.  And for the caramel topping cooking the sugar and water to caramel was fine, but after I added the coconut milk and was cooking the temp went up really slowly for a long time then shot up at the end so it ended up over 250, and the result was more of a soft crumble than a spreadable topping. Consequently the whole pie was kind of dry.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I experimented with the recipe in a 8x8 square pan because I wanted to cut it in squares for a party. MadI followed the recipe, video and instructions exactly. Perfect outcome. Thanks Vaughn, your video was fun.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I experimented with this recipe in a square 8 x 8 pan so I could cut it in smaller squares. I followed the instructions. I made the crust the night before. It came out perfectly. Your video was funny! Thank you, Vaughn.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone had a problem with the filling being too soupy?  I knew when I creamed the butter & sugar & then added the eggs that the consistency & color wasn't what it should be.  It was too moist then. Not fluffy.  I've creamed butter & sugar enough for me to be concerned.  It took 30min in the oven & while it did firm up & puff up, I feel it may still be too moist.  I used coconut flakes in the filling, but creaming the butter & sugar was done before those went in.  Any thoughts or insight?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: On Nov 22 or 23 I asked if the quantities of coconut in the Filling and Topping had mistakenly been reversed and I'm guessing that if the Filling had used 1 cup of coconut instead of 1/2 cup that that might have lessened the "soupy" characteristic

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a labor but results were worth it. Very rich dessert, I made this in a 17x4 in tart pan (crust recipe adapts to this shape   had some dough leftover for a 4 in mini tart pan) and we served it in one inch slivers. Tastes just like the girl scout cookies of my childhood. Agree that you can reduce sugar in filling to 70g and use more water for sugar caramelization.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This crust is made of crust!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I followed the recipe, and YouTube video, but it didn’t quite work out. A few lessons learned:
- do not use the weight for the coconut, use the measuring cup size 
- take the caramel off heat early once you add the coconut milk, mine ended up really solid once it set (too hard to cut)
- make the dough day before 
- really great taste!
-  1 to recommendations to use 1/4 cup of water when starting your sugar in the pan

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Crusted separated and fell in twice.  I double checked oven temp and added a pie shield the second time.  Caramel also a struggle.  Ultimately threw this out twice and wasted half my Thanksgiving prep day.  My sister is one her second batch of caramel.  Will move on to a better tested recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Did anyone else have trouble with the caramel? I'm on my third attempt right now. One lot stuck to the pot and the second batch crystalized.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had trouble.  One batch heat too high and burned; 2nd batch heat too low and never colored.  Caramel is very tricky.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sanibel Sally i messed mine up so bad 😩 mine crystallized and turned basically into a crumble lol. it was good but man 😅

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not good enough for the labor and skill investment. Using the times in the recipe, the crust was over cooked by the time it was baked with the custard.  Should have used my little foil edge protectors.  The custard filling was a doddle, but the caramel layer was a horror.  My first attempt got a lovely color, then burned. 2nd try didn't color at all.  Used it anyway.  Tasted ok but with the mixing, cooling, baking, cooling, plotzing and schussing, I found it more trouble than it was worth.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Whoever said making caramel was achievable was lying to you. It took me 4 attempts and a completely different approach (thanks YouTube!) to get to something that even closely resembled caramel color.  I also had to remake the pie crust twice bc it kept falling apart. I ended up just using my fingers to press the dough directly into the tart pan and worked fine! This pie takes time but is definitely worth the effort! Made it for thanksgiving yesterday and it was a huge hit. My friends were raving about it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The dough for the crust did not roll out for me but scooping it up and pressing it into the 10” tart pan worked just fine. I will do that from the start next time. Never made caramel so I heeded the advice to up the water to 1/4C with perfect results. The rest of the directions were followed to a T and each layer baked up as described. The result was delicious! Winner of yummiest dessert of the Thanksgiving spread!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There is someone in my house with a nut allergy.  Is there a good substitute for almond flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My first batch of caramel crystallized and second batch ALMOST crystallized, until I was saved by an online tip to add a bit of corn syrup, which prevents crystallization. I added 1 tbsp to the second batch and had no issues from there (added it straight to the water and sugar after most of the sugar had dissolved, pre boil). 

I also added about 1 tsp of coconut oil to my melted chocolate at the end to make it easier to drizzle. There’s 2 non-purist tips for you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The amount of salt in the caramel topping made the pie almost inedible. Would recommend cutting the amount in HALF. Would also recommend baking the pie crust at 425 for less time, to avoid it slumping into the pan. Other than that, a great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for New Years. I took the advice of others and used less sugar (70 g instead of 100g). I will say that if I try this again, I would either use more than 70 g or use less salt. The saltiness came through even without the salt flakes on top. I used 1/4c for caramel & a candy thermometer using the 230 as final cutoff. The pie aged well. The crust would not adhere to the middle layer and kept falling off so not good for a pie. The recipe would work better as squares.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One other note - the weights and measuring cups / spoons info doesn't align - it's particularly egregious with the coconut flakes. I'd go with measuring cups vs grams. The first time I made the caramel (w/ grams) it was so out of whack, the ratios were wildly off.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There is no tip on how to prepare the crust by hand - although it says "See Tip".

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Pindie I can see the Tip! I am in the app.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Pindie  It’s there, all the way at the bottom.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Tips
To toast coconut, bake on a sheet pan in a 350 degree oven until golden brown, 4 to 7 minutes, stirring halfway through.
To make the crust by hand, whisk the dry ingredients, then cut the butter into it using a pastry cutter or your fingers until fine crumbs form. Mix in the egg and vanilla until a ball of dough forms.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is very rich. The butter alone in one slice contains the daily RDA for fat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
No, a little over 2 T. butter is not the daily RDA for fat.  Figure baseline 2000 cal. for easy math. Even the lowest guideline is 20% calories from fat, which is 400 calories.  There's about 250 calories from butter per slice.  Bear in mind, depending on personal needs, the percentage of fat in a diet can be up to 40%.

But, really.  It's a holiday dessert.  It's one's obligation to blow way past US RDA on the day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can one substitute hazelnut flour for almond flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Shredded coconut for both the filling and the topping? Or does the topping use flakes instead? Does it matter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes, need a non-nut flour substitute, please!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can the dough be made ahead of time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had great success making this with Earth Balance vegan butter (note: I used eggs because I'm dairy-free, not vegan). EB worked beautifully for all three segments - I definitely recommend! :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: wondering if Coconut Filling needs 1 cup coconut rather than 1/2 and Topping needs 1/2 cup coconut

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm making this now by special request, but it's my maiden voyage with this one. Definitely pay attention to the 1/4 Cup water when making the carame and it seems like that worked. My coconut filling is taking longer to cook, about 34 minutes for a clean toothpick in the middle. I've been checking every 4 minutes since the low end of 20 minutes. I'm using a pie crust protector to cover the edges of the tart or it would be burnt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do the tips not show up in the iPhone app? Normally they’re at the end of the cooking instructions but I don’t see them. Fortunately the comments have the instructions on how to toast the coconut.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tyson they should show up, but in recent days I’ve had recipes which only partially load. Occasional situation so I’m not sure if it’s an app bug or a network problem.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Took a long time, but yummy. The crust slid down, despite doing all the cooling/freezing. Had no problem using just two tablespoons water in making the caramel topping.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a favorite at Thanksgiving. It does take a long time to make because it's layered and has to cool at almost every step. Definitely use closer to 1/4 cup of water when making the caramel to get the right consistency. I'd make again when I have enough time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Wow! Made this for Thanksgiving yesterday to ensure that guests who had lactose sensitivity had a delish dessert. It did not disappoint. Thanks to everyone who suggested 1/4 cup of water for the caramel. Tried the 2T and that did not work. Other than that, I made no other changes. It was a huge hit. Highly recommend. 

Had a small slice with my coffee this morning while the house was very quiet. Wonderful!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made this for Thanksgiving. Sooooo good! My notes:
For the caramel…1/4 cup water, brought to 360F, added coconut milk, brought to 230F (thanks Phil) 1/3 cup less toasted coconut and I added 1/2 cup toasted pecans 😉. Came out soft and chewy! 
I used a different recipe for the chocolate crust (based on reviews).
While this recipe is extremely good, it’s more like a decadent candy bar than a pie. Next time I’ll make it in a square pan and cut it into 2x1” bites.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This was a labor of love. Now that I’ve made it once though I expect it’ll be easier next time. It does require a lot of cooling and attention. I don’t recommend trying to make it while you’re cooking other things. 

- Watching the video as you go is really helpful!
- I made my dough twice because I over trimmed it and it slid in a ceramic pie dish the first time. I chilled the dough overnight the second time. It still shrunk a bit in a metal tart pan, but overall was a better result and ended up being the perfect size for the filling.
- I used 100 grams of sugar but will reduce to 70 next time as suggested in other comments. It was a bit too sweet for me but others still liked it.
- The 2 tbsp of water worked with the addition of more water to brush the sides. The caramel seized up when I added the coconut milk but came back to the right texture after 10 more minutes of cooking and continuous stirring.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This turned out okay - not stellar, but people liked it.  I had problems with the caramel - whether the candy thermometer was malfunctioning or not, it would not register more than 200F (then tried an instant read thermometer, which kept switching from Fahrenheit to Centigrade), yet it was already smelling burnt and was still not as dark as in the video.  And despite some others' comments about being overly sweet, I had to add a lot of sugar to the caramel to make it at all palatable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was amazing! I saved a step by using 3/4 cup Cara-Salted Caramel Sauce in the top layer (warmed just enough to easily incorporate the coconut into it before spreading on top of the almond layer). It was the star of our Thanksgiving dessert table.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving to offer something different and everyone loved it! I followed another commenter to start my caramel mixture with 1/4 c of water and it was the right move. This is a delicious chocolate crust that I will using with other recipes too!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the notes from other users very helpful.  It's a great dessert!  I left off the chocolate drizzle, and it was quite delicious even without that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I should have known NOT to make this after reading the other comments about the difficulty with the caramel.  Starting at medium heat and then increasing to medium high was way too hot and resulted in a burnt mess.  The caramel cooking heat in the recipe needs to be re-worked.  I gave it one star because the coconut filling was good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sort of a disaster.  I should have read the comments.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for xmas dinner today. For the crust, the recipe says chill then cut it level with the top. But then it will "slide" down when baked. For a perfect crust up the sides, bake, then trim, similar to doing quiche. I agree the caramel needs  bit more water to avoid seizing. Mine seized as I don't have a brush handy to keep the sides crystal-free so I had to add water and roll back a step to let it dissolve properly. Good recipe overall.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Christmas and it was a hit! It was quite rich so small slices were plenty. The times were all off for me while cooking — the filling was still totally soupy at the suggested time and I had to bake it for more like 35 mins. The caramel came together wayyy faster than the suggested time. Im glad I paid attention to the visual cues and smells so that it still turned out great. Watching the video beforehand helped too. I found the discrepancy in times weird, as this doesn’t usually happen to me with other NYT recipes. I love Vaughn though so I won’t complain. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is very good. The hardest part is the caramel.  I took the caramel a bit too far so it set too hard. It was very challenging to cut and eat with a fork. It was fine if you ate it by hand more like a bar. I would make this again and when I do I'd err on the side of the caramel being too loose. As long as you've got some good color on the caramel it will taste great. If the caramel is a little too loose you can serve it chilled.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was good, but I agree with others that it was a lot of effort and steps for a just-okay result. I made it the day before eating though, so it might have been more satisfying when it was fresh. I followed others' advice and heated the caramel to around 350 the first time, then ~235 after adding the coconut milk. Like that, it worked fine and the final texture was good. I think an actual shortbread crust would be better than the chocolate pie dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie couldn't decide if it wanted to be a pie or a cookie. It was dry and chewy and really not pleasant to eat. I would not make this again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I give this a no based on the effort to taste ratio, combined with the decent chance for errors. I will still buy Samoas from my favorite Girl Scout though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I expected this to be tasty but I was truly unprepared for how incredibly delicious it turned out to be. I took the advice of other comments and got the sugar up pretty high, it was quite dark and very firm at that stage. I thought I'd messed it up but it melted down slowly once I added the coconut milk. It did require patience and I didn't heat it past 220 with the milk added. It was perfection! Perfectly matched the texture of the under layer and the whole thing was not too sweet at all! Will make this many more times for sure, one of the best recipes I've ever made! Thank you Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Despite what the "Tip" says Golden Delicious apples are NEVER preferred.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've been making a lot of apple pies this fall, and cortland apples result in a lovely pie - they hold their shape, but are not too hard, and have a wonderful apple cider like flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nice if you don't live on the West Coast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: West Coasters, grab up the Gravensteins in August. I know it feels early for apple pie but you can freeze your filling. Supporting small apple farmers keeps heirloom gems like these in existence. And what a pie apple;)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There are plenty of late season apples on the west coast that balance the tart and structure you need for a good pie.  Find a local farmer like Dave Hale or Stan DeVoto both in Sebastopol at a local farmers market and they will steer you towards their amazing heirloom apples that are best for cooking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I know this is heresy, please don't throw stones, but I really don't want to bother with the laminated pie dough.  I will use a simpler crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can this be made with pomegranate molasses? What modifications re: liquid or sugar?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
If you can find Stayman Winesap apples, they are my go-to apple for pies, superior to Granny Smith, IMO.  They are very tart and firm, not the prettiest apples, but it's the taste and texture that matter!

They are "late" apples, and I picked some here in a central Jersey orchard on Halloween, and made 2 excellent pies. If you have never tried Stayman Winesaps for baking, give them a shot.  They are exceptional, and I am surprised the Times does not mention them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jo Ann I just got Stayman Winesaps at our last farmers market and immediately thought of them when I saw this recipe, I’ll be making it for a Thanksgiving gathering

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Jo Ann 
We have a winesap tree here in our apple grove in PA. They make wonderful pies, but they are an old fashioned apple and not easily found. Just took it out of the oven. We’ll see.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Jo Ann 
I love Winesap apples, but have only found them once in CA - in Mendocino county.  I live in so Cal, never see them here.  I grew up in TN and used to have them as a kid.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This grew on me. Day 1, when the recipe was published, I was underwhelmed by the pie, but after a night in the fridge, the flavors, which are subtle, came out. Now I would make it again! Love the laminated crust and have been doing a Jim Dodge or Claire Saffitz version for a while.

I would imagine pomegranate molasses could be used - just add in the cardamom, ginger and lemon zest/juice and don't bother reducing it, just cook the apples in it immediately.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Anne A - Agreed!  Followed the directions to the letter, including the laminated dough. Don’t skip it! It’s one of the best pie doughs I’ve ever tasted. Had a nice mix of apples on the east coast - I think Jazz, Pink Lady, definitely Honeycrisp and Macs. This pie was nice on Thanksgiving but outstanding the next day.  The cardamom is at just the right level; not overpowering. The flavors are all balanced and complementary. My daughter loved it so much she is begging me to make it again this year. I think I will!  It’s a keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
What is the original oven temperature?
In Step 7 it states to lower it to 350F

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 400F, it says it in Step 3

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this exactly as instructed in the recipe for a Friendsgiving gathering last night and it was WONDERFUL!  The laminated pie dough is the best pie-dough experience I've ever had.  I love pie, but have always struggled with the dough.  This one was terrific to work with and turned out super flaky and luscious.  The flavor of this pie is also great--the pomegranate cardamom ginger syrup gives the pie a holiday feel but is also a little unexpected.  My guests loved it and so did I!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Northern Spies are for pies, as the saying goes.  I combined the Spies with 2 large Cortlands and this is an amazing pie. I froze the rest of the pomegranite juice in 1-cup containers for the next pies. The spice is perfect. I always have fresh-grated ginger in the freezer. 

Next, we'll test the new Sesame-Swirled Pumpkin Pie recipe from Vaughn Vreeland. I'm already thinking these two will be our Thanksgiving pies and become new family favorites.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this yesterday and was thrilled with the flavors. Used a basic (original Joy of Cooking butter crust) and 3/1 ratios of Honeycrisp and Granny Smith apples. Cut the apples slices about .33 inches thick, and then just tossed them in the pomegranate syrup rather than cooking them. It’s a wonderful variant on an apple pie that I’ll definitely bake again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The filling was too sweet.  Also, next time, I won't cook the apples before putting them in the crust.  When I did, they were over cooked, i.e., mushy, but very tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thoughts on making the filling the day before and then assembling and baking the pie on the day you want to serve it? I’m wondering if that would give it enough time for the flavors to deepen while also ensuring everything has enough time to cool and just saving time on the final day?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Courtney, I’m also wondering this. Did you try it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent pie! Did as instructed, preparing it in advance (pie dough Monday, filling Tuesday, baking it Wednesday). Used a mix of apples, Black Willow Twig, Norther Spy and McIntosh, all from the farmers market on the capitol square in Madison. Stuck with a pie dough recipe I've used for years, one learned in a L'Etoile Restaurant cooking class going way back. This pie recipe rivals the  Governor's Favorite Cran-Apple Pie, Norske Nook cook book, that I make every year for Christmas. Enjoy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My daughter made it for our Thanksgiving and it was absolutely delicious. The pomegranate adds a wonderful taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I omit the heavy cream to make it vegan?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Doriander that’s just to brush the dough before baking so I’m sure you could omit it and use something else

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was going to get nondairy heavy cream and use it the same way!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Butter ain't vegan either

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Black or green cardamom?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: green

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make a lot of crusts and will default to my favorite. This was underdone beneath, maybe being baked low in the oven? The top crust was beautiful, but too chewy. The filling is sublime.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this using the cardamom in my cupboard which I think is the black. The flavors are yummy fantastic. It was a practice bake for Tgiving. I used 1/2 Granny Smith and 1/2 Honeycrisp, but cooking the apples in the pomegranate syrup i think contributed to honeycrisp mush I’ll going use 100% Granny Smith for Tgiving.  I think I’ll  reduce the time the apples are on the stove. The mature pie was amazing and super sweet-tasted like a sweet tart-trying to decide if reducing the sugar is a good idea.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could I freeze this before baking to make ahead?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @A did you freeze before baking? I’m considering doing the same

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was delicious and got rave reviews at the office. I made the pie dough and filling (2/3 Honeycrisp, 1/3 Granny Smith) on Sunday, baked Monday night, and ate Tuesday afternoon. I don’t have a deep dish pie pan so I offloaded some of the filling into apple-pie cupcakes—worth the work!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Margaret did you refrigerate after baking before serving, or keep it at room temperature?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone subbed pomegranate molasses? And if so what quantity?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn, if I make the filling today will it keep in the fridge covered overnight so I can bake the pie freshly tomorrow, or would it be best to bake tonight and leave out covered?  Thanks!  Pie dough was so easy and a lot more fun than using my food processor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I needed two medium pomegranates to get a scant cup of juice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie is best enjoyed cold, straight from the fridge!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and the filling was just mush. I used 3 Grannysmith and 3 Honey Crisp, all very firm. I wonder if I should have moved the pie to a higher rack when switching to 350. (I did 400 for first 15 min and 350 for 60 min.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Big hit 2 days ago at Thanksgiving! I used red honeycrisps and they were wonderful, renders a sweet and moist pie. My tips are: 1) Don't do a lattice; I'll make a full top crust next time; and 2) The recipe says don't cut when warm, and I learned bitterly - after the Turkey day host couldn't resist heating it despite my instruction not to - that it can't be cut and served warm. The next day the room-temp pie sliced perfectly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made it with my regular pie dough and it worked fine. The recipe for the filling is outstanding. We used honey crisp and Granny Smith apples, three of each and it was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of the best pies I have ever made, and the crust is a total winner.  Use a mix of apple types and you will never make a traditional apple pie again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Everything about this recipe was wonderful, the addition of pomegranate really makes an apple pie sing. The laminated piecrust was the easiest piecrust and also the most beautiful and also the flakiest I’ve ever made. Will definitely make this again and again and again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Great recipe, best apple pie I’ve made. The pomegranate adds some of that red fruit brightness as well as the sour element of the more common addition of lemon to apple pie. An upgrade!. The cardamom was so strong added to the uncooked apples I was worried. It was better but still a bit intense after baking. By next day though the spice level was perfect, it really mellowed. Due to this I didn’t add more spice to sugar topping. 

I used store dough. Am sure homemade would be best but was still a fab pie without it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is very great!. what they say about let it sit is important. I made it as a dessert for friends and tasted the filling as it came out of the oven. I thought hmmm maybe not a good idea, But it sat for several hours in a cool spot before I served it.  It was fabulous. Everyone loved it! Nothing left; asking for second helpings. I served it with labneh.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was almost sad that this was the best apple pie I had ever eaten because it was also the most fastidious apple pie I’ve ever made. I remember telling everyone that it was alright if it was merely ok because I wouldn’t make it again. Looks like it’s back to apple pealing purgatory for me because it was too good to ever make a different recipe again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Beautiful color. The pomegranate flavor was barely discernible. Don’t completely cook the apples if you’re going to let the sauce cool naturally. The apples finish cooking with the heat from the sauce.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What temp does the pie start with for the 15 min. initial bake?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @pam its at 400

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @pam Step 3 says 400, but talk about burying the lede!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @pam 400, per step 3

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cranberry juice cocktail or pure, unsweetened cranberry juice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There's no cranberry juice; it's pomegranate juice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Costco has had some delicious Honeycrisps the past few years.   Never used them for baking.   Granny Smith is almost a cliche, but they are consistent and my faves for baking.

This is a little exotic with the cardamom and ginger, but it is an opportunity to utilize the pomegranates free on the tree here in Arizona.  Deal with the bitter seeds, and you got in made in the shade.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think pomegranate molasses would work b/c I use it what I make pear pomegranate pie, (Melissa Clarke) which has similar directions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Granny Smith apples with pomegranate molasses and didn't cook on the stove before baking. The pie was baked for 35 minutes at 425. It looked beautiful! Flavor was great! But the apples turned to apple sauce! I've never had this happen with an apple pie. Was this because of the liquid of the pomegranate molasses? I will try cutting the apples in to larger slices and not baking so long. Hope that works.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I read on another site that, despite popular opinion, Granny Smith apples do, in fact, turn to mush. I prefer solid, consistent results from a Gala or Honeycrisp apple, perhaps only using one or two Granny Smiths, Sorry for your loss. I hope you can still eat it yourself (not in one sitting, of course). Happy Thanksgiving!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I cooked my cooked apples in a steel (maybe aluminum???) rimmed baking sheet. I believe the acid from the apples reacted with the steel and turned them dark grey. Recommend lining the pan with parchment first.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Should the bottom crust be blind baked before adding the filling?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the laminated dough yesterday and filling and baking this morning. I made it as written just reduced oven temp last 30 minutes to 330 degrees. The crust was amazing and easy, yes easy, to work with. Can’t wait to eat it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would have blind baked the crust - mine was undercooked and soggy. The filling was delicious. The cardomom was a lovely addition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: so....no blind baking of the crust before adding the filling? normally with such a wet filling, i would blind bake at least for 15 minutes.  any thoughts/experiences on this front?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fabulous! Used King Arthur Measure for Measure gluten free flour for crust, otherwise followed recipe exactly. The best pie at Thanksgiving, ruby red apples, crisp sugary lattice! One of the guests was a pastry chef at a 3 star Michelin restaurant and she loved it! On cloud nine today!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you're wondering why there aren't actual pomegranate seeds in this, don't make the mistake of putting them in the filling. I tried this and they made the texture rather unpleasant and I ended up laboriously picking them all out before putting the filling in the pie. Luckily, the recipe is great. I was worried that there was too much cardamom, but after the pie was baked it didn't taste overpowering any more. This is a lovely variation on apple pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This pie was a huge hit at thanksgiving. It’s so simple and understated, and goes fantastically with some vanilla ice cream or whipped cream, and a little wine or bourbon on the side. I will definitely be making this often. 

I did not mess with the laminated crust, but just used a simple shortening crust. A butter crust would probably be ideal. Don’t skimp on the sugar topping. I pressed the pomegranate juice myself which was worth it, since I had some great juice poms. I live this pie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m an experienced pie baker. I followed the instructions to a T, including the instructions for which apples to use. I had my doubts about an apple pie using white sugar instead of brown but this recipe has five stars. The flavor of the pie filling was so sharp and citrusy that we could barely eat it. It went to the compost bin this morning. My family was annoyed at me for trying something new. I’ll go back to Joy of Cooking’s tried and true recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I followed this recipe pretty closely, save for a couple simple vegan modifications:
- salted miyokos butter I had on hand for the laminated pie dough linked in the recipe (just used half the salt in the dough)
- Trader Joe’s vegan heavy whipping cream to brush
I let it sit out overnight, and this pie was so perfect. The crust baked beautifully and the granny smith / honey crisp apple slices held their shape without being too crisp. The pie wasn’t overly sweet, but the cardamom was lovely and the pomegranate was such a bright touch my husband (who’s usually more of a rhubarb man) especially appreciated. Adding this to my holiday rotation from now on. Thank you so very!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried making this as a crisp rather than pie?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it with pears!!!  Good quality ones are generally more seasonal than apples.  I find they have a more complex flavor and mouth feel when baked.  Since they are sweeter than GS apples I slightly reduced the sugar.  Based on previous substitutions they can handle gutsy spices.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you sub cornstarch for arrowroot starch? I have an allergy 😔. Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Apples: Ida Red, Empire and Cortlands are crisp and flavorful, I like them on the tart side, since I’m adding sugar. I like a mix of apples. A good firm apple is a good pie apple. Delicious apples should be banned, cardboard! I find honeycrisp good for eating but not so good in a pie. I used my own crust, which I like to make, nice flakey crust, the lattice nice looking but fussy and too much handling. The pomegranate and cardamon are a great ideas! Why cook the apples ahead of the baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The pomegranate juice, apples, and cardamom combine to make for a delicious pie with complex flavor. Like others, I used a simple crust recipe and it turned out fine. The filling was very dark – almost gray – but I’m guessing this was because I cooled them on a metal sheet pan that caused discoloration.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried preparing the apples and syrup , then refrigerating overnight sso you can quickly assemble pie and bake in the morning?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving, finally giving a review.  The laminate pie crust was the easiest crust I've every made and was the most delicious and best texture.  This pie really elevates an apple pie which can lack the zing my family prefers.  And it's more beautiful. I might try tossing in a cup of pomegranate seeds next time for a fun burst and crunch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie is simply fabulous!!! I used different types of apples we have here in Italy and it worked very well with the filling, the pieces soft but not "melted" and not too hard. Using top quality cardamom is a must to have the right flavor and taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hello! Can you make the filling a day in advance?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Crust texture is lighter than it looks.  Even after pre-cooking and 1:15 in the oven, apples a bit less softened than expected.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The pomegranate adds just the right about of tartness and depth to this delicious apple pie. We made this for Thanksgiving at it was devoured. Cooking the apples in advance of filling the pie ensured it was cooked through perfectly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought this pie was good but not stupendous. Surprisingly the cardamom and the pomegranate didn't really come through much. But it was a very respectable apple pie and pretty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this right after it was posted and used for the chess pie recipe. Worked really well for me with all of Vaughn's additional tips (watch the video!). Thank you Vaughn for the great video and recipe! This method and fat ratio is extremely similar to the book "Sister Pie" which is my normal go-to pie dough, but makes a bit more which is ideal for me (I like to have extra for decoration or if I mess up the rolling etc)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Steph I love the sister pie book! Great crusts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Steph where is the video?  My crust is a nightmare, the edge completely melted and slid into the filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ugh sorry.. intended for another of your pie recipes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Will this work work gluten free flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Suegayle

Would love to know how this recipe can be tweaked for gluten-free!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How long and at what temperature to bake this for once it’s ’ready to use’?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tyler Depends on the type of pie you’re baking. Refer to the actual pie recipe, such as the author’s  fruit-based pies and custard-based pies. If pre-baking the crust (known as blind baking) is required, the pie recipe will tell you that, and exactly how to do it (including the oven temperature).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tyler Depends on the type of pie you’re baking. Refer to the actual pie recipe, such as the author’s  fruit-based pies and custard-based pies. If pre-baking the crust (known as blind baking) is required, the pie recipe will tell you that, and exactly how to do it (including the oven temperature). The accompanying video (link is in the recipe description) is super helpful. Vaughn Vreeland demonstrates making and par-baking this pie crust recipe. Highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @JJ Thanks! I understand there are associated recipes that have baking times and temps but I was curious about using this with a totally different filling/type of pie and was just looking for guidelines. Seems silly to me to not have any nod toward general baking instructions on a dough recipe but of course the filling dictate baking as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you want extra flaky pie dough use half lard and butter. Fresh rendered lard from a butcher or farmers market is the type of lard to use. Supermarket lard should be avoided

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @john golden I agree, half butter and half lard makes a wonderful pie crust. We buy our pasture raised rendered lard on Etsy of all places! It comes in packets, and it is easy to keep one in the freezer for when you need it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe a novice's question, but how do you keep the pie crust from shrinking so much along the sides? I used sugar as a pie weight (with foil) as I didn't have beans.  This has always been an issue for me with crusts  no matter what weight used. Help!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you are making for the Coffee and Maple Chess Pie, cooking times are listed. 425 degrees, center rack. Line the firm dough with foil and fill with pie weights. 425 for 20 minutes. Remove foil and weights then cook for 20 minutes at 350.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: ugh!  don't bother.  Crust was tough and not at all "flaky".  I had to make a second pie for Thanksgiving in order to have one to eat.  All the pastry chefs can turn up their nose at the mix and roll folks, but I get a flaky crust every time.  As Mother said, mix in the water a little and then "stop handling it"...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone have trouble with the crust being tougj and chewy?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dan A yes I’ve made it twice. Can barely cut it! I pre baked in both instances. It feels totally overdone by the time the filling is done.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dan A I was thrilled with the process up til baking, so I want to try it again. I wondered if I simply rolled it too thick. (It slumped rather horrifically in the dish — owing to all butter, perhaps?)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you freeze this and use?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s mentioned in the recipe description that you can, but my question is should let it rest in the fridge first to finish hydrating, etc. before placing it into the freezer? I would think that going right into the freezer without any time in the fridge might prevent some of that process from happening. Would love to hear more thoughts on when and how (before rolled out in a plate) to freeze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn has provided this detail in the write up..."This recipe makes enough dough for two crusts: freeze the extra round of dough, if you’re not using it, for up to 4 months. When you’re ready to use it, thaw it, wrapped, overnight in the refrigerator."

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used this for the Sesame Swirl Pumpkin Pie and it was awesome! Super buttery and flaky. But the bottom layer was almost impossible to cut through.  I followed the instructions , but wondering if my oven may run a little warm and I should cut a few min off of the par-baking next time? Bit of a novice with pies so open to feedback. Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would sure love to see this process on video!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Karen it’s on YouTube!!! It’s a 46 minute video because it includes the 6 pie fillings that he just dropped here, but the crust is at the very start!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Remember to roll out the dough so it’s very thin but able to be handled.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found this recipe to turn out quite tough, especially on day 2. However, I applied his lamination technique to my usual pie dough recipe (Alison Roman's from Dining In), which is similar to his but uses less flour and water for the same amount of butter. It created beautiful flaky, 5-star dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am concerned about the plastic wrap, which is very polluting.  Could the dough be chilled in a covered glass dish?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Penelope I use beeswax paper, which is reusable, and it works just as well as plastic wrap for me. Assuming more skilled bakers agree (I am very novice!) I would love to see it used in these tutorials or directions to promote less waste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sophia: I’m a very experienced baker and I use bees’ wax wrap most of the time. When I’m working with a dough that tears very easily, or if I’m going to put it in the freezer before/while rolling it out, I use wax paper, which can be composted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh, dear.  I followed this as precisely as I could and it shrunk and turned greasy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This crust shrunk considerably more than when I do a half butter / half shortening crust.  I love the idea of the lamination technique, so next time will use this technique with my tried and true half/half crust recipe (from PIE).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2/3 Cup water is 158 grams, NOT 115 grams

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What does he mean in using the the phrase “press into a round?”  Is a “round” a disk, a sphere, or a flying saucer shape?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lorenzo Kay he means a disk.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lorenzo Kay the shape is in the word—round!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is it me or is this a recipe for biscuits? Except I would use lard and buttermilk vs the butter and vinegary ice water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Me More like crackers, right? Biscuits call for leavening.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I can’t wait to make this, thank you :) any ideas on what to sub for heavy cream if I’m opting to go plant-based?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious and I like a thinner crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My grandmother from the farm in Ohio made the best pie crusts using these ingredients.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I usually sub in about a third vegetable shortening for butter in pie crust. Will that work with this recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: does anyone have a good substitute for apple cider vinegar? i unfortunately have an apple allergy :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: um regular vinegar?  wine vinegar?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used red wine vinegar because it was 5am and there was no other available. I could smell it but the flavor was not noticeable after baking (though that could have been due to the cinnamon in the apple filling).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've used vodka in pie dough (America's Test Kitchen's recipe calls for it). Vaughn also mentions that he considered it for this recipe but went with the ACV.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made the dough; what a great way to incorporate the butter flour water! Much more precise and easy to handle than my old way which was "hit or miss". The rounds are in the fridge until T-Day morning. Looking forward to the bake! Thanks for the video.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It took me a minute to understand what to do with the quarters of dough. I think they mean cut the square in half and set one half aside. Cut the other half in half again and stack. Pat into a square and cut that half in quarters. Stack all four quarters and roll in a circle. Then take the set aside half and do likewise.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I did one of this recipe and one with the Cuisinart processer. C's recipe is identical except for baking powder instead of vinegar, no sugar. 
This recipe. I got tired of hand blending and used a pastry blender. Likely used too much liquid. It was alarmingly stretchy & rubbery rolling out. With processer next.  I decided to laminate and bop it with the pilling pin also. It did hold together rolling out.
Both had hard flakes. Doesn't matter if its processed. Its the hitting. Not as good, IMO

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ran out of allowable text in my last. I used, as always, a third vegetable shortening and two thirds butter. I think pie crust has a more tender texture if you dont mess around laminating and hitting it with a rolling pin. My family could care less but I'll go back to the old way next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not to be dramatic but this produced the best pie crust I’ve ever tasted

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this dough and used it for the chocolate pie we eat every Thanksgiving. 
I agree with the commenter who said it would be helpful to have cooking instructions with the dough so you don't have to go to another recipe to find out what to do.
The pie came out beautifully and I'll use this pie recipe going forward.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After years of thinking I was good at making pie crust, I’d had some recent setbacks. I wanted to try new recipes and techniques. This is now my new favorite, and I’m so glad I found it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think my butter flakes were too big. It was as definitely flaky, but it slumped horribly during the blind bake, even though it was frozen for hours beforehand. Overall, I’ll go back to Genevieve Ko’s food processor method. It may not get the flakiness, but it’s easier for a reliable crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question: Does this recipe make a bottom and top crust for one pie or does it make enough pie crust for two pies - bottom and top? Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Question I made this recipe last night — it was the perfect amount for one double crust pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you do this in the food processor? The pie crust I usually make ( from nyt) I do in the processor!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Here's the video: https://youtu.be/WxVKqUtVnjo?t=1500&feature=shared

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made a combined version of Ali Slagels Creamed Greens Pot Pie, with some Mary Berry's Leek and Potato Pie thrown in. Divine.  Basically, made a covered pot pie with leeks, greens, mushrooms and potatoes in Mornay sauce. Parbaked the bottom a bit and just dropped the top round on after filling. The pastry is decadent, airy, light and puffy. It took a little more ice water than called for, but I live in CA, so could just be the dry air here. Definitely my new goto pastry.  Thanks Vaughn!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What is the mass of the salt in grams? There is a significant difference in the densities of different brands of Kosher salt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even using baking weights, these crusts came out dense and thick. People struggled to cut through them to serve.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thank you for the wonderful video! It was my first time making pie crust solo and the video + recipe helped me crush it! <3

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This has become my new go-to pie crust. It’s easy to put together, perfectly flaky, and the rich, buttery flavor is unparalleled. Fantastic recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would not use this recipe again. It was very greasy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The dough handles beautifully HOWEVER shrinks horribly. I had an inch of coconut custard on the oven floor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Flaky and buttery, even though I only followed the instructions 70% to the letter. Impressive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: While this crust came together beautifully and was a dream to roll, I didn't find it terribly flaky. As written, the hydration is tricky. My guess is why many people say it's tough is because too much water was added (1/2 cup at the onset is too much IMO). I added about 1/4 cup at first, then a tablespoon or two more over any dry bits of flour until it came together. Probably 6-7 tablespoons max. Decent but not great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best crust ever! Ingenious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe for the Maple Chess Pie. The recipe was easy to follow and the pie dough was light and flaky. This is my new go to pie dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Forget the pie. I just used the curd to make lemon bars with graham cracker crust. And then spread the cranberry topping on top. Much easier and a huge hit. If you want to be fancy cut into bars served chilled with a dollop of whipped cream on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Me what size pan did you use? 8x8? 9x9? 9x13?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hey team, Thoughts on whether the curd and cranberry sauce could be made ahead? (And how far ahead?)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would chance it with making the cranberry sauce two to three days ahead (you can probably make it even further ahead and freeze it), but I wouldn't risk trying to make the curd ahead since it goes into the shell while it's hot and bakes further to set up. If you have the fridge space for it and are just trying to split your labor across multiple days, you could try baking the pie through step 5 and then refrigerating it for a couple of days before topping it. Good luck to you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sara Grace I made the cranberry sauce yesterday, and then today I’m doing the crust and curd and then tomorrow I’ll do the merengue topping!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sara Grace I made both 1 day ahead. Very successful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made this twice now for various friendsgivings. The second time while the cranberries did their thing I used a potato masher to really open them up and get the flavor I felt like was missing the first go around.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The meringue looked lovely when I put it on the pie, but when I cut it three hours later, it had mostly liquefied. Tasty, but disappointing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Joe B. this happened to me! I even took a video of the stiff peaks once I lightly torched it before putting in fridge. I had executed each step and after being in fridge it basically slumped.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Joe B. This happened to me as well. The meringue whipped nicely into stiff peaks, but when I cut into it a couple hours later it was completely liquified. Too bad, all the components tasted great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Joe B. This happened to me too. It looked perfect going into the fridge. I did the meringue wrong the first time so I remade it and it actually formed stiff peaks, so I thought it was right, but it was basically soup after about 30 minutes. Oh well. Still tasted good

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sara Grace- I made the cranberry sauce and lemon curd two days before making the crust, blind baking it, and then baking the lemon curd in the crust. I haven't run into any issues and all seems to be working well! I think the steps could be streamlined, and I haven't actually cut into this pie, but so far each element tastes great and it's quite impressive to look at!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To avoid the liquified meringue I was reading about in others’ comments, I held back stirring in the cranberry sauce and instead drizzled it on top. I also added a pinch of cream of tartar to the egg whites before whipping. The meringue is a beautiful soft marshmallow-like texture and has held up beautifully for two days without weeping. This pie is truly delicious and was the showstopper at our Thanksgiving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have now tasted the pie... Meringue did get slightly watery and sank a little after 5 hours in fridge. I felt it was only okay... A lot of work for a somewhat "strawberry lemonade" flavor. I wouldn't make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree.  Meh

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is a labor intensive time consuming recipe. When I brought it to the table it completely collapsed into a runny mess. The meringue turned into soup—very tasty but a mess.  Fortunately I had also
Made the sesame pumpkin which came
Out better

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tasty but a goopy runny mess if you don’t eat it right away. I’m not sure who thought adding cranberry to meringue was a good idea. The color was so pretty but you need to serve it right away. This is not a make ahead pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Baked this yesterday with my daughter to great success!  We didn’t deviate from the recipe until due to our weeknight impatience at the end, we rushed the cooling time before adding the meringue.  Regardless, it was a beautiful festive pie that will do better on cutting with the full cooling time as written.  Tart, bright, not overly sweet and just a little bit of fun with the kitchen torch - we will bake it again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The curd turned out fantastic for me, was a tad sweet but the ratio with the meringue really helped it with a few changes i made. I added an extra egg for the meringue, and only really added a tablespoon of sugar for the berry sauce (which i also did not strain). Like someone else mentioned the meringue although great at first sinks quickly in the fridge and is not good for storing. I recommend making this if you plan to finish it, or perhaps making another kind of meringue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Nigel exactly! Should not be refrigerated as the meringue slides away

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you substitute ready-make lemon curd?  If so, how much?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this, and it was beautiful, but I found that it made way more Swiss meringue than was needed, after topping and torching the pie (which looked so pretty), the meringue was stable, but when we went to slice it a few hours later, it had totally liquified and was a huge melty mess. :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not cranberry curd? The Ocean Spray restaurant on Cape Cod served it in their bakery and restaurant.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made this recipe. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017817-cranberry-curd-tart It is beautiful and you could definitely put the meringue on it. I love the idea of this lemon/orange combo and will be giving it a try soon!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tried this today ahead of TG, but won’t be making it for the big day. The proportion of curd to meringue was imbalanced, and found the method of baking the curd to be ineffective. May consider an alternate version as suggested in some of the comments.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn, you owe me a pie. I made this & the Coffee and Maple Chess Pie for my birthday. I executed the Cranberry  Citrus Meringue Pie to perfection - made cranberry sauce in advance as well as curd (it was fine!) & the best crimping job ever. Reached 155 on Swiss meringue and videoed the light torching of stiff peaks. It was picture perfect until I put in fridge to wait for after dinner, only to discover the meringue had slid off and was spreading everywhere! It all tasted delicious but that is not a good step as evidenced by others comments here. Put the candle in other pie. Sad!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly, this is not a quick recipe or dessert. I also food processed the hot cranberry mixture and the. Strained it to maximize the flavor. Follow what others are saying about the meringue. You can also use a piping bag to swirl in the cranberry sauce and then torch it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I am
Halfway down the comments and every single person used the words “runny” “soupy” and “mess”. Seems like there is a big problem with the recipe and NYT should have done better vetting. I want to make this so much but it sounds problematic. Any way to sub a different meringue recipe, find one where fruit is added and it doesn’t ruin the meringue?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a pie to be eaten in one sitting as the meringue gets watery in the fridge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this for Thanksgiving, and despite being an experienced baker, I was super nervous. The pie is delicious, but it is a ton of work — I made the crust Monday, blind baked the dough and made the curd Tuesday, the cranberry sauce Wednesday, and (trying to avoid the weeping) finished by making the meringue immediately before serving. 

Like everyone else, my meringue wept too after being put in the fridge for storage. While I’m no recipe developer, I have to think the cranberry sauce is weighing down the meringue or throwing off the sugar ratio. Really disappointing, and I hope the recipe will be re-looked at.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a lot of work, but worth it for a show-stopping festive dessert. I had a lot of mandarins, so I made the curd entirely with them. This meant it was a little sweet for my taste, but some of my taste testers liked it that way. The meringue topping is stunning. Next time I probably wouldn't bother with the laminated dough, which was more work than my regular pie crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The meringue is very sweet. Would the process of making the meringue work as well with half the sugar?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sara Grace, since both the curd and cranberry sauce are chilled before use, I would say they can definitely be made ahead.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree that a better meringue recipe could be found. The streaking method is clunky, and regardless of carefully folding the reserved cranberry sauce in, it still mostly mixes in. An easier way to do this is to either pull a few streaks after putting some small dollops on top, or just spreading some reserved cranberry sauce with your finger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
