commentBody: This recipe was a disaster and I should have listened to other reviewers. The meringue broke and didn’t keep its form, the laminated pie crust was super tough, don’t waste your time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I love this recipe WITH the following tweaks:
As others have said the cranberry flavor is not strong enough in the meringue alone. I added a thin layer of the jelly on top of the lemon curd, after it had cooled, and this made a huge difference.
I also wanted to prevent an unstable meringue. Adding cranberry sauce will make this meringue very unstable. I tested it with and without--without held for hours while with held for an hour or so. Instead, just swirl it in. Do not mix in.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This looks wonderful, but a milk allergy demands thst I make the curd without butter. Would a milk-free margerine work, do you think? I usually use Earth Balance bars for baking... any thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @MA Citizen That should work. But I would leave out the salt because Earth Balance is pretty salty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like Miyoke cultured non-butter for a butter sub. You can find a comparison among the butter-substitute choices, and some work better than others. Miyoke scores higher in flavor and health and environmental considerations.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used no butter, nonfat yogurt for the curd...it curded....maple syrup instead of sugar in meringue....looks like the picture...almost

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
"Prep Time 20 minutes" – lol – you'll spend more than that just whisking and whipping the meringue. Who made that estimate?

Also I've only needed 2 oranges to get the necessary 1 cup juice and 2 T zest, not 5 oranges.

Not done yet, still trying to decide how to do the meringue differently to avoid the deflating and liquifying described in many comments.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Taste was great but meringue turned into a soupy watery mess after a few hours in the fridge. Absolutely disappointing after the many hours labor and multiple components. If you served right away maybe it would be worth the work.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As others have said, despite using a Swiss meringue, the meringue wept after being in the fridge overnight and was liquified ~20 hours later. I followed the recipe exactly and, yes, I made the meringue correctly—I make Swiss meringue several times each year. Perhaps it would have turned out better if I had prepared it the same day, but that is simply unrealistic for the average Thanksgiving host due to how labor-intensive the recipe is. It’s a shame, too, because the flavors are lovely—the cure in particular is sublime. But it was creamy soup by the time I cut into it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made this twice and I feel like I'm doing something wrong. Both times the meringue basically melted and looked like a warm whipped cream by the time they were served. Is there something I could be doing wrong? I even tried adding cream of tartar but it still melted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie was very disappointing - much too sweet! The meringue became liquid when we cut the pie open - I would make the meringue using this method again but wondered if using cream of tartar would help hold the meringue together.? Liked the idea of adding cranberries to the meringue - looked nice but could not taste them. Would not make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree. too sweet yet not good contrasting flavors. the cranberries were so pretty in the meringue but flavorless. Yet the cranberries were delicious on their own (though wouldn't cook down to that thickness if using just at cranberry sauce). The curd was ok, but nothing to write home about. The instructions for the meringue were vague. I googled and learned how to do Italian meringue that way, supposedly it was to make it more structured but the opposite happened. Ended up a soupy mess.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very disappointing. First of all, in what universe is the prep time on this 20 minutes? It took me at least two hours. I followed the directions exactly and had friends over to try about when it was finished. It turned into a soupy mess. It’s also too tart and too sweet. I ended up throwing it out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is the classic tale of "I should have read the notes before baking." Like many others, my meringue turned into a soupy mess. The flavors were great, but it was embarrassing. I will try a version with the curd and a different meringue and use the cranberry jelly as another layer, as someone suggested. 

At least seeing that others had the same result made me feel better that its' not just me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The filling-meringue ratio is off. If I made it again, I’d double the curd and halve the meringue. And stabilize the meringue. Mine got sloppy after about 3 hours in the fridge

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved this pie, ultimately.  I made it twice in one day, as I added to much juice to the curd the first go round.  While it coated the back of the spoon, it baked up weird, was very tart, and did not set fully. Second time went better though it still baked up 'weird'. The curd bubbled toward the end of cooking and nothing looked set. After much frustration, I pulled it.  In the end, it  did set up well and the meringue was great.  I think some temp guide for cooking the curd would help.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a beautiful and seasonal lemon meringue pie and a nice counterpoint to a heavy Thanksgiving meal. Everyone at the table loved it, adding it into our Thanksgiving tradition!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Flavors were lovely, but it didn't work for me technically. Not sure what went wrong, but the curd didn't set up at all. I'd rather cook the curd outside the pie and then assemble; the curd seemed overcooked, while the crust ended up a bit underdone. The meringue ended up okay, even making a day ahead, though it may have contributed to the messy, liquidy pie I ended up with. Am an experienced baker so not sure if the fault is mine or the recipe's. Might try same parts with different technique.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @CK I had the same experience with the curd not setting. Very strange and frustrating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: AMAZING TASTE. Store bought curd and cranberry sauce saved a ton of labor. The pie did turn out very saucy- did not hold its shape at all and was more like a loose pudding underneath the merengue. Will continue experimenting to get a firmer texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I also had a problem with the meringue. I beat into stiff peaks but then it just splooged all over. I wanted a big ole layer of meringue.I see others in these cooking notes with same issue. Also I used mostly red grapefruit for the curd and that came out fantastic, and tasted great with the curd.I increased the amount of the cranberry and used the sieved version for the meringue, and kept the other part with the actual cranberries-- made an excellent cranberry sauce.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The recipe said parts could be made ahead 1-2 days.  The curd was lovely the day made.  I made the meringue a few hours before taking it to a Xmas dinner with friends.  The meringue was half melted and h the liquid was weeping into the curd by the time we arrived.

First failure of a dessert recipe I have brought as a guest in many, many years.  Very disappointing.

I would never make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: same thing happened to me. I was so disappointed. It was a soupy disaster. Never a dessert fail like this before. Such a bummer as their picture looked delicious. I guess it was the food stylist skills that created that dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: throw the cranberry discards into a batter to make cranberry muffins! so good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think you end up with and extra yoke not a egg white?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does browning the top do anything texturally or taste-wise or is it just cool? Should I buy a kitchen torch? Is there anything else I can use?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Chelsea
  You could brown the meringue under the broiler if you don’t have a torch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Chelsea like a marshmallow that has been toasted vs. one out of the bag - the taste and texture of the meringue benefits from browning here.  The kitchen torch is, for me, an easy to control way of doing that but the oven broiler was my go-to method for most years and works fine but requires more careful attention.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lynette at Home thanks!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Is the reason for cooking the egg whites first because of fear of "uncooked whites?"
I've made a few meringues in the past and don't remember that step at all.

Also, seems to me a graham-cracker crust has always been traditional with citrus curd pies (lemon, Key lime), and it's much easier and could be made ahead. then brought to room temp.  (I'm not a big baker; don't do pastry pie crusts.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lynne, Swiss meringue is a cooked meringue (thanks, Great British Baking Show!) As for the crust, you could buy the pastry crust, just as one could buy the graham cracker crust. The point of the recipe is that it's a new take on traditional foods of Thanksgiving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a Swiss meringue and, besides tasting like a campfire marshmallow when torched, it's a very stable topping that won't weep like a French meringue (just egg whites and sugar). It will hold for a couple days — if the pie will even last that long. And because the meringue is cooked, the sugar dissolves and creates a creamy, dreamy texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I make parts of this ahead of time?  Curd, sauce, ?  How far in advance?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe and the end result was stunning.  However, it was a lot of work for a pie that I did not enjoy as much as a regular lemon meringue pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for a Friendsgiving and it was an excellent way to finish off a big meal. I was a little worried about the ratio of curd to meringue (it seemed like not much curd), but it was lovely.  I used kenji’s foolproof pie dough (with all water, no vodka), and brushed it with the extra egg white before adding the curd filling.  The crust stayed delightfully crisp and flaky.  I also followed the tip of another commenter to mash the cranberries and wonder about just a bit more cranberry next time. Because there will be a next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved the tart flavors of the curd and cranberry. They work beautifully together. But what I would have done for the clean slice and distinct layers shown in Vaughn’s video! I followed the recipe exactly but my meringue ran  over each slice after chilling for just an hour in the refrigerator. I think this pie would be best served right after topping with meringue so it holds its shape. In that case, if you are serving this for Thanksgiving and want to minimize the time in the kitchen the day of, you can bake the pie with the curd the night before, make the cranberry sauce in advance as well, and then do the meringue before serving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this a few days ago and it was a hit! I made the cranberry sauce 2 days in advance. I've made several of the NYT "curd" recipes including the lemon cream pie and the cranberry curd tart. I think the LCP is my favorite. The next time I try this one, I'll skip the meringue and use whipping cream and gently swirl in the cranberry sauce. The recipe could use more cranberry sauce for an extra kick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This was so delicious! It is a very long process with many steps.
It is so beautiful! Can’t wait to have a taste!
Kay

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My meringue also collapsed in the fridge. It was a soupy mess by the time we got to thanksgiving dessert. For such a time-consuming creation this was a big disappointment

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excessively complicated for Thanksgiving and sickeningly sweet.  I use a crust from Trader Joe's and skipped the 2nd phase of blind baking. The curd needed more time than that listed to get thick enough. Having read the dozens of comments about the meringue, I used the Serious Eats recipe for Swiss Meringue, which required going to 175 degrees. I got stiff peaks but adding the cranberry made it very soft like a fluffy pudding. The next day, the meringue still looks the same.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
sorry, guys. This did not live up to its expectations at all. It looked beautiful, just like the picture, but the taste wasn't great--it just lacked that certain oomph. Really disappointed me, as it looked so pretty, I thought for sure it would be a new holiday pie in circulation. 

One exception, the cranberry mix itself was delicious, so that will go into circulation for next Thanksgiving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was very technically challenging in my opinion (I’m more of a cook than a baker). I followed instructions exactly, but the meringue weeped and curd didn’t set well. However, amazing flavor. I would recommend cooking the curd at a lower temp in the pot to thicken. Not sure how to fix the meringue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are we missing a step here? Every other meringue pie recipe I read baked the pie at the end. I made this pie as the recipe states, torching the meringue slightly at the end. It looked great, however, in about two hours in the fridge, the meringue sank and liquified somewhat. I baked the pie at 350 F for about 30 min, which almost solidified the meringue. I also think that the addition of cranberry sauce to the meringue, which creates a pretty color, changes its texture because of the liquid

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made this for Thanksgiving and experienced a similar issue as others with the meringue. It whipped up beautifully to stiff peaks but after sitting in the fridge for 4 hrs before dinner the meringue became quite soft and soupy. Had to use a spoon to serve it instead of a pie server. We all laughed about it and ate it but it was disappointing. Any ideas what caused this since so many people had the same issue?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This did not hold up in the fridge at all.  I made it the day before and it was soup by the time it was served.  I made a second one at the same time that was served immediately after putting the meringue on top, and that one was fine, so I don't think I was the problem.  Tasted good but do NOT make in advance.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and followed the recipe exactly (made the curd and cranberry sauce the day before.) I browned it slightly in the oven because I was at my parents’ house and they didn’t have a torch. Everyone loved it, but to me it tasted too sweet. I would remove a few tablespoons of sugar next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was sensational. Worth all of the time and energy it took which was considerable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If making ahead, only go to step 7; broiling or torching the meringue does help it keep its shape if you have to make it fully ahead of time. Great flavors! Lots of work! I used a gluten free pastry crust recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and thank god it was so tasty and beautiful because it felt needlessly complicated. I’m not a confident baker so I followed the recipe exactly. It took me two days. If I make it again I’ll be less nervous about messing it up. Also, just a regular pie crust will do fine. None of this cutting and layering nonsense.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 11/10. This took a tin of time but was really fun to make and tasted amazing. Used a tart dough (no sugar) to balance out the sweetness. Agree with others that the meringue to curd ratio could be adjusted but we all lived it anyway. Had no issues with watery mess. Whipped egg whites till they were well beyond stiff. Was still a hit at dinner the next day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My son and I baked this together—our first time with lemon curd and with Swiss mirangue—and we loved Vaughn’s video demo. I watched it several times and it made me happy each time. Thank you for being so lovely and taking the stress out of Thanksgiving. Our pie turned out beautifully, even when using a plant butter and laminating a gluten and dairy free crust. It meant a lot to me to serve something beautiful and unique!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie is visually stunning and delicious.  Mine ended up weeping quite a bit,  not sure exactly why, but I may have not gotten the Swiss meringue hot enough in the first instance.  Tasted amazing.  I might add a layer of the cranberry compote to the bottom of the pie next time to add more sharpness, and reduce the amount of sugar in the meringue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fun to make and serve as long as it’s getting eaten the same day. I liked the laminated pie crust but next time might try something simpler. Getting the curd to setovercooked the crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe would probably be fine, except that the meringue should not be made in advance.  There was a note that it would deflate a little, but I made 18 hours in advance an it did not hold up well at all - and I can only imagine what it would be like up to 4 days later...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was delicious and well-received at Thanksgiving! I did strain the cranberry sauce, but kept the strained bits and found them to be a nice mix-in for oatmeal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yum! I made this for my bf’s family Thanksgiving meal and it was a hit! Instead of the pie crust, I made Claire Saffitz’s sweet tart dough (had prepped multiple of these doughs for the various thanksgivings I was attending over the weekend) and so all I had to make that morning was the curd and meringue. The curd was delicious and not as tricky as other recipes I’ve tried. The meringue I was intimidated by but it was actually so easy! I now feel confident to make meringues and would love to alter the flavors to make it even more my own.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie was a real showstopper. Not a slice left after Thanksgiving. I used a chopstick to make swirls and roses in the meringue that looked beautiful when blowtorch

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thought this pie was so delicious, the meringue whipped up nicely but collapsed completely within 3 hours which was so sad.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The meringue was kind of droopy after you mix in the cranberry. Also the flavors were kind of contrasting with super tart curd and sweet meringue. Wouldn't recommend

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lovely flavors!  But my gosh was this a lot of work that I had to spread over two days. The meringue was perfect initially but turned goopy after a few hours in the fridge. I'd love to experiment with using this same flavor profile in the much easier lemon meringue pie recipe I normally do.  This recipe is probably too fussy for me to want to do again, especially with the meringue not holding up to being refrigerated for a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Like others, I struggled to keep the meringue stable. I also, however, had the curd turn to soup by the time I was serving (it had set in the fridge overnight and seemed great up until I put the meringue on top). If anyone has any tips or thoughts, I’m a newer baker and had never attempted something this complex before. Any ideas on why the curd turned liquidy? The flavors here were delicious and before slicing the whole thing was really beautiful so I’d love to attempt again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made as bars in a 9x9 pan, parchment and buttered, with shortbread crust from Clark's olive oil lemon bars. Baked shortbread, then poured in curd and baked that and let it all chill in the fridge overnight. Made cranberry sauce and pureed half to create a thin layer over the curd and let that sit in the fridge to chill/settle, then made the meringue w/o added cranberry. It was beautiful & 3 egg whites is enough for bars (cut sugar). Mini-pavlova w/leftover meringue/cranberry was a fail tho :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I feel like there was just not enough curd, and the meringue collapsed. My family enjoyed the flavor but didn't even realize that there was curd in the pie as it had completely melded together with the soupy meringue.

I ended up adapting a different lemon meringue pie recipe (Stella Parks recipe from her Bravetart cookbook) and it was much more successful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was quite concerned after seeing all the comments, but generous torching really did help it to keep shape although it did indeed wet out quite a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was requested again after making it for thanksgiving last year-its delicious AND so pretty with the pink meringue. Lots of steps but you can do some ahead of time (cranberry jam, par baking the crust) and the contrast of the curd and meringue looks beautiful. I did all lemon this year just a personal preference.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Unfortunately my meringue did the same thing as others did. I finished with a beautiful Swiss meringue, cooked to 165 just in case, whipped to perfection! Added the lovely cranberry sauce, topped the pie and put it in the fridge at 2pm. By 8pm dessert time it had already lost a lot of its form. After 30 minutes on the counter it was practically a puddle. There has to be a better way. If I made this again, cause it was so amazingly delicious, I might keep the meringue separate from the cranberry and swirl them and top the pie last minute. But that’s a lot to do when I’m also trying to relax and enjoy the company.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the video was pretty useless. As a baker, if someone is looking at the video and v never made custard, it would have been disastrous.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe by using weight measures and without substitutes. (Note: Vaughn adds a pinch of salt to the tahini in the video, so I did the same.) If you like all-butter crusts, this one is just fine. I prefer half butter/half shortening, so I’ll probably use that in the future. My pie didn’t come out as nicely swirled as the photo, because I was stingy with the tahini at first. Go heavier with the tahini for the 1st swirl, so the tahini doesn’t completely cover the orange color when you make the 2nd swirl. I had to swirl it a lot (and too much) to make the orange visible again — my mistake. I was nervous about how loose the filling was after 40 minutes (about 1/2-inch set around the edge, but the middle was very liquid), so I gave it another 5 minutes. By then, the filling had a little wobble left, so watch it closely. It goes from looking very underbaked to overcooked quickly. Overall, I love sesame and pumpkin, and the combination works for me. The flavor almost reads peanut butter-y? In particular, I like how prominent the sesame oil is. It’s an interesting sweet/savory balance that’s nicely spiced. I will make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Erin Thanks for sharing your tips

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rather than thinning the tahini with sesame oil, instead of sweetening it with sugar, how about using a tablespoon of date syrup, honey, or maple syrup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dooberbug Y’all, I beg you, don’t do this. I did and it turned my tahini into a disgusting putty, so I had to start over again. Turns out that water (or residual moisture from maple syrup) will actually thicken nut butters, not thin them out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can we use a store bought pie shell?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Bill H. I imagine you could, I was thinking the same thing. I may brave it though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve had great results with Trader Joe’s or pillsbury premade pie crust. Just let it soften to room temperature then roll it with rolling pin and lay in pie plate. It doesn’t beat homemade BUT it is delicious and super fast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We love sesame-based or accented sweets, but we are a sweet potato pie household. Do my fellow NYT Cooking readers think this technique will work in that preparation— with Steen’s ribbon cane syrup sweetening the tahini?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Yes! First I'll try it exactly as written. I know it'll be a hit. 

Next, I was thinking of doing it with sweet potatoes, and yes, I believe it will work. It's going to be delish. Sweet potato pies I make typically have one and only one spice: nutmeg. However, for this, I'll use this recipe's spices, then follow the rest of my usual recipe - *and* add the  sesame oil and swirling the tahini mix in. It will be a new level of sweet potato pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I "veganized" this recipe with great results.  Served to omnivores to rave reviews.  

For the eggs, I simply used 1/2 cup  "Just Egg" egg substitute

For the heavy cream I used a proportion of 2/3 cashew crema (made from 3/4c cashew:1c water), and 1/3 neutral oil whisked together with a Tbsp of cornstarch to approximate the fat content of heavy cream.  

Only other thing I did differently was to not cool the crust before baking.  Cooking time was more or less the same.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Those of us that must be gluten free will make this w no crust & dig in w a spoon!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ellen GF Done! This was the best pie, GF or the other type, I’ve ever made. Deliciousness like this does not require a crust. Plus, it’s as easy as … you know what.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ellen GF I made this today using a store-bought, pre-made HF crust and it was also delicious. I just followed the instructions on the crust to parbake it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lovely combination of sesame and pumpkin. I worried the sesame oil was too strong when I tasted the batter, but the flavor is more muted when cooked. I might use more tahini next time. Really delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this on Friday for a dinner party and it was a hit!  I used a different pie crust recipe than the laminated one provided, but it worked out just as well.  

I agree with another comment saying the 40 min initial bake time left the pie quite liquid in the center and baked only about half an inch from the edge.   My oven is likely off, but I found I did need to add another 30 minutes to get a wobble and not liquid in the center.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this yesterday, following the directions. I wish I could show you the 5 facial expressions as we took our first bites. First responses ranged from ‘that’s interesting’ to ‘ick’. One taster said, upon first bite, "this is baby food."
Rarely have the five of us met a dessert we ultimately agreed was nasty. The sesame flavor entirely overwhelmed the pumpkin.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love savory sweets and sesame but found the sesame oil in the pie filling too much.  It totally overpowers the pumpkin. The combination of spices was great and the pepper gives it a nice kick. Next time I’d skip the sesame oil and use more of the tahini mix. Tahini was far too thick so I thinned with (lol) boiling pasta water and it worked very nicely!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi, all - a question: all of this year’s NYT Thanksgiving pies call for the laminated pie crust. But I am thinking that I can sub in any type d pie crust instead, is that right? The laminated pie crust recipe seems too complex for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazingly beautiful to look at but the sesame part of it was too intense for the mild taste of the pumpkin. If I ever make it again I would not include the sesame oil in the pumpkin purée and would decrease the tahini mixture by half.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Despite the par bake the crust was soggy under the pumpkin but the crisp edges were fabulous.  The pepper came through as a surprise!  Love the combo of flavors

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Regular pie shell, or deep-dish?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Donna It’s in the oven now. It fit into a regular (not deep-dish) 9-inch pie pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really good pumpkin pie. I had to add 10 more minutes to baking time since the pie was still liquid in the middle at the end. I'm really impatient when it comes to dessert and wanted to eat it when it was finally out of the oven, at which point it wasn't fully set and I felt like the sesame flavor was too strong. After waiting overnight for the pie to set in the fridge, it was perfect. Also making the pie crust is worth the effort but you could use a pre-made crust in a pinch and to speed it up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The idea of using sweet potato instead is terrific. And it reminds me of an amazing Samin Nosrat NYT recipe involving (steamed!?) sweet potato and tahini butter. So tasty. This is to say, tahini and sweet potato are amazing together. I will try this variation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you think you could freeze this before baking to make ahead? That seems like a better solution than just making ahead and refrigerating for 3 days...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @A many pies mature in their flavor when you let them sit at least overnight. I’ve not made this and yet and can’t say from experience with this pie, but I would opt for letting it settle afterwards rather than freezing and eating fresh from the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed this recipe to the letter and unfortunately was very unimpressed with the outcome. I typically love tahini, but in my opinion the toasted sesame oil and black pepper combo totally overpowered the pumpkin, creating a bizarre and unappealing flavor combo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Claire I didn’t use pepper (it sounded odd), and subbed Pumpkin Pie Spice for the individual spices.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Unfortunately, this did not go over well. I agree with the peanut butter comment. I would not make this again but a tip for future cooks would be to omit the black pepper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this today and it's incredible! The filling is so silky, so delicately flavorful. I used a frozen Trader Joe's crust which worked fine. The only changes I made to the recipe were using maple syrup instead of sugar/ sesame oil to thin the tahini, and leaving out the black pepper (just not personally a fan). I'm eating it warm with homemade whipped cream and it is so so fabulously delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you want pumpkin pie, use a different recipe.   The tahini/sesame flavor overwhelms the pumpkin.  I like sesame and tahini so the pie was tasty, but not at all what I was expecting given the absence of pumpkin flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The pie is wonderful, although I was skeptical about veering away from my traditional pumpkin. I used half and half instead of heavy cream, and cooked the pie much longer to get the center just right. When I tasted the pie, it as heavenly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those who said sesame oil was too overpowering , did you sub another oil or would you just halve or omit it entirely?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @RPDX I like the bold flavor of two teaspoons, but you could easily halve it for a less pronounced sesame flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I ever make it again, I will eliminate the sesame oil from the pumpkin purée and make only half of the tahini mixture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @RPDX I also wonder how many folks used plain sesame oil vs toasted sesame oil?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Beautiful pie but it tasted like peanut butter. I am a traditionalist when it comes to pumpkin pie so I went out on a limb with this recipe. As another commenter said it really did taste like peanut butter and that missed the mark for me. I think the toasted sesame oil was too much. I wonder if regular sesame oil or no oil at all would have been better?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added hot water to thin my tahini. Big mistake. It's a gloopy gloppy mess, adding liquids and syrups to nut butters apparently is the worst option.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have never given a negative review on this site, but this pie was so bad that nobody at my Thanksgiving table ate it and we threw it out. The tahini completely overwhelmed the pumpkin and it was so savory that it did not seem like dessert. Don’t know what the half teaspoon of black pepper was doing in there, but it made matters worse. Also, the pumpkin mixture was very thin so it was difficult to swirl in the tahini. Very disappointing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
It breaks my heart that the rating is currently 4 stars--what transformed this into a 5-star recipe for us over three tries was cutting the amount of toasted sesame oil in half (to 1 tsp). 

Adding the full amount produced too strong a toasted sesame flavor for everyone at the table (and we love sesame flavor, halvah, etc.). Hoping this tip will accelerate your journey to pumpkin + sesame deliciousness!

Re: parbake, blind baking a Trader Joe's crust for 20m was the winning time for us!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The strangest cake I ever made - and it was delicious. I used black sesame paste, which I had from Chinatown. In fact, I found this recipe searching for what I can do with this jar of black sesame paste. It is a bit thicker than tahini and deep black. The cake looked like a painting from Jackson Pollock and took some courage to dig in. But everyone took seconds.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Currently in the oven - I learned a lot from this recipe and from the laminated pie dough recipe! I did not follow suit with the tip to fill the dough with enough beans while par baking and alas the crimp fell apart, but it’s all good. I think it will be a beautiful pie.

One thing I added was some smoked maple syrup to the batter and a little to the tahini swirl, not much at all, just enough for flavor. I thought it would go nicely with the sesame.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any way to make this without the eggs?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would folks reduce the heavy cream for homemade pie purée, since it tends to be wetter than canned? Absolutely love the sesame pumpkin combo, can’t wait to make!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made with steamed sweet potato and gluten free crust (aran goyoaga recipe). Did not puree the sweet potato, but could have? It could go either way with chunky vs smooth. I like the spice blend and tahini swirl, will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried sweet potato version? I am curious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any ideas on how to make this without eggs? Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lorne  I bake sourdough bread etc and use sourdough starters (rye and wheat, both whole grain) to substitute eggs and baking soda. Will try out my wheat starter in this pie in the near future.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As another reader suggested, I made this exactly as printed, but without the crust because I’m not able to eat gluten.  Five gold spoons!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What size pie tin is everyone using? I have a regular one, and a deep dish one. I've seen comments about the amount of filling so thinking maybe the deeper one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Briefly cook the pumpkin in a pan until it darkens,  to deepen the flavor and remove a little of the moisture, which helps avoid cracks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm curious to hear from those of you using premade / store-bought pie crusts (where you simply let them get to room temp, unroll, and bake).  When doing this, do you still follow Step 3 to bake the crust alone, or is it that I should skip the step and just bake it all in one go?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ren I used a pre-formed, store-bought crust and parbaked it according to the package. Turned out great

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just refrigerate it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is heavy cream necessary or can I use half & half?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The tahini swirl was very nice, but the sesame oil in the pumpkin custard portion was really overpowering.  I liked the very warm spice mix though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was incredible. I had some leftover filling due to a shrinking pie crust (pie weight issue, not the recipe), so I baked the excess in ramekins for a chef’s snack. The sweetened tahini is really bringing an almost butterscotch or caramel vibe. Try it!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this fora Friendsgiving this year and it was the first pie completely finished after they were set out. An absolutely delightful flavor profile as tahini, sesame oil, pumpkin and the spices listed are SO good for savory and sweet dishes. Making two of them again tomorrow for Thanksgiving so we don't run out too fast!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oooof, in the middle of prep and my tahini (Wegmans brand) is quite thick.  Already added 1tsp of sesame oil plus one from the pumpkin mix.  Not sure how the swirl will go. Fingers crossed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m making this now but confused. Ingredients include both 2large eggs and 2large egg yolks but directions only include the egg yolks. What happened to the two large eggs?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kathryn Marteeny They’re in there. “In a large bowl, whisk the [two] eggs, [two] yolks and ¾ cup/150 grams sugar for about a minute, until smooth.”

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe directions say “eggs, yolks” so I think they mean both :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For anyone who is so inclined, here's some vegan substitutions! I made it for Thanksgiving and it turned out great. I did need to bake it for slightly longer as well.

 Eggs -> 1/2 cup silken tofu blended (mori-nu shelf stable version) + 2 tbsp cornstarch + 4 tbsp aquafaba
Heavy cream -> Full fat coconut milk

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made tartlets using the recipe and loved the results. The only derivation being less of the tahini mixture in the shells and a watchful eye on the bake. On a second attempt I put in a tablespoon of white miso at the suggestion of an umami freak in the family and it makes for an interesting addition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I love sesame in a dessert context, and the flavor was irresistible, but next time I will completely combine it with the filling instead of swirling. You don’t really taste the “swirl” effect, and (at least with the tahini I used) the resulting appearance was practically identical to “pumpkin pie streaked with mold.”

Also, note that this recipe is not great for making on Thanksgiving day, because it hogs a LOT of oven time. Between the parbaking with weights, parbaking without weights, baking with filling, and leaving in the oven to cool, it significantly delayed getting the turkey in the oven. (Not to mention the trouble and time of clearing a flat surface in the freezer for the pre-oven freeze time.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a delicious twist on pumpkin pie. I would make again. The tahini is such a complementary flavor and really was a subtle sesame addition that gave the traditional pumpkin pie much more depth of flavor. I made this crust recipe but would probably go back to my normal pie crust recipe in the future as I didn’t feel this pie needed such a heavy butter crust. I didn’t have any cream so I used full fat coconut milk and it worked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made as directed. 
Phenomenal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Followed the recipe exactly and ended up only being able to fit half of the pumpkin mixture in the pie crust. Still had a second round of laminated pie dough from the recipe so I got some more tahini and made a second pie. Didn’t have enough pie pans so I used a parchment lined 8 inch round cake pan, which somehow resulted in a better pie. 
Result was visually stunning, lovely and savory! A fun twist on a Thanksgiving classic, would make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did not time myself well with this and wasn’t able to cool the crust nor the actual pie as the recipe instructs… That said, I don’t think I can go back to a pumpkin pie without tahini again. YUM! I’m dairy free so used almond milk instead of heavy cream. I also toasted and sprinkled sesame seeds on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this exactly as written, except for cook time, and it was a big hit at Thanksgiving. Much more complex and interesting than plain old pumpkin! 

I did have to bake it almost 50 minutes to get what I thought was the perfect wobble in the middle, although that may have contributed to the fact that it cracked badly in several places, even though I followed the 45 minute cool down with the oven off. Still wonderful though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Pumpkin pie has never been a favorite of mine, but it isn't Thanksgiving without it.  This year, another guest brought this to Thanksgiving dinner.  It was BY FAR the best pumpkin pie I've ever had and I'd consider making it myself.  It sounds a bit gimmicky but it works and makes the ordinary and often cloying into something sophisticated and satisfying.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this and we thought the flavor was quite good. The only comment I have is the amount of sesame oil in the custard. It was nice and I like that flavor but next time I might halve it so it isn’t as dominant in the flavor profile. Otherwise, for a non baker, the laminated pie dough was a breeze, and the outcome was very good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After reading the comments, I decided I did want a pumpkin pie so ditched the sesame oil in the pumpkin but added a teaspoon to the tahini to make sure that was full flavored. I also halved the pepper. I ended up with a pumpkin pie with a touch of sesame. Not much different from a regular pumpkin pie except ever so much prettier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this following the instructions exactly as written. It was fantastic. A keeper for sure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This came out well.  I liked the tip to let it cool in the oven with the heat off, which avoided the major cracks that I sometimes get with pumpkin pies.  My guests like it quite a bit. I liked the lighter quality, almost mousse like, of the pumpkin/tahini filling but might add more tahini next time for a stronger flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wish I would have made this without the black pepper. I used less than the recipe called for and it still overwhelmed the rest of the flavors and gave it a bad aftertaste. The tahini and pumpkin went really nicely together and I would consider trying it again without the pepper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this as a back up pie but it was far better than the brandied pumpkin pie… it was visually appealing and everyone loved the sesame flavor.  I but the toasted sesame oil in half, and was glad I did.  I would definitely make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I too struggled to get dramatic color pattern but still loved this variation. Like any good pumpkin, it reheats beautifully. Might try as a crustless puddingor single size tarlets.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love this combination of flavors, pumpkin + sesame + spices. However, my sesame swirl wound up being sort of a taupe color, not super attractive next to the pumpkin.  Next time I'll pipe some stabilized whipped cream over the top.  Also, I used freshly grated pepper and, as a result, bit into some crunchy pepper bits while eating.  Next time I'll use finely ground pepper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This was good. The sesame adds a slight halva flavour without being overwhelming but it still maintains the character of a pumpkin pie without being too much the way pumpkin pie can be. I'd make it again but first I have to try your other pie recipes. 

The only trouble I had was that the runny tahini I had thickened up quite a bit when I stirred through the sugar, so I ended up having to add several spoons of oil. I used olive since I ran out of toasted sesame oil but it wasn't noticeable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is my new favorite pumpkin pie. Stopping the baking at 40 minutes and then leaving it in the oven to cool really is key to getting the best texture. I also always do all my baking by weight for consistency.

Two small swaps: 1) I used my own pie crust recipe because I already had it made, and 2) I used white pepper instead of black because I know from experience that I prefer it in fall/winter spice blends (I think it melds better with the other spices, especially ginger).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
nice balance of savory & sweet. a hit.

I made substitutions:

1 (-) black pepper
2 (-) 1T sesame oil
3 (-) 2T sugar
** 4 ( )1T molasses **
5 replaced cream w/ evaporated milk
6 made a gluten free recipe for a black sesame crust. it calls for 1/2C oil: replaced 1T with sesame oil

next time:
1 keep all the sugar
2 up the spice maybe, more nutmeg?

tip:
1. ( ) molasses: adds subtle flavor   really amps up the contrast of the marbling :)
2. poured tahini after adding pumpkin & worked fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe as is and it was okay. I would make it again with some adjustments. I think it definitely needs brown sugar and so I would replace half of the white with brown. I would also replace some of the sesame oil with melted butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this with black tahini. The visual was very impressive!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It looks like he is using fresh ginger in the video…anyone else try that? How much?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavors fight with each other. I regret wasting homemade pumpkin purée on this!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe worked out well, got a beautiful swirl and lovely, set custard. But the taste was just off: we all agreed that the sesame flavour was overwhelming in an unpleasant way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe says to add a teaspoon of sesame oil to the tahini if it's too thick to drizzle - it was still rock hard after one so I added a second before I thought about the implications, and then about a tablespoon or two of water. That extra teaspoon of sesame oil was fatal to the taste - way too much. Regardless I'd say the extra effort for the tahini is only a modest value for effort.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Didn’t care for this recipe at all. Two main problems:

1. The tahini gave the pie filling a bitter/sour taste. 
2. The timing instructions for parbaking lead to a VERY over baked crust.  I didn’t use the recipe they linked to in the recipe. Still, the cooking times are overkill. 

I wonder if using a toasted pistachio or pine nut butter instead of the tahini would be better? I haven’t tried it yet but am considering it. I don’t think the sesame seed flavor is quite right here.  Props to the NYT Cooking peeps for trying something new with an old favorite though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this with black sesame paste instead of tahini so it would have Halloween colors. Turned out fabulous.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nothing new to add except to emphasize that I too had to bake it much longer and it burnt, so watch carefully! I think the sesame flavor is overpowering and, although fun to try a twist on a classic, I can’t recommend. It’s edible though with a lot of whipped cream to cut the very savory flavors.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like the tahini swirl but I did not like the sesame oil. It completely overpowered the taste of the pumpkin and made me think this is sesame pie, not pumpkin pie. Would try it again and omit the sesame oil.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @jennifer maybe try a different sesame oil. One that's not so strong. I like the Japanese ones.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe. No issues. Best pumpkin pie of my life, by far.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made this three times and my question is, is this for a deep dish 9" pie? Because I always have too much filling. Usually, I make a couple of little custard cups, which are delicious. But it would be easier if that information were clear.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No one like this pie at my Thanksgiving. It was pretty to look at but the flavor was awful

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this pie as written and loved it.  It’s not too sweet, with a delicious nuttiness.  I also served with a little chocolate ganache and whipped cream on the side, which made it sublime.  It does take a really long time though, and thankfully I baked the crust the day before and started the pie filling first thing in the morning.  Otherwise it might not have been done in time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe by weight, the 40 minute cooking time was correct for me. The swirl was beautiful- I served it after about 4 hours in the refrigerator - we had 4 people, everyone liked it. Half the pie went back (covered) into the fridge. The next day the swirls were not discernible- but the pie still tasted great. The sesame flavor seemed more pronounced. For display purposes- I strongly advise serving it the day it is baked.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Candied orange peel can be made in the microwave. One needs to boil the strands of orange rind in three water baths to get rid of the bitterness.  On the fourth round add sugar (keep it wet).  I also love candied grapefruit peel.  You will need a sharp knife for thin strands of peel... and carve away as much of the pith as possible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Just watched the videos (which are very entertaining and delightful!)  He used espresso powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Terry
Where can I find the video?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Heidi you tube NYT Cooking channel. Title is 6 New Thanksgiving pies that will impress everyone you know. There is also a link in recipe description where it says « watch me make my thanksgiving pies ». It’s delightful

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this pie today. For me, it's actually a little one-note. The maple flavor is pretty delicate, and it gets lost between the vanilla/bourbon and the coffee. The orange gives it a nice lift, but the pieces of candied peel overwhelms the maple, too. If I were to make it again, I'd instead add a tiny bit of orange zest and some espresso granules to the filling, and serve it a maple whipped cream, so the maple is a little more forward.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Is this brewed instant espresso in the whipped cream or the granules themselves?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Felicity Merriman There’s a video on YouTube of him making it too, but it’s just the powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: There’s a link to the video of him making the pies above and it was just the granules. I would think that brewed would add too much liquid to the whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Vaughn just made this recipe on their YouTube channel and it does appear to be granules and not brewed coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: The pie was just okay. The taste of butter in both the crust and the filling along with the whipped cream topping was a bit much. Unfortunately, the maple flavor didn’t come through very strongly despite using a nice maple syrup. Im glad I tried it, but maybe this kind of pie just isn’t my thing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Rex Same! I didn’t taste maple at all despite having great syrup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this today right after it was posted. Literally tastes like pancakes and coffee as described! The spongey layer texture on the very bottom of the pie wasn't my favorite - but I've never had a chess pie before and I'm not sure if I didn't whip my eggs long enough or messed something up that caused that... The crunchy bits on top were divine. It did poof up like the video and the whipped cream was delicious. I used vanilla, definitely use the bourbon as flavoring for something extra IMO

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Hmmm, followed recipe exactly and butter floated to the top.  Also, it sunk like a lead balloon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Laurie I also had that result :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Same. :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This is a lovely pie. I made it exactly as directed, and it was great, but next time I wouldn't bother with the laminated dough because it didn't bring anything to the party that a regular pie dough wouldn't. I would probably make twice as much of the whipped cream topping because I love whipped cream, and I'd pipe it on to make it look even better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: You can also make the filling on its own as a dessert. Bake at 350 in a buttered cake or pie pan as directed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Instant espresso powder, not liquid?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: In his video it was just the espresso powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Mitschka Hartley

The espresso powder will dissolve almost instantly in the cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can the pie be prepared ahead of time and frozen, then thawed and topped with the topping just before serving?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Anna, custards don’t freeze well… boo!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I have never thrown away dessert. We made this twice thinking we made a mistake with the first one. Truly inedible. Just outright bad.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Candied the oranges myself, made the Laminated Pie Dough recipe, followed the videos to a T, and unfortunately the pie turned out essentially tasteless (with exception of the espresso whipped cream - which I will be making again perhaps with some kind of chocolate base). The chess pie itself tasted like a slightly sweet saltine cracker.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Here is the link to video. https://youtu.be/WxVKqUtVnjo?si=-4E8DmMP6CXrsylK I just googled NYT Cooking videos pies

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Bustelo makes a good instant espresso. Most Latin stores will have it. It’s also in some Caribbean markets.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: It's a staple in a lot of supermarkets in the northeast! I found the instant expresso at Market Basket in MA, it has on the label its often used for tiramisu - so a italian grocer could also fit the bill!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I watched the video, all my steps looked correct based on that, and I found the pie filling to be grainy and mostly just taste of scrambled eggs. A waste of good (expensive) maple syrup. Maybe this means I got my Thanksgiving fail out of the way before the day of?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: For more maple flavor, substitute real maple extract in place of bourbon or vanilla. I did this with maple ice cream and it made all the difference. I found Cooks brand online.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: The espresso whipped topping was great but the pie itself was a miss for me. I followed the directions exactly but the end result was rather flavorless. Having never had chess pie before I'm not sure if it's supposed to have a somewhat gritty texture due to the cornmeal. I was expecting a texture much creamier and smoother. As others have said, despite 2/3rd of a cup of maple syrup, the maple flavor didn't come through.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: The crust and espresso whipped cream were fantastic. I found the texture of the filling not so great, kinda wobbly. I've never had a chess pie before so perhaps this is normal. The maple flavor was not strong enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This didn’t live up to expectations. I agree with those who found the flavor underwhelming, with the coffee overriding the maple.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made the pie as directed and there was a strange bitter aftertaste. Not sure what happened. Everything was fresh and even used a bourbon barrel aged maple syrup. The coffee whip was delicious but this was not a favorite.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Mine curdled. I knew better than to be flippant with a custard but opted to follow the recipe exactly and it was a split, buttery mess with egg scramble inside. I did like the flavor so I may try again and take extra steps to strain the mixture and use a water bath at a lower temp, but it's frustrating to have issues with the recipe as was.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: The whipped topping was delicious with espresso powder. We found the custard fairly flavorless despite using very good maple syrup. It was quite shallow and I had to double the whipped topping to make a full looking slice. Next time I may double the custard and use maple flavoring to bring out the flavor

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Awful. Don’t ever make. Whipped cream is way too bitter and needs more sugar, the cornmeal does not react correctly, too many eggs.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I love the idea of this pie - and I would need to make it gluten-free. How would a GF oreo cookie crust or graham cracker crust fare with these fillings? Other suggestions?

(I have a decent GF pie crust recipe, but it "tastes GF," and I'm wondering if a cookie crust would be a good compromise to feed those with and without dietary restrictions.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: You could try a Keto pie crust made with almond flour.  A cookie crust unless the cookies are made with gluten free flour will not be gluten free.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I've never made a pie with a cookie crust that had to cook for 35-40min in the oven. Does the top of the crust burn? (I usually use them puddings or ice cream pies)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Honestly, the best GF crusts I’ve made (to sub for regular flakey pastry crust) is just Smitten Kitchens pie dough recipe with a good GF flour mix that has a bit of xanthan gum in it.  I like Bob’s Red Mill or KAF gf flour the best.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Wondering if a store-bought pie crust (like Pilsbury) would be a fair substitute to the laminated suggested?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Walker hi! You do you - there are no wrong answers. I will say I’ve never before made crust the way he shows in the video, with the piling of the quarters and rolling it out in Saran wrap. It’s time consuming, but easy and my results are very good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: After reading about the instant espresso powder vs instant coffee, I used some extra (about 2.5 teaspoons) instant coffee powder because I couldn't find the espresso powder in my local store. I would scale back next time. Coffee was definitely the dominant flavor with that amount of instant coffee in the whipped cream!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Great pie. If you don't serve it right away, and you have spread the whipped cream on top as suggested, then the pie has to be refrigerated.  Once refrigerated the laminated dough is tough to each and hard to cut. I would put the cream on the side.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Loved this, made as written. Will source decaf espresso for the whip cream next time. So light.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I make custard pies and quiches regularly.  My oven's thermostat is correct and I thoroughly pre-heated the oven.  All ingredients were at room temperature and properly measured.  After 45 minutes at 350, the filling had not puffed and appeared only partly set, though the top had browned.  It did not set up as it cooled.  I think my mistake was using the middle rack.  Those few inches must make all the difference!  Don't make my mistake!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Eric, I tried both lower and middle racks and got the same results.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I must have overcooked this because the maple layer turned out like rubber cement. Don't be like me: be sure to let the pie shell cool completely before filling, and start at the shorter end of the baking time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: If I'm interested in serving this to kids too, do folks think cocoa powder could be a stand-in for espresso powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Oh, how we wanted to love this!  But we didn’t.  The thicker bottom layer (presumably my where the cornmeal sank) was unpleasant,  the maple wasn’t nearly punchy enough, and the espresso whipped cream too bitter.   Oh well.    The crust recipe works well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Friendsgiving. It was a hit, though I found that the coffee flavor dominated and any flavor from the bourbon was lost. If I make it again I will double the bourbon and serve the coffee whipped cream on the side.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Agree with those who said the maple flavor was weak, in spite of the dark amber syrup I used. Loved the coffee whipped cream, which dominated the flavor, and I will use it again on my mother’s mocha chiffon pie. Used Starbuck’s Expresso powder, medium roast. It dissolved instantly. I might also try to coffee whipped cream on a chess pie without the maple syrup. An expensive ingredient if you aren’t getting the flavor!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can I do this with a store bought pie crust or is that sacrilegious?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I really think this recipe should be written with more specific instructions. I tried this twice and both times it did not come out. More emphasis should be placed on the need for the ingredients to all be the same temperature, the use of an oven thermometer and to be very cautious of over baking. The spongy texture that results from curdling is awful. I had to seek out additional instructions to even get close to this turning out. On my second try I pulled it out at 32 minutes and it was still over done. I am also not sure the bottom rack is a good cal either.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I don’t usually make my own pie crusts, but I tried it for this pie. For any other non-pie bakers, when it says, “Fold the overhang under itself to form a thick ridge flush with the rim,” it does NOT mean flush with the rim. It means make sure your crust is sticking up above the pie pan about 1/2-1 inch. I followed the directions as written and my crust slid down the inside of the pan :/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: It’s good but not spectacular, definitely got the maple flavor but it’s a little bit eggy. The texture is a bit odd and there was a lot of excess butter throughout making it wet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Like other reviewers, I didn’t find this pie particularly flavorful.  I think I expected more maple and bourbon to come through.  It also seemed like a bit too much butter and was slightly oily.  The crust was fine, but overall just not much flavor.  Maybe this is a characteristic of chess pie in general?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to love this but it just wasn’t good. The chess pie didn’t have much flavor and was hard. The coffee whipped cream was good, but not sweet enough.  Fortunately I made a killer apple pie too

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this as described and it was gross. I've never had a chess pie- is this just the texture? People like it? Coffee whipped cream was the only redeeming quality.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Forget the candied orange on top -- crumbled bacon is the way to go. Trust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Other reviews have said it: it’s not good. The texture is off-putting and not like what I would expect with a chess pie. There is so much butter that it pools on top of the pie and as others have mentioned, you need to mop it up because there is THAT much butter. I love the idea for this flavor combo, but I think the maple syrup makes things too wet and leads to the spongy/eggy texture rather than a smooth custardy filling. I’d love to try just a standard or coffee chess pie with a maple whip - maybe flipping things around might yield a better texture. Do not recommend baking as-is though. I hardly waste food and had to throw this one out because…gross.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I had the same experience. :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Not sure what I did wrong, but the “laminated crust” actually shrunk during the “parbaking”, and the whipped cream came out a little salty. The espresso was a good touch, but not strong enough, and the bourbon also was too subtle for my taste (though I don’t drink alcohol). I watched the video, thought I was doing everything right, but it was a mistake to make it for Thanksgiving without having made it before.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This comes out so rich and is nice after steamed lobsters. Gotta support the lobstermen and women when you live in Maine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This didn’t rise in the oven for me like Vaughn’s did. However, it still turned out delicious. It’s a very unique recipe and was a crowd pleaser. The frosting turned out to be way too salty and I likely will just omit it next time. Excited to try making it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I have now made this pie three times and it hasn't looked like the pie in the video any time. I followed the recipe exactly each time. Mine had little tiny granules on the top. I made it a second time and it was grainy on top. I threw it out and made it a third time, this time using a hand mixer to put it together. It was smoother but still had a grainy top. It wasn't the cornmeal - I used really finely ground organic cornmeal that was silky like flour. I'll never make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I did not love this one. I followed the instructions exactly, but added 1/4 teaspoon of maple extract to boost the flavor. I still found it lack luster in flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: My 86 year old mother’s family is from Georgia (prior to the Civil War). She grew up with Chess Pie as did I. She stood in the kitchen while I made this today. She was very skeptical of this recipe (too much corn meal). She was right. I don’t know what this is but it is NOT a chess pie. It was inedible and I had to throw it out. No Christmas dessert for us. First failure from the NYT food app.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Would this work with dark honey instead of maple syrup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @judith You could use honey rather than maple syrup, but you’d lose the maple flavor that is essential to this Pancake and Coffee dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: "Rap" the dough?? I am not a baker, so maybe this is a thing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I believe in this context it means to strike the dough (say with a wooden roller), to make it easier to roll out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: What is espresso powder? I see it in American recipes but I don’t think we have it in Australia. Is it finely ground coffee or is it instant coffee?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: It's instant coffee!  I think you could use regular instant coffee, the flavor just wouldn't be as intense.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Its a concentrated powder for baking. I use this one and it can be found at Amazon or Target near me: https://shop.kingarthurbaking.com/items/espresso-powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: There are two ways you might be able to get espresso powder where you live. One is to order it from King Arthur flour, if they ship internationally. Otherwise, if you have Starbucks there, they make really convenient instant espresso powders in little packets called "Via"s. They come in decaf and caffeinated. I always use the decaf because caffeine doesn't always cook or bake off. I used them for all my cooking and baking. Good luck!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Why the addition of the cornmeal?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @juliana As I recall, I don’t like the grainy texture of cornmeal in a custard or a pudding. It feels in the mouth like tiny lumps of cornstarch that weren’t adequately incorporated into the pudding. It seems to be a Southern preference. I’m sure the lemon Ches’s pie I was served in North years ago had no corneal. To me it tasted like pecan pie filling without the pecans.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Anyone have suggestions for an alternative whipped cream flavoring for those that don't like coffee? :) I'm thinking orange zest, natural cocoa powder, or nutmeg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Makenzie
Just plain whipped cream worked well for me as I do not like coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Amazon sells cafe bustelo decaf instant espresso powder in jars. Most places don't sell the decaf, only the caffeinated type. I use it all the time. It is in large crystals, and I usually dissolve them in the vanilla in whatever recipe it is. Takes 5 minutes to dissolve.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Do you think I can skip the corn meal?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: did you skip on the cornmeal?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Along with others here, I made this pie last night, allowed it to cool overnight and have already parceled it out to neighbors so it's not so tempting to finish it. It's excellent as written. I did one minor change in that I made a simple cranberry jam of sorts ~ really just some fresh cranberries cooked with some water and sugar ~ to serve on the side. It was helpful in cutting some of the sweetness down but then again, the coffee whipped cream does a lovely job of that as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This sounds great and has big family appeal but someone in my family is allergic to corn products. Any thoughts on subs for the cornmeal? Many thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Marina semolina should work

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Wow! The maple aroma wafting throughout the house was a signal of its greatness. The easy to make crust, was flakey perfection. The filling taste and texture along with the balanced espresso whip creamis a vision of a perfect Sunday morning breakfast. Skipped the candied orange peel, but did a slight grating of orange peel. It was the first dessert to disappear Well done Vaughn! I will have to give your other pies a try.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Pretty horrible flavor combination.  The coffee flavored topping DOES not match the very bland filling.  Give this one a pass.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was mediocre in every way: flavor & texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing & everyone inhaled it! The espresso whipped cream will be a fixture in our espresso Panna cotta!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: My pie crust came out rock hard, and I’m thinking maybe the parbake was too long? Anyone else experience this with the pie crust recipe? Maybe I’ll try it again with a shorter par bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I had the same issue - I think the parbake was too long and the edges needed a shield during the last bake. Didn't help that the filling needed an extra 10 minutes to set! (Followed the recipe exactly)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Yes- We could barely cut the crust. For all the work it was, I'll switch to a simpler method, or just use a graham cracker crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this with graham cracker crust - I also made a huge mistake by completely forgetting the butter!!! I put it in fridge to cool after melting and forgot about it. EVERYONE ate it up- ALL GONE! I did not know until next day I opened fridge and there was my butter. You would never know butter was missing. It was delicious. I’m going to make another one and add butter. I loved coffee in the whipped cream but next time I’ll add baileys to whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I found espresso instant coffee powder at my local Ingles, Medaglia Doro brand.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Unfortunately, did not go over well at our gathering. I reduced the amount of salt, but everyone still thought the cream was too bitter and salty. Definitely make it to taste. Did not get the pancake flavor to come through either.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe and I’m making another one for Thanksgiving- my only issue is the crust baking directions are weird.  Instead of 20 and 20 I did 10 minutes and 10 minutes and even so it was nearly burnt.    ?!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I was excited to make this recipe. Not sure why there’s so much butter called for in the filling, though. It made the final result much too oily. Won’t make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
My family did not know what to expect from this very unusual pie recipe, but we were pleasantly surprised. Since I am not fond of coffee, I just topped it with plain whipped cream. 
By the way, thank you for the best instructions ever for blind baking a pie crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I followed it exactly except I REALLY took my time beating the room temp eggs & sugar til very smooth. It was an accident: I got sidetracked and left my eggs/sugar in the bowl alone for like 30 mins before I went back and beat it some more - final product wasn’t gritty at all. Used just under 3tbsp extra fine yellow cornmeal & just over 1 tbsp flour. Once I added the dry ingredients it went straight into my crust and straight into the oven.
