My whipped cream was also a happy accident: I misplaced the instant espresso so I just used some old coffee from that morning that had gotten really concentrated - just under 1/4 cup made the cream only lightly flavored of coffee, not overpowering the maple at all. 
I loved this pie & will make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I used my regular crust (Melissa Clark's recipe) and it works great. I agree that this is not a dynamic pie. The maple bourbon flavors are mlld but it was still popular. There was a lot of melted butter that bubbled to top and stayed on top of the pie, so I used paper towels to absorb it. I'm not sure how to fix that. Use less butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Agree with others that the pie itself lacked the maple flavor I was expecting.  I struggled with the laminated crust, it got too warm quickly as I was rolling it, but it had good flavor. My pie also had a rubbery texture. But the coffee cream was a hit.  Next year I’m going to try a maple custard pie instead and put that coffee cream on the side for that and other pies (like pecan).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this and it was a nice compliment to other things on the pie table at Thanksgiving -- but I did find it a bit bland. Maybe it would be better with some extra maple extract? I was excited to make it and it was not hard. But I'm not sure I'll bother making it again. Another note - the filling bubbled over and fell on the floor of my oven so now there's a sticky mess that I have to clean. So if you make it, put a rimmed pan underneath it in the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was my first time making a chess pie, and it was a total hit, polished off before the night was through. The pie has very warm, interesting flavors. Reading through the comments, I think the common error is not whisking hard enough (I had to call some extra muscle into the kitchen to whisk it, I couldn’t get rid of the corn meal clumps by myself). I made the espresso whipped cream with very finely ground espresso beans (no instant coffee in our house) and it worked great, I kept it on the side as others recommended. Thanks for the great recipe, can’t wait to eat it again come Christmas time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Fell a little flat. Needed more punch of sweetness, it all felt a bit bland.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Novelty recipe only.  Nothing exciting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving - I really enjoyed it! I used a different pie crust https://www.chocolatemoosey.com/french-onion-quiche/ and it worked well. I also eliminated the cornmeal and had no issues with the pie setting! For the whip I also changed the amounts of espresso powder and sugar - I added 1 T of each turbinado sugar and espresso powder to 1 pint heavy whipping cream - it did not overpower the pie! I also used a robust maple syrup and definitely could taste the maple notes! Will make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: America’s Test Kitchen has a nice tip to make whipped cream in the blender instead of stand mixer to stabilize it. I tried it with this recipe and four days later it’s still creamy. Nice mix of sweet from the maple and bitter from the espresso powder!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Good. I thought the instructions for cooking the crust made it a tad overdone. And it was a bit salty even though we reduced the salt some. Loved the bourbon, maple, and coffee flavor. Overall, fun to try.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe almost exactly. Skipped the candied orange peel. Added 1.5 tsp instant vanilla pudding powder to the whipped cream to stabilize it so pie would hold up fully assembled without having to eat it all in a day. Pie was very rich and delicious. Whole family gave it a big thumbs up. Lovely espresso flavor of the whipped cream helped balance the sweetness of the pie filling. A small slice is plenty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie was best 3 days after baking, warmed in a pan on the stovetop, with a fried egg on top. The pancake texture and maple flavor finally came through. Will not make again though. XD

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: The espresso whip cream is certainly the star of this - in fact I thought it overpowered the pie. The filling cooked perfectly as written, but lacked Umphf of flavor. Came out like a faintly sweet cornbread. More bourbon, more maple, more butter?  I'm not sure, I'm not a baker, but I just wanted a stronger taste to match the whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie crust was a fail for me.  I watched the video for making the crust and really wish Vaughn had talked about making sure the butter was better incorporated into the flour mixture before rolling out...the video seemed to imply barely working it in at all.  The crust fell apart in places, leaving me to try and patch it.  I'll go back to more tried and true crusts from now on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Not sure where I went wrong but this pie went straight into the trash. I was sad, it was rather time-consuming and I had high hopes. The filling was very runny but after leaving it in the oven a little longer and moving it up to a higher rack, it suddenly set very quickly. But after it cooled, it was a soggy, yucky layer that was about 1/4" thick. Fortunately, since it looked so suspect while baking, I had waited to see the cooled pie before I went to the trouble to make the whipped topping.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was . . . interesting, mainly in a good way! I used a store-bought graham cracker crust, and I also sprinkled grated chocolate on at the end, which I thought really helped with the whipped cream - it was quite bitter otherwise. The maple flavor was very muted (if not non-existent), but I didn’t necessarily mind. I’m looking forward to having another piece tomorrow when it’s chilled overnight.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: LOVE reading the comments. I have had chess pie in the past and always wanted to make it. I'm going to make this recipe and I'm wondering if some of the grainy results could be due to coarser cornmeal? Just to be sure, I'm going to buzz it in the food processor. Also, I might consider using 3-4 yolks and two eggs to counteract the 'sponge' and the egginess. And one other thing; I have maple extract which I will add, as a general comment is the maple flavor gets lost.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
This is a recipe where I would try to follow the ingredients and instructions to a T. I first tried to make this with a pre-made pie crust (rolled it out and baked similar to what the recipe called for); but it did not hold the mixture well and it ended up seeping through the crust in areas.

The taste is a subdued sweetness. I agree with comments that it is similar to pancakes with maple syrup - kind of an odd taste for a pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: has anyone tried maple flavor (liquid)  in place of the vanilla/bourbon? My friend gave me his grandma's cookie recipe that calls for 1 Tablespoon of maple flavoring and it's fantastic, nice and maple-y.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: very sadly the rumors are true, this pie is one note and not that delicious. i’m really sad because it looked amazing in the youtube video but def does not taste of pancakes and coffee, maybe just coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Everyone is down on this pie in the comments but I have made it twice following instructions almost exactly (though using store bought frozen gf crust instead) to rave reviews. It’s definitely coffee forward and the maple doesn’t come through a ton, but it’s delicious nonetheless. I think the crust does not need to cook as long as advertised but that might be specific to my gf crust.

Once I tried to make the cream with decaf instant coffee (instead of espresso) and upon trying it before putting on the pie decided it was too bitter. This really needs instant espresso to get that Haagen-Dazs coffee ice cream flavor.

Don’t let the other reviews deter you, this is a tasty pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made for Thanksgiving 2024, requested again in 2025

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Taste was really good.  I don't want to be one of those "make a substitution and complain about the recipe" people, but I only had coarse cornmeal, so I decided to use semolina flour instead.  The pie leaked a lot of butter while baking and I poured it off after it was done, so I might reduce the amount of butter in the filling next time.

Didn't have the energy to make candied orange rind, so I just added orange zest to the whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie was quite good! I think if I made it next time I might put a layer of some kind of nuts on the bottom of the pie shell before adding the filling. I know it will no longer be a chess pie but I think it would be a great addition

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Anna I made mine a week ahead, froze, thawed in fridge then added whipped cream. tasted great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Coffee and Maple Chess Pie Recipe
commentBody:
For some reason I don't understand, the center part of the crust of the pie came out undone and the maple filling separed and baked as two layers (the bottom layer yellowwish orange,—I assume is the cornmeal with other denser parts.—and the top part baked out as a flan with the typical color of a flan—I assume is the eggy part and other lighter ingredients…) 
I cooked it longer (45-50 mins) bcs mine didn't souffléd like Vauhn's so I waited to see the burnished top and the soufflé-action.

Help me understand what happened bcs I don't know. The separation only happens when you over-bake, doesn't it? And if I overcooked it why the center crust came out undone? The sides of the crust look and taste completely fine, and so does the whole pie, but I don't understand what happened.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent recipe. Definitely the best custard I’ve ever made! I think next time I’ll back off the salt in the custard or the salt in the crust. Together they were a bit too ínstense for me, but just wow what a pie!!!  The process is intense as well but it came out beautifully. Don’t worry if your sugar caramel seizes when you add the milk and cream, keep stirring and it will melt back into the sauce. Making the caramel, tempering the eggs and simmering the pudding all in one go is a wild ride! What a rush! Make this pie and you’ll feel like a legend.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Brooke

Exactly!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Pie was a hit at Friendsgiving! Used roasted and salted pistachios and dark rum. Held off on top layer of sliced bananas until just ready to serve.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie sounds wonderful, but I wonder how to keep the sliced bananas on top of the pie from turning brown if the pie is stored for any length of time. Will the plastic wrap over the pie prevent banana oxidation?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Janet Keefer i believe one can use lemon juice to keep them from oxidizing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Janet Keefer cover the bananas with the crème fraîche topping. It will create a barrier against the air and prevent browning.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'd probably leave them off.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In 1970s Britain, this was called Banoffee Pie. (Banana + Toffee = Banoffee)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great British Bakeoff just had a 70's episode.  Their version had a full layer of caramel on the bottom.  I'm considering doing that to this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Pete, a banoffee pie is made using a can of condensed milk that has been boiled until it’s dark golden toffee-like, similar to dulce d’leche

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Pete So easy and delicious. As a Briton in the US Bon Appetite have a recipe I’d recommend, for the caramel I keep it simple with dulce de leche though. This is one dish that extended family demand that I bring to new years/ thanksgiving at this point!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This was absolutely delicious and the family loved it! A couple of tips for the commenters who have worried about things:
1. If you can’t easily find unsalted pistachios, no worries! I used salted and just omitted the other salt in the crust. Turned out so good!
2. The bananas don’t go brown on top if you cover them with the whipped cream. 
Will definitely put this one in the rotation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi Heather! I'm planning to make this pie for Thanksgiving on Wednesday. Did you make it the day before? Do you think it would hold up? I'm considering baking the crust and pudding on Wednesday and then assembling on Thanksgiving. I appreciate your thoughts!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm preparing to make this.  I can definitely say, if you heat the cream 24 hours in advance and steep a couple sliced bananas in it, you will get a much more intense banana flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We loved this pie as Vaughn wrote it, but I'm trying your idea next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dan I worked with a pastry chef who would use dried bananas or banana runts (the candy), when making the custard to punch up the flavor a few notches.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I know a vanilla wafer crust is traditional, but I'm just wondering if substituting shortbread would require doing anything differently.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lydia, I think the main difference is that would require using shortbread rather than vanilla wafers to make the crust

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yum! This pie sounds delicious. What would be a good substitute for the pistachios? (There’s a nut allergy in my family.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ashley There are definitely recipes that have the vanilla water crust that don’t have nuts. One of my favorites is a key lime bar recipe that also features the Nilla wafer crust, so I would recommend finding one similar. And for on top, you could omit them from the topping completely or find another attractive garnish that you feel compliments the pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ashleyi would use a mixture of Camilla wafers and ginger snaps.  Don’t make into a homogenous mixture, make it a speckled one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where’s the butterscotch?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ray, the brown sugar/butter mixture is butterscotch

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: you make it in step 3

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Ray butterscotch is made with unsalted butter
dark brown sugar, salt, whipping cream (preferably and vanilla extract. That’s all in this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dan's suggestion of steeping banana slices in the cream/milk mixture is genius. I just tried this and the custard turned out Awemazing! The flavor is over the top!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nuts.com is a great resource for all kinds of nuts (in addition to all kinds of other foodstuffs). They usually have multiple versions of all kinds of nuts. I highly recommend them and have been extremely pleased with the quality of product I get from there.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Second note. Second day. I thought the pie was divine the first day. It melded for 4 hours. But good lord. The next day it was whatever is beyond divine. Supreme? If you can make it a day ahead, that’s the way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sounds great but I have no idea where to find unsalted Pistachios. Could be a deal killer for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jim Amazon! There are many choices. My guess is Whole Foods as well, if you have one nearby.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jim. If you have a Trader Joe’s or health food store nearby, try that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jim To feed my addiction I buy Fiddyment Farms unsalted pistachios (in shell) at Whole Foods. However, not every Whole Foods in my area carries the unsalted version.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
SO SO GOOD!! Made this for Thanksgiving and it was the best pie on the table :).
I added some dark chocolate shavings on top and dried rose petals. Going to have to make two of these next year.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was sooooo good! I liked the added salt, next time I would decrease the sugar in the crust, a little too intense for my taste

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is literally the best pie I have ever made. We added a tad of rum but otherwise followed exactly!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My butterscotch turned out lumpy but nevertheless this pie is delicious! For those who commented that the crust was salty, that is true though I think that’s intentional since it balances out the sweetness of the butterscotch and the whipped cream. Highly recommend :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this at Thanksgiving and it won the “best dessert” title at our family gathering. It’s a perfect pie. 

I had a GF cousin in town this past week and used GF Oreos in place of the vanilla wafers in the crust. Yet again - a huge hit. The best part of this recipe is the butterscotch pudding, banana, and crème fraiche whipped cream - the crust base   flavors can definitely be changed up!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving, sans pistachios, and it was otherworldly. Fantastic. Added an extra tablespoon of cornstarch to make it neatly sliceable. Worked great. Held off that top layer of bananas until after refrigeration and I was ready to top with whipped cream to prevent browning. Question: I've been challenged to make this dairy free for a lactose intolerant friend who was sad she couldn't partake. Coconut milk/cream? Thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kathy I’m wondering the same for this Thanksgiving - I may just try it and 🤞

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have to agree with some other posters that the crust does not need all that salt. It was quite salty, but still delicious. But I would’ve left most of it out.  Also, my custard was not an appetizing color, although it wasn’t super obvious because it’s sandwiched in between bananas and cream so not a big deal – tasted pretty good. Probably would not make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Angela Were you using salted nuts? I thought the salt levels were fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do yourself a favor and make this recipe as written the first time, then adjust it if you feel the need. You won’t be disappointed, this pie is delightful. I couldn’t disagree more with the folks who felt the quantity of cornstarch was excessive (it’s not). The salt level is ideal, too, but reduce it a bit if you’re using salted pistachios.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I accidentally used salted pistachios and heartily agree with this comment - crust turned out way too salty! I'm sure it would have been perfect if I'd used unsalted pistachios.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for my birthday cake. Did one-and-a-half times the recipe for a 12” pie. (6 bananas!) The crust is difficult to handle, although delicious — I highly recommend it be thoroughly chilled before baking. And I might add a tbsp more butter. Ran out of wafers so I used plain breadcrumbs for about a quarter of the amount. So maybe that’s why it was too crumbling. Had caramel sauce so I used strings of that across the top of the whipped cream. And I spread the cream right to the edge of the pan. I have made banana cream pie for many years. This will replace my recipe. Two family members said this was their favorite pie ever. Browning of bananas never bothered me. For one thing it doesn’t change the taste and for another, who has leftover banana cream pie?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Swoon

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As others mentioned, this pie becomes significantly more delicious after a day in the fridge! I tried it after the four hour chilling period, and while it was perfectly set by then, the flavor didn’t blow me away. I dug back in after two days and it was such a hit! I highly recommend waiting at least overnight! If I were to make this again I’d probably use regular whipped cream instead of the sour cream whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie went over well! I thought it was a little sweet so if I made it again maybe I'd figure out how to cut down on the sweetness. One thing I'm glad I did is sprinkled the  bananas with fruit fresh which is just citric acid and doesn't add any flavor versus say lemon juice. The bananas never turned color.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just sensationally good. Luscious velvety butterscotch custard, the banana slices and the rum (an optional addition which I embraced!) amplifying one another beautifully, that lovely toasty nutty crust, and the just-barely-sweetened whipped cream layer keeping enough balance that the whole thing manages to avoid being overly sweet or cloying. So good that I ended up making a second one just two days after the first, because the first one got gobbled up and I barely had any to myself!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you love Banana Cream Pie, this recipe is not an improvement in my opinion.  There are too many competing flavors that are arguing with each other.  The creme fraiche added to whipped cream helps to cut the sweetness but, again, it's an added flavor element that doesn't elevate the basic idea, the banana.  Pistachios in the crumb crust is really nice and I'll do that for my next banana cream pie craving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this pie to bring to a potluck with a group of friends. It was a smashing success! Just scrumptious and quite different from other banana cream pies. I couldn’t find the unsalted pistachios so used finely ground walnuts instead and it couldn’t o

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this pie for a pre-holiday family get together yesterday.  It came out great!  Minor subs...macadamia nuts for pistachios.  Made whipped topping with just cream and powdered sugar.   Put top layer of bananas and whipped cream on just before serving.  Thanks to @Vaughn Vreeland for the great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made is directed and thought it way too sweet. Next time I'd cut the sugar by 35-50%.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Kinnikinnick Gluten Free Vanilla Wafers for the crust with just a little extra butter than called for, and it was absolute perfection. Also went a little heavy handed on the pistachios. My entire family went BANANAS for this pie. If you enjoy rum, go the rum route. 10s across the board, no notes, will make again, what a stunner. And yes like others have mentioned it IS very sweet, but what else do you expect from a banana cream pie, let alone a butterscotch one?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Makenzie Did you use a 36 OZ or 53 OZ, 9 inch pie dish?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i did the same thing with the crust and agree, it was delicious!! also will note i added vanilla extract and rum bc why not. it was so so good

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Since no-salt pistachios are hard to find I used Wonderful Pistachio Lightly Salted Shelled Pistachios. Opted for unsalted butter and cut the recipe salt measurement in half. Perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Makenzie

Trader Joe’s sells shelled unsalted pistachios.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Makenzie me too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What’s the ideal dish size? Apart from it being 9 inches, should I use a 36 OZ or 53 OZ dish? Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
DON’T quit stirring the brothy dairy and sugar slurry! I lasered the temp (up to high 160s, low 170s,while constantly stirring with a whisk until voila, the pudding came together!
Chemical baking is not as easy as shagging up a loaf of sourdough 😂

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I swear to God, fine people of the NYT, using salted (instead of unsalted) pistachios in this recipe will not result in a catastrophe. Some people may even find them preferable. Feel free to stick your neck out and contemplate substitutions to address personal preference and ingredient availability, for example, different nuts (or none) in the crust, and dried cranberries, coconut, or chocolate shavings for garnish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I just made it for Thanksgiving. I will pipe the cream on tomorrow, but the residual butterscotch in my pan was out of this world!

I used Chessman cookies instead of Nilla Wafers.  We do this for a banana pudding, which should rend an excellent result.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Be warned!!! This recipe doesn’t have enough corn starch. So the pie was completely ruined, it didn’t set overnight. Martha Stewart adds a whole half cup of corn starch and an extra egg. The milk soup tastes good but needs serious modifications so that it’s not a complete disappointment and time and food wasted. I followed everything to a T.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine didn't set correctly, either. I think I should have cooked it longer after adding the cornstarch/yolk mixture--I took it off the heat pretty quickly against my better judgement. Good news is that it is excellent served frozen!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Samantha not true. it’s perfect. maybe you didn’t cook it long enough. It was perfect

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for thanksgiving today and I don’t know if I’ve ever made something a delicious as this in my entire life. It was perfect, everyone loved it, and I was so glad the recipe was easy to follow and turned out exactly as planned!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: New Thanksgiving tradition! After stuffing ourselves with gumbo, we somehow managed to inhale more of this pie than we thought possible. We followed the recipe exactly, although we didn't let the crust cool all the way after baking, and that didn't affect adding the bananas and pudding. The pistachios atop the cream were a nice touch!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: for all the nut free people - I made for someone with a nut allergy and instead of adding nuts to the recipe i made the crust exactly as is but browned the butter - it gave it a “nutty flavor” and had chopped pistachios on the side! It was perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A big hit! We made it for thanksgiving in hawaii with local apple bananas and it was delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So easy and lights-out delicious!!!!  Followed the recipe exactly and it couldn't have been easier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe a tad too much salt in the crust (I did use salted pistachios) and I heated the pudding too quickly, leaving it lumpy, but this was still very delicious despite all of that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was very good. I waited to put the all the stuff on top of the pudding until serving time. I also was a little shy with the sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even with weights and ceramic pan, my crust collapsed like Vaughan did in the video. But I could press it back up gently and it all worked out. Used rum and covered the slightly browned banana with cream. Will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe was excellently.  written. All the steps worked and pie came out exactly like the picture. Topping was delicious. But if you don’t care for butterscotch do t make it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie might be the most delicious pie I’ve ever eaten. We’re usually chocolate people, but this one won over the other 3 pies we made for thanksgiving. I skipped the pistachios because I don’t like nuts in my food, and used an expresso dark rum. It kept in the fridge well, though it was a little bit difficult to make the butterscotch pudding filling. It’s very rich and decadent. I 100% recommend this pie to anyone who likes banana pudding, and to anyone who doesn’t like banana pudding too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie was the hit of Thanksgiving—now it’s a favorite Birthday pie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made for New Year’s Eve. A hit!!!

Used vanilla instead of rum. Omitted pistachios. 

Next time, I would do the nuts, reduce the sugar, and stick with whipped cream only. 

But it was so so so good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was superb. I doubled it and put in 9x13 Pyrex instead of pie pan to serve a large group. Easy and worked well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
We all went crazy for the nilla wafer crust, but everyone preferred the pudding sans bananas. 
I agree it was a little too cornstarch-y. 
There will probably be a next time but I’ll tot with the recipe a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'd toss the banana with some lemon or lime juice to keep them from turning brown

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Took this over to a friends house for dinner and it was a big hit. I didn’t think the crust was a little saltier than I expected so will probably cut that back a little bit in the future. I also replaced the pistachios with slightly toasted walnuts, and I thought they gave it a really rich flavor. But overall, it was a really big hit and will definitely make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a delicious hybrid of butterscotch pudding and perennial favorite banana cream pie! We made it for Father’s Day for 3 dads in our family and it was a hit! Easy to make and am super yummy. Don’t skip the pistachios for the additional crunch

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Soo good. After making it the first time, I would pack the crust a little less tightly and may try a 9.5” pie plate next time. Huge hit with the family and will definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this one night and boy was it god. Very decadent, slightly on the heavy side and very creamy! Would recommend more for the holiday season than offhandedly, since the flavors are strong and heavy they pair well with other foods than a stand alone dessert. I would make it during the Thanksgiving/winter season as suggested. Turned out delicious though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm puzzled.  How are you getting butterscotch flavor from a pie made with rum?  Scotch,  browned butter and sugar can work, please tell me how this works.  I love butterscotch but I think I might be disappointed by this effort.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Butterscotch flavor comes from the brown sugar. If you don't want rum, don't use rum. The recipe suggests vanilla as an alternative. Butterscotch is made from brown sugar and butter. It does not contain Scotch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for a pie baking contest in my neighborhood-I won. It is amazing. Because it was for a lot of people, I made a simple graham cracker crust instead of using the nuts. Everything else was magical.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for the birthday party of my daughter and father-in-law. A total hit. My daughter asked for banana birthday dessert, so I used this recipe. Changes I made: I added 1/3 cup of coconut to the crust as well as pecans instead of pistachios because I had pecans. To make this again, I'd double the crust. It wasn't enough to cover the sides to accommodate the pudding. And I threw all the bananas into the bottom of the pie instead of putting half on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My husband loves banana pudding,and I was so excited to make this for him. I made it exactly as written and followed the weights provided. It was a disappointment. The crust was way too sweet, and I didn’t think there were enough bananas. I did think the pudding was good. If I had to do it over I would make the pudding and use it to make a layered banana pudding.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Years ago, there was a pie place on Busch Blvd in Tampa that made a fantastic Banana Cream Pie. One of the bakers shared with me the secret to keeping bananas from browning-7Up! The sliced bananas were dipped/sprinkled with the soda. The citric acid in the soda retarded the development of browning. I learned over the years any citrus soda would work.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What a great pie!  With some of the notes here in mind, I used 3/4 teaspoon of Kosher salt in the crust, otherwise made exactly as written.  It's a little bit of work, but not hard at all, and the results outstanding.  I had 3 medium bananas, and two of them were under the pudding.  Next time, I'll up the quantity there a bit and make my slices a little bit thicker so that there's a more easily discernible banana texture.  The pudding on its own is worth making, btw.  Make this pie soon!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie is excellent and we had it for Easter dinner. I made the crust and custard the day before and assembled the pie up to the custard.  I put Saran Wrap over the top and refrigerated. Made  the whipped cream on day two then finished off the assembly. Worked out great!  Used Kraken dark rum in the custard and whipped cream and it tasted great. The family loved it in the combination of butterscotch and fresh bananas is perfect for spring.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I don’t know who needs to hear this, but if you live in the UK and can’t find Nilla wafers, try mixing the crust with Indian tea rusk biscuits - I got the tip from a Reddit thread and found some at Tescos. I left them without packaging on the counter for a couple days so they’d go slightly soft and stale. If I don’t eat the other half of the box, I’d like to try them in the magnolia banana pudding next! 

Also editors: can you please specify if the butter in the crust is salted or unsalted - thx

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you can’t find unsalted nuts, just cut back the added salt in the crust. Sounds good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Am i really stupid…where does the butterscotch come into play?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To T, The caramelized brown sugar with the custard, etc = butterscotch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make a version of this pie. I would replace the rum with scotch whisky. Maybe up it to a couple or 3 tablespoons.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The best pistachios come from Brontë Sicily near Mt Etna. https://www.gustiamo.com/bronte-pistachios-by-caffe-sicilia/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can buy unsalted pistachios at Trader Joes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this pie tonight, and it was a huge hit. A few things…

-I used salted pistachios and just didn’t add extra salt to the crust. Worked out fine. 

-I would cut the crust recipe in half. This made way too much crust. I had a lot of unused crumbs and the crust I did have was too thick. 

-I didn’t garnish with bananas on top because I didn’t want them to brown. Just add all the bananas to the bottom. 

-The cream on top is good. It’s only lightly sweet, so it cuts the sweetness of the pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: FYI Aldi carries unsalted and salted pistachios.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Soak bananas in peeling in hot water for one minute (or longer) and the slices won't 
turn brown for days.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this pie to bring to a potluck with a group of friends. It was a smashing success! Just scrumptious and quite different than other banana cream pies. I couldn’t find the unsalted pistachios so used finely ground walnuts instead. Tasted divine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How would one blend bananas into the pie base rather than slices on top, or use a few slices as garnish?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Unfortunately own experience with some watery and/or gray banana cream pies over the years is that you need to just use sliced bananas. Slices work.  All of my attempts to mash them into a pudding, including a Bobby flay recipe, were failures.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really enjoyed making this this past weekend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Woo is me, a Canadian. We cannot get vanilla wafers (Nilla Wafers) here any longer. Any tried and true substitution?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really can't think of any, though a standard graham cracker crust would probably work well.  Maybe add vanilla extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @christine you can absolutely sub other cookies for vanilla wafers, I have had success with ginger snaps, biscoff cookies, graham crackers, German Leibniz biscuits, shortbread, lemon cookies, cardamom crisps, etc. The trick is to look for a cookie with a grittier, slightly dry texture. If it pairs well with and afternoon tea or coffee, it probably works for a crumb crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy, beautiful and delicious. I let the crust bake a little longer, and I recommend using sour cream in the topping. And that rum! It warms you just a little from the inside on a chilly fall day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used salted pistachios, but it felt a bit salty to me, so I would follow others lead and cut out the salt of the dish in others ways next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie is amazing! The flavors play together so well! I made it for work last week and have a request for another one this week!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After 4 hours chilling it was still a bit messy, overnight did the trick though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Extremely delicious and was a hit for 2 dinners in a row (sorry for the guests of the second dinner - the looks of the pie were a little less gorgeous, but the taste was even better).

A few notes :
I did put less sugar than stated in the crust and in the whipped cream. The pudding was sweet enough. 
I used salted pistachios (didn’t bother to rince them) and it was delicious.
I baked the crust the day before, the pudding as well. I assembled the pie a few hours before dinner and refrigerated it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: has anyone used pecans instead of pistachios?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jill No but that sounds really good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made this recipe first as written, and it was great. When I made it the second time, I used pecans and graham cracker crumbs, because that appealed to my senses, and everyone thought it was amazing -- better than the original!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jilly Yes!! Wowza! It was SO good!! I didn’t have any pistachios on hand, so I used pecans… amazing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this 3 days ago... a big hit. I used two tspns rum (filling) plus a little vanilla extract (love vanilla). Half the salt (in pudding)-go by your taste. Crust is kinda thick but we loved it. My store had unsalted pistachios but they are very mild so I think any mild nut or pepitas (for folks w/nut allergies) would work well to get the nut texture. Bananas did go brown quickly (certainly by day 2) but the whipped cream coves them and still delicious. There is a lot going on but interesting!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this exactly as directed. Came out perfect  and was a huge hit. Will definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Outstanding

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this pie for Thanksgiving. I didn’t have vanilla wafers and used graham crackers for the crust. It was delicious. The addition of the crème fraiche was perfect for lessening the sweetness of the whipped cream topping since the filling, bananas and crust were quite rich and sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made this and it was great! I added a chocolate ganache before chilling and adding the whipped cream and I’m really glad I did.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was delicious. I used raw pistachios and I would recommend using raw, better consistency and color for the garnish. I thought the crust had too much sugar and salt, I would probably halve the sugar and just do a pinch of salt. The bananas on top did get brown even under the plastic wrap overnight, so next time I’ll wait to add them until just before adding the whipped topping after chilling. I used a bit extra vanilla and sugar in the whipped topping.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Sarah

Hi. My son, who is a junior chef, said the same thing - a bit too much sugar and too much salt.   

He did have two helpings though so he liked it.  

I refrigerated it for 7 hours and I thought the pudding hadn’t set enough.  Any thoughts why?
Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i ended up loosening the pudding with extra milk and cream, you could probably use a bit less cornstarch depending on preference. otherwise was so delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good. So good. But don’t top the pie with bananas until it is time to serve. The fruit will then look pleasing as it does in the accompanying photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The pie was very good.  I added one t of vanilla extract to the custard and one to the whipped cream also. Initially I thought there would be enough salt in the caramel but just a little more would be perfect.  I would make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This came out great but next time Id skip the added sugar in the crust. The vanilla wafers are plenty sweet enough and the added sugar seemed to be too much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Jamaican Rum, which I had on hand and that worked well! I would omit the salt in the crust as it overpowered the custard, which was the most amazing pudding I’ve ever tasted! You could wait to add the bananas on the top layer for when you make the whipped cream so they don’t brown if that concerns you. Great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very tasty. Banana cream pie and butterscotch pudding are two of my fav desserts items.  Be warned, this pie is super sweet - dare I say, too sweet!?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was the best pie ever and made Thanksgiving that much more delicious!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This has earned a permanent place on our Thanksgiving pie roster. Insanely good! The crust is a bit sweet so I’d make sure it’s a thinner layer next time. I also strained the custard through a sieve in its way into the pie. Otherwise everything was as per the recipe. Everyone loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very delicious! The whipped cream really helps balance things out, I’d maybe put more salt in the butterscotch next time around. Made it with walnuts instead of pistachios, still very good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a hit with most but way too sweet for me. Next time I would cut down on sugar both in the pudding and the crust. I added the top sliced bananas at the end instead of step 5 as I do not keep plastic film at home. I am in the UK so used double cream for the whipped cream which unfortunately led me to make butter instead so in my case it should not be on high speed. Live and learn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pudding has so much cornstarch, it just tastes like cornstarch.  Disappointing.  It’s a cheater way to thicken.  Also the filling has way too much salt, and this is coming from someone who likes salty things.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Katie I couldn’t disagree more on the cornstarch. This recipe is perfect in that respect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My pudding came out lumpy (a problem I always have had when attempting puddings!). Any advice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @julz Try adding the cornstarch gradually and whisk it really well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly, but the pudding didn’t set. It tasted good, for banana butterscotch soup…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So far this is my absolute favourite banana-toffee pie recipe and I have tried MANY. Pistachios are far too expensive here so I just used an extra 30g of graham crumb. Go with the 1/2 teaspoon salt - 1 teaspoon is WAY too much. Only gets better the next day - and who cares about brown bananas! Still tastes fantastic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sola I didn’t find the salt excessive, but I would reduce it if using salted pistachios.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @julz mine got lumpy too, not sure what I did wrong

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a disappointment. The bananas overpowered the butterscotch, which was the draw for me. The cream was not stiff enough. I watched the video twice, read the recipe several times. Prepared as directed. My pudding never solidified. Would recommend chilling overnight if you don't want soup!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
There is no butterscotch in this butterscotch banana pudding! To make butterscotch, heat brown sugar in butter until you get a deep Maillard reaction. 

Here, sugar and water combine to make a brown sugar syrup. Eventually butter is added a few steps down the line, too late to do the work intended.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you have a butterscotch pudding recipe you like?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I make the whipped cream topping the day in advance?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Granted my burners suck, but don’t be afraid to add a little more water if your brown sugar doesn’t dissolve.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I watched the video due to the comments about feeling accomplished at managing the tempering etc.  i really enjoyed the presentation, in part because he is a bit of a messy cook and I can relate!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Whoops! Didn’t see the Ritz cracker substitution just a few words down. And it’s not mentioned as a substitute in the recipe though. 

Love the subhead enough to suggest the recipe be renamed. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really dislike bananas but this custard sounds amazing. Would this pie hold up without the bananas? Perhaps double the custard recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’d love to try this, but my husband has an intolerance to eggs. What egg substitute would work best here?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie tasted amazing! Possibly the best pie I’ve ever had. I love the saltiness of the crust and custard. For some reason my custard turned out kind of runny - not sure what happened there. But not a big deal really because it was so delicious! I used spiced rum instead of dark rum in the custard because that’s what I had and it turned out great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: beautiful, best pie. My friends loved it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe. You can taste the saltiness of the nuts and butterscotch but it works so well in the recipe to balance the flavors. The surprise was the whipped topping. Loved the tanginess with the sweet butterscotch pudding.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious pie but mine was a little runny.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even better on the second day. I topped with a homemade butterscotch topping instead of additional pistachios, but the pistachios in the crust really elevate things. I just blended them in a food processor at the same time as the wafers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Easy to make
I made it in a springform pan:
-Crust bottom and sides
-Filling up the sides almost to the top
-When I put the whipped cream on, it was still in the pan and beautifully level and layered. 
-Sprinkled the crushed pistachios in an attractive circle and put three slices of banana on with serving.

It was too sweet for me, but I was the only one that didn’t have a HUGE slice.  It was fun to introduce and see everyone fall in love with what I think of as a classic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My pie was so messy when I cut into it. Did I do something wrong? Any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fabulous dessert, not terribly time consuming, I ended up making the crust one day and the pudding the following day. I used GF cornflakes for the crust to make it celiac friendly and that is what I had on hand, it was a big hit. I wouldn't bother with the bananas on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Combining two of my favorite things - butterscotch and banana cream. This was a hit with my people and it was easy enough for me, not a baker, to prepare. Will do this one again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had way more pudding than would fit in the pie…think I followed the directions correctly…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The salt in the crust is a really nice touch. Didn't have cornstarch so i used tapioca starch instead and called it mochi pie. It was good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: the hardest part of this recipe is getting the batter off my forehead after licking the bowl clean. BEST COOKIE EVER.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Debbie S
Hello! Can you please share how you modified this recipe to make a chocolate peppermint version? How much peppermint extract did you use and did you omit the vanilla altogether?  Thank you for your guidance!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Debbie S
A woman after my own heart. .

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Debbie S This is the best comment EVER.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No need to buy expensive (maybe not-so-fresh) espresso powder, just use your leftover coffee grounds instead! Spread grounds out on a cookie sheet and dry out in oven at 170° for approx. 2.5 hours (or until they feel "crunchy"). Use a coffee grinder to grind into a fine powder and voilà! Keeps for a couple months.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Simms
This is great advice - instant espresso is stupidly expensive just to make cookies. I used one tube of instant coffee I had for camping and otherwise followed the recipe exactly as is - the cookies came out fantastic. Thanks for suggesting another method!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Simms I agree! I’ve also done the recipe and used coffee extract which works just as well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Simms I use ground up instant coffee and that works well too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best chocolate cookie recipe in my opinion! I made the peppermint version many times and I still have friends who bring it up or request it for gatherings. I even made a special peanut butter brownie cookie version for my partner’s birthday. This recipe is very adaptable to whatever fun extra flavors you might want to add. 10/10 🙌

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How much peanut butter did you add

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Also, how much/form peppermint?   Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Too much Butternut Squash

Was looking to find out how much peppermint extract did was added.  Did you omit the vanilla?  Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They are so quick and easy! I will say I think they are very sweet.. I want to lower the sugar but I’m worried the eggs may not whip up

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Diana M
Maybe you can use a chocolate with less sugar instead of reducing the actual sugar so the egg mixture is unaffected.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Diana M
Just use 100% bittersweet chocolate. I prefer a less sweet dessert, and these worked out perfectly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Maureen 100% is not bittersweet it is unsweetened and I think most people would find that a little too bitter. I’ve cut the sugar by 30 grams and they are delicious and still sweet enough

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you don’t want to wash a pan or skillet (!) in the butter melting step, it still works to melt it in the microwave and pour it over the chocolate mixture. Maybe even use the same measuring glass measuring cup you used initially for the dry ingredients…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were so easy to put together and the baking time was so short - love this recipe. I used Dutch processed cocoa powder because that's what I had on hand. I thought the dough was going to be too runny to form a cookie, but I was wrong. They came out perfectly. Rich, dark, chocolatey taste and the salt on top was a very nice addition. Will make them again... probably tomorrow!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kay how much cocoa powder please?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Several people have mentioned a peppermint version. Is there another recipe somewhere? Perhaps substituting peppermint for the espresso powder>

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Susan for a peppermint version, I recommend sprinkling crushed candy cane on top before baking. Peppermint extract doesn’t always play well with chocolate in the batter (can taste muddy) and the candy canes will melt a bit into a delightful chewy-crunchy addition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Susan https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022771-peppermint-brownie-cookies?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: love the videos, they are really helpful. only noodge is that the text box often obscures what we're looking at, especially on close-ups of the subject matter.  any chance of putting the text off to one side so as not to interfere with the focus?  thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 70% Valharona which was too bitter for the amount of sugar. I recommend using semisweet or adding more sugar.  After tasting the batter, I decided to sprinkle the tops with demerara sugar to offset the bitterness. Helped a bit, but think they would have been better from the get go had I used regular semisweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They taste delicious but mine extremely flat and not so crackly,  I think I whipped the egg mixture enough, maybe I didn’t add enough flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jasper294 I had the same result, and can’t figure out why.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jasper294 me to. My first batch was perfect. But that two were also flat and I couldn’t get the cracks. Very strange.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was wondering why mine weren't shiny, and came to realize that I accidentally completely left out the dark brown sugar. For my preferences, the resulting cookies are a great level of sweetness and pleasantly chewy. If I make this recipe again, I'll probably use the same total amount of sugar that I used this time, but match the ratios of white and brown sugar to that written in the recipe. Good accidental experiment for a not-too-sweet dessert fan!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Peppermint version: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022771-peppermint-brownie-cookies

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent cookies, easy recipe. I took these to my first HOA meeting so that people would like me, and my mission was a complete success.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You must bake this cookie at the time of making it. Because it does not have any baking soda or baking powder in it, it does not hold well as a dough overnight before baking. It is an easy fast recipe and a delicious cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Just delicious! I will say that I collapsed the fiddly chocolate-melting this way: i melted the butter in a saucepan, turned off the heat, broke up an eight-ounce bar of baking choc into squares, and threw them in. When they melted, I added the cocoa. Worked just fine. No need to chop the chocolate!

Like another commenter, I reduced the sugar a bit. And instead of flaky sea salt, I dusted the finished, cooled cookies lightly with a bit of powdered. Sugar. Divine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A Vaughn cookie video in August - such a treat!These are so so good, I’ve been making this version from the peppermint recipe but without the peppermint, they are a hit. Added cayenne powder to the batter last time I made them and it was an excellent combo for those who like a little heat with their chocolatey goodness. I appreciate the whole melting the chocolate in the pot instead of transferring to a measuring cup update in this version;)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I intend to make these delicious sounding cookies with a couple of changes. We do not any longer eat animal products.  So, I will use 1 plant based butter stick and 2 " flax eggs." That is 1 T. of ground flaxseed to 3 T. of cold water per "egg." I expect the cookies to turn out very good! I have had to learn how to "veganize" lots of recipes since my husband almost had a killing heart attack in 2015. He has since then lost over 50 pounds and is doing very well. Hope this comment is permitted!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How did they come out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Catherine can you tell us how they came out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the recipe exactly as written and it was amazing. I love making brownies from scratch but sometimes you need something chocolate and baked in a hurry and this fit the bill. I didn't feel like getting out my stand mixer but I hadn't noticed the egg/sugar step, which is a bit more of a hassle with a hand mixer but it was fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just took a batch out of the oven. I made the peppermint version last December, so kinda knew what to expect. They will be my perfect for my husband who just had oral surgery and is on a soup and ice cream diet right now! I was out of expresso powder but added 1/2tsp of coffee extract and it worked great.  Thanks for this delicious addition to my cookie file!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful cookie. I have made this recipe several times and find the best results come after refrigerating the dough. I portion the cookies into balls and then chill overnight and bake the next day. This gives the exterior a nice firmness and crispy edges while leaving the center soft and fudgey. Always well received

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are spectacular.  Perfect recipe. No notes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The depth of flavor was 100 times deeper the second day. Amazing! Highly recommend baking the day before serving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Baked 4 cookies, put dough in frig fora couple of hours.  Then made balls and froze.  thawed for 20 minutes before cooking.  (We are only 2 folks so 18 of these super cookies would not be a good idea!).  Also used 3/4 cup light brown sugar and 1/4 cup granulated--I think would be ok with just the 3/4  light brown

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: WAY too sweet.  They were so fun to make, and the batter is delicious but the finished product is sickeningly sweet.   Think of it this way, most brownie recipes call for a cup of sugar as do most cookie recipes.  This recipe calls for 1.25 cups.  Next time I make them (I might, I said they were fun..) I will definitely cut down on sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @ncpinatl just wanted to give an update.  the next day they  are still very sweet.  But it’s more that  they’re very very rich.  Definitely not as cloying sweet as they first were.  In fact  they might be a great diet option because one bite will cure your chocolate craving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the instruction to bang the pans hard while cookies are hot for extra fudginess!  I have a home cook-created recipe for brownies given me years ago with the ‘bang-hard’ direction but have never seen it in another recipe.  It works!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For a spicy version add the following:
-1.5t chipotle powder
-3/4t cinnamon
-1/4t cardamom

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these exactly as written and they were delish.  Very much like a brownie cookie.  I like some of the additions mentioned in these comments. I'm gonna try some orange blossom water another time, and I really want to add pecans...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried to freeze the dough to bake later?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Chelsea yes, I froze balls of dough and baked them later. I followed the suggestion of another commenter and let them thaw 20 mins before baking. They were delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm very confused by the 5 star rating on this recipe. There is no way the cook time on these is correct. I bake very often, have an oven thermometer, and followed the recipe correctly. I baked them for an extra few minutes until they appeared puffed & shiny but they were still very underdone in the center. They're basically brownie batter cookies. Not chewy at all. They're still ok though because brownie batter tastes good. But there's something off with this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This was my experience also.  I followed the recipe to the letter. They were quite under-done on the inside......more like a "molten lava" cookie than a brownie.
Way too fudgie.  I would really prefer something a bit "cakier".....perhaps more flour????

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @welters are you using a scale to measure the flour? If the flour is too packed or too loose in a measuring cup the weight/amount of flour can be drastically different and significantly impact the recipe. I’ve made this recipe 5-6 times precisely measuring all the ingredients (most importantly the flour), and they’ve come out perfect every time. Highly recommend investing in a digital kitchen scale if you don’t have one! $10 on Amazon. :-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Cookie Monster I followed the recipe exactly as written and had beautiful results.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So not worth it.  Use better ingredients and make a pan of regular brownies. 15 dishes used plus I had to drag out my kitchenaid mixer.   They are tasty but regular brownies are fine for stuff that my husband will gobble down. OTOH I’ve been making brownies for literally 50 years and I’ve gotten blue ribbons for them. So yeah, brownies are way easy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Things I learned and will try in my regular recipe next time: beating the eggs and sugar until they are fluffy and folding in the chocolate and flour. 
Adding coffee (I used Cafe Bustelo instant) to the chocolate. 

Though this does mean that I pull out the kitchenaid.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Stephanie George

CAN YOU SHARE YOUR BROWNIE RECIPE? I’m always looking for a better one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I keep a small jar of espresso powder in my freezer as baking is the only time I need it. It holds up well, even after many months (or even years!).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If anyone has tips, please chime in! I followed this to the letter and the cookies were still raw after 10 total minutes in the oven. Even after another 4 minutes baking and then more resting time, they couldn’t even be moved with a spatula in one piece. They tasted DELICIOUS, I just don’t know what went wrong

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Madeline my cookies were totally flat and not cooked through. I couldn’t even get them off the parchment paper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had the same problem!  And it looks like a few other people did.  They were delicious, but not puffy like the photo, or the video.  @nytcooking do you have any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Madeline is suggest making sure your eggs are at room temperature and beating the heck out of them, since that’s the only ’leavening’ in the recipe. Don’t over mix the dough, and double check that your oven temperature is accurate. Hope this helps!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I skipped the espresso powder because I didn’t want to go to the store to get it. I added a little extra cocoa powder instead. Also, I used chocolate chips instead of chopped chocolate, and they melted with the hot butter just fine. That saved me an extra step, since I didn’t have to chop anything. The cookies turned out better than my favorite brownies— and I love brownies. These were irresistible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them but cut granulated sugar to 1/4 cup as I didn't want them too sweet. They were absolutely delicious! And had the best consistency of a brownie( the top and corners) but in a cookie! Didn't have sea salt flakes so used Himalayan salt crystals that I used with salt mill. It was fine but will get sea salt flakes for next time. This is going to be one recipe I will definitely make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I also add just a few drops--maybe a quarter of a teaspoon-- of almond extract to give the chocolate final dimension.  I do the same for brownies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Super delicious, just really thin and flat

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Will try again (maybe, someday), but these did not work for me. Got a beautiful crackly, shiny top, but they came out too thin. (I did set a timer and beat the eggs and sugars a full 5 minutes) I also baked an 2 extra minutes (total 12 minutes) but I need to cook them an extra 4 minutes, at least. Everyone’s oven is different. I also used silpats instead of parchment paper, so that may have had an impact. Most were just a gooey mess, but I did salvage 5 of them whole, and they’re tasty (really sweet, really chocolaty). They were quick and easy to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are delicious. I need to give them all away. 
I used unsweetened chocolate and they are just the right sweetness IMO.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I never write reviews. These are AMAZING. I followed the recipe step by step and these were a hit. A little sweet but they’re the perfect treat. If you love cookies and brownies this is truly the best of both worlds.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are magic!!! So yummy!! I have a gluten free family member and have made both the original recipe and a GF variation. I found that 113 gms of GF flour works perfectly. I also added a couple minutes to the bake time with GF flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi, curious what brand GF flour you used?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lisa Cobb, I’m looking for good gf flour.  Bob’s Red Mill and Trader Joe’s are so gritty/sandy that cookies are inedible.  I would love some help and advice from any gf bakers out there.  Many thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Marianne and Lisa Cobb: re Gluten Free flour. I finally tried Cup4Cup Multipurpose flour in a batch of vanilla cupcakes and the results were incredible. Not only could no one tell they were GF, but people were raving about them! I've been baking GF for my mother for about 40 years now and this is the best I've tried.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious, intensely chocolate, chewy with crisp edges. I used KA espresso powder and the coffee definitely came through in flavor of the cookie.  My eggs may have just been a little too cold when I started so cookies did not have quite the lift as in pics.  It's a keeper

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you freeze this cookie and bake later?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hey, can you just grind up some ground espresso coffee more finely for the espresso powder? I saw somebody recommended using coffee grounds but I don't know why I couldn't just use regular espresso, more finely ground?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My granddaughters LOVE these cookies.  In their teen age speak, they describe them as "insane"  and "sick".  My grown up friends and I love them.  Very special. This recipe has replaced the chocolate coconut cookies all my friends also loved.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good golly, Miss Molly - These are the best chocolate cookies I’ve ever had and I’m 77 years old. The recipe is perfect… right down to the no baking powder or baking soda and don’t skip the finishing salt. Each bite feels like you have become one with the best chocolate experience ever, especially if you close your eyes. They are * that* good. Don’t change a thing. This one is simply that good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies are easy to make and delicious. I didn’t have Espresso powder so I did a teaspoon of ground decaf coffee beans and that worked great! Also, you can freeze the cookies after baking, if need be, and no harm done.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this recipe for my parents and found the cookie form to be perfect for consuming fudgy brownies. Having this fudgy of a brownie in its normal form feels super difficult to eat (it sits like a brick in your stomach!) but I found this cookie form to allow me to enjoy the decadent fudginess without feeling sick afterwards! 
My father and I also despise adding salt on top of chocolate so sifting some powdered sugar added a nice visual element without ruining the chocolate flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Instead of espresso powder I just added 3 tsp of really concentrated espresso to the chocolate butter mixture! Worked perfectly and prevented me from having to go to the store :) great recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good. Is the salt measurement off? They are so salty. Next time I would reduce the salt by 2/3. And more espresso. The texture is perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was just reading the recipe to make and thought the same. Thanks for your note. I will adjust accordingly!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Michelle it may be the brand of kosher salt you used. A tsp of Morton’s kosher salt is much saltier than a tsp of Diamond Crystal brand and it’s annoying that recipes don’t specify this!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really didn’t like these, they were too sweet, and the texture was cloying. Try Dorie greenspans World peace Cookies instead. Amazing texture!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No need to buy expensive (maybe not-so-fresh) espresso powder, just use your leftover coffee grounds instead! Spread grounds out on a cookie sheet and dry out in oven at 170° for approx. 2.5 hours (or until they feel "crunchy"). Use a coffee grinder to grind into a fine powder and voilà! Keeps for a couple months.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cooking time is off and these are sickly sweet. Cooked 2 mins longer than recommended and they were still raw. Sticking with my bitter flourless chocolate cake recipe from another website when I want dark chocolate decadence.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had the same problem even with extra time in the oven. I wonder if this basically raw brownie batter cookie is what people are responding positively too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: have made this recipe vegan and it was utter perfection! just swapped out the butter and used egg replacer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Quick and easy to make, but too sweet, in my opinion.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I doubled the recipe for giving away as holiday gifts. Found the espresso flavor too predominant after baking. Now I am trying to figure out how to offset it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Deborah MK agree the coffee was way too strong! Made another batch without it and it was divine

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Deborah MK Perhaps a vanilla or caramel icing drizzle on top? Good luck!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these 4x. 1) My batter never spreads out on the pan or flattens in the oven. Whatever it looks like on the sheet, that's how it will look when done. I have to shape each cookie before baking—a pain. 2) My cookies never get that sheen. 3) They don't crackle when I drop the pan. 4) I halve the sugar amounts. I think the main problem is the cook time/temp. I have a thermometer so I'm sure I have that part right. I'll take them out at 6 mins next time and skip the extra 2 at the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sue if the structure of the cookie comes from the sugar whipping air into the eggs, it’s no surprise that halving the sugar causes major structural flaws

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A few tweaks that might help results.
If you have food processor grind together the brown and white sugars then sift them before whipping them together with the eggs.  You want the sugars to be as fine as possible to make it easier for them to dissolve as they mix with the eggs.  
Add the salt to the chocolate mixture instead of to the eggs/sugar.  It will bring out the chocolate flavor and improve the texture of the eggs/sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I left out the espresso powder. I didn’t feel it needed to coffee, flavor, and no substitution was necessary.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I did not make this cookie, but I sent the recipe to a coworker. She brought a bag of them in and I ate them all before 11am… 
A genius recipe.
We were confused about “espresso powder” though. We have access to high quality speciality instant coffee at work so she used this in the suggested amount. The cookies tasted equally of coffee and chocolate. Not sure if that’s what’s meant. Maybe we’ll use lower quality next time — or does it refer to coffee ground for espresso?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: these were delicious but I thought they were a little too salty and I love salt. I used diamond kosher salt and next time I will cut it in half. I did add 2 ounces of finely chopped hazelnuts because I think brownies need nuts. I also found I had to cook it quite a bit longer than the recipe called for. I took the cookies out of the oven after a total of 13 minutes. When they cooled off enough to taste it they were very very soft so I put them back in the oven for another six minutes and I thought they were much better. So as usual trust your instincts not the time called for in a recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It's a good brownie cookie recipe, but it's just a brownie. I did as Simms did and just ground some instant coffee.  I usually have espresso powder on hand but didn't today. 1 tsp. is not that much to make a difference.  I also added 2/3 cup chopped pecans.  What's a cookie or brownie without nuts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add 1/4 ts cayenne pepper.  Sublime.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made my cookies smaller, but I can't imagine that had anything to do with the problem I had, which was getting them to release from the parchment paper....impossible. I let them cool a few minutes in the pan on a rack and when I tried to get them off with a spatula, they stuck and fell apart. I returned them to the oven several times thinking perhaps underbaked for some mysterious reason, but just impossible!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those curious about the peppermint version, here is a link: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022771-peppermint-brownie-cookies When the recipe appeared, I made them without the peppermint--& ended up with the delicious cookies posted here. There's a NYT/ Claiborne cookie recipe that's even simpler: just 2 oz. melted semisweet chocolate stirred into a meringue (1 egg white, 1/4 c sugar). Add vanilla extract, maybe some choc chips. Bake small cookies at 350 on parchment until set.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The taste of the cookies was great (used the bittersweet chocolate option). But I am left wishing that I had not put them back to cook for the additional 2 minutes--maybe try a couple of test samples.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Exquisite, elegant and easy!
Not convinced it is necessary to whack the pan full of mostly cooked cookies. Would love to hear others’ thoughts on that part of the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree about the whacking. I made these four times. The whacking did nothing. And yes, my cookies were practically done. The extra two minutes at the end didn't do much to my batches, except maybe dry them out a bit. I'll skip that next time as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have not made these but I have a similar trick when I make cookies.  I wait till the cookies are just starting to lose the shine on top then take them out of the oven and press the edge of an offset spatula (or butter knife) into the cookies to make crevasses (for want of a better word) then I rotate the pan and return it to the oven to finish baking as usual.   You might try doing that instead of the whack when the cookies still look wet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have Celiacs, so I made these gluten free.  Just wonderful.  Chewy and chocolatey.  I didn't have to change a thing.  It will go in my permanent go to cookie file.  FYI I used Jules Gluten Free flour blend. It's all that I use.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, my oven was correct at 350 degrees per oven thermometer. After the 2 minute return to the oven, the cookies still looked wet. I took them out anyway but couldn't get the cookies off the parchment after cooling them on the sheet for 3 minutes. I put them back into the oven for 3 more minutes, then another 3 minutes when they still didn't look completely dry. Still struggled to get them off the parchment and they didn't seem baked in the center. What am I doing wrong?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In baking part of success is learning to trust your eyes and nose.  If they still looked too wet they probably were.  Also 3 minute cooling time is not enough for cookies to firm up on the sheet.  Wetting a spatula can make it easier to remove stubborn cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too sweet - and perhaps this sounds weird to say about a brownie/cookie, but too chewy....thought they tried to salvage the concept with the salt, which does help, but in the end this is just OK....$$$ cocoa & chocolate that could be used to a tastier end:  Joy of Cooking brownies for ex

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Though the recipe reads the eggs should be at room temp it will probably help if the eggs (still in the shell mind you) are placed in a bowl of very warm water before using them.  Room temp may still be too cold to get the volume needed.   Think of genoise or madeleine recipes and how the eggs are handled for those.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
