commentBody: Outstanding! And neater to eat than regular brownies.  I made a double batch and froze half the batter.  These cookies go fast--just the right balance of chewy, gooey, and crisp.  Warmed up with a dollop of ice cream and caramel sauce is another way to enjoy this great recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add 1/4 tsp, or more, cayenne pepper.  ¡Olé!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies will get you a boyfriend— guaranteed!!!!! Or girlfriend, whatever floats your boat. In all seriousness though, this cookie recipe is truly delicious, probably one of my favorite cookie recipes.  Love the flaky sea salt and crackly edges. Thanks, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I found mine took quite a bit longer to bake to get to a chewy consistency--and I used an oven thermometer. Just a heads up. Excellent flavor!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I always have a pack of the Starbucks Via instant coffee packets on hand, and they are perfect for any recipe calling for instant coffee of any sort.  Plus they stay fresh and last longer since each serving is individually packaged. And it makes tolerable instant coffee if needed in a pinch.  Works great in this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any suggestions for making this vegan?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For the peppermint version, look up peppermint brownie cookies.

These are delicious. Mine came out pretty flat but I didnt make any adjustments for high altitude so maybe less sugar, more flour next time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine also came out super flat ... and I was wondering the same thing about less sugar/more flour!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Kris min did the exact opposite. Barely flattened at all. They came out still more or less scoop shaped. They were done though. Chewy on the inside. 

But I am wondering what I did wrong. Would like to get them flat and cracked

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is the best chocolate cookie I have ever made. Thanks very much. People are impressed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these a few weeks ago while my kids were home from college. They disappeared. I suspect some were hoarded away to take back to campus. I'm going to make a big batch for Christmas sharing and am going to make some with crushed peppermint on top, and maybe crushed unsalted pistachios? Has anyone tried adding nuts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t know what I did (or didn’t do) but these turned out flat with smooth matte tops. They also were dense and a bit grainy. I made exactly as specified with top notch chocolate, but did double the recipe — maybe that contributed, don’t know. Anyway, inedible for me unfortunately.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For others who’ve noted that their cookies spread, I’m wondering if you made the same mistake I did - I accidentally doubled the butter. 1/2 cup is only 1 stick of butter. Most of the baking recipes I follow note sticks of butter or tablespoons of butter. Even though I looked it up, I accidentally converted 1/2 cup of butter to two sticks of butter. They were still delicious with double the butter but they didn’t look great and the texture was off. Once I got the butter right, these are fantastic! If you love brownies, they’re such a great treat. Another note - mine were a little under when only baked for 10 minutes. They’re perfect in my oven after ~12 minutes (still soft and chewy). This will now be my go to cookie recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! Didn’t have espresso powder so I used cinnamon. Also used cup-for-cup gluten-free flour and had no problems whatsoever. Very fudgy/chewy brookies. Will definitely save this recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are *outstanding*!! I had 3 of them for breakfast. (I was aiming for oatmeal, but happened to walk past the cookie jar, so….)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are soul filling. They make me melt! I reduced the white sugar to 1/4 cup (from original 3/4, meaning I took away 1/2cup) and it’s just the right sweetness for me. And the best part is it’s perfect for using up super old bloomed chocolate- really it just gets transformed into the most irresistible cookie. So now you know what to do with your kids’ old Easter bunnies and Santa Clauses :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How about publishing recipes using egg substitutes since eggs are now very expensive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can you make with gluten free flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even with gluten-free flour these were terrific!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I will be investing in a one ounce cookie scoop so that I may emulate Vaughn's smooth execution of the scooping process and have professional looking cookies. The texture is amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I've not made these but reading through the comments I did notice a common theme that the cookies spread too much.  This may be a result 
of over whipping the sugar and eggs.  Try whipping to the ribbon stage instead of until the mixture looks pillowy.
Weigh the ingredients.  Weigh the ingredients.  Did I mention weigh the ingredients?
Be sure you don't remove them too early from the oven after the initial bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tend to agree with Melissa Clark, who says if you are melting butter, you may as well brown it. So I browned the butter and then added about 10g (10ml) of water to make up for the moisture that boiled off while browning. Coffee upsets my stomach, so I used an extra tsp of cocoa. And I used eggs that had been previously frozen (in their shells) and then thawed. The cookies turned out great--exactly as described. I will make them again. I might add chopped walnuts next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazing. The teenagers that showed up after the adult dinner party devoured what was left of them and came to the balcony just to tell me how great they were. I’m DEFINITELY making these again. BTW I omitted the espresso since they were a dinner dessert and served them with vanilla bean ice cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made these and i used truvia brown sugar substitute instead of reg brown sugar in the recipe so i dont feel too guilty for stuffing my face w these and it worked out well. they are soooo good especially right out of the oven, almost like a mini chocolate lava cake from dominos.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It is great, but the baking time is really off. I had to bake for much longer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think this a recipe that requires exacting weight especially for the flour.  I've made it multiple times and we love them but they can be runny. Better to weigh.  And I am usually anything but scientific in my baking. Sometimes it can make a big difference.  I also think that leaving them to cool longer on the pan itself is helpful. They are great frozen too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Really delicious brownie cookies! Somehow even better in the days after.

I only used 50g white and 75g brown sugar, and that was plenty sweet for us. The batter was thinner as a result, so will try refrigerating next time before baking. 

The cookies cracked nicely but then melted smooth after a few minutes back in the oven. Next time will call it quits after cracking and topping with salt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so delicious and chocolatey! They are the best marriage between a cookie and a brownie. I made them exactly as described and we absolutely loved them. The only thing I would do differently next time will be to keep them in the oven for another minute longer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make these smaller than the 2 tbsp suggested! They're too rich to be that big. Also, take them out and bang them sooner, maybe at 5 min. Mine were already set at 8 min. and remained puffy, no crackle. And pipe them! The dough is super messy and sticky. Overall they were big, messy, puffy mounds of very rich dough that would be better if they were smaller, more elegant rounds of dough that actually crinkled.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agreed on size - I do heaping 1 TB portions, and that is perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the bake time too short on these. I followed the recipe exactly and used my scale to measure ingredients, but my cookies were still a little raw after the full 8 and then 2 minutes in the oven. I put them back for another 3 minutes to get closer to the texture I expected. Overall, the cookie is very sweet and I wish it was chewier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Complete fail! Was raw until 30 minutes in oven, then way too crispy.  I tripled the recipe so perhaps the egg sugar mixture didn't get fluffy enough. but it looked it to me. I don't think I'll make again.  More delicious and way less time to make boxed brownies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely HATED this recipe. Pan banging wasn’t successful at all. In one pan, it completely broke one cookie, made a chunk fall off another, while the other two stayed completely solid (zero cragging). My oven is extremely accurate and checked with an infrared thermometer. This recipe is a mess and I will definitely NOT be revisiting this. What a waste of effort and ingredients.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Freeze the cookies, then sandwich them together with some good quality (read: not sickeningly sweet) vanilla or coffee ice cream then freeze again for some absolutely fabulous ice cream sandwiches. (BTW, nothing wrong with this recipe!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you leave out the espresso to make it kid-friendly or that basically ruins the recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have made this recipe a few times before and I haven't even tried making it with Espresso powder. It has still turned out great every time, so you're fine to leave it out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How about calling these "Marry Me" cookies. And by marry me I mean Vaughn. But also the cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would chocolate chips be ok here for the chocolate?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As long as you chop them up small for greater surface area to aid in melting, no reason why not. But good ones without binders.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It depends on the chocolate chips.  For instance, Ghirardelli chocolate chip cookies are the exact same chocolate as their baking bars, just in a different shape.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Honestly it's easier to make a pan of brownies that have a better overall texture. Taste was exactly that of  brownie though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do these freeze well?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazing! My only complaint: a single batch doesn't make more. I ended up with 15 cookies - not nearly enough! I didn't get the crinkles even though I knocked the pan on a table and it was hard to get the salt to stick (maybe try earlier). But still delicious and devourable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I always put the salt on before I bake cookies, it sticks better to the uncooked surface.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Like Jasper294 - mine were flat and not crinkled. I rechecked all my measurements etc but only when I watched the video did I realize I had used the paddle attachment instead of the whisk. The flavor is so scrumptious I can't wait to make them again and do it the right way!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are SO GOOD. Followed the recipe to the letter and the cookies are perfect. Used KA Bendsdorp cocoa and Ghirardelli 60% chips, which resulted in a good sweetness level for us.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine didn’t really puff up and at first I thought they weren’t done all the way, but I did the 8 and 2 min baking interval, so figured they had to be. After sitting for a couple extra minutes, they are soft, fudgy and delicious. I’d make these again, want to try the peppermint version at the holidays. I got 27 cookies out of the recipe as is, which seems more than others.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What about adding chopped walnuts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added quarter teaspoon cayenne pepper yum

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Here is the link to the peppermint version. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022771-peppermint-brownie-cookies

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What does the espresso powder bring to this?  I hate the taste of coffee in any form.  Does leaving out the espresso lose anything?  We'll also leave out the salt topper - - doesn't belong on desserts ever, in my opinion.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Forget the coffee flavor and dash a drop or two of almond extract in addition to the vanilla! That's what I did...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To avoid the worry about burning the butter or the chocolate not fully melting, I microwave them together in a glass bowl—no need to cover—on very low power (3 on my microwave). I start with 1 minute, stir, and microwave again for shorter time periods, stirring in between, until fully melted. Then stir in the espresso powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To @Earth below: the video defaults to CC - close captioning- tap anywhere near bottom to bring up the features and tap on the white CC box to undo. Voila!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Here’s the peppermint version that folks are mentioning: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022771-peppermint-brownie-cookies?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't have a stand mixer. I only have a hand mixer, but typically only use a wooden spoon or whisk. Any reason I can't do this with a whisk, or at most bring out the hand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes that will work.  Use the whisk for getting the eggs and sugar started.  Once they are well blended switch to the hand mixer to get the required volume.  You could use the whisk for both but go ahead and give your hands a rest.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The peppermint version that others have mentioned is a separate recipe that Vaughn created and that he mentions in the video. It is linked here, and it is also searchable on the NYT Cooking website: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022771-peppermint-brownie-cookies

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I’d like to swap
Out the espresso powder for actual fresh espresso. Normally I’d just decrease one of the liquids, but given the only liquid in this is the eggs.. any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Here is the video for the peppermint version: https://youtu.be/BFEgBiFIDu4?si=GgU0r0UkfFoDP_Uk

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you want to skip a few steps, you can use a boxed brownie mix :o  as a starting point!  Just Google "brownie mix cookies" and you'll see several recipes.  I'm sure they're not quite as delicious as these from-scratch, but you'll save time.  I used Ghirardelli Triple Chocolate Chip mix.  Please don't revoke my subscription to NY Times Cooking! :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I reluctantly tried these as I prefer crispy cookies and find that things advertized as chewy are more cakey and never chewy. These are fantastic! I'd love to see the peppermint version but will probably wing it and follow the commenter below and reduce the sugar a bit. (Something I NEVER do because too and sweet should never go in the same sentence).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My go-to brownie recipe is from  Kate the Great, you may know her as Katharine Hepburn.
Thanks, Vaughn.  These cookies are a razor sharp second.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the recipe as directed with the addition of chopped walnuts. Excellent!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought these were good but not exceptional. I followed the recipe to the letter, using Ghirardelli 60% chocolate and cocoa powder, weighing ingredients, etc. I usually like a very dark chocolate, more on the bitter side, and am surprised to find that these could be sweeter. My chocolate loving sons thought they were just “ok” and wanted more sweet. This only made 12 normal sized cookies. One pan is fudgier than the other, so oven location matters. I will try again with a different chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Doni. Totally agree. Needed more sweet for my crew. I didn’t even bring to office as planned

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Take it a step further and brown the butter first. Even better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My partner cut sugar by a third, absolutely delicious! And great texture like a brownie, really!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Unfortunately, these did not turn out for me.  I have double and triple-checked the recipe and I followed it properly.  Yet my cookies were completely flat and very spread out.  My sense is that there isn’t enough flour in them and batter is too liquidy, but it worked for everyone else.  Any theories? I do notice that my regular brownie recipe does have 1/8 tsp baking side in it. Maybe I will try that next time.  Also, like other commenters, I won’t add as much salt next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I completely agree ... mine were exceptionally flat, and so soft as to NOT be brownie-like. If I do again, I will add much more flour and less sugar and see what happens.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Raised eyebrows and rave reviews from everyone at the office when I brought these this week.
Don't overbake, it's between 8-10 min max. 
For an orange-flavoured twist on these, replace the vanilla extract with orange extract, OR keep the vanilla extract but add grated orange zest. Delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had high hopes for these and followed directions carefully. They were quite under-done and mushed together when I tried to take them off the pan. Great flavor, though. Next time I would leave them in longer for the second baking phase.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t like anything coffee—flavored. What could I substitute for the espresso powder, or would it work just as well to simply increase the cocoa powder by that amount?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Chocolate Lover Not a coffee lover either. Like some others have noted, I used a 1/4 tsp each of cinnamon and cayenne pepper to give it a little kick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe very carefully but I found the cookies stuck to the baking sheet and were very difficult to get odd. Any ideas?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm fine with chewy, but these were gooey in the middle, even with extra baking time. Maybe because I weighed everything except I used measuring cups for the flour. Ah well. Going back to brownies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Question about bake time - the initial dashboard says 35 minutes bake time. Seemed much too long for cookies - they'd be charcoal by then.
But the written out recipe says 8 mins, bang, and 2 more minutes.
Deets, please!
MBW

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use Dutch process cocoa and semisweet chocolate to avoid bitterness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am pretty sure I baked them long enough, and they were nicely cracked on top, but I found them more gooey than chewy. And too sweet. I might try again, or just go back to Lidey Heuck's brownie recipe, which I think is great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Controversial question: Has anyone attempted a vegan version? If so, what subs did you use and how did it turn out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: MAJOR PROBLEM with this recipe! It’s almost impossible to stop at eating just one cookie! The first goes well with milk, the second with ice cream…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Turned out too thin and stuck to the sheet

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are the perfect cookie. I used King Arthur gluten free flour and they held up beautifully, no binders or additions needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are far too sweet for my taste. I might make them again, but I would replace half the bittersweet chocolate with unsweetened chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I let the butter melt the chocolate and never heated it up the microwave. It had slivers of unmelted chocolate that melted when they baked. I also used dark chocolate and instant coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Changed out the butter for olive oil, used whole wheat pastry flour, cut white sugar to 1/2 cup and brown sugar to 1/4 cup. Sprinkled with Maldon sea salt. They didn’t spread as much as in the original recipe, but the taste and texture were very good. A small price to pay for a healthier cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Spectacular. I didn’t have espresso powder and didn’t miss it one bit. I made a double batch with multiple pans going, so keeping track of the eight minutes in, whack, two minutes more was a challenge. Overall, I’m sure one or two pans baked as much as six minutes after the whack. Seems the only time that really matters is the first eight, because if left in too long, the crack doesn’t happen as easily. Point is, the timing after the whack is very forgiving. Every batch was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine turned out pretty flat, not sure why.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I ended up with exactly 18 cookies on two separate pans. I followed the exact recipe and found that the cook time was too short. The cookies were way too liquid and needed to return to the oven for more than 4 minutes. This was after the cookies had cooled on the pan and on cooling rack.
 I also think they either need more flour or less butter because they are very greasy. I guess some people like the salt on top, but that has never appealed to me. So I would leave it out next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I also had to cook mine four minutes longer, and I only discovered it after they had sat out for two minutes so I put them back in. Turned out great and they are now the family favorite!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 9/29/2024 - made without the espresso powder.  Came out a bit flat but still delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are maybe the best cookies that I've ever made. I figured these would just be a quick fun treat, but I can already tell you that they're going to become a family favorite. They were so easy to make, and the consistency is absolutely heavenly. It really feels like you're eating fudge or a particularly dense and heavenly brownie. SO good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed exactly. But my they were a gunky messy too wet batter that was very hard to handle and get out of the spoon to drop and did not turn shiny. I think they needed more flour. Anyone else have this messy problem? Almost thru out recipe but husband loved the flavor. Just need help with the dropping on the cookie sheet if they are this sticky glumpy mess.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Scoop fast! As soon as flour is in. Dough sets up Very fast!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lorna Lou - I recommend buying a food scale and weighing the ingredients. It's 100% worth it! The recipe gives the metric weights, so it makes it easy for you. Once I bought a scale, it made all the difference with my recipes consistently turning out right. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love love love these cookies. I just made a double batch for an event and got 65 cookies with my 1T scoop. I chilled the batter for about 15 minutes so they didn’t spread too much and I got a dozen on each half sheet. This time I made them with bread flour for extra chewiness and my 11 y/o said they were the best cookies he’s ever had. I baked them for 10 minutes straight without banging and they still came out nice and dense.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 10/28/24. Made with Aquafaba for my egg-allergic granddaughter.  WW pastry flour. No coffee powder so skipped it.  Only had unsweetened chocolate, so -- though I usually cut sugar in half in most recipes -- I used 1 cup white sugar. No flaky salt so skipped that.  Would be great with walnuts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is stellar. Any tips for adding peanut butter (Jif or natural, how much, when/how to add)? Cuz chocolate and peanut butter is my jam.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Try this.   Blend 3/4 cup confectioners' sugar with 1/3 peanut butter till well mixed and mealy .  Then after you have taken the cookies from the oven to give them a whack spread a dollop of this mixture on the cookie before returning them to the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Leaving out the salt in the mix.  I could taste it and not in a good way.  Used the tiny semi-sweet chips now available in bags, which act like chocolate that’s been chopped up and melt easily.  Thought the 2 T measurement for the dough was too much for each cookie, and in my batch wouldn’t have made the 18 that is noted.  So used healthy 1  T amount, making just right sized cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious!! No extra salt needed to my taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made everything as recipe stated, trust me make two batches, these are that good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine went flat, glommed together on the trays :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these recently.   Love the chewy cookie, but adding the salt on top was a bit too much salt for me. (I like my desserts to taste like a dessert)  Will probably skip that next time.   I'm also planning on taking a few of them and turning them into ice cream sandwiches - a little caramel sauce, some vanilla ice cream and two cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these cookies — subbed the espresso powder with a touch of cayenne — but I’ve made them twice now and no shiny top. I’m thinking of adding and extra egg white to the mix next time (so 2 whole eggs and one additional egg white). Any other thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are easy and adaptable. Oh, and did I forget delicious? I'm thinking of adding a drop of cream cheese to swirl into each cookie, or pistachios, or the peppermints, maybe some crushed hot chili candy. I think this could be a fun recipe to play with.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: yeah these are like unbelievably good. made them exactly as instructed and cannot stop eating them. these will be a frequent bake for sure

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies have the best features of brownies- crunchy edges, chewy center. They're easy to make to boot. Be sure to whip the sugars and eggs really well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
They turned out absolutely perfect. 
- added sugared cranberry on top for the Christmas season - wonderful burst of freshness halfway through the cookie 
- halved the white sugar 
- used bittersweet chocolate 
Perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For the person who said maybe she didn't add enough flour, in baking it is essential to measure flour exactly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: That is a LOT of espresso powder, which made them unpleasant to a non-coffee drinker.  When you add that to 70% dark chocolate, I found them nowhere near sweet enough to be pleasant.  I also found the cookies made by weight too thin to be chewy.  I went back to volume, which at least fixed that problem, and went to semisweet chocolate and 1/4t of espresso powder.  It took me three batches to get them where I wanted taste wise and they are still not better than other recipes I have.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Interesting - I absolutely HATE the slightest taste or smell of coffee, and I couldn't taste any coffee flavor. Maybe it is the brand used? I used Cafe Bustelo espresso instant coffee (after googling that instant espresso powder is what is used in recipes).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is my favorite cookie to bake and I am celiac!  I exchange the flour for a GF flour.  Has never failed me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added cardamom & cinnamon à la Nargisse Benkabbou’s brownie recipe & it’s crazy good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. I needed to cook them for about 25 minutes for them to be done and still gooey. 10 minutes wasn’t nearly enough time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very! Delicious and very ! Rich. No kidding about this also being a brownie. Ate one straight from the oven so can’t really tell yet if it will be chewy or if the banging on the counter did anything. I skipped the sea salt but it’s still pretty salty-sweet actually.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They taste amazing, but I've tried the recipe twice and both times they were totally flat. I followed the recipe exactly. If anyone figures it out, please let me know :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh canoli these were amazing! I added crushed candy to half and salt to the other and I can’t wait to make them again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are brownie pancakes. I’m a fan of both brownies and chocolate, but these did not come out for me - too rich, underdone based on time, and not cookies IMO! This is clearly not most people’s experience, so I do wonder if I did too many chocolate chips (which did not need to be finely chopped to melt btw). I added more because I figured “finely chopped” would yield more as measured.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing! I made them half size because I make so many treats at Christmas and this way you can try more.  Baked 5, banged salted then baked three.  I also added a touch of cinnamon and cayenne. Chef’s Kiss.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing cookies!  I made them half size as I have a lot of treats at Christmas and this way you can try more.  Baked, 5, pounded & added flaky salt, baked 3.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Surprised at the strong positive reviews on this one. WAY too rich, difficult to take more than a bite of two.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are cook time and prep time reversed? Cook time is 10 minutes. Prep time took me a while (1st time baking these). Mine flattened when I whacked them on the countertop after the first 8 minutes. No espresso powder or flaky sea salt on hand, but I expect deliciousness anyway on a very snowy day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy, delicious! Followed the recipe exactly but didn't have espresso powder so used some coffee I made. Turn out great and shared with friends who all loved them! Will be making again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Low-ish effort for a solid 4-star result. I microwaved the butter in a 2C measuring jug then poured the chocolate/cocoa in the jug to melt…needed an extra 20 seconds on 50% power to fully melt the chocolate once I was done mixing the sugar/eggs, but cuts down on dishes and effort. Used small cookie scoop, made 23. Beautiful crackle on top, salt cuts the sweetness nicely.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies last night and brought them to the hospital where my daughter was on call. They loved them. I did modify the recipe by using a 1/2 cup of each sugar and added 1/2 c chopped walnuts to the batter at the end. I used Ghiradelli 60% chips, as well as salted butter, and I found the sweetness to be just right. I do like some chocolate chunks in my cookies, and next time I'll add extra chocolate chips before baking for a little more bite. Cooked at 325 convection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazingly delicious

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I freez the the dough ??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yummy! I didn't have any eggs and didn't want to run to the store to pay a small mortgage on eggs so we used egg substitute.  It was fine, and the taste is great, but they did not rise as thick or shiny as the video. But doable if you need to sub out.  And trust the recipe: when I took them out of the oven the final time, I worried that they were too soft but my husband wisely reminded me they would harden on the pan and they did.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies taste great! But I must have done something wrong. They spread like crazy and I wound up with one big cookie. Had to cut them apart. Whipped the eggs and sugars for 4 minutes and it did look pillowy. Will whip longer next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don't whip longer.  That just adds even more air meaning a greater chance of the cookies collapsing and spreading.  Instead try whipping the eggs and sugars only till they have reached ribbon stage.  Also check your oven temp.   You might also have taken them out too early before returning them to the oven to finish baking.   Take them out when the top of the cookies just start to lose their shine.  Then bang on the counter or make hatches with a offset spatula (I do this for my cookies).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Whipping longer may add in too much air making it more likely the cookies will collapse.  Try just whipping sugar and eggs till the ribbon stage and then proceed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Um...this recipe does not work in Colorado at 5000' elevation.  The taste is terrific but the cookies spread into pancakes.  I'm going to experiment with adding 1/4 teaspoon baking powder and a bit more flour and then putting the balls of cookie dough in the refrigerator or even freezer before baking.  Any high altitude baker with additional suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Great texture. For myself, i found a level teaspoon of Morton's kosher salt was too salty. Didn't bother with flaky salt on top, therefore. Next time, trying with 1/2tsp. 
To those who found this difficult at 5k feet: I am in Denver and mine turned out fine. Possibly your butter-choc mixture was too warm when added to eggs, deflating them?

author: Vaughn Vreeland

Delicious! I used 105 grams rather than 90 grams of flour (I consider 1 c of flour to be 140 grams, not 120 grams as this recipe does, so 3/4 c would weigh 105 grams). Mine baked fully in 8 minutes, though they were also fine after 10 minutes.  I didn't top with extra salt but probably had about the same level of saltiness from using salted butter.

I can't wait to try the peppermint version. No way I'm waiting until Christmas for that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are fantastic. Addictive. I've made them a number of times and learned a few things. The first is not to use eggs that  are too large. 2 oz (50-60 g) eggs work best. Second, don't get lazy about beating the eggs and sugar together.  They need time and vigorous beating to truly emulsify. Third, go ahead and add the flakey salt before you put them in the oven. It adheres better and you don't have to interrupt the baking time. Finally, some chopped nuts are a great addition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For the chocolate measurement:  a 4 oz. bar of chocolate is 113 grams.  3/4 cup would equal 12 ounces?  This seems off.  Any guidance here?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Margaret, 4 oz bar is 113 gr and depending on how finely you chop it, it probably tallies out to +/- 1/2 cup of chopped chocolate. Since the recipe says 3/4 of a cup of chopped chocolate, I’m guessing you’ll use abt 6 oz of chopped chocolate. You are correct. The volumetric and weight amts don’t seem to correspond.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used weight vs volume to measure the flour and the batter was very runny. Instead of adding more flour, I baked 1/2 of them that way. They didn’t crackle (yes I banged the pan) and they turned out flat and soft. After adding at least 1/3 cup flour to the remaining batter, I had better luck, but was still disappointed in the texture after baking. I’d describe it as more cake-like, less chewy than a brownies. The flavor (using good chocolate and reducing sugar by 1/4 cup) was good. 

The remaining batter took an extra 1 up having to add about 1/2 a cup so the batter wasn’t as runny after starting the first 12 cookies. The next 6 with extra flour were better. I also decreased the sugar as suggested by others and was glad I did. The flavor is good but the consistency wasn’t as chewy as I expected.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these yesterday and they were a huge hit!

I love the delicious edges of corner brownies, and each of these cookies had the perfect balance of slightly crisp/chewy edge & fudgy interior.

I used organic decaf instant coffee instead of espresso, but otherwise followed the recipe exactly. 

I noticed that the cups & grams didn’t seem to match up for the cocoa. Grams equaled a scant 1/4 cup. I went with the cup measurement instead & the cookies were perfect. 

This recipe is a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were delicious! 1 tsp of fine coffee grounds made them very coffee-forward, more than expected. Our fault for substituting coffee grounds for espresso powder, but worth being aware of. We hand-mixed the batter since we don’t have a stand mixer, and a few extra minutes of mixing may have made them slightly airier, but the fudgy texture is honestly preferred. Overall amazing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these, and they indeed are tasty!  I made the mistake, possibly, of not letting them puff up enough in the oven (two cookie sheets, rotated them etc). I cooked them as directed, and after they cooled on the cookie sheets for “several minutes,” when I tried to remove them from the parchment with a spatula, they stuck to the parchment.  Before I went further, I realized that if I removed the cookies and the parchment together as one unit, sliding them off the cookie sheet to let them cool after the cooling time on the cookie sheet was over, they would peel off nicely.   And they did!!!  Great recipe.  Not sure if I should have let them cool longer on the cookie sheets before the additional step (?).  But all ok.  Anyone else have this issue? Advice for next time?  Thanks!

I live in the US but I use metric measurement for good reasons.  Your flour weight was shy. Using the common 5 oz/cup of unsifted flour,  the grams weight is 113, not 90.  This could explain it.  Next time....

The video mixing instructions are different than in the printed recipe and the video makes more sense.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Success! I followed the recipe exactly, (except I used a quarter cup less of white sugar, because I always use less sugar when making cookies) and they came out absolutely delicious! And they looked just like the picture, shiny, and with the cracks and everything! Very satisfying. My wife asked me, “Why make brownie cookies instead of just making brownies?” And except for my penchant for making cookies, I wasn’t sure what to reply. What do you all think?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: All those yummy edges of the cookies ... not on brownies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Rose Casa - Honestly, it's fun rapping the pan on the counter to get the crinkly top! You can get some frustration out. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yum, yum, yum. Batch did not last long for family of 3. Like didn't make the next day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Needed longer baking time due to our altitude!  Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i use begian choc chips and they don't melt all the way so i stir them in a bowl of hot water until they are small little chips and they add a wonderful taste treat to the brownie cookies.  this is the best cookies i've ever tasted...everyone i share them with agrees.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I watched the video and Mixing the Chocolates with the Butter is way easier and quicker.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 10/10.  Added a scant 1/4t almond extract, because I like it with chocolate, otherwise followed as written.  Great flavor, came out like the video.  Will try with peppermint soon.  Thanks again. NYT and commenters!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies were too chocolaty and underdone. I wanted a good chocolate cookie, not fudge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They are exactly what you think you’ll get in a brownie cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so incredibly delicious. They have a great texture but I found that they’re even chewier if stored in the refrigerator.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are insane!!!! They definitely have the intense chocolate flavor and chewy texture of a brownie but not as dense as an actual brownie.  This will be my new go to!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written and didn't think they were anything special. I chopped them up and mixed them into malted vanilla ice cream, which was good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just wondering if anyone else has made using the metric volumes? I’ve made this recipe 3 times and each time the batter is so runny I end up pouring into cupcake liners and baking for extra in the oven. The result is delicious, crackly top, although no chewy edge or base. I’m using Australian plain flour and 800g/dozen eggs. I’ve tried letting the batter stand to hydrate the flour but then I forfeit the crackly brownie top…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I brought these to a party recently. Someone who has worked in classy restaurants for the last decade told me they’re the best thing he’s ever tasted. So good and so easy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great deapth of flavor. Not one left!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone add walnut pieces?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am a cookie disaster. I can cook/bake almost anything but perfect cookies elude me. That all changes today. These are perfect! Such a wonderful texture, flavor, size … very easy to make. My kids (18 & 14) are brownie snobs and these got the stamp of approval. Warmed up and served over a scoop of vanilla ice cream would be terrific.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good over ice cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious and a crowd favorite! I added toasted pecans. These didn't even last a day!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone who has made this cookie think you could coat the dough in non-pareil sprinkles before baking? I understand that this could interfere with the development of the shiny, crinkled top; that's fine. Just want to know if the texture of the dough at that stage is workable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you like the crispy parts of the brownie you will love these! They are crispy on the outside and then soft and fudgy on the inside. 10/10 will make again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Very good cookie. I added a couple extra tablespoons flour as the batter was a bit runny. I then put them in a piping bag and piped out blobs about the size of a 50 cent pieces. These are smaller than the recipe called for
 Baked for about 8 to 10 mins all told. I think these works be amazing topped with crushed peppermints.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No baking soda??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Whipping the eggs enough usually gives height to certain baked goods such as brownies or sponge cake or pound cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic cookies, really nice depth of chocolate flavor and chewy just like brownies. It is important to whisk the eggs   sugars together until the sugar dissolves to get that crackly effect. I add a bit of coffee extract and chopped chocolate to the batter. Second batch, I added a dash of peppermint oil and chopped Andes chocolates. YUM!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A personal favorite. Chef's kiss!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe's simplicity belies its gorgeous decadent taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I only had chocolate chips on hand, but these were still amazing and didn't spread everywhere.  I did a batch with peppermint patties crumbled on top -- also good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be frozen after baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone substituted the egg for flax? How did that change/vary the cooking temp and time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There was a point where my 7 year old was trying to get the chocolate mixture in the whipped eggs and sugar and he flung a decent amount of chocolate across the kitchen. This slowed the whole timing down, which i think impacted the cook, so they weren’t quite glossy. I can’t imagine them being any more chocolatey despite losing some to the floor, counter, etc. They really held up! Batter is exceptional for spatula licking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe! I found that the espresso powder really came through as a flavor. If you want that, great! When I see coffee or espresso powder added to chocolate recipes, I usually assume it’s there to enhance the chocolate flavor, not to be easily identifiable on its own. If that’s all you want, maybe half the amount written in the recipe? Otherwise, absolutely loved these.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious cookies!! Two small notes: instead of espresso powder, I brewed a fresh shot of espresso and added two tablespoons to the chocolate mixture. For a more coffee-forward taste you can add the full shot. 

Also I like melty chocolate in my brownies and cookies so I folded in a handful of semisweet chips as the final step before baking. Crackly, chocolately and chewy!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I often see people mention videos. Like one for this recipe. Where do you access the videos?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are fantastic and super easy to make,  I made them with the sea salt and they are chocolate goodness. Great with a nice cup of coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!!  I didn’t have a 1 ounce measure, so I had to guess, and I ended up with 28 cookies.  As they were smaller, they cooked a little faster, and I took them out to whack them at 5 minutes.  Didn’t make any difference; they were spectacular.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was considering 1/2 regular flour and 1/2 almond flour.  Has anyone tried that?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are rich and fudge-y, much denser than any brownie I've ever had. You can hardly finish one cookie. Maybe because I only got 16 cookies instead of 18..? I did use the two tablespoon scoop as directed... Either way, these would be great for a true chocolate lover.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these into “hot cocoa” sandwich cookies by adding a layer of marshmallow cream cheese frosting—they were 10/10 would HIGHLY recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious! I needed a great cookie and only have a hand mixer - this was it!  I couldn't find espresso powder (I also decided to bake these 11 am Christmas Eve...) I popped open an Expresso Nespresso pod and pulled a teaspoon out.  Cookies were DIVINE!  
Only question is - how to best store them?  I put them in an airtight tuperware, hope they'll stay awesome until tomorrow...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am dreaming about these cookies. Brought a batch to Christmas and managed to snag one before they disappeared. Plan to make more as soon as I return home so that I can eat them all myself. Fudgy, rich, mouth-watering!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Reduced sugars both to 100gm. Used Lindt 70% and Nescafé espresso powder. Exactly 8min plus 2min. Not a second more. 

Perfectly dark and chewy! For ice cream sandwich, I used Ben and Jerry’s Half Baked and it was delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was worried that my rock hard brown sugar was going to ruin this recipe but it did not. I mixed the butter and sugars an extra few minutes and even though the brown sugar was still a little visible the end product was smooth, chewy and amazing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A delicious and chewy cookie -- very brownie-like. I will make again, but I will add nuts!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There’s not a better cookie in the world.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing!!! Chewy like a brownie, slight crunch like a cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are phenomenal! I reduced the amount of sugar by 30g or so, still amazing. I’ve tried many a chocolate cookie recipe, this one tops them all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were perfection

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely love these cookies - the best of both worlds, chewy brownie, slightly crispy cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My adult kids said love these with the combo of espresso and chocolate!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made these without the espresso? Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just put regular espresso (coffee) grounds in the mortar and grind them until very fine. It takes about 30 seconds. The cookies still taste fantastic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: At least 1x a week I have the internal struggle of deciding if I should make cookies or brownies. Never again. I will make this cookie recipe every time. It is the perfect combination. Love!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious! Made them almost every week during the holidays. I added some peppermint extract, melted chocolate on top, and crushed candy cane for a festive twist. But they are good as written. I love these cookies so much!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These worked out  exactly as described in the recipe and video. Irresistibly delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe without the espresso powder?  I absolutely do not like coffee or anything coffee flavored.  Even if it's the tiniest amount, I will taste it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: baked smaller cookies - approx 80 gr or small baking scoop.  reduce baking time to 6-7 for first round.  made the mistake of using paddle blade on mixer rather than whisk, so cookies were a bit flat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just wondering if anyone has ever added nuts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these cookies, I used instant coffee that I dissolved in the vanilla. I added salt to the tops of the dough before putting them in the oven. I kept them in the oven for the full 10 minutes Without taking them out after 8 min. Salting, then put them back in for two min.I slammed them twice when I took them out. I also did the recipe version, to compare and I like the way mine came out better with these changes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What is the temp we need to cook them at?? 160C or maybe 170C??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a combination of bittersweet (60%) and extra bittersweet (70%) chocolate because it was what I had on hand, and found it was just the right amount of sweet. I will definitely make again, but probably not with semisweet chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these in Denver, followed instructions to a T and they're flat. Not sure if there's an adjustment for high altitude?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sounds great. However, a quick search of "brownies" on this site shows that just about all the recipes take the same 45 minutes listed here.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe mixing by hand with a whisk?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you replace all purpose flour with almond flour ?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Stellar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I have made this several times,  It never fails.  A question about the brown sugar.  You call for Dark.  I use "light" brown sugar because that is what is available in the big box stores.  Still makes a great cookie.  Should I add a little Grandma's Molasses to compensate for the use of light brown sugar?

The peppermint version took a fantastic cookie to a new level.  OMG

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It should not be necessary to add molasses.  It might even alter the texture.  Instead increase the vanilla extract by another 1/2 tsp which will punch up the chocolate flavor and add some richness.  Just my opinion.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I only had bittersweet chocolate, and they aren't nearly sweet enough for me, so I second the comment about using semisweet next time.  If you really like bittersweet, though, this is for you.  Hubs likes them.  I had a slightly smaller scoop (1.5 T size).  I baked them the same time stated here for a larger scoop, and I wouldn't want them any less cooked than they are now.  In fact, I feel they could have used another minute or two in addition to the time stated after the counter wacking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Could any baking soda or baking powder be used to puff these up a bit?

These are the only cookies I have ever made (so far). They taste ridiculously good, but I would like a little more rise.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Super tasty. They are far fancier than average cookie. Eggs and sugar mix really works.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these following instructions, but whipped batter 6 min (per video of the peppermint version) instead of the instructed 3-5 and these spread a lot. Turned out large and thin :/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made as per recipe except no espresso powder on hand. Came out just as described -- best chocolate cookie ever with a brownie texture. Served with a generous scoop of vanilla ice cream and a few raspberries in each bowl. Perfection -- and enjoyment all around.

I was a little surprised by the lack of baking powder and soda and they were on the flatter side but loved them anyway.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So delicious, and very pretty! I used milk chocolate chips (that's all I had) and reduced the sugar a little bit, and added white chocolate chips. They turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I reduced the sugars by about 1/3 and they were plenty sweet. So soft and chewy, these would make great sandwich cookies or ice cream sandwiches.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Doubled espresso, reduced sugar by about 1/8 c, and added solid as chips instead of melting them. Result is pleasingly bitter and coffee-forward, with sweet little melty bits! Great texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used unsweetened chocolate and decaf instant coffee and these are delicious

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hello I would like to know how long in room temperature can keep them after cooking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy and fun to make. Ultimately it was a brownie though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these, and they are easy and delicious! I put in 1tsp of instant nescafé, but think I would put in less or reduce it next time, I find it a bit too noticeable. I also think a touch of acidity would be a nice constrast, maybe some barberries?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Someone may have asked this question already in the comments, but can I simply use chocolate chips instead of finely chopping a bar of chocolate? Is there something different about how chips vs. bar chocolate melts in step 2?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are unbelievably delicious.  Highly recommended.  One suggestion for the less equipped cook:  If you don't have a cute little scooper to put your cookies on the baking sheet, you are going to want to figure out how to get them on the sheet in some semblance of a blob that will settle into a round cookie.  I used a 1 ounce measuring cup with a narrow teflon spatula.  I ended up with chocolate everywhere and licked most of it up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where are the eggs????!!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Linda Gollober read the recipe- 2 eggs are listed in the ingredients.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: obviously a perfect cookie on its own and i've made them a number of times - but after making vaughn's triple (quadruple?) chocolate brownies, i couldn't resist adding some chopped chocolate - mix of milk and white - to the batter.  they are in the freezer now -

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious. You have to wait til the batter cools slightly or else it is a runny mess when you put it onto the sheets. I left them in a couple minutes longer to crisp the outside, and used bread flour because it was all I had, but I think it added a little extra chewiness that I appreciated.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are yummy (if messy to make!), but the banging on the counter the get the cracks doesn’t work for me - I keep trying, but nope.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. But mine came out VERY flat. Not sure what happened. I did use mini semisweet chips since that’s what I had on hand. Any thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: batter too runny for batter to keep shape.  Mounded batter as best I could but they spread out and look more like pancakes.   taste great but a runny mess.  what happened?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Struggled with this recipe that I want to love!  After having some very gooey cookies emerge from my oven, I reviewed the video.  It appears to make these bake properly, the 1 ounce balls need to be flattened some.  I rebaked the cookies to get them to a place that they were edible but they certainly did not come out as expected.  I will try again because it is the baking that is off not the recipe itself.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are a hit every time I make them. I followed the directions without any modifications and they are perfect. There is a peppermint version as well, but even for people who like peppermint, they still prefer the chocolate version best.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a number 30 scoop (2-tablespoon/1-ounce, heaping) as the recipe indicated and it produced 7 cookies.  I added a minute to the baking in the end to compensate.  They look great but next time I'll use a smaller scoop.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I incorrectly stated the number of cookies I was able to make with the scoop indicated.  It was 13 (not 7).  Just a bigger cookie and equally as delicious but next time I'll either use a smaller scoop or not make the 1 ounce one heaping.  Thant might do it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good and Easy, won't say they are the best cookies I ever had but they were good. I would stick to the smaller size when making them, or add time if you go with a slightly bigger cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Everything about this cookie is perfect. It is truly unbeatable. Thank you for sharing this recipe, I will definitely be making it again.  This is a winner!!! 10/10

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Despite being a very experienced baker, I'm sorry to say I was very disappointed in the results.  I followed the recipe very carefully, but the batter was much thinner than as described in the recipe.  I expected a deep chocolate flavor (I used Lindt 70% chocolate and Valrhona cocoa) but found the cookies bland.  I will say that one tray was a bit overbaked and was less chewy, but even so, I'm at a loss for why these rate so high(ly).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the instructions to a T but still had runny dough and flat cookies. I subbed in cacao powder, instant coffee and broken-up Hershey's bars for the cocoa powder, espresso powder and bittersweet chocolate. The cookies are glossy, crackled and delicious, albeit a little sweet for me- I'm sure using bittersweet chocolate next time will be just the fix. As to the flatness, I'll try chilling the dough for a bit next time to get it to thicken up and see what happens. I'm in Denver and baked for 2 min longer than indicated. I'll definitely make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would flax "eggs" work in lieu of regular eggs here (for a vegan cookie)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Without a doubt the best cookies I’ve ever had. Bakery ones included. SUPERB!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any recommendations for making these kid-friendly (no espresso)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are truly great! Made them yesterday to rave reviews. Even without the espresso powder (no added caffeine to allow everyone to enjoy them), they were fabulous. Baked or not (left plenty for me otherwise clean out the bowl), they are a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’d add less coffee the next time I make these and possibly more vanilla.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
It is the best chocolaty cookie, a brownie easy to hold! 
I used superfine sugar because I only have a hand mixer. 
I skipped the Maldon salt, low salt requirements for me, still great taste of course. 
It’s just an excellent cookie, easy hand held brownie! 
I will try the peppermint ones at Xmas for sure. 
I only have a hand mixer, so used superfine sugar which works well in all my recipes that need a good whisking in of the sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Trivial issue, but instructions should specify a small, heatproof, microwave safe bowl, since they say to microwave if necessary.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious.  Used Guittard 70% chocolate and decreased white sugar by about 25%.  Kept brown sugar the same.  Egg-sugar mixture turned out great using hand mixer with whisk attachment - looked just like his in the video.  Decreased flour a tad to make up for slightly less wet ingredients.  Cookies were perfect after baking as written.  Flaky salt on top very good.  Will try the crushed candy canes topping next time.  As other people have commented - the batter is SO GOOD.  Not much to clean up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too runny when first mixed. Next time I will leave the batter to rest for a while before scooping onto baking sheet. Also, cookie sheets are better for this recipe as they are thicker.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Made in Oz Definitely let the dough rest at least 15 minutes. I made the first batch with dough that did poured off the ice cream scoop that I used. Because of this the 2 trays contained just 12 cookies since I didn’t want them to spread together. The final 6 came out much puffier and looked like clumps when plopped onto the cookie tray.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried making a double batch? One batch is not enough for a hungry teenage baseball team, but don’t want to do the process twice if I don’t have to.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Marion did you find that doubling it you still got the glossy tops? My single batch was amazing but I would rather double it up!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi! I tried this recipe and the cookies were just completely unwilling to cook (and I have a brand new oven, so it can’t be that). I’m a novice baker, so I’m sure the problem is me and not the recipe. The sugar wasn’t super well dissolved in my batter so I think maybe I didn’t whip it right or I didn’t let the butter bubble long enough. Any advice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe and the video exactly. Unfortunately the cookie didn’t crack at the 8 minute bake. I got more cake like cookies not gooey brownie like.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @D You can omit the espresso powder and they'll still be delicious :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
One of the Best cookie I ate hands down !!
And so easy to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are chefs kiss! I omitted espresso powder. These cookies are fudgy, moist, and perfect brownie- cookie fusion. Will make them again and you should too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What did I do wrong if I couldn’t get the crackle ?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you add chopped pecans into the batter before baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made these twice now and absolutely love them. They disappear from my kitchen so quickly! Quick question that I've probably missed elsewhere in the comments - Can you freeze the dough? I want to prep them before I am out of town for a holiday party once I am back.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nice taste, but texture not at all chewy; cookies were much too soft, almost like fudge,  hardly a “chewy” cookie.Big disappointment. Followed recipe exactly, so won’t make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these yesterday and was disappointed they didn’t crackle after repeated tries.  I baked them for closer to 12 minutes.  Ditched them in the freezer and just had one.  Awesome!  Chewy, brownie-like and delicious!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used finely ground coffee instead of espresso. I had to cook them longer - about 10 minutes longer than called for and my oven is very accurate. The cookies are delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very versatile and easy recipe and so delicious! I added a chocolate covered espresso bean on top and made them a bit smaller.  It is one of the best I have ever made, and have been baking for 45 years!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you make the dough, shape them and freeze, unbaked? Or chill dough before scooping and baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jess, from Vaughn’s notes, you will lose the “brownie sheen/crackle” by not baking immediately. But aside from that, I would think you could portion and freeze then bake a few when the craving hits. I do that with most cookie recipes because nothing beats a freshly baked cookie! And no way could (should) I eat a whole batch! 🤣

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these for a build your own ice cream sandwich bar for an upcoming holiday party. We tested it out tonight with peppermint ice cream, we will also have coffee and vanilla,  and they were absolutely delicious, 10,000 stars!  I made them small, using a tablespoon scoop for both the cookies and the ice cream. Truly excellent and easy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Agree with Debbie from a year ago! Here it is Christmas Eve Day and I thought I was done with my cookie baking - but I’d made nothing chocolate! Happened upon this recipe - thanks Vaughn! - and oh my goodness!
So easy, fudgy and delicious. I had everything on hand, including candy canes to crush to add on top. As seriously excellent cookie. But heed the instruction about the cookie scoop if you have one - I don’t, and they came out a little uneven. Still awesome, but I’ll be getting one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @more whip I think I made the same mistake, but I'm trying to figure out what to do differently. What happened to yours? The texture of mine seemed all wrong.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When you say espresso powder, do you mean ground coffee beans with which you'd make espresso, or instant, powdered espresso? I watched the video for clarification, but it still isn't clear.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You'd use instant powdered espresso, not ground coffee beans, although regular instant coffee powder (or granules) would work just as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good. I had a little trouble at the end of mixing - the flour caked up in the whisk and I had to bang it to get it out. I might do that part by hand next time. Also, it could have used another minute or two of baking time to firm up the edges a little more.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were excellent. Mine came out flatter than I had hoped, but we're incredible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow. Chewy, crispy edges. Intense chocolate flavor. The salt is the chef’s kiss.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had no crackle top on mine. Not sure where I went wrong with the recipe. They also came out doughy like cake. They still tasted great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used decaf coffee and it good enough. Next time I’ll make the granules smaller.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were not fudgy or sweet and they didn’t off up.  I used bittersweet chocolate.  If I make again I would recommend using semisweet chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy recipe, simple steps, quick to make, and gives consistent results. If you had a bad outcome, it’s most likely something went wrong during the process.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies turned out very flat and under cooked kind of gooey. I use Dutch process cocoa. Do you think that has bearing? It doesn’t say what type to use.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Trish, 
It sounds to me like your oven may run cool if the cookies were underbaked at the specified time. It’s very unlikely that the cocoa powder is to blame. The only other explanation is that you may have used too much chopped chocolate, which would account for the gooey texture you encountered. Get an accurate oven thermometer to check if the oven is actually at the intended temperature. I speak from experience. My oven runs at least 25 degrees cooler than the temp on the dial, so I have to either bake for longer or up the temp I bake at to compensate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
"This chicken fries in a large, deep Dutch oven — and it fries mostly covered, which helps reduce any mess."

I'm curious if any of the commenters followed the instructions to cover the dutch oven.

And covered with what? It's my experience that if you use the lid you'd better have a burn suit on when you take the cover off. The heat give of steam, condensates on the lid and drops water into hot oil and we know what they say about water and oil. 

Might want to specify for newbie cooks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I put the lid on, it made the pot hotter.  I used a deep/tall Dutch oven pot and didn't have any trouble taking the lid off.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: At frying temperatures, it's too hot for condensate to form.  Water that's released during frying in a covered dutch oven stays in steam form.  When you lift the lid, the steam billows out, of course, but you won't have condensate dripping from the lid into the oil.  If you did it would cause quite a mess.  At the much lower temperatures (of, say, cooking rice) condensate will form under the lid.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My very, very lazy take… marinate skin/boneless chicken pieces in Ranch dressing (I use Bob’s), then roll in Italian panko crumbs, and bake on a rack/pan at 350 for 30-45 minutes depending on thickness of chicken. commentBody: I made this for my husband’s birthday, and it turned out beautifully.  I made it exactly as written, and didn’t think it was too sweet. author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I think there is a small error in Step 9. The instruction is “When ready to assemble, invert one cake layer onto a platter, flat side up.”  However, the instruction for when the cakes come out of the oven is to cool them, “then invert the layers onto a wire rack”. That means the flat side of the cake is ALREADY up.

I think the Step 9 instruction should read “When ready to assemble, PLACE one cake layer onto a platter, SO THAT THE flat side IS up.”

Of course, it’s 4am, I might be totally wrong.

commentBody: I made this for 4th of July and it is amazing.  I did cut the sugar down to 2 1/4 cups and it was perfect.  Everything else was exactly as the recipe, I used golden raisins and my son who despise raisins loved it.  it's a lot of prep but comes together easy. My family all loved it.

commentBody:
Some modifications that worked so well:
Amaro instead of rum, soaked chocolate covered raisins
skipped the nuts but would have been a nice crunch
Incredibly sweet (but good) so would cut the sugar amount 
Mine was I think 2-3 apples and 1 carrot, and no pineapple

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone know how this recipe could be converted from a layer cake to a sheet pan? In particular, the effect on bake time, temperature and quantity adjustments, if any? It would make a wonderful bar for an event I have coming up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you're considering using a 9"x13"x2" baking pan, the quantity of ingredients would remain the same. If you're considering using a 9"x13"x1" so-called "quarter sheet" pan, I'd reduce the quantity of ingredients by 50%. If you're considering using a 18"x13"x1" so-called "half sheet" pan, I'd increase the quantity of ingredients by 50%. Oven temperature would remain the same in all cases. Bake time for the shallower pans would probably be less. I'd start checking for doneness after 25 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this as cupcakes. it made 27 cupcakes but my tins are a little bit small. took 22 minutes to cook them. I was really disappointed that I could not taste the pineapple in any way whatsoever. for the frosting I reduced the sugar by half as so many others suggested it could have been a little sweeter but it was fine the way it is. you don't need this many reasons if you make cupcakes. although this recipe was good and the frosting was good it wasn't outstanding so I didn't give it a five. yeah yeah yeah I know I didn't follow the directions exactly but the votes to reduce the sugar were very consistent and I am happy I did it that way.  I'm not sure what to do to get more pineapple flavor.

It was moist but fluffy and not too sweet. I made this cake for a dairy free friend, so I used vegan butter and vegan cream cheese. The lemon zest brought it home. Candied ginger on top. Everyone who ate a slice loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a very interesting and rich recipe! The presenter's name was Vaughn? I think his cooking presentation was structured and acted to convey a dated and rather tacky rendition of a gay man cooking a marvelous dish he invented. Why? As a gay man in his 60s, I just don't get why?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Daniel, you should check out Vaughn's NYT cooking videos on YouTube - he has a ton of them. This is who he is - this video was no more "structured and acted" than any of his other work for NYT - and no more so than any of the other NYT cooking presenters. I'm a straight man in his late 70s and I just took him at face value - you may be a little sensitive. BTW, I agree with your assessment of the recipe - I just made it and it's good carrot cake.

commentBody: Made it w/o pineapple, cut the sugar by 1/3 as many suggested.  I would do both of those again.  I made 3 8" cakes, and it looked great! While we enjoyed the rum flavor, there are too many raisins for my taste between the layers.  I would halve the amount of raisins used between the cake layers (but I liked the amount of raisins in the cake layers themselves)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any substitute for nut allergy, or can you just omit the walnuts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for a dinner party and had leftovers for 4 others - the response was unanimous: superb. The sponges were moist and no issues getting them out the pans. The frosting surprised me most of all. I am one of the few who don't like cream cheese frosting... until this. That the frosting turned out great is more incredible because I only had 96 grams of the 298 grams of powdered sugar I needed and it was too late to run to the store. Luckily, I had caster sugar that I ground into powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This cake is really excellent.  It took me almost 3 hours to put together, as opposed to the '15 minutes for prep' that is written.  I attribute the difference to my rarely making cakes, and also not having a sous-chef or an elf presenting me with the next ingredient.   15 minutes is really impossible and I do wish the prep times would be more realistic.

That said, I know that if I ever make it again it will go faster because I can anticipate better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as is and it came out perfectly! Only thing I changed was using 8oz cream cheese instead of 16oz and the frosting was still great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I need to add to my original comment that when making cupcakes and using the full cake recipe, a half recipe of frosting is almost enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Friends tell me this is the best thing I have ever baked for them.  Takes a while but is worth it.

I do cut the powdered sugar in the frosting a bit.  I go by taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Holiday cookie baking pals, please note the 3 teaspoons of cinnamon in the recipe are *divided* - 2 teaspoons for the cookie dough, and 1 teaspoon for the cinnamon sugar rolling mixture. I’m not sure why the NYT Cooking house style doesn’t always indicate dividing within their lists of ingredients, but I wouldn’t want anyone feeling Scrooge-y over cinnamon confusion!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jess Bee I have to admit I made the mistake the first time, putting the 3 tsp in the batter, and they came out so great that I always do that - 3 tsp in the batter and a 4th for rolling in!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@diane
I just did the same thing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jess Bee I have made this mistake more times than I care to admit with NYT recipes. Thank you for mentioning this potential pratfall!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Better assembly approach: portion out the just-mixed dough and form it around the frozen marshmallows. Bake directly from the freezer, adding 1-2 minutes to the baking time. Easier, simpler, & better shaped cookie. 

As others said, the cold dough is frustrating to work. It cracks, and if you don't seal the cracks over the marshmallows, the cookie unravels and becomes a blob shape (instead of roughly circular) and the marshmallows end up on top as they bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm trying this tonight--dough is in freezer, and freezing them would also allow you to bake just a few at a time.  I assembled with the room-temperature dough and frozen marshmallows, though they thawed while I was assembling.  I'm hoping baking them from frozen will keep the marshmallows from "blobbing."  Recipe made 28 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sasha I put 4 marshmallows on my palm, lightly pressed the dough ball on top, then flipped it to make the ball. This prevents warm dough build-up on your palm.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sasha. Thanks for ideas about shaping the dough. I was frustrated with the dough, and disappointed with the appearance of the final product. But enjoyed their flavor. You’ve inspired me to give them another try.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I divided the room-temperature dough into 35-gram round portions, flattened them to fill with the room temperature marshmallows, then chilled them in the fridge, before rolling them in the cinnamon sugar and putting them into the oven. They came out looking identical to the recipe photo. I just didn’t want to frustrate myself with attempting to wrap cold dough around quick thawing mini marshmallows. I’m loving all of these unique cookie recipes that add some variety to the usual cookie lineup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Melanie thanks for the tip! I did this and it worked perfectly, they came out just as pictured

author: Vaughn Vreeland
