commentBody: @Melanie I did this as well (made dough, assembled cookie balls at room temperature, popped in fridge overnight then made according to recipe the next day) and cookies turned out perfect. Thanks for the tip!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great tip! I also under-mixed the dough just a teensy bit (30 seconds) which helped to avoid overworking the dough when forming the balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I prepped the whole cookie - with room temp  dough, and room temp marshmallows, and then froze overnight.   They were easy to assemble.  Pulled them out first thing in AM, left them out for about 15 rolled in sugar and baked.   They turned out beautifully.

I decided to go for 1/4 tsp cayenne since I was giving them to a family with small children.   The first bite - I was “not too much heat”, second bite I realized it was there.  I was happy with the 1/4 tsp, it was just enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @branchop I hadn’t read the recipe ingredients before coming to read the comments. When I read that you added Cayenne pepper because there would be children I was thinking , well this is one way to keep the kids from eating all the cookies LOL

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @branchop followed your instructions and they were perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made this recipe twice now and the dough always gets too hard when I put it in the fridge. Then it just gets crumbly and breaks apart when I try to put the marshmellows in. Tried a third time, skipped the fridge step and voila.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies will keep about 10 minutes unguarded.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Denis
Bravo!  The best tip of all!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Super delicious, but way to spicy for my weak children! Next time, I'd do it with half (or less) of the pepper. Otherwise, a huge hit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What do you think about leaving out the marshmallows?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jocelyn might add a little more sugar to the dough if not using marshmallows. The cookies would be delicious without marshmallows, crisp on the outside and soft on the inside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fairly fiddly to make. Frozen marshmallows thaw way before tray of cookies are filled. I'd try standard-sized marshmallows cut in half next time instead of minis; would be easier to insert. Flatten dough balls before refrigerating and only take out 2-3 at a time. The heat of your hand will allow them to form a cup to deposit marshmallows (5 was 1 too many to envelop). I'd also mix in a little sea salt into cinnamon sugar before rolling and stick in fridge for 20 mins before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I accidentally added all the cinnamon into the recipe.  They still tasted delicious! I appreciate a recipe where it still is fabulous even if you read the recipe wrong. Haha!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i did the same! Would have appreciated a note separate for the cinnamon + sugar dusting prior to baking

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are outstanding. I agree with an earlier poster.  Assemble the cookies at room temp and then freeze. When I made a couple using the method in the recipe everything warmed up to quickly, so had to pop into the freezer for an hour or so.  The other thing discovered is to not roll the entire ball in the cinnamon sugar.  The cinnamon sugar on the bottom caramelized, which made it difficult to off the baking sheet even with using a silicone.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as directed minus cayenne. Cinnamon adds nice enough kick. Used Vegan frozen mini marshmallows from Whole Foods. Cookies look like picture and taste great. I portioned dough into scoops night before, refrigerated and took out 6 at a time. Smushed flat with palm of hand. Pressed 4 marshmallows in middle and then rounded into ball with the MMLows inside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m sorry but the chef had so much star quality that I hardly paid attention to the recipe. They were both delicious, though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree--this is the first time I've seen a video with Vaughn and I kept thinking, this guy is really cute--and I'm straight!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would it be possible to freeze globs of marshmallow fluff instead of using marshmallows in this recipe? I'm vegetarian and veg marshmallows are hard to come by/expensive. Fluffernutter is vegetarian!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When does the other half of the dough come out of the fridge?  Not mentioned in the recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made a version of this cookie for decades. I would take out the mini marshmallows and sub in dark chocolate chips.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: While these don't scream "traditional Christmas cookie," their uniqueness makes them a perfect for a seasonal cookie swap. This is a flavorful, appealing cookie. I doubled the recipe, cut the cayenne quantity in half, and jammed in as many mini marshmallows as possible (four was the limit per cookie). Consider sprinkling on sea salt. The longer these are baked, the thinner and crisper they become. We found the thicker, softer cookies to be preferable. Best served straight out of the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Swap the cayenne for New Mexico Red Chile- it is delicious! Also, cut down the brown sugar to 1 cup- could also be 3/4 instead.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I ended up using room temp everything; 35g per cookie with 4 mini marshmallows, freezing the balls, rolled in cinnamon and sugar, and then baking right away from frozen for 10 minutes (take out as soon as white peeks through or marshmallow disappears), rotating halfway. The trick is to NOT wrap too perfectly and have the wrapped part facing up to give the cookies a chance to “blossom” and the white to show. Or they will just be brown. Used 1/2 of the cayenne (perfect kick) and 280g sugar. Yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does this recipe work with vegan marshmallows instead? thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @re: veggie cookie swap I used vegan mini marshmallows cuz it was all my local store had and they turned out perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Kennedy For future bakers’ reference, I used Dandies marshmallows (vegan tapioca starch based marshmallow) and they turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does this work with vegan marshmallows? That's all I have right now.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They worked just fine for me! They baked up well and poked through the dough, and they stayed gooey after cooling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent recipe with the perfect amount of cayenne. These were a smash hit with all my friends and coworkers and will keep this on my regular rotation. Also, the trick with freezing the marshmallows worked wonderfully. I only had the really large ones, but was able to cut them up and freeze them with no issue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you are a NYT reader but leaning heavily on type b personality (there has to be at least 2 of us), perhaps you added all of the cinnamon at once (it’s December… we’re just skimming recipes at this point).  Perhaps you have children in the month of December and rolling out dough and stuffing it with bs is about zero percent what you have energy for.  I’m here to help.  Go ahead and keep that dough.   Now, find some really old white chocolate chips in the back of your pantry or, let’s say you have a large bin of baking ingredients that you rummage through on occasion so it might be on the top of the bin by the cyclical movement of rummaging but it’s for sure old and possibly expired, go ahead and toss all of it in the dough.  All of it.  Give that a good kneading because stirring is too hard now.  Bake as directed.  Dare I say it’s even better than gross marshmallows.  You’re welcome.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 1/4 tsp cayenne and they are still really spicy. Next time I’d only do a pinch. Not sure my mom can eat these…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The different prep works.  I had large marshmallows which I cut in half (two smaller circles) and froze.  Made the dough (do not refrigerate), made 1 Tbsp scoops, then assembled a flattened scoop, marshmallow, another flattened scoop.  Roll around in your hands to make a sealed ball of dough slightly larger than a golf ball.  Put in freezer for a few hours, take out, roll in sugar cinnamon mixture and bake for 10 minutes. I watched the last minute and pulled them out as the tops cracked. Yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I too put all cinnamon into the dough and liked it. Skipped the sugar cinnamon coating. 
Assembly: instead of balls, rolled dough on top of parchment into 2 matching rectangular 1/8” sheets; placed marshmallows on top of one sheet and 2nd sheet on top of marshmallows. Tightly wrapped “sandwich” w/ parchment, then aluminum foil around that, then placed in freezer for 2 hrs.
Removed  from freezer to bake, first thawing 5-10 mins. Move sandwich to cutting board, cut into squares w/ pizza cutter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We’re not marshmallow fans but love chocolate, cinnamon,spice. Is the chemistry of the recipe altered if I leave them out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious! My coworkers raved about them. More so than other things past bakes I’ve brought into the office. I took the advice of others and cut the cayenne in half, which made for the perfect spice level in my low-tolerance opinion. It’s there, but subtle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Love to read, but never leave a comment. This recipe has prompted me to change that. 

I was a bit nervous about making these since it seemed unlikely that'd I'd successfully be able to chill, flatten, and then wrap marshmallows in dough. If you watch the video, follow the recipe almost exactly, and have a bit of patience, this is achievable! Added a bit more cocoa powder since what I had was expired but other than that, followed the recipe as written. Cookies are even better the next day!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are the perfect holiday cookie! My husband and I are not great with spicy so we halved the cayenne but left the rest of the recipe as is. These smell like pure bliss while baking and we are so impressed with the flavor, they really are hot chocolate! Yay cookie week! Keep ‘em coming Vaughn.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made these and then freeze them for use later in cookie trays. Wondering if marshmallow does ok freezing and defrosting after being baked. Also best if I freeze cookies or I eat them all! Happy Baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes. I baked the cookie balls from frozen, fully formed with the marshmallows inside. I even coasted them with cinnamon sugar before freezing and didn't notice any problems.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not gonna lie, the cookies are meh but the video makes it worth the time I spent on them! I agree with others who say to assemble the cookies at room temperature, then freeze them for 2-3 hours before baking. You must, must, must undercook these cookies by a minute (or three), or else you'll have hard little pucks that taste burnt even if they're not, thanks to the cocoa and spice. If you pull them out of the oven riiight as white marshmallow is starting to bubble out of the top, you've got it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these cookies 3 times in one week and have some tips (they are delicious): after you leave the cookies in the fridge for 2+ hours, let them warm up for half an hour and they will be easily pliable and very chocolatey. If the marshmallows have been in the freezer for too long (24 hours +), they will melt into the cookies. ~4 hours +/- is ideal amount of time to freeze the marshmallows. Be careful with the cayenne pepper - mine was too potent!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those you like a less sweet, more dark chocolate cookie, I used 2/3 of the called for amount of sugar in one batch and 1/2 in the second batch. Both turned out lovely. You don’t need so much sugar if you are putting the marshmallows in the middle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Put marshmallows in right after making dough, roll into balls and coat. Freeze for 10 mins and bake 10 mins straight from freezer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughan Vreeland is my new binge!  I woke up early Thanksgiving morning and watched Mexican Cookie video and my laughter rang out from my bedroom to the rest of the house. I immediately sent off copies of the YouTube edition to some close friends and the day was happier all over the country!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used King Arthur gluten free flour successfully, 1/4 t cayenne and 33 gram balls of dough. Flattened them in non-dominant hand and wrapped around 1/2 large frozen marshmallow to make a ball. Freeze on a sheet pan. Place frozen balls into a freezer container and return to freezer. When ready to bake remove from freezer roll in cinnamon sugar combo and bake. Nine to a cookie sheet on parchment bc they spread and can be sticky when marshmallow oozes out. Check for doneness at 10 min. THIS IS THE BEST COOKIE RECIPE EVER AND IM NOT EVEN A FAN OF CHOCOLATE!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After making (and loving) the recipe as written, I made a delicious alternative. I added peppermint extract and took out the spices and vanilla. Then I used peppermint marshmallows. Now they're peppermint hot chocolate cookies and they are absolutely delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have now made these cookies three times. The last time was by far the best. As many people have noted, chilling the cookie dough first is a mistake. Don’t bother freezing the marshmallows. After making the dough mold it around the marshmallows and chill the “dumplings.” Use all the cinnamon in the dough and don’t bother with rolling them in cinnamon and sugar. Make larger cookies. I use a regular ice cream scoop. They are fantastic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: these taste wonderful, i didn't put in all of the cayenne - a nod to my less "heat tolerant" friends and family. They seem pretty flat, but my measurements may have been off. They're really fun to watch cook, and would probably be lovely with some peppermint chips as well! i'll be making them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone try to makes these as a cookie bar (in a pan like a brownie)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have a hack to avoid the tricky assembly, cut down on prep time and still get a perfect cookie. I used large marshmallows cut in half as commenters suggested, but instead of putting them inside the cookie dough, I placed them on top of the cookies mid-way through baking when I rotated the cookie sheets. Just make sure you use room-temp marshmallows and place them cut side down. The marshmallows were ooey gooey and the cookies still retained a great structure and crumb. I also added a teaspoon of espresso powder to the dough to intensify the chocolate flavour. My take was a hit at my cookie exchange and I’ll definitely make them this way again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Moira brilliant! And what a great visual effect to have the marshmallow poking out just like a mug of hot cocoa.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These were so delicious and the flavors came together perfectly. I made the first batch as directed with freezing the marshmallows, chilling the dough, then assembling and baking from basically room temp. They turned out good, but the marshmallows kind of melted into the cookie batter and didn’t have the visual or texture I hoped for. I also over baked them because the gooey marshmallow gave the illusion of an underbaked cookie. 

Second round, I followed the commentors’ suggestions of assembling the cookies with marshmallows, then freezing the prepped cookie balls, rolling in cinnamon sugar, and baking from frozen about 12 minutes. Night and day results! I’m really thrilled with how they turned out and will be adding this to the holiday cookie rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fold the marshmallows in before chilling the dough, chill the balls, take out of the fridge 5 min before baking and then roll in the sugar/cinnamon before baking. i baked ~15 minutes. they turned out perfectly. i did half of the cayenne based on the comments and you still get a tiny spice kick. also vegan marshmallows work totally fine here! used full sized marshmallows that were cut into 9 pieces each then rolled in powdered sugar then frozen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made 20 gram balls and put 3-4 marshmallows in each

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have now made these quite a few times with several variations. 1/4 tsp of cayenne seems perfect. King Arthur Measure For Measure gluten free flour subs in perfectly. I've found vegan marshmallows don't melt even without freezing but are a little less gooey. I do refrigerate the dough balls to "hydrate" per the video instructions, but use half vegan and half frozen regular marshmallows, four mini mallows total. I put the former dough balls in the fridge for ten minutes before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is one of my new favorite holiday recipes. I was out of eggs so I subbed 1/4 cup plain Greek yogurt. I’d like to try them again with the egg just to see the difference - I thought mine were delicious though! So I’d say this sub works. I liked the strong cayenne though it’s divisive. I also enjoyed the process to make them. I like baking so the extra steps just give me more time in the flow state. If I do them again, I think I’d form them into balls with the marshmallows in them right away and then freeze them overnight. And bake for 9.5 minutes tops… I think keeping the marshmallows gooey is a science that when mastered correctly, yields a fantastic dozen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Use 1/3 tsp cayenne 
Form dough around 2-3 mini marshmallows immediately after making dough (make dough balls small)
Then freeze for 2-24 hours
Then put cinnamon sugar on only the very top of the dough ball
Bake frozen for 12-14 minutes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve used ancho powder instead of cayenne a couple times now and think the result is better and more similar to Mexican hot chocolate. Even using twice the amount of ancho is still nowhere near as overpowering.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Note that Morton kosher salt is much saltier than Diamond Crystal, so if you have Morton, only use 5/8 of what recipe calls for. I didn’t do this the first time and they were too salty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did not love these! Feels controversial to say, but I've had much better holiday cookies. The marshmallows have a weird texture once they cooled. My guests complained of them getting stuck in their teeth. The dough also feels like a mistake? Like theres not enough liquid in it. Mine was crumbly at room temp, even though I went by weight and triple checked the ingredients to make sure nothing was missing. Wont make again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just finished the gingerbread blondies, now on to this! Marshmallows in the freezer. Thanks

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Halved the cayenne and there was still a kick at the end. I’m sure vegan marshmallows would work but don’t skip the freezing step. Dough is crumbly coming out of the freezer but you get the hang of it. Shaping the cookies when they came out of the oven was a must as they spread quite a bit, but shaping was easy. Next time will test adding sea salt to cinnamon and sugar mixture as someone else suggested. Great recipe and so fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these as directed, but without the marshmallows and they were delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A bit of a FAIL for me ... and a SAVE.  I neglected to read the comments beforehand, so I found the dough pretty unworkable cold or warm ... too messy.  After making 8 so-so cookies, I pressed half the dough in a springform pan, loaded the marshmallows on top, and then pressed the other half of the dough on top.  Baked about 20 minutes and it's WONDERFUL!  Next time I'd let some of the marshmallows show through, but that's it.  Friends loved the bit of heat and great chocolate-y taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I opted for 1 and 1/4 cups of flour instead of 1.5 cups, and 1.25 sticks of butter instead of 1 so the dough wasn't as crumbly. The cayenne spice really brings out the chocolate flavor, these cookies are simply fantastic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Am I losing my mind? Followed the recipe to the T. Used a two TBS scoop. Yielded 15 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I have a couple notes about this recipe… 
1 - If your family doesn’t like spicy, I’d recommend leaving out the cayenne. It was WAY too spicy for us, but some of my neighbors thought they were delicious. 
2 - I rolled up the frozen marshmallows in the dough and then refrigerated, covered for 24 hours. I read a lot of the comments before I started and thought this way seemed easiest - it worked like a dream. The dough would have been way too stiff to do it the other way around. 
3 - Do. Not. Over. Bake. The first batch was beautiful - just like the pictures. However, I left the second batch in just a touch too long and the marshmallow just melted away. 
4 - I only rolled in sugar before baking - not a cinnamon/sugar mixture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made these last night with Namaste Gluten Free flour and Dandee's vegetarian mini marshmallows.Otherwise followed recipe exactly. They turned out perfectly. Delicious! No changes required for high altitude. (6300).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Having read so many helpful comments, I broke my own rule and made modifications before baking them for the first time, and was happy with the results. Made room temperature 25 gr. dough balls using 3 frozen mini marshmallows, froze the balls overnight and baked for 14 min. straight from the freezer. The dough handles very easily, as Sasha commented, the cookies are plenty large enough, and a heaping 1/4 tsp of cayenne suited our taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had the same issue of the cookie batter being dry and flaky. Not putting the cookie dough in the fridge is the trick. If you learned this mistake a little too late, like I did. Take the dough and place it in a lined 9"x13". press it firmly into the pan. Bake for 10 minutes at 375 degrees. After 10 minutes, pull the cookie out and put the frozen marshmallow's on top and bake for another 5 minutes. Let cool and to add a finishing touch. Torch the marshmallow's a bit. Hope this helps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great cookies! I made 20 of them from one batch. I added espresso powder to the dry ingredients. Love it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were a hit at holiday parties! I skipped the marshmallows; they made the cookies spread unevenly and look misshapen. Without em, they make excellent chocolate crinkle cookie and are super pretty. Chill the dough, covered, to allow everything to hydrate. Scoop and freeze—bake from frozen and add 2-3 min baking time. And measure the cayenne with your heart.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This dough is crumby and dry. Worse when cold. Nice flavor after baked but too crumby to be worth giving. I know cocoa has a drying factor so maybe the recipe is missing some liquid to compensate. Mine is already mixed - so what can I do to fix the dough - add a second egg?  PS. There are far better chocolate cookie recipes out there. Next time I will use one of those and experiment with cinnamon and marshmallows. Thanks team.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the suggestions from the other bakers, thank you. I skipped the cayenne because I don’t have any in my pantry for a reason. I liked the addition of the cinnamon sugar but the marshmallows didn’t add anything for me. I might try the recipe without them next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe, but I wish the ingredient list would have indicated that the 3 teaspoons of cinnamon was divided! I was moving quickly and didn’t focus on the steps and now my cookies are very cinnamon-forward. Be warned!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Instead of marshmallows, cut caramels (Krafts of Werthers) into halves and roll the dough around 1/2 caramel per cookie. The caramel melts into the dough. These cookies are delicious and moist.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were fantastic. Not a fan of marshmallows (thus didn't have any) so coarsely chopped some bittersweet chocolate and added that instead. (Also was a little short on cocoa powder so added a couple grams of quality hot chocolate mix. ) About 8 1/2 minutes in my oven. A huge hit (and a forgiving recipe) !

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. I do think I’ll add some espresso powder and chocolate chips to increase the chocolate flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mel, I added a pinch of espresso powder. Added some warmth and enhanced the chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just won my office Holiday Cookie Baking Championship with this cookie (against some pretty steep competition)! 5 stars, will bake again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My new annual Christmas cookie. I made the dough and immediately rolled the balls with the marshmallows while everything was still room temperature. Then I froze for 20 minutes, thawed 5 minutes, rolled in cinnamon sugar and baked. They turned out just like the picture. I also missed the part about dividing the cinnamon so I put all the cinnamon in the dough and rolled it in extra cinnamon in the sugar. Turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Thank you to all for the helpful notes and suggestions!  Found many helpful tips and excited to try this recipe.  

One suggestion I offer - Trader Joe's Mini Peppermint Marshmallows in place of standard one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out just as promised but tasted exactly like baking soda to me. It’s true that I’m sensitive to the taste of baking soda but I realized after the fact that Dutch process cocoa is not right choice for a recipe that uses baking soda — it’s pH neutral and baking soda needs acidity. I definitely won’t make this recipe again, but these did get eaten.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I gave up. I followed the instructions…then I followed all the wonderful suggestions…none of it worked. This was so dry it kept falling apart. I was forced to throw it all out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I clearly didn’t learn from the recipe and the other  comments about dividing cinnamon. I misread and added 3 tsp cinnamon to the dough instead of 2, but it actually tasted delicious with that much cinnamon so I think I would continue with the extra cinnamon moving forward. On the recommendation of other reviewers, I decreased cayenne to 1/4 tsp, which was the perfect amount of heat. 

I refrigerated the dough overnight, which, as noted by others, made for a very crumbly dough so assembly was difficult and time consuming. The frozen marshmallows were mostly thawed by the time I made my dough balls. I ended up chilling the dough for a while after assembling the cookies so moving forward, I think I will skip freezing the marshmallows and just chill after assembly.  

Overall, time intensive but an insanely delicious cookie. Will definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Appreciated all of the comments here. I opted to weigh each one and got 20 cookies at 35g each. The dough was sticky, but I formed the balls right away with 4 non-frozen mini marshmallows per cookie. They all went into the fridge for a few hours. Brought them out on the counter and waited 10 minutes or so before rolling in the cinnamon sugar. 10 cookies per sheet pan and baked about 12 minutes. Very good cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I really loved these cookies when they were fresh. One thing to consider if you're freezing the dough for more than a few hours is you may want to cut some of the cayenne. I had to make the dough, freeze for a week, bake, then freeze some of the baked cookies. Those came out much spicier after sitting in the freezer. 

I also baked some with Earth Balance in place of butter and it worked well. The dough was a little softer, but the cookies came out the same as the regular butter cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these today. Came out just like the picture! Delicious!

Following comments about the dough getting crumbly, I realized the best thing to do was take my time and put the dough back in the fridge before baking. Worked perfectly. It took a bit longer than the instructions to beat in the chocolate/flour mix. Be patient.

BTW, at 5 minutes into cooking, it didn’t look like the dough would spread at all. I was all set to be disappointed but the next time I checked the oven, perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Please define "mound of dough."

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I’ve made these cookies twice and find the mini marshmallows, which I froze, to be chewy after cooking. I was expecting them to be soft and fluffy. Is there a brand of marshmallows you recommend?

Also, I added a touch more cayenne the second time. I like the pepper kick.

Overall, they are really delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really had a hard time with these. The dough was difficult to work with, hard to get to pick up the cinnamon sugar, and my cookies didn’t spread or flatten nor did the marshmallow really soften in the time it took the dough to cook, which made it tough to tell when they were done. The flavor is lovely but I was ultimately disappointed. Maybe my oven temp is off….

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi Alex, I had a similar experience - the cookies and marshmallows didn't spread or flatten. I think my oven temp is pretty accurate. I suspect that discrepancies between recipe weights and volumes were the culprit. How did you measure ingredients? I weighed them. 35 gram dough portions yielded only 15 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious!!!  I followed others' suggestions, and made and rolled up everything at room temp., put in freezer for a couple of hours. While preheating oven, I took them out of the freezer, rolled them in the cinnamon sugar mixture, and baked for a couple of minutes longer and they turned out wonderful, exactly like the picture!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is the best cookie of all time. 
Also assembled the dough and marshmallows at room temp and then froze for 2 hours. Baked for 15 minutes… perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Way too complicated. Since all I had were dried up little marshmallow rocks (soft marshmallows are nonexistent in the high desert),  I skipped the refrigeration and freezing and just constructed the cookies Chinese dumpling style: I made a little bowl with the dough, inserted a few marshmallow rocks, wrapped the dough around them, and pinched off any excess dough. I’d wet my hands to lessen the sticking so it probably imparted a little moisture to the marshmallows. This method was quicker and simpler, and the cookies turned out great! Plus I was finally able to use up those dried up marshmallows!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I need to try this process next time--I've now made them twice with the refrigeration step, and by the time I'm done shaping them and taking them out of the fridge, the cookies are so dry that some of them immediately flop open and apart in the oven!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delightful, but I'd appreciate info on why Dutch-processed cocoa is preferred. As I understand it, you need the acidity of regular cocoa (i.e., NOT dutch-processed) when you bake with baking soda.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these as cookie bars / brownies in an 8 x 8 pan.  I buttered the pan and then used the cinnamon sugar mixture on top of the butter.  I also sprinkled cinnamon sugar on top.  I baked the batter, without marshmallows for 12 minutes, then added the frozen marshmallows and baked another 15-20 minutes more.  The bar / brownies need to cool completely before cutting.  They were lovely — both in texture and taste!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Took advice and rolled the just-made dough around the already frozen marshmallows and then froze.  I halved the amount of cayenne called for and substituted gf flour instead of the real deal.    Skipped rolling them in cinnamon sugar because I forgot.  These things were the best thing I ate over the holidays by far. I hid in the kitchen and shame-ate them and would do it again in a heartbeat.  Will make them every year.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so amazing and the video is helpful. The spices are perfect. I only wish the chocolate was slightly more forward and richer but I'm not sure what could be change to help that. I had no trouble with the cold dough because as was suggested, I let it warm up a little. A really wonderful cookie recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Annie P try adding some espresso powder to the dough to boost the chocolate flavor

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was thinking that they would be delicious without the marshmallows. Has anyone tried leaving them out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was thinking the same...hope someone will try it and report back.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've failed in the past when making regular cookie recipes with all-purpose gluten-free flour. Any tips for making a gf version?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve had good luck using King Arthur GF Measure for Measure flour

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I omitted the cayenne and the cinnamon in the dough to make them more kid-friendly. I did roll them in cinnamon sugar. My dough was a little crumbly, and putting the marshmallows in took a bit of time, but the finished product is delicious! Some of my cookies even look as pretty as the picture. :) Crispy on the outside and soft on the inside, as promised. A keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were so delicious! I was a little shy about the cayenne and added more of a scant 1/2 teaspoon but I think the spices ended up being perfect and go so well with the chocolate. The texture is great too, being a little crispy on the outside and chewy on the inside. My only complaint is that following the weight measurements, the dough was a little dry before baking, which caused it to crack a bunch trying to fit the marshmallows in, but it worked out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I were to do these again (big if), I would freeze half regular-sized marshmallows rather than minis. I found 5 was one too many. It is easier to flatten the dough balls before refrigerating, then when forming cookies, letting the heat of your hand help mold it into a “cup” shape. Followed the directions to remove half the dough at a time from fridge, next time I’d take out  only 3-4 at a time. The marshmallows thaw very quickly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you freeze for later, is that WITH the marshmallows inside?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes. I baked the cookie balls from frozen, fully formed with the marshmallows inside. I even coasted them with cinnamon sugar before freezing and didn't notice any problems.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are absolutely a mug of hot cocoa in a cookie! Spot on texture and baking time. It’s a little shy of the Chocolat experience spice-wise, so I’d add more cayenne for sure. I did add a little pasilla negro chili powder also and used dark brown sugar rather than light for richer taste. The only disappointment was that even frozen, the marshmallows mostly melted. Contemplating doing the thumbprint technique next time and adding them on top just towards the end of baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm a big fan of Vaughn's cookies each year so making these was a must! I ended up with about 16 cookies. Definitely a little tickle of spice at the end, but just perfect in my opinion. I could see someone with a wimpy spice tolerance finding more than 1-2 a little much. I did fall victim to not catching 3 teaspoons of cinnamon divided but didn't seem to be too cinnamon-y. Another Vaughn hit in my opinion!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does it work to freeze these cookies to enjoy later? Or would that mess up the marshmallow?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Forgot to roll them in cinnamon sugar before baking, they came out great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use large marshmallows cut in 1/2 and then frozen - sprinkled with flaky salt out of the oven they turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great cookie, perfect amount of spice! For those asking about vegan marshmallows, I used Trader Joe's and they did not melt/spread as much as I expect regular marshmallows would. I did some frozen some unfrozen and the results were the same. Still delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 1/2 of a Hammond’s Peppermint Marshmallow instead of the minis and they came out so yummy. They taste great with some sprinkles on the marshmallow bit that pokes through. At high altitude (5600 ft) they only take 6 min in 400 degrees and then 5 min on the cookie sheet to be the perfect consistency.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree on cutting back cayenne. But these sure are great. I found it impossible to fit 4-5 marshmallows into the dough, so reduce to 2-3. Perfect at 6 minute each side. A keeper for certain friends!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone frozen these successfully after baking? Or would the marshmallow get hard and not soften once thawed?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Cookies are great!  Agree that 5 marshmallows were too many for my cookies. I made 26 cookies and used 3 marshmallows per cookie.  On the spiciness, I thought could use a touch more cayenne,  We’re a spicy family but maybe my cayenne wasn’t so fresh?  
Unfortunately, the dog ate 6 cookies off a hot cookie sheet before I could stop her, so I’m off to bake the rest of this batch.  [Dog permanently banned from kitchen]

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A few things: Agree that less cayenne is better, a full 1/2 teaspoon is overpowering. I found that warming up the dough with my hands before adding the marshmallows worked okay. Too warm, or too cold, the dough tended to crack. I thought the added sugar was unnecessary-just added graininess and was too sweet. I’d use less 1/3 sugar overall but that’s personal preference. I find almost all dessert recipes too sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used vegan marshmallows because that was the only thing I could find at Whole Foods. They worked but I would be curious to compare and contrast with regular marshmallows. The cookies were a huge hit at my office with everyone trying to guess the spices. I loved the taste and texture and look forward to Vaughn's next cookie creation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can the recipe be increased without adjustments to leavening? If I'm going to make one batch why not make three at once? One batch would never make it out the door as gifts. Would friends really appreciate a crumb filled cookie tin? Maybe if I included the recipe on a card.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would these work with margarine, for a friend who can't eat dairy?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dena, I’m vegan and would use high priced non-dairy butter rather than margarine, which doesn’t taste as good. If you do this, you should be fine. Lots of the good brands offer theirs in sticks as well as tubs. Hope this helps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thanks so much, I made these cookies for a birthday party and they were a massive hit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those wondering if you can make these ahead and freeze them. I tested today and they kinda work. They are absolutely delicious but most of the marshmallows dissolved into the middle. I left on counter for about 10 min and then baked at 350 for 8 min and 325 for 8 more.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Used a 2T scoop and made 19.67 cookie balls, each easily fit around 5 mini Jet-Puffed marshmallows then froze overnight. Resulting cookie is tasty but the marshmallows were totally lost when baking batches of 10 cookies for 15min. 
The shape, heat, taste, texture of the cookie are right but I wish it featured molten marshmallow as Vaughn demonstrates. Next time I will try with more marshmallows, sub 1t almond extract for 1 of 2t vanilla, and add espresso powder to deepen the chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Still advocate doing the assembling and cinnamon sugar rolling fresh, then freezing overnight. Key to the marshmallows not dissolving is to keep an eye on the bake and take out as soon as the cookies puff and marshmallows start oozing through, maybe more like 13min from frozen. Will set as they cool.
Almond was a decent sub but does more to hide the cayenne. I'll stick with the vanilla.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We didn't have marshmallows on hand but did have white chocolate chips which worked great and we did not chill the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent! Can they be frozen after baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My six year old says “these are spicy! These are delicious!” That about sums it up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t know what all the rave is about.  I’m sorry but for all the effort and time these cookies take there is just nothing remarkable about them. They certainly look different and the Cayenne pepper brings out some heat, but other than that I did not find them to be worthy of a repeat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very frustrating dough to work with. I watched the video, but, following the recipe as written, the dough is very crumbly even when given time to hydrate. It yielded 15 2 Tbsp cookies and that was barely enough dough to cover 5 mini marshmallows for each cookie. I love the concept but think there’s something off with the dry/wet ratio here.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Two notes: 1. Definitely take these out of the oven on time when the dough's puffed and marshmallow is just peeking through; with overcooking the marshmallow thins and becomes clear. 2. I found it easier to embed the mini marshmallows by taking each dough scoop in the palm of one hand and with the other hand making little pinch-pots with raised edges rather than flattening slightly the dough scoop.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I am not a chocolate lover, but my wife wanted me to make these.
I made several batches to figure out what went wrong with the first.
1. Do not over refrigerate the dough. It dries out rapidly.
2. Watch the video for how to form the balls. It is tricky to get them right.
3. Mini Marsh don't freeze well and return to room temp in about 1min. So don't bother to freeze, they still work.
4. I did not use cinnamon in the coating too much. I used demaira sugar and finishing salt.
Perfect on trial #3

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of the most incredible treats I’ve ever had. As other comments say: you can do all the steps, including rolling them in cinnamon sugar, and freeze them before baking. I did 1/4 tsp cayenne and found they were perfectly spiced but not too overpowering.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Our cookies stayed domed and our marshmallows didn’t really melt together. Advice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I read all the notes in advance, and still had trouble getting the marshmallows and dough work right. 

In the end, I refrigerated the dough for about an hour or so, then scooped out small balls and rolled them in the cinnamon and sugar. I popped them in the oven for about nine minutes, then took them out and put four or five marshmallows on top, put them back in the oven for about three or four more minutes. They look pretty, taste great and much less fussy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be made without the marshmallows? In all my years living in Mexico, I've never seen a white marshmallow. They are usually pink and green.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are they flavored? In any case, these would still be tasty without the marshmallow. If you're willing, you can also make meringue/marshmallow and use it as a topping/frosting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these using home-made vanilla bean marshmallows and they are great.  I cut the marshmallows into cubes about 1/2" to 3/4" and used 40 grams of dough.  I doubled the recipe and got 30 large cookies.   I think the dough should be refrigerated for flavor reasons, but it had to come to back room temperature to be workable.  It was easy to work it around a frozen cube of marshmallow, probably easier than individual minis. Then, following comments here, I froze the balls,  and baked them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is a little more finicky than I’m used to. I didn’t refrigerate the dough as I saw someone recommend but the dough was pretty dry to work with, and didn’t seal around the marshmallows cleanly, even with fewer marshmallows. It may be more helpful to measure the ingredients with a kitchen scale. They tasted great and spicy though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree wi5 the suggestion to make the cake balls, with marshmallows inside, while dough is soft. I froze for about an hour, the rolled in cinnamon sugar and baked for 12 minutes. Worked well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These cookies are amazing but the recipe seems to add unnecessary steps. Here’s what I did and they came out just like the picture:

Make dough according to recipe. Weigh out 35 g dough, flatten and stuff with 4-5 room temperature marshmallows. Roll in cinnamon sugar and place on a parchment lined sheet tray. Repeat for remaining dough. Freeze the sheet tray of dough balls for at least two hours. To bake, let dough stand for 5 min at room temp and bake for 13-15 min at 350.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thank you to Jess Bee for pointing out that the cinnamon should be divided. Her note and the fact that the recipe calls for 3t of cinnamon rather than 1T should have been sufficient red flags to not put all 3 teaspoons (1tablespoon!) of cinnamon in the dough. However… the brain is not always engaged, so I put in all 3. Decided to counteract with 1T espresso powder. Amazing depth of flavor. Sure the original recipe was great, but try it with the espresso!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are great cookies,yes a little fiddly, but worth it.Second batch I let them sit out until more pliable (like the recipe suggested but I was too impatient for). Then flattened them all at once BEFORE taking the marshmallows out of the freezer. Big difference and yes, you can get all five minis in. For the first impatient batch, I plopped a few marshmallows on top and put in a warm oven briefly - Looks like the top of a cup of hot chocolate. Cayenne was perfect but depends on your cayenne.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Omg. Addictive. 
Read the notes and wrapped unchilled dough around unfrozen mini mallows.  Then refrigerated a few hours/overnight before baking.  Baked 15 minutes right out of the fridge.  Delicious!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Please indicate that the cinnamon is divided so we don't dump all 3T into the dough!  Other than that, I made these as the recipe is written and watched the video for guidance.   No way would 4 or 5 mini-marshmallows fit in one cookie.  I use two.  Then I made the mistake of reading the ingredients on the marshmallow bag - they are basically little corn syrup balls!  Something I take measures to avoid.  Too late.  But the worst part is that they just taste weird.  Too salty and unbalanced.  Yuck

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I haven't been able to find Dutch-processed cocoa at any of the stores near me - can anyone confirm that regular cocoa works? Thanks in advance

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @slyon i have the same question

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a huge hit! I reduced the cayenne to 1/8 tsp and there was still a nice sneaky heat to the cookies without being overwhelmingly spicy. Watch the cookies closely at the end of cooking. The marshmallows go from pretty white details to sticky clear mess very quickly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I veganized this recipe and it makes a really excellent cookie. 
Substitute “Just Egg” instead of the chicken egg, Earth Balance vegan buttery sticks (stick is better for baking than tub) & Dandies vegan mini vegan marshmallows

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Marshmallows definitely need to be frozen just before baking otherwise simply melt to sugar. Not bad if they are peeking out. Couldn’t see any mallows in mine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Adding 2 eggs instead of 1 makes this dough more pliable and easy to work with as described in the recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The marshmallows completely dissolved/disappeared/melted and I was left with Mexican chocolate cookies sans marshmallows. The cookies themselves taste fine, but the appeal for me was the marshmallows and they vanished. The dough was also hard to work with and keeping it wrapped around the marshmallows was tricky. I doubt I would make these again since there are other spiced chocolate cookies out there.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Try cooking them for a shorter period of time.   The minute the marshmallows show it could be time to remove from the oven.   Also I wrapped the marshmallows in the dough before I chilled it and that worked for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Couldn't find mini marshmallows at the store, so I bought whole ones and cut them in half! Cookies still turned out just as pictured, and I imagine it was easier to wrap the dough around one solid piece of marshmallow rather than five squirmy little ones

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these last night for our Christmas party. Prepped scoops the day before and had guests get into cookie making mode! Everyone had a glass of wine and competed to see if they could get 5 marshmallows into a ball. Baked them 12 minutes and all declared them heavenly (even the 8 year old who yelled chocolaty and spicy real yummy! Fun video made me want to give them a swirl :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I read the warning comment beforehand about the cinnamon but still made the mistake and added all 3 teaspoons into the cookie. I also cut the cayenne in half as I have kids that do not prefer spicy cookies. Still turned out great. I will try to make them correctly next year!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For any vegetarians wondering, I made these with Marshmallow Fluff and they turned out great. Followed the advice of others to skip the chilling step, and I also didn't chill the Fluff. Going to send these in a care package to my vegetarian girlfriend :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe Canadian marshmallows are different, but they didn't seem frozen at all after hours in the freezer and they all dissolved into the cookies. Pretty disappointing result for the amount of work. I might try this again after an overnight freeze of the assembled cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just did a version where I left out the cayenne (my 84-year old mom doesn’t do well with even that little heat). Instead I candied the peel from a large  orange, then cut the pieces into 1/2”-1/4” strips and mixed it in with the finished batter. I had added 1.5 tsp. orange extract with the vanilla at the wet batter stage. It turned out to be a fantastic combo, esp. with the marshmallow gooeyness and the cinnamon sugar dusting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After going to the effort of freezing the marshmallows & wrapping the dough around them when they came out of the oven they completely disappeared into the chocolate cookie.   I smooshed some leftover marshmallows into each cookie immediately after they came out of the oven, while still molten hot, then, after about 60 seconds, I pushed them down again so they looked like white blobs. If I make these again I would skip the marshmallows inside and add them afterwards.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think next time I’d make them either with more cayenne, or none. The cookie as written is too middle-of-the-road, with a confusingly subtle aftertaste of heat. Like you accidentally baked the cookies while you were also roasting peppers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For my second attempt to make this recipe, I followed Melanie’s prior suggestion of first assembling the cookies while the dough and marshmallows were at room temperature then refrigerating the dough or assembled cookies.  And the assembling of the cookie was not as frustrating as it was following the original recipe of refrigerating first then assembling. I would recommend adopting Melanie’s tweak to the recipe.

Also, I substituted vegan mini marshmallows which lead to chewier cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi Guys! I made these with vegan marshmallows and it worked. I followed the recipe as written. I could only find large vegan marshmallows so I cut them in half and used a half in each cookie. Just be sure to completely encase the marshmallow in the dough for full squishy delightfulness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Did what others suggested and put room temp miniature marshmallows into room temp dough and rolled to seal, refrigerated overnight. Baked 10min from fridge. 
Came out round but marshmallows either disintegrated or busted through bottom and hardened a bit. The extra little sticky crunch underneath is fine for me but not sure how my husband’s English teeth will do. 
Used all the cayenne and not too spicy at all in my book.  Delicious not too sweet flavor for chocolate and spice lovers!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Of all of the Cookie Week recipes, this one was my favorite. I took the suggestions from the comments and assembled the cookies and marshmallows while they were room temp, and then put the whole tray in the freezer for 30 min, and baked for 15. They came out amazing!
 Unfortunately, my kid thought they were "too spicy" (I did NOT), but I'll try reducing the cayenne out next time for her sake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I put these together before freezing using 4 marshmallows. Then froze. Put them in the oven totally frozen. They taste great but the marshmallows seemed to have disappeared; absorbed into the cookie. Did anyone end up with cookies that actually have marshmallows you can see and taste in them???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had the same problem, but I let my set out for 15 minutes before baking. Some had visible marshmallows but others were not. I wish I knew why.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Decided to give this cookie recipe a try with Bob's Red Mill Gluten Free 1 to 1 Flour. The first chilled ball of dough I attempted to smush and mold around the frozen marshmallows, stuck all over my hands. So ... for the remaining dough balls, I oiled my hands between each. Not sure if that was due to using the GF flour or not, but it certainly helped. AND those cookies turned out great. Thank you for the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these vegan with vegan butter, a flax “egg,” and vegan marshmallows. I used half the cayenne and they were great. My husband asked me to tone it down next time - but I think they’re good and it prevents me from eating too many at once! 

I didn’t refrigerate the dough before adding marshmallows, and I didn’t freeze them in advance. Once assembled, I put them in the fridge for 2 hours and then baked. Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cayenne was far too spicy for me and my kids. We substituted 1 tablespoon of ketchup for the cayenne, and it came out perfectly!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Prepped these on Christmas eve and they were ready to make Christmas morning — followed previous suggestions of using room temp. batter and marshmallows. 

I used a “clicky” ice cream scooper, filled the spoon halfway, and stuffed it with my mini marshmallows. Using another spoon, I filled the rest of the ice cream scooper with batter. Saves me the trouble of having chocolatey hands and ensures your marshmallows are enveloped in the cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tried this with a flax egg as we are vegan.  Used frozen vegan m-mallows too. Did not freeze dough.  Still came out great!  However, I forgot to roll the dough around the mmallows.  We got great cookies—topped with brown mmallows!  Not bad but…..Note to self…….

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I’m an unfussy (lazy) cook so I read previous notes and assembled cookies with room temp dough and room temp marshmallows. Kept in freezer overnight then baked the next day. I underbake cookies so they are soft rather than crisp. These has a fudge/brownie texture and they 
melted in the mouth.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Don’t bother refrigerating anything till after shaping the cookies. 

Used quartered jumbo vegan ‘mallows. 3-4 for each cookie. They didn’t spread much while baking, but it was OK.

Best eaten ~10 min after baking! Also very good when microwaved 10-15 sec, just long enough that the ‘mallow starts to puff up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After reading the comments, I assembled half my batch at room temperature and did the other half according to the recipe (frozen marshmallows and refrigerated dough). The latter was much easier—the cold dough was easy to handle, whereas at room temperature it was too sticky—AND those cookies turned out better. I should have just listened to Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I followed other people's advice of stuffing these cookies before chilling them, freezing them, and then rolling them in the cinnamon sugar right before baking. These came out amazing with those different instructions! It reminded me of eating a slightly spiced and softer Oreo, which I can't complain about.

I air fried the extra 25th cookie, and even though the poor fella was absolutely torqued due to my negligence, it was still great dipped into some milk.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these a few times over the holidays and they were a big hit. I did accidentally add all the ingredients together (not saving the sugar and cinnamon to roll in) and it came out great anyway - but the dough was pretty dry and came out messier than the photo here. I then made them as directed and they were also great, but still a little dry. I patted the dough in a little non-dairy milk to make it stick better and they turned out really well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are so fantastic that I’ve made them twice in one week! If go step-by-step, they’ll turn out perfectly. I suggest freezing a whole bag of marshmallows so the mass keeps them from melting once you pull them out of the freezer, and taking just a few pieces of dough from the fridge at a time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Solid cookie! Made them once at Christmas and again after everyone gobbled them up. I did end up freezing the marshmallows but noticed they didn’t stay frozen too long; I never tried chilling the dough but for the second batch I put the rolled up cookies in the fridge before baking and they turned out great. Nice and spicy and warming on a snowy day:)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: liz, I had the same experience with the marshmallows. Three minutes after mine came out of the freezer, the bag they were in wasn't even cold any longer. I think it's probably wiser to just wrap the just-mixed down around room-temp marshmallows and then maybe refrigerate the cookies for a few minutes at that point. I wonder if they need refrigerating at all, and am going to try them that way next.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wish I’d read your helpful comments before I made these.   The fridge step made the dough so hard to work with even after sitting out and kneading it.   But I persevered.  Wonderful tastes and texture overall.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made exactly as written the first time, then adjusted based on comments here and from my coworkers...just a hair less cayenne in the dough, and a tad more cinnamon in the cinnamon sugar. For the cookies, I portioned them out, let them hang in the fridge as directed, then rolled them cold, baked for 5.5 minutes, then when I rotated the sheet, I added half a full sized marshmallow to each, put back in the oven for another 5.5 minutes. Raves!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I cut the cayenne in half because I am sensitive to it. I still get the heat.  Cooked perfectly in 10 min. Turned the tray at 5 min.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious--however, the stated baking time was far too long.  I made 24 cookies (the high end of the suggestion) and froze the unbaked cookies.  The low end of the suggested time for baking from frozen of 13 minutes resulted in absolutely hammered cookies, with the marshmallows completely melted and unseen.  Even baking directly from frozen, 10 minutes (or even less) was the correct baking time to get those bits of marshmallow peeking through and visible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As another baker said, these are fussy! I ended up oiling my hands to flatten the dough. I had no problem putting five marshmallows inside each ball. I constructed them completely, and then put them in the fridge for a couple hours. They baked up fine and tasted really good, however, in most of them, the marshmallow completely disappeared. Maybe that’s the point of putting them in the freezer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely give the balls a lot of room. I made the mistake of crowding on first batch, corrected by only baking 6 per sheet. Makes it super easy to shape cookies out of the oven. Next time will use a 1.5 scoop instead of 2.0 and use 3 marshmallows per cookie. Delish and a huge hit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I formed room temperature dough in 35 gram portions with room temperature marshmallows, froze overnight, and baked the next day. Let them thaw while the oven preheated and then rolled in the sugar. Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There is a middle way.  The freshly mixed dough is ridiculously sticky.  I was unable to get it into mounds and absolutely unable to shape it around the marshmallows per other suggestions.  I refrigerated the dough for a shorter time -- 15-20 minutes -- and then easily portioned out 4-5 cookies around the marshmallows, keeping the unused dough in the refrigerated and the rest of the marshmallows in the freezer.  It was a lot of opening fridge/freezer doors, but worth it in the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Great tasting cookie, however the dough was incredibly crumbly and difficult to work with. 

My recommendation…find your favorite chocolate sugar cookie recipe, add cinnamon and cayenne to the dough, top with half of a large marshmallow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good cookie, pretty different form than described. I halved the cayenne and it was still too spicy. Marshmallows did not quite get that consistency, but overall fine cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I absolutely loved the cinnamon + cayenne flavor.  My cookies did not turn out soft at all though - they were very flat and crispy.  The culprit is possibly my oven being too hot.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vegan marshmallows work BETTER than regular!  We have these cookies premade in the freezer to bake a few each night.  Chill dough, then assemble with vegan marshmallows.  Preheat the oven, then bake from frozen about 12-13 minutes.  We found with regular marshmallows, they sometimes liquefy before the dough is done.  Vegan marshmallows take longer to melt and give the dough a couple more minutes to bake.  Fantastic cookies!  (Great with a sip of bourbon too!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 25 g balls makes 2 dozen

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I omit marshmallow?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A difficult bake, to be sure. And the instructions could use some editing. Like others, I mistakenly put the full 3 tsp of cinnamon into the dough because it was not noted to be divided. Oops. About half my cookies turned out well with the marshmallows intact. The other half saw the marshmallows melt and disintegrate away. These were on the same pan, same size cookie. I put them into the freezer for a spell after assembly because the marshmallows very quickly warmed up and I didn’t want them to melt away…I think others will enjoy eating these. They’re a bit sweet for my taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Suzanne B Sorry this recipe didn’t work for you.  I long ago learned to read my recipes before starting to cook.  Now that I’m getting older, I read new recipes two or three times.  That way I don’t screw them up.  Which I definitely have !

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These worked better at 375°. Used a 1T scoop and just mashed two together with marshmallows in between.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Watching the cooking video was a sweet treat on its own/also extremely helpful. Not sure I would have made these delicious joy packages without the encouraging visual instructions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dry. Marshmallows completely cook out

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Surprisingly delightful cookies, made as written. The chocolate is rich, the marshmallow adds a nice creaminess, and the cayenne is the perfect amount of kick (and this coming from someone who finds banana peppers spicy). My coworker is a Mexican hot chocolate enthusiast and gave these full-throated approval at the holiday party. For my first batch of cookies, I rolled the dough balls a little too smooth and the marshmallow had a hard time peeking through. Be sure to leave a little fault line.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They turned out super hard and crunchy on the outside I would add another egg yolk or more butter next time. Also I wasn’t too fond of the marshmallow idea so I used Ghirardelli chocolate chips instead.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I agree with the comment about specifying when an ingredient is to be divided. Three teaspoons directly into the dough wasn’t fatal to the end result, but a heads up would have been nice. 

Also, why specify a 2 tablespoon scoop if you aren’t making 2 tablespoon balls? I spent extra time making dough balls only to find out in the next paragraph that it wasn’t necessary. 

Yes, I know. Read the instructions all the way through before you start.  Next time…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish but seem way too sweet. How much sugar could safely be cut back?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: it's a cookie, Carol. if you're looking to cut sugar, this cookie (or any) may not be the snack for you.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for a cookie contest at work. I doubled the recipe, except for the cayenne, and I used extra dark cocoa. They didn’t win, but it was close!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavor and aroma are amazing, but I really struggled with the shape and texture. As other reviewers said, it is extremely difficult to wrap the cold, crumbly dough around five marshmallows. The resulting cookies spread out into huge, flat messes. Had more success with three marshmallows, but next time I will follow suggestion to assemble the cookies at room temp and then freeze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the instructions but put them back in the fridge for about and hour after I wrapped up the marshmallows. I may have left them in the oven too long (I went the full 12 mins). The marshmallows completely melted away after cooling. It’s a fussy recipe and frustrating that just a minute or two too long in the oven will give you a flop. I love the taste of the cookie though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This year we used last year’s marshmallows and the cookies came out much better! Frozen Stale is best, we suspect it’s because they have less air.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so good! Gotten a lot of compliments on these. My only suggestion is doubling the recipe if you want more than 8-12 because it doesn’t make a ton. Which is nice when making them for just my household

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 1/4 teaspoon of cayenne and it was the right amount of heat. The dough was difficult to work with. I tried making a few balls with room temp dough, chilled dough and freezing them with marshmallows but the cookies just spread out so much and did not get cooked.  With half of the remaining dough I just made the balls  and added a few marshmallows to top in the last minute of cooking. The other half, I baked like a brownie and then covered the top with marshmallows and cooked until they were lightly browned.  The brownie bars came out the best.  Even though they taste good, the time making them is to long if you have other things you want to bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely pointless to refrigerate the dough. Crumbly time consuming mess to flatten and then reform cold balls of dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Totally agree with Melanie's comments:
Divide room-temperature dough into 35g round portions, flattened to fill with the room temp marshmallows, then chilled in the fridge for 2 hours.  Then roll in sugar and bake.  They came out looking identical to the recipe photo. I just didn’t want to frustrate myself with attempting to wrap cold dough around quick thawing mini marshmallows.

Also, I halved the cayenne and there's still plenty of heat which dissipates nicely.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
used 1/4 of paprika and 1/4 chipotle chili powder because i couldn't find cayenne! seesm to taste the same as the version made with cayenne i had recently! 

appreciated the tip to roll them and fill them before freezing! super helpful : ).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic cookie. Followed others and wrapped the frozen marshmallows with unchilled dough. Reduced the cayenne to a 1/4 tsp. I baked and then froze them, before setting them out for Christmas, yikes if I had used the 1/2 tsp of cayenne I doubt anyone could have eaten them. Some guests love spicy foods and even they said it was just at the edge of too spicy, for the less spicy tolerant it was too much. If eaten the same day as baking the spice was barely noticeable but if left to meld watch out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So delicious. I did not have cayenne so I substituted chipotle morita powder and they were still 10/10.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did the following:  Skipped rolling in cinnamon sugar, and used all 3 tsps of cinnamon; after making the batter, formed all of the dough (room temp) into 1 1/2 inch diameter balls; then took each ball, flattened, stuck in a marshmallow and chocolate chip, rolled (in my hand), added some more chocolate chips, rolled, and then at the end, added a marshmallow, rolled, but allowed a marshmallow to be visible; froze all for 10 minutes, then baked until marshmallows spread. 15 min for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dough turned out super crumbly, like sand, not sure what I did wrong. I ended up adding water to fix it. I struggled to get more than two marshmallows in there but they tasted good

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You under beat it, I think. Mine was crumbly at 2 minutes inn, perfect at 3.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I sandwiched the marshmallows with two guittard semisweet chocolate wafers. It added an extra chocolate flavor that really enhanced the flavor of these cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious.  Highly recommend.  Made them according to the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made this for an office cookie exchange and people went crazy. i'm not even kidding. went to a meeting after putting the cookies out and came out of it to no less than 7 messages BEGGING me for the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vegan version- I substituted vegan butter, and 60g of banana for the egg. Parfait!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After several bakes, here’s how we do it: Don’t bother chilling the dough before assembly. Instead, assemble cookies then freeze before baking. Use 2 T scoop. Marshmallows not strictly required.  Full size mardhmallows cut in half and frozen also work fine, if desired

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made them without the marshmallows because I didn’t have any at home, they were still delicious! Chilled only for two hours - a great quick treat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can skip the refrigeration phase. Used stale mini marshmallows put in the freezer just while mixing the dough. Everything turned out perfectly and looked like the picture. I don’t mind complicated or fiddly recipes but this one can be simplified with no ill effects. So happy I read the reviews and skipped refrigerating the dough before adding the marshmallows. If you’re wondering what to do with the leftover stale mini marshmallows in your cupboard this is a great use! (Using a 2T scoop this recipe made 16 cookies - fewer than the recipe indicated)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is my new all-time favorite cookie! I am Mexican so the amount of cayenne in the recipe was perfect. It didn't come across as spicy to me, but instead as a warm feeling that mimics the sensation of drinking Mexican hot chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made a double batch for two Cinco de Mayo parties I was going to. Halfway through making the cookies it was too time-consuming and too difficult to keep the cookies together. I even tried using fewer marshmallows, same problem. I then decided I was just gonna make brownies out of the dough and other ingredients.  I ended up using a Pyrex pan, sprayed it with the oil.  Took the remaining dough balls and put them in the bottom of the Pyrex dish and used a potato masher to mash them down. Then I put the marshmallows on top, sprinkled them with the cinnamon sugar. Had a few dough balls left, which I crumbled on top and then put a little bit more of the cinnamon sugar on it and baked it for about 30 minutes at 350 degrees. I let it cool and then cut it into squares and it came out perfect. The brownies  looked way better than the cookies.  This was much easier and tasted great. I got lots of compliments on the brownies. I’ll use this exact recipe again, but for brownies instead of cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did what some recommended and didn't freeze anything. I did chill the batter so it would be easier to handle. This worked fine and saved some time. At the end, had a little batter left and didn't add marshmallows, just balls coated in sugar and cinnamon. I like these better. The marshmallow gets sticky when the cookies cool. Without marshmallow, I was able to taste the rich chocolate and spices. Very good either way but if you don't have marshmallows on hand, make anyway.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. I admittedly added about a cup finely chopped bittersweet chocolate to the dough and added 1 tablespoon of instant espresso to amp up the chocolate. Followed others’ advice to shape the dough while at room temp and it was easy! 10/10 will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these and was happy. Saved half the  frozen dough balls for Dia de los Muertps. Thanks to those who made helpful shaping suggestions. I benefited. But those of you who are Mexican hot chocolate cookie fans, may I suggest Cheryl Day’’s recipe from her Back in the Day bakery book? So good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Marshmallows or no marshmallows? I vote no.  First I made them with 4 mini marshmallows, assembled and froze the balls and then baked. (The marshmallows had no taste, and the marshmallows in one of the two I baked completely dissolved. The insides of the cookies were wet and gummy.) Today I baked them without marshmallows and felt they were much improved. I flattened the room temperature dough slightly after rolling in the cinnamon/sugar. The cookies were crispy throughout.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Classic for our Christmas, now. Don’t bother chilling the dough before assembly. Instead, assemble cookies then freeze before baking. Use larger (#40] scoop. Marshmallows not strictly required.  Full size mardhmallows cut in half and frozen work fine, if desired.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used only ¼ teaspoon cayenne, which provided enough heat for adults and children alike. Most comments were it was hard to fold cold dough over frozen marshmallows. I assembled balls at room temp, THEN refrigerated them. Much easier, and get the same beautiful result. Counted out 20 groups of 5 marshmallows before rolling; do this ahead of time. Covered only ¾ of ball with sugar mixture before baking so they wouldn't stick to the pan. A keeper with these adjustments.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I LOVE these cookies.  Cinnamon cayenne all of it. My kids love it.  Enough with the whining.  Yes you must be patient with a dough that has frozen mmallows.  
Read the notes: plot your strategy: bake em.  There’s nothing like em.  
Four stars.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious! (even though I did not divide the cinnamon and all 3 teaspoons went into the batter...) If making for children to consume, eliminate the cayenne. Otherwise the heat is truly lovely. It just warms you up as a Mexican Hot Chocolate would! 

Note: I scooped out 2 Tablespoons worth of dough as written in the recipe and basically doubled the size of the cookie. I'm not mad about it. They turned out so incredibly rich and moist as the marshmallows melted IN to the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these cookies. Follow tje recipe mostly perfect.. I do add extra spice;) I also use powdered sugar & cinnamon as the coating to get a sweet fluffy coating and it gets rave reviews every time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can someone explain to me how 1 1/2cups of all purpose flour ends up being 192g when 1 cup all purpose flour = 120g?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Anne D. I also found major discrepancies between weights and measures

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As others have noted these can be a bit “spicy” with the pepper. Thinking you could omit and just keep the cinnamon as that adds a good bit of heat. Also/ I didn’t have mini marshmallows, so I cut regular sized ones in thirds- that was enough and easy t work with as you now just have a frozen disc to work around. Did the trick with forming around them and *then freezing. Great result. Delicious warm out of the oven and with a glass of milk :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
