commentBody: @Grace I didn’t have minis either and used your tip. Cookies turned out great and it was much easier to roll without having to wrangle the little marshmallows. Will do it this way from now on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Next time, will add 3 tsp and a touch of nutmeg to the dough. Reduced sugar from 300 to 240 grams, but could go a bit lower if using dark brown.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made a batch of these cookies today, and I don't even have words. They are AMAZING. I followed all of the ingredients as written, and weighed my dry ingredients.  

When the dough was fresh, I used my small Oxo scoop, added 4 mini marshmallows, and rolled in cinnamon & sugar. Then I refrigerated the dough balls for an hour, and let them sit out while I preheated the oven, using the convection setting.

After they've cooled for a few hours, they're a bit chewy, and a bit crunchy. Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Eeek!  Made an error.  I used my medium Oxo scoop, which made 20 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were good but a little finicky. I ended up constructing the cookies with room temperature dough and marshmallows, then freezing before baking. For some reason, some of the cookies came out just like the recipe photos but for other batches the marshmallows melted into the cookies. They still tasted good either way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these gluten-free using Bob's Red Mill 1:1 Baking Flour and they were delicious.  You may just need to warm the dough up a tad more with your hands before adding the marshmallows but otherwise, I am sure you couldn't tell the difference. Next time I make them, I will balance the flavors out better by using a scad bit less Cayenne. It was slightly overpowering.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I confirm the dough gets very hard to work with when chilled. I used a 1/4 tsp cayenne and made dough, rolled 1-Tbsp balls with fresh-made dough, added 3 marshmallows (not pre-frozen), and chilled them after assembly in the fridge fir twi hours. I tolled in cinnamon sugar just before baking and they turned out great! Pulled batched as the marshmallows “appeared” in the centers and started to brown slightly, so they were a bit gooey — around minute 11 in my oven. The only thing I will change us upping the cayenne to a 3/4 tsp since my 16YO wanted just a tad more of the heat at the back of the throat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the method others said about forming the cookies with marshmallows at room temp, then freezing the dough. The cookies just out of the freezer baked the best with marshmallows intact; the marshmallows in the cookies that I left out to thaw during the first batch baking mostly melted into the cookies. They taste great, though. I used 1/8th tsp of cayenne and that's plenty spicy to me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Be careful not to over bake! These cookies were not my favorite. The cayenne comes through too strongly. The chocolate cookie part is fine. But I won't be re-making these. There are better vehicles for good cocoa/chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed this recipe exact, I just let the cookies sit in the fridge overnight. I had no issues with crumbling when placing the marshmallows in the center…I used 3 per cookie and my batch made 24 cookies. They came out perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love these but make the process simpler: just make little discs with dough right after mixing it, put 3 mini marshmallows out of the bag on top, wrap into a ball.  It is ok if marshmallows peak out.  Bake 13 min. At 350.  No need for sugar cinnamon topping.  Makes about 3 dozen. Sooooo good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@JSS

I mean 12 minutes!!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies came out as described. Two problems though. My mini marshmallows froze into a lump. I had to buy new ones and spread them on a cookie sheet to freeze separately. I will cut the cayenne in half next time. Thanks for offering the weight of each dough ball. I got 24 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
not sure how these have five stars, the assembly is horrible. i wish i would have read the comments before hand, then i would have assembled the cookies before chilling. and to be honest they don’t need to be chilled! 

they taste delicious, the cayenne is great and comes through at the end, but the steps need to be amended.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yeah, these are a hot mess to put together. I followed the directions and even tried to follow adaptations based on the comments — still hot mess. Visually they’re a great representation of the show “nailed it” but they do taste OK.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am obsessed with these. The marshmallow goodness, the crinkle texture, the heat on the end. So good!! First time I chilled the dough overnight, 10/10. Second time I had to freeze the balls for a few days, they came out tasty but flat. I’m super excited I get to make them again this week!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made these a couple of times. We use Penzy's spices, and the cayenne is stronger than grocery store-bought spices. The 1st time we followed the recipe and used 1/2 a tsp of cayenne, it was too much. The second time, we used 1/8 tsp in a double batch, not enough. The next batch will be with  1/4 to 3/8 tsp for the double batch. the frozen mini marshmallows were all stuck together. Make the cookies with soft marshmallows and freeze them for a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these the day after making a similar sugar cookie, so i felt confident in doing the following:

Make dough. Keep in bowl & put in freezer 10 min, marshmallows too if they’re out of the freezer. Take out of the freezer, take 33-34 grams dough & flatten. Put 4 mini marshmallows in middle, roll into a ball with marshmallows in the center, roll the ball in cinnamon sugar. Place on parchment covered cookie sheet & bake. 

They looked & tasted great. They were eaten quickly by my family.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are my absolute favorite cookies. I love the sweet + heat + touch of salt. I roll the dough with marshmallows into balls and freeze, then bake later. They always come out good this way. The last time I made them I didn’t have enough brown sugar so I made up the difference with white sugar + maple syrup and they came out perfectly chewy. So glad I accidentally made this discovery and will always make them this way going forward.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 5 stars with adjustments, 4 without. Delicious cookie base, so will make again. Definitely form the ball around the marshmellows and roll in sugar before refrigerating, as others noted. My marshmellows melted away instead of making a marshmellowy pool, so I would try another way of adding them - maybe melt on top in the last few minutes of baking. But they are delicious even without the marshmellows, so I might not bother. Mine took 11 minutes to bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
It’s true that the marshmallows need to be frozen.   With my first batch the marshmallows were in the freezer overnight and the cookies were chewy with the marshmallows shining in nice white swirls.   For the second batch the marshmallows  were only in the freezer for about. 30 minutes.   The cookies came out without white parts showing, and the texture was not as pleasant.   
I would agree that more cinnamon would be nice.  The cayenne adds a good deal of interest , so don’t skimp on that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came together easily for me and were tasty! I followed all the advice and inserted the marshmallows into the cookies right after making the dough. I then refrigerated for two hours and baked per the recipe.  My marshmallows were in the freezer for days so were very frozen.  Most of the cookies came out looking just like the photo - crackly and with strands of melted mushrooms. Oddly, a few came out looking just like big brown blobs - but they still tasted good

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Took other's advice and formed cookies with room temp dough and room temp marshmallows.  Froze them overnight and cooked them for 13min.  Turned out exactly as shown in picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with dehydrated mini marshmallows? I’ve read they end up a tiny bit crunchy on the outside and melted on the inside. I read through about 60 comments but didn’t see a comment re. dehydrated minis.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookies and I’m excited to riff on the flavors when I inevitably make them again. Very glad I read the comments to know to adjust the assembly process. I scooped the dough just after mixing, added marshmallows with both dough and mallows at room temp, then froze the whole bundle for a couple of hours. Put them in the oven frozen and only added a minute to the baking time for 13 mins total. They were absolutely perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Storage Problem:  I was able to get the desired outcome with refrigerating and freezing, tricky but okay.  They are delicious. But, how does one store these?  If you put them in an airtight container, they loose all crispness and melt into one another creating a chocolate and marshmallow blob.  I can’t take these cookies to friends this weekend like this.  I just separated them and left them on a cooling rack, but I know that’s not a solution.  I cooked the last batch a little too long and they spread way out, but are crisper which completely melted the marshmallow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really enjoyed these cookies! I was worried about the spice so I didn’t add the cayenne and doubled (could’ve tripled) the cinnamon. Rolling in the marshmallows was hard, couldn’t fit more than one or two in each cookie. These are best served right out of oven bc the cookies harden and the marshmallow kind of disappears. Baked them straight from freezer and was perfect

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have some traditional spiced Mexican drinking chocolate - can I substitute that for the cocoa and spices?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I assume it’s fine if it’s unsweetened cocoa.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mexican hot chocolate, like Abuelita, has its own sugar content not to mention cinnamon. You'd be altering the balance of ingredients. If you're just experimenting have at. When I bake I try to follow what's written the first go at a recipe (though there is no way I'm putting a half teaspoon of cayenne into a cookie dough!).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were incredible! The texture was perfect and the spice was spot on. I could even go spicier. My 8 and 9 year olds also loved them and asked for seconds. I only baked off 7 to start and plan on freezing the rest of the prepared dough for the holidays. An original showstopper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m trying to figure out what I did wrong. My cookie spread out quite a bit on the sheet and no longer resemble the lovely puffed cookies I saw in the video. Advice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi Hilary, I'm definitely not an expert, but my guess would be your baking soda. I'd check to make sure it hasn't lost its leavening properties. https://www.tasteofhome.com/article/testing-baking-powder-and-soda/ Hope this helps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I would add more cayenne next time for a stronger kick. Also, I needed to cook for several minutes longer because these were pretty big cookies. 
Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How many Mini Marshmallows?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How much mini marshmallows?  And, are we talking the tiny marshmallows like in hot chocolate mix or a standard bag of mini marshmallows?  Are we using the whole bag or just a cup?  I wish the instructions were a little more explicit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used the standard grocery mini-mashmallows from the grocery. I made 16 cookies x 5 marshmallows, so 135 overalls. I think it was about a cup!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cut back cayenne to 1/8 tsp, which gave a nice amount of kick. The dough was a bit stiff and I had a job trying to add the marshmallows. The dough spread a lot in the oven. Ok, but not amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I only got 14 cookies out of this recipe, not 20-24. I weighed all my ingredients and made each cookie exactly 2T, as specified.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Me too...until I realized that I was weighing out 2 oz cookies instead of the 1 oz specified. Then I ended up with 24.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did a second batch and got 16 cookies. Better, but still far short of 20. Not sure what the answer is but I wouldn't recommend making each cookie smaller without reducing the number of marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Karen you *weighed* them to 1 oz? The directions say 1oz of volume, not weight, so it sounds like a nice coincidence that it worked out :) 

Either way it sounds like 2T/1oz is just slightly more dough than 1/24 of the whole batch would be.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Menacingly delicious

Spice was spot on with a Mexican hot chocolate

(If your kids are familiar with flaming hot cheetos, they will be more than fine with these cookies)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone know how many days these cookies will last once they are baked? I want to ship them to my son so they won’t be eaten on the same day.  Thank you for your input.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would this be doable with almond flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love this recipe it yields closer to 15 cookies per recipe when followed as directed. I didn't have a 2 TBSP scoop so I weighed each cookie out at around 50 grams.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did have a 2 TBSP scoop, but the dough was very sticky and a pain to get out of the scoop. It was way quicker to portion the dough by weight. Two tablespoons of dough weighed 46 grams on my cheapo kitchen scale. A double batch produced 32 cookies. Next time I'll triple the recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I subbed in spices like cardamom and clove for the cayenne and the flavor came out beautifully. The dough was soft and pliable for stuffing and rollling, however, the "look" of these cookies was not quite right. None of my white marshamallows ever showed through, and some of them bled through onto the pan and became crystallized, which gave it a nice crunch, but didn't look very appealing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Seriously if one has access to good cayenne pepper a finger tip of cayenne would be sufficient. Otherwise the compost gets Christmas cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with homemade marshmallows?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Contrary to the vast number of sweet and savory recipes in the Cooking site that insists on adding "heat" ( ie some form of chili) all that chili does is overwhelm the recipe.  You need to cut back on the cayenne and let your taste buds work again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The point is that mexican hot chocolate often will have a bit of heat. It's not some trend. Should you wish, find a different cookie or adjust the recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried giant (?) marshmallows?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One word: disaster. The dough was too dry and required far too much handling and time to secure five marshmallows - several fell apart on the cookie sheet. I might try a different cookie dough recipe that is more pliant and moist and wrap it around the marshmallows, while still including the spice mixture, which is delicious. You can't win them all - the Technicolor cookies were a resounding success.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this at high altitude?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm in Denver, and my comment (Dave Petzinger's above) shows my results!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you use a half regular-sized marshmallow instead of five mini ones?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do we use sweetened or unsweetened cocoa?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Baking cocoa is unsweetened.  Its much easier to get good quality in Canada, so ask for the best one in the USA, if that is where you are.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cocoa powder for baking is unsweetened.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious, but like others have noted, don’t turn out the way they look in the photo! I think if I try them again, I will chill them after rolling the marshmallow in and then roll in the cinnamon sugar before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to send these to my daughter in New Zealand for Christmas.  Do they really last only 3 days?  The mail takes at least 10-14 days.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Beth did you end up putting them in the mail for your daughter? I was wondering about this as well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tasty, but sticky.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there a better way to keep them?  Three days is not nearly enough for holiday cookies.  What happens after 3 days?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is delicious but fiddly. The dough was hard to work with and the marshmallows thaw at the speed of light. I used jet puff mini marshmallows. I couldn’t get a cookie with a marshmallow center like the recipe image. They disintegrated into the cookie, still delicious but missing the wow factor :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You should have made a note in the ingredient list that the 3 tsp cinnamon doesn’t go all into the flour mixture. I put all 3 into my flour mixture already ugh.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really high maintenance! My first batch didn't look anything like the picture, but rather a wide, flat pool of a cookie (which was delightfully crisp). Since the marshmallows don't add anything taste-wise, I'm going to ditch them entirely and save myself the approximately 45 seconds/cookie that it takes to make them. I'm going to amp up the pepper next time, since I didn't get much of a sense of it the first time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just wanted to ask, a whole bag of marshmallows? The recipe doesn’t give a quantity.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Big hit at choir rehearsal. Gloria in excelsis Vaughn Vreeland!

Agreed that the cayenne level is perfect for people who like spice but would recommend caution if you're baking for people who generally don't eat a lot of spicy food.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cayenne pepper was too strong and the dough was finicky. Not great results for the amount of time/effort this recipe requires.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use a hand mixer and the dough didn't come together at all.  I had to add more ... and more butter until it was 2 sticks of butter to have the right consistency of dough. (I used Kerrygold butter / European butter and I don't know if that makes it different but I use that for all of my baked products). The comments here are helpful. The recipe should read: 3 tsp cinnamon divided (cinnamon sugar for outside). I used one mini marshmallow (vegan marshmallow) and it was easier to mold.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What if you accidentally made the dough with two sticks of butter instead of one? Said dough is chilling in the fridge....

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fridge? Yes. Room temp dough sticks & makes a mess. But just 20 mins in the fridge in the metal mixer bowl with scoop. Perfect. Pliable and not sticky. But work fast. Keep dough on fingertips so it sticks less. 1.5 Tbsp scoop allows for enough dough to cover marshmallows. Cookies spread a lot and are about 2.5" round. Yield:20. They are crazy d-e-l-i-c-i-o-u-s.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I subbed ~basic~ chili powder for the cayenne because I’m soft (and I have a 2-year-old who loves cookies) and these cookies are still a SLAM DUNK. Definitely recommend the cinnamon sugar coating before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Great outcome with some vegan substitutions: 
- Vegan cooking and baking sticks (earth balance) for the butter 
- Ground flax (1 tbsp mixed with 3 tbsp warm water left to sit for a few minutes) for the egg
- Regular-sized vegan marshmallows (Dandies) cut into 4 pieces before freezing

I added the marshmallows after mixing the dough, then popped the combo in the fridge for a few hours before baking.

Couldn't believe I achieved the gooey, stretchy marshmallow effect in the video!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the recipe almost exactly as written. Cut the cayenne to 1/3 teaspoon but I think that 1/2 teaspoon would have been better.  This one is a keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow! So good.  I omitted the marshmallows , used a heaping 1/4 tsp of cayenne and cut the sugar to 200 grams.  Had trouble getting the cinnamon sugar to stick to chilled cookies so had to warm them up in my hand.  I cut the size to 1 tbsp and baked for 8 minutes.  Had to flatten the cookie balls slightly before baking.  They came out perfectly and have a nice kick of heat to them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Texan here who is very well-versed in all things Mexican Hot Chocolate. These are some of the best cookies I've ever made, but I changed quite a bit: Left out the marshmallows entirely, way too sweet. I kept the total cinnamon in the batter and a little extra cayenne and salt. Did not refrigerate them - just rolled balls from 1 large tablespoon and rolled in sugar (ex-cinnamon). Baked for 10 minutes. These were gone in the same night and then a "demand" to make more the next day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are you kidding me? These are insane. Everyone who has had one agrees. SO GOOD. I definitely put 3 tsp of cinnamon in the cookie dough though. NYT should definitely add a *divided note where applicable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are my new favorite cookies! So fabulous. However, I haven't managed to freeze the marshmallows "solid" even tho they were in overnight. Maybe my freezer isn't cold enough? I think I'll try to unwrap them and put in the ice tray area and/or do the "freeze the whole assembled cookie" idea. Yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was out of cayenne the first time I made the recipe. Rummaging through the spice cupboard came up with a bottle of Pico Fruta chili that I got from Penzeys. I put in the amount listed for the cayenne. When the cookies were done, I couldn’t taste chile until the final swallow and then a just hint came through. I just remade the recipe for a second go round and put it in 2 teaspoons of the Pico Fruta, followed by 1T of chipotle for some smoke. Lovely warm flavors without any burn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are delish! Stuffing the marshmallows in is a finicky task but would take this any day over rolling out and cutting shapes of sugar cookies!

I made the dough, portioned into balls overnight and then stuffed and baked the next morning. They are so tasty and the kick of cayenne makes these unforgettable!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I found that a 2 tablespoon scoop held 5 marshmallows best.
You also need to wait more than 5 minutes to start flattening and rolling. If they aren’t warm enough for the assembly process they crumble along the edges and when folding.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We picked up some ‘designer’ marshmallows and decided to give this a try. I cut the large marshmallow squares into small cubes, froze those and then folded into dough w/o refrigerating and then formed balls, rolled in sugar/cinnamon coating, popped in the oven and they turned out well but will use regular marshmallows next time as these type sort of melted…still yummy and we loved the kick of cayenne!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Can anyone tell me
How many marshmallows please. Thanks

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Cold dough is hard to work with, as is the just-made dough. I had success chilling the fresh dough scooped into 1oz balls for about 15-20 minutes, then making a little pinch pot to nestle in 4 marshmallows. Then I closed my fist gently around the bundle and pinched the gaps closed.  I then froze the balls for about 2 hours. 

5-7 minutes of thawing allows a little cinnamon sugar to cling. 

Baking time of about 9.5 minutes allowed marshmallows to just emerge; longer, & they melted away.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 1/4 tsp cayenne rather than 1/2 tsp. Really delicious and unique cookie I’ll be adding to our annual Feliz Navidad party dessert lineup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I didn't have cayenne, so used about a 1/4 teaspoon ground Ghost Pepper (my son grows variety peppers). Love the spicy-ness! And Oops--I didn't realize I was supposed to divide the cinnamon--so mine had all 3 teaspoons in the dough. Still added the cinnamon-sugar coating because who doesn't love a snickerdoodle? Agree with others that freezing the mini-MMWs didn't seem to hold up, since by the time they were wrapped the MMWs had thawed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you use a few less marshmallows, they'll hold their shape better.  I used 3 for the second batch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These re delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
"frozen" marshmallows warm up really fast. 

I was kinda confused by the pre-cooking directions, between getting things in the fridge.

So the Recipe made 24 balls (about 2 Tsp each). I chilled those 2 hrs. After, I then split each ball in two, flattened each in my palms, then recombined the two, with only 2 marshmallows each. There did not seem to be room to incorporate 5 marshmallows per cookie.
But the dough was tasty, and kitchen smells nice now too. can't wait to try the finished product.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So yummy! Fun to make! They're "spicy" but in a warm, toasty way that doesn't linger/burn your mouth off.  My dough didn't crack, a couple minutes outside the fridge made it pliable. I think two big tricks for success are to weigh your ingredients and use a good quality dutch process cocoa (I personally like Anthony's).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: why do they only keep for 3 days??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for a Christmas cookie swap. I didn’t realize the cinnamon was divided and put all three teaspoons in the batter, but the end result was delicious. For the second batch, I watched the video and used a large glass to shape the still-hot cookies back into circles, more or less. My only frustration was that I ended up with only twenty cookies that still weren’t big enough to wrap around 4-5 mini marshmallows. Yes, this recipe is more fiddly than others but the flavor is worth it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had to lower marshmallows to 3 per cookie to get cookie around them. I would lower salt if I make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the flavor.  I made half with the marshmallows and half without. Will skip the marshmallows in the future.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These cookies were a hit! So delicious and chewy. I thought they were well worth the little bit of extra assembly effort, and I think they'll be a xmas staple.

I found the best way to assemble was to refrigerate the dough before shaping it into balls. Then when I was ready to bake I took the cold dough out and let it warm up a bit before attempting to wrap it around marshmallows. I worked one tray at a time so the marshmallows stayed frozen right up until I was ready to pop them in the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good cookie! Based on reviews, I halved the cayenne and formed the dough around the frozen marshmallows before chilling it. My kitchen stays relatively cold, so the dough was not too sticky. If I made these again, I would omit the cayenne. It’s good here, but a little distracting given everything going on with the marshmallows and chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just echoing what others have said, working with the chilled dough is too much of a pain. Better off forming the room temperature dough around the frozen marshmallows, easier and works just fine. The cookies are really delicious with a real bite from the cayenne on the back end. Not for people who don't like a little spice but great for those who do. Will absolutely be making again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can this recipe be made without the marshmallows? If so, are there alterations to the recipe needed?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I also assembled and froze the cookies and this worked perfectly. My scooper was smaller, and I ended up with around 30 cookies, each with 3 mini marshmallows. I used a little less cayenne pepper than what was called for (1/3 tsp), and felt comfortable serving this to a non-spicy crowd. 
Great cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I combined two similar recipes.  Added about 6 oz of chocolate chips and rolled in turbinado sugar (makes a nicer presentation).  But I must have rolled the dough around the marshmallows too perfectly because I didn’t get the peekaboo effect but rather a marshmallow surprise.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't have a mixer with a paddle. Can I use just a conventional mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love the heat and spiciness of this recipe but hate marshmallows.  Has anyone tried omitting them?  How did it ho?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: HOT! HOT! HOT! HOT CHOCOLATE! The flavors in these are FAN-TASTIC. I agree that the could dough is quite sticky and a bit difficult to work with when it’s super cold. The suggestion about filling with marshmallows *before* chilling is a great one, and I’ll definitely be doing that next time. Other than that, this is a great cookie (and with a serious kick)!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Tricky to assemble quickly! These definitely require adhering to 3 inches apart on the sheet. I was surprised by how wide they spread .

Agree with assembling, forming into balls, refrigerating, THEN rolling in the sugar, cinnamon mixture before cooking!

Super tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Thank you to the person who recommended weighing the batter when portioning out the dough... better than trying to scope them evenly.  Weighing them made for  more uniform cookie sizes.   

Watch your cookies to make sure they do not overcook!  I found that in my oven (which runs very accurate re temperature) that the lovely white marshmallows became clear and crispy instead of chewy at the 10 min mark.  Using convection,  the cookies were perfect at 6 min.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were so good! I made my with ritual cacao I had in hand instead of the coco powder. Mine had cinnamon, reishi, chaga, and lions mane added to the cacao mix. I loved these! The had such a nice soft chew to them ugh I had 3 on the first night.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We left out the marshmallows but dabbed the cookie dough balls in crushed candy cane for a peppermint crunch on top and the result was excellent!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made gluten free with KA Measure for Measure flour. Used maybe 1/8tsp of cayenne and there's still a nice little burn, 1/2 tsp seems like waaaay too much. Shaped half the cookies at a time, keeping the rest of the dough in fridge/marshmallows in freezer. Cooked for 14 mins (GF baking usually takes a little longer). They're quite crumbly, might add xanthan gum if I tried again. They're extremely delicious, very rich and chocolately with nice chew from the marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mexican hot chocolate doesn’t typically have or need marshmallows, so I was dubious going in. Pain to make and not worth the effort, I will stick with my trusty post punk kitchen recipe, which turns out perfect every time and and is almost impossible to mess up. Also is vegan so I can cover cookies for a lot more people.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried to make these substituting a gluten-free flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My marshmallows disappeared once the cookies cooled off slightly. Perhaps they weren’t frozen enough?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used a smaller cookie scoop (1 tablespoon/.5 oz), didn’t chill the batter, wrapped the dough around 2 frozen mini marshmallows, and they turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a hit at my annual cookie exchange! Subbed instant espresso for the cayenne. I followed the tips from other reviewers and did not chill the dough at all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This dough was so crumbly that I couldn’t get a ball to form around the marshmallows. I kneaded in additional butter and a tablespoon of heavy cream just so I could work the dough. Viola! Perfect for inserting marshmallows. Easy to roll in cinnamon sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these as directed and they are DELICIOUS. The warmth from the cayenne is delightful and lingers. No issues getting frozen marshmallows into the slightly warmed dough, and I was able to work quickly to do 8-10 at a time. Will be making these again for sure.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Total fix! Yes cold dough was a problem but I got beautifully round cookies w marshmallows perfectly contained by putting ball of dough (5 min removed from frig) on a plate, smashing it w back of cookie scooper, placing 5 mini marshmallows in center and then forming a volcano shape by pulling up the sides to hold but not cover the marshmallows. That bottom stays flat. Covered w cinnamon and sugar mix. They bake by spreading out and marshmallows stay in place!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wondered about the comments of folks concerned about the cayenne pepper. Growing up on Kansas City barbecue and living a few years in New Orleans, I thought I would try the whole dose. Not a problem! Only felt the mild heat at the end of eating the cookie. It gave my thumbs a workout though to make the indentation for the marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cut cayenne down to 1/4 teaspoon and it's perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dough is dry and crumbly out of the fridge. Doesn’t hold its shape around the frozen marshmallows. Going to try 2nd time with room temp dough. Prob 3-4 mini mallows per cookie instead of 4-5.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really delicious and chewy. I can see how some find it too spicy. If you’re not a fan of spicy food, I’d recommend just doing a pinch of cayenne or omitting altogether and have a cinnamon s’mores cookie. But if you can handle spice, these aren’t super spicy. You feel the cayenne in the back of your throat, but it’s balanced with the sweetness of the chocolate and marshmallow. It’s not a cookie where you indulge in a whole plate, unless you really like that burn.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Most of the marshmallows faded into the cookie.  Freezing after assembly might help or using half of a full sized marshmallow.  We thought the flavor was great (with the 1/2 teaspoon of cayenne).  I plan to try them without the marshmallows, like a chocolate crinkle cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Baked to 16 maybe 14 next time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are absolutely fantastic and taste just as expected! After reading about the challenges with cold dough and frozen marshmallows, I decided to follow Branchop's method. I used room temperature dough and incorporated four room temperature marshmallows, froze the dough balls, and followed the suggested thawing time before rolling them in cinnamon sugar. The results were outstanding—they looked exactly like the photos in the recipe. Most popular cookie of the season!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I ended up refrigerating all of the dough overnight, then forming the balls and adding the marshmallows immediately before baking. I also flattened the dough balls a bit before baking, so that the edges didn't crack. It was an easier approach and worked great!
Everyone I served these to really liked them, but agreed we could cut back on the cayenne a bit. 
Would make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A Tasty cookie. Some things I learned: 
I used RG ancho chili powder instead of cayenne - quite subtle at 1/4 tsp - would increase next time. I added some cayenne to the cinnamon sugar topping and liked it.
I didn't have mini marshmallows and tried cutting some (not fresh) jumbo marshmallows in fourths. Do not recommend this - they stayed in a lump and made the cookies look like the snake from The Little Prince. I tore apart the rest and replaced with minis - much better. No need to chill dough

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wanted to love this recipe, but I agree with the other comments. The dough was super crumbly and difficult to work with. The flavor is good so I will use this flavor combination with another basic chocolate crinkle cookie dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are complex to make and in their taste. I haven’t mastered the marshmallows; they just melt into the cookie and do not pull apart. Maybe I should cook them less? It tastes amazing and the slow burn is fascinating and fun. It is an interactive cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were amazing! I made them gluten free by subsituting 170 g of white rice flour, 30 g of tapioca starch and 1 tsp of psyllium husk powder. I kept the original amount of cayenne and my kids (9 and 11) LOVED them!! I did not have issues with the cold dough cracking… when it did crack it was easy to mold back in shape. I wonder if my GF dough made it easier to work with? I also used Kerrygold butter. The cookies lasted less than a day in our house!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
No matter how you assemble these cookies, it goes from marshmallow to melted sugar very quickly.  The key is to keep an eye on them after 8 min and the *instant* you see the white peaking through, pull them out.  Let them finish cooking on the pan or the marshmallow just melts into the cookie.

I did 21g of dough w/3 mini marshmallows, assembled right after mixing, then froze them all.  Took them out while the oven pre-heated and then baked. Made 34 cookies. Heaping 1/4 t cayenne was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I saw many. bakers found the "smooshing" of marshmallows was tedious  and so this was more easily omitted. I did forget to roll the rounded cookies in the cinnamon-sugar & definitely made them less sweet and brought more complexity with the heat. When they came out of the oven, I then placed one mini-marshmallow in the center of each one. Tah-Dah! Next batch I will dust the cookies with cinnamon before placing them in the oven to bake. One mini-M is enough for this baker. Cheers!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these over the weekend with almond flour and they were great. I made half at a time to keep marshmallows frozen. I would make them smaller next time. It made for very messy but good tasting hands.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I second wrapping the room temp dough around room temp marshmallows, rolling them in the cinnamon sugar, and then freezing them. I did make a batch where both the marshmallows and dough were just slightly chilled, and those turned out fine, if a bit more melty. 

I gave some to my coworkers, and one remarked that they were the best cookies he’s ever had!

Also, if you like spice, I recommend using the full amount of cayenne from the recipe. I thought it was the right amount of heat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just tasted the dough…. Whoa spicy!  And I’m a person who eats hot sauce in everything.  I hope the heat simmers down after baking…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too fussy..too much cinnamon. Cayenne is good..its supposed to have a kick. Nyt recipes have been disappointing  last decade or so..

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazing, I’ve now made two batches. Two tips! 1) Don’t refrigerate the dough for longer than ~30 mins before shaping the cookies! The dough is way easier to work with when sort of warm. 2) Set the trays down very carefully when you take the cookies out of the oven; my first batch collapsed when I placed the trays down. They look much prettier if they retain their puffiness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out absolutely delicious! I used room temperature dough and marshmallows since others reported that the dough is hard to work with when cold. The recipe yielded 18 cookies for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Man, what a mess these cookies are. Love the flavor and the kick of spice. But I won’t be making again because they are too messy with the marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used only 1 tsp cinnamon and 1/4 tsp cayenne.  I didn't bother refrigerating the dough.  I just rolled into 1" balls, flattened them and then wrapped around 3 mini marshmallows instead of 5.  This way I was able to make 36 cookies per batch.  The cocoa came through more with less cinnamon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great! Thanks to Branchop for the suggestion of using all room temp. I found it worked great, as long as I rinsed my hands with cold water every few cookies.  4 minis was just right, and 1/4 tsp cayenne worked great for us.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vegan marshmallows were okay - they were a tiny bit too thick even after cooling. We’ll use standard marshmallows next time. We borrowed the flaky salt suggestion and it was nice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Although several of you warned me, I still managed to put all 3 tsps of cinnamon in the dough. This recipe also makes far more cinnamon sugar than necessary. I would make half unless you have a use for leftovers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Although I think freezing the marshmallows is a brilliant move, which I will be stealing for other recipes, I don’t feel like they ultimately brought much to the table. They still tend to melt away into the dough and the spice overpowers their sweetness. White chocolate chips would be a great addition to get the look of mini marshmallows and add sweet contrast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these last night sans the chilling step since other people had trouble with crumbly dough and only used 1/4 teaspoon of cayenne. Delicious results. Beautiful crisp edges and soft inside and more than enough heat. Happy Holidays, all!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The marshmallows didn’t work well, even frozen. The cookies are delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The recipe as written works.  Putting the cookie in the palm of your hand and wrapping your fingers around the cookie is key when putting the marshmallow in. Then just treat the dough like play dough … it can be stretched over the marshmallow. 

This is a great cookie - especially out of the oven!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious! And finicky! I cut the cayenne in half because so was worried they would be too spicy. I don’t think that was necessary and I am a wuss. Make sure not to over bake them - they took my over just shy of ten minutes - otherwise the marshmallow will completely melt into the cookie and ultimately harden and you’ll be left with a crunchy cookie that is not the same texture as what you’re going for. I also rolled the marshmallow into the dough pre refrigeration. Not easier!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My marshmallows disappeared into the cookie! It's still delicious, but I really want that marshmallow-y gooiness inside. I don't know if I did something wrong. I assembled the cookies at room temperature and froze them, as others suggested. I let them sit out for about 5 min before coating them in the sugar mix and popped them straight into the oven. Is there a trick I missed?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are bananas—def going in my holiday cookie rotation.  Tips for success… I followed the advice of other posters and formed the cookies around 5 mini marshmallows with unchilled dough, flattened the dough with the side of my hand, plopped the marshmallows in the middle and folded into a ball.  Froze the balls solid, theb dipped in cinnamon sugar from frozen, baked at 350 for 12,minutes—my oven is old and wonky but just watched them until puffy & marshmalllows peeked through.  Best cookies!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are pretty neat. I followed the recipe as described - chilled the dough during the workday - and then mashed each cookie up like playdo. Voila! I’d take them out of the oven as advised - when they crack, as I did 11 min and they are a bit on the crispy sufe. And, while I am not someone who finds mint toothpaste to be too spicy - I would recommend 1/4 tsp cayenne as the 1/2 tsp is too much lingering pow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used peppermint extract, peppermint marshmallows  and extra kosher salt in place of cinnamon and cayenne. WOW! Also agree with others on working with room temp dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wanted to love these but way too many steps-reading the notes here helped so much as it’s frustrating to work with the way it’s written. My son loves them which is the only reason I’ll ever make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
good crackly top chocolate cookie recipe.
maybe use coarser sugar to roll in. 
got 23 cookies with scoop.
added some cloves and subbed some rye flour.
cooled the entire dough briefly before forming (1hr) - easy to work with.
frozen minis are indeed fiddly and thaw quickly - may try halved lg as someone else mentioned (but was afraid might affect appearance and wanted this batch pretty)
possibly more important than frozenness of marshmallows is baking tho. take out when puffy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: as an update, after these cooled the not-overbaked ones were absolutely spectacular

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I do not rec. these cookies.  Dough was very dry and crumbly -- no way could I from it around the marshmallows.  And they are VERY hot -- way too much cayenne??  I feel I'd have to serve them with a warning.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Heaven in a cookie. I formed ball around marshmallow before freezing as per another reader’s suggestion with excellent results. I also wet the frozen cookie balls ever so slightly to adhere better to the cinnamon sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Terrific cookies!  We were out of cayenne pepper so I substituted chili crisp oil- 1tsp.  I chilled the dough right right in the cuisinart bowl without forming and then followed the recipe as written.  My 20 year old son said they were great and admitted he wouldn’t have known about the chili crisp unless I told him.  Maybe next time I’ll try 1 1/2 tsps,

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Prepared dough with room marshmallows mixed in, then refrigerated overnight in balls and baked as directed. Turned out just like the photos. Not too spicy and nice crunchy sugar coating on the outside!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did what others suggested - formed with un-chilled dough and then froze.  Baked for 15 minutes, which I guess was too long - the marshmallows disappeared and the texture did not come out like the video (a crunchier cookie).  Used a 1/4 tsp cayenne and they are plenty spiced.  Will still gift them but will probably would not revisit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and as others have said the cookie dough is too loose/grainy to wrap around the marshmallows, even when I let the rounds come to room temperature. 5 marshmallows is way too many given the amount of dough. As soon as they went in the oven they fell apart. While they taste fine, they won’t make the Christmas platter - probably good crumbled over vanilla ice cream though! I’d cut the cayenne in half.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I cut down on the cayenne pepper to 1/4 tsp and thought that was even a bit much, at least for my taste. Make sure the marshmallows are frozen solid or they will melt into the cookies. The dough is stiff, but manageable, and will be easier to work with as you roll it up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Outstanding! Made with GF flour because I have to, wrapped flattened 35 gram dough ball around unfrozen marshmallow and froze. Rolled in sugar combo after 15 min thaw and baked. They spread so 2 to a row and 4 to a column. Leave on cookie sheet for 10 min to cool. Reduced cayenne to 1/4 t bc I don’t like spicy but family does. Loved by all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: don't refrigerate before oven! also did it without cayenne and it worked fine

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was an excellent cookie with a nice little "kick" -- not too overwhelming.  I followed the recipe as written however the frozen marshmallows melt into the cookie after 10 minutes of cooking.  They still tasted fine, but if you want the look of the white marshmallows, be sure to start checking at 10 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
is there any other reason why these would've turned out puffy and cakey other than mismeasuring the baking soda (which I really dont think I did)?
still good but not flat like the picture. perfect amount of spice!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: wrong weight of flour, wrong temperature of butter?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this without the cayenne, but used fresh ground cinnamon. It was delicious! I didn't have mini marshmallows available, but full size marshmallow is cut into quarters with kitchen shears worked perfectly.
As others suggested, I froze the marshmallows, portioned the cookies with a disher, and then wrapped them with the room temperature dough. I then rolled them in cinnamon sugar and froze them prior to baking.
They came out perfectly!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 0utstanding flavor but agree with others that adults may enjoy more than children do. Wish I'd read the comments about freezing the entire cookie after assembly, as I found the assembly instructions a bit unhelpful. I placed the marshmallows (4 is probably better) on the dough and ended up lifting the entire cookie and forming it immediately into a ball in my palm, smoothing out the dough over the marshmallows. Cookies turned out exactly like the pictures. Will probably cut down sugar next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lost the marshmallows completely, they just made the cookie very chewy, and not in a good way. They ended up too thin and hard, but not crispy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: they were overbaked, then

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 10/10 incredible cookies! So gooey and lovely. I ended up having to make them with normal (jumbo) marshmallows bc my grocery store was sold out of minis. The recipe worked with the jumbos tho there was more splooging than I’d assume would happen with minis. But TL;DR, yes you can make this with regular marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I followed the advice of others and wrapped the marshmallows in the balls right after I made the balls, with no cooling in between. I then put them in the freezer for about 30 minutes, let them thaw for 5 at room temp, coated in sugar, then baked for about 13 minutes.
I cut the cayenne in half (0.25tsp) and they are still spicy enough to taste it, so I think 0.5tsp is way too much. Also, for the cinnamon sugar coating, 1/4 cup is too much, I had more than half left after coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I read many comments and skipped the fridge step as suggested - the cookies came out perfect, exactly as pictured.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We subbed vegan butter, vegan marshmallows, and flax egg. The cookies turned out incredible! The texture was perfect and fluffy. Highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Following others advice, avoid chilling the dough before trying to form the cookies. Complete all steps for making and sugaring the dough balls THEN chill for 15 minutes to half an hour to make sure that when the cookies bake you actually will see the marshmallow in the cracks and not have it melt away.  If the cookies spread a little too much, you can push the edges in while still warm after removing from the oven. Do not try to remove the cookies from the baking sheet until they are cooled!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 10 stars - these cookies were divine! I used a cinnamon, nutmeg, and chia spice vs plain cinnamon . . . upped the cayenne a bit and the result was fabulous!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these vegan with aquafaba and vegan marshmallows (homemade because I couldn't find any in a store) and they are terrific!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm fearless in the kitchen, so I was pretty far into prep & mixing before realizing I should have read the whole plan... Wow! FREEZE the marshmallows... how many?  N x 5 is the answer, revealed in step 5. Rest in fridge for 2 hrs or OVERNIGHT. Huh? I want this done BEFORE bed. Take ½ out, warm some, stuff them like a pierogi. Double wow! I lack an attention span for all this. Spoiler alert. I ignored a lot & went gonzo, and made 15 yummy cookies, just like in the picture !!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these. They're delicious. Opted for less cayenne. They do harden up after a couple days. Mine were pretty big - only 17 cookies. I also didn't bother with the refrigerator. The dough is a bit crumbly already. I might try to use this dough but make a non-Mexican version, skip the cinnamon and cayenne, and try to squeeze in more marshmallows and a few chocolate chips. I like a gooey cookie : )

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A very fun and delicious holiday cookie! I too fell victim to the ingredient list, and added all 3 teaspoons of cinnamon to the dry ingredients. I realized it before I had whisked, so I attempted to remove about a teaspoon of cinnamon. My batch made 24 cookies, and I put between 3-5 marshmallows in each. The ones with 3 sort of just dissolved, so definitely use the recommended 5.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the flavour. I had the same issue with crumbly dough after refrigerating. I formed the cookies using wet fingers which helped and formed cups with marshmallows inside rather than trying to cover them fully in the dough. Worked out.  I got a nice marshmallow puddle in the middle of each cookie.  I also skipped the marshmallows on some, just rolling the dough balls into the sugar and baking like that.  Those were also excellent ... a spicy brownie cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tried 1/4 tsp cayenne. Does catch up with you as you eat a whole cookie but could probably go a little hotter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The first dozen I assembled per the recipe I only used 3 mini marshmallows because I thought there was no way I could get 5 in there and get it all enveloped before the cookie dough got too warm from my hands. They came out as expected - barely any marshmallow. The second batch I did 4 and and it was better. My last batch was 5 and the look is much better. I will try this again with the suggestion of assembling first then fridge or freeze and then roll and bake. They taste yummy and spicy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yikes. I followed the recipe exactly but got crumbly dough.  Double checked each step because this was first time making the.  Definitely needs some more fat or liquid. I was able to form some balls from the dough so I’m chilling these  and will try to bake a few.  Suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Did not refrigerate the dough.  Rolled into balls and flattened slightly.
Added frozen marshmallows rolled again. Froze. Removed however many 
needed. Rolled in cinnamon sugar and baked at 350 for 10 minutes.
Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wish I had read the notes of others about assembling then freezing. These took a long time to prepare, working the refrigerated dough enough to mold it around the marshmallows and reaching into the freezer for marshmallows. They were very tasty however. I would make again and try the tips of wrapping room temp dough around the marshmallow and then freezing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these mostly as directed, and they turned out amazing! Just like the photos. 

I used gluten free flour (Schar Mix C). 

I only used half the amount of cayenne as I was nervous and it wasn’t spicy enough, will use full amount next batch. 

I formed the cookies around the marshmallows while everything was room temp, then froze overnight. When ready to bake, I followed the instructions by letting them sit at room temp for 5 mins, rolling in the sugar/cinnamon/cayenne and baked for 14 mins.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My family and friends love these cookies. The assembly can be challenging but not insurmountable and I became quite adept on my second attempt. We have made nearly every single 5 star NYT Cookie recipe and this is at the top of the list!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thank you all for the helpful suggestions.  I froze the marshmallows but did not chill the dough. I added the frozen marshmallows to the freshly made dough then refrigerated them for a couple of hours ( I was making other cookies at the same time so lost track of time). I removed from the refrigerator and let sit for 5 minutes then flattened them only slightly and baked. They came out great.  I had reduced the cayenne but think next time I’ll add the full amount. Happy holidays!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies were too big, so I divided each one as follows, bring up chilled dough around marshmallow like a potsticker.  roll into oblong shape, cut this in half, reshape to cover filling, cook 9 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree with others who suggest wrapping frozen marshmallows with room temp dough. Take out on the early side as marshmallows will melt into the cookies with too much time - this happened to a few of mine during cooling time so they were just extra sticky sweet - still good but you lose the marshmallow look / stretch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love the flavor of this cookie. I got about half of them to look pretty on my first attempt. I think next time I will leave the cookie balls with marshmallows inside to cool overnight in the fridge. Then bake them straight from the fridge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out like little pieces of pottery fired in a kiln. ??? The marshmallows utterly disappeared, melted into the sugar crust. I followed the recipe, except for reducing the cinnamon and skipping the cayenne (didn’t have any) no funny stuff. What the heck? I thought the dougb seemed quite dry when I was mixing and chilling it. 1/2 cup butter also seems a bit scant? I don’t know what it needed but they were hard bricks. Flavor was fantastic though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did 3 marshmallows (room temp) and froze the balls (with cinnamon sugar coating) overnight then baked the next day straight from freezer. They looked almost as good as Vaughans .. next time I’m adding more marshmallows to achieve the look!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Wow, these are astonishingly delicious! Thank  goodness for all the comments since I skipped the whole freezing step and just put  the frozen marshmallows into the dough I’d just made.  I substituted about 1/2 -3/4 tsp of gochugaru powder for cayenne and also out about 1/2 tsp of it into the cinnamon sugar mixture that I rolled them in.  Gochugaru is less hot than cayenne.  Also, the leftover sugar is fabulous on sliced apples.

Too bad this recipe doesn’t make more.  Doubling it next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious but as others have mentioned, we need to work on the assembly. Ours really were too big, following the directions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I usually follow the recipe closely  the first time, but I am a spice wimp and don't have cayenne in the house. So I used paprika--just a quarter teaspoon. Tastes heavenly to me. I watched the video--twice, he's so fun!--and had no trouble with the cooled dough and frozen mini marshmallows. This is the first cookie I have ever had that tastes so indulgent that one is enough. This is a keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dough was very crumbly and hard to work with. Even though the marshmallows had been in the freezer for days, they never became ‘solid’ and they sadly vanished completely into the cookies. A bit of a cookie fail.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies look just like Sarah Meuser’s Hot Chocolate-Marshmallow Cookies from the 2014 Pillsbury Bake-Off. Sarah’s recipe has only six ingredients (including refrigerated cookie dough), is easy to make, and tastes amazing! You can find the recipe here: https://www.pillsbury.com/recipes/hot-chocolate-marshmallow-cookies/0d29c112-6d03-4fcf-8e89-690205e67459

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the advice of numerous folks, and put these together at room temperature. I cut a hole in the top of the marshmallows bag, and since we have a drawer freezer I propped the bag up in the freezer and grabbed frozen marshmallows as I need them, to keep them frozen. I worked with a tiny little bit of flower on my hands, which helped. The first batch went in at room temperature and came out flat, the 2nd I took out of the fridge and they came out a bit more puffy. They're looking great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Freeze marshmallows. Portion cookie dough into individual sizes. Flatten and wrap around 4 marshmallows. Make into balls and refrigerate. Roll in cinnamon sugar before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did 1/4 tsp of cayenne instead of 1/2 tsp which is my preferred spice level. Also I messed up and added all the cinnamon to the dough when I made it instead of dividing it, but it still turned out okay. If I make it again, I think it would be good to put the dough back in the fridge to chill after adding the marshmallows because my dough got too hot and spread quite thin.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Only make a few at a time and keep everything else in freezer/fridge. Cook 2 mins shorter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these gluten free with vegan butter-like substance. I followed the comments and added the marshmallows before refrigerating. They came out fabulously.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A new favorite. Happy it didn't make tons because when I am not eating them, I am thinking about eating them. Definitely an adult cookie.

Love the flavor and texture. 

Will try the non-refrigerated method for the shaping next time. Shape, refrigerate, roll in sugar, and bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could high altitude impact the rise? My cookies came out flat with melted marshmallows in the middle. I may have baked them a bit too long or they thawed too much before getting in the oven, but altitude might also have been an issue. Great tasting though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Friends that like spicy loved these cookies. Important to keep the marshmallow from spilling out of the cookie while baking; you end up with burnt marshmallow on the side. Which is actually pretty tasty but detracts from the cookie appearance.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Assembly note! 4 marshmallows works!  I am going to give this recipe one more chance and take the advice of Branchop and assemble the whole cookie next time and freeze them overnight.  I will then pull them out of the freezer for 15 minutes and roll in the cinnamon and sugar and bake.  As a taste note; these are not children's type cookies they are probably more for adults. by altering the cayenne you take away the MHC taste  My first go turned out flat and not picture perfect so one more time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Baking times are off - at least for me, and yes, my oven is running at the advertised temp! my later batches were cooked for around 7-8 minutes, following the notes in the recipe ("until cookies puff slightly and bits of molten marshmallow peek through the surface") so would definitely recommend that approach versus the timer!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I wish I had read the comments about shaping the cookies first. They tip readers have shared make way more sense than the shaping directions in the recipe. The marshmallows thaw almost instantly so what’s the use of pre-freezing? The chilled dough crumbles and warms up in your hands anyway so what’s the point of chilling? I followed the instructions as listed but put the shaped cookies back in the freezer so they re-chilled. 

Stuff and shape at room temp and then freeze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved the flavor of these, but what a mess! And so hard to get those marshmallows into cold dough. Tried again with reader tips. Used newly created room temperature dough. Made the cookies smaller. Put in 2-3 mallows. Rolled and froze them.  Rolled them in cinn/sugar. Baked at 325 convection oven. Pefecto! Thanks for the comments!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2023 double batch. 1/4 c black cocoa 3/4 c Venezuelan cocoa powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed a mix of other’s notes - Put cookies together with 35 gram balls of room temp dough and marshmallows, put balls of dough on cookie sheet and froze overnight. Let sit at room temp for 5 min., then rolled in sugar.  Baked for 16 minutes and came out perfect!  Also, made the recipe vegan using Miyokos plant based butter, chia seed “eggs”, and Dandies vegan mini marshmallows.  Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Just baking these. My test cookie was delish. Yes, it’s a little spicy. I mis-read the size of dough balls, so ended up with twice as many, so most I combined sandwiching the marshmallows in between 2 and rolling into a ball, which gave me 20 full-sized. 

Agreed that just freezing the whole thing makes sense. But I had 1 extra “half”, used that with 3 mini marshmallows. Baked 10 minutes. That’s what I’ll do next time. Nice size cookie for a cookie tray (plus you can have more!).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Should have looked at the notes before making these!  Dough was super dry, live in CO where indoor humidity is in single digits in winter. Brushed the flattened rounds with milk before stuffing and rolling.  First batch turned out very flat with marshmallows disappearing.  Putting them back in fridge for 10 min after stuffing and rolling solved this, came out just like the photos. Used 1/8 tsp cayenne for batch, reserved half for kiddos, then added remaining 1/8  tsp to rest of dough for adults.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too much cinnamon sugar. I would try 25g sugar and 1/2 tsp cinnamon next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t bother chilling the dough, as other notes said this made the dough challenging to work with. The cookies turned out perfect that way. I did find that parchment paper on my baking sheet was essential as some of the marshmallows left a sticky mess.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What do you mean rotate?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are outstanding. My only change is that I went a little lighter on the Cayenne. Recommended without hesitation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Creates crisp edges with a soft center. Used vegan marshmallows and it worked well. 

Also made a 1/2 batch vegan (miokos vegan butter, bob’s red mill egg replacer, and vegan marshmallows) and they worked well. Fluffier (didn’t pool out as much) with slightly crisp edges.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and they came out perfectly. Had no troubles shaping and adding the marshmallows - I only left the dough balls to chill for 2-3 hours. Next time would add extra marshmallows as I wanted a bit more gooey-ness - definitely don’t skip on the marshmallows!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
adjustments I made (thanks to helpful comments): (1) refrigeration makes dough too crumbly. I skipped that step entirely. (2) I cut the cayenne by a bit. (3) during assembly, I found it crucial to have the marshmallows completely covered by the dough to get nice looking cookies with in-tact marshmallows. any exposed marshmallows led to misshapen cookies & dissolved marshmallows. 

after some problem solving, the cookies came out beautifully. my family loved them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious! A great cookie to make with young children - folding the marshmallows into the dough has a playdough quality to it. Did as others had suggested - formed the cookies with just-made dough around frozen marshmallows. Put the formed cookies in the freezer for 10-15 minutes and then followed the instructions for baking from frozen. Skipped the cayenne for the sake of the kids, and they still have a ton of flavor from the cinnamon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A delicious but unforgiving recipe! My first batch did not work. Second batch tasted amazing, but only half of them looked look the photos, though all were delicious. My recommendations: only take out enough chilled dough and frozen marshmallows to roll six cookies at a time. Watch the cookies closely when baking and don’t put more than 6 on a pan. They spread a LOT and if you overcook them even slightly, the marshmallows evaporate and are no longer visible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did not freeze the marshmallows or chill the dough before assembly. It was fine. I chilled the balls for about 30 mins. Very tasty! I make a similar holiday cookie using Devils food cake mix and rollo candies. The rollo version wins by a smidge!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Even with cutting the cayenne in 1/2 it is too spicy. The flavor lingers at the end. The sweet notes hit first but the cayenne spice is not pleasant for me. And the sugar/cinnamon on the outside is messy. 

I would make these again as a simple chocolate brownie type cookie with marshmallow filling - that aspect is very nice. Maybe a little sugar on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tasted the dough before & after cookies baked, and FYI to the spice-wary, heat increased after baking. On the aesthetic side, when I baked two trays at once, I got more cookie spread and less marshmallow peek-a-boo-ness for the lower rack cookies, boo! Overall, lovely crinkly gooey yumminess. I will obviously take top place at my holiday cookie exchange tonight, even though no one else knows we're competing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had high expectations for these especially after reading all the comments. Put mini marshmallow filled dough in the freezer overnight and took them out a couple of minutes before baking. Marshmallows all melted so, while they still taste good, they don’t look like the NYT photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I find cayenne to be spicy without much flavor (and also I’m a wimp), so I substituted ancho chili powder instead. Delicious! I might even add a bit more ancho next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didnt refrigerate the dough. Did freeze the minimarshmallows. I put 2 in each cookie and rolled them in the sugar mixture. They came out perfect. It made 24 cookies. About 1 1/2 Tbs each.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used vegan marshmallows and the cookies came out great. I could only find the regular size so I cut each one into eight pieces before freezing them. Because of time constraints, I went the form and freeze route mentioned in other comments. When cooking the cookies, at 12 minutes I wondered if I had made a mistake but by 14 minutes they looked great. Also, I ran out of brown sugar so my cookie batter only had 214 grams - turned out fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
We made these as written, except with closer to 1/3 tsp cayenne, and they were a hit even with some young children.  

I will definitely make these again, but next time we'll skip the marshmallows.  I don't think they added enough taste or texture-wise to the cookie for the extra effort of working them in.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Cut cayenne in half

Make the cookies at room temp w/room temp marshmallows and then freeze.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious and a big hit!  Rolled 28g of dough and used regular sized marshmallows, frozen and cut in half.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine came out better when I only shoved 4 mini marshmallows inside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Oh my goodness I am obsessed with these cookies. They are delicious and everyone I’ve given them to also loves them. 

I did as others suggested and wrapped room temp marshmallows in room temp dough, then freezed the balls overnight. Then I coated the frozen balls in cinnamon sugar and baked, didn’t have to add any extra time. So unique and so good!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and these were amazing. Baked on parchment and seperated while warm the few that met at the edges. Only dipped the top of the ball in the cinn/sugar mix. Outstanding and will make more. Love the afterglow these provide with the cayenne.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You won’t be disappointed if you use vegan marshmallows. I did, and they didn’t melt in the oven — I found it very pleasant to bite into full soft marshmallows in the middle of the cookie. Following other reviewers, I halved the cayenne and found that to be the right amount.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Was hesitant to try a different Mexican hot chocolate cookie recipe--I LOVE Isa Chandra's. My girls are into baking so trying to savor that time together--store only had vegan mini marshmallows which we used. They didn't really puff nor melt, and might have been why they didn't spread--that and the super low humidity here in Tucson. Flavor was pretty good--but a little sweet for me. Would like to try again with other marshmallows

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these and they’re fabulous! Based on some of the other reviews, I cut full-sized marshmallows in half. I didn’t chill the dough or the marshmallows. I managed pretty easily to wrap the room-temp dough around the marshmallow halves. Then I put the formed cookies into the freezer for an hour or so. They baked perfectly and look like the photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe I didn't make the batter balls big enough...but I found it difficult to fold the marshmallows into the cookies, so I just put them on top and they melted into the batter.  This recipe yielded 19 cookies. So delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Slightly larger and alternate ingredients recipe: 256 g flour, 68 g cocoa, 1 Tbsp baking soda, 1 Tbsp cinnamon, 3/4 tsp cayenne, 1/2 tsp salt, 1 egg 2 1/2 tsp vanilla.  1 cup vegetable shortening (butter flavor or plain) with 305 g brown sugar.  Roll <2 Tbsp dough and insert just 1 mini marshmallo.  Skip chilling the dough or freezing the marshmallows.  Cookies stand up fatter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 4 yr culinary school grad here so I’m experienced in the kitchen. Mine don’t look like the picture! First 8 out of the oven spread a lot. There was a circle of completely melted marshmallow, almost like a window (touching the bottom of the pan) with a mostly disconnected from the middle thin dark chocolate cookie around that (almost like brownies that got run over by a truck). I froze the second batch before 15 min before putting it in the oven and the same thing happened! What did I do wrong?!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm having the same result - they taste great, but they are way too thin. I'm wondering if it's not the result of dough to marshmallow ratio spreading the cookie dough walls encapsulating the marshmallow too thin.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent! I skipped the marshmallows altogether and these were a big hit with all. Such a great flavor profile. I think they're going to land on the every-year list.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I could not make as small as recommended without having the marshmallows peek through, so I increased the size and baked them in a muffin pan, then did a little squiggly white and red icing on top. Very presentable, but more of a dessert, than a cookie snack.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Newbie here. Why do the cookies need to be frozen? Can’t you roll them in cinnamon and sugar at room temp

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the note about freezing is just if you want to make them ahead of time and store unbaked for a longer period. Generally though, chilling a cookie with a lot of butter in it before baking is so that it holds its shape and they don’t spread all together into a mess. Hope that helps!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent cookies! I made them with vegan mini marshmallows which were successful. Not quite as melty as regular marshmallows but they did melt and taste good. I made balls of dough with 4 marshmallows in the middle and froze them. Rolled in cinnamon and sugar right before baking. Much better assembly than recipe directions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: First, the cookies are great!  But I used a 1 ounce scoop and only got 13 cookies from the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tried with vegan marshmallows and it worked quite well. Not as much melt and pull but they look just the same and taste delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these! Used 1/8 tsp cayenne (1/4 tsp next time but def not 1/2), no final sugar roll. For prep–I froze marshmallows, made dough, rolled marshmallows into soft dough, and refrigerated for a few hours before baking. Used a tbsp cookie scoop and wished they'd been bigger––mine could only hold 2 marshmallows! Next time, I'm going to try dividing the dough into two, rolling out, piling marshmallows on one side, blanketing w the other, then cutting into rounds before cooling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious even if the marshmallows dissolve some into the cookie.  I did go for 1/4 tsp cayenne cause a lot of folks seem shy of hot pepper.  The warmth is there still with the 1/4 tsp.  Next time I try these, will follow advice to shape cookies right after making the dough and then freeze them before baking.  But even if that does keep the marshmallows from dissolving and adding a chewy caramelized flavor to the cookie instead of a marshmallow center, I'll be okay with it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree with @Sasha, for the balls, stuff the mini-marshmallows in and form into round ball. Pop into fridge for 2 hours or until well chilled. Then preheat over while bringing cookies to room temperature and roll on one side with the cinnamon sugar as another suggested. I placed them on parchment paper leaving enough room as the cookies spread. I used a glass, after they cooled on sheet and before placing on baking racks, so they would be somewhat uniform in size. Will be making again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
