commentBody: Wow! These are to die for! Possibly the best cookie of my life. I modified as per others' thoughts. I enclosed the frozen marshmallows in the dough without refrigerating it and then froze the balls. Also used 1/4 tsp cayenne which was perfect for my taste. Removed the frozen balls, rolled them in cinnamon sugar and baked maybe 11 or 12 minutes. Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have made these 2x this week. The 1st time, I read all the comments about dry, crumbly dough and followed the suggestion of one comment to add a 2nd egg. DO NOT DO THIS! You will end up with very sticky dough that is also weirdly crumbly. The 2nd time, I went on Vaughn's ride. The dough needs to be warmed in your hands after its fridge rest but it is then very pliable. The other comment I followed said to pull the cookies out as soon as mallow peeked thru. 8 mins in my oven. Perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious! I followed the advice of others to scoop the dough and assemble with (unfrozen) marshmallows right away and then chill before baking. These will go into my regular rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We use leftover Covid gloves or your hands get really messy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree with others that the cold dough was crumbly. I also couldn’t jam 5 marshmallows in. I warmed it up in my hands and it worked a lot better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I froze the mini marshmallows but they warmed as I inserted them in the refrigerated dough and melted during baking for a non-noteworthy result. Not like the photo at all. A better idea: insert chocolate chips instead, to boost the chocolate factor in a cookie that tasted more like ginger bread cookies than anything chocolatey. Too much time and effort for the result.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The finished cookie is chewy & has a nice finish with the cinnamon sugar.  Those cookies where the marshmallows did what they should look pretty.  However I found that 5 mini marshmallows was way too many to roll into the dough (given the 20-24 total, that's about 1 oz of dough). The cayenne kick is interesting, but distracting.  If I were to make these again, I'd make them smaller with fewer marshmallows & omit the pepper.  And as already noted, watch the cinnamon amount in the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are really tasty!  I adjusted according to so many notes here - 1/4 tsp cayenne (totally delivers the heat), not chilling the dough first (a little gooey by the last 3 cookies), 4 marshmallows, 35 grams (made 20). But I did chill the finished dough balls - but couldn’t wait more than an hour because I wanted to taste them. At the 11 min mark, no marshmallow peeking through. At 12 min, they looked just like the picture. Fun stuff!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful!  I would caution on adding more cayenne before trying this as is.  At first, we thought the recipe could use more heat; however, after they sat a day, the heat became more pronounced and IMO it is perfect.  I took others advice for not chilling the dough.  I had large marshmallows; so, cut the in quarters, thinking that I'd use 3 quarters; only 2 fit.  Next time I will just cut them in half.  The cookies are very large (perfect with coffee) for my typical Holiday-sized cookie trays.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are pure genius.  I have become pretty good at resisting eating the cookies I make during my annual holiday baking spree, but I couldn't hold back from eating these.  The heat from the cayenne is perfect.  I'm hooked!

I experimented a bit with cold vs warm dough and frozen vs thawed marshmallows (made a few batches), and it all seemed pretty forgiving, I would not stress about it!  I ended up having two bowls of marshmallows and swapped them out of the freezer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Firstly, if you accidentally put all 3 tsp cinnamon in your flour the cookies still turn out amazing! For that batch I rolled them in in sugar. I also made a batch with vegan mallows and butter. The non-veg marshmallows were gooier than the veg ones. Veg were delicious, but more grainy than gooey. Pretty sure that's due to the tapioca thickner vs gelatin. 
Also assembled room temp with frozen mallows and then froze, half-squished for 30 min. Baked frozen and they were AWESOME!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a frustrating recipe for me. The dough was crumbly both before and after chilling. No way I could “envelop” those marshmallows. I tried but the first batch fell to pieces so I pitched the rest. What a waste of time and ingredients.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I just say: watch the video! He’s hilarious! Made making the cookies even more fun & festive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing flavor and texture, but way too much cayenne pepper. The flavor of the cayenne lingers too. I will try the cookies again with 1/8 or 1/4 of a teaspoon. The process is a little cumbersome with the chilling of the balls and then the rolling up of the marshmallows, but the texture is delicious - especially with the sugar rolled on the outside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Dandie's vegetarian marshallows and they worked really well. They melted in the middle but didn't break through on all cookies. Still, the result is great. I wrapped 3 frozen Dandie mini marshmallows in the room temperature dough and then chilled them in the fridge over night. I set them out for 5 minutes, then rolled them in the cinnamon sugar mixture and baked them for 12 minutes. Hope this is helpful to everyone wondering about vegetarian marshmallows! :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are really good. I took the excellent advice to skip refrigerating the chocolate dough, and I simply molded it around the frozen mini-marshmallows. I then froze the balls. When ready to cook, I took them out of the freezer for about 10 minutes and rolled them in the sugar mix. I wasn't certain it would stick, as the balls were still rather firm, but it worked fine. 

I'd recommend not worrying too much about the marshmallow poking out. I think the ones I took out a bit earlier were nicer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was so excited to make these, and so disappointed! Dough was dry and crumbly, and fell apart when trying to envelop the marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Three strikes you are out NYTimes cookie week.  These were just weird.  No one liked them.  Followed precisely - no substitutions.  Too sticky sweet with the oddest aftertaste.  But the chef in the video is certainly yummy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these and they are really good.   I made the recipe exactly as written aside from one change others suggested (stuff the marshmallows while the dough is soft).    Came out great.   Making another huge batch for New Years Eve party.     Wouldn’t change anything else.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with my 5 year old granddaughter, who couldn't stand to wait two hours for the dough to cool, so we just made the cookies right away -- did freeze the marshmallows ahead but they thawed quickly.  We also eliminated the cayenne for the kids' sake.  The cookies came out fine and were a hit at our holiday gathering.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: OR, after assembling the cookie balls and marshmallows, pop in freezer for 10 min to chill the marshmallows!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The mini marshmallows I got didn’t quite fit 5 to a cookie, so we did 4. I recommend rolling small batches at a time, as it is really hard to keep the marshmallows frozen while rolling up the balls. I had to re-refrigerate the dough and re-freeze the marshmallows several times because they got too sticky or difficult to use when warmed up. I froze the marshmallows and thoroughly chilled the dough prior to forming the cookies, and froze them again before baking so they held their shape.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow these are good. I used 1-to-1 Bobs Red Mill GF flour and followed the advice of some to put the marshmallows in pre freeze. I also froze them for 2  hours and put them nearly directly into the oven for 13 minutes. They were so delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Replace 1/2 the cinnamon with cardamom for a much richer flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love this new twist on a chocolate cookie!  The flavor with cinnamon and ceyenne is amazing, and the marshmallows make them special for Christmas.  Word to the wise though - I made them to bring to relatives as part of a cookie box, but they're really delicate. The marshmellows in the center make them really inclined to break apart.  The only and obvious answer is to enjoy them at home...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My daughter and I made these yesterday for a party and they came out nice. We made them gluten free (with GF all purpose flour). We also added some nutmeg, cloves, and cardamom along with the cayenne and cinnamon. 
We made a batch and a half and the party guests devoured them quickly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Daughter and I made these yesterday for a party and they came out nice. We made them gluten free— with GF all purpose flour.  We also added some nutmeg, cloves, and cardamom along with the cayenne and cinnamon. 
Made a batch and a half— quickly devoured by party guests.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Daughter and I made these yesterday for a party and they came out nice. We made them gluten free— with GF all purpose flour.  We also added some nutmeg, cloves, and cardamom along with the cayenne and cinnamon. 
Made a batch and a half— quickly devoured by party guests.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious! Love the spice (it's just enough without being overpowering). The main change we made was that we could not fit 5 marshmallows into each cookie. We used 2 marshmallows and it was enough. Also, as others have said, it is easier to roll room temperature dough around the marshmallows, and then put them in the fridge while pre-heating the oven. If you put them in the fridge first, you have to wait for the dough to get soft again or it crumbles apart.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful recipe as written. I experimented a little and added sparkling sugar to the cinnamon sugar mix, as well as a little bit of cardamom. One batch, I added, when there was about a minute, left of cooking 4 to 5 marshmallows, and turned the boiler on until they were golden. Wonderful recipe as written. I experimented a little and added sparkling sugar to the cinnamon sugar mix, as well as a little bit of cardamom. One batch, I added, when there was about a minute, left of cooking 4 to 5 mar

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed directions exactly as written. Didn't have any problems. Then I tried it again using some of the suggestions about doing it all room temperature. The cookie was good but  just as the recipe said, the marshmallows melted into the dough. Either way, the cookies were chewy and gooey while warm. When they cooled they became dunking cookies because they got hard. They tasted wonderful though. And as far as chili, perfect.  I guess some people don't eat spicy food very often.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I LOVE THESE AND SO DOES EVERYONE ELSE.  I've made these 4x, 3x as I'm sharing below, and 1 time with allulose--which didn't quite work.  

I have an egg allergy, so I've never tried the recipe as written. But substituting 1/4 c applesauce for the egg works!  My other preferred modifications: 1/4 t cayanne + 1/2 t gochugaru (adds a wonderful depth of flavor); and 2 t cinnamon in the dough is better than the 1 t as written.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A neighbor gave me a couple of these. He seemed unsure if they were "worth the effort." I told him they definitely ARE! Excellent cookies! Perfect amount of heat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Question— is the brown sugar packed, as in most recipes?  Even packed I didn’t come any where near 305 grams!
Confusing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Suzy I also weighed the brown sugar and felt it was WAY more than 1.5 cups packed. It made a stiff, dry dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious! I made these gluten free for someone with Celiac and they turned out great. I also accidentally added all the cinnamon to the dough, but we enjoyed them just rolled in sugar, so I might just keep doing that when I make them again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent balance of flavors! I love spice and only used 1/4 tsp of cayenne pepper--I can't imagine using more. If you're making these for people who don't like heat, use 1/8 tsp.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don’t overcrowd them on the cookie sheet. Only bake one tray at a time and be sure to rotate. You want them to stay puffy; if they aren’t puffy it means the marshmallow has disintegrated, which, while still delicious, takes away from the surprise of biting into one. I took others’ advice and halved the cayenne (I would not do that again, we like spice) and made dough and marshmallows all at room temp, then hit the fridge for two hours. Far superior I expect to the hassle of freezing marshmallows

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These were fun to make! Something oddly satisfying about wrapping chocolate dough pancakes around mini marshmallows. (Though 3 seemed to be a manageable quantity).

The spices kicked in after a few moments. I'd be tempted to use half of the cayenne next time -- a little goes a long way!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didficut to work when the the dough was cold, too crumbly and it was nearly impossible to surround the marshmallows with any dough. I warmed the dough in my hands in order to make a ball shape, which defeated the purpose of chilled dough. The marshmallows became warm, but that didn’t affect their molten appearance. The cookies did not flatten out in the oven, so I flattened them with my hand. Measured precisely, total fell far short of 20-24. I love cayenne but 1 tsp was way too much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was swayed by one commenter's appeal to watch the video and do it per those instructions when I should have listened to the many other user notes that suggest taking the just made dough, wrapping in the frozen marshmallows, then freezing the whole shebang, and cooking after that point. I think next time I'll also cook them with the peekaboo side down. Delicious, but they were not going to win any beauty contests. Subbed 1/2 tsp chipotle for cayenne, and they had a wonderful warm subtext.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I didn't divide the cinnamon and it came out ok.

Cooking chocolate pastries is a little scary because it's hard to tell when it is cooked by appearance alone but the 12 minute mark was right on in my oven, right as they began to crack open and expose lightly bubbling marshmallow. They remained soft for a bit but firmed up nicely after about 15 minutes of cooling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yum! Not too sweet, nice kick. Followed the recs of other reviewers to roll in the marshmallows before chilling. Next time I’d roll in some chocolate chips with the marshmallows

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are extremely delicious! Soft, very chocolatey, and only a little spicy. Made exactly according to the recipe and they turned out beautiful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My neighbors are raving about these cookies. 2 Tbsp scoops yielded 21.

Definitely follow the suggestions to fill with marshmallows and shape into ball right after making the dough (without chilling). Room temp marshmallows are fine. It's easier to stretch the edges of the disks with the no-chill version, and you can repair tears with a dab of dough. Freeze then bake straight from frozen. 

I was raised proper, so I used 3/4 tsp Cayenne. Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These taste great - halved the cayenne because ours is super hot.  These came out better for us using 3-4 marshmallows rather than 5.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were so good!  They ended up chilling in my refrigerator for 36 hours before baking them.  I stuffed as many mini marshmallows in the cookie as I could and I’m so glad I did!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made a batch of these and I’m already certain there won’t be any left by the time Santa gets here tonight. Per commenters’ recommendations, I made marshmallow balls with the dough, chilled, then baked. I do wonder if anyone has tried this using a big marshmallow, maybe halved? It would have made the dough balling a little easier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Long time reader, first time commenter. These cookies are excellent and unique. I followed others’ suggestions to mix room-temp marshmallows into the just made dough and then freeze. When I was ready to bake I thawed the dough balls for 15 minutes, rolled the tops and sides ONLY in cinnamon sugar and baked for about 13 minutes rotating halfway. They turned out perfectly!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great flavor. Great nuance. Will try some of the assembly tips written about here next time I do them. Did not divide the cinnamon...wasn't a deal breaker but will as written next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For us, 5 minutes, turn the pan, and another 5 minutes was perfect for our oven.  We did frozen marshmallows in room temp dough then chilled in fridge, allowed to warm briefly before rolling in the sugar.  A few of the cookies had melted but still tasted amazing.  Great recipe-we did rounded 1/4tsp of cayenne and it was a perfect hint of spice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are absolutely delicious, but you 1000% have to use parchment paper or silpats or they will not stay pretty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I love these cookies!  

I have baked them several times. Step 3 - I decided not to refrigerate the scooped cookies. Instead I immediately put in the frozen marshmallows and baked them.  Must easier to mold the dough around the marshmallows. And great results!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Excellent and puffy with some changes. 
Used ancho chili powder, half amount. 
Added 1 tsp baking powder and extra flour. 
Skipped the dough chilling and freezing. 
Skipped the cinnamon sugar. 
Rolled with lightly floured hands. 
Baked for nine minutes just until cracked. 
Delicious!!!! I wish I could add a pic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 13 mins is perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Veganized it using plant based butter, egg replacer, & vegan mini marshmallows. Ran out of 1/2 the brown sugar so subbed 1/2 with white sugar & molasses. Still delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe and decided a better title for these cookies are chocolate marshmallow crumbles, because that's how they turned out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Flavor and chewiness are good but dough isn’t easy to form neatly into intact balls and nicely round cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I formed the balls right after mixing the dough, then froze them for a day before rolling in cinnamon sugar and baking (for 2 minutes longer). Came out perfect! I thought they were amazing as is but next time I’ll cut the cayenne in half because it was too much for my husband.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out amazing. Universal favorite of the 5 kinds I made this year. I did add one extra tsp of cinnamon, on purpose. I might try a bit less sugar next time but will definitely make again. The video was super helpful to understand what to do after the dough was made. After time in the fridge, I took the whole pan out (not just half). Scoops stayed cold enough to work with. My kids helped mold the dough around the marshmallows and roll in cinnamon sugar. So much fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Spicy, chocolatey, and chewy!  Next time I'll try the good suggestion of bakers like Melanie and branchop to chill after assemby and before coating in sugar.  I cut regular marshmallows with scissors before freezing.  The marshmallow pieces drifted apart as the cookies baked, resulting in 2 pools of marshmallow on most cookies, so the aesthetics varied a little.  Vaughn's suggestion to reshape while still hot is helpful.  My cayenne was several years old, so the 1/2 tsp worked.  Fun recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn is so adorable I'm sorry to say that these cookies were a total miss for me. The dough was dry and crumbly before refrigeration and became hard rocks that crumbled into pebbles on my counter once chilled.  I had to ditch the marshmallows and I did my best to assemble rounds of cookies to bake off and salvage the dough.  The underlying cocoa flavor turned out rich and pleasing but the dry crumbly dough made for a dry crumbly cookie. 1/2 tsp cayenne is WAY too much... and I like spicy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @KB I had the same issue. I'm also in Colorado. I was thinking that perhaps I didn't mix them enough? I used my handheld instead of my large mixer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I'm glad I read the comments and followed the "community tip" to put the frozen marshmallows in room temperature dough. Proceeding in this manner worked out very well, it was easy to roll them up. 

The recipe as followed made 20 cookies after some stretching (initially a little short but after halving some of the larger cookies and adding marshmallows to each "half" we had 20),  so the 100 mini-marshmallows I froze proved just right for the mix.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Also confirming that -
* 1/2 tsp cayenne is just too much.  I'm pro-spicy, but I also like to taste the other flavors.
* creating the dough-balls with the just-mixed dough is the way to go.
* amounts to be divided should say so in the list, although more cinnamon is fine here.
These show beautifully, but this is at least my third recipe for this flavor profile and I'm not sure I'll go through the multiple steps for this version again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Most popular cookie of this year's selection. Will definitely make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: SO DELICIOUS. My friend baked these over the holidays and I haven’t been able to stop thinking about them. A unique and show-stopping cookie, in my opinion. The one trouble I had was with the dough not sticking together as well as Vaughns when smashing them for the marshmallows. But, they still tasted phenomenal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought filling each with 5 marshmallows was too much trouble for not enough payoff. They're good, but I would not make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these per instructions except I froze the balls with marshmallows inside for 2-3 weeks before baking. Cookies got rave reviews when I took to the extended family Christmas party. One comment — my marshmallows melted and were not white and bubbly as shown in the photo. Did I bake too long?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: the suggested cayenne was too hot for my waspy family, had to cut it down to 1/8 tsp (although 1/2 was perfect for fellow hot sauce lovers). i rolled the dough w room temp marshmallows in cinnamon sugar before refrigerating for 2 hours and they turned out beautifully.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was THE cookie. I used 3/4 tsp cayenne for a double batch. I will use a full tsp for the next double batch. Just watch the time, as they go from almost perfect to the marshmallow being melted. They are good either way, but the visual is better with unmelted marshmallow showing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wish I had read the notes about not refrigerating the prepared dough; it was difficult to work with when cold. The finished cookies looked very good, but I found them to be slightly bland and they had a slight raw flour taste, which could have been an issue with my preparation. Improved the taste by adding Betty Crocker dark chocolate frosting to the tops of the cookies (which does unfortunately hide the nice look of the cookies).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made several batches. Like others have mentioned, the dough is pretty dry and tough to work with when adding the marshmallows. With my latest batch, I microwaved the butter to soften it and didn't use a room temperature egg. The dough ended up way creamier and was much easier to handle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 1/4 tsp. cayenne (scaredy cat), added 1 tsp. Espresso powder to the dry ingredients and 1/2 tsp. flaky salt to the cinnamon sugar. Used turbinado sugar instead of granulated just because. The dough was def finicky and I’m glad I watched the video (Vaughn!). Yield was 23 cookies: 1 oz dough and four mini marshmallows each. Geez does this place smell lovely right now!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these yesterday and my family and I loved them.  I did half the cayenne pepper and mistakenly put all of the cinnamon in the dry ingredients but the cookies were perfect and I wouldn’t  change a thing.  They are warm and spicy and yummy!  I will make them over & over!  I watched the video and made them accordingly.  Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Consistent size of the dough balls is really important. I lessened the cayenne - half a teaspoon and it was enough for kids.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rename these Schadenfreude Cookies. The only satisfaction one can achieve is relishing the experience of pleasure and joy that comes from contemplating the failure, pain, and humiliation of others attempting to make these cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use smaller 1 oz scoop. Only use 1.25t cayenne. Bake for about 23 minutes total for chewy cookie. Marshmallows will not brown.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Literally fell flat - completely my fault. So accustomed to cookie recipes using 2 sticks of butter that I didn't read this correctly. Very rich, brownie-like, but not as pictured. Maybe next time. If there is a next time. These were a little too messily labor intensive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
made these twice and would recommend a few things:
-only coat the top of the cookie in the cinnamon sugar, they get very hard and inedible if the whole thing is covered in caramelized sugar
-either don't wrap the marshmallows in the cookie or add more on top because they kinda dissolved into the cookie and disappeared
-this could vary, but I had to add almost another half stick of butter for the dough to come together, it was a crumbly mess without it and the extra butter did not affect the bake

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t find that 5 mini marshmallows fit so mostly used 3 which worked out fine

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mini marshmallows definitely need to be completely frozen before inserting into the cookie dough, as otherwise they melt during baking.  Still delicious,  but not as pretty. I used 3 per cookie, 5 were just too many.  I made the same mistake as others regarding the cinnamon,  3 tsps in the dough instead of 2, but I just omitted the cinnamon in the rolling sugar.  They are delicious and just get better with time--if they last that long.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe it was just the batch of cayenne in my spice cabinet but it was a bit overpowering in this recipe.  I cut the amount in half the second time around and it had a more appropriate subtlety to me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Unless you want your cookies to be absolutely hideous stick to 4 mini marshmallows per cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine turned out dry and never flattened out, yet were still a big hit with guests. Next time I’d skip the freezer business. The dough was really difficult to work with and it was very fiddly getting the marshmallows sealed into the hard dough. (I’d also skip freezing the mallows as they just touched out within five minutes of taking out of the freezer.) Also, be sure to reserve some of the cinnamon to roll the cookies in vs. using it all in the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did 225g of brown sugar, used 1/2 tsp of baking soda & 1/2 tsp of baking powder. I also skipped the cinnamon sugar since they were already sweet enough with the marshmallows. Great recipe! Define will make again! My friends loved them fresh out of the oven & still gooey, but I bet they’re good when they’re cool & smashed into ice cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I was obsessed with making these cookies then was disappointed with the texture of the dough having followed the recipe  exactly. I attributed the texture issue to using Whole Foods 365 light brown sugar which is way more grainy than C&H which I prefer. I also used Clover butter and I prefer Kerrygold. 

Now I see that many others had issues with crumbly texture and I’ll take those notes into account for next time. The flavors were so good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: these cookies are perfect.  a new holiday classic around our house.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were, by far, the most popular cookie I made this holiday season! I made these by freezing the dough balls and then baking later. The first time I baked them I cut the cayenne in half (I am a bit of a wuss with heat!), but the second time I did the full 1/2 tsp, and they were really perfect! Thanks, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am mystified by the bakers who have written that this recipe worked out well for them. I have tried every suggested version of freezing the marshmallows, freezing the stuffed dough balls, varying the baking time, etc. Every time, I end up with a cookie devoid of marshmallows; they melt away into a sticky residue.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The baking time was far too long for me (and I usually have the other issue with my oven).  You really need to take them out as soon as the marshmallow starts to show through the cookie--or even a bit before.  For me, that was a max of 10 minutes baking from frozen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the size adequate with a "heaping TBSP" of dough, made 25v per recipe.  Refrigerated for 30 minutes made them perfectly workable; without this they were gooey to work with.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don't chill, warm. Flatten the cookie a bit and put on a warm (not hot)parchment covered cookie sheet and let sit for a few minutes then flatten in your hand and add marshmallows and close up the top. Worked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This seems like a great idea, but it didn't really work.   After making them as directed, I tried a second time with a smaller cookie, using just 2 mini marshmallows, and about half the size.  That was better... but the balance of spices, flavor... just didn't work.  Maybe it's a question of the cocoa used?   One brother-in-law took a bite and then called these the "bad cookies" for the rest of Christmas...   :-/    

I really wanted it to work...  but it's a lot of work for a very mixed result.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is the absolutely best chocolate cookie dough I’ve ever made! 
Kudos to you Vaughn!
I’ll be making this part of my holiday cookie wish list!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A unique and interesting take on a chocolate cookie! Easy prep and instructions too. Everyone is always pleasantly surprised by the heat

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For perfect cookies at high altitude, cut back on the brown sugar by a third to reduce oven spread. Double the recipe and use tsp of fine sea salt if using salted butter. Add 4 Tbsp water, cut back vanilla to 1 Tbsp, cinnamon to 1 Tbsp and cayenne to .5 tsp. Refrigerate dough for 30 minutes, then portion out 36 Tbsp sized scoops, flatten and press 6 mini marshmallows between 2 scoops to make 18 cookies. Roll in cinnamon sugar and freeze on baking sheets 20 min, then bake 17 min at 350 degrees.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is the 4th cookie from cookie week.  It’s taken weeks for me to get to it, but they were worth the wait. I teach and school went remote today because of snowstorms. My husband and I made the cookies together. The cayenne makes you warm up fast! Perfect cookie for today.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful cookies—not overly sweet and perfect level of heat from the cayenne. I concur with others that it is best to form the cookies with marshmallows inserted before freezing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these once, mods for next time:

1. Less cayenne. My goodness. I love spicy food and loved Eric Kim's gochujang caramel cookies. This was too much.

2. Put frozen marshmallows in dough while dough is still pliable then chuck the whole lot in the freezer.

3. Divide cinnamon correctly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wish I had read the notes from our cooking friends. These cookies involved a lot of waiting, were not easy to assemble (as others said) and the taste just wasn’t that great. I will not be making these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookie holds up to a number of simplifications which are previously described. Freezing the dough in portions is not helpful unless the dough is being  made to store, bake in portions on demand. I also put caramel bits into my cookies and I could see adding choc chunks as well along with the marshmallow fragments. making it a Mexican Lava cookie. Will make again. Maybe a bit of molasses to further friendly up the dough and the flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree with others that it is best to form just-made dough around the unfrozen marshmallows, roll in the cinnamon sugar, and freeze the assembled balls for a few hours. For me, 10 minutes seemed to be the best bake time even after freezing…  after 12 min. the marshmallows started to disintegrate, so def. start with 10 min. and watch them in case your oven runs hot.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these cookies first using the giant marshmallows that are made for S'mores because I didn't have any mini marshmallows. Also cut down on the cayenne a bit and made them gluten-free. They were fabulous. Favorite cookie of all the Christmas cookies I made. Made the second half of the batch with mini marshmallows, and they turned out flattened, and a mess, like others have said. 
I would recommend using the bigger marshmallows, cut up, no need to freeze either.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Okay - what I did was chill the dough overnight just like I do with most cookie dough. Scooped them up directly from the tupperware, pressed the marshmallows in, and voila! Worked easily. 

Only thing is, I've made them twice now and I never have enough liquid to mix the ingredients properly and end up adding milk. Anyone else with this problem?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love that I can omit the cayenne or sub with garam masala and still have a delicious cookie! Also agree, make marshmallow stuffed balls first, then freeze!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I read the comments thoroughly and adjusted the assembly by creating the marshmallow-filled cookies and chilling them before baking. Unfortunately some of our marshmallows melted into the cookies before they were consumed, but they were still delicious. The kids didn't really appreciate the spice, but that's ok - more for the grownups!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved them! Glad I read all of the helpful comments before I attempted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were very good but i did struggle a little wrapping the marshmallows in the dough. I think that part is more trouble than it’s worth. Next time i might try just putting the marshmallows on too, maybe partway thru baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these last night and they are delicious! Do note that the cinnamon should be divided into 2 tsp for the dough and 1 tsp for rolling. I accidentally put all 3 tsp into the dough, but luckily we all like cinnamon so it isn't a problem. I do think the chocolate flavor would shine through more with the recipe as written. I followed the suggestions here to wait until folding the marshmallows in to refrigerate the dough and it worked like a charm!  Dough was easy to work at room temperature

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: O...M...GEE! These are mind-blowingly wonderful! I am grateful for others' suggestions to simply fill the just-mixed dough with marshmallows, rather than trying to fill the chilled dough with marshmallows, which was frustrating. I was only able to get 4 mini marshmallows in the tablespoon-sized scoop of dough, but that's no big deal. These things are absolutely glorious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best use of old, stale hard marshmallows!  Followed suggestions to not frig the dough but form the cookies around the hard bits of marshmallows, then froze them.  forgot to roll them in sugar! But sat them out while oven heated.  Great. and marshmallows stayed  gooey - like I said, best use of old, stale, hard marshmallows!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn't have a stand mixer so hand beat the butter and sugar and it worked fine. I don't think you need 305g of sugar - we used ~250g and was fine. Chopped in some dark and milk chocolate, which added melted puddles of chocolate into the mix. Used 45g cacao instead of cocoa, which added a more complex flavor. Froze individual dough balls with mini marshmallows integrated overnight ahead of baking, and avoided the cold dough issue mentioned by others. Yielded v fun and delicious cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I accidentally added all 3 teaspoons to the batter but still tasty! I would probably halve the cayenne in the future because it was a touch too spicy for me, but while I love spice in savory foods I’m not a huge fan of it in sweet stuff so ymmv. Will definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved this in concept, but didn’t love this recipe. It’s not very precise. Loved the flavors but I thought the chocolate cookie itself was meh. Going to try this again with a richer chocolate cookie and see if I have better luck.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was excited to try this new cookie with the 5 stars rating, I used Guittard cocoa and everything, followed directions to a T. I’m very underwhelmed and I don’t understand why anyone would give these 5 stars. I’m not throwing them out but I just don’t get it…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I wish NY Times allowed 1/2 start.  I thought this was better than a 4 (99% of Times recipes seem to be 4 star), but not quite a 5.....so a 4.5 for me!

I assembled 100%, then put all 24 balls on a baking sheet and covered.  Popped in the fridge for a few hrs.  took out 8 at a time and placed on parchment covered baking sheet.  at 11 minutes they looked exactly like the picture. I agree that refrigerating the dough first would have made assembly nearly impossible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the half teaspoon of cayenne is just nuts. I used 1/4 teaspoon or ground chipotle pepper instead. It lends a slightly smoky taste to the chocolate and the heat is just a mild hint at the end. I also substituted turbinado sugar (Sugar in the Raw) for the 50g of granulated sugar that is mixed with cinnamon which the dough is rolled in prior to baking. It makes for an attractive and tasty adjustment.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use brown sugar!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out a bit dry and hard to work with, so when reattempting, I would consider having a bit of milk on hand if the dough proves difficult.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I would agree with a lot of the others that the cold dough did become very crumbly and difficult to wrap around the marshmallows. Next time I make them the plan is to make the dough and freeze the marshmallows. Add the frozen marshmallows to the warm dough and bake immediately. I also found they needed about 2 minutes longer than it said. 
I made these for a party and they were incredibly popular!! I will say a handful of people thought they were too spicy so maybe keep that in mind.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s delicious but the marshmallows melted in my cookies

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had so much fun making these cookies. Took me back to my first experience with an easy bake oven watching with excitement as the cookies bloomed with creamy, gooey marshmallow lava. The flavor combination is perfection. This recipe is a KEEPER! 5*****

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really delicious cookies, absolutely recommend making these! I followed user comments and didn't chill the dough or freeze the marshmallows which worked really well. If the dough gets too sticky, just pop it in the fridge for a couple of minutes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Chilled dough is difficult to work with. Stuff them, shape them, then freeze. If I’m baking a casserole, I pop 6 cookies on a qtr sheet pan at the end. Delicious. For the cayenne, 1/8 tsp was perfect for our taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best cookies I've ever made.  I  followed the advice of most comments & rolled the cookies  into balls w/unfrozen marshmallows then refrigerated for 2 hours & baked.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought this was a fussy recipe for the taste it yields.  I had leftover regular marshmallows so I cut them in 8ths and froze them.  I used 4 pieces per cookie in the first half of the batch.  Some came out pretty messy.  So in the 2nd half I only used 3 pieces per cookie and after each ball was made I put it in the fridge until all were ready.  The 2nd batch came out better.  I won't make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I had a yield of 18 cookies.  
I froze the marshmallows but did not freeze the dough before scooping and shaping the balls with marshmallows inside.
Froze cookies for about 1 hour before baking for 10 minutes.
I would reduce the cayenne pepper by half next time and add a bit of sea salt or flake salt to the top of each cookie before it goes in the oven.  
We're a Morton Kosher Salt household so I cut the kosher salt by half which was fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a hit at holiday gatherings and with friends and family. (I dropped some off for friends who were recovering from COVID, and they loved them so much that they were eating a quarter of a cookie at a time to make them last.) I read the comments and formed the cookies with room temperature dough wrapped around frozen marshmallows. I used the vegan marshmallows from Whole Foods but could find only the regular size, so cut them in half with kitchen shears.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Omg!  So delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My teen just made these for the second time - I am not a cookie person, not a fan of marshmallows, not the one in the family who seeks out spice. And I am in LOVE with these. I wasn't paying attention to the preparation, so I don't even know if they followed the recipe exactly. But what I am eating is so lovely! Definitely going to be asking for these for my birthday. Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree with others that is it hard to get a consistent shape and keep those frozen marshmallows from melting to smithereens. Nice flavor though, so may try again. I think the key is to assemble them and then freeze and put them directly in the oven from the freezer. For our part, I added almost twice the cayenne, and still felt that they could use more. My kids agree! What can I say, we like things on the spicy side. And don’t underestimate the difference that high quality cinnamon can make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Next time I will freeze the balls before baking, like it suggests in the "tip". I went as fast as I could and put them in the fridge, but they spread out way too much as they baked. I even put the marshmallows in the deep freeze to freeze them, but they thawed so quickly. They tasted good, but I was missing the gooey marshmallow middle. I also think the suggestion someone made about leaving the bottom without the sugar/cinnamon mix would help with sticking. I will use my silpat next time, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a hit however I was afraid of too much cayenne so I reduced the amount by half…and people wanted more heat! I did not freeze the marshmallows first either. Like others, after making the dough, i formed the balls and stuffed with marshmallows. I refrigerated until set overnight and froze the balls to bake as needed. They came out perfect. I’m making these again the same way but with the full amount of cayenne.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vegan marshmallows worked perfectly for by batch. Like others, I also assembled the cookies and frozen marshmallows before refrigerating for two hours (instead of refrigerating the dough before assembly). They turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are excellent! Next time I will make sure the mini marshmallows are totally frozen, because some of them did melt into the cookie, They still give the center a great chewy texture. The roll in cinnamon sugar adds great texture to the outer cookie. I didn't refrigerate the dough and it was just the right consistency to wrap around the marshmallows. I will definitely make these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think I have a larger dough scoop, and it turned out to make only 16 large cookies. I was making them to take to the office, and my family was BITTER that I didn't have enough for them. Making them again and will shoot for a smaller size with only three encased marshmallows. They certainly are delicious! I used  Ghirardelli 100% Dutch processed cocoa powder, and the chocolatey chewiness was rapturous!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Temperatures solved! Use a cold-pack from the freezer. Refrigerate dough in metal bowl with scoop for 20-30 minutes. Marshmallows at room temp. Before scooping dough rest fingers on cold-pack for 10 seconds (each cookie). With cold fingers, place dough on fingertips, not in the palm, flatten, add marshmallows, pinch closed and roll into a ball. Easy peasy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ok Xmas in July after a weather power loss event in my chest freezer-frozen dough and marshmallows fine like in May but texture changed in July-freezer had heavy ice buildup- and I couldn’t get it to hold around marshmallows that were cut up -plopped dough on top of marshmallow on parchment and baked 350 12 minutes flavor fine and used crumbled up with strawberries-so dough freezes ok use mini marshmallow i don’t think it has to be frozen if dough is cold partially defrosted

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Adult cookies FTW! added a handful of chocolate chips as I didn't have marshmallows and scaled brown sugar down to 240g. Amazing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I gave these to my 4 and 7 year old. I followed the recipe as is. Way too spicy for my 4 year old but my 7 year didn’t mind it. Overall, I would not make these again mostly because I strangely found them to be slightly dry vs sweet and chew.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I only chilled the dough for an hour and a half, so the assembly process was much easier for me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I read some of the comments before making these. I made the dough and put the marshmallows in the room temp dough, rolled into balls and put the dough in the freezer for 1 hour. Took them out, and let sit at room temp for 10 minutes. Rolled in cinnamon sugar and they came out pretty good! They spread quite a bit, I would space them further apart. Dough was tricky to work with next time I will chill dough for about 20 minutes before wrapping it around the marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used King Arthur gluten free flour successfully, 1/4 t cayenne and 33 gram balls of dough weighed on Wirecutter recommended Escali scale. Flattened them in non-dominant hand and wrapped around 1/2 large marshmallow to make a ball. Froze on a sheet pan. Placed frozen balls into a freezer container and returned to freezer. When ready to bake remove from freezer, bring to room temp, roll in cinnamon sugar combo and bake. Check for doneness at 10 min. I can’t handle spicy but think I’d like the recipe’s recommendation of 1/2 t of cayenne. Next time. They’re fun to make and the best cookies ever!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A note to British bakers! Our marshmallows will melt in this recipe (even if you freeze overnight!), something about not having the right stabilising ingredient, so seek out US produced if you want the distinct marshmallow look. I’ve made twice with melting mallows and still incredibly delicious *chefs kiss*

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dividing the dough into 2-tablespoon mounds yields me 13 cookies, nowhere near the 20-24 cookies the recipe indicates. No matter, they all get eaten (and quickly)!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didnt have mini marshmellows so tried to cut larger ones into 6 and freeze the chunks.  My marshmellow part came out bubbly/foamy and wonder if that is why.  Delicious anyway.  I also did not put dough in fridge- worked pretty well but the prep made a mess of my hands. That was fine.  My cookies were too big.  Anyway absolutely delicious and next time will watch the size and use real minimarshmellows.  Also used only a pinch of cayenne- still came through.  An exceptional cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: First batch came apart while baking and looked ugly.  For the second batch, I pressed them down before baking and made sure to seal any cracks that developed and they came out much better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out DELICIOUS.  Used half the cayenne per other reviews and rolled frozen marshmallows around unchilled dough.  (Messy, but when it got too soft, I gave the dough 5 mins in the fridge to firm up.) Unfortunately then I popped the whole tray into the freezer before baking and they melted before they baked.  Next time I’ll bake straight away.  And there will definitely be a next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: very good!! someone else suggested assembling them at room temp and then freezing. i did that overnight and baked them the next day and they turned out well. the marshmallow didn’t stick out like in the photos for all of them so maybe next time i’ll leave a little vent. i like the heat but half it if you don’t want too much

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I thought they were really good.
To those who get a crumbly mixture, you can always add a tablespoon or two of cold water to help a cookie dough mixture set. 
I do this often if I end up with medium eggs in my fridge. The mixture will often come out crumbly if your egg isn't large enough but 2 tablespoons of water and the cookies come out perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thank you to all who suggested filling the cookies prior to freezing and to those who recommended decreasing the cayenne. Worked like a dream! These cookies were divine and I loved the hint of heat. Will definitely make again. Ty, NYT, for another stellar recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these and the chilled dough is tricky to wrap around the marshmallows.  Mine turned out quite chewy and many had flattened during baking but they were certainly delicious.  If I were to make them again, I would wrap the cookies, chill, roll, and bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do not miss this video!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: these are not only delicious, but were much easier to make than you might imagine from reading the instructions.  The chilled batter was very pliable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazing! I made them with vegan marshmallows, so they didn't melt as much as regular ones, but nevertheless, they tasted and looked sublime!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these. I love them.  They remind me of the Seattle chocolate bar Mexican Hot Chocolate flavor.  They definitely have a spicy kick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Vaughn is my spirit animal. I love him. The GOAT. 

Cookies came out yummy; maybe not as pretty, and slightly thicker than video shown, but tasty and crispy. Recommend 3/8ths tsp cayenne, making slightly less cinnamon sugar—we had a lot left over. Used 1 tablespoon to scoop cookies, made slightly more than 24 (26? idk we ate them the moment they came out so we didn't get a good count). 

Vaughn Vreeland, you go girl.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The marshmallows distract from how good the dough. I think I’ll leave them out next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so delicious!! I doubled the recipe, weighed out 2 ounce balls, flattened them and wrapped the dough around 10-12 mini marshmallows each. Froze the whole tray of dough overnight, then baked accordingly. They were wonderful!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A little disappointed in the flavor.. would definitely cut the cayenne in future. The marshmallow seemed to evaporate in many of the cookies even though they were sealed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Following the recommendations of others, I also froze the marshmallows overnight, then folded them into the just-mixed, room-temperature dough, then immediately popped them in the oven. It was challenging to work quickly as the marshmallows were rapidly defrosting. I also found I could only squeeze 3-4 marshmallows per dough ball (I ended up with exactly 24 cookies). Even then, the cookies seemed to lack structural integrity and have flattened quite a bit. A few of them have marshmallows that melted more than I was hoping for. That being said, they still taste wonderful! I also used only 1/4 tsp of cayenne given I was bringing these to a cookie swap and wanted to play it safe with the spice-level. There’s enough of a kick to feel a tingle on the tongue without being overwhelming. I’d definitely make these again but would probably try out another dough-prep method, like freezing the dough with the marshmallows and baking from frozen

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were just ok. Kind of a pain to make with the wrapping around marshmallows. And I found the flavor not that interesting or chocolatey.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I spent hours on this and it was too spicy!
Now im crying

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out spectacular.  The spice was just right for us . I almost cut the cayenne in half after reading the reviews, but if I did that, the spice would be undetectable. To each his own spice tolerances though! I followed the recipe to the letter . Chilled just under 2 hrs and baked for 10 minutes. My first batch was picture perfect, but my second batch, some of the marshmallows melted into the cookie. No bother, they tasted so good. I didn't mind that a few  had no visible marshmallow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Straight up excellent. No changes needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tasty but definitely not worth the time. After baking two time-consuming pans, I scooped the remaining dough in 1 T portions, rolled in sugar and baked without the marshmallows. They tasted great, just like the ones I fiddled with and look like oversize gingersnaps. The results of the marshmallow version were not attractive enough to make the effort worth it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good! I had no trouble with the dough.  Refrigerated the balls for 1 hour.  Removed 5 at a time. Flattened them on a cookie sheet with my hand.  It was easy to pull the sides up over the marshmallows and even out cracks as the dough was very pliable at that point.  My only disappointment, I couldn't taste the marshmallows.  That could have been because I accidentally added all 3 tsp. of cinnamon to the dough.  This made the cookie nice and spicy but might have overpowered the marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The dough was really easy, but I have a very high spice tolerance, and so does my family, so I put in 1 and 1/2 teaspoons of cayenne. There wasn’t enough marshmallow, as I had a hard time folding the dough over, but I only put in 4. I also put in some cayenne in the cin

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made my dough portions too small at 24 balls - I would make less cookies next time to have an easier time with the marshmallow filling part. I think bigger portion of dough:marshmallows would help with the round shape too. These are chewy and crisp and chocolatey and ugh, I could eat the whole damn tray. Vaughn! How did you have enough left for the cookie boxes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: took advice of someone else and wrapped the marshmallow into the cookie right after  making the dough and did not refrigerate.  Just DELICIOUS. I found 10 min total with a rotation in the middle was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This video is everything. Great instruction and I giggled throughout. Love it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Personally really enjoyed the level of spice, others did not! So if you're serving to anyone who is less spicy tolerant, I'd consider lessening the amount of cayenne

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To flatten the cold dough easily, use a tortilla press. Place the dough between saran wrap or a plastic bag so it doesn't stick to the press.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very yummy - Went with 2/3 of sugar, 1/2 tsp of cinnamon and 1/2 tsp of cardamom. Difficult to get marshmallows cold enough but so good. Doug loved.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the cookies too large so I don't think there was enough marshmallow. The family says they are good by I'm taking some to a party. Should I melt and drizzle more marshmallows on them???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies turned out pretty and looked like the picture.  I used a size #30 cookie scoop to get 24 cookies.  I also quickly used the bottom of a 1 cup measuring cup to flatten out the chilled dough balls and then wrapped 5 frozen marshmallows. The less time the chilled dough is spent in your warm hands, the better.  I omitted the cayenne because of kids.  Even with my mess up of adding all the cinnamon, the cookies were delightful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to add that the chilled dough was smooth, pliable and not sticky. You can use the bottom of a cup to really flatten out the dough ball and it was a breeze to wrap the frozen marshmallows (mine was overnight for convenience). If you have dry and crumbly dough, you may have added too much flour or you didn’t beat the ingredients well enough. If using measuring cups, spoon the flour into the cup and then level with a knife.   I personally use a scale to weigh everything.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Correction- I used a #40 scoop. I just prepped a double batch of dough for holiday cookie boxes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m at a loss as to how this makes 20-24 cookies, I tried doing a cookie scoop and only got 14. This time I’m using a single Tblspn and managed 20. Also, I agree with the comments below - no need to refrigerate before adding the marshmallows. The cold dough is crumbly and hard to work with. Otherwise this recipe is delicious

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: All this work. The struggle to encapsulate those marshmallows into the stiff dough drove me nuts, but it looked so easy in the video.  There are so many other choices.  Now I have 99% of the marshmallows left to stuff in my mouth when I'm having a bad moment.  Oh well, I still love the NYT recipes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I split the recipe (holding out the spices in the dough) and made half Mexican spiced flavor and the other half vanilla bean flavor. Only 3 mini marshmallows can fit in a cookie reasonably. Makes sure you seal it up completely or melted marshmallow will leak out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought it wouldn’t make enough cookies so I made the scoops of cookies smaller to have more than 10 cookies for my party of 30… but then was hell to wrap around the marsh mallow

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I swapped traditional marshmallows with vegan ones. The vegan marshmallows worked perfectly and didn't overpower the cookies with sweetness. I used King's artisanal flour and had no issues stuffing the cookies and rolling them into a ball after the two hour refrigeration process.  I swapped the 1/4 cayenne with 1/2 teaspoon for a slight kick at the end of the bite.  May add a little more next time as the baking seemed to neutralize the kick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you quantify the amount of mini marshmallows needed? Is it a whole bag? A cup? Handful?Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mo the recipe ended up making 16 cookies for me with 5 marshmallows each—was maybe 1/4 of a 10 oz bag for me

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not a whole bag. I doubled the recipe and used maybe half a bag.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and only needed about half a bag.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I accidentally froze these cookies without reading the note at the bottom, should I be okay baking them if I already rolled them in the cinnamon sugar or should I try again?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use two topmost racks. From frozen go 13 minutes. I baked for 15 and they were flat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I shared an initial comment but I want to come back and comment again to say I am not a fan of these. I don’t think the cayenne works.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Now this was fun! Last night at 11:00 p.m. I was trying to wrap chocolate dough around tiny marshmallows! I just have to say it was well worth the minor added effort. These cookies are rich and zingyI may use a touch less cayenne next time but my friends enjoyed them. Great with coffee or hot chocolate or just on their own.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Overall these are dope. 

I followed the bake after freezing method outlined at the end of the recipe and they were a bit puffy/somewhat unlike what was pictured.

Next time I bake, I’m gonna do a smidge less cheyenne and will bake right away.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were delicious.  The combination of the chocolate with the marshmallows, so good. I refrigerated them overnight. They were easy to assemble the next day. Like the other commentators, I would cut the cayenne in half, as it was a bit spicy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A very fussy cookie to make.  I think the relative difficulty of forming the dough around the marshmallows has to do with the moisture content.  I live in Colorado, so I added a tsp of water to the dough.  It still crumbled, so I did my best mashing the dough around the marshmallows.   If you rotate the cookie sheets halfway through, don't give up at what looks like a lumpy mess.  It all bakes out in the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really loved these cookies! The texture was perfect and I found them to be even better the second and third day. As others have noted, they were a little tough to shape after chilling, so next time I will probably skip the chilling step and bake them right after mixing. Will definitely make these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Careful with the spice levels. I love spicy things but they were way too spicy for my young kids and even a real kick to me. Other comment is it was hard to portion the marshmallow inside the cookie - but perhaps not essential to get it right.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed recipe to a tee and they came out delicious and not too spicy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not sure I've ever had a better cookie! I'll be making these year after year. I will double the cayenne and try with less cinnamon. Outrageously yummy cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Myra don’t double the cayenne! I accidently did that and the cookies are too spicy to eat. And I love spicy food. Just not spicy cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed instructions, reduced cayenne to a dash. Rolled room temp dough around mini marshmallows then froze overnight. Remove while oven preheats. Dip tops in cinnamon sugar. Bake 15 min, let cool on pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I hate baking, but I am obsessed with this recipe! a lot of american baking recipes can't be easily adapted to what a standard german grocery store offers, but this worked out perfectly. our vegetarian marshmallows however did not melt as beautifully as the standard ones. still insanely delicious and so, so easy. we streamlined the recipe a little bit by omitting the freezing steps in between and only had one proper session in the fridge before baking. new fav nontraditional holiday cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When you freeze the mini marshmallows should you lay them out flat ?  Do you have a hard time getting them apart after freezing?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Deborah R. 

 Do not clump together when frozen, you don’t need to spread them flat. I froze in a small bowl uncovered

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are fun to shape if you are relaxed and do yield beautifully different individual results. The flavor is complex.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wah!  I can’t even get them to stay in balls before I refridgeraye them. What can I add to make this a bit more moist so it will hold together?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Try weighing the flour and cocoa powder instead of scooping? Sounds like the dough is too dry. I would only add butter or egg (already in the recipe) to try and correct the ratio, so you're not messing with the recipe too much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't know what I did wrong, but not only was the cold dough (warming up for about 30 minutes) difficult to flatten, but very difficult to fold the edges over the marshmallows AND form into a ball (split and cracked all over).  There was nothing molten about them.  And yes, too much cayenne overpowering the chocolate. I wouldn't be able to take these to a party. Next time, I'll make without the marshmallows, flattened, no cayenne, and sprinkled with sugar or sea salt. Expensive project.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Gave up… the dough fell apart and wouldn’t cover the marshmallows…no matter what I tried…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are incredible! 1/2 t. cayenne gives them quite a kick, so if you're making these for spice-sensitive friends adjust accordingly. I also added 2 t. espresso powder to deepen the chocolate flavor. I was worried by how dry the dough seemed, but everything turned out just like the photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
If you accidently double the cayenne these make you breathe fire. Obviously first batch was ruined with that misstep, but a great cookie when your measurements are correct. 

I agree with forming the dough and stuffing with marshmallows at that time. Get you the best shaped cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My son really wanted to make these cookies and they seemed fun and yummy! We were excited to taste them and of course making them is always fun, but I have to unfortunately say that these cookies were not very good at all. The spice overwhelmed the chocolate flavor to the point where they were almost inedible. Normally we don’t mind spice but you could not even taste any other ingredients. I cannot understand how these have a five star rating, but if you like to eat spoonfuls of cayenne pepper these are the cookie for you.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I halved (1/4t) the cayenne and it was plenty spicy for me (I'm a wimp), which dissipated nicely.  You could even try 1/8t.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Note: there are different kinds of cinnamon. If you can, get the cinnamon from a Mexican market. You’ll get a more authentic flavor. I also get cinnamon from our Indian market to use in those recipes. They all smell and taste like cinnamon, but definitely differences. Same goes for oregano!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Suggest weighing out to 50 grams per cookie. Needs more like 10-15 minutes to come to malleable temp to work with if dough has rested in fridge overnight. Really lovely, if not hard to make consistent) results, with a welcome kick!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My dough was incredibly dry, like sand. Ended up adding an extra 4 oz of melted butter to make it come together. 2 or 3 oz might have been enough. Baked for 2 - 4 extra minutes to compensate for the extra butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Pafa mine also came out super dry. I did accidentally add all 3 teaspoons of cinnamon into the dough but it was so crumbly, I added a few teaspoons of cream to get it to come together. I haven’t baked them yet so we’ll see…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They were good👍

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are some of my favorite cookies I’ve made. They came out beautifully. I cooked most on AirBake cookie sheets but one of my batches was on a non-insulated aluminum pan. That batch got hotter than the others and the marshmallow melted too much. The others were picture-perfect. I made these exactly as instructed, but I like people’s suggestion to add marshmallows and form the cookies at room temp, then freeze them before baking. They were fairly time consuming but oh-so worth it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for some of my students and because I have Muslim students, I subbed vegan marshmallows.  They held up so much better than normal marshmallows!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them the first time with half teaspoon cayenne and that is WAY too much! Overpowers the chocolate, cinnamon. If I make them again with cayenne, I'd use 1/4 to see how that goes. I love the texture of the cookie and the marshmallows! Will be making them again without the cayenne as an excellent hot chocolate cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Found these a little underwhelming, just chocolat-y and sweet, not as interesting as I expected them to be based on the spices. Baking from frozen (after thawing for about 10 minutes), I had to up the baking time to 17 minutes. I filled them with a larger piece of vegan marshmallow which apparently don't melt as easily so they did not flatten out as much as.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To edit: everybody in my family loved them so clearly I am alone with my 'underwhelmedness'

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn is hilarious - what fun to cook with him! Ty

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are SO GOOD! Following the advice of many other reviewers, I skipped the fridge step, and they turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Help. Why did the marshmallow completely disappear into the cookie when I baked them? No beautiful streams of marshmallow. The cookies came out looking like regular chocolate cookies. I first read through the different techniques for stuffing and freezing. I decided to pack frozen minis into room temp dough then froze the marshmallow stuffed balls for a day. I thawed for 10 minutes before baking. Perhaps I didn’t thaw enough before baking? Tasty but no wow factor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  Only thing was that the dough was a little crumbly so had to wet my hands when rolling into ball with the marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: you need to actually put 5 marshmallows in each for it to marshmallow pull properly but super yummy either way!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Asian markets sell these marshmallows that are between mini and full sized that are perfect for this recipe. One bag has a few more than one per cookie which is all that is needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Omitted cayenne. These are fantastic.  I made the dough ahead, portioned and chilled 24 scoops,then had my nieces over, and we marshmallowed, sugared, and baked them together.  The dough is a little fiddly until it warms a bit, but not a big deal  We refrigerated the balls for ten minutes or so before baking.  They came out just like Vaughn’s.  I thought the marshmallows might be fun for kids but too much for me, but they transform in baking to something awesome.  These are REALLY good cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used white chocolate or white chocolate meringue instead of marshmellows for the flavour. The consistency of the "stuffing" is quite different. The white chocolate is crunchy, the meringue (aquafaba, sugar, baking soda, lemon juice, white chocolate - raw or frozen in spoonfuls) is soft and spongy. I also did not roll the cookies in sugar. They tasted amazing nonetheless. I made three batches in less than a week, none lasted more than two days. The whole family loves the recipe, spice and all!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: From the same dough, the cookies I made with higher quality mini-marshmallows came out with much better structure and had more visible marshmallow form. The cheaper marshmallows melted into the dough and almost disappeared. That said, this is a super recipe and my family loved the cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Half the amount of cayenne

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These were great. Based on suggestion from a friend, I cut the cayenne in half and it's perfect that way. I had a very hard time with the dough and getting five marshmallows into it until I realized I'd used a 1T cookie scoop instead of 2T. I had already adjusted down to 4 mini marshmallows which was working, when I figured that out. I think though that I would keep it that way going forward as it made a nice sized cookie. 
I had to keep the timing down to 10 minutes in order to keep them soft.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just like a cup of hot chocolate, these are definitely improved (though already delicious to begin with) by adding whipped cream on top. Better yet, pop a can of whipped cream at the table and spritz with each bite. The addition was festive and helped cut the spice of the cayenne for my deeply Midwestern family. I second the suggestions about shaping your cookies/adding the marshmallows before chilling the balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these exactly as directed, minus the cayenne. They were well-loved and delicious. I think it's easier to just freeze the marshmallows before making the dough, then make the balls before chilling the dough, stick in the frozen marshmallows and then refreeze the dough balls, removing what you need from the freezer and rolling in cinnamon sugar mix and baking. That way you have fresh cookies right when you need them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  I did have to wet my hands with water to roll the cookies into balls as the dough was a little crumbly.  Still 5 stars!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Skipped the cayenne for my kids but used Saigon Cinnamon for a little more kick. The addition of the marshmallows was a bit fussy- might skip them next time but the gooey texture was fun in the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used Sasha's method of adding marshmallows to the dough before freezing, not after, and they turned out great. I did however only need to bake them for just 8 minutes instead of 10-12.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As others have said, rolling the marshmellows into the dough before chilling and them came out great. I treated them like a making a pupusa (cookie dough is the masa, and the marshmallow is the filling).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Whoa! This video and recipe are the bomb! People who complained about the refrigeration part and assembled at once, then refrigerated? I don’t get it—followed instructuoms and they came out just as he said!!! Plus he is adorable!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Took the advice to assemble and then chill. Didn’t have freezer room, although I had frozen the marshmallow I found it thawed so fast I’m not sure it mattered. Also it’s now clear you want to cover the marshmallow entirely. They break through but don’t leak if you do. Love the flavor profile. Excellent cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m a pretty good cook, but I’m intimidated by baking. All went well with this recipe until I added the cocoa powder mixture to the mixer bowl. I put a towel over the top like he suggested in the video, but lost at least a quarter of the Dry ingredients, as they flew all over my kitchen. I haven’t cooked the cookies yet and I’m hoping they come out OK but I thought I would give you all a good laugh.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cut the cayenne pepper in half.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Everything was great except that my son made the cookies too big (we ended up with only 16!) and they were too spicy. If we made them again we would cut way back on the cayenne, by half or more.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Many suggested halving the cayenne, but I used the full amount and am glad that I did. This little kick was the best part of the cookie. I  found the texture of the cookie to be too grainy/dry. I did follow others' suggestion to form room temp dough around the marshmallow and then freeze the entire thing. Even the room temp dough was crumbly and did not stick to itself around the marshmallows. It took a ton of time to try to shape these for a so so end result. Won't be making again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really confusing directions: "Remove half of the dough from the refrigerator and let sit at room temperature for 5 minutes if the dough is very stiff. Take a mound of dough and flatten slightly in the palm of your hand. Pile 5 frozen mini marshmallows on top of the flattened dough, then bring the outer edges over the marshmallows to envelop them. Roll into a ball and then roll in the cinnamon sugar to coat..." Are we talking about the ball, or a portion of it, and if a portion, how large?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is very dry. I recommend doubling the butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They were easy to make after I read all the comments on rolling marshmallows into the dough balls before freezing.  I am disappointed in the flavor.  I like the cayenne but overall not that great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! There was a nice kick from the cayenne - noticeable but not obtrusive. I used big marshmallows and cut them into quarters (nine big marshmallows in total), which worked fine even though they did defrost quickly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is such a spicy cookie- not a fan. Looks pretty but the heat was too much. Not my cup of tea! Sorry. I don’t quite get the 5 stars. I do think it makes more sense to chill the cookie after stuffing with marshmallows since it didn’t want to stay rolled and closed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good, but I do agree with another review about cutting the cayenne in half.  The half tsp has a bit too much heat. I used the exact method as the recipe describes and they came out delicious.  A different twist!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
