commentBody: These cookies are delicious. I used salted butter and cut back on salt added to 3/4 tsp. I did not use marshmallows but the cookies were pure chocolate heaven. Definitely on repeat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious, but the dough and marshmallows were really difficult to work with. I'll experiment more next time to make the process easier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree with this approach shared earlier-/portion out the just-mixed dough and form it around the frozen marshmallows & I even rolled the dough in the cinnamon sugar prior to freezing and they turned out beautifully & delicious.  I added 4-5’ mon of baking time straight from the freezer.  As an aside, I decreased the sugar throughout by 20% and it was great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved this. I added some guajillo for a little extra spice and chile flavor. I also added some maldon sea salt on top for some yummy saltiness to cut the heat. I threw in some cardamom and ground cloves just for fun. Came out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used all 3 teaspoons of cinnamon in the batter and I cut the cayenne to a quarter teaspoon. I did not roll the cookies in sugar and cinnamon before baking. I used regular size marshmallows cut in half and then frozen overnight. I scooped a 25 g of batter into my palm, pressed a marshmallow into it, and set it on parchment covered cookie sheet, then refrigerated that tray while I made the second. I then molded the cooled dough around the marshmallows so they’re looked like little dumplings and put them back in the fridge for half an hour. Finally I baked them for 10 minutes and they came out with marshmallow white and cookies baked through. Another batch that I let sit out for five minutes before putting in the oven came out with overly melted marshmallows, so I would skip that step in the future. Made 25.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best of the best unique cookies! But DO NOT use VEGAN marshmallows. accidentally used them and it messes up the texture and spread of the cookies (still tastes good tho).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Used 260g of sugar for dough. Demerara sugar for rolling.
Wrapped marshmallow in dough before chilling. Gochugaru for heat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for a Mexican food themed potluck at work. They turned out really good! The flavor was excellent. I used about 3/4 of the recommended amount of cayenne and it was plenty for my taste. I formed the dough over the marshmallows at room temp then chilled in freezer for about 40 minutes before rolling in sugar and baking. The cookies spread nicely and had a gooey middle with crunchy exterior. Most of the cookies did not have the white marshmallow peeking through. However, I noticed that the cookies that had been sitting out on the counter for a few minutes before baking (rather than going to the oven straight from the freezer) had more of the marshmallow peeking through and looked more like the photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these last year and I’m going to be include them in my holiday cookie gift boxes this year - they’re so good! Side note to NYT Cooking - I’ve made several of Vaughn Vreeland’s recipes and they’re always a knockout, plus he’s so fun to watch on video. Keep up the great work!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Extremely tasty and I would happily bake the dough on its own as an elevated chocolate crinkle cookie. The marshmallows disappeared from a lot of ours, though this may be the consequence of baking the entire cookie from refrigerated, if the marshmallows got too hot? Forming the dough at room temperature around frozen marshmallows was very easy, however. On the whole they spread and turned out very thin. The ones where the marshmallows persisted are really wonderful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies taste fantastic and have a great chewy texture. But they are a real pain to make. I read a lot of the comments and tried both using the dough at room temperature and chilled according to the recipe. Both were crumbly and time consuming to form. I finally added a couple tsp of water to the dough and this helped. But as much as I liked eating them I don’t feel like the payoff is worth the effort to work with this unforgiving cookie dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the first batch from refrigerated dough wrapped around frozen marshmallows, just like the recipe said. Baked for 10 minutes and they really flattened out. Tasted amazing! But so thin and unattractive. So I read your comments and now assembled and froze the rest. I will bake them tomorrow morning. Fingers crossed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lisa M. How did it turn out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I accidentally added all the cinnamon to the recipe, wrapped dough around an entire large marshmallow, and didn't roll in sugar.  Still turned out great!  The spice is an unexpected surprise to a very sweet cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a little fussy but worth the effort. Mostly the issue was  folding the marshmallow's into the dough. After reading the notes and trying several methods what worked best was dampening my hands with a little water and working with room temp dough, flattening dough, adding marshmallows rolling dough into a ball - then freezing. Dampening the hands keeps the dough from sticking to them - voila no sticking easy to roll. Halved cayenne because of kids, loved the subtle kick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Taste delicious! As others have said- the cinnamon is divided!! Mine were very spicy and although not ruined, should be noted in ingredients to set aside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fun process of making them — and delicious. Thank you, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious! Because I was taking them to a large group, I  only used a 1/4 tsp of cayenne pepper, and that was plenty. You could definitely taste it, tiny bit of kick, but more than that would probably not be welcome to a general crowd, in my opinion. My marshmallows, even though they were frozen, mostly melted into the dough. Still delicious, just not as pretty. I think I baked for 11 minutes, but also was using an oven cooling from a prior higher temperature. My bad. Will make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavor of these were really good, but they were quite putzy to make and the mini-marshmallows just melted away and did not retain any "marshmellowness".  They were nothing like the picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My approach was to portion dough into one ounce pieces, roll into balls, refrigerate.  Take out about eight balls at a time.  Roll each one between parchment into rough circle.  Add frozen marshmallows, roll into a ball, enclosing marshmallows, throw back into fridge.  Do all the rolling at once.  Then take out a dozen balls, roll into sugar mixture, bake.  
Nancy Silverton has a similar recipe that includes dulce de leche, but her instructions for shaping left a big sticky mess.  Next time I’ll add a bit of dulce de leche to the marshmallows in this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jean Terranova I have the same question. I’ll report back if I try.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Liked these but only okay. The flavor is great and the look cool but not as exciting as I had hoped.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @branchop it was the same for me on the bites. I like heat but in cookies? It wasn't fun. I'm going to make them again today and try it with the 1/4 teaspoon of cayenne.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do these freeze well as dough or baked cookies? If so I'll make extra!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sid the cookies freeze great

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The dough, prepped at room temperature around frozen marshmallows, freezes beautifully. Bake straight from the freezer for 14 min.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be made without the marshmallows?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used a 1/4 tsp of ancho chili. Not a good choice--there was a kind of...meaty...flavor to the cookies. I'll use a 1/4 tsp of cayenne next time. 
And I added all 3 tsp of cinnamon to the dough--despite having read the warning in several comments--because I spaced out. The extra was just fine.
I dusted a few with more cinnamon sugar and a bit of chipotle, and that was nice--gave the cookies a whiff of campfire S'mores.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a double batch of these for a Christmas party today. I used 3/4 the amount of cayenne but stuck to the recipe otherwise. They’re delicious with the perfect amount of heat! I should’ve read the instructions more closely l, though, because I definitely left them in the oven for too long, so when they rested on the cookie sheet, the marshmallows melted too much. When I realized my mistake, I baked them for about 10 to 11 minutes and when they started to puff, took them out of the oven and let them rest for five minutes on the cookie sheet. They were perfect and just like the photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I only had room for 3 marshmallows/cookie. They turned out great still. 

My cookies flattened quite a bit. The handling of the dough required to add the marshmallows *after* chilling caused the dough to soften a lot. Next time I will make the dough balls, add the marshmallows, and then chill.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made half with cayenne and half without. Both versions are delicious! The cayenne adds just a little heat at the end, but probably too much for little ones if sharing with kids.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wish I had read Jess Bees note before assembling!  Good thing I like cinnamon!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I baked all 24 together, why would you cook one pan and then the other?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made these twice and they have become my husband’s favorite!  Agree with many of the comments about the dough - I shaped room temperature dough around frozen marshmallows then let them sit in the fridge until I was ready to bake.  That said, my cookies seem to come out quite flat.  Not sure what I’m doing wrong - maybe overheating the butter and sugar?  Thanks for any tips and happy baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have made these cookies time and time again. They are now my favorite cookie. When I give people cookie boxes, these are the ones that disappear first.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is it possible to make these cookies gluten free? Any recommendations on what would need to change?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes, Bob's Red Mill 1 to 1 Baking Flour is the best GF flour we (a chef and a baker) have used. It's a straight substitute for regular flour. GF flour typically works well in chocolate recipes. Let us know how it turns out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely great recipe, and really easy, as commented by others, if you form cookie balls with just-made dough without freezing the marshmallows.  I froze the cookie balls, defrosted them for 12 mins before rolling in sugar and baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were amazing. Everyone’s new fav.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious but require your sustained attention! At 10 minutes I thought the cookies needed just a little longer and went to do another baking task. When I returned the marshmallow had melted and the cookies were very thin and spread into each other. The attractive ones will be gifted and the less presentable will be saved for our home stash. Amazing flavour though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to try these for Christmas. How spicy are they?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you serve these days after you make them? Or are they not as good if they are not hot

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a huge hit at a party.  Some folks though the spice level was a bit much, but most people liked it, including me!  I watched the video--which is always a big help in seeing the entire process.  I took the advice of someone else in these comments and didn't freeze the marshmallows separately, but rather put the cookies together at room temperature and then froze them overnight. The next morning, I baked them for about 13 minutes.  So good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were pretty spectacular cookies.  I used a 1 TBSP cookie scoop with only 3 mini marshmallows.  Formed the balls at room temperature and then froze everything overnight.  I dipped them 1/2 in green sugar and 1/2 in red sugar, for an extra festive look.  They were tricky to bake.  I recommend keeping them in the freezer until ready to bake, rotating the pan 1/2 way so that the cookies bake evenly, and resting for 5 min before trying to remove them from the pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: my daughter made these for a cookie contest at her dad’s office. don’t know if they won yet but they are incredibly delicious. we used mini peppermint marshmallows bc that’s all we could find and the wind chill here is 0 degrees so no second grocery stores this time. gave a bare hint of peppermint which was fine given it’s christmas time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing cookie. Have made a few process changes that I think help. Wear food prep gloves and put marshmallows in cookie dough right after mixing dough - no need to chill dough or freeze marshmallows at this point. Then chill assembled balls for an hour or so. Then roll them in the cinnamon sugar and bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a fiddly recipe, as someone else said. I followed as written, except that I made the “all cinnamon in the dough” mistake many others seem to have made. They tasted great, anyway. The dough did crack when trying to wrap the mini-marshmallows, but if I squished them with my whole hand around them, I could more or less get them wrapped up. Five marshmallows are hard to fit; I ended up mainly doing four. If I do this again I would try the room temp wrap at the beginning then freezing method. Made 20 cookies. Some looked like the picture, some were a bit more exploded. On the whole, looked alright.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I probably wouldn’t make these again because I found them so-so, but if I did, I would cut the spice in half, add chocolate chips or chunks, and not bother with getting the marshmallows inside the balls. It was a lot of effort, and after some experimentation I discovered they looked better if I simply put the marshmallow on top of a flattened cookie and then folded up the edges a tad.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: this is one of my favourite cookie recipes. earlier this year i went through a phase where i would make these once a week lol. i like mine quite spicy so i quadruple the amount of cayenne and it's perfect

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these per the recipe except a little less cayenne. Fantastic but very laborious to shape with the marshmallows. I made them again , omitted the marshmallows, and added 100 grams each white chocolate and semisweet chips. Formed into 22 gram balls and baked for ten minutes. We liked these even more!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I enrobed the marshmallows in the dough at room temp (for easier handing), then froze the balls.  I let the balls sit for 10 min or so, then baked.  I don't know if I measured something wrong or it's my oven, but they baked very flat, and the marshmallows partially melted, making the cookies very, very difficult to get off the wax paper. They taste delicious and I love the chewy texture, but considering the amount of effort, I will not be making these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As other commenters have noted it’s basically impossible to put marshmallows in the center of the frozen dough - the dough cracks and it’s a mess. I gave up after doing 11 of them. The cookie is delicious but too heavy on the cayenne, it overpowers the cinnamon IMO. I probably won’t make again because of the effort required but would suggest others A) halve the cayenne and B) skip the freeze before putting marshmallows in the center.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love the flavor of these! Though when I make them they NEVER look like the picture. The marshmallows kind of float to the top and look like toasted and the cookie is puffier. Would love some insight into why this may be!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @VAUGHNVREELAND Is there another way to prepare the dough for this cookie? It's just a bear to work with - whether chilled as directed, or straight from the mixer. I do find it slightly easier to wrap the dough around the marshmallows and then chill/freeze before rolling in sugar and baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We opted for regular ol' hot chocolate cookies - just omitted all cinnamon and the cayenne.  So good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great! Flavor was on point, but if you’re not the biggest spice fan I would omit the cayenne or use 1/4 tsp instead. You can definitely taste the 1/2 tsp immediately but to me it’s nice warmth in your mouth. My best batch was only baked for 8 minutes, turning the pan after 4 minutes. It was very easy to over bake these cookies which causes the marshmallows to melt fully into/out of the cookies. Keep a close eye and take them out when you can still see white marshmallows poking through. Overall a fun holiday cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are excellent cookies. The dough is a bit dry and tough to work with, but I wet my hands and had an easier time. These were a hit in my house and it was hard to stop eating them. I will be making a double batch next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Second year, making these. I use a one and a half ounce cookie scoop, I put three mini marshmallows inside the cookie. I baked the cookies for 10 minutes. I find it easier to use the dough when it is a bit softer as other people have noted. After I have the cookie prepared for the oven, I chill them ( on my back porch). It all worked out well even though the cookies were smaller.
 even though they are a smaller cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Did the same w the 3 tsp in the dough! But nbd…
Also made the mistake of refrigerating w/o covering so dough dried out and when adding the mallows dough kept breaking apart… didn’t come together when baking but still tasty… arguably rookie error but be warned!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After reading through many comments and tweaks, I settled on 1/4 tsp cayenne instead of 1/2 tsp - that amount of warming was perfect.  I also deviated from the original instructions by portioning all dough, then flattening, filling with the frozen marshmallows, then refrigerating the balls overnight. Prior to baking I dabbed water on the balls, rolled them in the cinnamon sugar, then baked. It took 5 more minutes in baking time, but the result was a beautiful sugared crinkle top. Tasty recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these yesterday and they are fantastic!
They have a lovely depth of flavours that your classic cookie doesn’t have, they are really easy to work with and came out delicious. Mine did come out flatter than in the recipe photos, but it’s an issue I’ve had before with my oven, so I’m sure next time I can tweak it to have it come out perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m a bit slow, so I had to bake these half a dozen at a time or my marshmallows thawed too much and the cookie came out delicious but without the fluffy white veining.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @ mayno I froze heaping teaspoons of marshmallow cream until hard, and used that. Turned out much better than when I made these with vegetarian marshmallows. They just don't melt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wasn’t very happy with these. I had frozen my marshmallows overnight but then followed other commenters’ suggestions to just insert them right after rolling and then freeze the entire thing before baking. They still spread considerably to the point of being thin and almost crunchy, and the marshmallows melted entirely. They still tasted ok, but were not pretty or chewy. I wonder if it had to do with my higher altitude.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the cookies to be very dry and I followed the recipe exactly. I did have help from many small hands though! Any suggestions to avoid dry cookies? Not usually a problem for me. I used vegan marshmallows, easy to find at my grocery stores now.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Shannon I had the same problem with the dough measuring ingredients by weight. I did use plant butter and it usually has more moisture than butter. If you mean the final cookie, then they’re over baked. They need to come out when they’re soft. There’s quite a few minutes between that point and being obviously overcooked.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Did anyone try adding a bit of coffee powder? Or flaky salt to the cinnamon sugar mix?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: DELICIOUS!!!  Favorite cookies of the season and a great, spicy addition to a cookie plate.  We made the recipe twice—the second time we halved the size of the cookies as we wanted more, smaller cookies, and it worked well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Keep it simple! Skip the refrigeration and freezing and just wrap the dough around a few dried up marshmallows. Those little rocks soften right up during the bake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found that it was very hard to get the dough to come together with a hand mixer. I won’t make them again if I don’t buy a stand mixer, just not worth the work!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Charlotte I agree that it was difficult with a hand mixer! Next time I make these I’ll use my stand mixer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think this is a perfect cookie! no notes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I finally just made these! I did not find the refrigerated cookie dough difficult to work with! I found the opposite: the last cookie was very crumbly and when I attempted to bring it together with my hands, it totally didn’t work so I put the scooped cookie dough directly into the fridge after covering. 
Not a huge marshmallow fan so I halved the amount of them and in most of the cookies, the marshmallows just dissolved into the cookie. Not mad about that!!!
These are DELICIOUS and unique!!!! 5/5 stars. I did halve the cayenne as well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: OMG - delish! I used 1/2tsp of cayenne and it was a little too much. My cookies spread quite4 a bit, so next time I will refrigerate them again after stuffing them with marshmallows

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these, following the advice of the reader who suggested

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious! I halved the amount of cayenne and slightly increased the cinnamon (maybe 1/2 tsp more) and the spice level was perfect. My butter wasn’t soft enough so the dough was a little dry when mixing, so I added approx 1.5 tbsp vegetable oil which didn’t seem to affect the flavor or quality (could have used milk too). I formed the dough into balls at room temp with room temp marshmallows before freezing overnight. When baking the next day, I rolled some in cinnamon sugar and tried some without — both were delicious! The ones rolled in cinnamon sugar had more of a cinnamon flavor of course. The marshmallow mostly melted into the cookie but a small amount was visible which made the cookies visually pleasing and it added an awesome texture to the cookie regardless. Excellent with a cup of coffee or milk! Definitely adding these to my cookie baking rotation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I accidentally added all 3 tsp of cinnamon in my dough. But it still turned out delicious! So I used a total of 4 tsps (that includes the cinnamon for the rolling sugar).

What I did:
- I used a 2 tbsp cookie scoop
- Left them inside the fridge (not freezer) overnight
- Let it thaw for a few minutes (3-5 minutes)
- Flattened and placed the mini marshmallows that were left in the freezer in the middle of the dough
- Folded the dough to envelope the mini marshmallows
- First batch of cookies were baked for 10.5 minutes, second batch for 12 minutes.
First batch: Less cracks
Second batch: More cracks and marshmallows (some) seeped out 


I like the texture of the second batch. But both iterations were equally good. 10/10 would make again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these at altitude (~6000 ft) so it could be because of that, but my cookies completely spread in flat pancakes. Even when I turned the temp up by 40 degrees and shortened bake time, these were very flat. (I made a couple cookies day of, and the rest after freezing the dough for 24 hours, and got the same results). 

Tastes great though! I also used a little less cayenne and these are wonderful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these as suggested by many bakers.
l left all ingredients as they were, no substituting except for the marshmallows. I used the vegetarian ones (fish gelatine)
I prepared the dough balls ahead of time, flattening them, then added the 24 hr frozen marshmallows, but the marshmallows did not stay frozen during the process of assembling. No worries, I rolled them up and froze the cookies as such. 
I took them out of the freezer 15 min before cook time, I rolled them in cinnamon sugar and baked them to perfection!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tip: I’m lazy . I like skipping steps too much - I scoop 2oz balls, stuff in 3-5 frozen mini marshmallows , close them up , then roll them in the coarse sugar & cocoa nib coating from Claire Saffitz’s Yule log cookie recipe. Roll them after they’re stuffed, load up a sheet, gently press & then refrigerate for an hour or so. The textural contrast of the coating puts this already awesome cookie recipe completely over the top. Thank you Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The butter and brown sugar did not get fluffy, it flattened against the walls and bottom of the stand mixer bowl. What did I do wrong? Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Didn't work as expected.

Divided the batch in three, and tried it with room temp dough, frozen marshmallows and frozen dough and marshmallows. Every way the marshmallow melted. 

Good taste, but marshmallows didn't work for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cayenne overpowered the delicate vanilla and cinnamon flavors. They basically turned into cayenne chocolate cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be frozen after baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Sasha’s idea to stuff the marshmallows into and form the balls first and then freeze is excellent. The dough accumulates on your hands and becomes sticky and difficult to roll and press. Cheat: I formed cookie dough pancakes pressed onto a paper towel and then pressed the marshmallows into the dough, then peeled that off of the paper towel and then formed the balls.  Avoided rinsing and drying my hands every 5 cookies.  

Fantastic recipe, and I am not a cookie person at all.  TY NYT Cooking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Robb Roberts I wear gloves when I roll these out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies are delicious even without the marshmallows...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried adding fruit to this recipe? If so, what fruit? Was considering trying dried mango

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Vegan version 
-direct substitute butter with lurpak plant based, butter brought to simmer and spices added to enhance flavour
- substitute egg with orgran egg replacer (2tsp: 4tbsp warmed water)
- vegan chocolate chips in centre rather than marshmallows 
YUM

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
It may be because I baked these from frozen (following the Tip), but my cookies didn't flatten in the oven, and ended up being thick and fudgy balls with hollow centers. The centers, where the marshmallows were, hollowed out, since the marshmallows collapsed and left space behind. For the second batch I baked, I squashed the balls with a spatula a few minutes into baking, and the shape was a bit better. 
The dominant flavor in these is cinnamon rather than chocolate, and they are very sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When i was baking, there was no need to freeze the marshmallows. it actually was much more enjoyable when it was fresh, not frozen. The cookie gained unique textures- chewy, crunchy, sticky, i could go on and on. The marshmallow baked fully and it felt like you eere chewing something sticky, but also fluffy. The best cookie ever!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would dehydrating my marshmallows first do the same job as the freezer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is one of my favorite and most requested cookie recipes. A few tweaks: I use 3 tsp of cinnamon in the batter and an additional tsp to roll in. Did this by accident and they were so good I see no reason to correct. Sometimes I remember to add a little espresso powder to deepen the chocolate flavor. I cut the cayenne to 1/4 tsp and that meets the palettes of more people, including kids. I always roll them with marshmallows and sugar before chilling - dramatically easier. I generally make them in 30 g balls but have also made 20 g minis with 3 marshmallows to great effect. I’ve also added a few dark chocolate chips when I roll them and so far no one has complained.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you're using a glass pie plate, taking it  from the freezer into a preheated oven isn't a good idea. I've been making a similar chocolate cheese pie for fifty years and I've never pre-baked the crust. It's a go-to recipe for a quick dessert and nobody has ever cried "soggy crust."

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One only needs one shattered glass pie plate, complete with unbaked pie, in one's oven to remember that lesson forever.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: And even if it's better pre-baked, this recipe calls for crust to spend a total of an hour and 10 minutes total in the oven! I'm going to try pre-baking it for just 15 minutes, as French tart recipes often dictate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add half a cup of buttermilk or sour cream and it the salty acidity will cut the sweetness nicely and lighten the thick custardy filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s unfathomable to me that this has only 4 stars. I cooked exactly as directed, including the homemade pie crust. I used the directed amount of ingredients (someone here had issues with not wanting to use all the water it called for and then saw the crust shrink…) i had no issues. In fact, this was arguably the best pie I’ve ever had.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Divine.  Period.  You'll wish you'd made two.  One for the guests and one to eat hidden in a closet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add 1 cup of pecans before you bake the pie. It takes it to a whole new level.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wouldn't call it a chess pie without buttermilk.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Chess pie needs something acidic to balance out the sweetness.  Buttermilk works, but isn't essential - vinegar is often used. Here, chocolate and cocoa powder lend acidity.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @TraceyF I have never heard of adding buttermilk to chess pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Laurie H Buttermilk is mentioned in the introduction of this recipe indicating that in classic chess pie buttermilk is used.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you want a homemade look, thaw a frozen store bought pie crust and roll it out on a floured surface to make it a little larger. Put it in your own pie plate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I bake mini-pies for my husband one at a time, so your mileage may vary, but I had to cut way down on the time. I always do, of course, but this time, it was because of the crust, not the filling.

After the first 30 minutes, the crust was past where I would have expected on the finished pie. I wrapped every bit of exposed crust on the filled pie with a double layer of foil, and it turned out ok in the end.

PS: I think that autocorrect changed “par-baking” to “parables.”

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is great with 3  Tbsp makers mark and 2 tap vanilla plus agree with buttermilk addition. Delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Needed a quick dessert for unexpected company.  Made this, poured it into a pre-made graham cracker crust, baked it 30 minutes; guests raved about how good it was.  So rich you sure don't need more than a very small piece.

I'm sure it would be a big hit in a home made crust but when time is short, . . .

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The filling is delicious. Rather than making the pie, I used a half recipe of the filling (spiked with rum) to make a bar cookie. I baked a shortbread crust pressed into a small pan lined with parchment. When the crust had cooled a bit, I spread it with a thin layer of apricot jam, then poured the filling on top and baked until set. They're great, but for Thanksgiving I think I'll bake the pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very creative. Love new ideas to try---thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Because I make mine into small (4”) pies, I was able to try out several of the suggestions I read here:

1) Graham cracker crust: Oh, yes! Worked great, and saved enough time that cloverleaf rolls are back on the TG menu.
2) Adding a bit of buttermilk/cream/crème fraiche: Improved the flavor a bit, at the cost of a slightly looser texture.
3) Adding pecans on top: Two thumbs way up! They toast as the pie bakes and puffs up around them. Less sweet than typical chocolate pecan pie. Thank you, CJ!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I've made this pie twice this past week, once exactly as the recipe stated and once incorporating suggestions from the comments to cut down on the blind baking time and add 1/2 cup of buttermilk to the filling.

I give the recipe 5-stars exactly as written. 4-stars with the modifications. Still a very good pie, but less deliciously like gooey brownie in a beautiful crust than the unmodified recipe. If I make it again, I will make it exactly as the good folks at NYTimes cooking recommend.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yeah...there are some issues with the cooking time for this recipe.  I've been making pies for 50+ years now and have never seen a recipe with this length of cooking time...needs some attention to the language here...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you're using a store-bought frozen pie crust and heat it at 425 degrees for 30 minutes, you will end up with a piece of charcoal. Follow the directions on the packaging.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Count me among those who say it's not a chess pie without buttermilk.  I was also raised to believe that a chess pie has cornmeal in it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My filling to crust ratio was way off, but it smells and looks delicious so we’ll just have to mind the gap w whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It's all great, but for some reason I need to make half again as much filling in order to fill my crust...which was precisely a 9" pie crust.  Did anyone else encounter this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Karenyes I do 1.5 of the recipe and add 7 minutes cooking time for this exact reason

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wouldn’t call it chess pie without cornmeal, although it does sound tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
No, no, no. This is not a chess pie. It’s a brownie in a pie crust with three times the effort & no better flavor than a boxed brownie mix. I am very disappointed. Children would love this pie. Grown-ups not so much.
I should have known though, without buttermilk, it’s not a chess pie at all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've never pre-baked a crust for a chocolate chess pie and I've made it for 15 years using a store bought crust. I think you can bake this all in one go, which is why it is so easy. Yes, the top will crack. No biggie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this yesterday and after seeing the end result and re-reviewing the ingredients, it’s basically a brownie in a pie crust. Still very good, but just wanted to share this observation. Maybe others already knew this would be the outcome? Note: I have never had a true “chess pie”, so I can’t compare to that at all…

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this the day after Christmas 2023, mostly to end the misery of a leftover Pillsbury pie crust languishing in the vegetable drawer and because chocolate. Did a 15min pre bake on the crust, added 1 C chopped Texas pecans to the filling but prepared all else as otherwise written. Served to a clutch of friends who all stated they weren’t that into chocolate and who then proceeded to gobble up their servings, eating seconds and taking the rest of the pie home afterwards. Smashing, absolutely delicious, will make again and again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made some modifications and it turned out incredible. I turned it into a tart and used a Ritz cracker crumb crust. The richness of the chocolate works well with the thinner slice that the tart shape offers and the saltiness of the Ritz crackers was a great balance. I also added o tablespoon of espresso powder and 1/4 C of buttermilk. I was worried the tart would be a little one-note without them and can happily report that the tart had a complex and full-bodied flavor with their inclusion.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @dana cohen I’m going with your modifications, Dana.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this pie a few days ago with a store-bought crust.  Put the crust in the pie plate straight from the package, added filling (used dark chocolate chocolate), baked.  Easy peasy and tastes wonderful.  May try graham cracker crust next time.  There definitely will be a next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good god. This was fantastic AND easy. I would have liked a bit more direction on when it's done, but I pulled it from the oven when the the whole pie shook in its shell, rather than just the center. That worked, and it was cooked through. It had a perfect clean cut too, which is always nice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good! Basically a brownie in pie form but delicious. Made with All Butter Pie Crust and a weird flour so the results were...interesting but filling was great and much enjoyed, especially with ice cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This simple, delicious recipe disappeared first from the Thanksgiving dessert table. I used the darker end of semisweet, a 65% cocoa chocolate, and I recommend sifting the cocoa powder before adding it to the mix in step 7. Freshly whipped cream infused with a liqueur or dark liquor pairs fantastically with this chocolate pie. Thank you, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made two of these. Used premade pie shell because I’m not a great crust maker. I agree that the lengthy prebaking of the crust is unnecessary. Otherwise the pie was super easy to make and got demolished at thanksgiving dinner. I made a second one with pecans in the bottom and that was a hit too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ooh that’s a nice alteration. Did the pecans rise during the bake?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe I use comes from a 1951 small town NC woman's club cookbook.  It uses 1 oz of baking chocolate, 1 cup sugar, 1 stick of butter, 2 eggs, teaspoon of vanilla.  It cannot be beat!  Hard to imagine using 4 ounces of chocolate plus cocoa.  Whoa!  You can bake the crust but it also works out great without baking the crust.   25-30 min at 350.  Simplify, simplify, simplify!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I read this recipe out of curiosity as I always make Bill Neal’s version. He comments ( and my Mississippi grandmother would have agreed) that chocolate chess pie never includes cornmeal. He also doesn’t use buttermilk, but does use two tablespoons of heavy cream. This isn’t lemon chess pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Use a *shallow* pie pan. The volume of filling is quite modest.
Also - yes to a bit of buttermilk. Yes to less baking the crust (I blind bake at 375 for 20 min, then uncover and bake an additional 5 or so, so the crust is just starting to brown. Also, there's no need to cool the crust before adding the filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe with a premade graham cracker crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I haven't but I bet it would be tasty! Go for it and report back!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same question, was reading through the recipe and comments thinking about the chocolate/graham combo!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would the recipe be successful with a graham cracker crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: YES, it was great.  So easy.  I'm into EASY!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't think it's a proper chess pie without cornmeal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why is the subtitle called “buttermilk chess pie” if it has no buttermilk in it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think that the author was just referring to the traditional chess pie that they were linking to in the description of this variation?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: after reading above recipe I'm left wondering if the final baking (step 8) is to be done at the mentioned 350 degrees or the initial 425 degrees?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wondering the same thing! Crossing my fingers and assuming 350.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you add 2 tablespoons of corn meal for authentic chess pie?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Will this be just as good if made a day ahead?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I want to add buttermilk or yogurt…how much?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What temp do you bake the pie?  350?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found the filling lacking for a 9 inch pie plate, an 8 inch would be more visually pleasing. I have several inches of crust with no filling on the sides of the pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is just delicious and very easy. Really recommended. I used a store-bought graham-cracker crust and  followed the recipe exactly. I bet a chocolate crust would be great too, maybe whipped cream with a little coffee syrup in it.  This pie looks impressive and kids and adults love it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this for Thanksgiving. It was divine! Following advice by others here, I did the following: 1) I use Melissa Clark's crust recipe and blind-baking instructions for her Brandied Pumpkin Pie (NYT Cooking). It's always perfect, and has a shorter blind-bake time than the one here.
2) I added 2 tbs whiskey, a scant half cup sour cream, and covered the top with pecan halves (but used none inside the custard). 
This all added up to a big hit with the chocolate lovers at T-day dinner.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am a solid bread maker and cook, but a beginner pie maker, and this recipe was incredibly easy to follow with my skillset! Turned out perfectly. I used Kenjis pie dough recipe which was equally as easy to follow, but substituted spiced rum for the vodka which lent excellent flavor. The salt from the crust also gave an excellent contrast to the sweet fudge filling, reminiscent of salted caramel. I made some whipped cream with lemon zest to dollop on top-divine! 5 stars for sure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had to buy a crust, not the end of the world. Filling is amazing. Not quite a brownie but fully baked…great…..try it you will be happy you did……also make the whipped cream. Wonderful recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just plain delicious! My daughter had made some bourbon whipped cream which was the perfect topper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I took this for thanksgiving for a dessert for one of the attendees who is lactose intolerant. So I used cashew butter, dairy free chocolate, and Oatmeal whipped cream. It was delicious. Next time i will make it the regular way but just wanted people to know it will work for those who cannot tolerate milk products.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and it was a huge hit. I made the filling as directed but didn't follow the crust instructions. I used a pre-made crust (from ALDI! of all places). And I didn't have the time for all those steps. No freezing, not 30 minute bake and no pie weights. I did a 15 minute blind bake at the temp recommended on the package. Then I let it cool as I made the filling. It was delicious and I've been asked to make it again for Christmas.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious pie, a hit at holiday dinner. Used a metal pie plate, placing in the freezer did help keep the shape. Pre-baked the crust at 425 for 15 min, which was more than sufficient. Another great tip found here was using coffee filters to hold the pie weights, this worked better than prior attempts with foil or parchment. Definitely recommend adding 1/2 cup of buttermilk to the filling. Final bake at 350 about 30-35 min until puffy. Yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used a roll-out premade crust in a tin pie pan set directly on the oven rack. The baking sheet was on the rack below to catch possible drips. I did not prebake the crust and all was well. (I prebake crusts in glass or ceramic pie pans, but tin doesn't need prebaling in in my experience.) 
 I used bittersweet chips, (personal preference) and black cocoa as well as a quarter cup of buttermilk as suggested in other comments. Good densely chocolate pie, unlike pudding pies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Terrifically good! Followed the recipe exactly and added a tablespoon of bourbon as Vaughn suggested. My store-bought ready-made crust was a tad small (only 7 inches) so I put the extra filling in a ramekin and baked it alongside the pie. Both versions lick-the-spoon DELICIOUS.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this in a tart pan with a removable bottom. I used a Pillsbury Pie Crust (the one you get near the butter in your supermarket). Simply made the filling as direct, poured it into the crust in the tart pan and baked for 30 minutes at 350. It came out perfectly (no pre-baking the crust) and looked very elegant as a "chocolate tart". Served with whipped cream. Got rave reviews and so easy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe this isn’t a real chess pie. Maybe this is a brownie in a pie crust. I don’t really care because it’s delicious. Made as is other then only pre baking shell for 20 min at 400.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Way too much salt.  I’ll add 1/4 teaspoon next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I pre-baked a frozen crust for 13 minutes- this was plenty. This is really good, but much better after an overnight in the fridge. It is some marvelous juncture between pudding pie and brownie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I underbaked it a little on accident and it almost tasted like molten chocolate cake. The crust recipe I used wasn’t sweet, so I might make a different crust next time with a bit of sugar in it or maybe even a cookie crust so the flavors pair a little better. Either way, this was a hit and I’ll be making it again! Don’t skip whipped cream or vanilla ice cream on the side!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My inside came out a bit gooey, which I gather is the preferred texture. How do you store this pie if you don’t eat it all at once? On the counter or in the refrigerator?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I blind baked my own pie crust as instructed and my pie shields protected it from getting overdone. Super flaky and delicious. This chocolate pie was a HIT! It's so rich and creamy I wanted to eat the whole thing with a fork

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Due to some last-minute scrambling, I ended up using a store-bought Diamond pecan pie crust (skipping steps 1-5) and substituted Hershey's Special Dark chocolate chips for the semi-sweet chocolate. The pie turned out very fudgy and rich, which went over well with the chocolate lovers at Thanksgiving dinner.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it on request for a friend and she, like all of us, loved it! Easy to make and utterly delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is the filled pie baked at 350 degrees?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you bake the filled pie at 350 as well?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So, the pie crust bakes, in total, over an hour when all is said and done?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I had the same concern, and my crust nearly burned during the par-baking period, even being careful. I was careful to put it in the freezer for 15 minutes beforehand, but I par-baked for 30 minutes, and 25 total would have been better.

BTW, Ken Haedrich in Pie Academy recommends a coffee filter rather than foil to hold the weights, which breathes better (in the caption under one photo). It also blots up any excess fat that leaks out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My chess pie calls for chopped figs and chopped walnuts/or pecans.  I'll add it to this recipe.  Can't hurt!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Going to add pumpkin to this for T Day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there any reason you couldn’t use a grahm cracker crust for this custard?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It seems like a really long time to prebake a pie crust! I’m going to wait to make it till someone who has actually made the recipe tell us how it turns out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you just add the buttermilk if you are altering?   Does it change the consistency or baking time much?  Thank you

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would like to make this without a crust, maybe in ramekins or just in a pie pan with no crust. Any thoughts on the best way to do it or how I might need to adjust ingredients, preparation, cooking time, or temperature?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone turned this into tiny two bite pielets? If so, please share any tips! Thanks!L

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made this a day ahead and refrigerated it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Skimpy filling for 9” pie pan. Add 1/2 cup of cream do increase to a respectable volume?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What temperature are you supposed to cook the whole pie at?  Am I supposed to assume the last oven setting of 350?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yeah, I wondered that too. I baked it at 350 and it was perfect -- though I did have to keep going perhaps 10 minutes longer than the recipe suggests.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cooking time on the pie crust, and the original high heat, destroyed my first pie crust. I made another, prebaked it at 350 for fifteen minutes and then cooked it with the filling for the suggested time. For me, it worked much better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Prebaked for thirty minutes with foil and pie weights and it was VERY dark and did not need 10-15 more minutes at the lower temperature.  Covered the edges with foil for the rest of the baking time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is outstanding. Made it today for thanksgiving tomorrow and it smelled so wonderful coming out of the oven I just had to try it, to make sure it will be suitable for consumption. I’m happy to say it will, but now am sad I have to share!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any tips on how to store overnight?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cover with tinfoil and leave it on the counter. I made mine Thursday morning, and it's now Saturday afternoon and I just ate a slice with lunch. What remains of the pie is still perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This par baking seems a bit fussy to me when I’m making 4 pies. I just put the whole pie in the oven. I will report back when it is done. I am a bit concerned that the pie shell was under filled. Hope there will be some puffing. This amount of filling might work better for a tart.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as written except for a little less time prevailing the crust. It was delicious! I have never had the original so can’t say how it compares. It’s like an extra buttery and gooey brownie in a pie shell.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree with those who question the length of time the crust cooks. My crust was burnt on the edges after 30 minutes with pie weights. Couldn't use it. I tried a different recipe that only parbaked for 15 mins with pie weights and 3 minutes without. It turned out perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe was easy to make and the texture and chocolate taste was outstanding.  I added 1/4 of buttermilk and that helped with the creaminess.  Also added 1 tbsp of bourbon but we felt that overwhelmed the chocolate taste, so I would recommend leaving out the alcohol.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My Great-Aunt Thayne was a champion pie-baker...her chess pie looked like a chess board & the colors went all the way to the bottom of the filling (not just colored on the surface). I don't know if there's a mold that looks like a chessboard that one can use to pour the two colors of filling into...?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Add 1/2 C buttermilk
1 tsp coffee powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cooked with salted butter and still added the salt. It cut the sweetness just the right amount. Used a frozen gluten free crust. Defrosted the pie crust, added the filling, and cooked it all at the same time for 45 minutes which set the pie and browned the crust. I live in Colorado, and added the time due to altitude. I jiggled the oven to test for it being set when the pie no longer jiggled.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed this recipe with a frozen pre-made pie crust and it definitely overcooked. I debated starting again and baking another crust for a shorter time, but I decided to just use this one since it was brown but not actually burnt but it definitely adversely affected the flavor.  But this is a great recipe and I used a tablespoon of bourbon and two teaspoons of espresso powder and use Ghirardelli cocoa powder and good semi-sweet chocolate and it was fantastic

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We used a Graham Cracker crust the second time around. Great recipe. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very easy recipe. I didn’t have cocoa powder, so I used a pack of hot cocoa mix and reduced the sugar to 3/4 cup. Tasted delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This pie is just WOW! Rich, chocolaty, crispy crust, creamy filling. So, so good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Every Southern cook knows how to make a chocolate chess pie that is much easier than this. Just Google it and you’ll find the classic recipe - 1.5c sugar, 3 Tbsp cocoa powder, 2 eggs, 4 Tbsp butter, 5 oz can evaporated milk, and 1 tsp vanilla extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 70% Lindt chocolate bars. It was perfect. And I loved the recommendation to add a 1/2 cup of. buttermilk. It’s delicious! Definitely a repeat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Froze a homemade crust for 15 minutes and then par baked for 20 minutes, but that seemed like too long on the par bake. Will only do 15 minutes next time or will have to put tiny foil pieces around the circumference strip of pie crust. Ooey gooey brownie texture is a spot on description and I think worth it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried browning the butter first?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have also been making chocolate chess pie for over 45 years and I have never pre backed a pie crust.  I also make this pie dairy free and use vegan butter.  It's not as rich a pie but it's good.   Nance

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m going to make this and want to add buttermilk.  Suggestions on how to modify?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a brownie, baked in a pie crust

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Note to self - when using store-bought pie crust, don’t follow the directions for pre-baking. I only did the first 30 minutes and it came out looking done already. Wrapped the crust in foil and it should be fine, but next time I’ll probably only pre-bake for 10-15 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Making this tomorrow and prepping now. Like to weigh ingredients but finding that 26 grams of unsweetened cocoa is a lot more than 1/4 cup. Anybody else have this happen? Checked my scale and it is weighing everything else fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was fantastic! Very easy. from the other comments I thought it would be a brownie in a pie shell, but the filling was rich and creamy. Will definitely make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do not make this in a 9" OxO glass pie pan. It is not nearly enough filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
In my experience, the potatoes will cook through more evenly if you start them in cold water and then bring them to a boil, rather than adding potatoes to already boiling water (which tends to cook the outside too fast). 

Love Vaughn’s videos!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I came up with a easy way to keep potatoes warm after they’re mashed.  I put the potatoes in a 3 quart aluminum bowl and put it in a covered 7 quart stock pot with a little water in the bottom.  Then I just leave the pot to simmer on a back burner.  The effect is similar to a mini steam table.  The potatoes stay warm and taste great.  I’ve  left them this way for up to 45 minutes, occasionally stirring every 15 minutes or so.  Happy Thanksgiving!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these to pair with a meatloaf and they were pretty great, but I would highly recommend using salted butter instead of unsalted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No, a hand mixer or potato masher does not work "just as well" as a potato ricer. The results are completely different. If you're satisfied with the results of a hand mixer or potato masher, using a ricer will feel like too much trouble. If you like the results of a potato ricer (fluffy and perfectly consistent), you're not going to settle for the results of a potato masher (lumpy) or a hand mixer (gluey).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Put a couple cloves of garlic in with the raw potatoes and cook and mash them in like they were part of the potatoes .
Side note amazing video voughn

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ok - So you did a whole video to conclude with this recipe. However, in the video you add chopped chives, fried shallots, and fried potato skins - yet these are nowhere to be found in your recipe!! It might be nice for your viewing audience, if you would write up the recipe the way you depicted it on camera.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Boil potatoes whole, unpeeled. When done, cut in half crosswise. Run through potato ricer with cut side down; the peels will stay behind (you may need to remove them every few potatoes). Then proceed with adding the other ingredients. This is also how to prepare potatoes for gnocchi.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Re tip: We peel and cut the taters 24-48 hours ahead, keep submerged in cool water (in the fridge), and replace the water once or twice. The coloring is retained but much of the potassium is leeched away, so that our family's kidney patient can enjoy the feast with the rest of us.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for 2 with a one pound potato cut into 8 pieces, half a stick (4 Tbsp.) butter, and half a cup of milk, cooking the potato in a saucier. Perfect with Kay Chun's meatloaf!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A suggestion for variants of mashed potatoes, called: mashed potatoes of the four seasons. 
For spring time, add some green herbs and some green onions and garlic. 
For summertime make it red using some red spices like paprika, red chili and finely chopped red peppers, and/or dried tomatoes. Use tomato paste to add color. 
For autumn’s use chestnuts and nutmeg. For winter make your classic white mashed potatoes.
Of course you are going to need salt, pepper, butter, milk, or cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Potatoes should start their cooking in cold salted water, not boiling water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree, but I actually wait for the water to boil before adding the salt, as I once read somewhere that salt added to cold water in a pot could “pockmark” it.  I don’t know if that’s true though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I always add nutmeg. It’s Divine

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mother-in-law made the best ever mashed potatoes much like these except she diced a medium onion and boiled it right with the potatoes... and left out the garlic since several members of the family hated it. Onions instead of garlic would make it a 5-star. She did use browned butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: NEVER, EVER beat potatoes with a mixer! you may end up with a glutenous mess. Ptoperly cooked potatoes can be mashed with a fork.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For a "why are these mashed potatoes so good?!" alternative to garlic, my friend  coarsely grates about a tablespoon of raw onion into the potatoes before she mashes them.  The enhancing  flavour doesn't identify itself as onion.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Brown butter appears to be the new rage. My Mom was making brown butter years ago as she ignored things on the stove. We just didn’t have a name for the result back then.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Charming video, but since I use a ricer, I don't peel the potatoes. I cut them in half before cooking, dig out any big eyes and then just drop them cut-side down into the ricer. The skin will stay in the ricer. Squash, remove the skin, reload. It saves some time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No garlic or herbs in mashed potatoes!   Just salt, pepper, milk, and LOTS AND LOTS of butter!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mashed potatoes can be made a full day ahead, covered, and refrigerated. Reheat in microwave and drizzle remaining heated brown butter over.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I too disagree with the hand masher or electric mixer. Do yourselves a favor and use a potato ricer. Sub russet for yellow potatoes,they are less starchy and coupled with the ricer. They turn out velvety and super consistent.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This delicious recipe appears in the wrong order. To accomplish it quickly: put a large pot of water on to boil, peel the potatoes, begin melting the butter, put potatoes in boiling water, then finish cooking the butter while the potatoes boil. Drain. Mash. Add butter. Enjoy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These weren’t bad, but they weren’t a showstopper, and didn’t taste that different from the classic mashed potato. I would just stick with classic for Thanksgiving and maybe try these in a smaller setting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I prefer using sour cream to mash with instead of milk.  It also adds a little bit of saltiness.  Great recipe - even better with personal preference tweaks (texture, saltiness, herbs, quantity of garlic).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are russet potatoes typically used for mashed potatoes? I've always thought of them as baking potatoes. Yukon gold or red-skinned potatoes are good for both mashing or roasting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Watch the video.   It inspired me to do exactly as he has written.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
So it says 1/2 sticks of butter and the second step of the recipe says to pour all of the butter except a scant 1/4 cup

Sp how much butter was used in the first step? Should I use 1 stick or 2?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
It says 2 sticks of butter = 1 cup.
Pour out and reserve 1/4 cup to add at the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The ratio by weight is completely off. To match 2 sticks of butter and 2 cups of milk, you need at least 6 lbs of potatoes. I went by weight and these potatoes would have been liquid if we had used all the butter/milk. Flavor is good but agree with others it's not so spectacular as to be worth the extra time and planning.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made 5 lbs of potatoes and used more milk than recommended.  

I agree, I would not make this again.  The garlic was offputting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the ratios using 5 lbs of russets.  At first I thought I would have a sloppy mess but it firmed up nicely as I continued to mash.  Everyone at my table said they were the best ever.  Note, a friend had just made them, and said that her family didn't love the rosemary, so I left out the steeped herbs part, and I used a modicum of garlic powder since I knew my crew wouldn't love a garlicky spud.  These will grace my table again, now that I know that the soupiness goes away!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This was killer!

I used about half the garlic, per other comments, which was the right amount for me. Also, when I was browning the butter, I fried sage leaves in it. I fished them out before continuing. At the end, I topped the potatoes with the crunchy sage leaves as well as the butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Needs more butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Adding to the "to ricer, or not to ricer" question: my family loves old-fashioned chunky mashed potatoes and hates creamy ones. Everyone raves when I do this: scrub the potatoes really well, leave the peelings on and cut into 1 1/2 inch chunks. Boil as written but note that they won't take as long to cook, then drain and pour in the warm milk mixture. Mix by drawing a knife through and then stirring just enough combine. Add the butter and boom! You're done! Chunky but not gluey potatoes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why is it important to dry the potatoes in the pot just before adding milk?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hand mashing yields mashed potatoes. Electric mixer favors puréed potatoes. (No culinary degree but 65plus years of peeling, cooking, mashing and when ill, using an electric mixer.) Finally, cook in a pressure cooker, use less salt, and use some of the cooking water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Sounds complicated and takes too long..I use sour cream ..a generous amount
mix in some garlic powder and whatever herb you like ..add some milk to make
the potatoes creamier..and voilous..there are great mashed potatoes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've never outright panned a NYT recipe before, but I really did not like this. Even a close relative who is guaranteed to polish off leftover mashers told me to take them away. I LOVE brown butter so may try again but won't stick exactly to this recipe. For one I wouldn't just quarter the potatoes (more like 1" or smaller pieces) and wouldn't be indifferent about mashing vs. ricing vs. hand mixing. I think next time I would try straight milk butter garlic, saving the brown butter for the top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone have any insight or advice on whether these can be made ahead of time?  Like the day before Thanksgiving?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jenmac you can make them a day ahead, but reserve that last 1/4 C browned butter. When you want to serve them, spread them in a flatter dish (like a 9x13), cover with foil so they don't dry out, re-warm in the oven and then pour the last of the browned butter on top before serving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I cook the potatoes in milk, keeping a close eye on them, adding milk as needed.  When ready to mash, add cream and butter, salt and pepper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We grew up in Lancaster County Pennsylvania having Brown Butter Mashed Potatoes at almost every big extended family meal.  I assumed this was a Pennsylvania Dutch thing.  We put brown butter on lots of things.... mashed potatoes, boiled potatoes, lima beans, noodles, boiled beats, yellow string beans, and we made fried eggs and egg omelets in brown butter. Is this recipe possibly taken from Pennsylvania Dutch tradition?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made with less butter, add nutmeg and very low salt cream cheese. Perfect

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The milk curdled and I had to do it over. Don't let that happen to you. Keep the heat very low!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Masher vs Ricer depends on the masher. Mashers with large curves are labor intensive and I would opt for a ricer. My masher has small holes and produces a smooth and airy mash. Peeling potatoes is easier to me than cleaning out the ricer multiple times

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My Amish friends add cream cheese to their mash potatoes.  They are to die for.  I would imagine brown butter on top would be like the cherry on a sundae.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone know why when I use a ricer, the potatoes come out grainy?? Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Undercooked potatoes?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made for Thanksgiving- these were phenomenal. Husband and preschool aged children didn't really care, of course. But I was delighted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used 1 clove of garlic for 5 lbs of russets and it was too much garlic.  I don't know how anyone could eat this with a head of garlic!  Ugh.

I didn't even notice the browned butter because the garlic was so overwhelming.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Like other readers, I adapted these brilliant potatoes, aided significantly by Vaughn's video. I felt a little like a moron for investing so much time in "just" mashed potatoes, but it was 1000% worth it. I steamed a mix of Yukon gold & russets. To ensure moistness when reheating, I added creme fraiche & floated some of the milk mixture on top. On Thanksgiving day, 40 mins at 350º, a robust stir + brown butter made for the best holiday spuds EVER!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love these potatoes. I love everything about them. No changes. I made as is with an old school masher and they were creamy as all get out. Also, the video is excellent! Thoroughly enjoyed it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How about metric?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I need to change to or from metric they have lots of apps or just Google lbs to gram and it will give u an area to input ur  amounts and then choose what measurement ur starting with and want to end with. Promise it's easier than it sounded

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What you made is ghee that Indians make. Separating the milk solids from the fat gives the fat that smoky flavor. Heat the butter long enough till the solids separate and you have a honey color for the fat, Strain to remove the solids and you will have the same flavor

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: But wait, where’s the killer version with fried potato skins and maybe shallots on top, like in the video? Also, now I’m craving mashed potatoes for breakfast. Thanks, Vaughan!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Peel & dice. Boil with salt, drain, add LOTS of butter, a bit of pepper & nutmeg. Cover with lid so butter melts. Mash. Then add evaporated milk. Adjust seasonings. Yummy

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why peel the potatoes?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The herb I prefer is dill.... Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A friend of mine, taught me to add mayonnaise instead of milk, they come out nice and creamy. Also works with vegan mayonnaise.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would you add vegan butter to the vegan mayonnaise if you made vegan mashed potatoes?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a great favorite except I use whipping cream instead of milk and of course the ricing seems to really absorb more flavor compared to a hand mixer.  Brown butter is simply amazing for making scrambled eggs as well as ice cream . . .  (Thanksgiving is a feast and I am grateful!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe writer could have easily added metric measurements to the measurements where appropriate, such as for butter, potatoes and milk. There are many Times readers outside the USA

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I now steam my potatoes in a double-boiler rather than boiling them directly in water. Much better flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have been been pressure cooking potatoes for mashed potatoes, same as my mother, for 30+ years for the same reason.  Both methods cause the potatoes to retain more flavor.  That said, I'm definitely trying this recipe next week!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What about pressure cooking whole unpeeled potatoes in a pressure cooker or instant pot to save on cooking time? I don’t peel the pressure-cooked potatoes. I cut them in half and push them through the potato river, taking the peel that stays in the bottom of the river out with a spoon. Anybody else doing it like this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've added a few tablespoons of my homemade chicken stock at the end. Just adds an extra level of taste. Another good recipe that I'll probably forget.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question about picture - what is sprinkled on top of the potatoes besides the browned butter - it looks like paprika or nutmeg maybe? Just curious. It is driving my husband crazy to know.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Potatoes in cold salty water, crack the lid and lower the heat to keep them simmering until there is no resistance to a fork. Drain very well, place back over a very low flame uncovered until they are white, dry and fluffy. As they dry in the middle of the pot place a slab of butter on the bottom and recover it with the potatoes and add about 4 tablespoon patties distributed evenly along the sides of the pot. When the butter has melted I add milk and mash. Dry, add milk, stiffen them with butter

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: (ahem) The point of baking the potato is that you don't have to peel them AT ALL. You just hold the potato with a towel, slit it with a paring knife, and then scoop out the insides with a spoon. NO BURNT FINGERS! Also the brown roasted bits add some flavor. Also you can cook other things in the same oven, like heads of garlic, butternut squash, maybe even a turkey! Still, I appreciate the shout out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can use a hand mixer, but you have to be quick, which might/will result in an occasional bit of potato.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My husband said “these are the best mashed potatoes I’ve ever had in my life”

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made three changes… Baked. not boiled the potatoes, mixed Russet with Yukon Gold, made the brown butter/milk mixture the day before, added peppercorns to the simmering milk… actually that was four changes, but so worth it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think Jeff in Florida just explained the origin story of browned butter

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe. I added about half a cup of grated parm and a generous amount of fresh grated nutmeg. They were an absolute hit at thanksgiving dinner. This will definitely be my go-to method for mash from now on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made with rosemary. No edits required. Perfection!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I PRed Thanksgiving with this one, Vaughn. I’m a mashed potato hero. I didn’t even steep herbs or use garlic at all: just used milk, butter, salt and pepper. The brown butter was plenty to make the potatoes more interesting without competing with the more complex flavors of the main dishes. Browning the butter truly made a remarkable difference. My family has declared this our new Thanksgiving mashed potatoes recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used only a fraction of rosemary and simmered it in the milk only a minute or two, thank goodness, because the dish tasted soap like. Following the directions it would have been inedible. The brown butter too was highly over rated. I won't make this again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: While these are good mashed potatoes, they are really a lot of work, and next time we'll go with a more basic recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I find it easier to use a ricer to make mashed potatoes. I don't peel the potatoes, I just cook them whole or halved if they're huge. When they're done I cut the potatoes in half and drop them into the ricer cut side down. The skins are left in the ricer after the potatoes are squashed. It saves a step and the texture is better too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were tasty but the slightly brown color of the final mashed potatoes was off putting.  Brown is fine for many types of potatoes but mashed potatoes should be creamy yellow or white.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are decent mashed potatoes, but WAY too much work and clean-up. We simply boil skin-on potatoes in salt water, pour off most of the water, and then mash the potatoes with salt, a generous amount of butter and/or sour cream, and optionally garlic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: nom nom nom. THIS is how you make mashers!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good but not superb. I will add more browned butter next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What's the best milk substitute for those averse to dairy (and soy)? Almond? Oat? Cashew? Coconut? Rice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are a Thanksgiving hit! I used the leftovers for Shepards pie and the extra flavor added so much!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Late to the party, but totally enjoyed making this today!  We fed 23 - and I doubled the recipe, but not the butter, I only used two sticks for 10 lbs of potatoes, but did double all the other ingredients. Honestly, loved the flavors and browning my butter today, Vaughn you’re our hero! Well you and our teenage girl that mashed our potatoes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I grew up in Florida with a very prolific "Key Lime Pie Tree" outside my bedroom and squeezed thousands of limes over the years for my mother's pies.  Most Florida old timers like me assert that a "real" Key Lime Pie is topped with a lightly sweetened meringue, not whipped cream. It seems intuitively obvious that's what the first pie-maker would have done with the leftover whites.  I know, there's a debate, but take a second to think about it. And it's a lot more special than whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My family’s favorite substitution is making the crust out of crushed Ginger Snaps.  It wins over graham crackers by a mile!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The perfect crust is biscoff lotus cookies with with some dessicated coconut added in. It’s an interesting crust versus bland G crackers

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Oh, Claudia. Just stop. Find another recipe.
