In the meantime, I love sweetened condensed milk and will happily eat it plain. 

I don’t think you can make key lime pie without it because of how the ingredients have to work together to become the filling. 

See you in The Keys, everybody!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Key limes are a pain to juice. They’re grape-sized, so you need a lot of them, with each producing a tiny amount of juice. I’ve made this pie many times with key limes and with regular limes. There isn’t much of a difference in taste and no one has ever been able to tell the difference (including Floridian friends and family members). I’ve also made the pie with whipped cream or meringue toppings and they are equally delicious. Sweetened condensed milk is essential for the pie to set properly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use your garlic press to squeeze the juice of key limes. Halve them and place one half cut side down in the press. Much faster and more effective than hand squeezing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I've been using Nellie and Joe's Key Lime Juice for decades. EVERYONE adores my key lime pie and begs for me to make it. We all prefer bottled (real!) key lime juice to Persian limes, though the latter is perfectly good. As someone that ran a boutique dessert business, trust me and save yourself some time if that's the difference between making KLP and not.
Most important is the lime (or lemon in a pinch) and extra crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Audrey I could not agree with you more. I made Key lime pies for years. With real key limes. The Joe and Nellies is excellent. It tastes exactly the same.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: On the west coast, I don't really have access to key limes, so I make this recipe with local Meyer lemons in place of the limes. It's absolutely stunning and one of my favorite desserts ever!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: And chocolate, ginger snap or shortbread cookies make a much  better crust

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For a 9inch pie dish, the ingredients for the filling needs to be doubled. It did not fill the entire pie dish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Debra just made this as written and wish I'd seen this comment beforehand.  Yes - I needed a lot more filling for my 9" pie dish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s a pity that NYT Cooking won’t update the ingredients / proportions for a fuller pie. This tasted great, but looked rather meager only half-way up the crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No, no, no.  Regular Persian limes have a different flavor than key limes.  You can buy key lime juice in grocery stores.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Never buy bottled lime juice of any kind.  Taste horrible. You'll never taste the difference between limes in a key lime pie.  Cooks Illustrated did a blind taste test and absolutely no one could tell the difference.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wegman's sells fresh squeezed lime juice. Not key lime, but it is fresh-squeezed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm not convinced what you find in the store is key lime juice.  Nellie and Joe make two products "Key West Lime Juice" and "Key Lime Juice".  I have only seen the former in grocery stores, although there may be regional differences in availability.  The keylimejuice website shows that the former can be bought for about $4 for a 16 oz bottle, while the latter costs about $5 for a 12 oz bottle.  This makes me think that the latter is the real thing.  I have never made the direct taste comparison.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a great gluten free dessert to make.  If you are allergic to gluten you need only find a favorite gluten free graham cracker (I used Schar brand - 1 package 5.6 oz.) and a gluten free sweetened condensed milk (I used Eagle Brand).  This pie turned out great! I did not have key limes so I followed the recipe in the notes to make Persian lime juice mixed with lemon juice to give the filling a key lime taste. Delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Ugh on the sweetened condensed milk.  
I didn't know it still existed.   Is there a healthier alternative,  like yogurt?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sorry - no substitute for sweetened condensed milk. The lime juice reacts with the SCM to thicken it and help set the filling (the eggs are there for fat & creaminess), so yogurt is not an option. You could make a regular milk-based custard - but then it’s just a lime-flavored pastry cream pie. ;)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thanks for the options regarding limes. Surprisingly not every NYT subscriber lives in the USA.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1 full teaspoon of kosher salt for only 1.5 cups of graham is wayyyyyyyy too much salt!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To those who say no bottled juice, Nellie and Joe’s Key Lime juice is not the same as the old standby bottled Persian lime juice.  Nellie and Joe’s is real key lime juice and makes a wonderful pie.  I’ve been using it for years and people rave over my Key Lime Pie.  I do add the zest of key limes when I can get them or the zest of Persian limes if key limes are not available.  The zest adds a slight texture and a bit of brightness.  The recipe on the bottle of Nellie and Joe’s is perfectly fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really disappointing. I followed the recipe and the salt overpowered the filling. I literally had to to dump it Thanksgiving morning and remake with a different recipe. Key Lime Pie usually doesn't have any salt or at most just a pinch. I used 1/2 the kosher salt called for in this recipe, and still it was horrible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought it was ok, but my in-laws whom I made it for thought it was entirely too salty and said as much. I used the correct type of salt and followed the recipe exactly, so just be warned that it might not be to your liking if you aren’t expecting a salty dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Perfect as written. Make sure you are using kosher salt (not table salt) or it will be too salty. It only takes 5 minutes to juice enough key limes. Zap them in the microwave for about 15 seconds or so to release max juice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like salt, but even for me this was a bit much. I think dialing it back to 3/4 tsp in the crust would be about right. I also had too much crust relative to filling (another reason the saltiness of the crust stands out). Maybe this would work better as bars?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How about substituting sweetened condensed COCONUT milk instead? I see that "TBD" responded to Claudia in Sept 2023 of the importance of using Sweetened Condensed Milk as "....no substitute for sweetened condensed milk. The lime juice reacts with the SCM to thicken it and help set the filling (the eggs are there for fat & creaminess), so yogurt is not an option."

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ginger nut crust every time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nellie and Joes Key lime juice!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: TOO MUCH SALT IN CRUST! GRAHAM CRACKERS ALREADY HAVE SALT. I ADDED 3/4 TEASPOON AND IT WAS TOO MUCH. PERHAPS 1/4 WOULD BE CORRECT.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just want to echo a comment from a couple years ago and say that the filling needs to be doubled to fill a 9-inch pie dish. The 1x recipe just covered the bottom at a depth of maybe 1.5 cm leaving and inch of exposed crust. Perhaps the 1x recipe would fill an extremely shallow pie dish but for the average pie dish but 1.5x or 2x is needed. Don’t get caught like me having to whip up a batch with cold ingredients and having to mix it in with the temperate curd that’s already in the crust!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very creamy. Used Persian limes. Would really like to try w/key limes. Or Meyer lemons. Made this in an 8” square pan and cut into 9 squares. Didn’t add peel at the end. Very creamy. Everyone loved it. But for squares when I make it again, I’ll use half the crust. Too much crust for my liking. I guess I will make it whenever I can get other fruit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love Key Lime Pie, it is my favorite dessert.  A little bit of cinnamon really punches up the graham cracker crust.  You may not think that lime and cinnamon go together, but somehow it works.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! And easy to knock this one out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This pie was phenomenal! I suggest cutting the brown sugar in half (I only added ~25 grams) and it was sweet enough. I also subbed the regular whipped cream for mascarpone whipped cream. It’s more stabilized and adds a slightly more creamy flavor.

Unless your pie pan is really shallow, make the sides ~1/2 inch below the top edge of the pan. My filling only reached about halfway up my glass pan so the crust edges were rather tall!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used ginger snaps with crystallized ginger for the crust as opposed to graham crackers, added a little cognac to the whipped cream. Very yum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is very similar recipe to Joe's Stone Crab recipe, which is fantastic! The only thing they suggest is freezing the pie for 15-20 minutes just before serving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made key lime pie with real key limes a while back and found it to be bitter. If I make it again, I'll probably use regular limes. Also, I recently learned that ripe limes are more yellow and the green limes we find in the stores are unripe. That seems a shame and makes me wonder what ripe limes taste like.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Born and raised conch here recommends Nellie and Joe’s key lime juice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Lived in Key West for 3 years and worked as a pastry chef making hundreds and hundreds of KLPs. Nellie and Joe's all the way!
And NO ZEST in the filling!!! It turns bitter and grainy! Ick!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lynn M. Thank you. You really understand hiw to do this.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I lived in the Florida Keys in the early 70s, worked in restaurants, and my memories of Key Lime pie is that it was made with meringue as Rob Q mentioned.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you use a ginger snap crust, do you still need to add the sugar?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make a ginger nut crust using a 300g packet of gluten-free ginger nut biscuits blitzed and 100g melted butter. The biscuits I use  (Australian brand) don’t require any added sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For the lactose intolerant folks, replace the condensed milk with Kara Coconut Crème (2 6.8oz cartons) (which is unsweetened), add 1/8 cup sugar, and increase baking time 10 to 15 minutes. If you're using key limes, an easier way to juice them is to cut them in half and then use a garlic press. Still a fair amount of work, but easier then any other method I've found.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Very nice pie which received compliments from guests. The key lime pie I grew up eating is sweet and probably not very "traditional" (I'm from Texas). This pie is fairly tart, even with the sweetened condensed milk. I would recommend tasting the filling and tasting the whip topping as you go to determine the right balance of sweet vs tart for your tastes.

I added a dash of cardamom to the whipped topping and it was a nice compliment to the dish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This recipe makes a very small amount of filling. If you want a taller more substantial pie for more than 6 slices, you’ll want to double the filling and use a deeper pie dish. Also because the filling amount is only about 5/8” thick, my curd was set in only 10 minutes (Non-convection. Second rack from top), So if the recipe was baked as is, 15-20 minutes would have been too long.

All of that said, VERY DELICIOUS velvety smooth pie with well balanced flavors! Highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Instead of using whipped cream for the topping, beat the egg whites with some sugar into a meringue. Place on top of pie brown lightly under the broiler.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Key lime pie should be made with meringue, rather than whipped cream, in my opinion.  I have a postcard from Florida with the recipe, which is now over fifty years old (although even then some people were using whipped cream; easier but not as good.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I say yes to Nellie and Joes Key lime juice - been using it for years with great results!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I absolutely love key lime pie, but it’s so often ruined for me by the “whipped topping”.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don’t put 1/4 tsp salt in whipped topping! Too much salt!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Turned out perfectly. I am so glad I made it and got to cross it off my baking bucket list. 
And now I will never again have to zest that many ping pong sized fruits! Persian limes will just have to do in the future

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Perfect recipe easy to follow for a first time key lime pie maker and turned out delicious! Made this for my mother in laws birthday and she loved it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tried ½ cup mascarpone cheese,  ½ cup whipping cream, 1 Tbsp granulated sugar and a pinch of salt. Whipped until creamy with structure. This was my favorite topping thus far. Not too sweet and so creamy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Regular limes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found that this recipe does not make enough filling.  I would double the filling portion the next time I make this.  Overall its a good recipe and easy to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm in the UK and didn't even know a key lime was a thing until I read this recipe. I made it with regular limes, for the base I used half digestives half ginger nuts. So delicious, easy to make, a crowd pleaser and the leftovers went down great with a cuppa at breakfast 10/10

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make the crust with Ginger Snaps (Nabisco, really) rather than graham cracker. The flavor rush is wonderful. You will never go back.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
There are a smattering of allergies in my house. I used my “go to summer crust” which is a vegan buttermilk cornmeal crust- a gluten free dream

Trying to sub condensed coconut milk for the sweetened condensed milk DOES NOT work.  Biggest bake fail in a while 😑

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this as indicated - a truly great pie. Except, why the salt in the whipped cream? Doesn't need it. There's plenty of that salty taste between the crust and the filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Key lime zest is very bitter and will ruin your pie. We used to have a key lime tree and the one time we included zest the result was inedible. Also, meringue makes for a much better topping than whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the instructions to a T and the pie was delicious!  Will definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very salty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One trip to the Florida Keys I ate my way though a dozen pieces of key lime pie.  Traditionally a pastry crust was used, partially blind baked.  I got this straight from many of the bakers, who were invariably black women.  Also, I agree with the commenter about using the white to make a meringue, that is, if you're not saving them for making macarons.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved this pie even though my lime juice had a bitter aftertaste. I juiced key limes. Not sure if key limes normally have a bitter taste or if my bag was just off. The results were still delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this pie for Thanksgiving 2025.  The crust was great.  As for the filling, I did use the Nell & Joe's key lime juice, because there are no key limes in Massachusetts in November!  The taste was excellent.  The only problem I saw with this recipe was, as a few others stated, the amount of filling is in adequate for the pie crust.  I doubled the filling and baked the pie a bit longer (how long?  I'm not sure--I just kept checking it).  The taste was exceptional.  I will make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Salt overload in the crust and whipped cream. Totally had to redo the whipped cream without it because it was just too gross. Crust definitely needs less salt too. But man that curd is incredible!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My first key lime pie ever, & I'm pretty pleased w/ the result. Next time:

1. I'll either not use the key lime zest, or I will add only 1/8th teaspoon of zest. The bitterness ended up being a little overpowering, but the pie is still enjoyable (to me). 
2. I'll double the filling. This amount filled up only 1/2 of the pie.
3. I didn't have trouble with salt because I only added a tiny pinch in the crust & none in the whipped cream.
4. Crust was tasty but too crumbly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tasty and excellent texture for the filling, but there were drawbacks: 1. crust was too dry and a bit too salty--I would add half the amount called for. 2. Ditto for the whipped cream--too salty, even though I increased the amount of cream to a full cup and kept the same amount of salt. (And this is from someone with no aversion to salt. 3. Not enough of a lime flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The filling was like 1/8 in high in a 9 inch pan

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For years I have reduced the number of graham crackers by about one third and added ginger snaps instead.  It adds just enough "snap" to the crust. This is a great combo for other kinds of pies--banana cream, coconut cream, and some kinds of lemon pies. A local bakery makes a killer lemon curd pie and tops it with blueberries.  Often I will do this with the Key Lime and then add whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ugh again with the whole graham crackers? Does anyone have a conversion using the bagged graham cracker crumbs?  Shortcuts are always appreciated one person is preparing a meal from soup to, well, hopefully Key Lime pie. Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 180 grams, as the recipe says

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where can you buy Key limes? Have never seen them in any grocery store. My grandparents also had one. But now have only seen baby Persian limes being sold as Key limes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can buy famous Nellie & Joe's Key Lime juice bottled. I went through cindy@keylimejuice.com, if she is still there. Wonderful juice from FL.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does anyone have a gluten free cracker/cookie suggestion?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can buy gluten free graham "style" crumbs online. Also GF vanilla wafers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Gluten-free gingersnaps are terrific!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this crust and filling as directed and it was delicious.I don’t like Whipped Cream, so I swapped it for simple meringue, which I added after the pie was completely cooled. I first cooled it at room temp for an hour, then in the fridge for an hour before adding the meringue. I lightly browned the meringue under the broiler and then chilled it for a couple hours before serving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I recently had a slice of key lime pie in a restaurant made with a ginger snap crust. Think I’ll try that with this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you live in the Southwest, Mexican lime are very much like key limes and a good substitute

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I was surprised to see that the lime curd is not cooked first, as done with a lemon pie, then finished in the oven until set. Is it the condensed milk vs butter and sugar? 
Also - if you want meringue but don't want to put the pie back in the oven or it doesn't totally cover the filling, bake the meringue on parchment, like a Pavlova.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It's a chemical reaction - the acidity of the limes works with the condensed milk to set the filling without baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm curious about the difference between this recipe and Jennifer Steinhauer's Frozen Key Lime Pie;  does this recipe really fill up a 9" shell?  Jennifer's recipe has twice the volume and (in my experience) fills a 9" shell nicely so I'm wondering how this recipe manages do the same with half the ingredients? I've made Jennifer's many times with a baked shell and then briefly baked filling (no freezing) and it's lovely.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My wife made this pie for dessert tonight.  I've fished in the Keys for decades and have a pie or two.  
The pie was OK, but the "topping" was perhaps the worst thing she has ever "cooked".  I was expecting whipped cream but then I hit the salt.  Why on earth would there be salt in this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Salt in whipped cream is not unheard of. Maybe you just don't like it. If she used table salt, it was only 1/8 teaspoon. How bad could that be? You sound upset. Ask you wife to never add salt to whipped cream again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any tips for a non-US resident for graham cracker alternate crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ginger nut biscuits go perfectly with Key Lime Pie, depending on their sweetness I don’t add any sugar to the crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What does '12 full graham crackers' mean?  There are 9 in a package, so I added 3, and came out with way too many crumbs.  Typically 9 are enough for a crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use the recipe from Joe’s Stone Crab, and bake it. I use no topping, by choice. If I did I’d make a meringue topping!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone ever tried it with bearss limes? Such an abundant tree I think I’ll try it. Gingersnap crust sounds divine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I worked at Bonefish Grill and the trick to a perfect key lime pie if you can't find fresh key limes is to use Nellie and Joe's key lime juice, usually found in the cocktail mixer aisle 😉

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sorry - bottled KL juice is disgusting. whipped cream has my vote for topping as meringue can be very difficult to cook properly and have it not be weepy on the bottom where it touches the pie. Again - disgusting. Do it the right way (as here described) and you will be transported to the Florida Keys in winter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Living in the Southwest we know Key Limes are actually Mexican Limes. Naturally they thrive here and besides being essential in Mexican cooking, make fantastic Key Lime Pie. While people usually pick limes when they are green, if you let them ripen they turn the yellow of a Key Lime. What bothers me is when local bakers make Key Lime Pie they often use a green Persian Lime slice as decoration. It looks crazy to have a green decoration on a yellow pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: NOT an expert on Key vs regular limes vs whipped cream vs meringue, BUT I DO KNOW that the key lime pie at The Blue Heaven restaurant in Key West is a life altering event to eat. Like an oasis in a veritable sea of humanity, with attendant chickens and cats, their key lime pie is like no other in a unique ambience. Worth the trip folks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Google homemade graham crackers; quicker than going to the store to buy a box and SO much more tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use crushed Maria cookies as the pie crust and it’s always been a hit!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Key limes are small enough to juice in most garlic presses. You'll get the juice you need in just a couple of minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made one test pie prior to TGiving and made these adjustments.  More melted butter for the crust and slightly longer blind bake time so crust held together better.  Dont zest too deep avoiding the white because it makes the curd bitter. I made 3 pies for TGiving and they were a hit.  No leftovers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious pie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The filling was delicious, but there wasn't enough of it. I had to put lots of whipped cream to fill the 9” shell. And never again will I add salt to whipped cream! But my biggest criticism is that the pie was ruined by the crust, which was so over-salted it was barely edible.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Salt in the topping???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tried that and it came out badly. Omit it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We’ve made Key Lime pies for decades with varying mixes of Key limes, limes, lemons, and Meyer lemons from over over-wintered in a greenhouse Oklahoma garden. We’ve always used a recipe virtually identical to this one—lime juice, sweetened condensed milk, and egg yolks. This recipe was top-notch! Creamy but set, sweet and zippy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How many yolks?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have made the Dinosaur BBQ key lime pie recipe for years and this is similar.  (It uses 4 egg yolks vs 3.)  Key limes are alwsys dry, so I  use Persisn limes.  Bottled lime juice has an aftertaste, so use fresh limes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You left the lime juice out of the recipe? Am I missing something, I don't see it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Step 3 Add the lime juice

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do not sub sweetened condensed coconut milk. The filling will develop a leathery skin on top during baking which will loosely conceal a brown, soupy, oily, but oh so sweet lime flavored liquid beneath.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We have made this recipe several times now and really enjoy it. We use our own key limes when in season here in CA and a lime juicer/press makes it super easy and fast. We now prefer a bit less crust overall, just using 1 package of graham crackers. We also no longer salt the whipped cream. Lovely and easy recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This made plenty of crust but not enough filling. Next time I'd make 1.5 times the filing recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn’t have key limes so we used a mixture of lime and lemon juice per the note. The flavor of the filling was good but the texture was very grainy. The crust had a nice crunch but was too salty. I used to use a cooking light recipe years ago that was really good with a nice creamy filling. I need to find that recipe. I wouldn’t make this one again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make my key lime pie crumb crust with Pecan Sandies.  Super delicious!! However, because they are so buttery to begin with, use just HALF the normal amount of melted butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for the first time over the 4th of July and it turned out great! Don’t need any whip cream topping in my opinion, the pie turned out great. We made it two weeks later for a birthday treat we liked it so much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Salt in the whipped cream is too salty. Everything else about the recipe is great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My first attempt I didn’t read far enough and used table salt and didn’t see that I was supposed to use a lesser amount than the kosher salt.  Way, way too salty.  Had to throw it away.  I remade the pie using half of the amount of salt it called for and still too salty but edible.  I wish I had just used a different recipe the second time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I recently made this for a group of friends, one of whom has a severe gluten allergy. Since gluten free graham crackers are not good (in my opinion!) I used gluten free golden Oreos and it was a smashing success! Even the non gluten free people couldn’t tell the crust was gluten free. The pie itself was so easy to make and absolutely scrumptious. I’ll be making this again for sure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: does someone know how i can lower the sweetness? I followed the recipe and the condensed milk is way too sweet for my liking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would like to double the amount of filling.  Any hints on how to cook it and are proportions all the same?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Howard if you are looking for a thicker pie, I recommend 4 egg yolks, I cup l8me juice, 2 cans condensed milk.  Although discouraged here, I use bottled lime juice … either the Joe and Nellie Key Lime juice or Santa Cruz organic lime juice.  No one has ever complained about my key lime pie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very delicious. However, my curd turned out a lot paler than in the video. I substituted the key lime juice with Persian lime and lemon as recommended. Is that the reason?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was just in Florida where I had Key Lime pie and then came home and promptly made this!  And this version was so much better!! I followed the tip to use lime and lemon juice and it tasted superb. Easy. Delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too much salt in the whipped  cream as written (even when using diamond kosher salt)..... yikes! just a pinch is all you need.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe. I never add salt when baking - it is not necessary and it is not missed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Turned out great.
User 3/4 tsp of salt in the crust and used table salt on the filling due to all the comments about salt.   Used bottled key lime juice and was delicious. 

I read the comment about not adding zest to the filling as it would make the filling grainy, but with the graham cracker crust- who could tell!  Maybe if it was a pastry crust- but with a cracker crust there’s so much texture going on it wouldn’t be noticeable.

Added salt and sugar to the whipped cream to taste.  Always add it slowly and add more as needed.  Turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Have made this twice. First time, followed the recipe exactly as written and it turned out wonderful and was such a crowd pleaser! Second time, I added another tablespoon of butter to the crust because I felt it was just a tad too dry for my liking. Either way, this will be my go-to key lime pie recipe from here on out. It’s perfect! 

Don’t be daunted by the key limes! One bag from the grocery store is more than enough for one pie. It took approx 4 limes to get the zest amount needed, so easy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This, to me, is the best key line pie recipe on the NYT. The salt in the whipped cream is genius. My only alterations are using ginger snaps instead of graham cracker and adding 1/4-1/12 tsp of cayenne to the crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too salty.  Next time I will cut the salt to a pinch in both the crust and the filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t read the comments and followed the directions as written with diamond kosher salt. The crust and whipped cream were WAY too salty. But the filling was on point!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Please help. About how many regular limes are needed for the zesting and juicing? In the video he was kidding about using 70+, right? Right?!
I've never seen Key limes or its juice sold in store in NYC, so regular Persian limes will have to do.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Just NO.  

I was born in Tennessee, then moved to Florida when I was 6 (in 1968).  I tell folks I'm a native of two states:  I was born in Tennessee, but I was born-again in Florida (Marathon, to be exact).  Did I mention my dad was an evangelist?

In any case, the above being the case, I consider myself "God's man" when it comes to Key Lime Pie.  Now, admittedly, I am old-old-school, but let me provide a few helpful hints....

No substitute for key limes.  It's meringue, not whipped topping.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I took the time to juice and zest key limes and the pie came out terribly - too bitter and too salty.  i think key limes are way too challenging to zeat and avoid bitter pith (i did try).  next time i would cut the salt in the crust and avoid the zest or use regular lime zest.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This recipe is fine, as-is; and the addition of salt to the filling is something new to me and not objectionable- but I halved it (and always use diamond kosher salt)
Recommend adding 1/2-1 cup diced ripe mango to the filling. Tried blueberries in the past- disappointing. Also, better to be minimalist with the whipped cream garnish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for my book club and it was a huge hit. I used bottled key lime juice and zest from regular limes. It was delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: not enough filling for pie crust.  suggest double

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use a garlic press to squeeze my key limes.  It much easier and a lot faster than using a traditional juicer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t have key lime juice so I used the substitute the recipe recommended. Also, I didn’t have a pie pan so I used a tart pan which worked perfectly. If I ever come across key limes I’d make this again but I’d cut back on the salt in the crust. I used diamond crystal kosher salt and the measurement in the recipe but it tasted salty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I love canned cherry pie filling. 
There, I said it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
*Using Pamela's brand, or comparable good quality, gluten free graham crackers makes this a great dessert option for family members with celiac diagnosis.
*It is an error to turn up one's nose at premade cherry pie fillings.  Great ones on the market in jar and can made by/for cherry growers.  Look for those, not mass market brands.  Good brands include Chukar Cherries (OR), Red Hills Fruit (King Arthur sells this), Cherry Republic (MI), King Orchards (MI), Blake Hill and so on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dear Dimmerswitch ;) You believe "kindness doesn't cost a thing" when responding to M. M. and her cheeky reference to the can opener.  I open NYT cooking daily. I'm always inspired by the simplicity and complexity of the recipes. Greeted by Cherry Yum-Yum was wonderful. It reminded me of my aunt-who cooked like Julia Child-and always had cherry filling in her larder. Yum-Yum will be served on Christmas eve along with my Pavlova wreath. Best to you! Thanks for the cherry filling brands options.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So grateful for the cherry pie filling recommendations! Wishing you and yours a joyous holiday season!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We are cherry snobs— as a Midwest kid, my mom froze cherries that came in a can, unsweetened, from our little local store. Now the only way I can get them is to order IQF cherries from orchards like King Orchards. I keep them on hand and make spectacular filling using a lot of cherries, sparse sugar, and PermaFlo as a thickener.  No need to buy canned filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is perfect for an easy add to the holiday dessert table. I love it and will try it with Oreos! Actually, I’m going to have my kids make it two ways on Christmas Eve!
MM-Why the horrible tude? Not everybody has a church cookbook to look through. And not every single NYT recipe has to be a level above a can opener. It’s called variation and inspiration. A recipe is a recipe. I hope 2023 is a better year for you.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This reminds me of Christmas Eve when my grandmother made her Stained Glass dessert. She’d put it in her fanciest tureen which was a glass bowl held in some contraption. Part of the silver set, of course. Essentially it was red and green jello cubes suspended in Cool Whip with a Graham crust. I’d kill to have her secrets. Joy comes from any old place and the simplest recipe made with love can go far further than the most sophisticated. I think of her and that dessert 50 years on every XMAS eve.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh, my, I now know exactly what I will make for dessert for our Christmas Eve dinner for 2!  It's been years since I've had this.  One of my favorite things about NYT Cooking is that the recipes run the gamut - from no recipe recipes, to very minimal ingredient recipes that taste like they took hours to prepare, to the truly exotic, to good old fashioned comfort food.  Thanks for taking care of all our culinary needs.  And thanks for the memories shared with this one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add a smidge of grated lemon peel and juice to improve the canned cherry pie filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Come on!  This is already elevated by using whipped cream instead of Dream Whip from a box or frozen whipped topping.  You can use organic no color added pie filling but it loses the visual appeal.  This is AKA Cherry Delight if you don’t add the crumb topping.  We  love fine dining, but have to admit that this is still one of our family’s holiday favorites for over 50 years.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This no salted butter annoys me, use salted butter and cut back on how much salt to add. Just a thought.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mother used to make this as a “cheesecake” for my sister and it was her favorite thing ever. My sister is coming to my house for Christmas and I can’t wait to surprise her with this!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can easily make your own gluten-free filling, sweetened to your preference with the sweetener of your choice, by straining the liquid from two cans of tart cherries into a microwave-safe bowl (or saucepan, if you prefer). Add approximately 1.5-2 Tbsp. cornstarch and your sweetener (2-3 packets of stevia or anywhere from 1 Tbsp. - 1/2 C. sugar), and whisk together. Bring to a boil, stirring occasionally to remove any lumps and keep the mixture evenly heated, until clear and thick. (cont.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My grandmother made a version of this, but she did single servings in a cupcake pan. Nilla wafers in the bottom of a cupcake liner as the crust. Then cheesecake filling with canned cherries on top. Takes me back!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use recipes as guidelines, what about mascarpone rather than Cream cheese? Yes, use a known good brand for the cherry filling. I love cherry pie filling, there I said it too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This reminds me of the famous “dump cake” made with yellow cake mix, a stick of melted butter, chopped pecans, and a can of pie filling. A southern classic! I tried to figure out how to make it from scratch, and then realized its charm was the ingredients are available everywhere, so everyone can make it. Happy holidays to all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This brings back memories!  A lovely Auntie made this all the time.  You wouldn’t think you’d like canned cherry pie filling this much, but you will.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My grandma would make a similar dessert for those hot summer evening substituting layers of sliced banana for the pie filling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
After watching the flawless Vaughn V do his video I decided “That looks easy” and did the same on this year’s holiday Zoom call. My team loved it!! 

The recipe is quite special. We made small pantry-driven edits: used cinnamon graham crackers which added a nice note, organic cherry filling (may have reduced sugar/sweetness), and we also opted to use less salt. Overall we were yum-yum crushing on this last night following the obligatory half-a-workday soaking period.

Recommend this for all!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: IMHO Blueberry filling is better

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In the list of ingredients it would be helpful to note that the 2.5 tsp of salt are divided. I just dumped all the salt in the graham crackers. Might not be a recipe killer, might be… but some of us are cooking in a flurry and aren’t reading super closely on the instructions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy to make cherry filling.  Use frozen 8-10 cups sour cherries and sugar,  1:1 by volume. Mix and let sit an hour to 24. Add more sugar to taste. Bring to low boil while stirring, then turn down to simmer and continue to stir gently, scraping bottom and sides.  Mix a little tapioca or starch (corn, arrowroot or potato) with cold water and add when  the fruit is semi-soft. Note it keeps cooking off the heat. Cool to room temperature (4 hrs) before proceeding with the recipe. Make extra!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Thanks, Ella! This sounds like an amazing recipe. 

I would make this every chance I got (do you know, can this recipe be home canned?) IF I had access to fresh sour cherries. That's where store-bought pie filling comes in handy. Around here (Shenandoah Valley of Virginia) you have to beat the birds to sour cherries, which isn't easy. Even local orchards don't usually have sour cherries. I envy those who live near cherry orchards or in urban areas where they can buy frozen sour cherries.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Patressa
I lived for 15 years next to everyone’s favorite English teacher in a tiny town in south Jersey called Alloway. She had a Montmorency cherry tree in her tiny yard. I planed on on my side of the fence, too. What joy! Such flavor! Such fun to watch the Robins  steal from the tops to feed their babies! They are a small tree. I have two I planted 20 years ago and I freeze cherries every year.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was my childhood favorite. However, my mother only did a base crust… topping was the cream only. Incredibly easy and delicious. Thanks, Mom!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used to make a version of this with a baked graham cracker crust - no heavy cream- must find old recipe. My Grandpa loved this. Thank you for bringing back such a “sweet” memory.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe reminds me of the cherry cheesecake recipe my mother used to make in the 60's. It was a crust of crushed graham crackers, butter and sugar which was baked and cooled and then filled with a mixture of cream cheese, sweetened condensed milk, and lemon juice, which was then chilled. Canned cherry pie filling was added to the top. It was one of our favourite desserts! I think the recipe came from the back of the label of the eagle brand sweetened condensed milk.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made 2 pans.  The second pan I increased the whipped cream/cheese x 1.5 for easier and more generous covering.  Also added 1cup of tart frozen cherries to my 1 can of cherry pie filling. Yum yum

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thank you so much for this reminder of past Christmases! The simplest recipes often bring the most joy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Combine butter with gram crackers in food processor. Truly medium peeks for whipped cream, can’t make too stiff. Can use a LITTLE more almond extract. Added candied orange peel and left over night

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had plans for a complicated baked dessert, when my daughter suggested this. Well, it’s all gone, devoured by all, including the pickiest grandkids on the planet. Will definitely make again. A big hit. Has anyone tried it with blueberry pie filling? With maybe some grated lemon zest in the cream filling? Not that it needs to be changed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this as a pie about 40 years ago.  My sons loved it so much, we had it instead of birthday cake!  It gets 5 stars from us!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I had high hopes for this dessert. It was easy and quick enough to put together. But, eating it last night, I found the ratio of crumb crust to be too much, and the flavor of the total dish seemed just too sweet - I even bought the expensive pie filling with extra cherries and less sugar. 
I think if I make it again, I'd do more cream cheese/whipped cream, and add some citrus zest to it. Maybe even some sour cream? It just really needs a tart component.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The sour cream sounds like a good addition.  Same with the citrus zest.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@yamamama This was a 1960's Sunset Magazine recipe and did include sour cream----also had blueberry pie filling as alternate recommendation.
And, lemon juice !

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have my mother's handwritten recipe for this! (I'm 72) I'm going to share this with my grandkids because I want to keep the handwritten recipe card for now. They're 7, 10, 12 and can make this without worrying about the oven. They know how to use a mixer. Thanks for the reminder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
What a yummy, fun , easy dessert to prepare to serve tomorrow night for Christmas Eve family dinner. I put it all together in a 9” trifle bowl, though I didn’t use the full portion of crumbs for top layer. It does look quite special with nice layers.
I’m thinking I may adorn with some squirts of whipped cream before serving.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Wrote two days ago that I prepared this two days ago for Christmas Eve family dinner. 
I am delighted to say it was loved by all!!
Reviewing other comments I would say I prepared it all into a 9” trifle dish. The proportions were right on except for the crumbs. I reduced the top layer by at least half, and it was just right. The trifle dish allowed for distinct layering. I was proud to serve it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for a Christmas dinner dessert and it was a big hit. I didn't use canned cherries as I had frozen sour cherries on hand. I thawed and heated them and added 1 cup of up sugar and 2 tablespoons of cornstarch that had been mixed together and the juice from half a lemon. Brought to a boil and then cooled. I found the crumbs too dry and think next time I'll use more buttter. Also, I think the frozen cherries gave it better taste and bite.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I grew up with this dessert -  my dad's favorite. Our family recipe calls for a couple of Tablespoons of fresh squeezed lemon  - no almond extract. The fresh juice brightens the dessert and cuts the richness. These days, I use 2 good jars of tart cherry preserves instead of the can of cherries with bright red dye!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t like graham crackers, so I opted for oreos, and I used the tip of another commenter to bake the crust, which I did for 15 minutes at 350.  It smelled heavenly(like brownies) and stayed put in the pan. Made the assembly much easier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have been using Bowl and Basket (ShopRite) brand of canned cherries.  It is the only one I can find that does not have corn syrup/high fructose corn syrup, or artificial sweeteners. I use it in cherry kugels and cherry hamentaschen and have been very pleased with it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a Holiday favorite at our Midwestern house. As close to cheesecake as I ever got until I went away to college. Thanks for including this recipe cooks for everywhere can access the ingredients

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In our family we called it cherry glop. My grandma made it a lot, and not just for holidays! It was always a favorite for aunts, uncles, cousins, and more. I love that this recipe uses real whipping cream. I can’t wait to make it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cherry Paradox is they are the best fruit but also the only fruit where the artificial fruit flavor is even better than the real thing. It's the shot of artificial plus added sugar that makes the canned filling go to eleven. Going out for a cherry Fanta now.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mom makes a version of this but crushes pretzels with butter for the bottom crust. The salty with the overly sweet cherries and filling is chefs kiss 🤌

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you make this in individual glasses like you would a parfait?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not? It would take it back closer to trifle - but how can that be a bad thing?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: That sounds like a great idea!   I think I'll go to Dollar Tree and pick up some fancy-ish (disposable?) goblets to make/serve this in.  Not only will it look festive but there'll be a bit of potion control.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is pure memory bliss! In my family it was just called 'Mother's Cherry Dessert.' We should have called it 'Cherry Yum' because Mother's version was a bottom layer of graham cracker crust, a layer of cream cheese 'cheesecake' and then the cherry pie filling on top. Vaughn Vreeland's version gets the extra 'Yum' because of another layer of cheesecake and crust. It's an improvement on our family's old standard. 
Took it to a potluck last night and it was a winner!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have a similar recipe from my mother-in-law called a pretzel torte. It's the same cream cheese and whipped cream and cherry pie filling - but with a crust made with pretzels so it's salty-sweet. It's over the top when I make it with Italian Amarena cherries. When I take it to pot lucks, the pan comes home empty and I share the recipe with half a dozen people.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Meredith my mom does pretzels too!  I think Graham would be too sweet!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for my family's Christmas Eve dinner with no modifications. I tasted each component as I prepared them and got very nervous that it was going to be too salty. I am happy to say that I was wrong. After letting it set overnight and tasting the complete dessert, it was perfect. Thanks for a no-bake make-ahead dessert that allows me to spend more time with family and less time in the kitchen!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can make the cherry pie filling even better by adding a can of cherries, drained.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Christmas and it was a huge hit-people went back for seconds and barely any other dessert was touched, which was shocking!  I had the same issue as others in relation to the first layer of cream cheese filling lifting up the graham crackers, but I didn't use an offset spatula, so that may have been one of the issues.  However, if you watch Vaughn's YouTube video, he'll say that this isn't meant to be perfect, and clearly none of the 12 people who devoured this dessert cared!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: a half recipe will fill 12 125ml jars and they look really cute for gifting

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My grandmother made this with crushed pineapple in the summer. Thanks for bringing back a memory!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So easy to make and a crowd pleaser! My husband said it took him back to childhood Christmases at his grandma’s. They called it cherry cha cha

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I should add that I cut the sugar in the cream filling in half. Perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would suggest "Cherry Ho-Hum" is more accurate as written: instead, bake the graham cracker crust until browned and delicious (bake the extra crumbs on a cookie sheet and then crumble them), add more cream cheese (I used 1 1/2 times the recipe) and use an extra can of pie filling, but drain it and just add the extra cherries with the rest of it. Much more Yum-Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for NYE dessert for a group and everyone loved it! Found a new go-to recipe for a large group.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Forget throwing them in the river to see if they float, anyone who can spread the cream layer without horribly disturbing the layer beneath is definitely a witch. I even baked the crumb base to make it sturdier and didn’t help at all. (I know it’s all about taste and not looks, but I would’ve liked it to look a little more like Vaughn’s video)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I find it easiest to put large dollops of the cream across the pan, and then spread it by just going across the tops of the dollops. Be gentle and don't push down on to the bottom of the pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made this when I was a child, but not as a dessert, as a pie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn, you are delightful. I watched the entire video because of you. Hope you'll create more videos of cooking. Merry Christmas!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This makes a big dessert. It was great for my office party. But I’d like to make a smaller amount. Do you think it’ll halve easily? Btw, Bonne Maman just came out with some pie fillings. The cherry one worked really well for this. I am going to try blueberry next.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Richard Oh my, I’ll look for the Bonne Maman fillings! Always use their cherry jam for half of my thumbprint cookies! Thanks for the tip!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Richard Ciotti I always halve it in an 8x8 glass dish and it comes out great

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Canned pie filling is so yucky.  No fruit just glup.  I remember a time when it contained actual fruit, but over the years as costs have increased, the quality has declined so much.  If you want to make this, suggest cooked/sweetened frozen actual fruit, maybe thickened w a bit of corn starch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You need to be careful what you buy. Good cherry pie filling still exists.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Twas the night before Christmas and it was a hit.
Offset spatula (a smaller one) helps and work the cream from the sides toward the middle. Be patient.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We didn't love this. It just wasn't that good. I'm amenable to simple recipes and store-bought ingredients, but when we put this all together the flavor was just disappointing. The elements didn't really feel like they were complimenting each other. Don't make this unless you have access to a very high quality pie filling, or, probably even better, cook some fresh fruit with sugar on the stove to make your own. Lastly, this desert wasn't too sweet, if that's a concern.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those asking for a video from Vaughn related to this recipe, here you go: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6D-Je6R9ndU&list=PLYG6O_GQCZwhYieKJ8p2fsQqXwZuAOjuT&index=15

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for my cherry loving youngest son today!  I used two bags of frozen whole dark ripe cherries and made my own filling with lemon zest, juice etc.  two bags cooked up and cooled = two cans of cherry pie filling.  If I make again, I will add light brown sugar to the graham cracker crust.  Even with unsalted butter it seemed salty.  Good none the less, but I think it would be better just a little sweeter… a hit!  But it is rich….

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this for a family holiday gathering this year - I don't know why I bothered to bring anything else. A huge hit; I don't think I can show up without it next year. I used Biscoff cookies instead of grahams and American Spoon cherries. Went a little lighter on the top layer of crumbs. Delicious and fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: we called this desert cherry supreme. Used cool whip instead of cream. It still shows up at holiday gatherings every now and then. Its yummy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We needed to make this gluten free, so used GF Graham cracker to make the crumbs. I couldn’t find canned cherry pie filling so bought frozen red pie cherries and made the filling, very easy and quick. We honestly didn’t have high expectations for this, it looks really underwhelming in the dish but it was delicious, we loved it and will definitely make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made an almond flour crust and it was great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this for my Dear MIL who turned 96 today!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Surprisingly not too heavy and delicious! We made this for 12/25. Chose the recipe because of the name and that it required no baking. Halved the recipe and it was plenty. Half would be enough for 8-10 people with normal appetite. We will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just served this, using gingersnaps for the crust and apple pie filling. I won't use the kosher salt the next time--too salty. Other than that, it was a big hit. I have a request from my granddaughter for blueberry pie filling. Can do!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there an alternative to Graham cracker crust...I have a strong aversion to Graham crackers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Biscoff crust

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made this for Christmas Eve. If I were to change anything I would reduce the sugar a bit. That said it was pretty perfect and we'll definitely make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I ruined this recipe by dumping all the salt in the graham crackers. It was totally inedible. I recommend that the recipe should note that the salt is divided. Also, it just seems like too much salt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You likely used table salt instead of this type of Kosher salt. You have to use 1/4 the amount for it be equivalent.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Skip the salt if using salted butter, or if unsalted use just a tsp in the crust. Neufchâtel worked great here, too - had some available. It was softer than regular cream cheese, so didn’t need to wait for room temp before mixing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Am I the only one who put 2.5 tsp of salt in the graham crackers, step 1?  Come on.  Simple to add the word "divided" after the salt.  I agree with Kelly - we are in a hurry, and don't examine the recipe for 20 minutes before prepping.  Vaughn Vreeland, please add the word "divided" after the salt ingredient.  So common, and yet you skipped it, messing some of us up.  Unnecessary.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Decided to serve something very different for christmas as my son loves cherry. Followed directions, very easy to assemble. Tasted good. I would have  preferred less graham cracker just seemed like over kill. The family loved it though and are asking  for it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is very good but does not need nearly as much salt as it calls for.  Left it out entirely and just used salted butter to do the graham cracker crust and it was fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Think it would benefit from baking the bottom crust briefly......wouldn't be so goopy......

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious!  
I've made this twice now and am still having issues spreading the cream cheese-whipping cream topping. It keeps pulling up the graham cracker crust.  It's not too big of a deal because it all tastes so good I don't really care but I feel like i'm missing something or doing something wrong.  Perhaps my idea of a medium peak is too whipped?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is one recipe where a video demo would have been invaluable… Ok, so I couldn’t get specific pie filling and had to “doctor” it with corn flour, but the assembly isn’t for amateurs!  The taste outcome will certainly be as overwhelmingly caloric as imagined by the ingredients, but I’d like to watch how someone copes with the sticky ingredients and has them coming out looking as presentable as the photo!  Mine will be forgiven by a grateful and surprised—and greedy—family, but SHOW me please!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oof. Too sweet. I don't think I like the texture and goo of the cherry pie filling. I might like this with cut strawberries or other fruit. MaCall gave it a seven, the kids ate a little. Chris loved it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made this with blueberries, one small can plus mixed with a cup of frozen thawed fresh blueberries cooked with lemon juice to thicken. Which turned out to be more than enough as I halved the recipe, put in a glass loaf pan, and almost filled it! 
Salt doesn’t seem to bother this sweet dessert for me. 
I think next time the sugar and maybe the whip cream could be reduced because I’d like more of the cream cheese taste. 
I’ll also put 2/3rd the amount of the crumb mixture in the bottom.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Full of modifications, my Cherry Yum Yum was delicious. Cherry Republic brand cherry pie filling is far superior to the canned variety at the supermarket. It is in a jar and has a dark, fresh taste vs. the artificial red goo from the can. My attempt at this recipe began with halving it as I did not have 12-15 people coming over for dinner. When the grocery store did not sell graham cracker crumbs, I opted for a graham cracker pie shell which I crushed up. The result - yum yum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My grandmother would make something like this, using Cool Whip for the creamy layer. I loved it as a little kid, and then as I got older I cooled on it because it was so artificial. I may have to try this, as a throw-back!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wonder if anyone has a suggestion for how to make the top prettier. I want to make this for a holiday celebration. The top graham crackers just look ugly to me. Maybe I add a whipped cream and some mint leaves on top? Any ideas? Can you omit the top graham cracker crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Lise Thogersen
Cover or decorate with piped whipped cream😊♥️

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We had a very similar dish growing up.  We called it Cherry Delight- enough said!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My Mom used to make this when I was a kid, but we always called it Cherry Delight and I believe she used Dream Whip instead of whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My Mom made this also using Dream Whip from the recipe on the Dream Whip box (except there were only three layers: graham crackers, Dream Whip/cream cheese and cherries).  I believe the original recipe was called Dream Cheese Pie or something like that, but in our family, it's just known as "Glop" (probably because it "glops" all over when trying to take it out of the pan).

I am definitely going to try this recipe because I can't stand fake whipped cream - I despise Cool Whip.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m not a canned pie filling girl but I live with a person who will dig in the darkest depths of the pantry seeking that hidden can of filling and eat it on the sly and deny deny deny he had anything to do with the missing can when I go looking for it to complete the recipe I’m in the middle of making. He can’t understand why I fuss with pastry… so I don’t anymore. This is easier and he loves it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I halved this and made in an 8" glass pan. I only had ginger snaps, so used those instead of graham crackers. Turned out very well, everyone loved it! I think I will stick with the ginger snaps in the future, added a little something.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Every time I see a recipe call for a graham cracker crust, my first instict is to delete. I really can't stand graham cracker crusts. I don't like S'more because of the graham crackers. I like graham crackers on their own, just not as part of a recipe for something else. So! I look for other cookies I can substitute, like shortbread, chocolate wafers, or similar. 

How about Black Forest-style Yum-Yum with those chocolate wafers!?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I cut down on the sugar in the cream part? I like desserts that are mildly sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and made both graham cracker and Oreo crumb which I on top in a tiger stripe pattern (Tiger Yum Yum!). Fun recipe, easy pivot when my oven broke. I only regret not making my own cherry filling or using a better quality one (no shame on canned just that the one I grabbed was more goo than cherries).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is indeed yum yum.  I made it as written, had read the comments before I set out to buy the cherry pie filling, so I knew to read the labels and NOT buy an artificially flavored one. (EEK!)  

Next time I will see if chocolate graham crackers are as yum yum as I think they would be.  They are available in our grocery stores sporadically and on Amazon more often.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This!  My mother use to make this!  We all loved it. Thank you for reminding me!  It is easy for sure and a great place to jump off and create your own memories!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This reminds me of Cherry Cheese Delight which was a similar staple made by grandma at our family gatherings! Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there any way to cut down on the 4 hours in fridge ??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe says to "chill overnight or at least 4 hours". Don't you think you'd be risking it with 30 minutes? Trust the recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hello from Texas !!  I made this tonight and am sure it won't look like the picture for the recipe.  Someone in the comments mentioned 'glop', which is what we'll likely have.  I don't think I had the correct ratio of butter to graham cracker crumbs.  I couldn't "press firmly into an even layer."  Is the crust supposed to have the same consistency as pie crust? Mine was crumbs that wouldn't press down, which made it difficult to spread the whipped cream.  Any suggestions?  Thank you.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This looks easy and yummy! I won't have time to get the ingredients and make it for Christmas this year but I'll be making it next Christmas!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I haven’t tasted this yet, but I feel that I’m not Southern enough to master the recipe. That first layer of crumbs gave me a lot of trouble as it shifted with my application of the cream layer. Not at all pretty. I am not a salt lover, and I cut the amount of salt in half. I do believe that Southerners like more salt in/on their food than us damn Yankees: Even half the amount seemed too much to me. I hope I can remember to leave another note after I’ve eaten some of this.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Remember that the recipe calls for Diamond Crystal Kosher salt, if you are using Morton Kosher salt you should use less (a bit more than half as much).  If all you have is table salt  you need to use a bit less than half as much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy and tasty. My grown boys enjoyed it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used two cans of Oregon red tart cherries, cooked on the stove with cornstarch and sugar and butter, lemon juice, sooooo good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dessert by assembly, in the best possible way. It's perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can also use an immersion blender to pulverize the graham crackers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
#amyvan
so glad you put in the part about making your own thickened cherry mix. #amyvan
so glI bought four packages each 12 ounces of sweet, frozen cherries. No added sugar and spread two packages of frozen cherries over the recipe, then cooked two packages of frozen cherries with 2 tablespoons of cornstarch and a half cup sugar, till it boiled and chilled . The frozen cherries cooled off the boil mixture, so it didn't melt the cheese mixture underneath.

Looking forward to eating this tomorrow!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could this recipe be successfully halved?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I halved it! Fit perfectly in an 8-inch glass pan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I will be making this again and will not use salt. Too much salt in this recipe for my taste. Turned from Cherry Yum Yum to Cherry Yuck Yuck.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written with the addition on almond extract and in a 9x13 pan. My only complaint is how difficult it was to cut through and scoop up the bottom graham layer. Maybe I pressed TOO firmly? Otherwise a fun, easy nostalgic dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a variation of this recipe... I made an almond flour crust with stevia brown sugar and I used Splenda in the cream cheese layer. And otherwise halved the recipe because it was a small group.  It was delicious and everyone loved it. Next time I will figure out some sort of topping, possibly chopped nuts or diced maraschino cherries or something like that to give it a prettier appearance.  But I found this recipe inspiring and will make my variations again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made my own pie filling and made sure it was nice and tart. It was fine. My FIL loved it so much but he'd never been to a church pot luck. I used salted butter because I love salt in my desserts and that was noted and appreciated. For me, it wasn't anything special. Tasted like childhood church food.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Simple recipe to make, looks beautiful and everyone enjoyed it. Quite sweet. Lots of comments on how it tastes like an “old timey” dessert, in a nostalgic happy way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made it with biscoff cookies instead of graham cracker because it was all I had. Recommend halving the recipe it made a ton. It was nostalgic tasting, overall okay but didn’t get devoured like some of the other desserts (though I personally thought it was tasty). Probably won’t make it again since I hate wasting food.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe should denote separate salt amount for crust vs cream cheese mixture. I put all in the crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used to eat this during the holidays in Memphis, brought back some memories, although I have vague recollection that it was called something else... but definitely yum yum it is

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This reminds me of the chocolate lasagna that used to be served at every family reunion.  I appreciate that this recipe doesn’t use cool whip and may use that part of this recipe the next time I make that dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good God almighty but is this good! Thought it was going to be too sweet for 2 friends who aren't that into sweets but the 3 of us finished off half in one evening. Addictive!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Next time cut the sugar back - in half? Also, the top "crust" was kind of unnecessary. Next time make the bottom crust thicker and try piping the top cream filling on making it pretty. All in all it was delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Joining the salt chorus here. General consensus after eating this is that the Graham cracker was too salty. I used the appropriate amount of salt (divided it according to the instructions) and would half the amount of salt called for that section. Otherwise it was good and very easy to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My family made something similar to this and called it cherry cheesecake. It was definitely very tasty and something to look forward to! 🍒

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used salted butter for the crust (because that's what I had) and omitted the salt and it was fine. I cut the sugar to 1/2 c in the filling. I used the cherry pie filling available at Albertsons (Comstock/Duncan Hines) and it had an odd aftertaste, and was very sweet. I think next time I might just use a mix of frozen sweet and sour cherries.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have family members who don't like graham cracker crusts, so I use ginger snaps. And salted butter for the crust. Really tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great dessert. Used fresh cherries, so extremely sweet. Only suggestion is to cut down salt and sugar by a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make more whipped cream you won’t have enough! Call me stupid but I read one can of Cherry pie filling had to stop and run to the store! It’s a bit time consuming layering everything! Just made it wish me luck it’s Fourth of July and I decorated it for the occasion!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We had a bumper crop of tart cherries this year.  Many cherry pies.  Any reason you can’t use fresh cherries?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My 90+ ex-mother-in-law was famous (in her family and her community) for this dish. I always thought she was the creator and the person who named it. She died last year, but this beautiful and easy to make dish will live forever and is one of her cooking legacies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It is indeed Yum Yum. And Easy Yum Yum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just like the one use to mom make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this for a Valentine's Day dinner for my parents and we all swooned over it! Thank you, Vaughn Vreeland and NYT Cooking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes to the Yum Yum. Festive. Easy. Delish. No muss no fuss. Yum Yum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There is a company called Chukar Cherries in the upper west that features c heroes in all kinds of forms. I know they have cherry pie filling. The commercial ones are so full of starch you can't taste anything but that and sugar. Just saying.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Jeanie Diva Checked their website and a jar of cherry pie filling (not even cherries) was over 25 bucks! And that’s not including shipping. Wish there was a source for sour cherries near me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you replace the cherries with strawberries or another berry?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Strawberries, other berries, stone fruits (peaches, etc) and most other juicy fruits will work here. I just finished putting together a mango-lime variation for a party and I'm pretty happy with how the components taste. The hosts are doing a nacho-bar lunch so I opted for a more tropical flavor profile.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved seeing this recipe! It was a staple for my Mom, Grandmother and Great Aunts who called it Cherry Torte.  My Mom made it every time she hosted garden club.  I cannot wait to reintroduce this to my family.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This reminds me of my mother's cupcake sized cheesecakes made with the same cherry pie filling. They were delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This reminded me that last week my daughter asked about the "Dump Cake" recipe that was popular with my quilting group in the late eighties. It was a can of crushed pineapple, a can of cherry pie filling topped with a package of yellow cake mix and a stick of butter to make a sort of crumble. I have been wondering about the affect that the "shrinkflation" of cake mix sizes will have on the recipe and how I will compensate for the missing 3-4 ounces of mix.  Will chopped nuts be enough?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe sounds deliciously decadent. Would anyone have a recipe for making a cherry pie filling that tastes like canned but is homemade with whole ingredients?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mother made something very similar that was called, Cherry-O Cream Cheese Pie. The biggest difference was that the cheese layer was flavored with quite a bit of lemon juice. Of course being from Michigan I make my own pie filling with frozen tart cherries. I'm sorry that the rest of you don't have access to them but I'll admit it is small consolation for the access I lack to obscure ingredients that many of you take for granted!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mother made a different but similar dessert - 2 cans of cherry pie filling in a 9x13 pan, cover with one box white cake mix ( yes, right out of the box), sprinkle with sliced almonds, then drizzled a cube of melted butter over and bake. As kids, we loved it, especially with tons of whipped cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Our family makes a similar type recipe, layered and including chocolate pudding, cool whip, the graham crust, etc. We call it Sex In A Pan, because yes, it's that good. When we had littles around it became "That Pudding Dish." Can't wait to try this one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve been trying to wrap my head around this because I have some excellent bottled sour cherries that I lugged home from a small supermarket in British Columbia. But I went to too many Cub Scout potlucks in my youth, where desserts were cool whip and pie filling based, to be able to countenance this one. I am not longing for early sixties food. Music maybe, but not food. There is a recipe for Cherry Karpatka in this app that is based on choux pastry sandwiched with crème legere and cherries that sounds divine. I think I’ll try that one instead.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A true retro recipe would not call for Diamond Crystal salt. Morton's Iodized salt in the blue cardboard container is the way to go!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good!  Cherry cheesecake vibes.  This is a keeper.  Thank you for sharing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I make this every year, and every year I forget which is the better cherry pie filling brand. 
What are your favorites?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My husband was horrified when he saw the picture. His mom made this when he was growing up, but she only made 3 layers-graham cracker base, the cream cheese mixture, and then the cherry filling on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Tracy that sounds like the cherry delight (I’m guessing these two are variations of each other) that I had as a kid

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written and found this very salty. Did anyone else?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: While this was good, if you find the canned cherry pie filling to be a bit much - make your own.  Whether cherries or a mix of fruit, make a compote on the stove reduced to a nice thickness and let cool.  Then use as you would the canned stuff.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes yes yes! My elderly in-laws devoured this. Takes me back to childhood and so easy to assemble.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love it so much! Great for July 4th too❤️

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Oh my. Made these for a Christmas Cookie Exchange and I cannot wait to be the overall best in show winner! (... there are winners, right?)
It tastes like a "quad shot, half syrup gingerbread latte" because the espresso & dark sugar is dominant, and it's not too sweet. Like, this is a chewable latte. How? HOW! 
I somehow ended up with 23 generously sized cookies. I ain't mad. Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: King Arthur flour offers a special espresso powder just for this kind of thing. It isn’t instant coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious but fair warning: They are VERY caffeinated. I ate 3 before bed one night and tossed and turned all night. Still, I regret nothing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi all! I hope you love these cookies as much as I do. I'm a big coffee fan, and these don't shy away from the coffee flavor. I used both King Arthur espresso powder and Illy ground espresso when testing this recipe, and both yielded good results for me!
