Espresso powder is technically different from ground espresso, but it shouldn't change the chemistry of the overall bake if you have trouble finding one over the other. Happy baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These look wonderful.  I am concerned about such a large amount of expresso powder  - 1/4 cup.  Did you test the recipe for 1/8 cup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these about half the size recommended (~1 tbs balls) and cooked for about 9 minutes total- made about 45, and more of a snack size cookie. I think I would top these with some Demerara sugar next time- add a bit of a sugar-y crunch on top. They’re very gingery at first, followed by a nice coffee flavor, and you get a nice bit of heat from the fresh ginger that lingers at the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As a shortcut to grating fresh ginger, I used the little frozen ginger cubes from Dorot Gardens, available at Trader Joe's.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lynn H I just realized I have ginger in a tube. Wish I’d read your note before I grated that ginger!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve been buying Medaglia D’Oro espresso powder in my grocery store for years.  Delicious in all baked items.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Looks like they fixed the molasses weight

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excited to make these! With these, the eggnog snickerdoodles and the peppermint brownie cookies, you can make a whole Vaughn Vreeland cookie tin!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: for those who don't do caffeine: Amazon sells decaf espresso powder, Medaglia D'oro. The only place I can find it, and I use it all the time. Will try these cookies immediately!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do they have the powder or just the grounds? I've been looking for the decaf powder but can't find it. Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Molasses weight seems far too high - King Arthur's ingredients weight database shows 1/4 c of molasses at 85 g, so something seems amiss in this recipe - either the Imperial or the metric must be incorrect. I just made the batter with recipe with 158 g of molasses; we'll see if it firms up as it chills.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are spectacular and unique. My hubby says he feels "very sophisticated".  I refrigerated the dough overnight before baking.  I used Demerera sugar for the coating and added 1 TBSP of chopped crystallized ginger to the coating.  Crunchy exterior and super soft interior.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great cookie! If you can’t have the caffeine (or don’t like coffee flavor) you can leave out. I added a bit of cardamom (1/2 tsp) and King Arthur vanilla with orange blossom (1/4 tsp). Made 20 cookies exactly as described - soft and chewy and ginger forward. My new favorite ginger cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used half the amount of granulated sugar, and they came out with just the right level of sweetness. They are delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thanks for correcting the Molasses amount, Vaughn! I'm also finding a big discrepancy in the Espresso measurements using the Medaglia D'Oro Instant Espresso, where 1/4 cup = 16 gr. Anybody else have this problem? The first time I made these I used a full 22 gr. and it was overpowering for my taste, so I'm going to try 16 gr.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Suzanne  I agree. I'll try less expresso next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just read about how Norwegians pair ginger cookies with a slice of blue cheese. I think their cookies might be smaller and thinner. Makes me really want to try the combo!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi Julia, Swedes do this too!  We use gingerbread snaps (pepparkakor) for this purpose: they are indeed thin, and intensely spiced with ginger, cloves, allspice, cinnamon and cardamom.  Try putting thin slivers of blue cheese on top, and serving with a glass of bubbly or gluhwein (glögg).  Delicious as well as Christmassy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I normally get compliments from the cookies I bring to the office from recipes I get from NYT Cooking but people raved about these (and I did the recipe exactly as described).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy, no mixer, no surprises.  Very tasty and texture of crust crunch w soft chew. Wow. Like my cookies Molassis Chews with an intense coffee kick.  Full of flavor. A real treat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the recipe exactly as written, including using the correct espresso powder. I regret using the espresso powder in the coating because it made the cookies too bitter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I only had a teaspoon of dried ginger so added additional fresh ginger to the batter. I supplemented the dry ginger with a teaspoon of cinnamon for the sugar mixture. I left dough in fridge overnight and it was very stiff. Recommend less time or leave out at room temperature for 15 minutes before forming cookies. A delicious grown-up cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Medaglia D'Oro makes a decaffeinated Espresso powder.  I made my first batch using dairy free Earth Balance margarine and gluten free all in one flour and the King Arthur espresso powder.  Will make the next batch with the decaf Espresso powder.  I also substituted another 1/4 cup of brown sugar for the molasses as I did not have molasses.  Really good, powerful and pungent.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh wow these are killer! I added 1/4 teaspoon of black pepper with the other dry spices. I ran the espresso instant coffee through my food processor to make esperesso powder, and that worked great. I needed a bunch of cookies - more than the 18 as specified - so I made mine half the size - 1 tablespoon of dough per cookie and made 4 dozen. Watch carefully because I didn't need the full 10 minutes to bake - the first batch got a tad dark on the edges.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
One tip: keep your hands wet whenever working with a sticker dough like this and you'll have no problems with mess while rolling the balls. 

Otherwise, make these cookies as written. They are incredible!!! Huge hit and THE choice of cookie among many options. I will be making these many, many times.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For my taste, the coffe flavor overpowered the taste of ginger.  Next time I will cut the coffee in half, and omit in the coating.  Thanks to the reader about the King Arthur espresso powder!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazing.Fyi for those of you who felt like the espresso flavor was overwhelming, check your powdered ginger. On my first batch, I used some elderly ginger that didn't have much of a smell and had the same result with the espresso overpowering . I then used new powdered ginger, and wow, wow, what a difference! The flavor is well balanced and perfection!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @CJ I will refr to all of my old spices as elderly from this day forward.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Enjoying these as I write this. Don’t be afraid if the cookie still looks puffy after the ten minute mark, they will get hard as they cool. And for those who didn’t get a crackly top, I found that my smaller cookies tended to get crackly vs the larger ones.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Weighed all ingredients and chilled overnight, but these cookies spread into a puddle even 3-4 inches apart on the tray.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As a coffee and ginger lover, I had high hope for these, but they were just not good at all.  Very bitter.  If I were to make them again, I would remove the expresso powder entirely from the sugar coating and reduce it by 1/3 in the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't care for the texture of even the most finely grated fresh ginger, so I run it through the food processor and use the same amount of just the squeezed juice. I also add finely minced candied ginger and dried cherry cherries. This recipe is almost identical to a King Arthur Flour recipe for Cape Cod Ginger Molasses Cookies. I don't really understand the "latte" part. There's no milk or cream ingrefient--unless you stretch that to include butter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the "latte" was a little poetic license to refer to the espresso flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I'm an exp'd home baker & made these prior to Christmas so I could make modifications if needed. Yummy cookie but needs some work.

1) They spread, alot! I made them smaller (1.5 t size), they still spread - kinda. Some of them look exactly like the picture & others look darker brown with a more crinkled look vs cracked top & thinner. They taste great, but what gives with that? Not consistent in final product.
 
2) Increased the amount of espresso powder in the coating mixture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added some candied ginger to the sugar mix for rolling the cookies. They were a hit with my friends.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone perfected the measurements if you sub instant espresso for the espresso powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were (respectfully) awful tasting. I love coffee so I thought these would be a hit, but nope. I chilled the dough overnight and it was rock solid coming out of the fridge, I had to let it sit a few hours before it was scoopable again. All that work for nothing cause when they came out of the oven, the taste was almost burnt coffee like. No, the cookies were not overcooked, texture wise they were great. Taste wise thumbs down.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t realize that “espresso powder” is different from “instant espresso” so my cookies tasted more like a gingerbread macchiato than a latte. And I even reduced the instant espresso powder to 10g from the 22g listed in the recipe! Still, a delicious cookie that I will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish!!!  For added deliciousness make ice cream sandwiches with them. So nice with Vanilla ice cream. Or maybe try coffee, egg nog, or butter pecan ice cream.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: ***When making these or any cookie balls rolled in sugar mixtures, I measure and mix the sugars into a large vertical side mixing bowl, drop 3 or 4 or 5 hand-rolled cookie balls into it (depending on size of bowl) then pick up the bowl and quickly swirl them around in the sugars until they are all coated. (Don’t crowd the balls, they need space to roll around.) Then I place each on the baking sheet and roll the next few to coat. I find it faster and easier than coating each one singly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the recipe as is now written and it came out perfect! Right out of the oven, the cookies are spicy and the ginger flavor is dominant. Overnight the cookies mellowed to a nice blend of coffee and ginger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm disappointed! I didn’t have fresh ginger so used 4tsp ground ginger per 1/4tsp cloves. I can't buy instant espresso so swapped with coffee granules and preferred a lot less than written otherwise it tasted weirdly bitter (and my coffee is medium roast, it's weak to hardcore fans!). Ultimately it's a coffee flat molasses cookie, not a thick and chewy gingerbread "choc chip" cookie like I'd imagined. Hopefully it tastes better tomorrow (I used cookie dough overnight for best flavour and textur

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Holy smokes! I made the dough and refrigerated for 48 hours, then baked today. Except for having to adjust cooking time for my oven, these turned out fabulous. I’m going to have a very hard time giving these away. I’m wondering if next time I might experiment with a white drizzle icing. Regardless, these are going in the vault. The Vaughan Vault!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made me feel all warm and toasty - the flavors are spot on for a gingerbread latte. Made recipe as written, very easy dough to put together. Rested dough in the fridge overnight and baked off next day, I got about 20-24 cookies in my first batch. Will be making 2 more batches - so excited to bring these as my offering to the cookie exchange his year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question for the person noting that the molasses amount (in grams) is way off: Did your cookies turn out okay after using 158 grams??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi Amy! I would do about 80 grams molasses. Apologies! We're working on resolving that now :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No, I just baked them and they were goopy and spread way too far - which I thought would happen when I felt the consistency of the dough after overnight chilling.  We'll eat them anyway and adjust the recipe next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used instant coffee, and regreted it! Once added to the melted butter, it formed an almost solid mass that needed lots of coaxing (use a pestle) to break back apart. Lessons learned.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Now they have been baked, I am not sure I have loved a cookie more.  Such great flavors!!  

I used King Arthur gluten-free cup for cup flour; they were so good!  MAKE THESE for the entire winter, for all you know!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This happened to me too. I wonder why? I strained out the coffee solids and added a tablespoon of strong strong instant coffee and the flavor is good

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Another weight issue. My 1/4 cup espresso powder(medaglia d'oro) weighs 14 grams. Your recipe says 22 grams. Which is it???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: WARNING:  This is a cookie so good that you will stoop to hiding some so that other members of your family won’t find them and eat them before you do.  I feel terrible.  NOT!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rating this recipe under 5 stars should be illegal. Tastes like an edible seasonal latte, just as advertised.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn't realize until I started making these last night that all I had is Medaglia D'oro instant espresso powder so that's what I used. Not sure if you use the finely ground coffee vs. the instant powder if the amount is the same? The cookies were easy to make and tasted delicious! For variations on the theme, you could add a few small chocolate chips, candied ginger or nuts.  Great with a cup of coffee.  But definitely, will amp you up if more than one cookie is eaten!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made the batter and I realized I left out the molasses. Eek! Will the cookies still turn out ok? It's too late to add it after the flour has been added.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Learned the hard way to use a COOL cookie sheet to help avoid the giant spread. I had a cookie disaster although oh well - we will just have to eat them all vs giving away!
I will use 1 T scoop instead. Also I love the idea of using Demerara sugar so I will try that in the next batch…tomorrow!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies for the office and got absolute rave reviews including “best cookies I’ve had in 2024” “spot on” and “I ate three”… They are wonderful. A little crispy outside and perfectly chewy inside. The perfect holiday/winter cookie. I omitted the espresso from the coating but otherwise followed the recipe exactly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were so easy to make and soooo delicious. I am a big fan of chewy molasses cookies and these will be my new go-to. I used instant espresso, and I had slightly less than 1/4 cup, but they still turned out perfectly with a nice coffee flavor. Slightly crisp edges, thin and chewy throughout. Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these, they’ve become a family favourite . Had to make my own espresso powder but well worth it

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made these twice now and they have been a hit both times. I work as a barista and took some to my coworkers, who also highly approved. The only change I made the second time was that instead of getting an arm workout grating fresh ginger, I used a tablespoon of store-bought ginger paste. I couldn’t tell a difference in terms of the flavor and it made it so much easier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A white chocolate drizzle really amps up the visual impact of these.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are a “next day” cookie. I made these exactly according to the recipe. I’m at high altitude so I always expect flat cookies- check. The thing was, they tasted-out of the oven- like burnt coffee. I was so disappointed. I nearly threw them out. I saved them for no good reason. I tried them the next morning with my fingers crossed that they would at least be worth all the espresso powder I poured into them  They were heavenly! What a happy surprise! Can’t wait to share these!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: That seems like quite a punch! Cafe Bustelo is 2.6g per serving, so each cookie contains roughly half a shot of espresso?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Double the quantity of espresso powder if you want a full shot in your latte cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Or, like me, you can use decaf. I don't know about the rest of y'all, but I want to be able to eat one of these after noon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thank you for sharing. I would love these, but can’t handle caffeine, especially after midday. Can I use decaf instant coffee powder instead? (I’ve never seen decaf espresso powder)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This sounds like a caffeinated spin on my NYT cooking all-time favorite holiday cookie, Grammy’s Spice cookies. Can’t wait to try. Vaughn always comes through with the best Christmas cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there a decaffeinated substitute for the espresso powder? Doesn’t seem like you can add decaf coffee because it would add too much liquid. Can one grind decaf instant coffee crystals to a powder? Would that be comparable?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amy, in the US, it is possible to buy instant decaf expresso, though I sometimes find it hard to locate. Perhaps there is not an enormous demand. I'll bet it is also available on-line but I do not plan far enough in advance to make this work for me. Call me a Luddite.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could I sub maple syrup for the molasses?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made exactly as written and these cookies are so good. The espresso powder blends enough that i couldn’t even tell it was there but somehow it seemed to intensify the ginger. Yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought the 158 grams I used was way more than 1/4 c. (I bought a cooking scale at Melissa Clark's suggestion and now use it when I can, instead of measuring.) Still the cookies came out great, a little intense, but nothing that couldn't be solved with vanilla ice cream. I made ice cream sandwiches. Fabulous recipe! Looking forward to trying it with 80 g of molasses.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I only had 250 grams of all purpose flour on hand. What could I use as a substitute for those last two grams?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You could use whole wheat flour or bread flour in place of the missing all-purpose flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe does call for 80 grams of unsulfured molasses.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They are UNBELIEVABLE!!!! Best ever cookies. Love them

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these before the molasses adjustment and I thought they came out great. Definitely curious how much better they’ll be with the correct amount!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I knew I had to make this recipe the second I saw it. I'm prepping the dough as Argentina beats Australia in the knockout round of World Cup 2022. It's calming my nerves to bake. So far all I can say is the batter is absolutely delicious (and the game is even moreso). I'm bringing these to a friend's birthday gathering tonight. It's such a nice gesture to bring homemade cookies. Anyone can bring wine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was making these while watching the replay of the game, the NYT cooking comment section was the last place I expected to be spoiled for the results. That said, these cookies turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out even better than expected. The texture is perfect. I used Demerara sugar for the coating to make it extra crunchy. I made each cookie 40g and that gave me exactly 18. Such a rich, sophisticated flavor. Thanks Vaughn and NYT Cooking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lavonna, I just made these with Bob’s Red Mill gf flour and they’re delicious! I don’t think they’re quite as chewy as the glutinous ones (maybe a bit more cakey), but they’re still great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Note to self, don’t eat these cookies after 9p.  I was up all night due to the coffee in the cookie.  That said, it was an ok cookie.  The bottom tasted burned even following time to bake and rotate pans.  It didn’t remind me of a gingerbread latte.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree. One cookie kept me awake. This is a breakfast cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We enjoyed these but I'd like to know why I always have a lot of sugar left over.  I made most of the cookies 1TBS (after trying a few a 2 and finding them too big), so I should actually have used more sugar but either way I probably had 1/2 or a 1/3 of the sugar left.  Is there a way to pack more sugar onto each cookie?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This doesn't surprise me. I find this problem common with western recipe developers - just like excessive amounts of marinade, batter, dredging, etc. So much food waste SMH. I always use half the amount suggested, and it's usually more than enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these this weekend, wow! Will definitely become part of my Christmas repertoire.  One thing, the recipe calls for 22G of espresso powder or 1/4cp, by my scale 22G = ~1/2cp. I stuck with 1/4 cp, but might add a bit more next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m a huge coffee fan, but this was a bust for me. When you say latte, it should be a mellow blend of flavors. The coffee overpowers everything else in the cookie and the aftertaste is just straight bitter coffee. I would make again but take the espresso powder down by a quarter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One Tablespoon-sized dough balls seem just the right size for a cookie plate, and yield double the amount. Win win in my book!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Far too bitter for my liking. I enjoy espresso, but did not enjoy it to this quantity. I used a scale for the ingredients that had weights, but did stop at 18g for the espresso powder since it looked like way more than 1/4 cup. I want to give a huge thank you to those who reviewed with the correct molasses weight!! I would try again but cut the espresso powder from the final coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you can’t find espresso powder but have a mortar pestle use instant expresso.  That will quickly create powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Friends, a note. There is a big flavor difference between espresso powder and "instant" espresso" powder. I used the former and the resulting cookies were tinged with an unappealing bitterness/sourness. When baking I usually use instant espresso powder, which are more like tiny freeze-dried nuggets. Actual espresso powder is very finely ground beans, it's way denser and, I've learned, should be used sparingly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a medium roasted coffee ground a little finer than I would grind for espresso for both the dough and the coating and everything came out great. The issue could be the coffee you used was old (ground coffee oxidizes very quickly if not vacuum packed and frozen).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi! We can’t get anything other than blackstrap molasses where we live and it’s awful in cookies. Anyone try this one with a molasses substitute? How did it go? Any ideas about what I else I can use instead?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would try dark corn syrup, which IIRC, 1 part molasses to 3 parts light corn syrup.   Brown sugar also works.  You can search for ideas on the internet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought I burned these cookies but realized the espresso powder in the sugar made it taste a little burnt. Gingersnap part is delish but removed the espresso powder from the sugar coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Seems to be a lot of confusion here about espresso powder. If you use regular espresso that you use to brew coffee these will be bitter. Use instant espresso which is the same as espresso powder. If your instant espresso is more clumpy, you can just grind it up in a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Oh wow! These are amazing…maybe my favorite Christmas cookies ever. Followed the recipe (by volume) almost exactly. 

I rolled them only in Demerara sugar. I think the espresso/ginger is strong enough in the cookie and the raw sugar enhanced it, and looked great. After taking them out of the oven, I also pushed in some white chocolate chips on top of half of them, which added a delicious creamy (latte-esque!) flavor. Loved the ones without too though, especially with milk. Highly recommend!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used instant espresso powder because it's all I had and they still turned out great. The texture was perfectly chewy and I will definitely make them again. Next time, I will be careful not to get any stringy bits of the fresh ginger in my mixture because I ended up with a couple of strings of ginger in the cookies, my bad!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did something wrong.  My cookies spread over the pan and although they tasted fine, the look was horrible. :(   I can’t figure out what I did wrong.  Refrigerated for 4 hours and the had to bake them an extra 5 mins and the interior cookies were raw. I’ve checked my oven temperature and it’s fine.  Any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made this recipes three times in the last 10 days-here are my notes: 1. When combining the butter mixture w/ the dry ingredients, I recommend using a silicone whisk (cake batter whisk); a wire whisk will clump up you’ll be constantly banging it to remove the ingredients from its grasp! 2. The cookies that bake on the lower rack get the cracks look better than those on the top rack 3. You can flatten out cookies a bit more by banging the sheet on a flat surface after baking. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So half of my cookies burnt in the bottom. Any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Freeze the batter for 30 min and work quickly to shape the balls. The bake time should be the same; don't be afraid if they still feel puffy, they will harden quickly once cooled.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: don't put the sugar topping on the bottom of the cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the deep, rich flavor. I used a 1T scoop and they still ended up being 2 1/2” across — plenty big and made 32.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A minute or two too long in the oven gives u a darker almost burnt bottom sigh stay right there till u know how ur oven will bake this

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious. Followed recipe and they came out great. I did make 1 Tbsp balls as those weighed out to an ounce. They didn’t flatten completely out, but enough so you still got the chewiness

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I appreciate that these cookies do taste *exactly* like a gingerbread latte, but as far as cookies go I found them to be on the bitter side. Kids definitely won’t like them, which is fine—more for the adults :-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Okay.... stop the presses. This cookie is CRIMINALLY GOOD. I will say for our oven, the bake time was way off - I definitely needed an extra 4-6 minutes. But even for baking that long in my oven, they are still SO SOFT and chewy - the softest gingerbread cookie I'VE EVER had. But better yet, the ginger isn't overpowering at all, and the espresso does truly make it taste like a latte. I left mine in the fridge overnight before rolling and baking. Heaven in a cookie! Will 100% MAKE AGAIN.. A LOT.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They were good but they are a bit bitter and thus not a kid fave. Next time I'll try using less molasses and subbing some of the espresso powder for cocoa. They are excellent dipped in Masala Chai.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Two things:

1.) time - 35 mins is deceiving, it takes 20 mins to grate one tbsp of fresh ginger alone! Allow for much more prep time. 

2.) topping/sugar mixture - skip the ground ginger, it’s a bit harsh, and swap for cinnamon

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found that a 1 tablespoon scoop is a better size for these cookies. Also, make sure you don't skimp on the time the dough has to spend in the fridge! I did a double batch and 2 hours chilling wasn't enough. The cookies tasted great but spread out like pancakes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful rich aroma and the coffee and ginger/spice flavors blend well.  I appreciate the weight measurements, and for me the molasses weight was consistent with the 1/4 cup.  I also got about 25 cookies from the recipe.  I used coffee beans and ground them very fine in my coffee grinder.  Worked very well. Recipe is a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these once and made them again and doubled the recipe. Followed everything except cut down on espresso powder a bit. WOW! I thought i had a great gingerbread cookie recipe, buy this recipe knocked it out of the park. All the ingredient amounts worked for me (using old fashioned measuring cups and spoons) and everyone else who devoured these! Excellent!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Doubled the recipe - no judging :) - made 40 cookies and found the sugar coating quantities as described covered all 40.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! These have the exact look and texture of Lil Debbies but a more sophisticated taste. I might try making a sandwich cookies out of them for our Christmas platter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: ...going to add a bit of hot chocolate with cayenne to the rolling powder this time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is perfect, would not change a thing other than to double the recipe. They go fast. This will be my go to cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly; chilled the dough overnight. It was all going so well, but then the cookies cooked into one flat, thin blob! Can anyone advise me on how this could have happened? The flavor is great and I'll eat them, but was really hoping for an attractive cookie to share!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made almost every substitution possible and these cookies turned out amazing. 
Almond flour, coconut oil, maple syrup, coconut sugar, freshly ground espresso and still delish!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this with low glycemic brown coconut sugar. Amazing taste and texture. Cooked 9 minutes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe is great as written. I also made a variation where I skipped the sugar coating and frosted the cookies with vanilla buttercream. That mellowed the flavor nicely, like adding cream to your coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add vanilla ice cream in the middle of two cookies for an amazing sandwich!  You’re welcome.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were amazing! I had to cook a 2nd and 3rd batch they were so popular. Just the right amount of sweet yet zing - a grown-up ginger snap. The leftover sugar mixture is great in coffee as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
29 Jan 2023
Okay, 3rd round of making these. I see that the flattening out of the cookies may be related to softness of the dough balls. I prefer taller, rounder cookies than the flatter, chewier. Which means a very cold dough before rolling into balls & a longer baking time ~ 12-14 mins. 

I used about 1/3 c. Sugar & about the full amount of DK brown. I used kosher salt (lg grains) but would prefer small/fine grain distribution. Don’t like individual salty bites. Use fine salt & measure by wt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any recommendations for espresso substitutes for caffeine sensitive individuals. Perhaps cocoa powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is just a fantastic recipe! It is definitely an adult cookie and it goes well with coffee or tea in the afternoon, or a glass of red wine in the evening, esp by a fireplace.  And I'm not someone who gravitates towards sweets, desserts, and cookies, but OMG this crazy good. Absolutely perfect balance of spice, sweetness, and texture...{btw used King Arthur espresso powder for this recipe} Wish I had the option of giving it 6 stars!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious and a great shot of caffeine-we bake them for long road trips to keep us alert!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, but be aware of the amount of caffeine. After a sleepless night for the whole family, we decided these were ‘breakfast cookies’!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Bought Pumpkin Spice coffee at TJ's and didn't care for it. Ground it up and used it in this recipe instead of espresso and spices. Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this with 1:1 gluten free flour (Bob’s Red Mill) for family with allergies and it turned out fantastic!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these vegan also. I used Miyoko's unsalted butter and a flax egg. (two tbsp flax, 5 tbsp water) and they came out perfect. And they taste fantastic!
A delicious adult cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cooks Illustrated had a great hint a while back for cookies like these: Pat the dough in a square 8 or 9 inch pan, lined with plastic wrap. Flip over onto a cutting board, and cut into squares (so, 6 by 6 is 3 dozen cookies; 8 by 8, 5 dozen plus 4, etc.). Roll into balls in after cutting. This way, each portion is fairly similar in weight and size to all the others; saves a lot of time, too. (This also works well for making meatballs!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i followed the recipe exactly but my cookies are so thin. This has never happened before. No idea what i did wrong

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Those of you who found their cookies spread: Did you use parchment paper/silicon pad, or did you grease the pans? If the latter, that was your problem. I just made a batch, precisely following the directions, and they look great and taste delicious. There is a lot to be said for following directions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these for the first time and WHOA are they good!  I added about 1/4 tsp cardamom too.  I also made 1T sized balls and got 45.  They are just delicious.  Thanks Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Outstanding and easy recipe for a very adult cookie. I only made two adjustments- added 1/4 tsp of white pepper and pastry flour instead of AP. Bravo!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
To avoid hyping up kids, I used a generous 1/4 c of decaf Cafe Bustelo instant espresso and they are delicious! Because of the coffee texture, it weighed more like 15 g. 

I ground it up a finer powder to roll the cookies. I used a coarse sparkling sugar instead of granulated and the espresso powder didn’t mix evenly but they still look great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I tried making these according to the instructions, my spices/espresso clumped disastrously in the melted butter, and I had to start over. On my second attempt, I had success with the traditional creaming method. (This was probably user error, since no other commenters seemed to have the same issue.) That said, these cookies are excellent, and the espresso-ginger sugar is a perfect finishing touch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same thing happened to me. Second time I added the spices to the dry ingredients. That worked well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were good. I used 6 tablespoons of butter instead of 3/4 of a cup by mistake (I had sticks in mind when I saw the 3/4). I discovered this when the dough was chilling the the refrigerator so it was too late to fix. I used a 1 tablespoon scoop (needed more than 18 cookies) and baked for 8 minutes and allowed them to cool for 3 minutes before removing to a wire rack. The reduced amount of butter did not negatively impact the cookies. They were soft with a nice, chewy texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow!  Easily the best cookie I made for the holidays this year and it may well be the best ever!  Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing cookie, stayed chewy for over a week (which is how long they lasted.) I followed directions and weights exactly. I won't wait too long to make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: so delicious, fully of lovely flavors and the texture is just perfect!! i made my cookies pretty small so i got at least 50. baked for 9 mins. rolled them in green and red sugar for a festive look!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are absolutely delicious. The ginger and espresso flavors together are fabulous and cozy. Perfect holiday cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these and they are delicious! I was half way through when I realized I didn't have any nutmeg so I threw in some pumpkin spice which has some. Next time I'll be sure I have more nutmeg, but I can't imagine these being any better. The only thing is my did not flatten like it said they would. I used a rolling pin to flatten them out, otherwise they were quit thick and high. Not sure why that happened but no bigger. A real winner.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies exactly as directed.  Cookies had no structure and melted into each other when baking.  Also had a very bitter flavor from the espresso.  Flavor was not balanced at all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were GREAT!  Easy to make and very yummy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made these with gluten free flour? If so (and if you had a good result), which flour did you use?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you only coat the top part of the cookie dough the bottoms will not burn as quickly

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For the commenters with cookies that spread - I used to have this problem until I stopped overmixing eggs. This recipe calls for the egg, etc. to be mixed for 2 minutes. I left the egg out and mixed for 90 seconds, then added the egg. Also, since this recipe called for extended refrigeration, I used baking powder instead of soda. My cookies rose and did not spread. Thanks to the reviewers who mentioned a caffeine buzz - I halved the expresso powder,  1/8 C, which came to 8 grams for my brand.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 1/2 c sugar only (too much sugar!), spelt flour, chilled dough overnight, rolled in sugar only, and flattened with glass bottom before putting in oven. Came out perfectly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very tasty.  Just leave lots of room, these guys SPREAD out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
5 stars. At first glance the ingredient list does seem like a lot for cookies, but if you have all the spices on hand it's not bad at all. Other than hand whisking the butter step, I used my stand mixer to mix everything else. I prefer to weigh my ingredients so for everything that had a weight listed, I used that. 
The cookies taste like molasses cookies, which I honestly never choose to make or eat, on steroids. They are picture perfect and made the house smell like fall. Will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved these! I used Cafe Bustelo instant espresso, in a quantity that was somewhat less than given in the recipe (because I wasn’t sure what I had on hand was the same thing as “espresso powder,” and it seemed like a lot. Anyway, they turned out perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I make these w/o the espresso powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't understand this recipe. Where does the butter espresso mix come back into the mix? It says set aside for 5 minutes, but nowhere in the directions does it say when to mix into the dough??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Step 1 has you adding ingredients to a mixing bowl and in step 2 you add things to that same bowl and proceed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies turned out pretty good, but I am having huge issue with the bottoms burning. Decreasing the bake time only made them not set correctly. Is it possible the issue is I was only baking one sheet at a time? These taste so good minus the minimal browning on the bottom that's giving a slightly bitter taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are the best cookies I’ve ever eaten, and I’m 37. I’ve eaten many a cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Best ever! Made 1.5 batches. Here’s some modifications if you don’t have espresso powder or fresh ginger around:
For 1.5 batch recipe:
• Sub 4 TB espresso for espresso powder (I made some with an Aeropress.)
• Use 1 less T butter
• Add 2 T flour
• Sub 1 t ground ginger for fresh ginger
• Roll in sugar & ginger only

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these year for the office; will make them this year for my neighbors (and me). Love a cookie that is complex and tasty. Only downside is that people expect them to be chocolate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great! However a recipe that yielded only 18 cookies is a nonstarter at Christmas.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these and put the dough in the fridge overnight and they still puddled during baking. What did I do wrong?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Used King Arthur Flour's Espresso Powder to produce these cookies. 3 tbsp instead of 1/4 cup of espresso powder is more than enough in the dough. 

Otherwise, a fun cookie for the holidays!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 10 minutes was not enough bake time.  added 5 minutes and they were perfect.  might make them smaller next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these on a whim, on Christmas day, when I realized I had all the ingredients. Lovely cookies,  easy to make, their scent drew my husband out of his den (no mean feat that!) I should have baked them a minute or two longer, so the edges would have crisped a bit, but they still have good mouth feel. They taste like the essence of my childhood winters - warm, spicy, and a bit coffee-ish (we're from Rhode Island where kids get coffee milk from an early age.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this and we all loved the flavor but they did not spread out and flatten as they cooled. Has anyone else had this outcome? I would have liked a flatter, chewier cookie. Having said that, I will try again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Margaret S.  Mine didn’t flatten either, so now I flatten with a glass before baking.  That seems to work.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've been conducting an experiment with friends and family, serving them a variety of 5-star cookies. This one wins every time! My favorite as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Nescafé decaf instant espresso powder.  All the flavor without the caffeine.  They were a hit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have made these many times following the recipe exactly. They are delicious. I have given them as gifts and everyone raves about them. They will be my go to cookie for the cookie exchange this year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For the coating I used Demerara sugar and finely chopped crystallized ginger for  crunch and kick-these are  absolutely fantastic. Best snack ever for a roadtrip!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really delicious. The coffee is more of a background note, not leading the charge. Really nice and spicy. If you do 2 tbsp scoops know that they spread A LOT. They’re gigantic (like bakery size). For the second half, I made 1 tbsp scoops and that made a much more reasonably sized cookie. Baked for the same amount of time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would recommend only rolling half of the cookie in sugar. I find that if there’s sugar on the bottom half it usually burns (maybe an issue with using silpat mats?). I also had issue where the espresso powder and ginger clumped together into a burnt black sludge. I was able to mostly strain it out. But would recommend letting the butter cool slightly and whisking in each ingredient separately

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Holy spiced latte, batman! These are INTENSE. I thought I liked spiced cookies but these are too much for me. I followed the recipe to a 'T', but omitted the espresso & ginger coating, opting for brown sugar only. Now to find someone with a stronger consituation than mine to give these to!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They look beautiful.  They are not too sweet.  I found them to take significantly more than 10 minutes of baking.  They were portioned according to recipe so cookie size was not the reason for longer bake time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious and complex!  I halved the espresso in the cookie dough and used cinnamon in the sugar coating (in place of the giner).  A hit with my entire family!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are still my favorite holiday cookie. I baked these for coworkers this week and they were blown away by how good they are. The coating mixture amounts actually make enough for 2 batches of these likely. 10/10! Don't skimp out on these!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Good recipe with amendments by others. 
Only used the dehydrated coffee powder in the sugar coating not the dough, allowing the ginger to stay flavor forward.
If you chill overnight, allow an hour at room temp?  The dough was very stiff
 at 30 minutes. 
#40 scoop worked well and yielded 26 cookies
I used light brown sugar because that’s what I had. 
Hubby approved.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is espresso powder simply finely ground espresso beans-or instant coffee?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s not instant coffee, it’s dehydrated, Instant Espresso.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really works....delish......

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yay! So excited to add another V.V. beverage-themed cookie to our Christmas baking list. My kids get so excited about the eggnog snickerdoodles!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As someone with an espresso machine - would substituting brewed espresso work? I wonder about the liquid interacting with the cookie batter as a whole. I do have espresso powder I can use for the coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the idea of these with his other cookie recipes—those are just perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What could I use as a substitution for the egg in this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
You can do a flax seed egg if you're looking to go vegan. 1 tbsp flax seed meal, 2.5 or 3 tbsp water. Whisk well and let sit for a couple minutes.

Vegan butter also worked well for this recipe. Looked exactly like the picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m wondering that too. Maybe a flaxseed meal “egg” would be ok.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @James A Thank you! Always looking for ideas for my daughter who has a severe egg allergy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve not heard of espresso powder here in Australia soI’m with Debbra and also curious if it’s just finely ground coffee beans - which I can do as I’ve got a decent coffee grinder. Any advice? Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Marie, in the US, there is instant espresso powder, which is not ground beans. “Medaglia d’Ora” is the brand I have; I bought it at grocery stores and Target. Regular instant coffee would be fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No. Espresso powder is basically instant coffee (at espresso strength). I've used the brand Medaglia d'oro (an Italian one) since that's what I can find most easily, though even that can be a challenge at times. Didn't know that King Arthur (an American milling company specializing in flours and baking supplies) made one. I'll have to check that out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve not heard of espresso powder here in Australia, so I’m with Debbra and wondering if it’s just finely ground coffee beans? Any advice would be great thanks as I’m keen to make this recipe 😊

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you think these could be made as a bar cookie?  Any tips?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There is a lot of advice available on-line about how to turn a standard cookie recipe into a bar cookie recipe. Just google, and you'll find some articles that discuss the details. Usually the conversion involves adding eggs to increase moisture and decrease crumbling, but you may need to make other adjustments, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious! Next time I’m doubling the recipe. I used KA expresso powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where can you buy espresso powder?  Can you substitute for expresso?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried making these as bar cookies like brownies? I never have time for regular cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these today and they’re delicious! Mine were cake like, not crisp as I expected.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you sub coconut palm sugar for the white and brown sugars to lower the glycemic index?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you think I could sub butter flavored Crisco for the butter? I have a young friend with a dairy allergy, but her dad likes gingerbread.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You definitely can, it might change the thickness of the cookie, but lots of old cookie recipes used "shortening" which would be Crisco or a mix of butter/shortening. If I remember correctly butter was rationed during WWII and cooks used shortening instead in recipes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The Spice House sells espresso powder, they are based in Chicago but have an online store. Have used their products for years!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there an espresso powder alternative? I have ground coffee beans, would that work?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What type of unsulphured molasses? light, dark, or blackstrap?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't have fresh ginger. Can I sub cardamom? If so, how much? thank you for any help.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made this with gluten free flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would like to make these gluten free for someone.  Do-able?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies are absolutely delicious as is! Just one thing to be mindful of, when I did the math two of these cookies equals the amount of espresso grounds you would use for a shot of espresso. So if you are caffeine sensitive, maybe use decaf or avoid eating these at night!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a half batch of these today to test for an upcoming holiday party. I used a 1.5 tbsp cookie scoop and got 12 cookies nicely sized cookies. They’re a great flavor and consistency and turned out well. Next time I’ll try demerara sugar for the coating or maybe even a light frosting. They’re just a little too bitter for me as is. Perhaps a little lighter on the molasses would help too?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
We love these - even with the original molasses overload! Such interesting, complex flavors. Mmmm… 
I used sparkling sugar rather than white sugar in the coating because it adds crunch and - well, we can all use a bit of sparkle!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you freeze the cookies (prior to baking), do you bring them back to room temperature?  Or  bake frozen, and if so do you add any cooking time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What difference would 158g vs 80g molasses make? I should have read the comments first - I made the recipe as is, though smaller, and they taste great but a bit gluey in the centre (as opposed to chewy). If I used 80g molasses how do you think they might be different - maybe a little firmer? Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Ps I used about 15g quantity of Moccona
Instant coffee granules and 7g Dutch press cocoa - a substitute suggested online for the espresso powder which I couldn’t find even in a speciality shop in Australia. which worked well. Good flavour overall.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: what is an alternative to espresso powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cocoa powder works well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved this recipe!  I’m a sucker for ginger cookies but not a coffee fan but this called out to me anyway.  I halved the espresso powder and found it perfect.  Our friends loved the too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. They rise to a pillow in the oven then settle down in cooling. They remain moist and richly flavored.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this gluten free yet?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these as written yesterday and took to game night with our neighbors. Everyone thought they were delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly as written. Holy smokes, these are so delicious. As another commenter said, they start good and gingery, then the espresso comes through, just enough to know it’s there. Finishes with a fresh ginger kick. Nothing is overpowering, but certainly assertive. Texture is crisp outside, chewy tender on inside. A winner!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Saved this to my cookie collection of 99, this recipe went straight to #1 from 100. Made a batch- gone, second one doubled, disappeared. Now the last triple batch this weekend, I’m out of ingredients and lil tired. Although they are so easy to make (I’m wary of fast& easy as often less effort diminishes outcome). Such a bombastic adult flavor, just wow. Didn’t change a thing, perfect,  but thinking of browning the butter next batch. Single recipe made 26 cookies, dough measured at 30 grams each

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A lovely little cookie that has a mildly delicious coffee flavor. I used instant coffee crystals. I recommend baking higher in the oven, the cookies that were baked lower down had a bitter taste to them. I'll make these again for sure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you make these cookies gluten free (1 to 1 King Arthur GF flour)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The sugar coating makes a lot extra. Could cut proportions and still have plenty. I actually double-coated and still had 1/3 of mixture left over that I threw out since it had had raw sigh rolled in it

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing either the dough or the baked cookies? Preference either way? I'm trying to front-load my Christmas baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have never found a Christmas cookie that couldn't be frozen. Just be sure to package in an airtight container: That is important.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lisai have made these many times and they are a perfect chewy cookie. I tried freezing the balls for the first time (for a cookie exchange) and they are coming out dry and crispy. I am going to start all over again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Since I don’t drink much caffeine, I wanted to make these with decaffeinated espresso powder. I used Starbucks instant decaf coffee, which makes tolerable instant decaf.  (I’m pretty sure good instant decaf is non-existent.) The cookies are good but the coffee flavor is much stronger than the ginger.  Not sure if that’s the recipe or the coffee I used. I would make them again but add some chopped candied ginger for a bit more ginger punch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow, these are incredible! Mine turned out a little puffy and cake-like, yet I kinda like them for that! I got 24 1-ounce cookies out of it. I think next time (and there will definitely be a next time) I will use turbinado sugar in the coating—along with some of the Hair Bender Jacobsen salt  in our pantry.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried an espresso powder alternative? I have decaf ground espresso and was considering subbing this in brewed in a moka pot, but am concerned about the liquid to dry ingredient ratio with that

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think it is risky to substitute a liquid (brewed espresso) for a solid (the dry espresso powder). I am an experienced baker, but I wouldn't do it. Why not just use the espresso powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Realized I was out of espresso powder as I started to make these so I substituted cocoa powder. Absolutely fantastic and a major hit with friends and family. Definitely making again with the correct powder later in the week for a holiday party!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone discovered if this cookie delivers too much caffeine in a couple of bedtime cookies? If I am kept awake, I will just finish off the rest of the cookies I guess....:)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would love to hear from bakers exactly what espresso powder brand they are using. The video that goes with recipe shows a brand of instant espresso, not bakers espresso powder. Also anyone have issues with instant espresso in the coating ?  My brand is a granule—not a powder.  While it worked in the hot butter I’m not so keen to roll dough in this product. My brand is Ferrara instant espresso.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m in Canada and don’t have access to the Medaglia D’Oro espresso powder those in the US recommend so I used Nescafé Gold Espresso Powder. 22g of this espresso powder is more than 1/4 cup — I went with the lesser, 1/4 cup. I like coffee flavoured things (latte especially!), but even 1/4 cup was too much. Next time, I’ll half the espresso powder and toss in Demerara sugar like others have suggested. If anyone in Canada has a recommendation for another espresso powder to try, please comment!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Also in Canada! Don't know a substitute, but I also used 1/4 cup of Nescafe Gold Espresso powder. I ended up adding a lot more white and brown sugar to compensate, and rolled in turbinado only, and they turned out great! Also made them small enough to stack inside my Nescafe Gold empty jars, which made perfect gifts :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Looks like the weight of the molasses has been corrected.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would it work to substitute finely chopped crystallized ginger for the fresh ginger?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe as written and think it's the perfect cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Are these crispy like a ginger snap or more chewy? I’m adapting them to gluten free and so far they are crispy. It’s a work in progress though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cookie is a winner! Made them for a dinner party last night. All gone. Gorgeous texture and flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Great recipe and one that I'll add to my staples! Some notes for next time/future bakers:
- Molasses & espresso were strong, I would cut by half on both.
- For a dairy-free version, I subbed coconut oil for butter. The shape and texture were a bit different (doesn't spread as much as the butter version & is a little more cake-like) but it was still great! I'd do it again. Just be sure to set the right expectations.
- For a low-sugar version, I subbed monkfruit for the sugar and it worked nicely.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think I like regular ginger cookies better. These were good, but I didn't think the espresso powder added that much to the cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Awesome cookies, should definitely be rolled into smaller balls or thumb printed before baking to allow for correct texture and height.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How long did it take to flatten like the photo, if at all?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I made them they came out of the oven slightly puffy but with the desired craggy surface. As they cooled, they flattened out as in the photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added the espresso powder by weight and the amount seems excessive. It came out to more than 1/4 c and the coffee flavor is very overpowering.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious! So easy, and they’ll be perfect for the cookie exchange. Because I believe in “enjoy good things in moderation”, I made the cookies a bit smaller, baked for 9 minutes, and it yielded 34 very good-sized cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved this cookie but have to warn that they didn’t fly off the plate like other varieties.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fantastic cookies! The molasses weight measurement has been fixed. I was concerned I was burning them but it's the scent of roasting coffee. Since it's hard to tell with the color I thought it was worth mentioning. If you're a ginger cookie lover (and who isn't!) these are a must try.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make these cookies last night to give to colleagues and clients. One colleague just told me the cookie melts her mouth! They are delicious and so easy to make. Will probably make another batch soon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I prefer the sugar coating without the ginger and espresso powder  to offset the spicy, slightly bitter cookie. Place the butter in a mixing bowl and melt by microwave to make this recipe one-bowl.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Espresso powder is not the same as instant espresso. Go easy on the instant espresso. 22 grams of instant espresso is quite a bit over 1/4 cup. I added 12 grams an it packed quite the punch. No one complained! They were wonderful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Espresso powder and instant espresso powder are the same things. Are you thinking of finely ground coffee for making espresso? This would not be used. Also, an espresso grind is quite fine but not powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies but decided to leave out the Espresso powder. They were so delicious and didn't even miss the coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Substituted the espresso powder for cocoa powder - I also added chopped up candied ginger to the sugar mixture coating - came our delicious!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh my god these cookies are amazing. I used very finely fresh ground espresso beans and it worked so well! It wasn’t gritty or anything like I’ve seen on some websites. I did use less than 1/4 cup, probably half of that for the dough mix. I made these as a trial run before Christmas and I’m definitely going to be making them again, year round. Thank you so much for this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious!! Amazing texture or flavors. Just note that young children might find the flavors too strong, particularly the espresso and ginger. At least my 8 year old did. But my 13 year old, who baked them, is loving them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m wondering if this dough could be frozen.  If not, could the baked cookies be frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are unbelievable. I’m not a big ginger fan, and the idea of a gingerbread latte makes me cringe. But the comments made this recipe irresistible. And they lived up to hype. I coated them with turbinado, as another commenter suggested, and that took them to yet another level. I’ll be making these again. And again and again and again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used instant espresso since that's all I could find locally and it seemed to work just fine.  Also had some espresso salt in my pantry and added that to the sugar/ginger/espresso at the end and honestly I thought that made the cookie.  Mine were puffy rather than flat -- not sure why.  Overall, a huge hit in our house.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: OMG .. this are da bomb! I realized I did not have fresh ginger so I added a little more of the ground. I'm definitely making these again and will do it with fresh. On the suggestion of some others I used Medaglia d'Oro instant espresso - good call! This is a keeper in the rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were great - soft and chewy with an intense flavor, but I would skip the espresso powder in the coating next time - a little too bitter for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: SO GOOD!!! They honestly make a nice little breakfast cookie if you’re feeling indulgent. I made 24 tho the recipe says it just makes 18. Could not recommend more, esp for the coffee lover

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Everything you want in a gingerbread cookie - without the fuss of having to chill the dough, roll it out, chill again, roll and repeat.  The flavor is great! The crackle is fun!!  As I don't keep espresso powder in my baking stash, I take ground coffee and grind it even further to mimic espresso powder. It works out fine.  I was a bit worried by the 1/4 cup of espresso powder in the dough, but it just dissolves and add a wonderful richness to the molasses and dark brown sugar.  This is a keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a bit of a labor of love. They required more work than my typical cookie recipe, but I thought they paid off. The result was a chewy, spicy cookie, with just the right amount of coffee flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughan could you BE more adorable?? This is the best cookie EVER, and wins the best NYT video of the year.  How can I share the full video and not the short one on the IG account? I want to share the Christmas joy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these awesome cookies today, will try another batch without the espresso, think they’ll also be good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made these cookies tonight. Missed that the recipe requires fresh ginger but they had plenty of flavor without it and the nutmeg which I don’t like. Did not add ginger to the sugar at the end for fear that the flavors would be too strong for some. They are delicious but not for every palate. I like them but I bet my husband who loves sweets, will not. Similar to the Whiskey-Soaked Chocolate Cake I love.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Regarding M's molasses comment. Is there something we are missing? The recipe states 80g and 1/4 cup. Unless it was updated for the error, I don't know the 158g 1/2 cup comment came from. Really confused me. The recipe is correct

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It was updated, and noted in the comments. So the 80g is right and current,

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I omit espresso powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made two different batches of these cookies because I didn't particularly like them the first time. They had a tendency to burn and just tasted sooooo intense. Bought new spices and a different coffee powder to try again, and they were even more intense the second time. Unfortunately, just not a pleasurable dessert for me. (Note: I used Bustelo espresso powder the first time and a decaf instant coffee the second time.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love this recipe as well as the video.Wondering if you can freeze the cookies after you bake them. Thanks and Slainte !

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious. Made them exactly as recipe specifies, but mine did not flatten out. Made 30 smaller sized cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just finished baking those, and they smell fabulous. I added 2x molasses because I did not check the volumes, hope it will not affect the flavor too much. My cookies did not have the crackle, does anyone know why and what I did wrong ?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was finishing gingerbread biscotti and had no coating for the edge and then remembered your recipe! My powdered ginger was rancid, so I used galangal, results were amazing. Going to make your original cooky today, gluten and dairy free. Thank you!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought these were a little heavy on butter. I'm no food chemistry expert, but what would happen if you were to reduce it to 1/2 cup rather than 3/4? Otherwise absolutely delectable!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies really surprised me with all that espresso in the mix.  They're great.  And they must be accompanied by a big glass of cold milk!  And given that dark brown sugar is just molasses added to the granulated, I used a full cup of granulated and added 2 tsp more of the molasses.  Fortunately I was going by volume not weight.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn  these are amazing but we need way more than 18 cookies  at this time of year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had Becky Ross

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these for a fundraiser bake sale.  I am a semi-retired Chef and I really shouldn't have brought them, but I made up for it w/2 sourdough Challahs ( shout out to Claire S...I have adapted your Challah to be sourdough...oh my) and yummy miso white chocolate cookies from this site.
The Cookie recipe was runny and definitely not crackly and well domed and tender inside as one expects in a ginger cookie.  I hope this is the corrected recipe, as would give it another go...
LUK!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are CAFFEINATED. They are so delicious, but I’m afraid people will be turned away after taking a bite of it. It gets better as you keep going, but that first bite is crazy. I’m thinking I might scale back to 2 tbsp of espresso powder and maybe adding a little bit more ground ginger to the mix. The texture of these cookies are unreal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are intense!  (And I’m a coffee lover). Topped with powdered sugar to help cut the spicy afterburn.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Outstanding.  Used 2 1/2 tbs instant espresso; 1 1/2 tsp fresh ginger.  For rolling, sugar, 1 tsp espresso powder, 1/4 tsp cinnamon, 1/4 tsp powdered ginger.  Baked just under 12 min for 20 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These look amazing and just right for this season. I was wondering, if I don't have access to molasses, is there anything I could substitute that would work?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies today and just ate one hot off the sheet because I couldn't wait!  I love my coffee, but I find these to be a bit too coffee forward for me, and possibly too caffeinated (we'll see in 4 hours....) 22 grams of espresso powder was WAY more than 1/4 cup! I will try the recipe again using 1/4 cup as a measure instead of grams or trying decaf espresso.  I used Delallo Instant Espresso (think I got it at William Sonoma)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would this dough keep in the fridge for 2-3 nights?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The process of making the recipe smells incredible! I subbed the espresso powder for decaf instant coffee at a 1:1 ratio. I made the instant coffee crystals finer by grinding them up with a mudler. They turned out great, but had a more bitter coffee finish, which I don’t mind! I think I would pair this with a scoop of vanilla ice cream to balance it a bit more. I weighed each cookie out to 28-31g each before baking and ended up with 25 cookies total.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as is and turned out soft and delicious. Few people complain about the coffee taste being overpowering but as someone who is more on the savory side than the sweet, this makes these cookies perfect for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found these really bitter due to rolling in the ginger mix before cooking and I would omit that in the future

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like the flavor of these baked as directed and even chilled, frozen, and also attempted at a lower temperature, these came out flat and a real texture failure for me. Disappointing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Flavor was great, mine didn't crinkle like the photo tho

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Wow wow wow 
Definitely do the fresh ginger it adds the perfect kick
The flavor is incredible and I ate four before I dipped any in milk  😉

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe as written, and was very excited. Unfortunately the cookies were super bitter. We tried to like them but ended up trashing. Can't recommend, and sad about it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies had the best texture and was one of the most beautiful cookies I’ve ever made. My big mistake was to sub Starbucks Dark Roast packets for the espresso powder (as in my brain this was the most “espresso-y” sub available to me). I use this for other chocolate cake recipes no prob. However, in this case, the smell and the taste of the dough was so bitter. I was able to recover by coating the cookie in powdered sugar instead, which balanced it better!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Almost followed the recipe exactly. Thought I had nutmeg, did not; subbed in cardamom- no regrets. I baked for an additional five minutes because I panicked as they were too soft. I did not understand what pan rotation meant, so I did the best I could. Well whatever I did worked and these are brilliant. This must be why people enjoy making cookies all the time. Was also my first time making gingerbread anything, so extra happy about how well it went.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these and served them warm with vanilla ice cream after dinner. Very tasty, but everyone had trouble getting to sleep. The espresso was kickin’! The flavor of these cookies got better as the days went on, but the look of them was unappealing from the start—sort of like charcoal cookies. Maybe I used too much espresso with the rolling sugar. If I made them again, I think I’d skip the blend and just roll in sugar in the raw or sanding sugar. Overall, yummy, but very coffee forward.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Simply amazing.  Now our favorite cookie.  Great with coffee in the morning or nighttime dessert.  The fresh ginger makes them pop.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were a huge hit for my holiday party last night. They really appeal to a grownup taste and paired beautifully with the savory appetizers and wines. This recipe is a keeper!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the directions and weighed the dough to make 1ounce balls. This yielded 28 nice sized cookies. Not 18 as the recipe states. I like the cookie flavor--its robust- and the coating makes the cookies a bit glittery when baked. A nice touch. Has anyone tried mixing some chopped chocolate into the dough? If you have, can you let us know how it went. I'm thinking of trying that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly. My cookies did not flatten out when cooled as stated. Maybe 2 tbs is a little too much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had the same issue!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The first batch of test cookies, made exactly per the recipe, failed to impress. The espresso played a little bitter (not in a good way, we love espresso) and the cookie was definitely missing something. I think some coarse decorative sugar would add texture to the coating and maybe a little salt would help bring out the flavors. I'm also tempted to add some orange zest to the batter to add brightness. We'll see how the next batch goes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just divine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I feel like the espresso powder you use makes a real difference here, and that may be obvious to others but just noting in case not. I could taste the underlying sweetness and spice, but those flavors were overwhelmed by the espresso powder I used, which didn't have a very smooth flavor/aroma. I think these would probably be a lot better with a higher quality espresso powder, so I definitely recommend using some care when choosing this ingredient!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Would coffee extract work?  The only espresso powder I could find close to home had 
cacao in it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out great! I finely grinded some instant coffee and that seemed to work just as well as espresso powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really delicious and complex cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made them the first time-with the incorrect molasses amount-failure. After reading the community notes-tried again-and wow-these cookies are excellent. Tossed the first batter ( darn you NYT) but the extra effort was worth it. These are a keeper and a recipe to make on repeat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies did not flatten out. I used whole wheat white flour, and only a tbls. of dough per cookie. But others said theirs did not flatten out either. Delicious in any case, great espresso ginger flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic! Shaped into 1.5 gram balls of dough to make 18 cookies.  Used a measuring cup for the espresso powder. Everyone raved about these cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you're thinking you can just use your regular old coffee beans in these, I would think again. I ground up 22 grams of my beans for these (a light roast Sumatra), and while the recipe came out exactly as pictured, I found these were an acquired taste — they do taste like a gingerbread latte, but the flavor is super dark and rich. I can barely eat one at a time. Great texture, though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the addition of fresh ginger made these a bit too ginger-y for me.  Otherwise, I thought the coffee / spice combo worked well.  The cookies had a nice chewy texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cookies are definitely caffeinated but I actually would have preferred a strong coffee flavor. I’m not sure if I would increase the espresso powder though just decrease the ginger. I used squeeze tube ginger instead of fresh and it worked fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a nice change from the usual ginger snap. I weighed everything. I made the mistake of using instant coffee, which doesn’t dissolve in butter. Still, after I stirred the bejeezus out of it and fished the remaining coffee lump out, there was plenty of coffee flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! Made as written.  Chewy inside and crisp outside.  Coffee flavor really comes through- could even use less.  I made some and then froze the rest already shaped into balls for future baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good ginger cookies, and they stay soft and tasty for several days. But I don't feel the coffee added much, and I'm going to stick with my usual favorite ginger cookie recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are great. They're GREAT great.
