Coated mine in a 2:1 ratio of sugar and espresso powder and thought it was a nice balance to the sweetness of the cookie. I'm excited to make these again and again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for a party and had lots of people ask me for the recipe. Very glad they've corrected the molasses weight because 85g is definitely enough! These will be a new holiday staple.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies didn't flatten out- still puffy after cooling.  Any tips?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: mine didn't flatten out either.  Hard as a rock.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For any Celiacs out there, it’s super easy to adjust this recipe to be gluten free, especially since the flour doesn’t comprise a huge percentage of the recipe. I used King Arthur Measure for Measure gluten free flour and added the exact same weight of flour called for in the recipe (be certain to do this by weight and not volume since GF flour tends to be more dense). They came out just as described - soft, chewy, nice little crackled crust on the outside, and OH MY, are they delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are one of the best tasting gingerbread cookies. I used King Arthur’s espresso powder.  The coffee flavor wasn’t overpowering at all. It had a hint of coffee and a wonderful warm, ginger spice flavor.  I made no changes to the recipe. I let the dough rest in the fridge for 24 hrs before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I certainly enjoyed making these cookies, but I’m not so sure I enjoyed eating them. Perhaps it’s personal preference, but my partner and I found the cookies way too spiced. The flavor was simply too intense, and I’m a person who loves spiced flavors and coffee! My advice? Cut back on the cloves a bit. I found them to be the over-dominant flavor in the cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I accidentally used unsulphered blackstrap molasses - big mistake.  Will need to try this again one with the right ingredient :/

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I Used 3tsp grounds ginger and 3 Tbs fresh grated ginger in total and chopped crystallized ginger in topping

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made my cookies smaller (ended up with 32 rather than 18), but mine didn't spread much (either in the oven or once cooled). I suspect the reason was that the  dough was cold.  In the future, I would let the dough balls sit on the baking sheet for half an hour or so before I put them in the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are quite good. I don’t really get “gingerbread latte” — more like coffee-forward molasses cookie with a hit of ginger — but it’s quite tasty if you like those flavors. The texture is perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these tonight and had to try one.   They are delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were super delicious and not too time-consuming to make! A word of warning - they’re definitely caffeinated. Avoid having more than one in the evening if you’re worried about the caffeine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can expresso instant coffee be ground fine to be used interchangeably for espresso powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did and they came out fine (didn't grind them, either.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful!  Best of the season!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So disappointed.  Cookies did not spread or get craggy on top.  I now have 42 hard as a rock gingerbread latte balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these subbing GF all purpose King Arthur flour, dairy-free butter, and instant espresso for the espresso flour. These are the best gingerbread cookies I’ve ever made. The texture is spot-on, crunchy exterior and soft, chewy center. The espresso compliments the deep flavors of molasses, and the sharp spice of ginger, to perfection. I have a feeling I’ll be asked to make these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A lot of ingredients, but was able to track down the espresso powder at my local grocery store and everyone went crazy for these. They definitely give you a caffeine kick!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'd recommend freezing the dough—when I used dough out of the fridge, it was way too goopy and wouldn't roll in a ball properly, and halfway through the bake had splattered and thinned all gloopy on the baking sheet. Mine cooked fine in 11 minutes from frozen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious! Made exactly as written.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Just made these.  Not a fan.  I do like the taste of coffee but that flavor overwhelms the cookie.  

I print out successful recipes as I like a hard copy I can put in my binder and prop up.  This isn't getting printed out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was thrilled with the complex flavor of these cookies - note- made with lavazza decaf espresso - this worked very well if you are sensitive to caffeine.  Great texture too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ok to leave out the coffee powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t have espresso powder but instead rolled these in raw brown sugar before baking. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I froze the dough first because just refrigerated it wouldn't take shape in a ball. I think I baked too long though at 10 minutes, my cookies weren't chewy at all and were very hard.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I subbed in cocoa powder for the espresso since a child was going to be eating them, and reduced the sugars to 1/3 cup each. They are tasty and a really satisfying texture, even though they are rounded and not flattened like the photo. I find the ginger and cocoa flavors are unfortunately not that complementary - they seem to mute each other a bit instead of one enhancing the other - but it still makes a nice midday snacking cookie with a cup of coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Many people have said these are one of the best gingerbread cookies they've ever had. AGREE. Chewy, spicy, warm, delightful. Making again soon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made this dough and what a disaster! When I added the express powder it clumped into one huge ball and would not dissolve no matter how hard I whisked!??  Anyone else have this problem? I remedied the situation by adding illy coffee to the batter a teaspoon at a time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent cookie, really appreciate the touch of espresso flavor with the gingerbread. Great with milk. Will definitely make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My mom says, make the cookies bigger, can’t taste the ginger enough and next time and serve with ice cream (she is 96) My husband says, they could be a bit sweeter (?) I say they are excellent-very strong espresso, yes!!!! Thanks for the great  cookie recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For such a good looking cookie (and they do come out of the oven looking like the quintessential ginger cookie), they didn't deliver the flavor I expected. They are just too big and the coffee overpowers every other flavor. If I make them again, I will cut the espresso amount in half and reduce the size of the cookies to around 17 grams. Also the next day, they become just a soft cookie, not chewy. Overall, a disappointment.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t love these. I found the coffee flavor too overwhelming. 15g was 1/4 c and I did not do the rolling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A friend and I made these on a snowy afternoon with Medaglia D'Oro and they were very punchy! I think we may have overwhipped the batter so we got fewer cracks on the surface, but that could have been the demerara we used instead of the sugar/espresso/ginger mixture. (We got lazy, lol.) Beware: batter straight from the fridge is THIQUE. RIP to my cookie scoop :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had TJs instant cold brew so used 12g for recipe and 1/2 tsp for the rolling step. Super yum fresh out of the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made these and threw out half of them - not very tasty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Flawless, so delicious!! This is a new favorite, going to be a hit with friends and family!! Used DeLallo brand espresso powder, was worried 1/4c was going to be too much but the flavor is wonderful! Fresh ginger and freshly ground spices, if you can, make them smell amazing, like the most beautiful gingerbread smell ever. The coffee for me comes through subtly by taste only, not really smell.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Underwhelming and bitter. Going back to classic molasses cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even though I baked this batch from frozen they spread all over the pan and were unuseable :( what happened?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Starbucks Via and it was so clumpy.  Did anyone else have the issue?  I was going to try mixing the Via in the flour to avoid the clumps.  Bad idea?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't have molasses so I used date syrup, which worked well. Delicious and reminds me of the dirty chai espresso cookies also on NYT, but these are softer and have a better texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious!  Followed the recipe except I only had salted butter and then forgot to add a smaller amount of salt. Toss a small amount of salt with the ginger, sugar espresso powder coating and they came out delicious.  Can’t wait to make this again.  The fresh ginger add a depth that is divine.  Thank you for sharing this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these! The warm spices come through and because they aren't so sweet they really taste like a thoroughly "adult" cookie. I didn't have espresso powder so instead used 30 grams (up from 22) of powdered instant coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: There is no espresso powder on the island where I live, so I used my go-to coffee Santo Domingo ground beans. The cookies came out great. These are down right addictive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these yesterday and they were a hit!! Easy to throw together. I didn't have time to chill the dough for 2 hrs but to be honest 45 minutes of chilling was fine. Will definitely make again! Also the trick from the video to spray the measuring cup with cooking oil before measuring the molasses was a game changer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sigh. I was so looking forward to these cookies. I wonder if it was my use of gluten-free flour instead of whole wheat flour that caused the cookies to spread out into a single cookie on the baking sheet. The flavor is excellent, but the behavior while baking was not what I expected.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious, I will be making them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have been searching online for latte powder and I can find it for all types of things like macha and beet, etc., but not coffee.  Where do you get latte powder?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Bustello espresso powder. Worked fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these as written.  Perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Instead of rolling the unbaked cookies in the sugar/ginger/espresso mixture I brushed the baked cookies with ginger syrup. Fantastic!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine did not flatten upon cooling. Where might I have gone wrong? Good flavor but size and appearance was not as pictured.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these as directed, using my old measuring cups and teaspoons, etc., and they turned out fine. The favorite of the three new NYT cookie recipes I tried. 

Everybody liked them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I recommend wearing gloves for the cookie shaping portion, the ginger irritated my hands.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2nd time I cooked for 11 minutes. Much better. Tastes improves each day!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For this recipe, I weighed the ingredients rather than using measuring cups. Weighing the espresso powder at 22 g looked way more than 1/4 cup, but I rolled with it & the cookies turned out overwhelming coffee flavored & bitter. Ive since weighed out 1/4 cup of espresso powder and it’s 14 grams, not 22 g. With an ingredient so powerful, that small difference changes it drastically.

I’m eager to make them again, with a much lesser espresso amount. Though kinda bummed I wasted a batch of cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi! Has anyone tried substituting instant coffee for the espresso powder? Would that work?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same question here.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there any way to roll out the dough and use it with cookie cutters to make a gingerbread man? Or will it spread too much when baking and not hold the shape?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made walnut sized cookies & they spread to 2.5” in diameter. Hope this helped.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My Favorite Cookie of all time! I don't like nutmeg, so I added a little cardamon. Will make again and again. (Thanks folks for the heads up about the molasses measurement.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Amazing recipe! 
I’ve never had a molasses/gingerbread cookie turn out this wonderful! Yup, no need to worry about storage containers—they don’t sit on the plate for very long!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these tonight after chilling batter for 2 hours in the fridge. This recipe has been corrected regarding the molasses measurement to be 1/4 Cup. Ten minutes in the oven on center rack yielded about 32 cookies about 2.5” in diameter. I rolled them to be walnut sized. No scoop was needed. I used a Wilton 21” X 15” baking sheet. 
A lovely cookie. The fresh ginger isn’t sharp tasting after being cooked. 

Espresso powder is available from King Arthur Baking Company.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Repeat for sure. Used exact measurements; ten out of ten

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I just ate one of these hot out of 
the oven. Oh my!!! They are amazing. Dark, intense, not too sweet. I couldn’t source the espresso powder so used instant coffee granules and still got a wonderful flavour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these cookies exactly according to recipe and I was very disappointed. They taste bitter. Another person noted that the weight is inaccurate for the molasses—I can believe that. Why else are these so bitter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 30 grams of instant coffee

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are tasty.  The combo of molasses, ginger and coffee works well.  The texture is perfect--crisp on edges and chewy.  I found that I only had about 1/8 c of molasses so used another 1/8 cup of maple syrup to make up the difference.  Also added 1/4 t of cardamom.  If you're sensitive to caffeine, don't eat too many at night.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with pomegranate molasses and no espresso. These are a great base recipe for ginger molasses and the twist of pomegranate was amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: With regard to grams of molasses (following on M's note suggesting that the conversion in the recipe is off): molasses, as a liquid, is measured in this recipe in grams by volume, not by weight. 1/4 c of molasses is = to about 84 grams by volume. Volume is how much space is filled by the food (e.g., liquid); weight is more appropriate for dry ingredients. See, e.g., https://www.loveandoliveoil.com/2020/01/weight-vs-volume-measurements-in-baking-and-the-best-way-to-measure-flour.html.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe with gluten free flour? if so, what were the results?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow! Might be too sophisticated for small children, but these are spectacular! Made with no substitutions or omissions, and they're perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very tasty, but I think I undercooked a bit. They really spread out too, so make sure you give them PLENTY of room

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great cookies

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anybody else experience flat and thin cookies or is it just me?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good cookies! The cookies had a nice, fudgy texture. I shared them at multiple holiday parties. After tasting them, I found that I would prefer a more equal gingerbread to espresso powder ratio. Next time, I will up the amount of ginger and lessen the amount of espresso.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used decaf instant coffee ground finely instead of instant espresso powder. At first we didn't fully understand the impact that instant coffee would have to the finished product, but it surely brings a sublime flavor profile to an otherwise amazing ginger-molasses cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They are good. I followed the corrected recipe, the only grip I have is that I didn’t obtain the textured crackling look. Otherwise, great cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these and they are AMAZING! Will double the recipe next time.  My cookies were on the big side so I only ended up with20, and 40 would be so much better!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fabulous recipe, prepped and baked exactly as the recipe states. Love the soft, rich chewiness of the cookies - more robust flavor than your typical gingerbread. Will make these a holiday tradition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Wow, I should have had these before my coffee.  I feel jittery after a couple AND I couldn't taste the ginger AT ALL because it was SOOOOOO coffee bitter.
I wish I had read through the notes before I made this as I see a few others note that as well.

That said the texture is devine - like the best molasses cookie.  I will try again without espresso.

PS. I used King Author's espresso powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are goooood! I used Starbucks Dark Roast instant coffee. These are my new favorites.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were easy to whip up on Christmas Eve. I scooped and weighed 1 oz. balls and ended up with 25. Next time I will just roll in plain sugar before baking. I don't think the ginger and espresso in the coating added much, as the cookie dough is already well spiced. Might reduce espresso powder to 3T (rather than 4). My family and I enjoyed these cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these as directed. I ended up with 21 cookies. They did not flatten like the picture - mine turned out about twice as thick and domed in the middle.  I found the dough very difficult to work with so I might only chill for 30 minutes next time if I make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these! In my experience people either love the taste of molasses or aren't a fan. These pack a punch, and those who don't shy away from spice and coffee are sure to enjoy them. Soft, chewy, and perfect with a cold glass of milk to balance out the strong flavors.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow, made them just as written today, 12/24/2022 (after molassses adjustment I guess) and they are amazing :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don’t put ginger in sugary topping

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made these four times in the last few weeks and they're a hit each time. The only changes I've made are to leave the espresso out of the sugar/ginger topping an to leave the topping off the bottom of the cookie (burnt a little). Different and delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: too much sugar mixture for rolling.  could do half

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these for Christmas this year in addition to my signature chocolate chip and sugar cookies i make for my family every year as an interesting edition. I think overall, a very fragrant, soft in the middle and crunch on the edges, great cookie! Honestly, it taste exactly like gingerbread but in cookie form.
I think next time, I will add less ginger and less espresso powder. I was feeling a little TOO energized after eating one, and the ginger was strong enough to leave a burn in my throat

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out very bitter for me I assume because I used instant espresso powder. Sadly I ended up throwing them away.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of the best cookies I have ever made!! Really delicious. Followed the recipe, and chilled overnight. I did make bigger cookies, but they came out great, and so tasty. The coffee flavor went very well with the molasses flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are chilling in the fridge but the dough tastes yummy. Instead of spraying the cup for molasses with nonstick spray, I wiped it with whatever was left on the butter wrapper before I threw the wrapper away. Same concept.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Am I supposed to flatten the balls?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I prepared this recipe with 1:1 gluten-free flour and the cookies turned out great, light and pillowy. They taste better with age. At first, the espresso was a little overpowering but that mellowed after a day or two. Will definitely make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: *wow* these are delicious! They had a thin crunchy exterior and a soft interior…and they truly taste like a latte! They are MAGIC!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Experienced bakers: is the molasses important to the taste of this cookie? It’s high FODMAP.  Could I leave it out or sub maple syrup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these along with the Eggnog Snickerdoodles for a holiday snacks-for-dinner night.  Great spicy gingerbread flavor and the molasses and espresso makes it a real grown-up cookie.  Made a dozen and froze a dozen - rolled in coarse sanding sugar for extra sparkle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Thank you for fixing the molasses measure. These cookies are absolutely fabulous. I ran out of espresso powder so I saved the rest for the outside coating and subbed some freeze dried coffee for the espresso powder in the batter.

Wow — everyone agreed these are fantastic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Best cookies! I left out the espresso powder and did not roll in mixture. Cooked for 13 minutes and came out perfect. Great for dessert or with morning coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious. Slightly bitter in a good way. So yum. I rarely eat my own cookies. But when I tried this one to test it, I actually ate the whole thing! It’s a perfectly texture gingerbread cookie with a kick! I love these cookies. The only thing I would love to figure out is a way to incorporate the “latte” aspect. It’s got that huge coffee kick. But it needs the milk aspect. Maybe some milk powder. Or some kind of sweetened condensed milk swirls? Endless possibilities. 

Any thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Trader Joe's cold brew powder and chilled the dough overnight. The first dozen were huge. 2 Tbsp!?! I made dime-sized balls which fit on the saucer next to the beverage, Euro-style. Poifect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Overall, I love the concept, BUT the fresh ginger combined with the coffee and molasses creates a funky soy sauce flavor that isn't so pleasant.  I think straight up ground ginger next time and double the amount to compensate.  Also, having heard from a couple friends that these just turned out too cakey no matter what they did, I adjusted the flour to half all-purpose, half bread flour.  Still not as chewy as I'd like, so adjusting sugar ratio to more brown than white is probably needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are wonderful. We had a little more fresh ginger than the recipe called for and WILL NOT do that again. And since I like a little sweetness with my gingerbread cookies, I'm going to have some buttercream frosting on hand to put on my next one. Also, the purple-handle scoop when a little over-filled makes 1ounce.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ok so after reading the comments I see why mine were a bit too intense on the coffee (still ok though!). I've never heard of espresso powder so I just assumed it's ground powdered (as opposed to whole bean) coffee. So yeah, I didn't love them but I'm also not in the US and didn't know that ingredient.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't even like gingerbread and I loved these! Baked AS IS in the recipe. Don't know why others had issues....I only skipped the ginger in the topping and they were still gingery enough. Super spicy addition to our Christmas cookie repertoire.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I bought Ferrara instant espresso coffee, was the only thing I could find.  The 22 gram weight comes out to just under 1/2 cup.  Do I have an incorrect product? If this is ok to use, how much should I use, 1/4 cup or 1/2 cup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were good but I cannot seem to get the chewy texture or a flatter craggy cookie. They remain puffed up “balls” for the most part. I have the same result with the Dirty Chai Earthquake cookies and the Miso Peanut Butter cookies. Really good flavor but never flat or craggy enough. I am using the correct amount of baking soda and or baking powder. Even bought both powder and soda fresh in bulk from our co-op to eliminate this potential issue. Any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ultimately I didn't love the coffee flavour with the ginger, but the were pretty good with vanilla ice cream on the side.  Do not overbake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made these cookies.  They were soft, but entirely too much espresso for any of the 20 people at our Christmas.  So if you make these, I would use 1/2 as much espresso powder in the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these with instant espresso, and they turned out perfect. If you don't like coffee, you might not like these though. My younger kids were not fans.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used freeze dried coffee crystals and they did not disperse well. Next time I'd blitz them in my spice grinder first and add them to the flour. Otherwise the taste was excellent and out of the ordinary.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made six different Christmas cookies this year and these were the favorite by far.  Most of the reactions were "so so good."

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These were delicious. I thought the first batch, baked for 10 minutes, was going to be too soft so I baked the rest for 11:30. After they all finished cooling, the first batch was perfect and the others were a little hard. It didn't stop anyone from eating them, though.
Instead of espresso powder (I didn't have any), I used dark Turkish coffee with cardamom, which worked really well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I can't figure out where I went wrong here - they look nothing like the photos and came out as puffy and small. The flavor is very subtle - anybody else?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the dough on 12/24 and cooked the cookies on 12/25.  Really good, and different.  But 3 days later, they are really drying out.  They don’t last long, but great at first.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What do I adjust if I only want to use 1 stick butter? Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I liked these but will increase the ginger next time and look for espresso powder. I used instant coffee and they didn’t seem very coffee flavored. Great texture and easy since no mixer required!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really tasty.  The espresso powder enhances the ginger in a wonderful way.  Will definitely make them again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dear M: Instead of throwing out the dough, I'm assuming it was too wet, just double the rest of the ingredients, divide in half, bake half, freeze half.  Bake the rest latter.  I've had to do that when I've mistakenly added too much of, well, anything.  No one complains about more cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I really wanted to like these but didn’t and next time I’ll 
-let them rest longer than 2 hr (too flat)
-cut the espresso powder (in half?) (too aggressively coffee)
-start checking at 8 min (too crispy)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Trying to mix in instant espresso to the butter and it’s mostly all congealed. Is that normal?  What did I do wrong??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lisa I had this happen as well. Heated it up a bit more and whisked vigorously then strained out the remaining congealed chunks. I think my instant espresso was old and stuck together. Ran some of it through the coffee grinder, added it back into the butter mixture and whisked again. It worked!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great cookies - you DEFINITELY get the caffeine hit! I also like the zing and spice of the fresh ginger in combination with the powdered ginger. I opted for 1-tablespoon portions and the cookies are still generously sized for holiday gifting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are amazing! I made them exactly as written and they turn out smelling heavenly and taste incredible. Everyone raved about them. Julie peach said it best, her review is perfect.
 Make these!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were phenomenal, albeit a bit divisive in our household. I love them and can't get enough, but my husband was not a fan. If you love spiced cookies and coffee, please make them. I made one batch per the directions for cookies that were both crisp and chewy, then for another batch I baked them at 325 for 20 minutes to get cookies that were completely crisp. Love them both!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A wonderful recipe, and I’ll take the technique of coating with granulated sugar   ground spices with me. A warning: it came out more bitter than expected for me. Next time I’ll probably decrease the espresso powder. Also, this is the first time I’ve used molasses - it has kind of a meaty flavor which is interesting. I might cut down on that a bit too. Overall, such a unique cookie, and pretty easy to make, too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these exactly as written using measuring cups and spoons. 
Refrigerated 24 hours. 1/8 cup measuring cup for portions = 28 cookies. Didn't "crack" or flatten as much as pictured. Another reviewer said let sit on baking pan for 1/2 hour prior to baking as dough may have been too cold (will try that next time). Regardless, these are great tasting cookies and flavors improved/melded after resting overnight (covered). Soft, not crispy and loved the complexity of all flavors/ingredients!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Reduce granulated sugar by 20g, brown sugar by 10g for right level of sweetness. If you don’t like the taste of ginger, cut by half. I cut espresso by 6g. The cookies had a fantastic texture. The taste is too complex for a cookie but we enjoyed it. Definitely not for kids. Go easy on cinnamon, clove, nutmeg etc.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fantastic. Followed recipe exactly except skipped the rolling in sugar and spices. They look just like the picture and have a perfect  chewy texture with just enough of a crisp top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies never flattened out (maybe the 10 minutes cooking time wasn't quite as much as I should have done in my oven?) but they were still delicious!  I love when there's a little bit of forgiveness on cooking time in a baking recipe and I'd definitely make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Thank you, thank you, thank you Vaughn.
These are wonderful cookies; very adult. Everyone has loved them and kudos to Vaughn for giving us a recipe that is perfectly executed as is!
I used Trader Joe’s Instant Cold Brew for the coffee, figuring that it would be a good substitute for the espresso powder because it is designed to dissolve easily.
I have also found that Trader Joe’s salted butter is not only highly related, but gives an excellent consistency to baked goods.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe makes closer to 40 cookies—I suggest 16/tray as they run together. Starbucks dark roast instant coffee was a fine substitute firm for espresso powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are absolutely delightful. Recommend using decaf espresso powder so you can eat multiple of these all day, every day - you'll want to.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these and unfortunately they turned out awful. I think because I had to use instant coffee vs. espresso powder. No idea what went wrong. I’m a good baker, and even weigh my ingredients, but something was really off. Thanks to the other readers, who added all their comments as I felt they were helpful. Sadly, taking this out of my holiday, Christmas baking lineup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved loved loved these, will be a holiday regular for sure. They are more robust than your typical gingerbread, driven by the espresso powder which adds great richness to the flavor. And I love the texture, nice and chewy. They did spread a bit more than I was expecting so I'll double-check my ratios next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these twice this Christmas season and they are delicious. I definitely recommend getting the “medaglia D’oro” instant espresso. First batch I used something else and they weren’t nearly as good. They freeze well and bake great from frozen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This cookie is simply excellent! The combination of flavor and texture is comforting and sophisticated and wonderful. Fantastic for  Christmas holidays or any time during fall / winter. 

Note: the espresso powder is potent in terms of caffeine. If the situation calls for two batches, I might think about making one caffeinated and one decaf.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and the cookies were delicious. However, in the future I’ll omit the espresso powder — it created a slightly burned flavor and I don’t believe it added much value.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Reserve leftover coating mix OR make a bit extra - the blend of sugar, espresso powder and ginger is wonderful sprinkled on top of cappuccino!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were fabulous! Everyone loved them, and I made them gluten free by using King Arthur measure for measure flour!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you, like me, forget to add the molasses when you are making the dough, don't despair--they are still sweet enough, chewy inside, and (if you are my husband, at least), the most delicious cookies you have ever tasted in your life. I'll add that I find them mysteriously delicious myself!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When we first tried these, they were pretty bitter but we let them sit overnight and part of the next day and they definitely mellowed out in flavor. Another commenter suggested using buttercream frosting as a method of adding milk to the coffee essentially. We let our guests choose whether to add the frosting which worked pretty well since some people are bigger coffee drinkers than others and preferred the less sweet taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were the best cookies. Took a batch to work and everyone loved them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are basically my mother's gingersnaps plus espresso powder.  A family favorite for decades.  I make them regularly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are incredible! I prepared them for the holiday. My family raved about the flavor and texture. My wife who is usually "meeh" about cookies came back for more.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I weighed mine at 1 oz.  Within the same batch, very different baking results, but 2” is too close together.  About half were “perfect”, the other half collapsed.  I wonder what some baking powder would do?  All were delicious, however!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies today and they are awesome! All the ingredients just really meld well; and we love both the moistness and the chew. And someone in the comments suggested using Demerara sugar to roll the cookies in and I did that. Just spot on along with the other ingredients in the coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious! I used all my espresso powder in the cookies so I rolled the cookie balls in sanding sugar mixed with cinnamon and ginger. They were chewy and taste just like a gingerbread latte (even without that added coffee flavor)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I also found the espresso powder weight and volume differed. My Delallo espresso powder, about 1/4 cup, weighed more like 10 grams. Which measurements are definitive!?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavor of these cookies is wonderful.  Mine did not become "craggy" - they were flat and crisp like ginger snaps.  Perhaps I over baked them by a minute or so, but I was watching for cragginess as they baked and never saw it.  Any ideas why?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I make these again (not bad but I have a killer gingerbread cookie recipe) I would roll them BEFORE chilling dough - it was so hard after overnight in fridge that I had to use a spoon to scrape the dough couldn't use cookie scoop! Again not bad but does have a ginger tingle on tongue after taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these and they were somewhat bitter? Looks like the molasses was off by some comments I read. Want to try this recipe again this year...love coffee and ginger. Everyone enjoyed but I'm my own worst critic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i found these to be just ok. i love coffee and i love gingerbread, but the instant espresso was overwhelmingly bitter and unpleasant when added to the coating. could this be remedied but using nicer or specialty instant espresso? likely, but it bothers me when recipes don't work with basic, accessible ingredients. and fair warning - if you have a gas stove (like me) that heats from below, you may end up burning the bottoms of your cookies when using the bottom rack because of the sugar coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We love ginger so l doubled the fresh ginger and the powdered ginger. Didn't have molasses so used all brown sugar and substituted honey for the molasses.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these and they’re delicious. But I was confused about the baking time. I interpreted it as a 10 minute baking time, but mine didn’t flatten down. They taste fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 2 tablespoons per cookie and no matter how long I baked them, they never seemed done. If I do them again, I’ll make them much smaller.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. I could not find espresso powder so I used a dark roast instant coffee. My cookies did not flatten out but they are still delicious. I will make these again with the espresso powder! I’m on a real ginger kick at the moment and these are one of the best cookies I’ve ever baked at home.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought 2 c flour was 240 grams?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In step 1 I add espresso powder and ground ginger to ingredients and in step 2 I add the granulated sugar. By step 3 how much granulated sugar, espresso powder and ground ginger am I to use to make the coating? It's all already in the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe lists ingredients for the topping separately from the cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made two batches of the dough. Twice they never flattened out like the video and I googled why. Brown sugar and baking soda creates a CO2 effect and makes them rise like cake instead of spreading. I followed the directions to a T. They turned out thick like cake balls. The molasses and ginger flavors get overpowered by the espresso. I’d say skip the espresso altogether and skip the sugar/espresso coating altogether as well. It will make the cookies burn on the bottom.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a single batch of these and thought they were a bit too strong on the ginger. Although it sounded good,  I was not a fan of the espresso ginger mix, and it used quite a bit of espresso powder (I thought the amount of molasses as mentioned above was off too). My cookie baking buddy thought they were not sweet enough, I found them to be a tad bitter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really liked these cookies, next time I will use less espresso in the sugar when rolling the cookies. I thought the espresso in the sugar made them a little bitter. So I will cut down to 1 tsp of espresso in the sugar for rolling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't really get any "latte" element here, just a good, chewy molasses cookie basically.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made this without fresh ginger? Wondering if I can just add more powdered ginger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookies did not flatten and were super puffed up. They also took nearly double the amount of time in the oven to fully bake thru. At 10 minutes, they were basically raw in the middle. They also too ended up too caffeinated & buzzy for my taste. I would not recommend or make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made this as written with the addition of blitzed candied ginger. An addictive (like coffee!) grown-upcookie. 

I'm entering these in an office holiday cookie contest with the following adjustments: will use a 1Tb measure for a smaller cookie and heavily sprinkle sparkle sugar at minute 8 while baking. When cool, 'll place a pea-sized dollop of dulce de leche on each cookie and top with a chocolate covered coffee bean.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am by no means a good baker and have had laughable baking disasters. I made these cookies last night with spelt flour and completely organic ingredients. They came out looking exactly like the photo and taste absolutely delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent cookies combining coffee and ginger flavor. Not too sweet. Crunchy when cool. Nice flavor. Dough comes together well. Dough is stiff after overnight fridge, but metal scoop and hand roll yields nicely shaped cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were amazing!! The flavor was so rich and spicy- perfect holiday cookie. I agree with others about the topping though. I rolled them in Turbinado sugar and loved the crunch and the caramelized sugar taste better than the sugar, coffee, ginger topping.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these exactly as instructed and they were delicious and won me a cookie contest!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They were good. I like the sugar sparkle. I ground our dark roast coffee to expresso powder, but it was a bit too intense for the cookie. Vaughn's video is cute. He is like a young Jake Gyllenhaal that comes with cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Molasses weight has been corrected and felt like the perfect amount to me! Loved these cookies and so did everyone I shared them with.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Deeeelicious.  Followed the recipe exactly, measuring by weight (except for the espresso powder - 1/4 cup was 16 grams, not 22, so thanks to all the comments for that guidance).  Easy to make, wonderful to eat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I baked these in two batches, one after two hours of chilling, and the second after letting it chill until the following morning. They are both good, but the batch that chilled overnight is noticeably better. The flavors are more balanced and developed. In the first batch, the ginger is very strong and overshadows the coffee flavor. In the second, both flavors shine & complement each other. I also tested coating them in sugar   espresso vs. just sugar, I prefer just sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these as a very small but heartfelt thank you for the law enforcement officers who rescued me when I was the victim of a home invasion a year ago.  Because their uniforms are black, I rolled the dough in black sugar (from Joann's) etc. instead of white, so it wouldn't show.  To make the cookies uniform (different meaning!) I weighed out 30 gm per piece of dough.  The amount of caffeine per bite was as big a hit as the black sugar.  These are delicious and unusual -- Thank You Vaughn!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the cookies as described and brought them to the office where they were a huge hit. I froze the extra dough into balls but think a little less baking time would yield a softer texture for the leftover cookies when they get baked up. Love the recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe to the letter, including spacing the balls 2” apart on the baking sheet. They spread into a solid mass! What did I do wrong? They’re delicious, but look nothing like the photo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The coffee and dinner flavor is delicious, but very strong. More flavored espresso than latte. Taking an idea from Eric Kim’s matcha latte cookies and adding a smooth, not too sweet vanilla icing on top makes these perfect for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies smell incredible and flavor is great. No problems with the instructions for making the dough and chilling (we chilled overnight), but for us the baking time was a bit off in the recipe. We pulled the first batch out as instructed at 10 minutes and let set for 3 on baking sheet before cooling on wire rack. WAY under. We eventually came up with the right timing - 12 mins in oven, 5 mins on baking sheet, then the cooling rack. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly as written and these cookies came out perfect.  Grating the fresh ginger was time consuming but well worth it! Delicious, chewy, beautiful. Definitely a repeat recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi all, wondering if it will work if I make one double batch with this recipe, and just double everything? Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these according to the recipe with the exception of using plant-based butter and egg replacer to accommodate my vegan eater. These cookies turned out perfectly.  The ginger-zing is faint.  I personally like a stronger ginger flavor, but find these to be very enjoyable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These really spread out. I'd place them 2-3 inches apart on the baking sheet. They were OK - just OK and I am a ginger lover....

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these using vegan “butter,” aquafaba, and GF flour. Amazing flavor and perfect texture. No one had a clue they were vegan and gluten free. I did make them smaller, about half the size stated in the recipe, but still baked for 10 minutes. I got 32 cookies. Will be making another batch soon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are absolutely delicious but mine flattened too much. I'm freezing the rolled balls and maybe my dough in the first bake as too warm.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Mariangela the first time I made these they were flat. I replaced my baking soda and now the cookies are perfect every time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. I followed the recipe exactly and they are perfection

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious cookies I used instant coffee Starbucks Italian Roast as I couldn't find the espresso powder on time.  I made the dough ahead of time and kept it in the fridge for a few days in a sealed container. They turned out excellent! It's worthwhile to watch Vaughn's video for his     
 pro tips and his good cheer!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Perfect cookies I used GF flour and egg replacer. I followed the recipe exactly and made the dough ahead of time and kept it in the fridge for a couple of days in a sealed container. They are the best! Love your video Vaughn.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I baked these very early in the morning and loved the scents in the kitchen. I wonder if adding a bit of crystallized ginger would be a good idea, while skipping the ginger in the sugar/coffee coating. I used Demerara sugar for the coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love these cookies. Second year in a row making them exactly as written. They come out perfect and make a lot! I roll into 1oz balls for about 26 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious but heavy (from the butter presumably). my first batch burned on the bottom, had to flip each batch mid-baking, so all the sprinkley sugar melted. So they are delicious but kind of  ugly, not like the photo. I doubled the recipe and seemed to work fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used small cookie scoop and cut cookie dough balls in half to make a smaller cookies. Got about 60 cookies. Everyone enjoyed these very much at the cookie exchange!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Fabulous flavor! But the large cookies rolled around on the pan, so they are less pretty. They seem a little underdone- possibly related? I had one 1-Tbs sized cookie on the pan and I thought it came out perfect! 
Any ideas for making them less roly-poly?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these as directed using Medaglia D’Oro espresso instant coffee which I’ve always used in my baking and they turned out great. Deeply flavorful and crispy on the outside and chewy on the inside- just what I want in a cookie! Mine do not look quite as pretty as the ones in the picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I ended up substituting instant coffee instead of espresso (it's all I had).  Still came out delicious! Will try with espresso powder next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I rolled the chilled dough into a rectangle and cut into 24 cubes, then rolled those. These spread a LOT. I should have rolled the dough into balls and chilled again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made two double batches of these cookies because everyone loves them.  I did substitute decaf espresso, and it was great.  I found that freezing the balls of dough before baking, then baking for 13 minutes was much better than baking the refrigerated dough.  They kept a very uniform consistency, which didn't happen when the refrigerated dough started to warm.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The consistency of these cookies are great, but they have a very strong espresso flavor. I love strong coffee but even for me it was a bit much. I’d recommend reducing the espresso powder a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out great. I could not find the espresso powder so I just left it out which results in a subtle ginger cookie. I rolled the balls in colorful confetti sugar and baked for 11 minutes. It made 35 cookies. I agree that it might be easier to portion out the dough and refrigerate/freeze, then bake. The dough gets really soft quickly so I put them back in the fridge to chill before baking. It was an extra step but it kept the cookies looking uniform.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maria ‘s recipe for Xmas 2023

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these gluten free with 1 1/2 cups of 1 to 1 gluten free flour and 1/2 cup of corn starch.  They turned out so well!  They’re like just like a traditionally made cookie: soft and chewy even after a few days on the counter stored in an air-tight container. The only other thing I did differently before baking was after rolling them into balls was to flat the top and bottom to help them spread properly. This is a permanent add to my holiday baking. Thanks for this recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these with coffee beans ground finely in my burr grinder. Measured by grams wherever possible. Baked at 375 for 12 mins. Freshly zested orange on half right out of the oven, and next time i might rub some zest into the coating or add zest during the melted butter stage. They turned out great. Indeed, very adult cookie, not too sweet, crunchy cracked outer and chewy insides. When i make this dough again I'll try add/substitute malted milk powder for a portion of the coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
When using King Arthur's espresso powder, these came out as bitter as your average Twitter user. Almost unbearable, but have a slight shimmer of hope on the horizon by decreasing their exposure of acidic content (in this case, espresso powder).

I'd probably cut the espresso powder in half in both the batter and sugar coating and these would be great. I scooped them out before fridging, then rolled them into balls to warm them slightly for the sugar coating to stick.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Where did I go wrong?  Made these and they spread into a gigantic puddle of cookie.   Froze half the balls of dough and tried again, same result?  Very disappointing recipe for me.   Too bitter.  I used. medalia d’oro instant espresso powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Did the cookies sit out too long before going in the oven? I take mine from fridge directly to oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Flavor was delicious but mine spread too thin and I’m not sure why after watching the video and reading notes. My sister had nice puffy cookies. I will definitely try again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For the 1 tbsp of fresh ginger substitute 1/4 tsp of dried. So use

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for a cookie party to try something different but not complicated or over the top and these are amazing. I ended up powdering espresso beans (I live in a remote country and couldn't find espresso powder) and they came out just fine. Other than that I followed the recipe. Next time I'll find the right ingredient. They were easy and everyone loved them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Unbelievably good.  New favorite!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is mediocre. I was very disappointed in the flavor as they just lacked the sweetness. Texture was very good but cold not make up for the uninspired flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have a recipe very similar to this one. I add fresh ginger juice, minced candied ginger, and dried cherries. People are crazy about them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I left the espresso powder out of the rolling sugar as the batter was on the bitter side already. Happy with the decision! I'll be making these again. Do keep in mind that the recipe was SERIOUS when they said to spread them out at least 2 inches apart. They spread a lot and my first batch was more cookie cake than individual cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Here's a thing you can do, after you have mixed up all the batter:  get a dinner plate, lay out a big piece of plastic wrap over it, dump the batter into the plastic wrap-lined plate, close up the plastic wrap around it and put into the fridge.   Two hours later, it will be ready to be formed into balls.  And definitely keep your hands wet with water while rolling the dough into balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: And when do you add the butter/spice mixture?  Recipe does not say.  I alternated it with the flour.  The batter is in the refrigerator now, sure hope it comes out ok.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Zoey you add all the other ingredients TO the butter mixture

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To make them look like the photo, I add extra topping when they come out of the oven. These are delicious cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loved these cookies and found the preparation to be quite straightforward. I just didn't find mine flattened as described. I followed the recipe exactly. Thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is there any way of making these cookies without the espresso powder. I'm allergic to coffee (not caffeine), but love chewy gingerbread cookies? Thank you for any advice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were good, although mine came out tasting just like chewy ginger cookies - I really couldn't taste the coffee flavor from the espresso at all!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you cut down on the ginger?  I’m not a fan of it. Can I use coconut sugar in place of brown sugar?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: yes, you could cut down on the ginger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These sound so good...not sure what store sells expresso powder and I love ginger but haven't cooked with fresh ginger. Hand injury right now, so may not be able to grate it. Glad they corrected the molasses amt... So it truly is 1/4 cup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 25 cookies at 250 mg/ tbsp = 5 mg / serving

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thinking of a lemon glaze on these after they cool, instead of rolling them the sugar-coffee mixture before baking. I love lemon with gingerbread AND lemon with espresso. Has anyone tried something like that?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe with whole wheat flour?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These worked great with King Arthur 1:1 GF flour. I used Cafe Bustelo and HOLY moley the coffee flavor is quite strong. Will probably use 1/2 next time (don’t want to go buy special “espresso powder” so just used instant espresso).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made this with powdered Chai Latte mix or chai spice instead? I love gingerbread but have a bad reaction to anything with espresso powder or coffee in it. How about proportions in such an exchange? Suggestions would be helpful. I'm a great cook, but an infrequent baker.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How Re they without the  expresso ???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written using the weighted measurements (not volume). The result was, as some other commenters have pointed out, a relatively thin, but chewy *molasses* cookie. If you love molasses, then this isn’t a problem. However, given the name of this recipe, I was expecting to taste more ginger and espresso. I could barely taste the ginger and could not taste the espresso at all. Further, the sugar-ginger-espresso coating completely disappeared after baking, so they did not look like the cookies in the picture. Next time, I will chill the cookies again after forming the dough into balls (so they spread less and retain the coating) and reduce the molasses and/or sub with light brown sugar. There is another ginger molasses cookie recipe I use that is more balanced with the flavors and spreads less compared to these—I think I could also just add espresso powder to that recipe next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Terrific as per recipe!  Next time I will add more ginger because that’s my jam. Don’t be afraid to make the balls smaller. 1.5 tbs got me 20 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious!  I'm making my 2nd batch due to popular demand.  Seems like the measurement equivalencies are a bit off, however.  For instance 22 grams of espresso powder is  much more than 1/4 cup.  I used the cup measurements and they worked fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tripled the recipe. it needs 10 to 12 mins at 375 based on the size of the scoop, with a larger scoop needing more time. I got 56 cookies to take to my class. Add 20% less espresso, less flour, and less white sugar than recommended. Also, add ground black pepper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Might halve the sugars and see how it tastes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am sad to say these didn't turn out great. I stuck exactly to the recipe and chilled them overnight (maybe I should have taken them out of the fridge before baking, but that wasn't written). They didn't flatten during baking and stayed quite thick and started burning on the bottom. The taste is very strong and not bad, but I don't love it. I was a bit disappointed because I was really looking forward to making these, I love Vaughn's recipes and I used so many good ingredients and spices.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a bit of chopped up crystallized ginger and a tad less espresso. I originally made the recipe as published but I felt the espresso taste was overwhelmingly overpowering and you couldn’t even taste the ginger. I was happy with final modifications that I made. Very delicious cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are one of the best cookies I've made. I used regular unsulphered molasses, not black strap. Also used the espresso powder from King Arthur. My only change was rolling the dough balls in Demerara sugar and ground ginger. Came out chewy, spicy from the ginger, and perfect with a warm drink.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really good--it yielded 36 for me when I rolled smaller balls. It's like eating a gingerbread latte. Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent cookies! I’d recommend paying special attention to the butter/spice/espresso powder mixture. Actively whisk powder and spices slowly into the butter. I didn’t do that my first try and the butter mixture got gloopy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe to a T, but the batter isn’t the “consistency of thin pancake batter.” It’s much thicker. I double checked to be sure I got the ingredients as directed. Fingers crossed that they still turn out when I bake them tomorrow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The batter should be pancake consistency BEFORE you add the flour. Hope your cookies were great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone subbed in a shot of brewed espresso instead of using espresso powder? And if so, with what results?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ooooof the spread hit immediately. I do weigh my ingredients and watched along with the video to compare textures. I’ve had issues with spread before with other cookie recipes and can’t figure out what I’ve done wrong. I chilled this dough for three hours. Any advice?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these. They are delicious, but mine did not flatte as they cooled. Is there something I missed. I thought I was closely following the recipe. I would have preferred a flatter, chewier cookie. Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add extra brown sugar to compensate for molasses. Do not use less sugar. Not sweet enough   not chewy. Turns out crispy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can we sub some of the espresso powder in the actual cookies with cocoa powder maybe? or just eliminate some of it?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were pretty tasty. They spread waaay more than I anticipated though. Will make again. Not too sweet as others have noted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i replaced the espresso powder in the coating with dutch process cocoa . . .    was great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these weighing out my balls to mostly 1 oz portions and got 28 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The espresso powder/instant espresso amount in this recipe is crazy work. Definitely will reduce to 1/8c next time. Will also let the melted butter cool a touch before adding the spices (the butter-spice mix smelled burnt when I added mine right off the burner after reaching that "bubbly and foamy" stage quoted in the recipe). I opted to just roll in Demerara sugar instead of a seasoned sugar since the cookies are already a face-punch of flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I halved the espresso and still got a caffeine buzz, but YUM!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely not for everyone, but I love these.  Intense, with a nice balance of the coffee and the spice flavors.  I did find them softer and chewier than expected from the video - and so baked them a bit longer than the recipe called for - but maybe it was from using high-fat butter or maybe they needed a bit more flour?  Regardless, I'll be making them again.  Thank you, Vaughn.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are phenomenal, no notes. Thank you so much!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so good they have joined my core trio of annual Christmas cookies and I recommend baking them first - to power you through the others.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Have made these three years in a row for office cookie exchange, always a hit. I dream about them all year. They really are better than the Starbucks drink.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The best apple cake I’ve ever made! It’s vegan, but no one would ever realize; it’s just delicious. You don’t need to peel five apples; I found that it takes more about 2 1/2. I also wouldn’t add more than the required quantities of chopped apple or all fruit apricot spread. It’s already moist enough as it is. Another pretty presentation for it is to warm up some regular apricot jam until it’s runny, and then brush it over the outside of the cake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have searched through all the comments for a discussion of exactly what is meant by coffee “powder,” and I’m still confused. Is it finally ground whole beans, or is it instant coffee (made from coffee that is dried into crystals)? Both of those could be described as a powder, but one contains the actual coffee beans, while the other just extracts the flavor. I’m guessing that it might be instant coffee powder, since otherwise someone would’ve said that you could just take whole beans and grind them to the finest setting your grinder will allow. I’m also wondering if that explains why although most people said the cookies were amazing, some had a very negative reaction to them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: mine came out puffy and not flat like the picture. how do I get them to look that way?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am not a fan of most ginger cookies (or ginger in general), but these are the exception. The fresh ginger and letting the dough chill to develop the flavors mellows out the sharpness that I normally associate with ginger. The addition of coffee is like a cherry on top! I did half the size of the cookies (1 tbsp dough balls instead of 2 tbsp) and the size of the cookie is perfect. Plus, more cookies to share!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were lovely, but mine needed 12 minutes in the oven. I didn't catch the distinction about espresso powder needing to be very fine, but mine turned out just fine - no gritty coffee texture was in the finished cookie. Lovely flavor balance of both gingerbread and coffee - perfect Xmas morning cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: loved this! would potentially decrease the amount of coffee in this and increase the ginger flavor

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe makes a delicious chewy cookie. I used a decaffeinated espresso powder given other comments about the caffeine content and it worked well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just in case anyone wants to know: these work with canola oil instead of butter. I just took 75% of the weight of butter (because butter also has water) to get the amount of oil needed. (168 X .75)=126 grams

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Oh my! The best cookie I have ever baked. I let the dough set overnight. Was concerned about low bake time, but did the 10 minutes & they are perfect. Used instant coffee instead of espresso powder. 
These will be my go to cookies. Good bye chocolate chip.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don’t wait until Christmas. THE PERFECT COOKIE. Perfect chew with slight crisp on the edge. Followed recipe as written.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: People who have expressed awe and reverence about this cookie include: friends, acquaintances, family, friends of family, family of friends, boyfriend's mom. Multiple have called it "the best cookie [they] have ever had." This recipe is straightforward and fun and has consistent and inexpressibly good results. It is my favorite cookie and one of my favorite recipes. I always use decaf instant coffee. Great variation: omit ginger, cinnamon, nutmeg, and clove, and add chopped toffee after flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody:
Measurements for one drink from the Grey Goose site:
1.25 oz vodka
3 oz lemonade
½ oz Chambord

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Why would you use “store bought lemonade” when fresh lemon juice, sugar and water would suffice and be infinitely more delicious?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody:
@Jen S Because it might be all you have in hand, is easier, faster, less work and probably still tastes good. 

If that’s what you got and if that’s what works for you - go ahead and use ready-made lemonade! I am sure that you will still enjoy it!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is the exact recipe. It’s fantastic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody:
This was a big hit! I used low-sugar lemonade. We didn't thaw the melon balls in time, so we didn't skewer them and they served as tasty ice cubes. They floated, which looked cool, and indeed like tennis balls. My liquor store only had "framboise" liqueurs, which is the French word for rasperry. 

With the low-sugar lemonade, it was perfectly sweet, even too sweet for a couple of people. The party was not tennis themed, but one tennis afficianado was familiar the drink and told its story.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Unbelievably good drink. There’s nothing quite like double fisting the deuce from sun up to sun down

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! But does anyone else have a problem with it looking kind of muddy? I used fresh lemon juice with water and sugar rather than store-bought lemonade, Chambord, and vodka. Mine didn’t look at all like the pink drink in the photo. Methinks a little Photoshop was leveraged.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Don't mix the Chambord with the other ingredients. Pour the lemon/vodka mixture into the ice-filled glasses, then add the Chambord to each glass. That will provide a bit of a layering effect and a less muddy look.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Would love to see a non alcoholic version of this drink as well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: That would just be raspberry lemonade: Just muddle some raspberries (and strain if you don't want the seeds). Add to lemonade and garnish with the melon balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: You could mix lemonade and raspberry syrup (boil 1 cup raspberries, 1 cup water, half cup sugar and then strain). Then top it with seltzer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Very good but quite strong.  Added more lemonade for dilution.   Really a lovely cocktail.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Made this to watch the US open finals with some friends and it was an ace of a drink. Used Baron De Casterac as my raspberry liqueur since the local liquor store was sold out of chambord (perhaps due to other tennis and drinking enthusiasts). Would definitely make again for a non tennis related party. Looks beautiful and is easy to make and drink.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: A big hit at a recent party. The honeydew melon “tennis” balls were a perfect garnish. Very refreshing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: One of my all time favorite cocktails for summertime. So delicious and refreshing. Just be careful though; they do go down quite smoothly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made a version of this for years. The recipe I use is equal parts, Chambord and vodka, and then add sour mix to taste. I prefer it because it less sweet than this version.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Lovely! There does take a bit for the honeydew to thaw enough to enjoy eating. No modifications otherwise tho!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: I found this a bit bland, and very strong.  I wouldn't want it sweeter, though.  Meh! ( but the melon tennis balls were super cute?

commentBody: Any ideas for making a virgin version? I tried to do it with lemons and muddled raspberries, didn’t want it to be too sweet. But it came out a bit sour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Lovely! It's like a spiked raspberry lemonade. I followed the recipe for the first batch (with homemade lemonade) and it turned out pink, like a cosmo. For the next round, I will do as others have suggested and add the Chambord later.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
