commentBody: Made this without the added lemon juice because we had fresh squeezed lemonade from our local grocer. Delicious on a hot day!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: At the US Open, they use three scoops of honeydew melon so they look like the three tennis balls that come in a typical tennis ball can! Using two misses the point!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Also: don’t skimp on the melon balls!  Use 3 to resemble how they come in a can!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: I'd like to try substituting tequila for the vodka.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: All I can say is "Wow!". This is such a refreshing cocktail on a warm afternoon. Love the melon serving as tennis balls. Yes that is a pun. Make this and enjoy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
recipeTitle: Honey Deuce Melon Ball Punch Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as above but with watermelon instead of honeydew. Super delicious and refreshing. A great summer punch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I've just made these today, and made a few small changes based on the existing comments. The resulting cookies taste great, and (mostly) look like the picture in the recipe. My suggestions:

1) After step 5, take the whole mixing bowl and put it in the refrigerator (not freezer) for about 10 minutes. 
2) Make 24 rather than 12 cookies (keep cook time the same).
3) Don't break up your peppermint candy too much - you don't want any peppermint powder. 
4) Really whack those trays on the counter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@mechteach 
Your post was the first one I saw. Answered my question about making smaller cookies. Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the recipe as instructed but also added about a teaspoon of espresso powder to enrich the chocolate flavor. It made 16 cookies for me and they SPREAD when they bake, so space accordingly. Otherwise, this is a great cookie that came together easily and quickly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They definitely spread more than I thought based on the photos. Wonder if there is something to adjust in the recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I almost always make several types of dough over one or 2 days,  and refrigerate them all. Then all the cooking takes place on another day.  Try it with cold dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Excellent point about adding espresso powder to enrich the chocolate flavor.  Often times in a chocolate cake recipe when water is called for, I add black coffee instead of the water to enhance the chocolate flavor.

I have also used regular instant coffee with good results as well.

AND the coffee flavor is NEVER detected (not a fan of mocha on any level).  Thanks much for adding that very valuable suggestion.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe was easy to follow and they turned out delicious.  My only complaint is that the 1/4 c scoop made gigantic cookies.  Obviously the photo accompanying this recipe used a different sized scoop.  NYT food stylist photos are notoriously deceptive.  Super annoying.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Allie , I used a 1oz scoop = 2 Tablespoons, I got 15 cookies 3” across. Imagine the monsters I would have gotten if I’d used a 4 Tablespoon/ 1/4 cup scoop as written! 😲

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Alanna I do same

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Please start adding “divided” in your ingredient lists!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
They didn't spread for me too much, but I made them smaller like others suggested, maybe too small. I like the espresso idea as others have suggested and I switched vanilla for peppermint because I don't like peppermint. They need the flavor boost of espresso.  

Added hazelnuts on top instead of peppermint because I prefer hazelnuts and had them on hand and also because my roommate eats everything I bake without asking but can't eat hazelnuts and I need to save these for a party later.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really enjoyed these cookies, made them a little smaller than suggested. Of note the candy cane amount works out to about 3 regular sized candy canes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Here's the accompanying video — it's a helpful visual: https://youtu.be/BFEgBiFIDu4

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I loathe peppermint so left out both peppermint ingredients. The result was a seriously delicious and  elegant brownie! I may never make brownies as bar cookies again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm thinking of doing the same - no peppermint but adding coconut and dried cherries.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like  more of a hint of peppermint with chocolate, so omitted the peppermint extract and added a half tsp of vanilla. I made them much smalller, about a tablespoon of batter and baked 8 minutes plus about 45 seconds back in the oven so the peppermint chips would adhere. Scrumptious two-bite treats!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: People with GF concerns may want to look at the King Arthur Flour site. Along with GF recipes they can provide guidance on substitutions. There is a phone number for calling their customer service site with questions on baking concerns. Good luck.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to thank whoever made this because it actually worked😭 i’ve been looking for a good brownie recipe and my hopes were exceeded with this one. the cookies spread and overall they look and taste fantastic! if you’re on the fence about them MAKE THEM!!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don’t have a mixer, did the 6-8 minutes of beating eggs and sugar by hand. Worth it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today according to the recipe except I don't have any scoops and I ended up with about 18 smaller cookies. They were great. I wish I had read the comments and used some instant coffee in these to enhance the chocolate flavor, which I normally do. Next time, I might add a little bit of vanilla extract, too. Definitely making again this holiday season. If you want a more traditional cookie, then yeah, chill the dough, but they turned out like the photo without chilling for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Goodness! These are the most delicious cookies I think I’ve ever had!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Whisking the eggs and sugars for a long time may seem fussy, but this process gives the cookie body, makes the batter easier to scoop and ensures a shiny top, the hallmark of any good brownie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have had 100% success with King Arthur GF flour...measure for measure 1 cup = 1 cup. Use interchangeably with any all-purpose flour in any recipe. Voilà! No extra recipe suggestions needed...just this wonderful GF flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Pat Lewis agreed, always comes through for me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I took the suggestion of chilling the dough after Step #5. And I made little meatball size balls of dough. This recipe made 4 dozen cookies! And very good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @G Scott. Bake the smaller ones for the same amount of time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For a great gluten free substitute, use King Arthur Baking Measure for measure gluten free blend. Tried it on gingerbread cookies last year and they were a hit!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made today. Cut Peppermint extract by 1/4tsp. Followed others’ suggestion to make smaller cookies. (Thank you) Oh my these are good!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Chill dough an hour or so, then roll into balls. Made smallish walnut sized balls, baked 10, then three minutes. Made 4 dozen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used mini muffin tins to bake these and they turned out great, crispy around the edges and soft in the centers. Making each about 12 grams (for those using a kitchen scale) yielded about four dozen mini brookie bites. 

I’ve also tried this with canna butter and the results are fantastic.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I find that bob’s 1:1 gf flour (or whatever gf brand you prefer that is 1:1) works for any baking recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Tips:

1) After step 5, take the whole mixing bowl and put it in the refrigerator (not freezer) for about 10 minutes. 
2) Make 24 rather than 12 cookies (keep cook time the same).
3) Don't break up your peppermint candy too much - you don't want any peppermint powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Used comments and did following with great results (and may have been wonderful with no tweaks!):
- 1/2 tsp espresso powder
- used a 2 tbspn scoop and made 8 cookies per sheet (16 total)
- whisked first batter for 8-9 minutes. Baked both without chilling and second half chilled. No difference. Did not spread much. 
- used only 3/4 peppermint extract, found flavorful without being overwhelming 
- 3 full sized candy canes to crush 
Holiday excellence. Thanks Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved this very chocolate cookie! We live in a cold climate so we didn't need to chill our dough first but I could see why that would be helpful if your kitchen is warm. The crushed peppermint candy didn't really melt into the cookie at all like it did in the NYT video. We pushed the candy into the cookie as it cooled to make sure it stayed on top. We used a 1/4 C scoop and the cookies were soft in the middle and slightly crunchy on the edges.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious! I accidentally added the cocoa powder to melting the chocolate. I also used chopped chocolate chips. The dough was loose just like the recipe mentioned. They turned out perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used chocolate chips too!  Also the recipe calls for some cocoa powder to be with the chocolate as the butter melts on top and then you also add some cocoa powder when you add the flour so you did it right!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Super rich chocolatey cookies with a brownie texture. Whipping the eggs with the sugar was an interesting (albeit tiring without a mixer) technique. Loved the texture on these. Gave some to a coworker who said they went really well with coffee so I can see why people would add coffee to this dough!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love mint and chocolate together but when I used the full amount of peppermint extract called for in this recipe, the mint was overpowering and I couldn't taste the chocolate at all. I made them a second time with 3/4 teaspoon of mint and they are perfect. Also, the texture of these cookies is fantastic (and I did not chill the dough at all).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Used to make these over the years with crushed candy canes - the smaller the better to get the full colored stripe.
Then I found Andes makes Peppermint Crunch Baking Chips, "creamy chips with bits of peppermint candy". They are both peanut and gluten free. Under $4 for the 10 oz bag here in SF Bay Area, CA. Worth every penny!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these vegan with egg replacer (Bob's Red Mill) and vegan butter (Miyoko's).  Used Scharffen Berger Bittersweet dark chocolate instead of semi-sweet. Took the recommendations of others and reduced the peppermint extract to one-half teaspoon and added 1 teaspoon of vanilla extract.  Chilled the dough and made smaller cookies.  Best vegan cookie I've ever made!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't have peppermint chips and put in 1/2 cup cocoa powder in the beginning instead of 1/4 cup - this made the dough thicker than expected and I decided to just roll it in powdered sugar and make crinkle cookies! Would recommend rolling in powdered sugar if you don't have peppermint chips and still want a nice look

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed recipe almost exactly and came out delicious! Used 72% dark chocolate chips instead of a semisweet bar: sweetness was perfect and pockets of melted chocolate were amazing. Made around 20 cookies so they weren’t humongous

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make sure you double, NO, TRIPLE this recipe! Absolutely incredible!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent taste, but mine also fell flat. I took the suggestion to refrigerate (probably for 30 min-an hour?), which helped with keeping their form out of the bowl, but next time I would do an overnight in the fridge. Used a tablespoon-sized scoop and got around 20 or 22. Baking time the same. We're on the fence about whether they will make it into the permanent rotation. If they turn out pretty next time, I think they will.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a small cookie scoop and portioned these out to freeze for xmas cookie boxes. I think freezing helped with the spread as I did not have that issue. They were delicious, great flavor and texture! I used peppermint chocolate coated pretzels crushed on top for a little extra fun. Everyone loved them, will definitely make again even out of season since I love mint & chocolate

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can't wait to try these. The video was very helpful; great tips! And, Vaughn is charming as all get out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 41 grams of crushed candy is about 3 regular candy canes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The cookies are FANTASTIC! I lost a fight with a stand mixer once, they are ruthless! Hope you mended quickly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these according to the recipe, but using a smaller scoop to have 24 of them. However, they were *extremely* underbaked. I preheated the oven. I followed the timing. And even adding two minutes, they mostly turned out like fudge. Really tasty—really not done. If I do it again, I might bake these for at least 5-10 more minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for Xmas 2021. Put dough on freezer for a week and then made later. Delicious. Suggest small with big pieces of candy cane because they melt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I am not a professional baker by any means, but I made some adjustments along the way that produced a really great cookie in my opinion 

- I added about 20 grams more flour (the batter looked way too thin for cookies with just 90 grams)
- I added half a teaspoon of baking powder to the flour mix 
- I chilled them for about 10 minutes before baking.
- I also made them much smaller (just over a teaspoon) and they came out great! I was also able to use the same cooking time as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Forgot to add that I made it GF by using Bob’s Red Mill one to one gluten free flour.  No one could tell. Worked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve never mixed eggs and sugar by themselves for a cookie or even brownie recipe before…and ‘pillowy’ seemed vague so I mixed it for probably longer than needed (tried to ensure the sugar granules were incorporated). As another commenter recommended, you really do need to whack the cookie sheet, which was short-lived therapeutic. These went super fast at our family get-together, highly recommend.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've been looking for a great recipe for chocolate cracked cookies for years.   This (minus the peppermint, and adding vanilla and tsp instant coffee) is it.  Great texture and flavor, completely addictive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tried to make these cookies last night. I even watched the video. They were a lot of work, were way too big (I should’ve read the comments to chill dough and shouldn’t have used 1/4 cup to drop cookies as instructed in the recipe), no rise, and  really didn’t taste good (too much peppermint and salt). Made cookies from a recipe from the back of a bag today and they are so much better! I have made many other recipes from NY Times and loved them. This one - not so much….

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delish! Doubled the recipe and it came out with 30 (plus two I just ate). Very rich chocolate and sometimes hard to stir, though that may be because I substituted 3tbsp cocoa powder, 3tbsp sugar, and 1tbsp melted butter for each ounce of chocolate (in case you're like me and somehow never have real chocolate bars). It is very good, and the whacking was surprisingly cathartic. Made these with m&m muffins and gave out to friends for Christmas--absolutely banger. We love you Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: agree with other commenters that you should chill it for about 10 minutes in the fridge otherwise the dough will be too hard to work with, but i think portioning it per the recipe is fine. we listened to mechteach's suggestion to make 24 cookies instead of 12 and i felt like our cookies were just way too small (although texturally fine).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious recipe. I did end up using a 3tbs scoop as my 1/4 cup cookies were way to large for my taste. I also ended up having to use just mint extract instead of peppermint because that was all that was available. Batter spread, so I used a round cookie cutter to help shape them more. I didn’t end up with a shiny top, nor did the wacking of the pan seem to have any effect. I will have to try again and see if I can get the shiny tops.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe with some of the recommendations. Used a little less peppermint extract, chilled the dough first, and made the cookies smaller. These still had an almost overwhelming flavor of peppermint. My complaint is how gooey they were! I baked them an extra few minutes to see if they would firm up a little more. Even after cooling, they were hard to handle without smearing/breaking unless I cooked longer and dried them out more. I guess I should have realized they’re supposed to be brownie cookies! But not great for a Christmas cookie exchange.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: refrigerate dough after step 5.     make smaller cookies.   no peppermint extract--just candy cane. (don't chop to powder,  just pieces)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made these a few times and have the same issues as others, which is mostly due to a very soft dough. As someone else said, the photos are very deceptive. best case scenario you chill the dough and a cookie scoop can be used for a more uniform cookie. Even with 30 minutes on the fridge, the dough was too soft to use a scoop. Mine usually look like drop cookies. They do spread and so I use a tsp-tbsp sized drop. Sometimes they flatten when I hit the pan, sometimes the fling all over the place. It's always a gamble. Hitting them tends to deflate them, IMO. Finally, putting candy cane pieces on then and placing them back in the oven will absolutely start melting the candy so do not be disappointed when they look nothing like the above picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these using Bob's Redmill 1:1 gluten free flour and they turned out perfect. My new favourite cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m not a peppermint person but this is one of the best cookies I have ever made! Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Great flavor cookies! My first several batches were flat and fudgy. Next batch I refrigerated dough all night and increased bake temp to 375. This gave me the crispy edges and fudgy center. 
Took to work event and everyone loved the deep chocolate flavor with a nice peppermint flavor. I also  added the Andies peppermint crunch bars. Chopped up and mixed in.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookies! We used Trader Joe's Peppermint Baking Chips and are very pleased with the results.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Two batches disappeared very quickly.  Made as described except made 24 cookies per batch using a 1 tablespoon scoop. Also cut mint extract to 1/2 tsp and added 1 tsp vanilla.  Per the comments, I refrigerated for 30 minutes. Be sure to let them cool before you take them off the parchment. They come out of the oven quite soft. Will be making them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Put butter in small bowl and micro 30 seconds on 5 to gently melt.  Add chocolate and 1/4 C cocoa; set aside to soften. In large bowl, vigorously whisk eggs and sugar by hand. Sub vanilla (or other) extract for peppermint. Add chocolate, then rest of ingredients to egg mixture in stages, without overmixing. Refrigerate dough for 10-15 minutes. Drop large tablespoons of dough onto prepared pans or form balls to freeze. Bake 10 minutes, then sprinkle tops with chopped nuts, coconut or peppermint.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Figure it out yourself w/ a bit of experimentation.  I'm allergic to milk, my brother to eggs, two cousins gluten free.  I figured out (with 1 experiment) how to make my grandmother's sweet rolls, Cornish Saffron Buns, for a family gathering.  It took a bit of ingenuity and the texture wasn't quite like grama's and the rise was a bit low, but they were great and everybody could enjoy them.  It's a complex yeast bread, a bit like hot cross buns.  The same formula works for any other yeast bread.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Had a couple of minor snafus, but the cookies were a hit. Chocolate bits did not melt, so put the dish in the oven while it was warming and that worked.  When I poured the chocolate into the well mixed egg/sugar mixture, some of it hit the side of my KitchenAid metal bowl and hardened there!  Try not to do that.   Didn't have peppermint extract so left it out.  Still fabulous! Made them much smaller (still came out to 1.5"-2" cookies) and made 40.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How can you make these cookies without a stand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use a regular hand held electric mixer, or use a whisk or wooden spoon and beat vigorously. I don’t have a stand mixer. Also, when we were kids, we used a wooden spoon with great success.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and I thought they turned out well. However, the peppermint extract was excessive to my taste - used 1.5 tsp as called for (could only find generic grocery-store brand peppermint extract), but tasted too minty/harsh. Nothing wrong with them, but basically a brownie in cookie form, so I’ll probably just stick to the Ghirardelli’s box mix.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have to say that these ARE really good.  Like others, I made smaller cookies as these are very rich - got 30.  My banging was not very productive regarding cracks, however, so I pushed the candies into the cookies to make them stick.  Folks should consider doubling this recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What a great cookie! Batter set up well. Easy to make smaller ones. Mine crackled in ten minutes so I stopped banging them. Got 35 cookies out of a batch. It doubled well. Love the way they hold puffy shape and love flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe was easy enough to pull together but  a disppointment! The dough was unmanageable and impossible to form into cookies... so I ended up making peppermit brownies instead. Tasty but not the desired result! Please revise the instructions to include a chilling/freezing step, an essential part of most successful cookie recipies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I put an upside down cookie sheet on top of my gas grate stove and banged the cookie sheet that came out of the oven on top of that. Worked like a charm.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lacking candy canes, I made this substitution.  In a jar, mix  1/4 cup of Swedish pearl sugar with 1/2 tsp peppermint extract.  Add 2 drops of food coloring (I used green) and shake.  You ll still have the added flavor, crunch and color.  Pearl sugar doesn’t melt during baking

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these cookies as directed. They have a great texture but I thought the peppermint flavor was a bit overwhelming. Next time I make them, I’ll scale back to 1tsp. Otherwise, no complaints! I love giant cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a small cookie scoop and it made exactly 24 cookies -- definitely the way to go because they're pretty rich. I probably used about half the amount of candy cane as well. I also used about 6 drops of peppermint essential oil (NOT extract) because I don't keep the extract around and that worked fine. They're pretty yummy, easy, and brownie-like.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I also followed someone’s suggestion to make 24 cookies and that was a palatable size. Cookies are good but we found the mint flavor overpowering the chocolate so we are halving the extract next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used almond extract instead of peppermint. Tasted like truffles

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think I put the peppermint shards on too early because they completely melted. So maybe wait toward the very end

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Need ~3 candy canes.  Takes time to unwrap and crush. 
Add a scant more extract.
Add about 1 T. espresso powder to dry at end.
Chill dough for ~15 min., then drop by teaspoons.  Bake 7 min. at 345 convection, really whack trays, sprinkle with peppermint candy, push down a bit, then bake almost 2 min. more--longer will start to melt candy.
Makes about 3 1/2 dozen.  
Don't break up peppermint candy too much--don't want a lot of peppermint powder.  
Next time try adding chopped walnuts?
Yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added flaky salt on top and made it really good! Would also agree on making them smaller

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Chopped the candy canes in an OXO nut chopper, one at a time. Worked very well... and no pieces went shooting across the floor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Followed the recipe as-is, and the only challenge I ran into seems to be a common one - the dough was very batter-like and hard to scoop into cookie dough balls. (And my process of trying was MESSY!) They looked sloppy going into the oven. That said they did bake up into a convincingly cookie-shaped form. And most importantly were delicious! 

Would definitely recommend, with the caveat that you prepare for a little more mess than you might expect from either a standard cookie or brownie recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these equally substituting gluten free flour for regular. Turned out excellently and no one knew the difference.  Also made 24, not 12 using a small cookie dough scooper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it as directed, except I made the cookies smaller so I  got 2 dozen. They disappeared quickly. Next time I might add a little espresso as suggested.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great cookies! Followed Mechtech’s tips and subbed gluten flour. Also used peppermint choc chips instead of candy canes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'll try again, but at altitude near Denver these flattened considerably as written. We usually bake as is first, then do an altitude adjustment of +2TBSP flour and +2TBSP liquid (butter? egg white?) to most recipes. Since there's very little flour here, leavening isn't an issue, and I'm concerned extra butter will keep flattening these out. Maybe a smidge less time in the oven? We chilled the dough and used balls about 1 1/2TBSP in size. Still 4" across when baked! Smell amazing!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is flabbergasting. It’s mind blowing. It’s transcendent. I’ll be honest: I was a skeptic. I read the recipe and it didn’t give me faith. The part where you bang the hot cookie sheets on the counter made me incredibly nervous. I called family members in as back up because I wasn’t sure that I could do it. But I am a convert. I’m going to become a leader in the cult of these cookies. I urge everybody else to join me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: tasty! I like the suggestion to half the size so you make more, smaller cookies. I did a scant tsp of peppermint because it's quite strong. Also I just melted the butter and chocolates together on a double boiler because I already had one going and that was fine. Lastly, don't be a dummy and forget the salt like me. you really need it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Be careful to hold back 1/4c. of the cocoa powder to add to the flour for step five instead of dumping the entire 1/2c. into the chopped butter and chocolate like I did.  The 1/4c. font is tiny and easy to miss. A note in the ingredients suggesting half of the cocoa be reserved for later would be helpful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
*Melted butter halfway then stirred in choc chips + cocoa over VERY low heat in sauce pan.
*Mixed eggs/sugar for 8 minutes. No "pillow-y" texture. 
*Folded in flour/cocoa with spatula. 
*Rested batter in fridge for 6-7 hours.  
* Used small candy canes, cut up with kitchen scissors. 
*Used teaspoon as scoop. Baked for 9 minutes, took out to top with peppermint candy, baked for another 2 minutes. Cooled on sheet.
*Made 32 cookies. Very rich cookie. Can't imagine eating 1 of 12 big cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
1) After step 5, take the whole mixing bowl and put it in the refrigerator (not freezer) for about 10 minutes.  2) Make 24 rather than 12 cookies (keep cook time the same). 3) Don't break up your peppermint candy too much - you don't want any peppermint powder.  4) Really whack those trays on the counter.

optional: add 1 tsp espresso powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this exactly as written, and it was truly a "brownie cookie" with great texture.  I used McCormick's pure peppermint flavoring, and 1 1/2 tsp was definitely overkill, it left a bitter taste.  1 tsp of salt was also too much.  The second batch I cut back to 1 tsp peppermint and a scant tsp of salt, much better.  Also used a medium cookie scoop, probably smaller than the 1/4c specified, so I baked for only 10 minutes and they were perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and wow, it's like mouthwash. I literally have fresh breath after eating one. I think maybe a half teaspoon of extract would have been plenty. Sadly, I have to toss these. And they're the only holiday cookies I made this year. What a waste and what a disappointment from NYT Cooking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were excellent! I used them as a cookie sandwich with peppermint bark ice cream in the middle. Delicious holiday treat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is a lights-out, intense, chewy chocolate cookie that needs no embellishment. (But I made half candy cane and half chopped candied ginger.)

I made them on the smaller side. And never really managed to bang the tray hard enough…and it didn’t matter!

This is a keeper for sure!  Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These cookies were truly the best of both brownie worlds, the gooey fudgey center was absolutely perfect for middle piece brownie people and the crisp edges will please any corner piece person! 
some notes:
- you can make it without the peppermint extract and candy cane and it is just as delicious
- this recipe makes more cookies than 12, I made 16 
- the whacking on the counter top is CRUCIAL, for a perfectly crackled top!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these gluten free (King Arthur GF 1-1), and dairy free (Earth Balance), and no peppermint. Amazing brownie cookie! I will be making these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 90 g of flour didn’t make the batter quite thick enough so I ended up adding a few more tablespoons and this worked perfectly. I agree that a quarter cup makes enormous cookies and I needed more than 12 so I ended up dropping these onto the cookie sheet by generous tablespoons. Turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are my absolute favorite holiday cookies. Following the recipe on cookie size makes huge cookies, but when you halve it the cookies come out looking much less appealing and don’t have as much of the delicious soft chewy brownie middle. I’d recommend following the recipe, but that’s just me! :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good recipe, but the peppermint candy formed an unattractive molten blob on the cookies - I had bought pre-crushed candy, and it was apparently crushed too fine. I found by that it worked when I sprinkled it on after taking the cookies out of the oven for the last time - it was tacked on but not melted into a blob.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: At 5000 ft. elevation I added 1/2 Tbsp of flour and that was all the adjustment needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good deep chocolate flavor. Make them slightly smaller than the recipe says. The pans will need several hard whacks. Target sells chipped peppermint. Mine did not spread that much...looked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a vegan version of these using Myokos butter and just egg as substitutes. The result was very fudge-y cookies. They were big and I would do smaller scoops next time! I think that since there is a sugar and egg whisking section I didn’t quite get it as pillowy as I would like— had it been more pillowy I think the batter would have baked differently- perhaps less dense? It’s a recipe worth trying again & I’ll be making some for Christmas. Oh I also did this without the peppermint extract!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these gluten-free and dairy-free by substituting  1:1 GF flour and vegan olive oil butter. I added an extra egg and a little dash of gf vanilla. They turned out delicious, especially still warm out of the oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Texture is divine! But too much peppermint extract for my taste. It really overwhelmed the chocolate flavor. I’d cut it to 1 to 1 1/4 tsp next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As someone else said, if you want these to look like the picture (and isn’t that partly the draw?) the crushed candy cane needs to be added after they are done cooking. Otherwise you get unappetizing blobs that look like melted plastic. The flavor of these was fine but I wouldn’t make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I watched the video first (it was great) and then went ahead and baked these. They were absolutely delicious, and like he said, very similar to the “corner” brownie. I am a big fan of peppermint, so it really worked for us. Beware, use peppermint extract, NOT peppermint oil!!!  Oy! And make more than one batch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I double-checked my new peppermint "flavour" after reading this, which was good because it was actually oil!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed mechteach’s insight. Added 1 tbsp of ground coffee for a little peppermint mocha vibe, delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Heed the instruction to line your sheets with parchment paper. I used silicone mats on half the batch and they were very undercooked! The batch on the half with parchment paper were perfectly done. I would pull back on the peppermint extract to let more of the chocolate flavor come through.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Follow mechteach's notes. These cookies are simply divine and disappear a little too quickly!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I doubled the recipe which yielded 48 1oz cookies. I omitted the peppermint and subbed 2 tsp of espresso powder in the double recipe. Next time I would increase the espresso by 1 tsp and add more salt or else top with maldon. 
I chilled the batter for 30 minutes before scooping and chilled each sheet pan 10 minutes before baking as the recipe directed. The texture was terrific. Dense, soft and chewy slightly crisp on the edges. 
Looking forward to trying Mr. Vreelands gingerbread latte cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these today with vanilla extract, expresso powder and Heath bar bits instead of crushed peppermint.  This is a very versatile recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: While these are chocolatey, they are not as densely chocolate as the ginger-chocolate NYTC recipe. You can get the brownie-like texture even if you make them smaller (as most seem to do), but you may need to press them down lightly when taking them out of the oven, as banging doesn't do the trick. For me, they also needed less baking time to get that brownie consistency ~7min for 1/2 size cookies. Could add some chips to give them some more toothiness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were fantastic. Exactly what I wanted and expected. The only thing I could not find was peppermint extract, I had to substitute peppermint oil (in 1/4 of the amount). The kitchen filled will peppermint smell as they baked.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are DIVINE.  Last Christmas people in my house fought over who got the last one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really nice cookies. As other suggested - I added a teaspoon of espresso powder, and made 24, vs 12 cookies. Same cook time. They were a real treat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made them with 70 g brown and 100 g white sugar, kept all else the same. Great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fabulous, dark chocolaty, soft cookie with perfect peppermint balance.  Made them with a small scoop and got 25 cookies of just the right size.  Hope there will be enough for all the plates of cookies Igive away as I just might finish them myself!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved. Would make either one ounce balls or 1.5, chill beforehand but DO NOT freeze, then place on trays

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these today. A few notes to add - yes, put the batter in the fridge for a good amount of time before you go to bake them off. Make them smaller and finally, I baked them longer so they had some structure to them and more crunch as I'm not a fan of gooey.  Overall, pretty good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow, these are delicious!  Doubled the recipe.  Refridgerated for an hour or so, didn't spread too much.  If you use high quality chocolate, there is no need for coffee added.  These are now permanent in my cookie rotation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I decreased the amount of peppermint by 1/3 (instead of 1.5 teaspoons, I just used 1 teaspoon) and it was perfect.  Also, with the first batch I followed directions and sprinkled peppermint bits on top of the cookies and put them back in the oven for two more minutes--and all the peppermint melted!  So for the second batch I cooked for 11 minutes and then took out, banged on the counter, and immediately put the peppermint on--and did NOT put back in the oven.  This worked perfectly!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Subbed the peppermint extract for vanilla and the crushed candy cane for shopped roasted hazelnuts (added them a little earlier in the baking so they’d stick before the cookie tops set)
Brilliant!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Perfect as directed! Didn't make enough:)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely recommend cooling for 10 minutes after step 5.  I also agree that making 24 cookies makes a nicer size. It is easier than it looks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Probably the best cookie I have ever baked.  The video was very helpful. Thanks for the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved the richness of it but I didn’t get the crinkle on top? Maybe because I used King Arthur flour which has a higher protein count instead of Gold Medal?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made them as written last year and found them very sweet. This year, used unsweetened chocolate and cut back granulated sugar to 1/2 cup. They were perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were a hit. I made them with peppermint chips instead. They were still brownie-like and not overly sweet. I refrigerated the dough overnight, used my smallest cookie scoop, and added only a minute to bake time. Don’t be tempted to leave cookies in the oven if still soft as this will yield a less fudgy texture.  I think this recipe will work well with a gluten free flour if needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are excellent, but make sure the candy canes are pretty finely smashed. Large chunks of candy in such a chewy soft cookie is not a good combination of textured.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are lovely - perfect brownie consistency. I made the cookies slightly smaller (the recipe made 18 total), refrigerated the dough for 15 mins prior to baking, and kept the baking time the same. They didn’t spread too much and came out of the oven dense and fudgy. Even better at room temp the next day!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good! My mom made these for me with gluten free flour and they are my new favorite cookie! Perfect combo of sweet, a hint of salt, peppermint and chocolate- just delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use a kitchen scale to weigh ingredients so my measurements are correct. The batter was too thin to scoop and very sticky. I had to refrigerate more than 2 hours to form into balls. I think this is a recipe where you need to use a scoop because of the sticky batter.  The cookie texture and taste is excellent, chocolatey and minty but the batter made it a hassle. I made the cookies between 35-40 grams which makes a good size. Did anyone else experience the thin batter?  I didn’t overbeat it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn, you have a new fan. These cookies are fantastic as written. Tip from someone who learned the hard way - f you use individually-wrapped round peppermint candies, don’t whack them with your wooden rolling pin. They are so hard that they dent the wood.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these on New Years Eve because I had time to spare.  I like them with the cracked peppermint but I will join the crowd that said too much peppermint extract.  (It could have been my extract was too old).  Nice chewy texture.
Can't wait for cookie week next year.  Could it be in early November so we can prepare, please.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lovely cookies. Based on the comments I'm not surprised they've posted a non peppermint version. I do like peppermint so I was happy to make this version. I did need to chill the dough before scooping and I made them smaller.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Chilling the dough helps. I make half size — it’s a delicious but intense cookie , smaller is better. This is also such a jolly cookie! My go-to for exchanges.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These cookies are delicious!!! They have a fudgey interior but with some crunch to it! I didn’t use peppermint but they still come out great.

Some tips:
-If you don’t have a cookie scoop, just put the bowl of dough in the fridge for a few minutes. It makes it WAAAAY easier to work with by hand

-I would NOT scoop out 1/4 cup it seems like too much. I would scoop about a little more than 2 tablespoons 

-I would assume this recipe makes over 20 cookies. (This can differ from if you scoop out 1/4 cup of dough or my measurements)

-If you think your dough balls look fairly small (a little more than 1 tbsp), then put them in for 9 minutes instead of 10

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish! Added the espresso powder as suggested (I hate coffee and couldn’t taste any coffee flavor), chilled the dough, made smaller dough balls but did not whack the cookie sheets hard enough! So so good. These are an instant holiday classic!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good!!! Made this recipe gluten free with gf flour and 1/8 teaspoon of xanthan gum and texture came out perfect. I made 18 cookies, instead of 12, just by making smaller balls than recipe calls for. Baking time didn’t vary with gluten free substitutions. For a crispier cookie bake maybe 1-2 more minutes than the recommended time. Loved this recipe will bake again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed others suggestions and chilled the dough for about 15 minutes before scooping, and between each bake. Didn’t have espresso powder to add, but did have chili powder, and since I’m keen on spice, used that to enrich the cocoa flavor. Also didn’t have peppermint to crush, and forgot to add any salt until I’d scooped, so topped with flakey salt and it came out great. Not really the recipe, but still good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Flavor and texture is excellent. i doubled the batch and it only made only 16 cookies. This dough spreads a lot and makes large cookies. Next time i will follow readers’ advice and refrigerate the dough and roll smaller balls.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I should have read the helpful notes and froze my balls, but it turned out great anyway.  Maybe better, because the cookies spread and so I got more of that crisper surface area.  The cracking thing never happened when I hit my tray against the counter, maybe because they were too flat.  Good enough for me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out looking great with an ideal brownie texture, but I thought the mint extract overpowered the chocolate flavor.  If I were making them again, I would use about 1/2  teaspoon of extract so that the rich chocolate flavor comes through more. I also made 24 regular-sized cookies instead of 1 dozen.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I refrigerated the mixture overnight and the cookies baked up really well.  I put the peppermint candy bits on at the beginning of the bake and couldn't tell the difference between those and the ones I added near the end of the bake.   I don't like working with the cookies when they've come straight out of the oven so my method saves me from burnt fingertips.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish holiday cookie for peppermint cocoa fans. Followed instructions carefully, but made the dough into slightly smaller portions. Even with smaller cookies, I would bake them two minutes more next time. This batch was underbaked and a little "floppy." Still yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Love! Not too sweet, also not too difficult to make with a stand mixer. 

I subbed Andies mint chocolate chips for peppermint because I didnt't have any, and they still tasted great but obviously lacked the crunchy texture. As for peppermint flavor, the extract took care of that. They may look undercooked when they come out of the oven but don't be fooled, the timing/temp is just right. 

Would make these again next year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made these twice. Second time I halved the peppermint extract and it was perfect.  Peppermint tasted overpowering with the recommended amount.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
You could go lighter with the peppermint extract
Refrigerate the batter for 30 mins, helps handle it better
Increase the amount of time for baking by a minute and reduce baking post adding candy cane by a minute(if not candy cane could melt)
Use smaller scoops for a normal sized cookie
Next time try adding espresso powder.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I cut back the peppermint extract to 1/2 teaspoon, chilled the dough for 1 hour, and formed into 1-inch balls.  They were done after 10 minutes at 350.  Made about 4 dozen.  I didn't sprinkle with crushed peppermint, but next time, I'll either do that or up the peppermint extract to 3/4 tsp.  The cookies are chewy and a little crispy on the outside.  I will make them again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed recipe with 2 exceptions:  Smaller cookies for total of 18; and I used the creme di menthe chocolate (broken in smaller pieces) for a little green stripe.   My cookies tops did not crinkle.  What could have contributed to this?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish. Definitely fudgy. Added a bit of extra cocoa powder in the butter stage (this was accidental but it tirn

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would definitely recommend flattening out the cookies and shaping them so they get that crinkle look. I used the open part of a glass and made a circle. I also think you should roll them out not just scoop them otherwise it becomes more of a mound than what it is supposed to look like

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Came together easily and made as directed. I love peppermint but would back off on extract / maybe it was my extract as there was a bitterness that persisted. Not everyone noted but I did:)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Also, forgot to whack pans on counter and still turned out fine. Would do that next time though. Could’ve used another 1/2 tsp extract

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved this cookie. Was part of my cookie gift bag last year. Very deep, dark chocolaty flavor, which I love. I omitted the extract just because I don't have any and crushed up some candy canes for the topping.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This tastes exactly like a brownie in cookie form, one of the best recipes I have tried in a while. I refrigerated the dough before baking and I sub’d vanilla for the peppermint because then instead of all peppermint toppings I could top with m&m’s or peanut butter chips.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you use semisweet chocolate chips?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @debk I do. I use 4 oz of the Nestle mini chips

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes, they work just fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These Peppermint Brownie Cookies are fantastic!!! The only thing I did differently is make the cookies a bit smaller and therefore I had about 22 cookies instead of 12.  Made the recipe twice already.  They’re that good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made these today, used my one tablespoon cookie scoop, baked for 5 minutes, whacked them on the counter and sprinkled them with crushed candy canes, back in the oven for a minute or two, perfection.  These cookies are so incredibly rich and chocolatey a bigger cookie would be too much, you wouldn’t be able to eat all the other delicious cookies on the table!!  We got about three dozen this way.  A two bite cookie, leaving plenty of room for all the other treats!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We’ve made these many times over a few years. These are crazy sweet. I now use unsweetened dark chocolate and reduce the granulated sugar to 1/2 cup. I think I can reduce this even further. I reduce the mint to 1 tsp and it is plenty. I make 1 oz balls. They flatten out nicely in the oven. I like Xmas sprinkles instead of candy cane.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these much smaller than called for (12 per recipe?!) and decreased the cooking time by a few minutes to retain softness. The first batch was devoured so quickly I have already made a second and plan to make more. Simple, easy, big payoff. My grocery had a bag of crushed candy cane bits, which make the topping super easy. Nice for gifting. If you do make big ones (I started off following the recipe instructions) be warned that you will be able to eat an entire giant cookie by yourself! But they won't last very long, because everyone else can eat a big cookie too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used a meat cleaver to chop the peppermint candies because hitting them just created holes in the bag. Used 1/2 t peppermint extract. Didn't need to chill the dough. 1 T dough/cookie and adjusted baking times per another commenter: 8 min, topping, 1 min. Did not need to whack the cookie sheets.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are a must-make!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could you make these gluten free?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You should be able to substitute with an all purpose GF flour. I'm going to give that a shot.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Celiac baker here- Yes, for sure. Recipes that have a relatively smaller proportion of flour and are more heavily flavored usually adapt well to gf. I would modify by resting the dough for at least 1/2 hour at the end of step 5, and refrigerating for 20 min/freezing for 10 min before putting them in the oven. If your flour blend doesn't contain xanthan gum, mix 1/2 tsp to your measured-out flour. When measuring gf flour, always spoon into the measuring cup for the most accurate measuring.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For Sandra:

I've frozen both unbaked cookie dough and already baked cookies with success.

For dough, roll it into balls, then freeze the balls on a cookie sheet lined with parchment or a silicone baking sheet. Then transfer the frozen balls to a bag or container for storage in the freezer. Frozen dough takes a few minutes longer to bake.

You can freeze already baked cookies the same way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could I use a blender if I don't have a stand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You would be better off doing this by hand with a whisk and a spoon. If you can get a hand mixer (manual or electric) that would make it easier.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These were delicious! I made 24 smaller ones and the cooking time in the recipe was perfect. Roommates can’t get enough. 

Want to try making them vegan next time — what would the best egg substitute be? Flax meal, aquafaba, Just Egg? Maybe I’ll run an experiment.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In almost every cookie recipe, for each egg, I successfully substitute 1 Tablespoon of mixed ground flax and ground chia with 2 1/2 Tablespoons warm water, allowed to gel for 5-10 minutes.  My sack happened to be mixed flax/chia already, but I’m sure just ground flaxseed would work equally well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can they be kept longer than 4 days in an airtight tin?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would this freeze well? Says it keeps 4 days which doesn't seem long...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My family doesn’t love peppermint, any substitutions? I was thinking of candied ginger or orange peel?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make it without the peppermint and  you have a nice chocolate cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A friend and I are taking turns working our way through the NYT Cooking "24 Days of Cookies" recipes and WOW, these are delicious! My favorite so far! I will definitely be giving batches of these to friends over the holidays. I found the video helpful but not necessary (these are so easy). My batter did spill out onto my baking tray more than in the video (thinner batter). Once baked they were also thinner than in the video. But that did not detract from the taste one bit! Excellent recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: i followed the instructions exactly and these came out so good. the perfect holiday brownie cookie! on the first batch, the candy didn’t adhere totally so on the second batch i pressed them gently into the cookie and they stuck better. i will admit if i did not have a stand mixer, i may not have mixed for the full 8 minutes the recipe calls for. overall an excellent recipe, unique flavors, will make again for the holidays next year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these with King Arthur GF flour. Taste great but I wasn’t able to ‘crack them’ really well after walking a bunch of times. Any tips to make them crack better? Maybe more ball-like shapes or chilling the dough, or baking them a bit longer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I bake at 7000 ft. altitude.  I usually reduce sugar by 1-2 T per cup and add some flour.  Any recommendations for how much flour to add for this recipe?  These sound yummy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I live at 8,000 feet and made the recipe as directed. Cookies turned out perfect. I find that most cookie recipes don't need much adjustment.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am gluten intolerant and I modify every recipe just using a one-to-one replacement. There are a ton of resources out there for gluten free flours to meet your needs. I have never had any problem with any substitution.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Reading through the notes, I agree that 1/4 cup scoop for the cookie is too much. I halved each scoop into two cookies, which made for a good size, about 3-inches in diameter. The cookies came out perfect and are wonderful!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I can’t seem to find peppermint extract. I have pure peppermint oil, so how many drops would equal 1 ½ tsp of extract? It’s very strong!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I enjoyed these cookies but I think next time around I will make them without the peppermint. The fudgy brownie texture was great!  Crunchy edges and dense chewy center. 

I refrigerated the dough for a couple hours before I baked and used a cookie scoop to make about 16 normal sized cookies. I did not have to adjust the cooking time. 12 minutes on convection was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, including using a quarter cup scoop. I only got 10 cookies and the last four were scant quarter cups. They could have baked another minute or so…I did exactly 10 minutes followed by another two minutes with the candy sprinkles and they were a tad underdone. Next time I will use a slightly smaller scoop. But my son loves them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies spread so use a cookie baller and leave space

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are absolutely delicious, but I think there’s just a hair too much salt in them. I’ll make it a scant tsp of salt in future, but I’ll definitely make them again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Since I cannot find diamond crystal salt anywhere, it would be nice if you would give regular kosher salt equivalents.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think for any other kosher salt (e.g., Morton's), it's half of the Diamond amount.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use 3/4 a tsp of Mortons and it was way too salty.  Yuck!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like these cookies a lot but think I’ll cut the peppermint extract to 1 tsp next time. I refrigerated the dough overnight so mine didn’t spread that much. I got 19 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were easy to throw together but I had similar issues as others. The cookies were huge and spread very thin in the oven. I think it was a combination of not chilling the dough and putting both pans in the oven at the same time. The peppermint also wasn’t what I was hoping for. I wanted a peppermint hot chocolate type taste but the peppermint overwhelmed the chocolate. I’ll probably reduce the peppermint extract next time or leave it out all together.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can the batter be made in advance and then put in the freezer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I suggest adding the candy cane pieces sooner. I think after about five minutes of baking, the dough is too set to “grab” the candy cane bits.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
1. Doubled the batch & added 2 tsp of instant coffee to the chocolate/cocoa powder/butter mixture.
2. Did crushed candy canes on about 1/2, sprinkles on the other half. The cookies are nicely peppermint-y even without the crushed CC on top.
3. Used a 1 Tbsp scoop for a total of 4 1/2 dozen - fit 12 cookies on a half sheet pan, with each one spreading to ~2 1/2 inches in diameter.

Really lovely recipe - easy to follow and a terrific result.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I substituted regular for all purpose gluten free flour and it came out great! The texture is the perfect type of cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really enjoyed this recipe and so did everyone at my Christmas party. Instead of a 1/4 C scoop I made 2 tablespoon mounds and am so glad I did! With such a rich cookie, it was more enjoyable to be able to go back later for another cookie than committing to such a hefty serving size in one go.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious— but i wish i made many more, smaller cookies! If you make them as suggested in this recipe, they turn out massive!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I didn't realize how annoying unwrapping candy canes was! I ended up needing 3-4. They were also tricky to bash. Next time I make these I will use the softer peppermint candies vs. canes.

The cookie recipe itself was great and came out exactly as written! I added a bit of maldon salt along with the candy, which I recommend.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Replace some of the cocoa powder with espresso powder

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are amazing.  After eating only one, I sent rest to my sons so I wouldn't eat any more,  lol.  Like other reviews, the huge size needs to be about half that size, so will try again making smaller ones.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these cookies and feel these cookies should be baked longer. Next time I would add an additional 2-3 minutes to the initial 10 minutes. The cookies spread so much that when I banged them on the counter, it didn't make a difference.  In order to get the peppermint candy look, don't pulverize the candies like I did. Make sure to have chunks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought texture and appearance were perfect, but I felt the peppermint extract overpowered the chocolate flavor. If I were making these again, I would add half as much extract as it is very concentrated. And this is personal, but I would up the sugar from 3/4 cup to 1 cup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: ok, these delicious cookies are joining regular rotation, not just at the holidays. Agree wholeheartedly with refrigerating batter first & making smaller cookies. Next will be making these LESS peppermint PLUS coffee. And SOON since we've eaten the entire first batch already. Yay!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 1/2 teaspoon peppermint extract and thought it was plenty of mint. These cookies are great. I think I’d like them even better with no extract and just the crumbles on top.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great…except for one thing; it’s too sweet. If I do these again, I’ll reduce the white sugar to 1/2 cup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these today and they are delicious.  I did make them smaller (28) and they were the perfect size.  I followed the directions exactly and am delighted with the results.

Thanks to the contributor who mentioned the difference between Diamond and Morton's Kosher salts!  I didn't realize there was a difference so I used slightly less than 1/2 teaspoon of Morton's and that seemed to be just right.

I'll be making this again--it's a winner.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The batter was very runny, so added enough extra flour to make it form.  Also, made 24 cookies.  Baking times accurate.  Don't  know if I'd make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What can I do to help the cookies last longer than 4 days? Do I need to freeze them?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add more peppermint

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine did not rise at all, totally flat.  Any suggestions?  Using a hand beater bc I don't have a stand mixer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I had the same problem.  After watching the YouTube video -- www.youtube.com/watch?v=BFEgBiFIDu4 -- I decided I hadn't beaten the eggs and sugar anywhere near fast enough.  You really do need to "whip" them, which is the word my stand mixer uses for Speed #6, of 8.

Otherwise, these were good, except maybe a bit too salty for my taste.  And I'll definitely try chilling the dough next time, and using a smaller scoop.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Perfect one. Love it

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I might try it once again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely do not use 1.5 tsp peppermint extract. It came out extremely strong. I’d use 1/2 a tsp total next time, or maybe skip it altogether and just use the crumbled sticks

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Totally agree. Against my better judgement, I followed the recipe and thought the mint flavor literally overpowered the chocolate flavor. Am getting ready to make them again, using 1/2 tsp.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly according to recipe but cookies were as flat as a piece of paper.  Stabs mixer might be a requirement

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for the second time. Agree, a 10 - 15 minute chill in the fridge before scooping them onto the tray makes it easy. Doubled the recipe, made " regular" sized mounds and skipped the candy on top... too much work ( I like the suggestion for the Swedish pearl sugar on top) Still really good. Have a second double batch underway using almond extract instead of peppermint. Everyone is getting them as a gift... a cookie project that does not take all day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made with gluten free flour (Cup4cup) and it turned out fine.  Next time I will use a ⅛ cup scoop to make smaller cookies and increase peppermint extract to at least 2 teaspoons.  I will use 1 Tablespoon of peppermint extract for full peppermint flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly except I ended up making them a tad smaller. They’re delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the flavor and texture. Did chill abt 5 mins before baking and made smaller cookies as well. A real winner I will make every year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used just over 2T per cookie, ended up with 18. I banged the pan, but didn’t get the “craggy” top shown in the picture. They spread a ton, so my first batch ended up as comical round edged rectangles once I cut them apart. Cook time was perfect at about 11-12 min, but 2 min in 350 oven was way too much for the peppermints, even my large chunks melted. I cut down to about 20 seconds which was a bit fussy, but worked. I’ll probably try again with a touch less peppermint, chill, & smaller cookies

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine didn’t spread- just sort of lumps. Any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so good for the holidays. Forget the iced sugar cookies. These plus a nice drink involving brandy are all you need on a cold dark night. Cheers!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ingredients: 1 tsp — 1 1/4 tsp peppermint extract. The 1 1/2 tsp was a bit too much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a 1.5 tbsp cookie scoop and did two full scoops (equivalent to 1/4 cup) and was only left with 7 cookies so don’t do what I did! I would suggest one full 1.5 tbsp cookie scoop as opposed to the suggested 1/4 cup. Cookies still look great but I would have liked them to be a bit smaller for sharing purposes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these two nights ago. No modifications except chilled the balls of dough for about an hour before baking and made them significantly smaller. Really excellent taste, look and texture! Definitely a keeper.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’d be helpful if the ingredients list noted “divided” after the cocoa powder quantity. I just put the whole amount in with the butter rather than the half. My fault, but “divided” would have helped me avoid that mistake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these today using the scale for all ingredients. The batter was very runny so added a lot more flour (recipe said they should be slightly thicker than brownie batter) probably about double and the consistency was perfect. 1/4 c is a big cookie! They are delicious and I would add espresso powder to make it even better!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just finished making this cookie, and although it is delicious (made exactly as written), my cookies are flat. Any ideas on what I did wrong? I'm wondering if I should have refrigerated the dough.  Thanks in advance for your suggestions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I portioned these using a heaping tablespoon, which made the perfect sized cookies, and more of them. I netted about 25 cookies. I can't imagine them bigger. Great recipe! It's easy but looks and tastes special for the holidays. Slamming the cookie pan on the counter after the first 10 minutes is key!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I put the dough in the fridge for a bit to make it a little easier to work with. I used a coffee scoop (1/8 cup) to scoop them out and it made 2 dozen. I agree the 1/4 cup size is toooo big. I made a few to eat tonight and put the rest of the dough balls into the freezer to bake up another day. Yum - they are right on the line between cookie and brownie. I will add some espresso powder next time too, that's a good idea.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 1/2 tsp peppermint extract

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use silpats instead of parchment.  Could that be why they didn’t get the mottled look?  I will make them smaller next time.  Definitely don’t overbake.    Might use less butter as well, they were a bit greasy on the bottom.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used a smaller scoop and made 40 cookies. The smaller ones didn't like to flatten when smacking the cookie sheet.  So I gave them a tiny tap with my metal spatula to make them less round.  Used vanilla instead of peppermint extract.  Really rich and tasty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used Schilling peppermint extract, the only brand at the store, and 1 1/2 t. was good!  

Per others' comments:  Added about 1 T. espresso powder to dry at end and chilled dough for about 15 min.  Then I dropped by teaspoons, baked 7 min. at 345 convection, really whacked the trays, sprinkled with peppermint candy, pushed candy bits down a bit so they wouldn't fall off, then baked almost 2 min. more--longer started to melt the candy. Made about 3 1/2 dozen.  

Yummy and holiday festive!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A beautiful and tasty cookie. Looks like Christmas! Pretty easy — but I’d say add flour as needed. I used nearly a cup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these according to the recipe and they were a huge hit. The smaller ones cooked more thoroughly with a nice chewiness and I think were better than the bigger ones, which tended to fall apart but still tasted amazing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these gluten free with cup for cup flour and they turned out beautifully. Very chocolatey and festive! I would add an extra tablespoon of the GF flour if I did it again. They spread a little more than I wanted.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love mint chocolate and only liked this. Next time I’ll add more texture - I think these would be good with (unmelted) choc chips! And perhaps extremely dark baking chocolate / less sugar. I made them the 2oz size instructed and they are extremely photogenic

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is it possible to whisk by hand until I feel my arm will fall off (probably only a couple of minutes) and still get good results? My whisk attachment broke.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: yes, it will be a workout.  People made cookies with electric mixers for years.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not only did I not get the characteristic brownie top, I felt the cookies were under baked. I was disappointed in this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had enough dough to make about double the recipe, though I didn't figue that out until part way trough so had 8 very large cookies and 12 slightly smaller, but still substantial, ones. Doing it again, I'd aim for 32 with the same recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Help! My extract was too strong and cookies are way too minty. Can anyone suggest a post-bake edit? Some sort of icing? Thank you!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought the mint flavor was way too strong and actually overpowered the chocolate flavor. I'm getting ready to bake them again and will only use half the amount of extract called for.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved this recipe! Followed as written. However did not agree with the result from a comment here to leave the candy cane pieces bigger- they turned into jawbreakers. When I make it again, I would pulse or grind quite fine to top- the final 2 mins as advised worked well to avoid melting.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be frozen to eat later?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ok. I always find that these recipes are drier than they should be after following the directions. I added  about 2 tbs of milk to this mixture to achieve a loose mixture like they said. When I first added the flour, it looked more like a dough!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing! Will be my go-to chocolate cookie recipe, without peppermint outside of holiday season. Didn’t spread too much, I got 12 reasonably sized cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
So others can learn from me: 

1) Despite *knowing* that the recipe (frustratingly) does not call for the cocoa powder is divided, I dumped all of it in to be melted. I have no idea if it affected the resulting cookies, although they did not appear any different from the video and they taste great. 

2) I used a #40 disher — made cookies about 2-2.5” wide. I *think* the video shows a #30 disher, for bigger cookies, although that is never mentioned.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used to be a professional food writer, and whenever my recipe called for divided use, I ALWAYS put "divided" next to that ingredient!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't like peppermint very much.  Does anyone have thoughts on whether these could be made/would be good with butterscotch or some other flavor of hard candy instead? (Obviously would leave out the peppermint extract as well).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are good but very peppermint forward.  As others have noted, you can leave it out altogether or reduce the measure to 1 teaspoon, maybe a bit less.  And, again, as others have said, 1/4 cup of dough would make a HUGE cookie.  I used a 3 tablespoon scoop and a 2 T scoop.  I'll use the 2 T scoop alone next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today and they were a big hit.  I cut the peppermint down to 3/4 teaspoon, and that was more than enough.  Added 2 teaspoons of espresso; couldn't really tell it was there.   I crushed the candy cane down pretty small, and that seemed to work fine. Only left them in for 1 minute to melt the peppermint candy; that seemed like more than enough. I didn't find the 1/4 cup scoop to be too large; it seemed fine.  Next time I might add some chopped walnuts to add some additional crunch!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were the stars of this year's holiday cookie boxes.  I gave out the recipe at least 4 times in 2 days.  I am making extra this weekend just for us.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added 1 tsp espresso powder to the batter and made these smaller using a 1 tbsp drop and cooked 8 minutes. Simply scrumptious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is it true these cookies only last 4 days at room temp?  What makes them different from other cookies than can last one week plus?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious, my husband loved these. Not too sweet

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I cooked them exactly as the recipe says and everything turned out perfect. Everyone that tried one loved it. I will continue to make these every Christmas! 
Highly recommend

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made 1 1/2 the recipe, added 1 cup quality chocolate chips and weighed the dough at 1.2 oz each and got 34 cookies. Also, not sure how it happened, but my batter was super runny and I had to add at least 1/2 cup more flour. No idea what that was about. Finally, I had to slam the baking tray down on the counter several times to get the cookies to collapse and crackle. Turned out absolutely delicious !

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent cookies and easy to make, You’ll do better if you measure by grams! Don’t be fooled by the thin batter. Let it set after you add the flour and Dutch cocoa for a couple of minutes. Batter can be dropped by 1/4 cup spoonfuls on sheets. Do use two cookies sheets because cookies spread out when cooked.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yum!! I did not need to refrigerate the dough as some others did. I made some of the cookies 2T and some even smaller at 1T because I like to eat a higher quantity of smaller cookies vs one large cookie. These would be a delicious alternative to brownies any time of year without the Peppermint. I'm already thinking about different mix-ins and toppings.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I reduced the mint extract to 1 tsp and it was still a bit too minty for my taste. I would def do these again without the mint, maybe adding vanilla as others have recommended, and white chocolate mini chips or toasted pecan bits on top at the end.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't have a stand mixer. Can I use a food processor?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
For folks using gluten free flours, I used Bob's Red Mill One-to-One flour with great success. At first the batter was very runny. I have learned over time to wait at least 10 minutes and any batter made with this flour for it to thicken. Then I put it in the refrigerator overnight. 

I used a medium (1 T.) cookie scoop and did not roll the batter, but place it directly onto the baking sheets lined with parchment paper. The batter spread out nicely and looked just like the ones in the picture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not sure exactly what happened, measurements were double checked as correct but the batter didn’t come anywhere near forming balls. They went on the pan almost like puddles. Reduced the timing slightly and definitely didn’t need banging. Tasted good but was odd to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Half almond, half coconut flavors over peppermint.  Agreed on refrigeration and a much smaller scoop.  Also do not think they'll hold well four days, so keen to know if anyone's tried.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My dream cookie! Very minty but I'm way into that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these twice, great recipe. Didn’t have peppermint extract, so I used 1 tsp vanilla. Other mods: swapped 10 g white sugar for 10g molasses, swapped 20g semisweet for bittersweet. I added some cinnamon to the cocoa powder for depth. Using my smaller cookie scoop I can fit 2 pans with about 8 generous scoops each. (Cook for 9 mins, bang, then 2)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Couldn’t get the crackly tops but the flavor was still good, maybe I need to beat them on the counter harder next time. 

Also did smaller scoops for more cookies. Much better that way, the size of the cookies on the original recipe were way too big.

They are very rich so I can’t eat more than one at a time but the flavor is definitely delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I doubled the recipe intending to save one batch for later. The first batch spread and flattened so much on the wax paper that I couldn’t save it. I let the second batch refrigerate for a couple hours, and then let a third batch refrigerate overnight. The third batch was the only “cookie” like batch out of the bunch! So I highly recommend refrigerating dough overnight before baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Recommend putting these into the oven relatively quickly.
