I doubled my batch and noticed that my last sets of cookies (once the batter really hydrated), were thicker and lost the glossy crackly texture

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Instead of candy canes to top I used Hershey Candy Cane kisses broken up which is a good option!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Turned out amazing!! So festive. First batch flattened a bit more than I wanted so I added about a tbsp of flour to remaining batter. Don't own a mixer, just used a whisk and elbow grease and they turned out great. Huge hit with my family, will definitely make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these cookies and they are delicious!  Only thing I did different, was chill the cookie dough before baking and they were perfect!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these but as I am not a huge fan of peppermint I created a Mexican Hot Chocolate version using an Ibarra Mexican hot chocolate disc and plain cocoa chips instead of the semi sweet chocolate.  I also added about a 1/4 to a 1/3 more flour and used vanilla extract.  They turned out amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Interesting! What are plain cocoa chips?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you are looking for a recipe that adapts well to gluten-free, this is perfect!  I made it using Cup4Cup flour, and because of the low flour content and the wonderful strong flavors of chocolate and peppermint, none of my tasters (a basement full of college freshmen) guessed they were GF, and all raved (justifiably) about the flavor and texture. I definitely second the advice to chill the dough and use a smaller scoop - mine was tablespoon sized. Reduce the bake time accordingly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these. They did not spread and remained “balls”.  I thought I should have flattened them with a spatula before baking. Did anyone else have this problem?  The refrigeration probably helps.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Outstanding! This might be the best cookie of all time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I placed the candy canes in a folded “envelope” of parchment paper and whacked them with a meat tenderizer, worked well to break them up. 

I portioned the cookies with a #40 cookie scoop and baked them for 10 minutes. This made 3” wide cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these twice in the past two weeks. Accidentally over-mixed the second batch pretty well (was distracted by a movie), and still turned out well. I used some small, some large peppermint candies and they melted when they went back in and then recooled--I liked that effect. Instead of banging the pan to get them to deflate a bit, I just used the back of a spoon to flatten them a bit before putting the peppermint candies on--they weren't budging much otherwise. They also didn't spread at all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fabulous! The texture is amazing. They came out great. I think If I made them again, I might use a teaspoon of peppermint extract instead of 1.5... it's quite intense. Also agree that it makes like 22 normal sized cookies, no need to make them 1/4 cup each. That seems really huge!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is definitely a winner! I was faithful to the recipe and made as directed, with the exception of making them smaller. My philosophy is to make Christmas cookies on the smaller side so everyone try at least one of everything before they have a sugar stroke. I ended up with 26 reasonable-sized cookies. They are the perfect balance of chocolate and peppermint, and the texture is perfect. I'm going to need to make another batch!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm excited to try translating the base of this recipe into some variations, which I think would be really easy. I'm going to try swapping the peppermint extract for vanilla and perhaps top with some chopped mini peanut butter cups, yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Followed as written. Banging on the counter did not have the intended effect so I just gently pushed & sprinkled the peppermint on top. Made 21 in total using a slight under measurement of the small ice cream scoop.
These will be a regular addition to our holiday cookie line up! Very yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Also put the candy cane on later, like with 30 seconds left of baking instead of 2 mins left. They melt too much

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I must have been too overzealous banging the baking sheet, because these turned into little pancakes. A good tip for smashing the peppermint candies is to hammer them while they’re still in their wrappers. That way, candy doesn’t fly everywhere. I doubled the peppermint candies. Also, contrary to a previous comment, I think the smaller candy shards/dust actually add a nicer, almost filigree look to the final product.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are outstanding. We've made two batches so far this season and can't leave them alone. We followed the recipe quite precisely but used a smaller scoop the second time as they are indeed large, as another commenter mentioned. They didn't spread that much for us; not sure why. But so so good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As has been said, 1/4 cup scoop makes HUGE cookies, at least 4” round. Also, if you let your kids actually crush the candy canes with a rolling pin, they melt into an indistinguishable but festive puddle. If you want them to look like the photo, use a tablespoon to scoop the dough, small pieces of candy canes, raise the pre-cane time to 11 minutes and reduce the post-cane time to 1 minute. But our giant goop-covered cookies do taste amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Like some others, I added a teaspoon of espresso powder to the chocolate mixture. On top of that I also added a teaspoon of vanilla after I added the chocolate to the flower. Don’t stint on the chocolate! Use the best quality stuff you can find.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I substitute light brown sugar for dark in this recipe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Superb!  Really delicious! I made these exactly as written (with the exception of making 20 smaller cookies, but cooked at the same temp and suggested times). I used Brach’s starlight mints and I crushed about 16 (I had a few pieces leftover).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Like many others shared, this recipe calls for way too much extract. It really ruined the catch. I had multiple people try it and they all agreed. Disappointing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies satisfied my peppermint and chocolate craving in the most chewy, delicious way.  Made the recipe almost exactly as written just added 1 teaspoon of powered espresso per another baker's suggestion.  These cookies will have a prime spot in my holiday cookie repertoire!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used semisweet chocolate chips in lieu of bar chocolate. Worked great. These are delicious. I love what happens when you bang the cookies on the counter!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great - but got quite dark on the bottom.  Perhaps should move rack to upper third of oven instead of center rack.  Also please put divided in ingredients list.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing, absolutely amazing. I used a heaping tablespoon, and made 33 cookies. Reduced the first cooking time by 1 minute, then only cooked 1 minute after whacking and adding peppermint. For my permanent Christmas rotation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Put mixture in freezer before scooping mixture out onto sheets. 1/4 cup scoop is too big! Use mallet portion for 18-24 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today and they’re delicious! I stuck the batter/dough in the fridge, then scooped it with a cookie scoop. They are regular cookie size and didn’t spread much. However, I probably should’ve baked them a couple extra minutes because they started cold. They’re pretty fudge-y in the middle. Tasty though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are AMAZING! We ended up making nine chonky sized cookies, had to bake them just a little extra. The peppermint and chocolate flavors are just perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used Glutino flour. Made cookies smaller. Cooked 9 minutes and then 1.5 minutes. Refrigerated dough 15 min. before putting on cookie sheet. Do try it later with vanilla and nuts instead of peppermint. Great texture and chocolate richness. Heavy on mint. Tastes like an Andes mint but with brownie texture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those using a kitchen scale, I weighed mine out at 55 grams

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: yum. use no more than 1 tsp mint. small cookie scoop - spread a lot - 9 min, whack or smush w spoon, sprinkle a little candy, 2 more min. 30 grams candy canes was plenty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Like many others, I made 24 cookies, not 12. But delicious. . .although I probably should have baked them another minute or two. I took them out sooner since they were half sized.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
1) Refrigerated the dough about 10 minutes before rolling them. 
2) Didn’t have peppermint, so used regular vanilla and crushed pecans. 
3) Slamming the cookies after baking them the first time, didn’t do much, I just pressed in the toppings to make it look “crinkled”, which worked out fine too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Skipped dividing cocoa-just added all at once with chopped chocolate and melted butter. Used rounded teaspoon. Easy to roll into ball without prior refrigeration. Did not spread when baking. Very tender -melt in your mouth cookie. Seriously successful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How many eggs are needed?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed exactly but peppermint oil was way too strong.  Made a second time, no peppermint add vanilla bean paste and 1/2 tsp of instant espresso powder to melted chocolate.  Came out amazing.  While cooking on a wire rack dust with powders sugar.  Instant party hit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very forgiving recipe. I used chips instead of chopped chocolate, because that's what I had, accidentally used all the cocoa powder at the step with the chocolate instead of saving 1/4 cup for later, subbed light brown sugar for dark brown, had to whisk by hand because I don't have a mixer, and my oven is not great. They were still delicious. Chewy in the center and crispy at the edges with nice flavor.Next time I will try and follow the recipe more closely.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Either my peppermint extract purchased at Whole Foods is more concentrated than the extract used in this recipe or my family really dislikes peppermint extract,  We ended up tossing the whole batch and making the cookies again with no extract.  The peppermint from the candy canes was more than enough peppermint flavor to compliment the chocolate.  Throwing the whole batch of cookies in the trash can was a scarring experience. I doubt I will make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe and used a #24 food scoop which is a little less than 1/4 cup. I ended up with 14 instead of 12 cookies. The spread was perfect - not too much. Next time I will replace the kosher salt with a finer variety as I don't really like getting a strong hit of salt when I bite into a crystal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wonder what I did to get the bottoms to burn?  They were still tasty - phew. But could be better. Any tips?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these, and they are amazing!!  Made as directed, but I did not have peppermint extract, I only had oil, which is much stronger.  I added 5 drops, and it gave a nice subtle mint taste to the chocolate.  Used a small cookie scoop and got about 30 cookies.  I will definitely make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made them twice. I cut the peppermint extract by 1/2 tsp the second time I made them, which was MUCH better, less of a fake peppermint taste. Make them. Super easy and delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1 tsp peppermint extract, 1 tsp espresso powder. Refrigerated for 1.5-2 hrs. Used 2 tbsp sized scoop, got 24 cookies. Slamming the cookie sheets produced no effect. Rave reviews from everyone at Christmas. Next time use slightly less salt (personal preference).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a nice novelty cookie for the holidays, but the peppermint was way too overpowering to really enjoy the cookie. Would cut it back to just .5, or 1 tsp and throw in some instant espresso.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are a winner! As others have said- don't over bake them and make sure to slap down that pan. I did what another person suggested and made smaller cookies with the same bake time. It still produced a cookie with the brownie lovers' obligatory crunchy chewy edges, luxuriously fudgie middle, and the sparkly peppermint crunch was a great surprise which complimented to luscious chocolate. I've already made these more than once this holiday season and plan to spread the joy around

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The “cracking” instruction did not make sense - felt like cookies would go flying off the tray if we banged too hard

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used mint instead of peppermint extract, but these were still a huge hit. Made them at about 25g instead of 2 oz. Refrigerated the dough for more than a day, and they still got really sticky to roll. Will definitely use a cookie scoop next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Drop these in about the same size as other cookies you do…….using a soup spoon.  Candy cane was too sticky——maybe sub the red and white sprinkles for color.  Made about 30.  Similar to crinkle cut cooks with powdered sugar

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A keeper! I'm not nuts about peppermint, so I left that out and added a bit more salt. SO good. Like many bakers here, I made mine considerably smaller (about 15g), and baked them for ~7 minutes/sheet. One thing I'm curious about: the two test cookies I baked (again, ~15g) had shiny, crackly tops, but none of the subsequent ones did. The batter did set up as it worked, so I tried gently warming it to a thinner consistency, but that had no effect on the tops. Thoughts? They are still delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My husband termed these cookies the "sleeper hit" of the season. They were good, but mine spread a lot, even with chilling the dough. Otherwise, great flavor, and I like the crunch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As others have noted, I found that pre-bake refrigerator time is a necessity to firm up the "dough"

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a keeper. I made a few batches and will be making them in lieu of brownies, all year long. I swapped vanilla for the peppermint extract and found that the cookies turned out better when I mixed all of the cocoa powder in with the chocolate and butter, and also when I chilled the dough for 10-15 min after scooping. Maldon is a great topping if you don’t want to use the crushed peppermints.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: LOVE this recipe!  I did make smaller cookies than the recipe suggested, but otherwise, followed the recipe to the T.  These are delicious!  It's a great way to use up candy canes!  :)  I also loved that this is fairly easy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made 19 cookies (1/8 cup each) on New Year's Day, cooked for 10 min then 3-4 min with the candy cane. 

Also included 1 tbsp coffee grounds (in lieu of 1 tbsp flour). 

A bit too pepperminty. Might reduce extract. Or next time, use vanilla instead and add choc chips and nuts instead of candy cane. Still, a nice chocolatey holiday cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I add a sprinkle of cinnamon into the batter with the last cocoa and flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Don’t need a lot of peppermints. Chill, use medium cookie scoop to make 24 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Did anyone think these were too salty?  My husband likes them. Texture is good. Good peppermint flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn't have an issue with the salt in this recipe, but I have had a problem in the past using regular table salt like Morton which tends to be much saltier by volume. I now never bake with Morton salt, or will reduce significantly if it's all I have on hand! Hope that's helpful for you & check out this article explaining why (https://www.tasteofhome.com/article/types-of-kosher-salt/).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used Nielsen Massey peppermint extra and reduced it to 2tsp. I am glad I did because the peppermint taste was still plenty strong. 
Let the batter sit for 10 minutes of so before you scoop it out. It will thicken up and be easier to handle.
This won’t make as many cookies as it says if you use 1/4 cup scoops. We doubled the recipe and got 18 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these today; chilled the dough for 15 minutes. Used a smaller size scoop and cookie spread like picture. However, must have needed to bake longer as they were very soft - not cookie like or brownie like. Still had good flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a fun addition to my Christmas cookies! Thanks to others’ tips, I made them smaller with my regular cookie scoop and was happy with that decision. I also found the peppermint extract to be too strong. I intend to make these again but will reduce or omit the extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this last week.  I concur with refrigerating the dough; using a coffee scoop-size rather than a quarter cup; chunkier peppermint pieces.  My major comment is that two people (not me) consumed all of the cookies in four hours!  They're that tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a huge hit at Christmas, made in two ways: I made the original following the recipe, but for my grandson who does not like peppermint, I substituted raspberry extract for peppermint extract and skipped the peppermint topping. Surprisingly, the raspberry was even more popular than the peppermint!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Circumstances dictated that the dough had to be chilled for couple hours. Was difficult to scoop but scoop I did with a 2T sized scoop. Made 22 cookies.  Nothing happened when I banged the pan. My Dorshur’s candycane bits melted! Still delish so I’ll make again sometime according to directions and see what’s different.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When we made these, we omitted the peppermint bits, and instead added a few scoops of peanut butter to the egg/sugar mixture after it had been whipped. This made for a really nice chocolate peanut butter cookie, though the nut flavor was subtle and we probably could have added more peanut butter. We also omitted the last bit of cocoa power that you’re instructed to add, because we felt the cookies were rich enough already.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies really are all that. If you don’t like peppermint, just use some vanilla and/or expresso powder. If you do like peppermint, this recipe will benefit from a few drops of peppermint oil (as used in candy-making) instead of peppermint extract. It’s strong, so be careful, but it provides a much richer peppermint taste.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Used salted butter, cut salt in half
Used 1.5 tsp finely ground espresso powder, and didn’t use peppermint extract 
Recommend using 4 candy canes and go generous on the candy canes so each bite has it
Half cookies no candy cane (topped those with leftover espresso powder)
Really enjoyed!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Pretty on a holiday cookie plate. Makes 40 actual cookie sized cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful! Thanks to the community. Only a 1/2 tsp of pep ext. Abt a tsp of esp pdr. Cooled abt 30 min. then bake. They don’t spread all that much. Took out at 8-9 min then 3 more min. Makes 24. So go ahead and double why doncha?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In place of scoops, which tend to make bigger cookies, use measuring spoons or a melon baller to drop the cookie batter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious. I used a small ice cream scoop and got 30 cookies. I like the idea of substituting peanut butter, espresso, vanilla, etc as not everyone is fond of peppermint. They are a pretty cookie to set out. 

I baked for 8 mins, removed from oven and added crushed peppermint, baked additional 2 mins. Fantastic!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe.  Refrigerated the batter before setting up the cookies.   Made them smaller - 20 g.  Same cooking time.  Made 36 cookies.   Delicious 😋

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't love this cookie recipe. It came in middle of the pack in my Christmas cookie swap. I made 24 1 oz cookies. I was expecting it to be a little more cake-like and more sturdy but they were very fudgey. I don't think they would have much structural integrity if you made them as the recipe describes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These tasted even better on Day 2!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We made 24 cookies instead of 12, and they were the perfect size considering these are quite rich (and very delicious).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These cookies are delicious - a perfect blend between a cake-like cookie and a brownie!!  
The cookies need to stay in the oven about 15 minutes longer than the recipe suggests - at least in my oven, but it is just a few years old and usually holds the temperature quite well.  That is why I suspect that the cooking time in the recipe is insufficient.
I'm making 2 more batches - they have been such a hit!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We did not like these at all. They were super pretty and looked just like the picture, but reviews from family members ranged from “tastes like toothpaste” to “tastes like a mediocre brownie”.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent! The cookies taste like they are much more difficult to make than they actually are. Excited to bring to my co-workers tomorrow!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Not sure what I did wrong, but these turned out gooey in the middle, and almost burned on the bottom. Maybe a higher rack in the oven? Lower heat? Otherwise I followed the recipe to the letter. 
I will try refrigerating as well, and smaller scoops. They are big but not enormous.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It's a ton of work for incredibly flat, greasy cookies. I love brownies, and I love mint chocolate, so I thought I'd give it a try, but they turned out very flat, and the middles were incredibly soft and greasy despite whipping them in the stand mixer and putting them in the freezer for 10 minutes right before I baked them. Next time I'll just add peppermint extract and candy canes to the top of a pan of brownies and save myself all the extra effort for these cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the first sheet using a 1/4 cup scoop. Ridiculous amount of spread and they would not set up in the center. Switched to a 1T scoop, cooked for 12 minutes and they are delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved these cookies. Would HIGHLY suggest using a 1/8c scoop opposed to the 1/4c. The 1/4 was way too big

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe the best cookie recipe I've ever made. I wasn't able to find peppermint extract,m so I used some almond and vanilla extract instead (peppermint definitely would've been better though). I also got 24 cookies out of the dough, and I used a heaping tablespoon to parcel them out. I think they only needed 90 seconds to get the peppermint to adhere; 2 minutes and it was melting into one solid mass.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These spread so much! Next time, refrigerate for at least 2 hours to let them firm up first.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are crazy good, I made them last year for Christmas and everyone asked for them again. I chilled the dough the night before and kept them in the oven 22 minutes. I substituted peppermint extract for vanilla because my family isn’t crazy about mint. They are so chocolatey and soft and delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cocoa weighs almost twice what the recipe says it should.  Sugar is off as well.  Very odd.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Wow if you like brownie edges, you’ll love these cookies. I made them with vanilla extract instead of peppermint extract as some suggested because not everyone likes peppermint. Then I put the cracked peppermint on the top of half the cookies. They were fabulous. The only issue I had was that my peppermint chips melted even after only two minutes. I’m not sure why, so I had melted peppermint drops on the top instead of sharp chips.

But they are delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really love these cookies. I made them last year and I have been looking forward to making them again all year! As others have written, I made 24 cookies with an 1/8 scoop and while some were baking I put the other trays in the fridge. Added the candy cane as written but still cousins get the spread even though I really slammed those trays (to the dismay of my 2-year-old). Worth the work!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
An excellent recipe for gluten-free baking. I use Krusteaz and King Arthur gluten free flours, same amounts as recipe. Always let the dough rest refrigerated 18-24 hours. 
Important to let cookies cool on the pan before moving to cooling rack. The centers are still soft, and gf flour just seems to like to rest a bit before moving off the pan. Less crumbly. 
This recipe is delicious for everyone, no “off” taste, so everyone enjoys the cookies and the gf-ers don’t need to feel different.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were so good! But we didn’t want giant cookies, so these changes worked for us: We chilled the dough about 20 minutes, used a regular cookie scoop to make 26 cookies, baked for 9 minutes, then took them out and wacked trays on our kitchen island (very satisfying!), topped with crushed candy, and put them back in oven for two minutes.  The only change we made other than cooking times/portions is to halve the amount of peppermint extract because we didn’t want that taste to be overwhelming.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing these cookies after baking them?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Update: they're in my freezer and I'll add another update when they come out sometime in 2023.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yum

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookie.  Not done after 10+2 minutes!   These were cooked on 12/23/22, the day it was -9° outside, and pretty chilly in my kitchen.  Maybe that was the reason?  We baked them an additional 5 minutes at 375° and they seem to be perfect.  Lovely cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are very good! Will add to my Christmas cookie rotation.  I usually never pay attention to the reviews and just follow the recipe but I did chill the batter for 20 min before portioning and it was much easier to do.  I also made them smaller to play well in the assortment--got about 22 cookies.  Because they're so flat you don't need to reduce the baking time by that much.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These worked out well. Like others, I made them smaller. A heaping tbsp worked out fine. The cookies needed more time in my oven even with smaller cookies (12+2). I didn’t need a whack to get the craggy look and left them domed. They were very sweet. For my second batch, I switched a bit of gold baking sugar for the candy cane which cut down on the sweetness. Rolling the dough into balls was a lot easier after refrigerating for 15 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They taste fine, but the texture is nothing like how it looks in the video. They really spread out while baking and got super thin and crispy. I followed the recipe to a T. Super disappointing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are incredible - my college age kids fought over the last few!  Put these cookies into your rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Love these! I’ve made these cookies a few times and they’re always a big hit. I also make them smaller and use a cookie/ice cream scoop, which works so well that I don’t bother chilling the dough.

I also follow the recipe to t.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Loved this recipe with two revisions.

First revision I changed the flavor & amount of extract to 1 1/2 teaspoons of vanilla extract plus 1/4 teaspoon of peppermint extract. 

Second revision I put the chocolate batter in the refrigerator for 15 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious - made with gluten free flour and worked just fine. Made 24 smaller cookies as others suggested and subbed vanilla for peppermint extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I needed to cook them a hair longer and may have put too many mint sprinkles on top but very tasty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is delicious with a few changes. I took the advice of another note and added a teaspoon of espresso powder to chocolate mixture.  I changed the extract to 1 1/4 teaspoon vanilla and a 1/4 teaspoon peppermint extract.  I also put the mixture in the refrigerator for 15 minutes before I prepared the cookies on the baking sheet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: People go crazy for these cookies at Christmas! Definitely let the batter cool in fridge before making and make 24, not 12. Make 2 batches - they go quick!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these cookies without the peppermint candy and substituted vanilla extract for the peppermint extract.  I didn't have a 1/4 cup cookie scoop so I used a heaping tablespoon instead.
They were the absolute BEST cookies I have ever made or eaten.  Everyone in my family loved them.  They're the perfect combination of crisp on the outside with a gooey brownie batter texture on the inside.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In step 3 - thank you for including the mixing time and appropriate mixer attachment.  I tend to 'under' mix.   Very helpful.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sooooo amazing it’s literally like all the parts of a brownie in a cookie

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How gooey should these be in the center?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are great. That said, if you want 12 cookies, use a 3tbsp cookie scoop. One quarter cup is definitely too much. The cookies spread quite a bit but come out soft and delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the cookies as described and loved. Good for the brownie lover and very soft. Agree with other comments about bigger pieces of peppermint so they don’t melt

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make these cookies gluten free and they’re fabulous.  Instead of using 3/4 cup of flour I use 3/4 cup of almond flour. No one knows they’re gluten free.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Should there be baking powder in this recipe? They seem a little flat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are absolutely amazing! Instead of peppermint candy, I used peppermint marshmallows and that was really good. Definitely recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So Good!!!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious, quick, and easy! I followed the recipe as written and although I am generally not a fan of chocolate/mint, the final product doesn't have a "toothpastey" quality at all. I am pleasantly surprised by how much I enjoy them. The only modification that I made was making 24 cookies instead of 12, which resemble the cover photo very closely, and I used 60% chocolate because that is what I had on hand. I agree that this recipe would be a great blueprint to customize the flavors!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out beautifully, with gorgeous crackly tops. They are super fudgey, like the best brownie you've ever had. I used the Favorite Day peppermint crunch from Target on top because it's softer than regular candy canes and doesn't get stuck in your teeth (not a sponsored endorsement or anything, I just had when that happens with candy canes, but I love the flavor of them) and it worked out great - the only thing was that I had to press them down a bit to get them to stay in the cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these in 2022.  Did NOT like them at all.  Won’t make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A few notes:
-These cookies are delicious!
-This recipe makes up great with gluten-free flour
- Rather than crushing candy canes, I snipped them with a kitchen shears inside a sandwich bag.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! Truly feels like a brownie in cookie form. I reduced the peppermint extract to only 1 tsp instead if 1.5 and it was perfect—*definitely* minty but not overpowering. Would make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This cookie is definitely the lovechild of a brownie. It’s like eating the brownie edges, which is my favorite part.  Downsides are that it’s rather sweet and rich. I can only eat half of one (made tablespoon sized batter balls) before running to my fridge for a tall glass of milk.  Next time, I’d only make a few with the peppermint, as I get tired more easily of it. Finally, my cookies didn’t come out as pretty as the picture, but it never does….

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I'd maybe play around with the amount of peppermint extract next time for a more mild peppermint flavor. I added a little espresso powder and rolled many of the cookies in sugar for a shiny texture on the outside, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I follow the recipe exactly and they always come out fantastic. These are now a family favorite. We make them every Christmas.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are hard to screw up.  I accidentally turned off the oven instead of stopping the timer, so they were only partially baked, took them out, had to wait for the oven to heat up before sticking them back in, and I still thought they were delicious!  These will go into our 
holiday rotation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
When you’re adding the flour it says to add 1/4 cup of cocoa, but it’s not listed as an ingredient. Only the 1/4 cup that gets combined with the baking chocolate.
I added a 1/8 cup with the flour and it made them way more cookie textured

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Refrigerate dough 10  min. Use medium cookie scoop 1.5 T. Sprinkle peppermint before baking for 12 min. Delish.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A very nice cookie indeed. I was nervous about the looseness of the batter but it scooped out ok. I make smaller cookies to yield 24 vs 12 and of course they are thinner but taste wonderful anyway. If I do them again I will make them bigger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love this recipe.  I've made it about 5x since last year and am asked for the recipe over and over again.  Easy and delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great cookies!  based on some comments, I also made about 24 instead of 12 and put them in the fridge for a little while after step 5.  I also remade them for someone who's not a fan of peppermint, and subbed vanilla extract.  The blend of the cocoa with the semisweet chips is really delicious in this cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I subbed 1 for 1 gluten free flour and these turned out perfect! Lovely brownie texture in the centre but hold up we’ll like a cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The instructions should specify to switch from the whisk attachment to the paddle attachment on your stand mixer before adding the flour and 1/4 cup cocoa powder. much of the lovely chocolate batter got wedged into the whisk attachment and was very difficult to scrape out and add back to the bowl.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this recipe, following some of the suggestions, smaller cookies less mint extract, a bit of espresso. They are decent cookies, but I found that in my oven which is generally reliable, they needed at least twice the cooking time the recommended times have flat puddles, more time was needed for the rise

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lovely soft cookie. Will be great as an ice cream sandwich, I think. Used medium cookie scoop (1.5 T). Chilled balls before baking. Baked 10 minutes. Sprinkling after most of the bake did not work for us. Tried sprinkling before baking. Worked fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fine. Mine needed quite a bit more time in the oven than the recipe suggests, so my peppermint candy melted on top by the time the cookies were cooked through. Also, wouldn't 4 oz of semisweet chocolate be 1/2 cup, not 3/4 cup?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious! Easy to make with my 10YO daughter, but also has some complexity to the recipe. The cookies were chewy on the inside, with a slight peppermint crunch on top. A holiday keeper, for sure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out really well!  I love brownies, so it shouldn't be a huge surprise.  I was not able to get the cookies to "crack" on top, but it didn't affect the look or tast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1 tsp of diamond crystal salt would make these unpalatable. I used half a teaspoon (based on other disasters and they were very, very salty. I added a little superfine sugar at the end to counteract. If I make them again I'll start with 1/4 tsp of diamond crystal salt.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: vaughn, you nailed it!  love these - and they are now a go-to.  my only changes were to use a #30 disher for scooping and refrigerate overnight. perfect bites of brownie edge with a chewy center. really really nice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these and they came out really well! I followed some suggestions made here— made 24 instead if 12. Also stuck my batter in the fridge for a little while(15 min) before scooping out and baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Following the suggestions of others, I cut down on peppermint extract and-used 1/2 tsp and 1 tsp vanilla. Added 1 tsp espresso powder. I happened to have peppermint candy bittersweet chocolate bars so used that for 1/2 the chopped chocolate.  I refrigerated the batter overnight. Used a much smaller cookie scoop, but kept the baking times the same.  These were delicious, the texture is wonderful. I made 6 batches over the holidays and everyone loved them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these sans peppermint ingredients. Substituted vanilla extract. Was skeptical about the amount of sugar for so much semisweet chocolate. As someone who deeply dislikes overly sweet desserts, I went only slightly lighter in full measurements of cane and brown sugars, but not my typical third or even half cuts I make for most dessert recipes. All to say, the sugar quantities listed work well in delivering a balanced flavor that is not too sweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you want more of a Christmasy feel add cinnamon and caramel to the batter

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This hybrid Brownie/Cookie recipe isn't better.  Going forward...if i want to make a brownie, I'll make a brownie.  And if i want to make a cookie, I'll make a cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yum. I was apprehensive about making these for some reason but these are delightful. I did a test run and putting the rest in the fridge to make in two days when I need them> I sure hope this works to keep dough in fridge then bake. the amount of peppermint means they are quite peppermint-y, but that's sort of the purpose of a peppermint cookie, no?!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these last night for a cookie swap! Lots of compliments, the texture is very nice! Only change was I did half the amount of peppermint extract and it was still very potent so I’m glad I aired on the conservative side. Best cookie recipe I’ve tried so far!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I couldn’t wait to make these again this year. My tips: chill the dough in the fridge for a few minutes before shaping into balls, make the balls smaller than the recipe suggests, and press the candy cane pieces on after baking (don’t put them back in the oven or they’ll melt).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really delicious and easy, made 24 Roth the exact same recipe nothing changed

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies were very tasty! I ended up making half the size for each cookie (it made double the number and were a bit easier to package in gift bags) though each were a bit on the crumbly size. If you end up creating 1/2 sized cookies, note that the cookies will be a bit fragile.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tripled this recipe because I’m attending two cookie swaps 😅 and you do not need to triple the peppermint extract. 3 teaspoons was plenty. I followed other tips like refrigerating the dough for 10 min after step 5 and using a smaller scoop than 1/4 cup and they turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made to a T and they went completely flat. No clue why. Amazing taste. Wish I knew what I did wrong!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are truly a revelation.  Loved them!!  Agreed that the dough should be chilled a bit prior to cooking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Not at all tricky to make. No need to rest in fridge. Used a rolling pin to crush the candy canes (w/ a paper towel bet the candy and the rolling pin to prevent the shards from flying). Was worried if the candy would stick to the cookie, but it melts on. Delicious and easy. Perfect for the holidays and mint chocolate lovers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious. I made them smaller and got 24 cookies, which was the right size for me given how rich these are. I also cut back on the peppermint extract to 1 teaspoon. It was just right for me.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I made these cookies for the first time yesterday for my work’s holiday cookie exchange party, and they were a hit! They’re chocolatey, soft, slightly chewy, and have the perfect peppermint flavor. Honestly, they’re as good as my favorite cookie from Crumbl!

I did notice I needed to bake them about three minutes longer than the recipe suggested. Next time, I’ll use a little less salt and cocoa powder to tweak the flavor to my liking. The dough was denser than I expected based on the video, but the cookies still turned out perfect and absolutely scrumptious. This is officially my new favorite cookie recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The taste was good. Presentation, not so much.
There are no uses of leavening agents such as baking soda or baking powder whatsoever. When I ended up baking these the first time around, as a test batch, they looked like little crunchy concrete slabs and ended up throwing them out. I would recommend adding baking powder + baking soda to give them a little volume, or refrigerating them. The taste was good, however, the presentation was lacking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't think a brownie recipe ever uses a leavening agent.  These are supposed to replicate the dense, moist texture of brownies, but the cookies are obviously thinner than brownies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sadly following the recipe does not provide you with cookies that look anything like the image. They spread way too much and didn’t crackle. In hindsight I should have used half the batter per cookie and refrigerated the dough but I just blindly trusted the recipe. They are still yummy but way too big and thin and missing the brownie crackle :(

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I hear “peppermint” chocolate and I thought  Andes mints would be excellent as the chocolate base. And yes it smells wonderful! I tried a bit of batter and it was yum! 
BUT my cookie scoops spread SO MUCH it basically filled my baking sheet. Making one big blobby cookie. Or a very thin and regrettable looking brownie. 
Please save the Andes mints for a chocolate chip cookie.  Don’t use it in this recipe. Use actual chocolate. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
- Refrigerate cookie batter for at least 15 minutes before baking
- Do smaller scoops than 1/4 cup (something more similar to regular chocolate chip cookies)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were delicious, but no matter how long I ran my mixer to beat the eggs and sugar, I never quite achieved the amount of fluffiness shown in the video, and my cookies came out very flat. Could it be because I doubled the recipe? Would you suggest adding a leavening agent when that happens to give the cookies more body?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found these intensely sweet and overpoweringly minty as written. I repeated the recipe reducing the granulated sugar to 1/2 cup and using unsweetened chocolate. 1 tsp of peppermint extract was plenty. I made 1 oz balls and rolled them in Christmas sprinkles. They were perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as written, but chilled the dough overnight then let it sit at room temp for a bit before making 1 oz (half recipe size) balls. They didn't spread excessively. No luck getting them to crack at 10 minutes though, and almost lost cookies trying! :) It didn't matter, as these wound up the perfect size, flavor and texture for me. Excellent cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made as directed, but a bit smaller and 1/2 as much peppermint extract. They were excellent! 
Used room temp eggs. Did not need to refrigerate dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used coconut flour and added a little instant coffee. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were requested on repeat over the holidays. So yummy! Took to a cookie exchange and made for the fam.  Again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Watched the video and followed recipe (except for only 1 tsp peppermint extract and chilled 10 mins before baking). Made18 cookies. Sadly they didn't crack when banged and at end, while pretty, the cookies were flat as pancakes.
Anyone have tips on baking the cookies at high altitude? We are at 7,200 ft and wondering if that is why our cookies didn't look like those in recipe/video.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Reading comments on peppermint extract amount--maybe brand differences in strength? (McCormick vs.Nielsen Massey vs. Flavorganics....)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies for Christmas and my husband rates them as the best cookies that i have made. 2 weeks past Christmas and we are still enjoying them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good grief! Made these for a cookie exchange… the most popular cookie there! Guess they will be in my rotation for next year too! Highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s not the holidays, but was craving chocolate peppermint. Great recipe. I wanted to make 24 smaller ones, but used the wrong scoop. Next time will use the 40. I got 14 cookies with the 30. I used pre-crushed peppermint candy and next time I’ll crush my own in larger pieces because it did start to melt. Forgot to whack the trays but they crackled anyway. Might put these on the holiday cookie list. Thanks for a great recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is just perfect as is!  Wouldn’t change a thing.  My family begs me to make them every holiday season.  I’ve even sold a few.  People just adore them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delish. Definitely fudgy. Added a bit of extra cocoa powder in the butter stage (this was accidental but it turned out well, so..). Also, added a splash of vanilla extract. The cookies aren’t huge; they resemble the photographed cookies in size.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This didn't work for me. I made it to almost to the letter (subbed in 1/2 tsp of vanilla for 1/2 of the peppermint) but I got no spread and no crackling-brownie look on top. They kept the same shape as when they were placed on the pan. They really don't look good. Also, the peppermint didn't stick when I put them back in. Saving grace: they taste good! But quite disappointed. Any tips? Will try again bc it was an easy recipe to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: All stores were out of peppermint extract, so I substituted the chocolate by Andes mint chocolate chips and it worked for the peppermint flavor. The batter was very runny, maybe from the different fat and sugar content form the mint candy vs chocolate but still very good cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a hit at my birthday celebration and run club! I found that using a mug to smash the peppermint sticks (I got the tiny ones) works really well! Also I hope your eye is doing better, Vaughn! I had some something recently, not fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe.  Nice and rich.  Definitely make cookies smaller than in recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does this recipe work with hard or soft peppermint candy?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They are a lot of work, but they are good. Used the suggestion to put them in the fridge, and they are a lot easier to form and put on a cookie sheet. Also made them a little smaller, and they were the perfect Christmas cookie size. Didn't have espresso powder, so added 1/4 teas. almond extract.which always brings out the chocolate flavor in my brownies. and 1 teas. peppermint.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Admittedly, I love the peppermint-chocolate combo at baseline (Thin Mints 4ever!) but these are fantastic. I used a small melon baller (~2 tbsp) instead of 1/4 cup scoops and added 1/2 tsp espresso powder to the chocolate mixture (since I had light brown sugar and wanted some more depth), but otherwise made faithfully. It's very satisfying to whack the cookies on the counter and see the crags appear. So good, will be in the rotation from now on at the holidays!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was a bit apprehensive after reading so many reviews about crazy spreading, but I didn't have that problem at all! I know Vaughn recommends good quality chocolate but I did use chipits and they turned out great. I made some adjustments, since I wanted extra chocolate gooey-ness I threw in a handful of chocolate chips with the flour, I also used a teaspoon of peppermint compared to a teaspoon and a half and found it was the perfect amount. My biggest tip: work fast! My first batch was the best!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Anyone else have trouble with the peppermint candies melting when you put the cookies back in the oven?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sheryll yes… shortening the cooking time helped a bit, and the still taste good!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes, I just bake the cookies for 12 minutes and put the candy canes on after they come out. (Although they don’t stick to the cookie very well, so shortening the time like Kas suggested is probably better)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Recipe says stay OK for 4 days in an airtight container. I want to make and ship these. Thunk they’ll be safely edible a week or so out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Please flag in the ingredient list when ingredients are going to be divided, as the cocoa powder is here.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these yesterday as a practice run for a party I'm going to next weekend...and they're a winner!  I made them smaller and didn't get quite as much crackle on top but still very delicious, and they look festive too.  Question - is it possible to double the recipe and get the same results?  Or should I make two separate batches?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: hate lol. they are the hardest cookies ever to work with. was shocked that their wasnt baking soda or powder in the recipe, and the outcome really shows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made 3x the recipe and turned out great. Very big cookies, even trying to do less than 1/4 cup.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made a batch of these, but made them about 1/3 the called for. Using them as part of holiday cookie assortment for friends & neighbors. They should fit nicely in a smaller cookie box, along with the Lemon Ricotta (w/fresh lemon glaze) I made today. Tip: If you're running out of counter space (and you have an attached garage), cool them slightly on the baking sheet & bring them to the garage to cool a bit; then. Glaze lightly and let them finish cooling in the garage.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I followed others advice, adding baking soda, 1/2 teaspoon baking powder 1/4 teaspoon.  Batter was very sticky and nearly impossible to form into cookies. They did taste good. 
Second batch, I made into a big pan cookie to cut up because if it was just too labor-intensive trying to form round cookies    I did kill the dough thanks for the others advice   I’ll probably make these again cause they’re really good although a lot of work  chewy and brownie like

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious. 
I live at a higher elevation,  so I added a third egg and baked 2 min less. They are perfection!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the recipe twice. The first time, I followed the recipe exactly. It was too much peppermint extract for me. The second time, I decreased the peppermint to 1 tsp and added 1/2 tsp of vanilla. Now it is a mellow chocolate, with the peppermint in the background rather than dominant. I like it much better. It is a festive Christmas cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good, great addition for the holiday

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are delish, but when I make them again I will:

Chill the dough as others suggested - probably overnight 
Make dough into smaller balls for baking
I got a bit lazy with crushing up my candy canes and the larger candy cane chunks are weird, I want smaller pieces next time and more than just a sprinkling (I like peppermint unlike many commenters it seems!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Chilled dough overnight. Made smaller half-sized cookies (1 oz each). Baked 6 minutes then added candy cane pieces to finish baking for 2-3 minutes. Found that the cookies were mostly flat, but craggly.  Got better domed shape by slamming the tray just after the end of baking. Good chocolate flavor but I'd also recommend adding some espresso powder. I like the amount of peppermint flavor with this strong chocolate flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Too much peppermint!  Will reduce significantly next time.  Also, my cookies were undercooked after the 10 min. + 2 min. Indicated in the recipe.  I used a conventional oven - should I have used the convection oven?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with a few small adjustments (as an aside, I copied the recipe and comments into ChatGPT and asked for the top five suggestions). I made the cookies smaller, reduced the peppermint slightly, added ½ teaspoon of vanilla, and chilled the dough so it was easy to scoop and shape. I baked them for 8 minutes, added crushed candy cane, then returned them to the oven for one more minute. They turned out perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I added a teaspoon of espresso powder, used a one-tablespoon cookie scoop and refrigerated the dough while the oven heated up.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious! Crunchy bite into a rich, chewy, chocolate center with nice peppermint flavor.
Just for reference, you get twice as many decent sized cookies (24 vs 12) by using a heaping tablespoon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made these before but never wrote any notes to myself. I agree that 1/4 C dough is too big a cookie, especially one as rich as these.  2025, I used about half that much dough and got 21 cookies. I wish Mr. Vreeland would revisit this and experiment with whacking and baking times using a more modest amount of batter. I tried 6 plus 3, 8 plus 2 minutes but don’t know the desired texture to determine what is best. We will definitely eat these and enjoy them, even if possibly underbaked.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them following people's recs- making 24 instead of 12, chililng the dough etc. Contrary to what everyone said, mine didn't spread at all, so I flattened them before making and used a spoon to squish them down and crack them at the 10 min mark. I also only put the crushed candy cane in for about 30-40 seconds as they were starting to melt on the cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Put plastic wrap over batter while waiting because it starts to stiffen and you lose the silky texture. Otherwise perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good, intense chocolate and good peppermint too, fairly easy to make. I agree with the comment about the 1/4 scoop size, I did more like rounded teaspoon and even then needed to bake for 9 minutes, then sprinkle with peppermint, then bake another two minutes. My batter wasn't runny. I would make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made the recipe as is with the addition of a scant teaspoon of instant coffee with the chocolate, per the recommendation of other comments. I also made the cookies smaller but kept the bake time the same. These are 5-star cookies: chewy, deeply chocolatey, and just the right amount of peppermint. Thanks, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I noticed York peppermint pattie brand chocolate chips in the baking aisle recently and tried these with them - replaced same weight of chopped chocolate with the York chips and eliminated the peppermint oil. They were amazing and multiple family members wanted the recipe. I’m still thinking about them a week later :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was worried about my children rejecting the cookies completely, so I left out the peppermint extract, added some chocolate chunks, and baked half of the batter that way. To the remaining half of the batter, I added some chopped peppermint Andes candies. My heathen offspring told me the plain cookies were better. They don’t know what they’re missing. I loved the addition of the peppermint candies and will probably make them again this way.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used gluten free flour and subbed vanilla for the peppermint oil as some commenters suggested. The cookies were delicious, both fudgy and crackly. The crushed candy cane added texture and color to the otherwise all-chocolate cookie plate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A great cookie!  We cut the mint extract to 1 tsp and it was just the right amount of mint.  Also made smaller cookies (with little scoop).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Subbed 1:1 GF flour and made the cookies much smaller (1.5” cookie scoop). HUGE Christmas hit, and will be added as a holiday tradition.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Husband of 37 years “these are the best cookies you have ever made”. 

I made some of the recommended changes - equal parts vanilla and peppermint extract, espresso powder, 1/3 unsweetened chocolate. I also reduced the sugar by a quarter cup (husband is diabetic and I wanted to reduce the risk of a sugar spike - No spike ! Yay! Because we were making pizza for dinner, I chilled the dough for about 90 minutes and used large spoons from my flatware to scoop out the dough. 

These cookies are awesome and I plan to swap the toppings depending on the season!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a HIT! will def make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
10/10 for inspiration.

Even people in the family who don’t love mint chocolate loved these. 

Couldn’t be easier, unless I cheated with brownie mix - oops, I made it easier and it was perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I haven’t made these yet but I did make the other version of this cookie and it was delicious! Looking forward to trying these ones out!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are too sweet for me, but they have a great texture. Next time o will reduce the sugar a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies didn't turn out for me. They spread out across the entire tray in a goopy/flaky dry mess. Had to scrap them. This is the first time a NYT recipe had to be thrown out. Really bummed considering they smell delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm so excited to make these! Does any1 think that they would be good to pack in kids lunchboxes? Obviously I would only pack 1, but they could be a nice sweet treat for the kids long day at school!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These bars taste so good, and were a big hit with neighbors.  Adjustments: omitted the 1 t salt from the crust; added 1.5 t of pectin to the filling for firmness (worked perfectly); added zest on top of the bars, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No need for a food processor or zip lock bag to grind the saltines.  I crushed them inside the sleeve the crackers come in--first with my fingers, and then with a heavy pestle to press further.  The bag held up nicely until they were fully crushed, and I had fewer dishes to wash :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These boozy bars are unique and a crowd pleaser. They are definitely tequila-forward, very true to the flavor of a Cadillac margarita. While delicious and easy to prepare, they unfortunately get very soft at room temperature (68°F in air conditioned home) and best kept in the freezer or in the fridge.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I brought these to a party and they were a huge hit! I made the recipe exactly as written, sprinkling the tops with more lime zest and Maldon salt. The saltine crust is *everything.* The filling is pretty soft so keeping them frozen works best, but they did okay sitting out on the table for an hour also.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Happy to report that if all you’ve got is an 8x8 pan, you can still make these AND they will still be delicious. We baked them a few minutes longer to accommodate the difference in pan size, and they turned out just fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: WOW! Delicious, taste just like a margarita! I took someone’s advice and mixed the flaky salt with lime zest for the topping and that was a hit. Made them in cupcake tins-baking time for the crust was the same and a few minutes less for the curd. Easy and will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a double batch of this - one with alcohol one without for my kids. The one with alcohol I used Cointreau since I had it and that worked well. They’re delicious and you can adjust the amount of salt you want on each bar. They didn’t taste as boozy as some described but you can taste the tequila just like a margarita. For the non-alcoholic version I replaced the alcohol with orange juice and that worked just fine. Both versions are best coming from the freezer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does cooking these bars cook out the alcohol?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Lissy since it doesn't boil there may be some left.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We make these but with a pretzel and butter crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To make them dairy free, substitute sweetened condensed coconut milk.  A good health food store should carry it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: And then you can have the lime with the coconut... sounds like a good combo!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've made these for years but use pretzels instead of saltines, it's divine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @KT B is it the same recipe just switching crushed pretzels for the saltines?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were delicious, even with the cheap tequila. More cookie recipes I’m not allowed to share with my kid, please.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Took the advice of other commenters and fully zested all 4 limes and used the rest to sprinkle on top with the flaky salt. This is very salt forward so if that's an issue for you it's probably not going to be a dessert you like. Use your good tequila as the flavor will come through. This is not the time to steal your college kid's rotgut tequila.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For those who don’t have the booze on hand…I made these with mini bottles of silver Patrón and Grand Marnier that I found near the register at my liquor store. All the flavor of a top-shelf margarita for a few bucks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Atlantic Beach pie is no doubt the inspiration for these bars. There is no no booze in it, so it’s better for the kids. Plus, the idea is you will have everything you need in the beach house, and the kids can make dessert!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Double your pleasure and mix up two batches of this delish wow. Keep the leftovers (as if) in the freeze and grab slab as you please. Morning, noon, or night, these are a delight.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My friends - please do not learn this the hard way as I did: condensed oat milk will NOT work for this. I saw comments that said condensed coconut milk worked, but oat milk is all my grocery store had for non-dairy options. Complete failure - turned out like quiche. I hope to save someone the heartache!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone morphed these into Sugared Mojito Bars? I'm going to give it a shot, substituting rum for tequila, Creme de Menthe for Grand Marnier, and a sprinkle of sugar for the flaky salt. I'll let you know.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Dan what’s the verdict on the mojito version?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Straightforward and quick enough to make for a summer gathering.  Delight your friends with these sweet, salty, surprising bars and then resent them when they go back for seconds, ruining your plan of hoarding the rest for yourself.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh yes! It's always a little scary taking a new recipe to a party the first time making it, but took these to a dinner party & after the first bite everyone said Please Deep Making This! Didn't have a 9x9" pan so used a 10" round tart pan (~same area) and cut it into pie wedges. That's how I'll do it in the future. As suggested, just before serving, sprinkled w/ Malden salt & lime zest.  I was also generous with the zest in the filling. We thought it would serve 10. Thanks, it was perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I bloomed a tsp of gelatine in about ⅛ cup cold water and then dissolved over low heat just before adding to the tequila egg yolk mixture. I immediately poured it into the crust and baked for 16 minutes. The bars came out perfectly and held their shape even when sitting out for over an hour. They were super delicious as well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For the question on whether alcohol cooks out in cooked foods...no it doesn't...It may cook out a percentage of the booze but it's not all gone.....I am allergic to alcohol and never, ever eat foods cooked with alcohol...https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooking_with_alcohol#Alcohol_in_finished_food

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these for a patio dinner party and they are indeed fab. The flaky salt really turns them up a notch - don’t be shy, the flavours of the curd can handle a generous scattering!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made per recipe using salted saltines. I think they’d be better omitting the added salt in the crust; or using unsalted crackers with the tsp of salt. In any case my guests loved them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fun to make .. requires a sophisticated pallet;)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was curious about this recipe but ultimately I think I'd rather stick with a key lime pie and just have a margarita separately. Glad I tried it though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tried to make this recipe exactly as stated, but oh darn - I absentmindedly zested all of my limes, and reluctantly tossed two heaping tablespoons of zest into the mix instead of the two teaspoons asked for. "Oh well", I thought as I put it in the oven, "I guess I'll reap what I've sown." As you can probably guess, it turned out to be the most happy of accidents! These are absolutely a margarita on a crust, and I'll make them again and again!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly as written and am not, as I’d hoped, in love with these. I used a good quality tequila. It’s right on the edge of too salty and not quite as good as key lime pie. I wanted it to be a salty/sweet dessert but it did not meet my expectations. I will not make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe reminds me of an old Rock Hudson / Doris Day movie in which an advertising agency pitches a line of candy wafers that are loaded with concentrated booze. The wafers are served at a party where everyone gobbles them up – and everyone gets smashed. This was a long, long time ago. I'm sure that these bars – which sound absolutely delicious – won't lead to such embarrassing scenes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I guess substituting rum for the tequila and cointreau and adding crushed mint leaves would yield mojito bars.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these! I make them in cupcake tins, then cut in half to make a lime shape. :) perfect bite size to grab on the way to the pool. The zest and salt topper is a must.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A lot of people asking about the alcohol. 1/4 cup tequila is 2 oz.  2 tablespoons cointreau is an additional oz. Before cooking there is 3/16 oz of liquor per serving. Less than a quarter shot). Cooktime shoudl reduce that alcohol content by 25% to approx .04 or 1/25 oz of liquor per serving.  Negligible at most.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I recommend topping with a sprinkle of Tajin!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I absolutely loved these and combing the flaky salt with lime was a great tip. Here is another tip….don’t decide you want to try making the crust with shortbread cookies like I did. While the bars still tasted amazing, I didn’t take into account the huge amount of butter already in the cookies and then the stick of melted butter on top of that. The crust became more of a solid butter brick than a crust. Lesson learned :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This brings to mind boozy Margarita cheesecakes I have made. Those used a pretzel crust. Mentioning this as some might want to try that version.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Top shelf! Definitely adding this to the list of go-to summertime desserts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sadly, these are terrible. Followed the directions exactly. Friends were laughing at how bad they were.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'm willing to bet that the comments here complaining of saltiness are using Morton kosher salt instead of Diamond Crystal. I'm usually pretty sensitive to salt, and these were just as salty as a good margarita, which was the goal!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used top shelf liquor and I’m glad I did! These are very booze-forward (in a fun way). Likely to please fans of a quality margarita and they were amazing straight out of the freezer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is very similar to the Recipe for Atlantic Beach Pie. Saltine crust. Lime juice. Egg yolks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought the very same thing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these with Schar gluten-free saltine-like crackers. Turned out great. Ate them right out of the freezer. Don't think leaving them at room temp for long would be a good idea. Lime zest garnish is a must.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would like to make these but freezing would be challenging because of space.  Im wondering if adding an additional thickener to the filling would help stabilize it.  Agar agar powder doesn’t melt at room temp.  Instant clear gel May work as well.  Any thoughts?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'd also love to hear about experiences with thickener - I'm thinking of adding one tsp of unflavored gelatin

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 1.5 Tbs no-sugar pectin plus 1/4 tsp xanthan gum. I also used 2 whole eggs + 1 yolk (because I didn't want to have 5 egg whites left over). Turned out fine. Texture was similar to a stiffish key lime pie or creme brulee. Didn't need to freeze it ( keep it chilled).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great crust and lovely lime flavor but the filling had a bitter aftertaste. I used a micro plane and was careful to not scrape into the pith, and used Cointreau instead of Grand Marnier. Anyone else have the same issue?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would a graham cracker crust be better than a saltines one?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The idea is to imitate the saltiness of a margarita.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does baking cook out the alcohol?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: you can taste the tequila pretty easily in the finished bar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a miss for us. The tequila does NOT cook off and it was too boozy. Definitely an adult dessert. Some might like it, but it was not for us!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not use graham crackers for crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: International readers beware! I followed this recipe to the letter, and ended up with a luscious, creamy curd on top and a mass of broken saltines on the bottom that had no shape whatsoever and crumbled as soon as I cut into the "bars". When I watched the video for the recipe on youtube it dawned on me. In the U.S, one saltine cracker is half the size as it is in my country (I'm in south america). So there was no way my crust was ever going to hold its shape, I used wayy too many crackers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: That's where a gram scale comes in handy. I wouldn't bake without one!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used coarse salt so it was a little over salty added to the crust and the curd. Next time I would cut it in half so I can add to top along with the zest. Aside from that, a decadent ending to the grilled scallop, peach and corn medley. And, frozen is preferred over cold.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent. I made a couple of tweaks based on others notes. I tripled the batch and used a 10x15 jelly roll pan for a large party. Left out the salt in the crust (plenty salty with just the saltines). Triple sec for the orange liqueur. 2 whole eggs & one egg yolk (6 eggs, 3 yolks) for the filling. Added about 1.5 T (about 1 t or a bit more/batch) of pectin to keep the filling firm out of the freezer. Mixed lime zest with salt for the topping but I found this made the bars a little too salty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sweet, savory, salty and slightly boozy, these are absolute perfection. Don’t change a thing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Great dessert for a party or potluck.  Yes, it can be made GF:  I used Schar brand lightly-salted GF saltines.  Measured the 113 gm but it seemed a bit thin in my 8" square pan so added a few more.  Cooked the max of the times suggested.
Made twice:  first time was with regular Premium Saltines. I first tried Hornitos gold tequila since that is what I had.  They were a bit strong;  bought Hornitos white tequila which were smoother.  I did leave out the salt since we do low sodium.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Respectfully, these turned out horribly. I’m not sure what kind of limes the recipe writer bought, but I used six limes (recipe said 4) and only had 1/4 cup of juice. I was in too deep at that point, so I went ahead and made them anyway. The result was a very salty and almost savory tasting bar — no tangy lime flavor. And, they also had no sweetness, which surprised me considering the entire can of sweetened condensed milk. Takes a lot for me to dislike a treat, but I threw out the batch.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In Canada sweetened condensed milk comes in a 10 oz tin. That's what I used and still perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious and unique! I agree with other commenters that they are best served straight out of the freezer. I also didn't use any extra salt for topping as the crust is plenty salty for my taste.

Whipped cream is perfect with these. The extra fat complements the extremely bright flavors of the lime and tequila.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Took these to a summer potluck and they were a big hit. They do get sticky when they sit out in the heat, but nobody seemed to care! Used pretzels for the crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tequila is a migraine trigger for me. Bummer. Can I make these with vodka? They look so good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They wouldn't be margarita bars then, but what about maybe try a daiquiri bar, with rum instead of the tequila? I think you'd want a liquor you can taste a little.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this recipe with a half as much added salt in the crust, coconut tequila, and fresh orange juice (instead of liquor). It was a great salty tangy treat!  Lots of texture: I loved the frosty texture straight from the freezer and my last bite at a bit more room temp was equally as enjoyable.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love margaritas but I was disappointed with this dessert, which was just unbalanced between the salt,  lime,  and alcohol flavors.  Simultaneously too boozy, overly salty, and sour. I'll stick with Atlantic beach pie, or key lime pie, and real margaritas to drink.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made pretty instructions.  But disappointed with this dessert,  which was just unbalanced between the salt,  lime,  and alcohol. Simultaneously overly salty, sour, and boozy.  I'll stick with Atlantic Beach pie, or key lime pie for dessert and margaritas to drink.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Our group was split down the middle of liking this dessert and thinking it was too boozy and salty. I fell in the boozy/salty group. They weren't terrible, but not my favorite. Probably won't make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Big hit.  Pretty rich.  Cut into smaller pieces.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These bars were a huge hit at our July 4th cookout!  Based on other comments, I only used a half teaspoon of salt in the crust.  I baked this is an 8x8 pan and cook time was about 20 minutes.  After freezing overnight, I sliced them up and topped with more lime zest and freshly ground sea salt.  I moved them to the refrigerator shortly before serving and the texture was perfect - no need to worry about keeping them cool after that because they will be devoured in no time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Big hit at a summer BBQ. Even though the bars got soft sitting out, people put them in cups and ate them with spoons. I used a pretzel crust, which sounded better than saltines.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these, they're very good, but VERY salty. I suggest leaving the salt out of the crust (the saltines have plenty of salt) and being careful with the sprinkle of flaky salt in the final step.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
