commentBody: sampled after the 2hr freeze time and gave this 3 stars. sampled again after it had been in the freezer overnight and am updating to 4 stars. flavors seemed to mingle more with time. eat it directly out of the freezer!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fantastic recipe.  Personally, I believe the amount of tequila could be lowered, depending on how much you like the taste of tequila.  However, for someone who wants a simple recipe that is extremely delicious, I would recommend this.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found these too tequila forward, almost in an off putting way. Also found the crust to be too thick which made the ratio of filling to crust less than pleasant. Next time I might try reducing the tequila amount and subbing in something to cut it such as OJ.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excited to make this, but aren’t we about due for another recipe video, Vaughn? I am imagining the witty commentary already.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would you really consider these boozy I mean I can down 1/4 of tequila in one go....this is over the span of an entire square inch sheet pan....you'd have to entire consume the entire bake to get the equivalent of like....a shot and a half of booze....

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these when my daughter ( great cook) and some friends came to visit. Found the lime to make it bitter more than flavorful ( I know how to properly zest...) and just not an interesting dessert for the time that it took. Not a crowd pleaser and ended tossing all of it--

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Brought these to a BBQ and everyone raved about them. Tastes just like a margarita! I did have to pop them in and out of the freezer a few times to keep them from getting too soggy. I may try the pectin additive another suggested. These are going to be a summer staple for sure.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: At first I was hesitant, given the crust was made of saltine crackers. But it so works! We loved these.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Has anyone tried using cocnut milk as some folks here have suggested?
Curious about how that turned out.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this in an 8" springform pan, pushed the crust a bit up the sides. Cooked a few more minutes and it released from the sides and bottom perfectly. Garnished with more lime zest and cut into wedges. We thought the amount of salt was just right, it's needed to compliment the filling, like the salt on the rim of the glass. It was delicious! And so pretty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was delicious and a big hit. I added a drop of lime extract to dial up the lime flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I will use less tequila next time

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of only a few recipes that I've made repeatedly. Always a hit at the summer parties. Doing them for Easter this year with different types (colors) of salts on top to make them colorful. Yum and fun!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Oh my goodness these are amazing. I made them as written, except I did not use parchment paper because I didn’t have any, and that was a mistake. Cutting them clean and getting them out of the pan was the hardest part. 
I used Lalo blanco tequila and freshly squeezed lime juice. As others have described, I did add some rind along with kosher salt to the top. The sweetness plus the tartness of the limes, plus the salt from the crust and the topping, plus tequila, make for an absolutely amazing treat. And the kitchen smelled amazing :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a perfect recipe.  The bars are amazing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was somewhat skeptical about the saltine crust in this recipe but as soon as I had gotten the ingredients together it smelled amazing and my doubts evaporated! I made them with reposoado instead of blanco tequila, and they were absolutely delicious. A big hit with my family for Memorial Day, and an absolutely perfect dessert for hot summer days. Cool, tart, and refreshing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excuse me: Blanco is "preferred for its milder taste"? Surely you jest. Please try a good Reposado or Anejo and get back to us.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As I was making these, I wondered if I should hold the additional salt in the crust. I think I should have- too salty for my taste!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would it be at all possible to make this with whole eggs? Any time I have leftover egg whites, they always get forgotten and then thrown away weeks later.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Freeze egg whites, or make meringue or macarons.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What to do? I have a crush on Vaughn Vreeland due to watching the video of how to make these!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
@Elizabeth Tomic
I read your comment and was inspired to watch the video.  
He was definitely entertaining to watch before trying out this recipe! 
I would encourage people to check it out before trying the recipe.  
Just out of the oven and ready to put in the freezer.  Girls weekend, hope they are as good as the comments and Vaughn says they are!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I liked them, but I think we would have liked them better as regular lime bars (without the tequila!) The tequila comes through very strong, so make sure you use one you like. They were very rich so we cut them into smaller pieces. We also found that we liked the flavor more once they had warmed up from the freezer a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would NOT recommend using sweetened condensed coconut milk. My mixture/split curdled in the oven and would not set. Stick with regular sweetened condensed milk!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What are the chances. I have saltines and tequila that I need to use up. Can’t wait to try these.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In the video, the food writer compares these to jello shots, and that's really what they taste like. They're boozy, bitter, salty, and sour. But not really a dessert.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I wanted to make these non-alcoholic what would I substitute in the filling?  Water or ??  I know that’s not the intent of these but …

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could just leave out the liquor, would basically be a variation on key lime pie bars.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Look up Atlantic Beach pie, on this site. Same idea, no alcohol.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to make these dairy free.  Could coconut cream work instead of the condensed milk? And also wondering if using agar agar would help stabilize the texture so that they don’t get runny quickly?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: if i don’t have orange liqueur, what could work as a substitute?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The orange liqueur is part of the true-to-a-margarita flavor, so it depends on how interested you are in mimicking that flavor. If veritable margarita-ness isn't a priority, you could sub an equal quantity of orange juice or omit it entirely. If you want the full effect, it's worth picking up a bottle. If you like margaritas, you'll find a use for the rest of it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: the booze doesnt cook out?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: No. Please see this Wikipedia link; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooking_with_alcohol#Alcohol_in_finished_food

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It's not clear to me why this wouldn't be family fare. Isn't the tequila tamed by its time in the oven?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't know about the alcohol content, but the tequila flavor is definitely there and meant to stand out. I don't think it would be favorable to most young palates or non-drinkers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: so, with baking is this alcoholic or could my kids eat it!?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would a mix of orange juice and (mostly) lime juice be able to replace all of the alcohol?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question, does the alcohol cook off in the baking process? I hope not ;)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What is the alcohol content after baking? Has it all evaporated?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I just leave it refrigerator until I serve?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would not drive a car after eating more than one of these booze bars. Seriously thought the alcohol overpowered the lime flavor, but my company really liked them. Would choose orange liquor next time instead of orange brandy, which might enhance fruit flavor and lower alcohol blast.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Disappointing. The base is very salty. The custard has a scrambled-egg texture in some places and didn’t set in others.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone subbed gluten-free saltines in this recipe? I want to make it for my friend, but she’s celiac.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not use pectin in the filling to keep the filling gelled, instead of keeping the bars frozen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them yesterday. I used a combination of low-sugar pectin an xanthan gum as thickeners, and they turned out very well. Didn't have to keep them frozen (although I understand the concept of "frozen margaritas." I also followed the recommendation of another reader and combined some grated lime zest with the Maldon salt for finishing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The freezing made them more refreshing I found.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Okay to sub mezcal for the tequila?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious, and best served straight from the freezer and cut into small bite-sized pieces. The saltine crust makes the recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does the alcohol cook out? Like, can I bring these to share at work?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Refreshingly unique bars. Serve frozen, not refrigerated. This way they won’t freeze solid, they hold their shape, and have a satisfying bite to them. If refrigerated, they’re too soft to handle without making a mess. Try not to leave these out at room temp otherwise you might as well drink these instead of eating them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Key lime-y. Added a little unsweetened/unflalvorled gelatin (like 1/3 packet), hoping that won't affect the freeze

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone baked these in a convection oven?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How long do they keep in the freezer, if I want to make days ahead for event?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Serve on day 2, straight from freezer.  Amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am a big salt lover, but these were too salty, and the crust was not firm enough for my taste. Filling was delish,  so I think I'd try with a pretzel crust, no salt added to the crust, and bake the crust at a higher temp.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Sorry but this was disappointing.  I tossed half of it.  I much prefer Bon Appetit's margarita pie.  Much lighter and no eggs.  Thanks anyway NYT.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as directed. Inedibly bitter and salty.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How do you make the curd, I don’t see it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Steps 4 and 5

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Real disappointment.  Overly boozy, dry texture from being in freezer.  Sounds way better than it is

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
A big hit! Although next time I will add less salt to the crust and measure the rind carefully in the filling as it seemed a little bitter for my taste. Definitely make-ahead-able! 
  What is the best way to measure grated rind? Do you pack it down? Tamp it a little? Any advice would be appreciated!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love salt, but these bars are over-the-top. I was also not a fan of how cold they need to be in order to be relatively firm. Not a recipe that I will revisit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So can you get drunk on them? Or does it burn off all/most of the alcohol? What’s the vibe?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Does adding pectin or xanthum gum make this stable (not too soft) at room temperature?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I will definitely try these. With 1/4 cup of tequila and 2 tablespoons of Grand Marnier mixed, baked, and then cut in to 16 squares, there is only going to be a trace of alcohol in each piece

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wayyyyy too boozy and not sweet enough I wish I had added more sugar a few tablespoons of tequila and Cointreau would have been plenty. I used top shelf liquor as well

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these and loved the real margarita flavor. I used graham crackers instead of saltines and thought the crust was too salty, just barely edible. Next time I’ll cut the salt in the crust in half. I will also try two whole eggs and two yolks instead of 5 yolks to try and get the filling firmer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I had a Margarita Maddness party and made these to top off the evening. Major hit!
Even my skeptacle chef friend's eyes popped when he tried them -- and then wanted the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really good! Reduced tequila to 3 tbsp, do the full amount next time. Gets soft at room temp, keep in cooler. Next time try with rum and mint

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Good but maybe a tad too much tequila for my taste. I’d cut it to an eighth of a cup next time. Tastes like summer though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The base was way too salty, but the curd is divine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for a BBQ this past weekend, and I'm already planning to make them again for a party this upcoming weekend. They are the perfect summer dessert. So good! Definitely keep them in the freezer until you're ready to serve them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A little too salty for me but friends loved them.   Try eating them upside down to avoid getting hit with too much salt up front.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: VERY FUN and a big hit at the beach party - will definitely make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I agree with past comments that they need to stay in the freezer or at the very least the fridge after making. But that crust is divine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are super yummy! I made these for a pool party and they were a hit. I served them from the freezer, and did not let them sit out. I used a muffin tin and they came out great. The curd was definitely a thick layer for muffin sized treats, so I baked it for a few minutes past the time called for and they held up pretty well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I accidentally forgot the booze (found it on the counter when cleaning up). I then grabbed the pan from the freezer; it was not solid yet, so I slowly stirred the alcohol with a fork into the topping until it was all even and creamy, and returned to freezer. FANTASTIC! Nice crisp crust remained intact, and topping was VERY margarita like.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The theme of today’s get together was brownies and bars. These were the first and only recipe I wanted to do.

It was a huge hit among the crew. I almost did not get any. I cannot wait to make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made it in an 8x 8 pan. So there was too much crust for the top. I used graham crackers not saltines and added salt and sugar but it was too salty so next time leave the salt out. It was very hard to get out of the pan and cracked as I was trying to get it out. So you need a pan with a removable bottom.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these for a party, and even though we had lots of other food and deserts, this was crowd’s favorite! I’m so happy to have discovered these, will be making them for parties all the time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are sooo salty. If I made them again I would not add salt to the crust, filling, or top. The salt from the crackers is more than enough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As easy and delicious as they seem.  I cut them into small bars and took them to a taco party, and everyone enjoyed them.  A great sweet for a casual dinner!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These bars are stellar. I used a shortbread crust from another recipe, adding a few crushed-up saltines, but other than that, I made it as written. Vaughn Vreeland's recipes are always perfect. I will make this recipe again and again and again. It's that wonderful. I took a pan of these to a Memorial Weekend cookout tonight and they were a huge hit! 🇺🇸

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do you need to cover (plastic? Foil?) when freezing the crust before cooking? Same question for freezing prior to cutting it. 2 hours seems a long time to be in the freezer without any covering.  Also, it’s a curd and I’m wondering now to stack bars for freezing without them sinking into each other. Is parchment between layers sufficient?  I’m not used to freezing baked goods or making curd, so many thanks for any tips.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great. Didn’t taste too much alcohol (if you’re worried about that). Very fresh, full of lime flavor. I sprinkled lime zest on top for some added color pop.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Truly unique pot luck or apres Mex meal finale. Fully effective at capturing the margarita flavors! Wonder how it would pair with one of those nearly brandy-like dark tequilas? 

Missed opportunity to name them Frozen Margarita Bars.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great flavor. Made in 8x8 pan without problem. Didn’t like the need to keep in the freezer.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: You can also use NA tequila such as Almave or FreeSpirits and DHOS Orange or Lyres for and alternative.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: How could one make this gluten free? I don’t know of any gf saltine brands.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don’t know of any GF saltine brands, but I have used Schar Entertainment Crackers (GF) in crab cakes and shrimp cakes with great success.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone successfully used graham crackers for the crust instead of saltines?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Oh my!  I have made these three times now and everyone clambers for seconds… and thirds. I don’t eat dessert very often but these are super addictive. I have substituted pretzels and ritz crackers for the crust as well as the saltines. I think the ritz were my favourites but all are delicious. This recipe is a keeper and every time I make it I’m asked for it. Well done!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe as stated and used good tequila. They were delicious! I think the trick is to no skimp on the tequila. Go with a well crafted boutique spirit not just an everyday brand. You'll taste the difference.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'd adjust the grade of liquor to the audience served. Frat party? $20 tequila blanco. Wedding reception? The reposado or an añejo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If I make a few days before serving, should I cover the dessert before freezing?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So tasty as written. I think I will cut back the alcohol a bit next time and see how that goes as these were pretty boozy/not as balanced as I would like.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't taste any tequila at all. The bars were fine, but just tasted like lime bars, andI have a much better recipe for those. Waste of tequila.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these. It’s basically a key lime pie with tequila and salt, which gives it a margarita vibe. Love the crust - it’s like a graham cracker crust with more of a shortbread flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: can anyone suggest a gluten-free alternative option for the crust?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made Paloma bars by doing 1 limes worth of juice and filling the rest of the 1/2 cup with grapefruit. be sure to strain the grapefruit, its pulpy! Good, refreshing, boozy and bitter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone used the egg whites to make a meringue topping? Sounds similar to Atlantic Beach pie

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I never understand why anyone would butter a pan and then put parchment paper on top of it. Isn't the parchment sufficient to keep from sticking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was excited to make this...followed the recipe...and I'm wondering why it tastes bitter? I used a citrus peeler - no white from the limes at all. Limes were not old or odd tasting (used the extra juice in soda water.) Any ideas?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone made these in advance and just stored them in the freezer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 2 tbsp of gold rum instead of rum extract, and added another 1 tbsp flour to compensate. I baked them for 14 minutes. Tastes pretty good! Now, to pour the bourbon...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rum extract is disgusting, and rum isn't sufficiently concentrated for one tbsp to add much flavor. The best solution I've found thus far is to lightly brown the butter and then use 3 tbsp dark (strongly flavored) rum in place of the extract. Evaporating the water in the butter helps make room for a little extra liquid without unduly increasing spread.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sue any specific rum recommendations? I’m not very well-versed in the different types haha

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Gillian meyers is my go to for baking, it’s spicy and sweet

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lovely flavor and texture.  I made them exactly as the recipe called, but decided 45 gram sized cookies were a tad too big (for sharing with multiple other holiday cookies), so I made the majority of mine 23 grams and cooked them for the same amount of time.  Perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: GREAT cookie recipe! Made it with rum, not extract — worked fine. Easy to make, dough is very easy to work with. The crunch of the sugar, the cakey egg yolk dough and the nutmeg flavor are an addictive combination. I made them smallish, and got 5 dozen. My kids and I ate so many straight off the cookie sheets that now I have to make another batch for our cookie boxes. This goes into my permanent Christmas recipe collection...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To store for later, roll the dough into balls, skip the nutmeg-sugar step, and freeze. When ready to bake, warm the balls a little with your hands (to make them stickier), dredge in nutmeg-sugar and bake as usual. No need to thaw. I added a minute (10 min. in my oven for fresh, 11 for frozen) and they came out perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have the same question - can we use rum as a substitute for rum extract?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today exactly as written. The texture is very good and they taste good but I would prefer a stronger rum and nutmeg flavor. They mostly taste like a snickerdoodle with a hint of nutmeg. I will make again but double the rum extract and add some nutmeg to the batter instead of the nutmeg only being in the sugar coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just used rum instead of the extract and they came out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe was disappointing in texture and I think I know the reason why.  They came out cakey, and they did not flatten out with cooling.  This is because there is too much flour.  Maybe it would be different if you weighed out the amount instead of using cups, which is what I did.  All other recipes (eg. Cook’s Illustrated, Nancy Baggett) call for 2.5 cups for the same ratio of ingredients.  The flavour was good, however.  I will make these again but with an adjustment to the flour.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Sophie same thing happened when I made them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can we use 1 T (or some other amount) vanilla extract instead of rum or rum extract? My 9-year-old doesn't want the rum flavor. Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Second this question!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
We ended up using rum. I was disappointed with this recipe. I'm an intermediate to advanced baker, so it was not the execution. I think the flavor is just subtle (even though we added cinnamon and nutmeg to the dough, and cinnamon to the sugar-nutmeg mix). I have weak taste buds and they tasted like regular snickerdoodles to me. 

I'll be trying the gingerbread snickerdoodle recipe today, which I'm guessing will have a strong enough flavor for me to perceive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Honestly, these might be my new favorite cookies. Like...ever. I used preground nutmeg to no disappointment and added cinnamon to the rolling sugar as well. Will probably try adding nutmeg to the dough next time, ‘cause why not? And I know that there definitely will be many, many next times.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: What a great cookie!  First time I’ve ever grated whole nutmeg and it made quite a difference.  So fresh tasting!  Agree with another reviewer that 45g makes too large of a cookie.  23g was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Substituted bourbon aged in port barrels for the rum extract. Success! The fresh nutmeg   sugar coating is wonderful and the cookies have a creamy center. Made smaller truffle sized balls and ended up with 4 dozen cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Ash I followed your suggestions—everyone LOVED the bourbon taste. I made some without bourbon for the kiddos, who typically dislike boozy additions, but we all felt that the deeper flavor of the boozers was better. I used the Bulleit bourbon we had on hand. Thank you!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is an outstanding cookie. After reading some of the other comments, I decided upon making two adjustments to the recipe that I believe worked out splendidly. I used Myers rum instead of rum extract, because that was what I had on hand, and I added 1 tsp of freshly grated nutmeg into the batter. They were ceremoniously enjoyed with friends and neighbors, and these have certainly made my "make it again" list.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved these cookies! My first try failed and the dough was way too crumbly following the 3 1/2 cups of flour. I made them again and used my scale to measure the amount flour and they were amazing! Such a perfect recipe for a Christmas party.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This happened to me too! Thinking I mixed the flour too long in the stand mixer. I ended up adding another 1/2 stick of butter and they still turned out delicious! Just more plump than flat. I am going to try again and weigh the flour as you suggest...and be a little more careful while mixing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I live at ~6,000 feet, so for elevation adjusted the baking soda to a rounded 1/2 tsp. Also used 2 Tbsp rum instead of rum extract, added 1 tsp nutmeg to the batter, and added a dash or two of cinnamon to the nutmeg/sugar mixture after reading through the other comments. Made each cookie approx. 23 grams and baked for 9 minutes 30 seconds. Turned out great!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
30 g 350 10min
Set edge, flat cookie, underdone center, excellent texture soft and custardy, phenolic flavor

23 g, 350, 12 min
Too dry

23 g, 350, 10 min
Perfecto

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are wonderful. One note: 2 sticks of butter don't weigh 225 g. I had to add 3.25 tablespoons to the 2 sticks to get to 225 g. Bakers who are having trouble with crumbly dough may not be using enough fat. I used a tablespoon-sized cookie scoop and got 48 cookies. They were a BIG hit with my family.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is what happened to me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @bigbang same problem. Either customer flour or add a little butter. I will weigh the flour and butter next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 2 tbsp of gold rum instead of rum extract, and added another 1 tbsp flour to compensate. I baked them for 14 minutes. Tastes pretty good! Now, to pour the bourbon...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We got a quart of eggnog that's about to be 'best by' tomorrow, and I was hoping these cookies had eggnog IN them. Bummer. I'll still make them because eggnog is delicious :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I had the same reaction when I saw the soon-to-expire eggnog in the fridge and remembered this recipe haha

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Some family members have celiac disease so I substituted 1-for-1 GF flour and the recipe still worked perfectly! Also subbed rum for rum extract (because we couldn’t find any...) and that worked well too. Overall, super easy recipe and a HUGE success!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Do everything this man says, and you will have delicious cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent cookies...the only revision I made was to add a half teaspoon of nutmeg to the flour mixture so that taste was inside the cookie too. These are fantastic...great texture, absolutely delicious...a crowd-pleasing twist on snickerdoodles.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've used other eggnog snickerdoodle recipes over the years and this one's the best!  I did add eggnog extract and nutmeg to the dough to make it even more eggnoggy.  Also put cinnamon in the rolling sugar.  Make sure you watch the baking time so they are chewy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I strongly recommend skipping the sugar coating in favor of a light icing made with eggnog or cream, powdered sugar, and about a 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon and/or nutmeg and a pinch of salt. Not as quick as rolling in sugar, but the cookies can be dipped if the icing is thin and a quick swipe of an offset spatula will ensure coverage. Just let the icing set up and separate cookies layers with wax paper or parchment when storing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: love this tip!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are phenomenal! Totally worth hunting down rum extract. I will definitely keep some in my pantry from now on - just for this recipe. Because of the high ratio of flour, these cookies don’t really spread, so it helps to slightly flatten the dough balls first. Also, if using a cookie scoop, make sure you really smooth out the dough balls between the palms of your hands, otherwise the cookies retain any craggily bits on the surface.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Started by following the recipe as written. Found the dough to be a bit dry and difficult to work with, so I added a little water to make the dough easier to shape. Happy with the result. Nice, cake-y cookie and the rum flavoring is present, but not over the top (which I was a little worried about). Would make again

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made so many mistakes and yet these were fabulous!  I left fresh nutmeg at home so doubled the ground nutmeg to get a nice kick of flavor.  Ended up using McCormick Rum extract and added according to recipe.  Everone devoured these cookies!  Such a nice twist on my regular snickerdoodle recipe.  I never make snickerdoodles with anything other than cream of tartar- this recipe is such a festive twist on a family favorite.  Thanks!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For 50 years, I’ve been making a new cookie recipe for my family every year and then let them vote thumbs up (make it again next year) or thumbs down. Very few new recipes make a unanimously acclaimed leap to the top of the charts as this one did. Absolutely delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I took it down to 41 grams with a degree of guilt, since I always try to follow a recipe exactly the first time--and WOW! HUGE cookies that couldn't stay away from each other on the cookie sheet. (Needed 16 minutes in the oven too.) So next time I'm shooting for 23 grams as suggested.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a delicious, easy and festive holiday cookie recipe.  My kids love them. I don’t like big cookies, so I made mine 25gm. Also, I used real rum, and next time I will add 2 Tbs instead of 1 Tbsp.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am obsessed with these. They are so soft and flavorful and easy to make. I considered using rum instead of rum extract, but after watching the video of them being made, I decided to go ahead and buy the extract. I will definitely make them again, and soon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Agree with others - 3 ½ c is too much floor, crumbly dough, but workable. I should’ve weighed it. Added nutmeg to mix, about 1tsp freshly ground. Could’ve used more. Made them smaller, a level Tbsp is 23g, wouldn’t make them any bigger.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rum extract is terrible. Ruined these cookies for me. Use regular rum, beside that these were good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: SO MUCH BETTER THE NEXT DAY - I know it’s recommended but it should be required to give these at least overnight to rest at room temp before eating. Also, for me the cook time was on the long end of the spectrum at least if not longer, I’d say 13-15 minutes unless you like your cookies very, very doughy. Still yummy but a bit texture-less if you only do the 10.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I highly recommend making these cookies. The texture is wonderful and I used Kerrygold which gave them that buttery yellow hue. I made one small adaptation and added a glug of Meyers’s dark rum. I found this a perfect recipe and will definitely make again next holiday season.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as written except made them 1/3 the size (15g); yielded ~54 cookies. Perfect size for snacking, party tray, sharing. Handy to have ready to bake on moment’s notice. Prepped up to the point of making the balls. Froze on baking sheet then popped into zip top. To bake: Preheat oven, roll balls on nutmeg sugar, and bake per instructions. Perfection. Everyone has loved them

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added 1 tsp. nutmeg to the dough will add twice as much next time. For me eggnog needs to be really nutmeg-y. I used dark rum instead of extract. Also, they are way too big. Half the size sounds perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as per recipe except added 1 tsp of nutmeg directly to the batter instead of rolling in sugar nutmeg combo as we’re not fans of sugar on cookies . Turned out delicious, creamy,  slightly chewy on the edges . Will definitely make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A delightful twist on the good ol' snickerdoodle! The rum extract really swoops in to make this a lovely eggnog reminiscent recipe. The only thing I did differently was use 1 tsp freshly grated nutmeg and 1 tsp cinnamon in the sugar coating and these were perfect. I could have shoveled the whole batch of dough in my gullet if I didn't have enough self restraint (which I barely do anyways)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: wow these are incredible. i halved the recipe,  swapped 1/2tbsp rum extract for 1 tbsp cognac (since I didn't have rum!), and swapped 1/4 of the granulated sugar for brown sugar (145g granulated, 30g brown) and OMG i am amazed at how delicious they turned out!!! even used pre-ground nutmeg since i didn't have fresh on hand, i'm so so happy with this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The texture on these cookies is amazing, soft with a slight chew, airy. I'm not a fan of nutmeg/rum, so I've been making this with lemon/almond/cardamom flavor combination that is incredible. Try it for a flavor variation!

- whisk 1 tsp cardamom into dry ingredients
- zest 2 lemons and rub it into the sugar before creaming it together with the butter
- add 1.5 tsp almond extract after whipping in eggs

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are SO good. Used a hand mixer it worked a-ok. Also subbed lemon juice for cream of tartar at a 2:1 ratio   mixed it with the wet ingredients and they came out great! Will definitely make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Butter and eggs at rm temp 
Make them smaller 
More nutmeg in coating and add some to batter

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wonderful cookies. Follow the recipe,  the rum extract (not rum) is necessary for enough & correct flavor. I highly recommend grating your own nutmeg, much more flavorful. I did substitute 2.25 teaspoons of baking powder for the cream of tarter. It worked fine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Amazing cookies! Big hit with a real sophisticated flavor.  I am gluten-free so I used 1-1 gluten-free baking flour.  Works great (warning, maybe because of this, the cookies do not appear entirely set as I take them out of the oven - waiting for them to be set means they are actually overcooked). 

Thank you for all the commenters who suggested freezing the dough and making more of this pure deliciousness at a later date.  So… I did that and forgot to roll the cookies in the sugar/nutmeg mixture.  I recovered by sprinkling the cookies right out of the oven with powered sugar mixed with nutmeg.  The sugar sinks into the cookies and the cookies are even more fragrant with the nutmeg.  Maybe better than rolling in sugar/nutmeg before hand!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For more intense flavor, make these with rum extract rather than rum. I've made these cookies both ways and using rum resulted in less flavorful cookies even when tripling the volume called for in the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Bravo!  It's Perfect!  Thank you!  Love the video of you and your family.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these. Was going to let dough rest in fridge overnight or more when, after an hour or so, it occurred to me that the cream of tartar and baking soda might react adversely if left too long. Reddit confirmed! So bake them soon after mixing. For me, 25 gram balls rolled in sugar mixed with fresh grated nutmeg and baked for 11mn = 42 perfect cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Have to comment because these were unbelievable. I made them for a cookie exchange and am super sad I didn’t save more for myself. Will be making them again soon. Used rum instead of rum extract and still felt like there was that hint of rum flavor in it. Based on other comments, I also added a tad nutmeg and even some cinnamon to the dough itself, and cinnamon to the sugar coating. Used a coarse sugar for the roll which gave a nice crunch. Overall - amazing!!! Thank you, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used dark spiced rum instead of rum extract, as I would use in eggnog. Also added a splash of bourbon. Very good cookies as they were but added about 1 tsp cinnamon to the rolling nutmeg and sugar mix after the first tray came out and they were even better. Not enough cinnamon to overwhelm but it softened the nutmeg somehow.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn’t have rum or rum extract so I used 3/4 T actual eggnog and 1/4T vanilla. Turned out super yummy. Also didn’t want to pull out the stand mixer so I just did this by hand. Took a little extra effort but the cookies turned out delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great! An admirable sugar recipe cookie with an eggnog twist. Had to sub gluten free flour to accommodate some guests and it still worked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are fantastic. I took Sue's suggestion of browning the butter and using rum instead of rum extract. I scaled these to 30g each and they are pretty big, could go even smaller. Perfect crispy exterior and wonderful interior chew. Definitely will be in my regular holiday cookie rotation from now on!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these but accidentally left the dough out uncovered for an hour and half instead of 10 minutes like the video. I got side tracked with other dishes! They were still easy to roll and came out perfect!!! I didn’t have fresh nutmeg and used nutmeg powdered instead. The rum extract smells excessive but the amount is perfect for these cookies, trust the process! I will definitely be making these again and again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
last year I made these for the first time and they were so good that I'm making them for my christmas cookie boxes again this year.

I'm from New Zealand and rum extract is quite difficult to find so I used 1 tbsp of Kahlua instead and it works really well. This recipe yields about 24 cookies if you go with 45 grams per cookie as stated in the recipe. I ended up with 1090 grams of cookie dough so I did some maths to work out how many cookies you'll get at different sizes:
30g = 36 cookies
35g = 31 cookies
45g = 24

I think 45g is the perfect size for this recipe but if you want to make them smaller, you definitely can but just keep in mind that the consistency or texture won't be quite as good. 

If you don't know what to do with leftovers egg whites, definitely make Vaughn's rum buttered cookies as well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can rum be used in place of rum extract?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absent rum extract (or straight rum for that matter) or fresh nutmeg, I used a tablespoon of blended scotch and jarred nutmeg. The nutmeg was a little strong, but these definitely are reminiscent of eggnog. And Mr. Vreeland is correct, they were better on day 2!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Same! not finding rum extract at any local shops. may just give it a whirl with a little rum this weekend.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: REALLY wanted to be the first comment here since I think Vaughn is an angel, but nonetheless I have lost. These are divine. I don't even like eggnog. I might now need to try to like it again. Never actually used whole nutmeg before (always did ground) and wow, will never use ground again. Love that I didn't have to chill this dough for 2+ hours, etc etc as so many cookies require. Freezing a bunch of unbaked balls to make for people closer to the holiday. Chefs kiss BB.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’d say yes to rum rather extract. The flavor would be richer IMO. And once you’ve used whole nutmeg, you’ll never go back. Penzeys online.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Please, please, dear bakers, do NOT use pre-grated nutmeg (or almost any spice). The flavor of freshly grated nutmeg compared with pre-grated is heaven compared with sawdust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dang these are good. Couldn’t find unground nutmeg so just used the normal stuff. Beyond pleased.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Well this is perhaps the perfect cookie. Kudos Vaugn Vreeland!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious! I didn’t deviate from the recipe at all and they were perfection.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Toddler sous chef and I made these tonight and they really could not have been easier to make. They're getting rave reviews, especially the texture. These will be a great addition to our cookie plate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why does anyone make cookies that aren’t these cookies?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These taste amazzzzzzzzzzzzzing and were super fun to make. Mine turned out super pillowy which I'm a huge fan of. I can't wait to share them with a few closer friends. Cheers!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were good still warm, but after the first day they have an odd mouth feel and aftertaste. Followed the recipe exactly. Not my favorite snickerdoodle recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are very festive and tasty. A little overly sugary for my tastes, but that's sort of what a snickerdoodle is, I guess. I didn't have rum extract and used vanilla which seemed fine, though not as "egg noggy". I'd make these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: AMAZING! Used a glug of rum on lieu of extract and lots of ground nutmeg since I didn’t have fresh. I think going big on nutmeg is key.  About to make another batch as soon as I finish posting this.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Living in Germany, I often don't have easy access to ingredients like cream of tartar.  I used two teaspoons baking powder to replace *both* the cream of tartar and baking soda ingredients. The cookies turned out delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 1Tbsp dark rum and 2tsp vanilla extract as I didn't have rum extract. I also used 1.5tsp of nutmeg, .5tsp of cinnamon and a pinch of cloves. Excellent!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like everything about these cookies.  All exact to recipe.  I enjoyed making them and did everything exact to recipe.  Next time I’ll switch to cinnamon, consider a different rum (as others have done) and buy a second baking sheet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I halved the recipe and ended up with 26 golf ball sized cookies.  Not a problem, just a word of warning since the recipe says the entire batch would only be 24.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I measured out exactly 45g for each cookie, I got 24-25 cookies for a full recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was diagnosed with celiac last spring, so this is my first holiday season without wheat flour. I'd be lying if I said I wasn't sort of dreading it. I really wanted to make "normal" holiday cookies, and I wasn't sure if these would turn out using gluten free flour, but I decided to take the plunge, and am happy to report they turned out great! I know gf all-purpose mixes can vary, so they may not always be successful, but right now, I'm pretty excited.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Eggnog snookerdoodles, where have you been all my life?  I generally make the same cookies every year, but this being 2020, I decided to try a few new cookies.  This was a real winner.  Please don't make me wait until Christmas to make them again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think this was the best cookie I’ve ever baked! I prepared according to the recipe, only reducing size  of each to 23-25 grams. Wonderful flavor!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Captured the holidays in a single cookie recipe! I made this recipe using weight measures and followed the recipe exactly. These cookies are perfect...and I don’t even like snickerdoodles that much. I was only planning to make one batch, but I loved them enough that I stayed up late to make a second batch. I wanted to give my cookie tin recipients some extras (with a few left for me).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these vegan by using Earth Balance butter sticks and 3 tbsp of ground flax seed mixed with 8 tbsp of water (set aside for at least five minutes to gel before adding in incrementally), and coconut sugar/turbinado sugar. I also used 2 tsp of cream of tartar. They still tasted great! Next time I try them, I may used chickpea flour as the "egg" substitute to achieve the slight yellow tinge and more of an eggy flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: RUN DON'T WALK. This is the greatest cookie I have ever eaten in my entire life. I made a batch for my family and then made several more batches because they had to be shared. The world must know. No cookie compares. (I don't even like snickerdoodles). WOW.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I halved the recipe and they turned out deliciously. Like others, I used rum instead of rum extract (Bacardi gold if it makes a difference, it was what we had open).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hi Cassandra! I was interested in making half of the recipe as well, and I was wondering how you approached the eggs? Did you end up using 1/2 whole egg + 1 yolk? Thanks!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these twice so far. Two tips: 1) The sugar/nutmeg mixture was enough for two full batches. 2) It doesn’t say so but I pressed the balls slightly on the baking sheet with the back of a wooden spoon before baking so they look more like the photo instead of dough blobs.

Definitely use real rum if you have it!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I love these cookies! I love eggnog so much and anything with this much nutmeg I am here for! They came out perfectly soft. I'm so sad I only got to eat one since the rest went out on my Christmas cookie plates to my friends. 

Since it's a hot topic in the comments I have to add, DON'T SUB THE RUM EXTRACT FOR REAL RUM!!! The extract adds a stronger flavor you can't get with actual rum. The flavor from actual rum will bake right out. If you want real rum dunk the cookies in some boozy eggnog

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added 1 teaspoon to the flour mixture and I put the other teaspoon in the sugar mixture. I chose to do it this way so the nutmeg was incorporated into the cookie. They came out delish!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious and the recipe is forgiving. I made them in a beach rental kitchen with an old hand-held mixer and self-rising rather than AP flour and they still turned out really well.  Added 1 t nutmeg to the batter as suggested by others and used rum extract. Thanks for a great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these just before Christmas and they were excellent! Since I didn’t have rum extract, I substituted a tiny bottle of rum. Worked just fine.  A keeper!!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I mentioned these in a staff meeting the other day and was told I had to bring them in ASAP. Well, I did and I think I may be getting a raise. Such a wonderful holiday cookie. Great texture and rich flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Made as the recipe stated, except I made the cookies 25g instead of 45g (yielded 40 cookies). Sparkly, crispy outside with a soft inside. I just finished baking them and have eaten 5 off the tray already. Incredible! These will be in my holiday cookie boxes for years to come!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Stayed true to recipe except adding half tsp grated nutmeg to flour and choosing Myers’s dark rum over extract.  Easy prep but underwhelming first sampling. After a night in the icebox, the flavours awoke are all 30 cookies inhaled by crew of docs and nurses by 11am.  Thanks

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: When I first read the recipe, I thought it was going to be WAY too much rum extract, but it was great!  If I was a better baker, I’d think about one more yolk, but I don’t know if that would work.  Will definitely make these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fabulous cookies and if you like the nutmeg flavor consider adding some to the dough and increasing the nutmeg:sugar ratio in to coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The description says soft inside and crispy outside. Despite following the recipe to the letter, including covering the dough to let it hydrate for 10 min. before forming the balls and being careful to not overbake, my cookies turned out crispy throughout. Good flavor, but not the texture I expected. Any ideas about where I went wrong?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Five stars! Added a bit of nutmeg (Penzeys grated) to the flour mix. Used one T Meyers dark rum instead of extract. A bit of cinnamon in the rolling sugar with the nutmeg. Cookies pre-bake were about 22 grams. Thirteen minutes at 350 F. Easy and delicious, perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious and so easy! Definitely going to try the straight rum instead of the rum essence.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wanted to come back and report on these, but they disappeared so fast I never got to taste one... I guess that's a five-star review!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were a huge hit at Xmas this year.  Boy oh boy does the eggnog flavor enhance each day following baking.  I opted for smaller cookies, so measured each one out at ~28g and could have gone smaller (I prefer a 2-3 bite cookie).  I followed the recipe exactly, aside from the cookie size.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Vaughn Vreeland is adorable in the video demo'ing his eggnog snickerdoodle recipe. Be sure to watch it all the way to the end for example of the wonderful family connection of food. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LQDNDcpgPe4 Worth seeking out rum extract. McCormick is at most groceries. Better brands available via online order. I drink bourbon in eggnog so interested in trying bourbon extract too. Agree with others who made these smaller than specified in recipe. Lovely recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made according to directions except substituted rum for rum extract. (I find most extracts to be too artificial.)

I found I missed the punch of rum though. I might make again adding in 1T more rum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe as written except for adding 1/2 tsp nutmeg to the batter and making the cookies a little smaller. Made 38 cookies in total. I was initially skeptical because the dough smelled strongly of rum extract, but I couldn’t stop myself from eating three of the finished cookies immediately after they cooled a bit. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these today nearly as written but used 2.5Tbsp Myers Dark Rum instead of rum extract. (Lazy, didn’t want to go buy) I did grate fresh nutmeg. Aside from my kitchen smelling divine I think these will need to be hidden or I’m not going to get any more of them. New holiday favorite.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed other's advice and reduced the flour from 3 1/2 cups to 3 cups. I also put nutmeg in the batter.  They came out perfect! A lovely, soft, pillowy cookie with subtle flavors of rum and nutmeg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this by weighing out the ingredients instead of using measuring cups like other commenters suggested and they turned out perfect! Crunchy outside, soft center…definitely adding these to the annual rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Divine. Definitely weigh the flour. I used vanilla extract instead of rum extract and didn't miss it -- they still tasted like eggnog. Freshly grating the nutmeg was worth the effort

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would add the nutmeg to the batter and definitely use real rum as others have suggested.  I think the flavors would be more cohesive.  Then roll them in regular sugar with no additional spice.  However, I can't say I would make them again.  To me there is a little bitter after taste perhaps due to the rum extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy and tasty!  Used 3C flour as others recommended, extra nutmeg in batter,  1 T of Bacardi; added a shake of cinnamon in the sugar mix. I think the rum flavor is light at best and that the freshly grated nutmeg is the star of the show. Made smaller to yield about 40, so double the sugar mix.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Perfect cookie - used whiskey and subbed baking powder for cream of tartar cause that’s what I had.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
End of the baking day, had only one egg left and no cream of tartar.  Made a half recipe, just used the one whole egg and a tsp of baking powder as a sub for CT.  A generous TBSP of rum. 
 They turned out great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I swapped 100g of the flour for cake flour, extra fluffy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! Per community recommendations, I added a teaspoon of ginger in the dough (and the freshly ground ginger did make a difference!) Instead of the rum extract, I used Lorann's Rum Emulsion, which provided a strong rum flavor to the dough with the provided measurements.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’m a cowboy cook from Texas and I’ve made this recipe on a wood fired grill about two dozen times in the last month. They never burn despite the wind. Mr Vaughn, this is one heck of a good recipe. Try it sometime with a sliver of melted Camembert. Also like your Mexican Hot Chocolate Cookie recipe (you can also bake it in a grill). We like it with chile infused chocolate chunks better over marshmallows.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Subbed 2 T gold rum for extract as I couldn’t find the latter. Added 1 t nutmeg to dough per others’ notes. Used 27 g of dough for each cookie. First batch was underbaked. Added time for second batch. Starting with third batch, flattened the balls with my palm and baked for 12 minutes. Pretty good. Wondering if extract would make for a more pronounced eggnog flavor. Yielded about 40 cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
“Like biting into a cloud but the cloud is made of eggnog” - my boyfriend, Colin 

I’ve never worked with rum extract before, and the comments made me a bit nervous. But after baking, the intense extract-y smell went away to reveal wonderfully fragrant and eggnog-y cookies! I used a bowl and a rubber spatula to make this bc that’s my life right now and the texture was wonderful. Great cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly as directed and they're was delicious. I did add a tiny bit of salt to the sugar/nutmeg bowl for rolling in. (And that mixture made way too much, you could half it.) BUT do yourself a favor and let them sit on the counter for a couple of hours (or in a tupperware) before tasting one. I ate one hot out of the oven and it was just kind of so-so, but then ate another one two hours later and the flavor punched through more strongly. I can imagine that tomorrow, they'll be even better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 25 g of dough for each cookie, and could fit 12 on a 17 x 12 inch pan. Cooked for 14 minutes at 350 F. Cookies turned out beautiful! And delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious as a regular snickerdoodle, but not much of an egg nog flavor—and I used 3T of rum instead of extract. I recommend doubling the amount of nutmeg in the surgery mixture. I used freshly grated nutmeg and it was barely noticeable. Refrigerating the dough for a couple hours before making balls is recommended.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made these smaller than recipe called for  -  24 grams.  Still plenty big.  
Made 42 cookies and these are delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am so glad I read the comments beforehand about making a 23 gram cookie instead of 45. At 23 grams they are still a generous size! Next year I will just use my 1 1/4” cookie scoop. Otherwise, the recipe made a great, easy, fast cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 45 grams makes quite a big cookie!  Next time I will  make them significantly smaller.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn't have rum extract so put in about 3/4 T of bourbon and 3/4 T of vanilla. Yum!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These fall under the dangerously delicious category of homemade treats! I made two batches of these fantastic cookies. I made two small edits, I added  a few dashes of nutmeg to the cookie batter to drive home the spice and I portioned the cookies a bit smaller at  about 30 grams each vs. the recommended  45 which yielded me 34 cookies in the batch and a size I really like. We really adore eggnog in our household, and this cookie will be added to my annual cookie bake!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this recipe and followed the metric measurements to a tee. I personally found the cookies a bit too dry; as others have commented, the flour:butter ratio is higher than other recipes (even compared to NYT's stock snickerdoodle recipe by Samantha Seneviratne). Baked a tester and found it didn't really spread out at all, so I took a commenter's advice about slightly flattening the dough balls into pucks before baking. Would probably reduce flour by maybe 60g (or 1/2 cup) next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe. Per others recommendation i reduced flour to 2 1/2 cups. Also added cinnamon and nutmeg to dough. Slightly chewy and very delicious

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2 tbsp dark rum, add nutmeg to dough as well as sugar coating. Perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can add 1/2-1 tsp of vanilla extract

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
To paraphrase Pro from The Great British Baking Show, "These are lovely."
I made them tonight and I used Lauren's suggestion to substitute rum and add flour. They turned out wonderfully! I don't need the bourbon, but a cuppa goes well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use 2 TB rum instead of extract, increase flour by 1 TB

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After reading the comments about too much flour, I made these with 400g flour. Made 30g cookies and baked for 12 minutes. Yielded 34 cookies. Otherwise followed recipe as written. Really good! Even better the second day as others said.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love these exactly as the recipe is written. After reading other comments, I thought I might want to amp up the rum flavor.  So I made a very thin royal icing, replacing half of the egg white with rum. I drizzled that over the top of the cookies and added a tiny bit more fresh grated nutmeg. Very pretty, and it did amp up the rum flavor a little.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thanks for all the tips! These came out great. I modified as follows - 2tbs Myers dark rum instead of extract. I measured flour, sugar and butter by weight and the dough was a perfect consistency, although I threw in a scant tbs of extra flour for the rum substitution. I’ve started using organic cane sugar baking, although I used granulated for the nutmeg sugar coating - I may use the cane sugar for that too next time. Added about 2tsp of nutmeg to the dough. I made the cookies 23g of dough and 11 min of cooking time seemed perfect. I baked two dozen and froze the rest of the dough for later.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 2.5 tbsp of dark rum instead of extract. DELICIOUS!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these last year with dark rum and they were pretty good. Made them this year with Cooks rum extract, and they are amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really wonderful recipe.  They are "plush" as I rolled them in nutmeg-sugar.  They baked up with the crisp outside and creamy centers.  I used rum for the cookie and grated nutmeg into the dough; about 1/4 teaspoon.  They smell heavenly.  Thank you.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: They have a wonderful texture, and they taste good, but I don't get much of an eggnog flavor (used rum instead of rum extract).  The cream of tartar gives the "bite" of a traditional Snickerdoodle, but without cinnamon, I don't get the traditional Snickerdoodle flavor profile.  I will keep the recipe and play around with the flavorings.  (I weighed the flour and sugar and am very pleased with the texture).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After reading the comments of other reviewers concerning the rum extract, I decided to use a rum emulsion from LorAnn Oils. Wonderful results, judging by the number that are missing from the cooling rack!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: how many cookies does this make??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tried using a tablespoon and a half of dark rum and a teaspoon of vanilla instead of the rum extract. I think it worked!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these following the recipe exactly, using weights and rum extract. Holy moly. 10/10, no notes. Follow the recipe exactly, trust the process. The rum extract is not too strong and these genuinely taste like eggnog. Even better, they’re fast enough to make on a weeknight to bring to lucky coworkers tomorrow. Loved the video of Vaughn making them, too!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this cookie as written. Used McCormick rum extract. I also watched Vaughns very sweet video. (Love him!) These cookies do not disappoint. They smell and taste like eggnog. Wonderful cookie texture. Easy and fast to make. They will make it into the Xmas gift boxes this year for sure. I also froze half the dough balls to bake up fresh as needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made exactly following the recipe, I weighed the ingredients that have grams listed, and they came out great! I had never used rum extract before but I liked it! I don't typically like snickerdoodles so these were a fun change up. They were pretty quick and easy to make too. I'm always a fan of brown butter so next time I'll probably try that to compare.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I also make these with rum intead of extract and they taste so much better (I compensate for that just as others below here have suggested).
I also make my cookies half the size that is given here - so about 22 grams each and get twice as many cookies ...
When stored properly - in a well closing tin - they can last up to 2 weeks, for those who want to plan ahead.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are absolutely outrageous and going in the permanent Christmas cookie recipe stash! 

He does say in the video that the rum extract is the most important ingredient, so don’t skip it. Per other commenters,  I added some fresh nutmeg and cinnamon to the flour before adding to the wet ingredients. I made 23g balls and baked for 12 minutes. Pillowy, eggnog-y perfection!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I will be making these again as the flavor and texture are excellent, and I normally don’t care for snickerdoodles. They are soft and flavorful, as well as easy to make. I added double the amount of actual rum (not rum extract based on previous comments) but I wish it had more of a rum flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Take them out in the earlier side for chewy cookies. They go really quickly from perfect to way too crunchy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @emma I’ve made twice for cookie boxes and found storing them with a fresh flour tortilla in a Tupperware keeps them soft (if not overbaked)! Lasts about a week, change tortilla every few days.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavour is nice, though it does just taste like a regular snickerdoodle, but the dough was way too dry. As a result, the cookies did not spread much and became very hard. I wouldn’t waste your time and ingredients on this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I did 1 tbsp of almond extract and 2tsp rum extract to tone down the intense flavor of the rum extract. Still tastes eggnoggy and delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like more flavor so added 1/2 tsp nutmeg + 1/4 tsp cinnamon to the dough. Used 1 + 1/3 tbs spice rum instead of rum extract + added 1 tsp almond extract. Delicious! Next time I would weigh ingredients, or add 2-3 tbps of butter as a bit too much flour. Note: Cooking time for golf ball size was more 15-16min for me!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as directed, and they were tasty but lacked the nutmeg punch that is so indicative of eggnog.  Next time, I will add some freshly grated nutmeg to the dough as well as to the sugar coating. (I will give these another shot next year.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can these be made without a stand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I'd say no, medium high mixing for 4 minutes would be hard to duplicate.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely. People have been making snickerdoodles for generations with wooden spoons and elbow grease. There is absolutely no reason to  call for a mixer to make cookies, so long as you're able to adequately cream the butter and sugar together until they're fluffy. That step is essential for good cookie texture, but it's extremely easy to achieve with a spoon and room-temperature butter. Just make sure that your butter is room temperature!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can you use jarred nutmeg instead of freshly grated?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Brandy extract works just fine, if you happen to have that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them exactly at 45 gms and they were extremely puffy and domed.  I made one batch on a Silpat, and one on parchment and think parchment is preferable as those browned better on the bottom.  Cooked with convection at 325 and they required 15".

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is it possible to make these with actual eggnog?  I have extra in the refrigerator.  They sound yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Actual eggnog in the cookie is a recipe for disaster, unfortunately. Cookies can't accommodate much moisture. Adding a little bit of a liquid like eggnog will make them cakey; adding a lot will make them pancakes. Best to stick with flavoring extracts, which let you add a lot of flavor with very little moisture.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve been making a similar recipe for years and use both rum and brandy extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tastes exactly like eggnog! Thrilled to find a gateway cookie for all the eggnog naysayers

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone baked these after freezing the dough? If so hints?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Nearly all cookie doughs can be baked straight from the freezer with no changes other than adding a minute or two to the baking time. When I back snickerdoodles from frozen, I give them just a few minutes to defrost on the kitchen counter before rolling them in my sugar-spice mixture and baking, to help the sugar-spice mixture adhere to the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made these, but with some substitutions. I substituted AP flour for gluten free rice flour, and rum extract for white rum. In an effort to just try this I only made a half batch, but they are GOOD! Pretty crumbly but the flavor is still divine

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could these be made with a handheld mixer rather than a stand mixer?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just made these tonight with a handheld mixer - added an extra minute to creaming the butter/sugar/salt and it worked just fine. They turned out perfectly and are delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are totally killer made exactly as written! Baking two pans at the same time (rotating halfway through) worked perfectly, even in my notoriously terrible oven. This will be a new go-to holiday cookie for me—along with Grammy’s Spice Cookies, another NYT winner. Thanks for the great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Melissa, I just made these tonight (Dec. 12, 2020) with my handheld mixer - added an extra minute when creaming the butter/sugar, and it worked fine. They turned out perfect. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Instead of 2 tsp of nutmeg, I did 1/2 tsp of nutmeg and 1/2 tsp of cinnamon. I thought this was more subtle/tasty

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delicious! I will definitely be adding them to my Christmas cookie traditions!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious. Made the dough balls slightly smaller--33 g. Baked for 10 minutes in convection oven. Substituted Bacardi dark for the rum extract but the rum flavor was very subtle. Will use more rum in next batch but don't want the dough to get too wet. Great Xmas cookie! Definitely going into the cookie boxes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used Austrian Stroh Rum - it has the most intense flavour and is used in a lot of Austria-Hungarian home baking. I wish I had put nutmeg into the mixture, as the spice from the sugar is very mild (though I used freshly grated). I also thought there was just a bit too much cream of tartar in the recipe as I can really taste the chemical.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, with the exception that I only have a hand mixer - and my dough came out completely dry. Any tips?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Did you follow the cup or gram measurements? I used the cup measurements first, and the batter came out too dry (too much flour). Once I used the gram measurements, it was perfect (we used around 2.5 cups of flour). I also used a hand mixer, but I mixed the flour in manually to avoid overmixing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Similar to another poster, I used 3 tbsp of gold rum instead of extract, and another 1 tbsp of flour to compensate. I had read somewhere that 1 tsp of extract is the equivalent of 1 tbsp rum, which would convert to 6 tbsp for this recipe. I was hesitant to add that much liquid, so added half. These cookies were definitely good, but I wasn't getting eggnog or honestly rum at all. They just taste like a snickerdoodle. A good one though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The best Christmas cookie ever! Definitely double the recipe if baking for more than just yourself

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow wow wow WOW! Vaughn!! This is so good! So soft and custard-y and lovely. This recipe makes a lot (35 cookies from the batch I just made). This will be a holiday staple from here on out. Excited to share these with my co-workers tomorrow (if they last that long).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love the recipe, I used rum instead of extract as well. They do need a bit longer in the oven however, otherwise they still have a bit of raw dough taste through the middle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I added a little cinnamon to the nutmeg & sugar mixture for rolling (why not). Didn't have rum extract so poured a shot worth of rum in but I think I could've added more to really pump up the eggnog aspect of the cookie. Otherwise, a solid cookie! My first time making a snicker doodle.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were good! They are in fact better the second day. The nutmeg in the nutmeg sugar was very subtle, I agree with previous poster that they would mix some nutmeg into the dough.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as the recipe is written. I bought rum extract for this, and have already made them twice so it was worthwhile. These cookies are heavenly. The rum flavor and nutmeg make them very adult-friendly but my 4 year old is also obsessed with them. Truly a perfect holiday cookie. Will be keeping this in our rotation forever. Thank you for a new cookie tradition!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can make much smaller; can use rum if you like

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Has anyone tried making the dough and refrigerating it for a few days before baking?I think it would be fine but wanted to check and see if anyone had tried it.

I am putting together a cookie box and trying to make the dough in advance. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mmm. I too could not find rum extract, subbed in 2 T. of rum and added a little flour. Also put 1 teaspoon of fresh nutmeg into the batter. Put the dough in the fridge for the time it was resting. Rolled balls that were more like 35 g (about 1.5" diameter) and it made 4 dozen. Still baked them for about 12 min. Delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was my first cookie recipe from the NYT, and I have to say, they turned out great. I was worried about the size of the cookie (they are large), but the crisped up nicely and were golden all around. The rum flavor is nice and mixed with the nutmeg, it gives gentle eggnog flavor-not cloying or oversweet.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these to add to the Christmas boxes and to use up the yolks from the royal icing. What a hit! Having fun with all the Christmas cookies this year!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make a similar cookie with a butter-rum frosting and dust it with nutmeg after frosting.  Heavenly.  I've used gold rum and rum flavoring, wondering if a good dark rum would be even better -- might try that this weekend.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these exactly as written except made them 1/2 the size.  They are good when they come out of the oven but they are delicious the next day! It really takes them a day for the flavor to develop.  This will be a holiday tradition now!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really delicious!  This will be a rare permanent addition to the holiday cookie canon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this recipe today.  used Rum Extract, and it turned out pretty good.  Next time, will use Rum.  easy to make.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great recipe. We couldn’t stop eating the cookies. I too dropped the size to 28 grams....followed recipe exactly and cookies were dee-lush. Thank you Vaughn....

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were tasty, even with 1/2 the sugar in the batter, but for some reason the ball shaped cookies did not spread in the oven, so they are more spherical and cake-like than cookie-like.  I even tried to press down the second batch slightly, but still did not go down.  Not sure whether it's because my eggs were out for a short time, or because I used vanilla extract instead of rum.  Still good though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Did you use the cup or gram measurements? I used the cup measurements for my first batch, and my cookies did not flatten. For the second batch, I used gram measurements and they turned out perfectly! I noticed that I used ~2.5 cups for 445 grams of flour, so I think that's the main reason my first batch did not go well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made them 35 grams size,same baking time, they are really delicious .The recipe is spot on and the rum extract nice flavor, not overly rummy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are perfect in every way! A bit of holiday sweetness in every bite!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Except for subbing rum for rum extract, I followed the recipe exactly. My cookies spread like crazy! Next time I will try refrigerating the dough before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this with gluten free flour (per dietary restrictions). I made sure to fluff the butter a bit longer, as gluten free tends to be dense. Otherwise, no changes, and OMG, these are insane. Best cookies I’ve made in YEARS. People didn’t even know they were gf, the recipe is THAT good. PS - I watched the YouTube video, and it was so great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Found the cookies a bit boring but I used ground nutmeg, not fresh. The texture of the cookie was nice so I may use this for a more traditional cinnamon snickerdoodle in the future.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you only get 24 cookies out this, they are going to be HUGE!  I made cookies that are a reasonable size, 2" to 2-1/2" across, and wound up with 44.  Letting them sit for a time is key to the best flavor.  I used gold rum, and also put some allspice along with the nutmeg in the sugar mixture.  They are really tasty!  Does anyone know if they can be frozen successfully?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are a perfect Christmas cookie - we will make them every year! I used lemon hart brand 151 rum instead of extract - no regrets. Also used a #40 scoop to measure out dough balls (~27 g/each). Amazingly good and taste just like eggnog!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My cookie balls never fully settled, any tips?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were fantastic. Used rum instead of rum extract, and made the balls 23 grams instead of 45. Even four days later they were soft and chewy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
