commentBody: This is a simple and delicious cookie! I didn’t have cream of tartar on hand so I subbed out baking powder 1 to 1. I added a little espresso powder to the sugar as an alternative final coating. Perfect! Thanks for this great new addition to our holiday cookie repertoire!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are decent, but not great, and with all these great reviews I have to wonder what I’m missing?? I followed the recipe exactly. I get some rum, and some nutmeg, but not eggnog. And honestly eggnog aside, these aren’t even a great snickerdoodle. So these cookies are a big meh from me (and the rest of the household)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Of all the amazing varieties of Christmas cookies, I can’t imagine making these again. While the cookie itself bakes up nicely, is both chewy on the inside with a slight crunch, and is a very pretty cookie, the flavor is just monotone. The rum extract and nutmeg overshadow everything else. I baked a batch tonight and will be putting them in the break room at work. I’ll make something else to have at home with the family. This recipe just cannot compete with the classics!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good.  48 oz per cookie makes for a pretty big cookie.  I think 35 oz would be better.  I used butter rum flavoring and it worked great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I would recommend using one tablespoon of freshly ground nutmeg instead of two! Really delicious recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think the recipe calls for 2 teaspoons of nutmeg.  Would you still recommend halving that amount?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Agreeing with others here--I think they are a bit large, and I'll definitely reduce the size down to 25g. I also substituted the rum extract with bourbon (hey, it's how I take my eggnog)!

One other note is about doubling the recipe--I doubled mine (they are Christmas gifts for my team at work), and I went by the weight. 500g ended up being more than 4 sticks of butter, and the result was that they sort of flattened out way too much! Next time, I'll use the weight for everything but the butter).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are delightful! I too used regular rum and they turned out great. I can taste a mild rummy flavor

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 1T of dark rum.  Not enough of an egg-noggy taste.  Should have used 2T of rum, or else bought some rum extract.  But the cookies are still outstanding.  Will make again for sure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so amazing! Used 3 T. of gold rum instead of rum extract. Worked great! Definitely a redo.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 12/16/20: made w 2 T Bacardi gold. Yummy. Could try more rum or rum extract

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I needed a smaller cookie to serve with several other types of holiday cookies so I made these smaller. They weighed between 18-20grams each. I baked them for 11 minutes. The recipe made 52 cookies. I used rum extract and freshly grated nutmeg. They are delicious and I’m expecting them to be even better tomorrow. The texture is crispy and chewy!  They really taste like eggnog.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Incredible recipe! Made it exactly as written. I’ve been told by several people that this was the best cookie they’ve ever eaten!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly as written, including the rum extract. One question: my cookies spread out significantly, yielding thin and crispy edges. Still good, but any advice on how to remedy this (and achieve more of the uniform, circular look that is pictured)?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rum extract was not easy to find in 2020, but it finally turned up at Publix. Good to hear that some were successful using real rum. I wish I had made a smaller size cookie, though.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used rum rather than rum extract, and it wasn't enough. If you can't get rum extract, maybe put in at least a teaspoon of vanilla extract with your tablespoon of rum? That's what I'll try next time. The texture was divine!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: As for that quart of Eggnog, AllRecipes has a terrific Eggnog Cheesecake III, made many times, delicious! And, I somehow found a recipe for Eggnog Muffins, can't remember where so you will have Google!  Also, yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Substituted AP flour with King Arthur's Measure for Measure gluten-free flour.  Also, substituted 2 Tbsp. of Golden Rum for the rum extract.  I also made them about 1/2 the size.  They turned out fantastic!  Will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used dark rum. Needed the full 13 minutes. This is a lovely cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Did anyone else find this dough incredibly dry and pebble like? It was as if I hadn’t added the wet ingredients to the mix. I let the dough rest as directed but rehydration did not seem to occur— after an hour in the fridge it was even more crumbly. I added a bit more rum extract and then a few drops of water and finally a tbl of whole milk, then reworked the dough by hand until it resembled more of a normal cookie dough. I hope I didn’t miss something!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This happened to me on my first try. It was my first time using a stand mixer, and I ended up mixing the flour in too long. I was very careful on the next batch- Pulsed and then mixed on low speed, and stopped right when it was combined. About 20-30 seconds if I recall correctly. Thankfully the dough turned out perfect this time and I regained my confidence in baking!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
1.75 cups/222.5 grams all-purpose flour
3/4 teaspoons cream of tartar
1/2 teaspoon baking soda
1/2 cup/112 grams unsalted butter (2 sticks), at room temperature
175 grams granulated sugar, plus 1/4 cup/50 grams for rolling
0.5 teaspoon kosher salt
0.5 large egg plus 1 yolks, at room temperature
0.5 tablespoon rum extract
1 teaspoons freshly grated nutmeg

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
These are spectacular!!
Only deviations:  I used 3 TBsp of dark rum
and loaded dough with nutmeg per others’ recommendations

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing cookies. These took over 20 minutes to bake, not 10 - 13. I wonder if my butter was too cold. Regardless, they are absolutely delicious cookies. Well done, Vaughn!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 3 tbsp of rum instead of rum extract. Turned out great. Next time I may make them a little smaller.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 1 tbsp of rum, probably could’ve used more for a more eggnog-y flavor!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I made one recipe for myself with exactly these ingredients and instructions and they were perfect. I made another batch of “traditional” cookies for the rest of my family and subbed vanilla for the rum and cinnamon for the nutmeg and these were some of the best cookies I’ve ever made. This recipe is a keeper. Now for that bourbon...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as directed and they turned out great.  Really smelled of eggnog, crisp on outside and chewy on the inside.  Light spice flavor that evokes eggnog.  They don't spread much so you can squeeze a lot on the cookie sheet. These were a big hit with the family and I made extra to give as gifts.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Loved this recipe! I cheated a little & added vanilla powder & a touch of cinnamon to the coating mixture. Very delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these Christmas Eve and they are yummy. I would take the size of the cookie down to maybe 35g next time as they came out pretty big at the suggested size! I also grated some additional nutmeg on the top of the cookies after I got them on the cooling racks. Can’t wait to try them today (2nd day is supposed to be even better than right out of the oven...we shall see!)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are the best cookies ever!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I got 44 cookies out of this 24-cookie recipe. Just FYI.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I cooked mine just a tad longer until the tops were beginning to show cracking.  Otherwise they were soggy in the middle.
  These are terrific and will be a regular in my cookie repertoire.  I used regular rum instead of extract.
  Warning: These are very addictive. You can't each just one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies following the recipe as stated and they were perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookies!  I love them. I’ve always loved snickerdoodles and this recipe greatly improves on them. Amazing.  I also added cinnamon to my sugar and some vanilla extract with the rum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I couldn't find rum extract, so I bought a 50 mL bottle of Bacardi to sub in. I stirred in one tablespoon...then another...then the rest of the bottle. Perfect amount! My family all liked these cookies, but most found these are best paired with a cup of coffee, to offset the sweetness and strong nutmeg flavor. I would make them again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Searched in stores for rum extract to no avail and ended up ordering online.
I added a little cinnamon (1 tsp) to the nutmeg sugar for rolling. Delicious cookie

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were AMAZING.  Make sure to cook until they look cracked on top.  I found rum extract at Harris Teeter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Super cookie!  Made smaller cookies (got ~45), reduced baking time to ~12 minutes - they were perfect and just the right size (think diameter of a Keebler Pecan Sandie).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made this as written.  Really good!  It’s really hard to get a strong eggnog flavor with eggnog or rum.  So the rum extract definitely helps achieve that flavor profile.  I added cinnamon to the nutmeg sugar mixture.  Will make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Fair warning! Made last week (for Christmas) and they are simply addictive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Boring, didn’t rise, too hard on the next day.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly. This made good cookies, but not great. Too sweet for our taste, and as others have noted (but I failed to read), the cookies were BIG. I used “rum flavor” which I’m guessing may not be the same as rum extract. There was a bit of an artificial flavor with my end result.  As noted, the dough came together well and was easy to work with.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rum flavor and rum extract are indeed the same thing, and do indeed often have an unpleasantly artificial flavor. Unless you've found outstandingly high quality stuff, or unless you know that you enjoy the flavor of the extract, using a few tablespoons of rum is the way to go.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were delicious!!! However, I'm pretty sure there is an error in the recipe: The flour amount should be 2 1/2 rather than 3 1/2 cups (2 1/2 cups = 445 grams). We made our first batch using 3 1/2 cups, and the cookies turned out very hard and round. In our second batch, we corrected this and the cookies turned out exactly like the recipe indicated!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is an amazing recipe. Some of the best cookies I’ve ever had. Came out perfect. But I made them half the size they recommended bc I don’t like big cookies. The fresh nutmeg is wonderful. If you’re looking for a great cookie recipe, this is it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made this with rum instead of rum extract as some others did - and mine tasted like delicious but utterly regular snickerdoodles. I would make again if (and only if) my store restocks the rum extract!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is awkwardly written out. You should heat the oven first, then prepare the pans and the nutmeg-sugar, and then start the rest.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes to preheating ovens and preparing pans first.  Making the sugar mixture later in the game is done so the dough can rest and hydrate a bit, although I might be apt to do this first and just take a break for 10 minutes :-)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This might be a new favorite cookie. It’s just so well balanced. Custardy in the middle and in flavor, and using real nutmeg   the rum extract add so much to this cookie!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so delicious. I’d been meaning to make them all winter and finally did so today— worth it no matter the temperature. I even managed drop roughly 4 eggs while baking and had to settle for 1.5ish yolks and they were still amazing! 11/10, even for clumsy bakers.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I skip the cream of tartar?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These kind of tasted like Alfredo sauce! They were good though, nice fluffy light texture and flavor. They were similar to a sugar cookie. Not sure I'll need to bake them again, but I'm glad I tried them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use real rum instead of rum extract!  The extract is overwhelming and artificial tasting, use the real deal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Thank you Melissa Clark. Every recipe of yours always turns out well, including this one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. Texture, perfect. Made with a wooden spoon. Had to have my partner stir for a bit. Next time I will put some of that wonderful grated nutmeg in the batter. it seemed wasted in the sugar mixture. one could definitely use less sugar in the mixture. used rum, rather than extract. Not quite enough rum flavor. will try rum extract next time. will definitely make again. they are addictive.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Mine come out puffy, and don't flatten like the ones in the pic. What am I doing wrong? still delish cookies!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ground up a handful of nutmeg pods, which turned out to be way more nutmeg than I expected. Included 4+ T in the cookie batter, and another 4+ T in the sugar mixture. Which made the sugar mixture inedible; had to add more sugar. Was worried that I might've killed the cookies, but no!  They're delicious. Albeit a bit heavy on the nutmeg. Also used 3T of rum (not extract), and added an extra spoonful of flour to compensate. Rum flavor is subtle in the cookies, and very nice. Will do these again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good, with 1/2 t nutmeg in the dough to ensure flavor didn't just fall off. Used the two egg whites to make vanilla bean meringues with the latent heat from baking the snickerdoodles.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Amazing cookies! I weighed my ingredients and baked them for 11 minutes at 160° fan oven. I did add some freshly grated nutmeg to the dough as I was worried it would fall off the outside (never made snickerdoodles before). They are dangerously more-ish!!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tastes just like eggnog

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2021 Christmas

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was a good recipe but the cookies spread better and baked more evenly when I pressed them gently with the bottom of a glass before baking.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made a lot of changes (2 extra egg yolks, 1/2 C less flour, added 1 t vanilla, 1 t of nutmeg in the batter) and we still don't think they taste like egg nog. G. Ko's gingerbread snickerdoodles, on the other hand, are ace!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Subtle and lovely cookie! I added 1 tsp nutmeg to the dough per suggestions and made smaller cookies. I was interrupted a few times between batches and refrigerated the dough, which became easier to handle (playdough consistency) for a very uniform, round cookie in later batches.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Messed up and put the nutmeg in the cookies so rolled in sugar only.  Really good. Phew

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think it needs more nutmeg ... in the dough, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question: One the eggs, is it 1 complete egg (yolk and whites) and then 2 extra yolks?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes, that is correct.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Easy to follow recipe and absolutely delicious.   I went with the 23 gram portions and 12 minutes.   Not too much rum flavor but the texture and sweetness were perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 2 tbsp rum and 25 grams each. Weigh flour. Add cinnamon to topping

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I grabbed the almond extract instead of the rum extract - and I only realized my mistake when I tasted them - but they were really good anyway!  At some point, I'll make them as intended because I love the idea of anything that tastes like eggnog. In any case, a fun variation!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The first few times I made these they were pillowy and soft and subsequent times they were more crunchy. Any ideas on what to do to ensure a soft fluffy cookie?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these with a tbsp of bourbon because I didn't have rum extract. Mine flattened out a bit compared to the photo here, but I also didn't weigh my flour (feeling a little lazy today). They still taste great and I'm looking forward to making them again with rum extract. Definitely a great addition to my holiday cookie catalog.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I freeze these AFTER baking?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used black rum instead of extract, boiling down briefly to get rid of the alcohol and concentrate the flavors a bit. Worked fine, although I would not say the final product tastes especially boozy. Not sure if it was supposed to or not? Otherwise, only comment is that these turned out much flatter than the photo for me. I am at high altitude, so may try to adjust next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were fantastic. We couldn't believe a recipe that was this straight forward, simple enough could be this good. I used rum instead of extract, about 1.5 times the amount to get more flavor- I did think our cookies were a *bit* boozy tasting, and would be curious how they would be with extract - but overall the texture is phenomenal. Also, I let the dough chill in the fridge for an hour while I did some clean up and would do it again just because the texture was so perfect!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used the pink Zeroll scoop to make 20g balls, and the recipe yielded almost 5 dozen cookies. I made a bit more rolling sugar and nutmeg since there's more total surface area in smaller balls. I used rum emulsion and that worked nicely. I grated the nutmeg on my flat citrus rind Microplane and it made short work of grating a large amount of nutmeg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly except for using aged rum instead of extract. These cookies are amazing! I love the soft texture. I ended up doing 13 minutes and rotated half-way as instructed. I plan to make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: GREAT cookies! I used almond extract instead of rum extract and they're delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: After reading the comments here I used three tablespoons of dark rum but did not add extra flour. The recipe made enough dough for almost 48 24-gram balls. The first batch spread nicely, but after sitting on the counter the next batches barely spread at all. Flattened the last batch a bit with the bottom of a glass, but either way, the taste is divine, as is the structure, which remains chewy on the outside and soft on the inside. Bookmarking this one.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious cookies! Don't over cook them or they will be a little on the tough side. Pull them out before they brown on the bottom.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I used actual rum, Appleton Estate, as I had it on hand and it's very fragrant but any rum that's got good flavor and smell will do, even less expensive rum like Captain Morgan. 
I used 2 tablespoons of rum and didn't adjust anything else the dough was still fine. 
I like smaller cookies for a party, so I used a tablespoon to make balls and baked for 10-12min.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are divine! I was just cooking away and didn't realize that the two tsp gr nutmeg didn't go into the batter. (I mixed it in with the flour.) So, I used an extra tsp for rolling with the sugar. They DO get better the second day. These are going into the holiday rotation henceforth. A keeper. SO easy, too.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I want to use bourbon instead of rum extract - has anyone tried this? I tend to like baking with bourbon..

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Made as directed except that I cooked them a bit longer (7 extra minutes) and flattened them at minute 14.

Delicious !

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I substituted 2 Tbsp dark rum for the rum extract, and added a half Tbsp of flour to compensate for the extra liquid, though I don't know if that was strictly necessary. The flavor was wonderful, and the texture was delightful! I'm a snicker doodle fan, and these are a great variation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used 2Tbsp dark rum instead of the rum extract. I'm wondering if the cookies would've had a stronger rum taste if I had used the extract? I baked for 14 minutes in a fan-assisted oven. Texture was wonderful although taste-wise, these cookies are a bit too sweet for my taste. Even so, they are delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love this cookie but thought they were too large. Made my second batch slightly smaller and enjoyed immensely!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is worth it. I was worried when I smelled the rum extract during preparation, but it totally works in the finished cookie. Not too strong a flavor. Plus texture is amazing. Will make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Delicious, super texture (crunchy outside, soft inside as noted by others) and crackled exterior.  Added nutmeg (and cinnamon) to batter.  Combo of run and vanilla extracts.  Made 1/2 recipe with one large egg, no yolks (was worried dough was dry but turned out great).  Dough balls about 30 g (45 g would be too large IMO).  

Baked closer to 14 min.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made 4 new cookie recipes for holiday sharing and this one was the favorite by a mile!  For me, it was as close to a perfect cookie as I have ever made - texture, flavor and holiday spirit- all 10s in my book.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these twice.  The first time I thought I'd somehow messed up the recipe, or the flavor of the rum extract was off.  The second time I used bourbon instead, and cut the nutmeg in half. I couldn't eat either version. I wish I'd just stuck to old fashioned snickerdoodles.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone frozen these for storage?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes.  Worked really well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think next time I’ll try regular rum the extract is a little too sweet

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly.  Dough was a little dry and crumbly, and they looked weird & lumpy while baking, but the final result was delicious. Great texture & unique flavor. Adding this to my regular Christmas cookie rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Although tasty these are more of just sugar cookies with nutmeg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make cookies 20 grams. Add nutmeg to dough. Double rum extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make cookies 20 grams each. Add about 1/2 teaspoon nutmeg to dough. Double rum extract. Add a bit of cinnamon to rolling sugar. Double nutmeg in rolling sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very tasty!  followed recipe with the exception of using dark rum instead of extract and throwing 1/2 tsp of nutmeg in with the flour and making 23gm cookies (as suggested by others).  Will definitely be baking these again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve baked these 3x pretty much as written except with 2 tbsp rum. I let them sit in fridge overnight for flavors to come together. They always come out well - chewy with just a hint of rum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These tasted exactly like a carton of eggnog from the grocery store - delicious! The rum extract and nutmeg really worked for me. Texture was great too - soft and lovely!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This is an excellent recipe for using gluten-free flour. I use Krusteaz GF flour and King Arthur GF flour. Important to let the cookies cool and get set up on the baking sheet before removing to cooling rack, the gf flour seems to like the rest, less fally-aparty. 
Also use Crisco, not butter, for the lactose-intolerant people. This recipe works great for that.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Everyone wants these cookies or the recipe after they eat them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were great! Had to make a couple of modifications only due to lack of ingredients, 2 egg whites instead of 2 egg yolks and 2tsp of baking powder in place of the cream of tarter. Fortunately the subs didn’t affect the bake - the cookies are a huge hit and I think the fresh grated nutmeg makes all the difference. Rum extract does smell awful but I didn’t notice any bizarre taste or smell in the cookies. We’ll make these again for sure!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A tender and comforting cookie. I asked my wife if she wanted me to make these cookies, and she no because she thought they sounded boring. I made them any way, and guess what. My wife ended up eating more than anybody! They are delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I set the oven temp to 360 and set the timer for 13 minutes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I followed the recipe as directed and they tasted great, but the cookies didn’t spread out. They were just puffed up balls. Any idea why?

I did let the dough hydrate for 10 minutes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn’t have rum extract, so I used 2T. bourbon. Didn’t taste the bourbon at all in the cookie. Also, like others recommended, put 1/2tsp. Nutmeg in the dough. Then, still rolled the balls in 2tsp nutmeg and 1/2tsp cinnamon in the sugar. Still, with all of that nutmeg I wanted more after they baked so I sprinkled some more they came out of the oven. I also formed the balls to 24g, and baked at 350° for 14-15min. They’re good, nice chew, and so far everyone likes them. I’m just not wowed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these as written and baked 14 minutes. They are delicious and another big hit!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Add 2 tbsp rum, measure flour, make 23 grams, cook 9.5 minutes

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Phenomenal cookies! They have an unassuming look that doesn't scream "pick me!" from a cookie assortment plate, but once tasted they disappear quickly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Incredible. Great work Vaughan!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these exactly as written and they were incredible. Tastes exactly like egg nog in crispy-chewy cookie form. The prominent flavors are the nutmeg and the rum extract, so I would recommend buying a good quality rum extract. I think I will be making these every year for now on.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are amazing! Weighed out smaller cookies at 27 g/1 oz each and baked for 9 mins (check at 8). Rum extract was just fine. Fresh grated nutmeg a must! A new favorite for our holiday cookie tray.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To weigh in on the question of subbing (more) rum for rum extract… nope, couldn’t discern it in the final cookie, order some extract.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I took others advice and added some nutmeg to the dough as well as in the sugar.  I made smaller cookies and still had sugar mixture left over.  (I just combined it with cinnamon sugar and saved it for breakfast toast). 
This will go in the rotation for holiday boxes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Soft, pillowy and egg-noggy! Really nice cookies. I made them all about 30g; better size for us and gave us 36 cookies or so. I'd make less sugar mix next time, just because I had a lot left over.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Overall very tasty but using 3/4 or even half of the sugar next time. That much sugar in the cookie plus the sugar coating is too sweet. I think I will also put nutmeg next time as well. Definitely making again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so much better than regular snickerdoodles, which I find unexciting.  I  used plain dark rum, 2 tablespoons, and they were quite good.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe in crease the rum slightly and add some nutMeg to the dough itself

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Is this recipe text? 3 1:2 cups of flour? My cookies came out more like a biscuit than a snickerdoodle….

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Didn’t have dark rum, so substituted a nice tequila instead.  23g balls (as suggested by an earlier post) for Christmas sharing.  The only problem is my husband scarfing them down straight off the cooking rack.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Could these be made with Grand Marnier as a substitute for the rum?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
It was good to read comments about using rum instead of rum extract. I'm not opposed to the extract for occasional use, but haven't seen it in grocery markets in several years (yes, I've looked and asked). 

I live in the northern Great Lakes Region.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Why not use real eggnog?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have not made these, but will this week. Why can't you use a couple of Tbsp of SoCo Eggnog?? Instead of the rum or extract??

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent! I weighed the ingredients and had no trouble with the dough. I don’t like the taste of extract, so I used 2 tablespoons of Bacardi’s rum in the dough and, based on on other comments, put ground nutmeg into the dough too (I couldn’t find fresh). They are tasty!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very nice with a soft texture. I used rum and more of it. I also added nutmeg to the dough because I really like nutmeg. These cookies are quite pale — don't overbake. The texture is lovely — I may experiment with other flavors such as orange and tea.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made with gluten free flour, subbed half the sugar with erythritol, and lacking rum extract used 3 tablespoons of dark rum. Nice texture, easy to make, and delicious. Thank you for a great recipe!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So good! I got a mild eggnog flavor but the cookie was fantastic! Gosh do I love my snickerdoodles!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Cookies were huge and kids mostly chose other cookies before these, o they got stale. They weren’t bad… but actual snickerdoodles are better. If I make again—make them much smaller

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I thought I had left comments a year ago, but I don't see them. This is a GREAT recipe, made all the better by Vaughn's presence and narration. I did make two small changes, 1)subbing out 1/2 coconut oil for the butter per a Stella Park Bravetart recommendation for Snickerdoodles; and 2) using bakers ammonia in lieu of baking powder to increase puffiness and crisp edges.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these smaller, as someone else suggested, and I got five dozen perfectly shaped cookies. Easy recipe and easy dough to work with. But, tasteless - maybe bc I subbed rum (1.5 Tbs) for the 1 Tb of rum extract. But not even a classic snickerdoodle taste. And I used good unsalted butter!! Still good, nice texture  - my husband likes them - but I was disappointed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: huge hit at my office holiday party! added cardamom to the nutmeg-sugar mixture

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make these every holiday season without fail! Easy, reliable recipe, and always a hit!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly per recipe. Hate to disagree with all the five-star reviews, but I didn't care for these. To me they taste like a sugar cookie with a strong artificial alcohol backdrop. Perhaps they would be better with dark rum vs rum extract? Will not make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Great soft cookie with the crisp sugar/nutmeg coating outside.   I made a batch with the recommended 45-gram size, but like others found it to be too large for European expectations.   The rest I made in 32-33-g-sized cookes and they were perfect.   I had no access to rum extract, only "rum essence" (whatever that is), but used it in the same amount as the extract called for in the recipe, and it seemed a little low on the rum flavor.   Tastes like a combination of eggnog & snickerdoodles.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: #30 cookie scoop. Makes about 26

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These came out very well cooked as directed. Not sure I am a fan of the flavor so I'll make regular snickerdoodles next - I missed my childhood favorite!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious. I was a bit cautious of the 45 gram size—smaller Christmas cookies, more servings— but I tried a pan of six at that size, then switched to 13-gram. Large cookies got 14 minutes from me and had almost no color on the bottom, but the texture was perfect when cooled. He smaller cookies needed less time. I baked one pan a bit longer to get some slight browning on the bottom but ended up preferring them pale— that creamy texture!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Measured everything in grams. Added ~1/2tsp nutmeg and a dash of cinnamon to flour mixture. Substituted rum extract with 1tbsp navy strength rum and 1 tbsp Meyer’s dark rum. May have had more than 2tsp fresh nutmeg, hard to measure. Made 25-30g cookies. Baked @ 350 for 10 minutes each. Let sit on pan for 3 minutes then to cooling rack. Made total of 42 cookies. Excellent cookies, very soft in the middle, hint of crunch at the edges. Good flavor with rum and nutmeg, my boys love them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I didn’t have run extract, so I used a generous tablespoon of high quality rum.  We couldn’t taste any rum flavor.  Overall, these were a disappointment.  I significantly increased the spice and they were still bland.  

I agree with the comments that if you are going to share these for the holidays, make them much smaller than the recipe indicates.  I’m going to delete this from my saved cookie recipes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Inspired by a coworker, who makes homemade coquito every year, I made a coquito snickerdoodle. Did about 2/3 of the amount of rum extract, and remaining 1/3 amount of coconut extract. Did half nutmeg, half cinnamon with the sugar for the coating.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made these cookies substituting the rum extract for vanilla and incorporating 1/2 tsp. fresh ground nutmeg into the cookie dough. Chose to make smaller cookies and adjusted bake time appropriately. Absolutely better the next day!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were so good I shared the recipe with my mother, the Cookie Queen, who does not deviate from her prized and cherished holiday cookie staples often. I’ll likely try this some other time with nutmeg in the dough itself (I love a heavily spiced cookie). “Golf ball sized” was too big for the cooking time mentioned and made gigantic cookies. I reduced the second round to somewhere around the size of a ping-pong ball (slightly larger than an inch in diameter), which suited me better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I really loved the base of this recipe and will use it for soft and chewy sugar cookies going forward.  I made the recipe pretty close to “as written” (I made my cookies 40g) and they needed 11 minutes to cook.   The rum extract was absolutely disgusting and ruined the cookie for me but I think the dough can handle extra liquid from actual rum.   I will also add nutmeg and cinnamon to the dough next time - it was very subtly spiced as is.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used the rum extract in quantity listed and it is *strong* - the taste is not child friendly, and honestly a bit sharp even for me. I am not one for holding back a bit of cooking booze from my kids, be it in desserts or sauces, but in this instance the smell and taste turns them off before first bite.  Quite disappointed.  Perhaps using real rum, maybe boiled down, would be better?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so tasty! I followed one commenter’s advice and used 2T of rum   1 additional T of flour. The flavors and aromas of this cookie really perked up this cook and made the kitchen smell great 😊

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added a dash of nutmeg to the batter and made the cookies much smaller - a 2tsp scoop made cookies a perfect size for sharing. Baked for 12 minutes. Rum extract was impossible to find but vanilla didn't disappoint!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely better the next day, after the rum has a chance to mellow. Very good and surprisingly noggy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Something went wrong for me with these. The dough ended up dry and more like pie dough than cookie dough. Then they didn't spread at all. I added some half and half but still no avail. I used two sticks of butter, but they didn't weigh the amount needed. Any suggestions?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These turned out beautifully! I accidentally put the nutmeg in the cookie, but it still tasted incredible. I ended up making 30 g cookies and I felt like the size was perfect and I was able to get a few more cookies to share!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I make these and freeze the "balls" for later. Such a wonderful cookie

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The amount of flour in this is way too high. The consistency is completely off. I made them twice to make sure I didn’t make a mistake. Would not make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: McCormick Rum Extract says 1/2 tsp. equals 1 Tbsp. Light Rum, so using the Tbsp of extract it’s like 6 Tbsp of Rum!  It is flavorful and delicious!  If only adding one to two tablespoons of real rum, it’s not nearly there.  I made them exactly as directed, weighed in grams, and they were perfect.  If dough is dry,  ensure your eggs weigh enough.  Do not overbake, they do not color much and will be quite delicate and undone looking inside the cracked crust. Sitting on the tray a bit sets them.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed the advice to measure flour by grams and the cookies turned out perfectly. I added a couple shakes of nutmeg into the batter. I don’t know if that made much of a difference, but they were delicious. These are my new favorite Christmas cookies.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Halved the recipe with just one egg and no yolk but it came out well. Never used rum extract before but bought it for this recipe - it smelled pretty funky when baking and I now get comments saying it’s disgusting. Thankfully the cookies are still delicious - fun holiday flavor, bonus points for being an easy, quick recipe

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and could not be more pleased. This recipe is now being added to our family's Christmas traditions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I whipped a batch of these this morning and they are simply lovely. They bake up pretty as a picture and they are just delicious. I have never used rum extract for anything before, but the cookies are great, so I have no quarrel with it. A wonderful addition to my cookie boxes this year. :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The flavor on these is great. I just substituted bourbon and that worked fine. I do think maybe it’s a bit too much flour. They don’t spread at all and look like balls when cooking is done. I smashed the second batch before baking and that worked better.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as written multiple times. My favorite holiday cookie, I like putting a little nutmeg in the batter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I baked these this afternoon and was quite disappointed at the lack of "eggnog" flavor. The rum flavor was barely distinguishable as was the nutmeg. As a snickerdoodle it was delicious but not as one with an eggnog flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I followed one reader’s suggestion to cut the flour to 2.5C, and thereby produced a wholly different dessert, the Snickerdilla!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Package separately. The rum extract will make all the other cookies taste like these. It’s very potent.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Dance 10, Looks 3 . . . DELICIOUS! I’m celiac so made w/ gluten free (Bob’s Red Mill) 1:1 flour exchange and vanilla extract instead of rum (had on hand). Added nutmeg (1 tsp) to the batter and doubled sugar/nutmeg rolling amount as other cooks suggested. GF flour likely made these spread out thinner than photo. Perfect taste and I’d make again, but rather plain looking compared to other holiday cookies at the exchange.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these exactly to the recipe and they were wonderful!  I definitely will make them again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: To sweet for me - next time I'll reduce the sugar significantly and add nutmeg to the batter as well. I like how others are making smaller cookies - less chance of burning on the bottom yet cooking all the way through.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly as written though 2 tweaks: 1) somewhat larger cookies. 2) Once rolled, I placed in the fridge for about 10 mins before baking. Debated buying rum but opted for extract - Rum extract is what truly brings this cookie together. Baked for 11.5 mins and they were perfect. Bonus if you dip egg nog cookies in egg nog!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Smaller / more spaced out than 45g! 
Also didn’t have enough rum flavour

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found this to be a very greedy recipe in terms of ingredients, and so I had high hopes for cookies that had that much butter and flour. Was sadly disappointed, even after waiting the next day (and several days after) as suggested. I tried to love these cookies, but they're just "meh" for me. Won't be making this one again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t love these - neither the texture nor the taste - and I’m surprised they have such a high rating. First off, I don’t know if the cookies are underbaked but they certainly taste that way, and I definitely left them in until they were “just set”. Secondly, the flavor the first day had a strong aftertaste of flour, but the rum and nutmeg mixture was great. Contrary to the recipe, the flavor has mellowed quite a bit the 2nd day and they taste like plain snickerdoodles with a hint of nutmeg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Reporting live from Christmas Eve...made these around noon...it's 11 PM...mostly gone and the Jolly Old Elf took a big bite out of the one we left by the fireplace....yummy. Would be great for a Christmas bake sale

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
The rum extract is… not great. I made these a second time with 3tbsp bourbon in its place, and they are fantastic! 
Also worth noting that I’m in California and the dough was suuuuuper dry when made as the recipe is written. The added liquid from the bourbon helped a bit with this as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! And I am not usually a big snickerdoodle fan.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: In case I haven't already commented here, these are the best Christmas cookies EVER!  They're always a surprise hit to those whom we share them with.  I tend to make 26 gram balls, which yields more cookies! I use a combo of brandy and vanilla extract since that's what's usually on hand.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Used 2 tbsp rum, not enough flavor. Grated entire nutmeg into and on top.....very good. Got better after they set.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Straight out of the oven and warm? Heaven.

Accidentally made too much of the sugar and spice mixture, so sprinkled a bit on top of each cookie, just as they were coming out of the oven. Not necessarily recommended, but sure was yummy!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Use just a teaspoon of rum flavor. Add vanilla.  Roll in nutmeg mix with some cinnamon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Added 1 tsp of nutmeg and cinnamon to the batter, three tbs of dark rum as sub for rum extract, and an extra 1.5 tbs of flour to compensate

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made exactly as written the first time, and they were delicious.  The second time, I added 2 tsp of nutmeg to the dough in addition to the nutmeg-sugar combination for rolling the cookies (we really like nutmeg).  I also added 1 tsp of dark rum in addition to the rum extract.  Used the smallest cookie scoop in the set.  Baked for 10 minutes.  Seriously amazing.  My husband's colleague said he'd propose to any woman who baked these for him.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I prefer the original cinnamon snickerdoodles.  The nutmeg is muted.  I love cardamom and will try snickerdoodles with that next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are now my all-time favorite cookies. I used rum emulsion, rather than extract, and I put the nutmeg directly in the recipe. I also didn’t roll them in sugar. Everyone who has tasted these has absolutely loved them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Honestly baller cookie.  The texture so soft and pillowy, the little bit of sugar dusting on the outside offering the slightest sweet crunch.  That first bite is a slight crisp break that transitions to a melt in your mouth pillow of eggnoggy decadence.  Every last crumb that dissolves on your tongue leaves you searching, yearning for more.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made the recipe as written and they were delicious! They had a distinct egg nog flavor. Whole nutmeg was very expensive, so I used a fresh jar of ground nutmeg. The only issue was that my little kids did not like them. I may just dust half the batch with cinnamon-sugar instead of nutmeg-sugar for them next time to see if that mutes the flavor enough for kids. The adults loved the egg nog taste though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Maybe 10-11 mins better? 
Stay nice and light colored

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is excellent. Made exactly as is and it was absolutely perfect. 10/10!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I have made these 7 times, with no adjustment to the recipe other than I use a high-quality, local salted butter.  I think the extra net-salt-amount is a plus.  Used McCormick rum extract.  I live overseas and have to import the cream of tartar and rum extract in my luggage - specifically for this cookie!   Makes a great cookie

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The dough was dry and the cookies didn't flatten out.  The flavor is great though

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious! I made with about 4T of light rum (what I had on hand) and they were good. I also made the cookies smaller than they suggested - about 1 T. or a bit smaller, probably closer to 25-30 grams than 45. Baked for 10 min. I wish the directions had you mix up the nutmeg sugar first because that would prevent you from throwing the whole 1-3/4 c. in the mix, which I did. Not a fatal mistake but frustrating. Why does the NY Times do this all the time?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love these cookies.  I make them over and over again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is my first nytimes comment despite years of reading everyone else’s (thank you). The cookie dough for this recipe was such a failure which surprised me based on the reviews. I followed the recipe exactly and the dough was dry and unworkable, even after the 10 min rest. Once I searched for “dry” I was not the only one with this problem. I measured ingredients using cups (not weights) and it was way too much flour. I rescued it with 2T of water and a splash of milk at the end. The rescued dough was okay. Didn’t fully spread into snickerdoodles. Overall disappointing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @DJ I don’t think its fair to give them an overall rating when the execution (even if it wasn’t your fault) was off. I would have added whole milk or cream instead of water to loosen them, and definitely all the extra mixing developed some gluten that would keep them from spreading.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I am sorry that you had that issue.  I bought a scale to make sure that the "weighing" did not throw the balance off.  Before I bought the scale, I had problems with the dip and sweep.  This is a great recipe.  My family, who generally dislike anything but chocolate recipes, really enjoyed the texture and flavor.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
My dad said they’re the best snickerdoodles he’s ever had. Followed suggestions and decided to lightly brown the butter (turned off heat about 30 seconds after browning began), weighed the butter and added enough brandy (my preferred liquor for eggnog) to get back to 225 g. 
Would highly recommend weighing your flour, as I ended up with a lot more when measuring by volume than going by weight, so I’m glad I double-checked. Added maybe 1/4 tsp of nutmeg to the dough and rolled the cookies in 1 tsp of nutmeg   1 tsp of cinnamon for a more traditional snickerdoodle vibe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made them just as written, and they were amazing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Some reviews state dough is dry, so don’t add any.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yes. You should make these.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: made them gluten free with the KAF measure for measure flour and they turned out amazing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious though the baking time was off for me. I rested the dough in the fridge so they were underbaked when taken out at 13 minutes. I should have factored in refrigeration, but the cookies were puffed and cracked on the surface at 13 minutes so I couldn’t tell that they weren’t done. After they were almost cool and I’d tested one, I put them back in for 5 minutes. I also reduced 5 tablespoons of regular white rum to one tablespoon for the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think in reading these comments I’ll give fat washing some rum with butter and then use that butter in this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I did 3 cups flour instead of 3.5 cups, then also added 1 tbsp of half-and-half so the dough was a bit more sticky for the sugar coating. 

For the coating I added 2 tsp cinnamon to the sugar and nutmeg. 

These cookies were excellent, they taste just like eggnog!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I actually like the flavor of the rum extract in this recipe, lol.  To each his own.  I also added some of the grated nutmeg into the cookie batter, and added some pumpkin pie spice in with the sugar coating.  Delicious and easy to make, and makes A LOT of cookies!  (I made the cookie balls the size of small walnuts and partially flattened them on the cookie sheet.)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Liked these, but where I live (high altitude, dry climate) they were too dry and didn't flatten out. If I make them again will reduce the flower. Maybe use actual rum as well.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Maybe it’s just me (not a fan of snickerdoodles in general), but my family and I found these very “meh.”  I mostly made this because Vaughn Vreeland is adorable and we love his almond rum butter cookies, and this used the extra egg yolks from that recipe perfectly.  But next time, I will save them for a custard (or for actual eggnog!).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made these cookies multiple times since they debuted on this site, and they are wonderful. The dough is a dream to work with, they taste great made exactly as the recipe is written, and they make a TON of cookies if you make them on the smaller size. Perfect for holiday cookie boxes.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I sub the rum for bourbon and it's great! It really does taste like eggnog!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This are excellent. Used TJs bourbon/vanilla extract and gf flour. I’ll say that these are best eaten day of or day after - they can get stale after a couple of days.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are wonderful! The batter was easy to work with…nice and soft inside with a sugar crust.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were excellent! I misread the recipe and added nutmeg to the batter - still tasty. Next time I might put half of the nutmeg in the batter and half in the sugar mixture. I will also likely weigh the flour next time, my dough seemed a bit stiff though the final texture was good. After I made these I made the rum-buttered almond cookies, which use of the leftover egg whites from this recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Watched the charming video and made these as directed except with jarred nutmeg for the rolling -  I’m sure fresh is better but these didn’t suffer. Delicious, will make again!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tried the method of browning a small portion of the butter and adding 2 T rum to it, then gentle simmer to evaporate the alcohol. Then I chilled that browned butter cooler, whipped it with cooler butter, and proceeded with the recipe. It whipped nicely.  I also added some ground nutmeg to the batter as others had suggested.  The cookies were a disaster.  They completely spread out, and had too strong a nutmeg taste and also had a greasy texture. It was probably baker's error.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Having to make a second batch now because I ate one for “quality control purposes” 😅 before taking these to Christmas Eve tomorrow and -oops!- ended up eating 3 of them. They are simply amazing. Change nothing, make exactly as directed and they will not disappoint!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Followed the recipe as written. Friends could not believe there was no actual eggnog in this cookie. Another holiday winner.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are great! I used 1 Tablespoon rum and 1 Tablespoon Kahlua instead of extract. I did not add extra flour as the dough was pretty stiff. I rolled it in coarse sugar and Trader Joe’s Cocoa Cinnamon Sugar blend (which has nutmeg in it).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Mine did not spread 
They were hard and tasteless. Not sure what happened. I followed the recipe exactly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Excellent! In my regular rotation now. Great flavor, great consistency. I’ll make these again and again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I wish I would’ve read the comments more carefully before making these. I agree that it’s way too much rum extract. The extract is overwhelming (drowning out any eggnog flavor), and tastes fake.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made as written. Very good. Will keep in the Christmas rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Really great cookie. Made a half batch with 1-to-1 gluten free flour, weighed  (Better Batter). Followed reviewers suggestion to make 23g cookies (basic 1T scoop). Baking at high altitude, so increased oven temp to 160, and baked second batch for 12-13 minutes because 10 minutes made for a very soft cookie. Rolled a few of the cookies in sprinkles for 6-year-old grand daughters.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Baked these using 3 1/2 cups of flour. Way too cakey and they barely spread out at all. Afterward, I ended up weighing 3 1/2 cups of the all-purpose flour I use and it’s 523 g. Way off from what the recipe calls for. Moral of the story is don’t trust volume measurements. I will be trying this recipe again using the measurements in grams.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Only gave it 4 stars because I found the artificial aftertaste of the rum extract to be unpleasant ( used Cook’s brand). But my extended family raved about them and requested the recipe. So perhaps there’s a better extract out there? Please send recommendations.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are great, but I think a little heavy on the rum extract. Next time I’d make with real eggnog and maybe a tiny splash of the extract. Texture was perfect.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: lol I’m a teenager and I made this for a Christmas party and everyone loved it! I found that it still tastes really good if you add bourbon extract instead of rum, if that helps anyone! 💖

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of the new family favorites year after year.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Any alternative to butter? I love it but it doesn’t love me

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Made with browned butter and 3 tablespoons rum.  They didn’t flatten as much as I expected but they taste great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Next time will make more, smaller cookies.  But they tasted good.  I did have to bake them for 15 minutes because of my oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Has anyone tried these with less sugar (less sweet)? Did it work?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are SO delicious! I made exactly as written, just smaller (about 24g balls of dough) because I needed a bunch for a cookie swap. Rum extract worked well and didn’t taste at all artificial. I love that they can be whipped up quickly and the dough doesn’t require chilling.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Wow, I will forever weigh my flour when making snickerdoodles- mine usually end up cakes, but these were perfect and chewy. Instead of rum extract, I used bourbon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Be careful not to over bake! They seemed underdone when I took them out, but if you leave them in till they’re more than “just set” they lose their chewiness.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tasty but not eggnoggy

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies were given to me in a cookie exchange and I LOVED them!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are so good! I love seeing people’s eyes light up when I say they are EGGNOG snickerdoodles, and then watching them sigh after taking the first bite. This is cookie nirvana.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Very good as a straight pillowy snickerdoodle, but not eggnog forward at all

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Family are egg nog lovers and really enjoyed, made as written except added some nutme to batter and made about 35 gr, still a big cookie.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 10/10. I veganized these, using Bob's egg replacer for the egg and sunflower lecithin for the egg yolks (my first time trying something like that) and vegan butter. It turned out GREAT. My oven is chaotic and fluctuates in temperature (endlessly annoying), but even with that, these came out great. I think the recipe must be pretty forgiving. It's certainly delicious!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I found these lacking flavor and I followed the recipe to the letter, using a scale for all measurements.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I decided to do a test batch with rum extract because a small bottle of dark rum was quite pricey. But I agree with others who don’t think it tastes good. It adds an odd flavor—kind of synthetic and hard to pinpoint if you don’t know it’s rum.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these after reading about 40 comments.  I adjusted the dough with nutmeg as suggested and I used a scale to measure all ingredients.  Not much flavor to these.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This is a riff on an old recipe my family has been making for years called "Nutmeg Noel's". I used vanilla instead of rum because it's what I had. I also used a bit of dye free plant based red sanding sugar to give them a sparkle for the kiddos! Highly recommend!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I've tried this recipe twice. I wasn't successful with the first batch. I read in the comments that there was too much flower. The second time, instead of using measuring cups, I used my scale and measured the flour exactly to the recipe. AGAIN, they did not flatten. This is a dud.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I threw a teaspoon of nutmeg and an extra 1/2 tablespoon of rum extract into the batter, and I made the cookie balls 1/2 the size of a golfball.  Aside from that - made it as described (except the whole turning of the cookie sheets - silly).  Good cookies!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I used both rum extract and dark rum, and these still just taste like regular snickerdoodles to me. Tasty, but basic. I added fresh nutmeg and cinnamon to the batter per comments. I’m glad I at least did that. Made all cookies between 23 and 27 g and they baked perfectly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I make the dough in advance of baking.  Say, a week at most?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Freshly grated nutmeg was overpowering to me so I made the rolling sugar half nutmeg half cinnamon.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These were incredible! I brought them to a Christmas party and they were a hit. I saw the notes about how the flavor wasn't pronounced enough, so I added 2 teaspoons of nutmeg into the cookie dough and doubled the rum and added a teaspoon of vanilla. I did a test cookie without and with the addition and I liked it better with the additions.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I made these as written and they were a huge hit. They were everyone’s favorite cookie in my cookie boxes this year.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I love me a Vaughn Vreeland cookie recipe. These turned out delicious, but I made a few small changes: I made them smaller, added nutmeg to the dough along with ½ teaspoon of vanilla, mixed red sanding sugar into the rolling sugar, and skipped rotating the pans.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Just made these. I had fresh rum extract and brand new whole nutmeg to freshly grate. I put an extra teaspoon of not made in the batter per the suggestion of somebody’s comment, and I added an extra tablespoon of rum to the batter in addition to an entire tablespoon of rum extra extract.  

While the cookies are lovely in texture and baked perfectly, to me they taste just like a good sugar cookie-definitely not an eggnog cookie. I’m disappointed and wished I had added some cardamom, some ginger and doubled the nutmeg.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I adjusted the recipe to make 3 dozen cookies for a cookie exchange.  That left me with little space in the mixing bowl of my vintage stand mixer but the cookies came out so well and they really taste like eggnog.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Lovely cookie. Didn’t have rum extract so I used bourbon, also popped them in the fridge for 15 minutes before baking to limit spreading— they turned out great.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies are simply divine. I didn't have fresh nutmeg, but even with the packaged variety these are still 10/10.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: One of my favorite cookies over the past year. I’d make smaller cookies next time for a xmas cookie. They are too big when you have a large assortment of cookies. Also, who is ready to ban ozempic users from the cookie bar? They take a nibble and toss the rest of the cookie. #wastefulsinner

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: VERY good! Used pre-ground nutmeg, did 1 tsp  nutmeg and 1 tsp cinnamon for rolling, and did 1 tsp rum extract, 1 tsp eggnog liqueur (eggnog, rum, and brandy), and 1/2 tsp vanilla bean paste. Definitely adding to my cookie boxes next year.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Yummy with some modifications! I added extra nutmeg (I measured with my heart when I added extra so not exactly sure how much) and for next time think I would use dark rum instead of extract- the whole tablespoon used left a slight artificial taste at the end of my palate I could have went without! One other commenter mentioned lightly browning the butter to evaporate some moisture allowing for extra rum to be added and I think I’ll try that next time!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: That video is priceless!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: A delicious variation on my favourite cookie. Will bake these every December. I added some cinnamon to the nutmeg rolling sugar which was nice.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These are seriously some of my favorite cookies ever. My family had be make them three times over the holidays. The are soft flavorful and easy to make. I followed the recipe but did adjust cooking time for my oven.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I highly recommend Mount Hagen organic instant coffee for this recipe. It has fruit and chocolate notes and is very smooth and non-bitter. I used 2 TB coffee and one TB sugar and it got thick and creamy, maybe not as foamy as it’s supposed to be but it was absolutely delicious, albeit more caffeine  than I would usually drink. Perhaps I will try with just one TB of coffee next time as my face is tingling as I type this.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: @Juliette they also make a decaf variety that is surprisingly decent as well, if you don’t want to get all tingly.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
I studied in England during college. After a long night of studying a student asked if he could make me coffee. I jumped on the offer. The student was an exchange student from Greece. I was anticipating a great cup of coffee. I could not help but cringe when I saw him take out the instant coffee crystals. 

My mistake! It was the Greece version of whipped coffee- and absolutely delicious.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
That is EXACTLY how I learned about it.  First year in Miami (years ago!), S. American friend pulls out the instant coffee. Me (internally, horrified): "Noooooo...." 
I had forgotten all about those coffees until seeing this article!  Just whipped one up! Memories!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It is VERY strong. You could easily half the coffee amount. That said, mix with ice and milk and you’d have a Tim Horton’s Ice Cap.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: nothing beats Tim's!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This was fun to make & drink.  My 13-year old and I shared it. The recipe is STRONG, but it seems you could make it with 1/2 the coffee and sugar and all of the water without a problem.  I used Cafe Listo - a Salvadoran instant coffee that, to my taste, is the best on the market.  I poured it over a 1/2 milk and 1/2 hot water in a 12 oz glass.  I used an aerolatte - best 15 bucks I spent 8 years ago - to froth it up.  Next time, I may pour it over ice cream: affogato hack.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: "Affogato hack"--I like that and may well try it. I was using the dalgona recipe from some other site, but this sounds more appealing with the slightly lower amount of sugar.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: For reviewers complaining about the taste - the key is to *combine* with the milk before drinking. It will take on a frappe effect and be far less bitter.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I also felt that there was too much coffee in here, and I’m generally a strong black coffee only kinda guy - except when I want to make something pretty to impress my wife ;-). After playing around a bit, I landed on 1tbs coffee, 1tbs sugar, and 2tbs water. Simple, easy, and tastes and looks great!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I use Medaglia d’Oro instant espresso, and I use two tablespoons each of sugar, coffee and hot water. With an immersion blender, it’s so easy and amazing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We used to make this growing up in India! Of course now it’s a trendy drink in the US just like turmeric ghee “lattes” and the like which our moms used to force us to drink...Glad Americans are finally “discovering” what we’ve been doing in India for ages. Because of course it doesn’t exist as a thing unless Americans are commercializing it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I topped mine off with some cold brew and regretted nothing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Love this  - my summer drink. It's imperative to use the instant coffee - I tried a other methods, it's just...no. As someone mentioned, I have halved the recipe and it works well. I have tried on flax, almond, and oat milk. All worked great. My current choice is chocolate oat milk! (I do about 1/2 tbsp less sugar if I use sugared milk). I've read other reviews which stated the bowl material is important, I use a stainless steel bowl & hand whisk, not as frothy but works (hate washing beaters!).

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made “pheti hui” coffee all my life. I suggest 1 slightly rounded tsp of instant coffee and less than 1 tsp of sugar per 8 oz cup. Whip with small amt of water or milk until very LIGHT IN COLOR. Divide equally into desired number cups. Add approx 2-3 Tbsp of v hot milk into each cup and mix well. Add rest of the very hot milk slowly in each cup, while stirring v gently. This makes well-mixed & heavenly coffee with foam intact on top. At least 2 servings are needed to make it frothy.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 1tbsp coffee, 1tbsp + 1tsp sugar - for two coffees.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Just the antidote to groundhog days! Delicious treat. I found it necessary to whip this using a whisk attachment on a hand mixer. Wasn’t able to do it by hand.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Tasty, fast and fun to make. I had some very dark instant espresso powder so I upped the sugar slightly. I generally get my coffee drinks in 8-10 oz. sizes as opposed to the 16-32 oz. you might find in a typical coffee chain. There was a good 1 cup of whipped coffee and that divides nicely into two drinks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My daughter made this with PERO, an instant caffeine-free beverage powder made from grains. It’s dark brown and tastes vaguely similar to coffee. We didn’t have any instant coffee. It was really delicious! The next day her arm was sore from 7 minutes of vigorous whisking. But hey, the gyms are all closed, so why not get your workout in the kitchen?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: We've found that a 4:1 ratio of hot water to instant coffee crystals yields the best strength and taste for the foam. So, 1/4 cup water for 1 T of crystals.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: An immersion blender works great in place of a hand mixer or (gasp!) trying to whip this by hand. This coffee drink is very strong as is; I typically will divide the drink in half and add milk to the half I’m drinking at the moment. But if you are a fan of very strong coffee, leave it as is! Happy caffeination, friends.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I just made this and it’s delicious! For one drink: 2 tsp instant coffee (I used decaf Nescafé), 1 tsp sugar, 2 tablespoons hot water, dash of cinnamon. Added to a large mason jar (minimized splatter) and I used my immersion blender whisk attachment and it whipped up in less than 2 mins. Spooned on top of warm almond milk and it was divine. Next time, I’ll add it to a glass with ice and milk. Thank you for this NYT!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made this without the sugar and it’s very good. I have a Breville Barista espresso machine and generally make my coffee drinks with that. This was a nice change and will make it again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I’ve made this with a few types of different instant coffees and it always turns out. I’ve done it with equal parts sugar. Great spooned over warm frothed milk of any type. Prefer hand mixer bc whipping by hand is an exercise. It’s a very special treat.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even though coffee over milk looks prettier I like to pour milk over whipped coffee since I end up mixing it together before drinking. Also add a bit of cinnamon sometimes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This recipe is quite strong and has a relatively bitter taste. I added a dash of vanilla and topped the whipped coffee over double the amount of almond milk than I’d normally use. Next time I make it, I’ll reduce the coffee by half (I used Cafe Bustelo) and up the sugar a bit.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I was so proud when I told my daughter that I halved the sugar amount and she still loved it but then she noted that two tablespoons of instant coffee is the equivalent of six cups of coffee in one drink.  Oh well!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Ha ha.  Yes, it's very strong, the way I like it.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This still tasted like instant coffee but was pretty.  Just when I thought it wouldn’t froth, it did.  This may be how I travel in the future but not sure about drinking it at home...

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I tried this with the Starbucks Via instant coffee since that's what I had on hand and it didn't foam up very well.  I got some froth with a hand held frother but it quickly faded. The proportions may be off too since it's a fine powder. I'll have to pick up some regular granulated instant coffee on our next supply run.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
Most recipes for this call for 2 tablespoons each of water, sugar, and instant coffee.  The article linked here says 1/2 tablespoon, but this recipe says 1 
 and 1/2. In either case, the Times is trying to keep us healthier but it's not worth it.  Use the full 2 tablespoons of sugar for full fluffy effect!  Using less sugar makes it take much longer to froth!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I don't know if Trader Joe's instant coffee is stronger, but the 2 tablespoons was way too strong for me. It wasn't sweet at all. I will use 1 tablespoon next time.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It’s very pretty, but thats about it. I’ve made it twice during lockdown. First time I was worried my sugar was old because it tasted... dusty? Used fresh sugar the second time and it still tasted dusty. Also yes it’s pretty but you have to mix the shut out of it to get it drinkable and then, honestly, what’s the point?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Exactly!! My mum who grew up in Pakistan and all my aunts have been vigorously stirring a paste of Folgers powder in their mugs for decades. My favorite way to have coffee - it’s like a latte but better!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: If you don’t have instant coffee at home, what could be used to substitute?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My wife and I disagreed on how long to whip the ingredients with a hand mixer. I was satisfied after 2 minutes but she read online it takes 7 minutes. At that length of time, it was VERY thick. Too thick in fact. Considering putting it on pancakes though!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I think 7 minutes was for hand whisking! I’m going to try that method for an arm workout! :)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I like it with half cream and half milk. If I'm feeling frisky I add vodka.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Whipping with a mixer is easier starting at slow until it gets foamy, and then turning speed up for final consistence. This way spilling is minimal.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Rather than putting on top of milk I blend together almond milk and ice (think frappuccino texture). I then fold the whipped coffee into it, and drink through a large straw (occasionally stirring as the parts desperate)

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I substituted the hot water for warmed heavy cream.  Game Changer!  I realize this ups the calories but what a creamy delicious treat!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Greeks call this Frape’ and have been drinking it before I was born using Nescafé. It is humorous to me that it is now the “hot” cold coffee drink.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: 20 summers ago I was on a river trip in Colorado with a friend of Greek descent. She introduced me to "River Frappes." Put a can of Nestle crema, scoop of Nescafe, dash of turbinado sugar and filtered/purified river water (brain-numbing cold at that altitude) in a Nalgene bottle and shake like hell until it's frothed up. Best summer drink ever. She strongly cautioned against using anything other than Nescafe! Having tried other brands since, I have to admit she was right. Nescafe or nothing!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely use an electric hand mixer. Use only one beater. A fun and tasty drink. And God knows we could all use some fun nowadays.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My college-aged daughter made this drink & loved it.  But, she does not usually drink coffee and was hyper for the entire day!  Our instant coffee says to use 1 rounded tsp with 6 ozs. of water to make a cup of coffee.  With 2 Tbs. of instant coffee, this recipe has caffeine equal to about 5 cups of coffee.  We are going to try it with only 1Tbs. in the future. Is this drink usually made with expresso?  Expresso has a stronger taste and less caffeine than regular coffee.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The recipe is too intense for one cup of coffee. Two tablespoons of instant coffee equals six cups of coffee, according to what’s on the jar of instant coffee I used (Cafe Bustelo)! I’m not sure what is right. Experiment.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: It's verrryyy strong. Next time I'm going to half the amount of coffee but same amount of water.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: this is a great drink and it soo good and i also love the fact that you can make hot and cold

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
No Fat milk whipped, then heated below boiling will give your a stupendously high heated froth

We use regular, not instant expresso, 2-3ozs of NF milk, 1/4 teaspoon of real vanilla flavoring(the alcohol enhances the froth, any(!, repeat any!! fat in the milk reduces the froth), heated a microwave minute after a quick whip, added to a cup with the hot expresso and a 1/2 teaspoon of cocoa, a few drops of coconut oil(to help the cocoa meld  into the expresso) Add the cubes last, if at all.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: I didn’t check the notes before making it. Hopefully you did :D It was TOO STRONG for our tastes!

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Question: is the sugar necessary?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: This looked amazing but when I made it, I took two sips and threw the rest away.  Way, way too strong.  If made with much less instant coffee, it might be a great beverage, but not with the recipe as written.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: My instinct would be to pour over nice black coffee, not milk... Anyone done that?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Blend 1 tsp instant coffee, 1 tsp sugar and 3/4 cup of  cold milk per serving. Pour into a glass with a scoop or two of ice cream. Very refreshing

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
reply to dd (4 years ago as of today, 7 march 2025)...
i drank that in greece almost 30 years ago, and it was called Nescafé Frappé.  divine.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Would this recipe work WITHOUT the sugar? I'm always looking for new ways to drink coffee, but I just can't stand sweet coffee. Or tea. Thanks.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
